**[p. 167]**

# III. The Course of Events

## 1. The Armed Uprising and the May 10 Elections (April 3, 1948 – May 10, 1948)

### a. The Situation on April 3, 1948

#### 1) The Slogans of the Armed Uprising

Around 2 a.m. on April 3, 1948, beacon fires blazed red atop every volcanic cone (orŭm) along the middle reaches of Halla Mountain, signaling the start of the armed uprising led by the SKLP Cheju Provincial Committee. Three hundred fifty armed guerrillas simultaneously attacked twelve of the twenty-four police substations throughout the island. They also targeted the barracks of the National Police, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, and the residences of leaders of right-wing organizations such as the Association for the Rapid Realization of Independence (ARRI) and the Taedong Youth Corps. This marked the beginning of a bloodshed that would persist for six years and six months until the restricted zone on Halla Mountain was completely opened on September 21, 1954. The armed guerrillas raised the banners of resistance against the oppression of the National Police and right-wing youth corps, opposition to separate elections and separate government, the reunification and independence of the homeland, and anti-American national salvation struggle. These slogans of the armed guerrillas are documented in *A History of the 4·3 Armed Struggle of the Jeju People* (*Cheju-Do inmin'dŭl ŭi 4·3 musang t'ujaengsa*), co-authored by Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-ju and published in Japan in 1963. This pamphlet contains two "proclamations" (호소문) that the armed guerrillas are said to have distributed when launching their action on April 3. Below is the text of a warning letter sent to police officers, government officials, and members of the Taedong Youth Corps who were targets of attack:

> Dear Police Officers!
>
> Oppression invites resistance. The Jeju Guerrilla Corps protects the people and stands together with them. Conscientious police officers! If you do not wish for resistance, then stand on the side of the people. Conscientious government officials! Quickly seize your opportunity, discharge your assigned duties faithfully, defend your posts, and fight to the end against your morally corrupt colleagues. Conscientious police officers and Taedong Youth Corps members! Whom are you fighting for? If you are a Korean, you must repel the foreign invaders who trample our homeland. You must overthrow the traitorous and corrupt elements who sell out the nation and people and massacre patriots. Police officers! Turn your rifles on them! Do not turn your rifles on your mothers, brothers, and sisters. Conscientious police officers, youth, and democratic figures! Rise up quickly on the side of the people and respond to the anti-American national salvation struggle.[^3]

The other proclamation was sent by the armed guerrillas to the people of the island:

> Citizens and countrymen!
>
> Dear fathers, mothers, and siblings! Today, on April 3, your sons, daughters, and younger siblings have taken up arms. We have risen in determined opposition to the treacherous separate elections and separate government, to fight for the reunification and independence of our homeland and complete national liberation! To eliminate the massacres perpetrated by American imperialist cannibals and their lackeys who have imposed hardship and misery upon you! To avenge the deep grievances etched into your very bones! We have taken up arms and risen in struggle. You must protect us who fight for ultimate victory, rise up together with us, and answer the call of the homeland and people.[^4]

These proclamations express approximately three main arguments, which appear to be listed in order of their importance. First is the message of resistance against oppression by the National Police and right-wing youth corps, succinctly captured in the phrase "Oppression invites resistance." Second is the demand for the absolute opposition to separate elections and separate government, and the achievement of national reunification and independence. Third is the expression of resistance to the U.S. Military Government, as conveyed by the phrase "anti-American national salvation struggle." These slogans in the proclamations go beyond merely confronting the arbitrariness of the National Police and Sŏbuk Youth Corps, and clearly articulate a politically colored objective that combines opposition to separate elections and separate government with anti-American struggle. This objective foreshadowed a direct collision between the USAMGIK and domestic proponents of separate elections, who were planning to establish an anti-communist state south of the 38th parallel through the May 10 Elections.

[^1]: In Jeju dialect, a term for a subsidiary volcanic cone (*ŭrŭm*, 側火山).
[^2]: Co-author Kim Pong-hyŏn (金奉鉉) was a former history teacher at Ohyŏn Middle School who served as director of the cultural section of the Democratic National Front (민전) on Jeju Island in 1947. He departed for Japan shortly before the outbreak of the 4·3 Incident. Co-author Kim Min-ju (金民柱) was a student at Choch'ŏn Middle School in 1948 who entered the mountains and joined the armed guerrilla forces.

**[p. 168]**

[^3]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-ju, *Cheju-do inmin'dŭl ŭi 4·3 musang t'ujaengsa* [A History of the 4·3 Armed Struggle of the Jeju People], Munyu-sa, 1963, pp. 84–85.
[^4]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-ju, op. cit., p. 85.

**[p. 169]**

#### 2) Twelve Police Substations Attacked

In the early morning of April 3, the armed guerrillas simultaneously attacked twelve substations out of the twenty-four throughout the island: Samyang, Hamdŏk, Sehwa, Shin'ŏm, Aewŏl, Waedo, Choch'ŏn, Hallim, and Hwabuk under the First District (Jeju) Police Bureau, and Namwŏn, Taejŏng, and Sŏngsanp'o under the Second District (Sŏgwip'o) Police Bureau.

However, official materials published by the military and police over the years[^5] contained numerous errors in their accounts of the April 3 Incident, and several of these errors appear in the description of events on that single day. These materials state that thirteen or fourteen substations were attacked. For example, the "Mosulp'o Police Substation," which was not established until some nine months after the uprising began, appears in records of that day's events. Similarly, Kosŏn and Chŏji substations, which were not attacked at all, are described as if incidents occurred there. Moreover, through the phrase "all thirteen substations attacked," the materials misleadingly suggest that there were only thirteen substations on Jeju Island and that all of them were attacked. This error, originating from the *Complete History of the Korean Police* (*Taehan Kyŏngchal Chŏnsa*) published by the Interior Ministry's Public Security Bureau in 1952, has been repeatedly cited without correction through the 1982 *Jeju-Do Gazetteer* (*Cheju-do chi*). Furthermore, the *Complete History of the Korean Police* describes the events of April 3 as follows: "The ringleaders of the riot seized and occupied the Jeju Police Superintendency and the Jeju Police Bureau, thereby delivering a decisive blow to the entire Jeju police force at once, and then attempted to seize the whole of Jeju in the hands of communist elements…"[^6] This description became the source of errors that continued to be repeated in recent publications. Park Kap-tong's work on Pak Hŏn-yŏng, the relevant portion of which Park has elsewhere corrected as not his own writing, states: "At 2 a.m. on April 3, Mun Sang-gil deceived military personnel, had them fully armed, and loaded three trucks to make a surprise assault on and occupy the Jeju Police Superintendency and Jeju Police Bureau as the signal, then attacked all fourteen substations throughout the island simultaneously."[^7] However, the Jeju Police Superintendency and Jeju Police Bureau were never ambushed or occupied at any time throughout the 4·3 Incident.

Such exaggerated descriptions can also be found in *A History of the 4·3 Armed Struggle of the Jeju People*, written from a leftist perspective. This pamphlet lists fourteen substations, ARRI, Democratic National Front, and Taedong Youth Corps offices attacked, and the homes of forty-one "reactionaries" including Governor Yu Hae-jin and Police Superintendency Chief Kim Yŏng-bae raided and destroyed.[^8] However, the actual number of right-wing figures attacked was considerably smaller.

The actual circumstances of April 3, examined by substation in alphabetical order, are as follows.

The Samyang Substation (First District Police Bureau jurisdiction) was attacked by armed guerrillas, but there were no casualties. One police document records: "Twenty communist bandits attacked, but were completely repelled with no losses."[^9] The *Report on the Struggle of the Jeju People's Guerrilla Corps*,[^10] an armed guerrilla document, also describes the April 3 substation attack, stating: "Police: 6 men; our unit: 16 men. Weapons in possession: one Type 99 rifle, 2 units of dynamite, 4 incendiary grenades. Engaged in direct combat, pressing to the front gate of the substation and breaking the glass with sharpened bamboo spears, but the enemy's fire was intense and we withdrew for fear of reinforcements arriving. Casualties on both sides: none."

The Sehwa Substation in Kujwa District was attacked by armed guerrillas and temporarily occupied. About ten police officers were on duty; all fled and survived, though two sustained injuries from Japanese swords.[^11] However, the armed guerrilla document records the killing of one police officer of North Korean origin and the capture of two rifles.[^12]

Of all the incidents that day, Ku'ŭm-ri in Aewŏl Township, North Cheju County—in the jurisdiction of the Shin'ŏm Substation—suffered the heaviest casualties. Ku'ŭm-ri was a village with strong right-wing influence. According to armed guerrilla documents, 120 personnel were mobilized for the attack on Ku'ŭm-ri, armed with 4 rifles, 5 units of dynamite, and sharpened bamboo spears for the rest. Forty personnel were positioned around the substation when the assault team attacking the nearby Aewŏl Substation advanced approximately thirty minutes ahead of schedule, causing the attack to be discovered and drawing return fire from the substation forces, which prevented the seizure of the substation.[^13] Nevertheless, Patrolman Sŏng Wŏn-hwa (宋元和), a native of North Korea, was ambushed at his lodgings and stabbed in fourteen places with swords and bamboo spears, sustaining severe wounds, yet managed to reach the substation and survive.[^14] Beyond right-wing figures, the armed guerrillas also attacked their family members, killing two teenage daughters of Mun Yŏng-baek (文永伯), who was engaged in right-wing activities. In total, five right-wing figures and family members were killed in Ku'ŭm-ri that day, with about ten more wounded. One police officer was seriously injured, and two armed guerrillas were killed in the fighting. An explosive device thrown into the yard of the Aewŏl Substation failed to detonate, causing no damage; rainwater from the previous day had collected in the yard, and the homemade explosive malfunctioned. Eighty armed guerrillas were mobilized for the Aewŏl Substation attack.[^15]

At the Waedo Substation in Jeju Town, Patrolman Sŏn-u Chung-t'ae (鮮于仲泰), a North Korean native, was shot by the armed guerrillas and killed. Fourteen armed guerrillas were mobilized, armed with one Type 99 rifle.[^16]

The Choch'ŏn Substation, which had been in a relatively disadvantageous defensive position due to the torture-death incident of March 6 (about a month earlier) that resulted in the replacement of most of its officers, suffered no casualties when attacked in the early morning of April 3. Patrolman Chŏng To-il (鄭道日), then stationed at Choch'ŏn Substation, testified: "A patrolman happened to look outside and spotted armed figures approaching the substation surreptitiously. He fired one warning shot, and they all fled—that ended the situation."[^17] The armed guerrilla document states: "Forty personnel with two Type 99 rifles completely succeeded in the encirclement, but withdrew when discovered in advance."[^18]

The Hallim Substation merely maintained its defense without actively engaging when attacked. The armed guerrillas likewise did not press their attack actively, so there were no casualties within the substation. However, Patrolman Kim Nok-man (金祿萬), a North Korean native, was attacked at his lodgings (an inn) and killed. Two other officers were ambushed at their lodgings and wounded. The armed guerrillas confined the police to the substation, then sought out and attacked the homes of identified right-wing targets. Figures such as Hyŏn Chu-sŏn (玄周善), who served as Inspection Committee Chair of the ARRI Jeju Island chapter and chairman of Hallim Township, were wounded. One police officer died, two were wounded, and six members of right-wing organizations including the ARRI and Sŏbuk Youth Corps were wounded.[^19]

The armed guerrillas attacked not only right-wing figures but their family members as well, killing two teenage daughters of Mun Yŏng-baek (文永伯), who was engaged in right-wing activities. Ultimately, five right-wing figures and family members died in Ku'ŭm-ri that day, with about ten more wounded. One police officer suffered serious injury, and two armed guerrillas were killed in the fighting.

**[p. 171]**

The Hwabuk Substation suffered heavy damage. In particular, it was engulfed in flames set by armed guerrillas and completely burned down. An office attendant (사환) on overnight duty inside the substation died in the fire. Patrolman Kim Chang-ha (金章河) and his wife, who rented a room in a private home, were attacked by armed guerrillas and killed. In total, three people died in Hwabuk: one police officer, one police officer's family member, and one office attendant. The substation was completely destroyed. The attack was carried out by fourteen armed guerrillas armed with one Type 99 rifle.[^20]

The armed guerrillas who attacked the Namwŏn Substation (Second District Police Bureau jurisdiction) numbered ten, armed with two Type 99 rifles, Japanese swords, and sharpened bamboo spears.[^21] The first to fall victim were Taedong Youth Corps members serving as auxiliary patrol officers. Auxiliary officer Pang Sŏng-hwa was shot in the abdomen and killed as the armed guerrillas rushed inside the substation. Another auxiliary officer, Kim Sŏk-hun (金碩訓), was struck with a cudgel and sword but barely managed to escape. The armed guerrillas then captured Patrolman Ko Il-su and killed him with a sword. Finally, they broke open the substation's weapons storage, took American carbine rifles, air rifles, and ammunition, and disappeared. One police officer and one auxiliary officer died at the Namwŏn Substation that day, and two auxiliary officers were wounded.[^22]

The Taejŏng Substation, located in Taejŏng Valley (대정골),[^23] also came under attack, with one police officer seriously wounded. The substation, a small facility using the Posŏng-ri township office as its administrative space, had only three police officers; two were on duty that day. One of them, Patrolman Yi Mu-ung (李武雄), was shot and seriously wounded.[^24] The armed guerrilla document, however, states that seven personnel participated, killed one police officer outright with two shots.[^25]

The Mosulp'o Substation was located in Mosulp'o, also a garrison location of the Constabulary's 9th Regiment. For this reason, it was not attacked. However, armed guerrillas hurled explosives at the residence of Kang Pil-sŏng (姜必生), the Taedong Youth Corps representative for Taejŏng Township. Kang sustained injuries from explosive fragments but survived, and afterward joined the National Police.[^26]

The Sŏngsanp'o Substation had fourteen police officers on staff, but only three were on duty that night. Forty armed guerrillas carrying two Type 99 rifles attempted an attack, but when police opened fire, all fled, resulting in no casualties. The armed guerrilla document reveals that both rifles malfunctioned, forcing a withdrawal.[^27]

The Hamdŏk Substation suffered no direct attack by armed guerrillas, but two police officers went missing. One account holds that one police officer connected with the armed guerrillas kidnapped another police officer and brought him to join the guerrilla forces; the armed guerrilla document is worded to this effect.[^28]

Based on on-site investigation, the casualties on April 3 were as follows. Police: 4 dead, 6 wounded, 2 missing. Right-wing figures and civilians: 8 dead, 19 wounded. Armed guerrillas: 2 dead, 1 captured. The casualties by location were:

- Hwabuk: 1 police officer dead, 2 civilians dead, substation completely destroyed
- Shin'ŏm/Ku'ŭm: 1 police officer wounded, 5 civilians dead, about 10 civilians wounded, 2 armed guerrillas dead, 4 houses burned
- Namwŏn: 1 police officer dead, 1 civilian dead, 2 civilians wounded
- Hallim: 1 police officer dead, 2 police officers wounded, 6 civilians wounded, 1 armed guerrilla captured
- Waedo: 1 police officer dead
- Hamdŏk: 2 police officers missing
- Sehwa: 2 police officers wounded
- Taejŏng: 1 police officer wounded, 1 civilian wounded

These statistics differ from the armed guerrilla records. The armed guerrilla records claim ten police officers were killed in the April 3 attacks. Even accounting for the killing of one of the two missing Hamdŏk officers, this leaves a discrepancy of about five. As the cases of Sehwa and Taejŏng substations suggest, when armed guerrillas fired from outside and an officer fell, they appear to have reported him as "killed outright" to their superiors, when in fact the officer had only been wounded, according to various testimonies. The armed guerrilla document also mentions "Sŏbuk Youth Corps: 7 dead," but this cannot be verified.[^29]

**[p. 172]**

**[p. 173]**

Memorial stones commemorating police officers who died that day have been erected in villages throughout the island. Following policy, all patrol officers (sungyŏng) who died were posthumously advanced two ranks to sergeant (kyŏngwi), and all sergeants (kyŏngsa) were advanced two ranks to inspector (kyŏngam). This policy of posthumous promotion by two ranks applied not only to that day but to all police officers who died throughout the course of the 4·3 Incident.[^30]

[^30]: *Dong-A Ilbo*, May 15, 1948; *Kukje Sinmun*, October 7, 1948; *Hansŏng Ilbo*, November 19, 1948.

### b. Organization and Military Strength of the Armed Guerrillas

#### 1) Composition of the Armed Guerrilla Forces

As soon as the armed uprising began on April 3, the primary concern of military and police authorities was to determine how the armed guerrillas were organized and trained, and what their manpower and weapons capacity were. This question was also related to the subsequent issue of whether the armed uprising was organized and planned in advance, or whether—as official sources claimed—it was connected to the central South Korean Labour Party (SKLP), North Korea, and the Soviet Union, and whether the Jeju armed guerrillas received weapons and personnel from external sources. The 1967 *History of the Korean War* (*Hankuk Chŏnjaengsa*) published by the Ministry of National Defense describes the military strength and weapons of the armed guerrillas as follows:

> During the Pacific War of World War II, the Japanese military stationed a total of 60,668 personnel on Jeju Island and maintained posture for a final homeland defense. With their defeat, the Japanese military buried fortified positions on and enormous amounts of weapons and ammunition in Halla Mountain and withdrew…. The Jeju People's Liberation Army collected the weapons the Japanese buried in the mountains upon withdrawal, armed themselves with them, and military training was conducted by personnel who had served in the Chinese Eighth Route Army, employing guerrilla warfare tactics used in China, proceeding with considerable confidence. Their armed forces numbered 500, with an additional 1,000 who were drawn along, bringing the total to approximately 1,500.[^31]

This passage, under the sub-heading "Military Background of the Jeju Riot," summarizes three main claims: first, the armed guerrillas dug up enormous quantities of weapons and ammunition buried by the Japanese in Halla Mountain and armed themselves; second, their numbers reached "500 armed rioters and 1,000 non-armed rioters"; and third, military training was provided by Eighth Route Army personnel using Chinese guerrilla warfare tactics. In a word, this content—summarized as "powerful armed force"—has since been largely replicated in other official publications and cited as justification for harsh military operations. However, the claim that approximately 500 personnel were armed with Japanese weapons and that Eighth Route Army personnel conducted guerrilla training differs considerably from the actual situation in the early stages of the armed uprising.

[^31]: Ministry of National Defense, War History Compilation Committee, *Hankuk Chŏnjaengsa* [History of the Korean War], vol. 1: *Haebang kwa Kŏn'gun* [Liberation and Army Building], 1967, p. 437.

**[p. 174]**

**[p. 175]**

Exaggeration about the armed guerrillas was equally evident in leftist materials. *Noryŏk Inmin* [Labor and People], the organ of the SKLP Central Committee, in its first report on the April 3 Incident, described the armed guerrillas' manpower and weapons as follows:

> Composition: Comprised of approximately ○ thousand leaders of the democratic movement—especially those who had gone into hiding after being pursued by reactionary police in connection with the February 7th National Salvation Struggle against the UN—the force consists mainly of young and middle-aged men centered on youth corps members who had served in the student soldier program and the Chinese military. Noteworthy is the bold participation of students from Jeju Agricultural School and Jeju Middle School, who launched a school strike when the struggle began on April 3 and joined in, forming an ironclad formation.
>
> Weaponry: Weapons and ammunition concealed and stored by the Japanese during wartime military preparedness for 200,000 troops, as well as weapons thrown into the sea after the American military landing, have been recovered and secured. In addition to sharpened bamboo spears, steel helmets, and Japanese swords, there are reportedly pistols, rifles, and machine guns, and even artillery. Ammunition, hand grenades, and other ordnance are said to be in virtually unlimited supply.[^32]

In *A History of the 4·3 Armed Struggle of the Jeju People*, written from a leftist perspective, the armed guerrilla force is listed as numbering 3,000.[^33] The claim that they were armed with weapons buried by the Japanese is consistent with official sources, but the assertions that the guerrilla force numbered in the thousands—and that they possessed machine guns, artillery, and seemingly unlimited ammunition and hand grenades—reveal an even greater degree of exaggeration than that found in official materials. While the exaggeration in official sources appears designed to justify harsh military operations, the SKLP's exaggeration seems intended for propaganda and agitation throughout Korea following the failure to block the May 10 Elections.

By contrast, John Merrill, who strove for relatively objective expression through analysis of existing materials and U.S. classified documents, wrote in his scholarly paper: "Of the main force of 500, roughly half were armed with rifles, the remainder with swords, sickles, sharpened bamboo spears, homemade hand grenades, explosives, picks, and shovels."[^34]

What, then, was the actual military strength of the armed guerrillas, and how were they organized?

[^32]: *Noryŏk Inmin* [Labor and People], May 25, 1948.
[^33]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-ju, op. cit., p. 83.
[^34]: John Merrill, "The Cheju-Do Rebellion," *Journal of Korean Studies*, vol. 2, 1980, pp. 166–167.

**[p. 176]**

Examining the initial armed guerrilla force at the outset of the uprising, it appears to have numbered around 300. The *Report on the Struggle of the Jeju People's Guerrilla Corps*, seized from the armed guerrillas, records that the personnel just prior to April 3 were organized as follows: 100 guerrilla corps members, 200 self-defense unit members, and 20 special police unit members, for a total of 320. On April 3 itself, 350 personnel were mobilized for the substation attacks.[^35] However, police adjusted these numbers based on their convenience. The armed guerrilla force was first mentioned in a report received by the Police Administration Bureau on April 15, when the figure of 300–400 was cited.[^36] Kim Chŏng-ho, who served as Chief of the Public Security Division at the Police Administration Bureau and was dispatched to Jeju immediately after the incident as Commander of the Jeju Emergency Garrison, spoke to reporters on April 29 and inflated the armed guerrilla force to 2,000.[^37] On the same day, Kim Tae-bong (金大鳳), spokesman of the Police Administration Bureau, stated the armed guerrilla force numbered "500–600."[^38] Days later, Kim Tae-bong reduced the figure, saying "the number truly qualifying as rioters is only 200–300."[^39] A few days later still, Kim Tae-bong stated: "The actual rioter count is estimated at 15–16 ringleaders and approximately 500–600 others."[^40] Kim Il-ryŏl (金益烈), then Commander of the 9th Regiment of the Constabulary stationed on Jeju, stated the armed guerrilla force numbered 300.[^41]

The confusion over armed guerrilla numbers stemmed entirely from police convenience. A group of detectives dispatched from the central authorities who returned from Jeju around mid-May reported that "police have engaged in approximately 30 exchanges of fire to date, arrested approximately 500 rioters, and seized numerous weapons," yet the figure they cited for the armed guerrilla force was '2,000.'[^42] Having already arrested approximately 500, they could not maintain the earlier figure of 500–600. The Jeju police authorities even released statistics after the crisis was resolved, stating: "The number of communist bandits reached 16,900 at one point, of which 7,893 were killed by suppression forces, 2,004 surrendered, and approximately 7,000 were captured alive."[^43] An August 1948 magazine satirized these rubber-band-like figures:

> According to official announcements, the number of what the authorities call the "People's Liberation Army" (rebel force) has consistently been stated as 500. Despite official sweeps and announcements of results, the enemy's main force is still said to number around 500. Is this figure of 500 truly a riddle? Between the sweep results and the figure of 500, one must be false for the other to be true.[^44]

[^36]: *Dong-A Ilbo*, April 17, 1948; *Uri Sinmun* [Our Newspaper], April 17, 1948.
[^37]: *Dong-A Ilbo*, April 30, 1948.
[^38]: *Tongkwang Sinmun* [East Light News], May 4, 1948.
[^39]: *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, May 6, 1948.
[^40]: *Dong-A Ilbo*, May 18, 1948.
[^41]: Kim Il-ryŏl, *Sillok yugo: 4·3 ŭi chinsil* [Record and Essays on 4·3's Truth] (reprinted in Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 299).
[^42]: *Chayu Sinmun* [Freedom News], May 15, 1948.
[^43]: *Cheju Sinbo*, April 3, 1957.

**[p. 177]**

Meanwhile, the police maintained from the outset an "external infiltration theory" regarding the composition of the armed guerrilla forces. Immediately after the armed uprising, Kim Chŏng-ho, Commander of the Jeju Emergency Garrison, stated: "I acknowledge that the riot did not originate from the initiative of the Jeju Island people, but was brought about by vicious and disreputable elements from the mainland who, through intimidation and threats, instigated the people to action."[^45] Days later, Kim Chŏng-ho claimed: "When we apprehend and interrogate rioters, we find that they are largely butchers; the leftist faction has deliberately recruited butchers from various parts of South Korea and dispatched them to the front lines as tools for murdering police officers, their families, and election officials."[^46] Kim Tae-bong, spokesman of the Police Administration Bureau, also disclosed: "We have discovered cases where butchers were bought with money to commit targeted killings."[^47] The claim that armed guerrilla personnel were recruited outside as butchers was disseminated through the media and used to propagandize "the cruelty of the armed guerrillas." Subsequently, the claim emerged that Chinese Eighth Route Army veterans were involved. Regarding this, Kim Tae-bong clarified that rumors of Eighth Route Army participation were false and that rioters were mainly Jeju Island natives,[^48] but reports continued to appear that military leadership included individuals who had received considerable training from the Eighth Route Army.[^49] The external infiltration theory expanded further into a theory of North Korean military involvement. The North Korean military involvement theory first came from General Dean, the U.S. Military Governor. After a fact-finding trip to Jeju, General Dean told reporters on May 6:

> North Korean communist agents have landed on Jeju Island to direct guerrillas engaged in terror warfare against the May 10 Elections. Both my investigation and that conducted separately by South Korean authorities have reached the same conclusion: outside agents have misled impressionable youth, inciting them to murder supporters of the elections and burn their homes. A village was attacked from both directions by two units… [based on the manner of these attacks,] it appears that operations are being directed by radio from North Korean soldiers present within the island.[^50]

However, an article in the July 1948 issue of *Sinthanji* [New World] stated: "Speculation about the insurgents on Halla Mountain runs in every direction—Chinese communist troops have infiltrated, Japanese communists are present, North Korean People's Army officers are directing via radio. But no one has actually confirmed any of this; only their clever tactics generate discussion and create a stir." The article denied the external infiltration theory.[^51] *Sinthanji* similarly stated in the August issue: "It is becoming clear that the early rumors of assistance and direction from North Korea or the Eighth Route Army are unfounded."[^52] In July 1948, the Commander of the 11th Regiment then stationed on Jeju, Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok (崔慶祿), when asked by reporters "It is said that a considerable number of Eighth Route Army or North Korean People's Army personnel are involved, and that prisoners have been taken...," replied with a single sentence: "There is absolutely no such thing."[^53]

Thus the external infiltration theory regarding armed guerrilla personnel was quickly exposed as unfounded, but at the time it exerted great influence on public opinion. In particular, the "North Korean military involvement theory" was linked to the subsequent "North Korean vessel sighting theory" and "Soviet submarine sighting theory"; all were eventually proven false, but at the time served as important justifications for harsh military operations.

#### 2) Weapons Status

Speculation about the armed guerrillas' weapons ranged widely, as noted above—from the claim that they were armed mainly with swords and sharpened bamboo spears with only a few Type 99 Japanese rifles, to the claim that they possessed machine guns and artillery with unlimited ammunition and hand grenades. Around late April 1948, Kim Chŏng-ho, Commander of the Jeju Emergency Garrison, claimed that the armed guerrillas had three months' worth of ammunition and food stockpiled and that he had personally observed some carrying machine guns.[^54] However, around early May, Kim Tae-bong, spokesman of the Police Administration Bureau, dismissed the rumor that the armed guerrillas possessed machine guns as baseless.[^55]

[^45]: *Cheju Sinbo*, April 8, 1948.
[^46]: *Dong-A Ilbo*, April 30, 1948.
[^47]: *Tongkwang Sinmun*, May 4, 1948.
[^48]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, May 6, 1948.
[^49]: *Dong-A Ilbo*, May 8, 1948.
[^50]: *Uri Sinmun*, May 8, 1948.
[^51]: Cho Tŏk-song (趙德松), "Yuhyŏl ŭi Cheju-do" [Jeju Island in Bloodshed], *Sinthanji*, July 1948.
[^52]: Hong Han-p'yo, op. cit., p. 110.
[^53]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 21, 1948.
[^54]: *Sŏul Sinmun*, April 30, 1948.
[^55]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, May 6, 1948.

**[p. 178]**

These two units withdrew and regrouped, then retreated into the mountains. Judging from the state of this attack, it appears that the attack was being directed by wireless communication by North Korean military personnel present on the island.[^50] However, the July 1948 issue of the magazine *Sinjŏnji* denied the external influx theory, stating: "There are various speculations about the bandits on Halla Mountain—some say Chinese Communist troops have infiltrated, some say Japanese Communist Party members have entered, some say North Korean People's Army cadres are commanding by radio. But in fact, no one has confirmed any of this, and only their clever tactics have become the subject of gossip and controversy."[^51] In its August issue, *Sinjŏnji* also stated: "The early rumors that were circulating, claiming that North Korea or the Eighth Route Army were providing assistance and direction, are being disproven as more is learned."[^52] In July 1948, Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok (崔慶祿), the commander of the 11th Regiment stationed on Jeju Island, flatly dismissed journalists' questions about whether the Eighth Route Army or North Korean People's Army were participating in large numbers, saying: "There is absolutely no such thing."[^53] Thus, the theory of external influx among the guerrilla forces was soon revealed to be mere rumor, though at the time it had a significant impact on public opinion. In particular, the "North Korean troop influx theory" paralleled the later-appearing theories of "North Korean ships" and "Soviet submarines," all of which were later proven false but at the time served as important justification for hard-line operations.

### 2) Weapons Status

Speculation about the guerrilla forces' weapons varied greatly—ranging from reports that they were armed mainly with swords and bamboo spears and possessed only a few Japanese Type 99 rifles, to claims that they had machine guns, artillery, unlimited ammunition, and hand grenades. In late April 1948, Kim Chŏng-ho (金正浩), the Emergency Constabulary Commander, claimed that the guerrilla forces had stored three months' worth of ammunition and food, and that he had even seen individuals with machine guns.[^54] However, in early May, Ministry of Defense spokesman Kim Tae-bong (金大峰) dismissed rumors that the guerrillas possessed machine guns as mere hearsay.[^55]

[^50]: Uŏri Sinmun, May 8, 1948.
[^51]: Cho Tŏk-song, "Bloodstained Jeju Island," *Sinjŏnji*, July 1948.
[^52]: Hong Han-byŏk, previous article, 110.
[^53]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 21, 1948.
[^54]: *Seoul Sinmun*, April 30, 1948.
[^55]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, May 6, 1948.

**[p. 179]**

In fact, when the April 3rd armed uprising occurred, the guerrilla forces' possession of firearms was extremely limited. Among approximately forty armed guerrillas who surrounded the Sŏngsan Police Box, only two Japanese Type 99 rifles and other similar firearms were present; the rest carried hooks, bamboo spears, and clubs.[^56] Yi Un-bang (李雲方), from the South Korean Labour Party (SKLP) township of Taejŏng, testified: "At the start of the uprising, the weapons situation showed approximately thirty rifles, and Taejŏng township had only three rifles."[^57] The paucity of the guerrilla forces' weapons is further confirmed by captured materials. In June 1948, Yu Tong-yŏl (柳東悅), the chief of the Liaison Bureau, released a statement on Constabulary operations in Jeju. This statement reported the Constabulary's results for the month of April 27 through May 27, listing "3,126 prisoners and surrendered personnel, 7 deaths, 1 abandoned corpse," with captured weapons totaling only "3 Type 99 rifles, 15 hand grenades, 5 dragon swords, 17 helmets, 12 bamboo spears."[^58] The field report by Cho Tŏk-song (趙德松), a *Chosŏn Tongsin* correspondent who conducted reportage in Jeju in June 1948, contains similar content. Having examined the weapons that suppression troops claimed to have seized when they raided the "Taejŏng Township People's Military Command Post," he found clubs, obsolete fowling pieces, Japanese swords, bamboo spears, iron spears, and concluded: "Their weapons are impoverished beyond compare; where then can we find the absolute necessity to stake life against the most modern weapons?"[^59] U.S. military commander Colonel Brown also noted in his July 1 report: "Approximately 10 percent were armed with rifles, the remainder with Japanese swords and conventional spears."[^60]

The meager weapons of the guerrilla forces were somewhat augmented after the initial uprising. Some firearms were seized from police boxes on April 3rd, and subsequently some Constabulary personnel defected to the guerrilla side. A U.S. military report documents: "The rebels seized 6 American carbines, 1 Japanese Type 99 rifle, and 119 rounds of ammunition in a single day on April 3rd."[^61] On May 20, 1948, forty-one soldiers from the 9th Regiment deserted, taking personal weapons, equipment, and 5,600 live rounds. When twenty of the forty-one deserters were captured just two days later on May 22, a total of 19 rifles and 3,500 rounds were recovered;[^63] however, the remaining twenty-one deserters' weapons significantly augmented the guerrilla forces' arsenal. On February 4, 1949, when a company transporting rifles came under guerrilla attack, 150 Type 99 rifles were seized.[^64]

Nevertheless, despite these rifle seizures, the guerrilla forces' weapons situation remained marked by ammunition shortage, and these weapons were inadequate against military and police units receiving continuous supplies of modern American weapons. Furthermore, though rumors circulated at one point that weapons support was being received from North Korea or the mainland, such claims were merely speculation. This situation is evident in a classified U.S. military command document analyzing the Jeju situation on April 1, 1949:

> The most pressing problem among the rebels at present is ammunition shortage. Captured and seized food supplies are abundant. Caves left behind by the Japanese Army provide fairly safe hiding places, but there is no means of supplying ammunition. The light machine guns and mortars seized from the Korean military have become useless due to ammunition shortage. According to captured rebels, current ammunition stocks consist of only 800 rounds of M-1 rifle ammunition, 90 rounds of carbine ammunition, and 400 rounds of Japanese Type 99 rifle ammunition. The sole source of supply for captured American weapons is seizure from suppression forces. However, the rebels appear to possess reloading facilities for approximately 2,000 rounds of Type 99 ammunition. Some report that the rebels are receiving logistical support from the mainland or North Korea, but there is no evidence whatsoever to substantiate such reports. Continuous naval patrols by Korean naval vessels, aerial reconnaissance, and thorough coastal village defense by police have cut off all possibility of external supply.[^65]

As examined above, at the time of the armed uprising, the guerrilla forces numbered merely around 300, and their weapons, though somewhat augmented thereafter, consisted of only approximately thirty Type 99 rifles at the time of the initial uprising on April 3rd—extremely meager indeed. This fact is corroborated by the guerrilla forces' captured document, the *Jeju Island People's Guerrilla Corps Struggle Report*. According to this material, at the time of the armed uprising, the guerrilla forces numbered 350, equipped with 27 Type 99 rifles, 3 pistols, 25 hand grenades (dynamite), 7 smoke grenades, and the remainder bamboo spears.[^66]

[^56]: Testimony of Kim Yang-su (金暘洙), Sŏngsan Police Box officer at the time (Jemin Ilbo April 3 Coverage Team, previous book, 96).
[^57]: Jeju 4·3 Research Institute, *I am now telling history* I, Hanul, 1989, 225.
[^58]: *Tongа Ilbo*, June 5, 1948.
[^59]: Cho Tŏk-song, previous article, 91.
[^60]: "Report of Activities on Cheju-Do Island from 22 May 1948 to 30 June 1948," July 1, 1948, The Rothwell H. Brown Papers, Box 3, US Army Military History Institute, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.
[^61]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 801, April 5, 1948.
[^62]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 842, May 24, 1948.
[^63]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 844, May 26, 1948.
[^64]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1058, February 8, 1949.
[^65]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1097, April 1, 1949.
[^66]: *Jeju Island People's Guerrilla Corps Struggle Report* (edited by Mun Ch'ang-song, previous book, 19).

**[p. 180]**

### 3) Organization and Composition

How were the guerrilla forces organized and structured? In the absence of surviving guerrilla leadership to provide testimony, it is difficult to know their organization and composition with precision. However, some understanding can be gained through books published by leftist sources, documents captured from the guerrilla forces, and U.S. military materials.

##### ① Leftist-Source Materials

*The 4·3 Armed Struggle History of the Jeju Island People* described the organizational transformation of the guerrilla forces over different periods. When events such as torture deaths began before the armed uprising, self-defense units (*chawei-dae*) were organized at the face and village levels; upon this foundation of popular hopes and support, guerrilla units were organized in various regions including Halla Mountain and dense forest areas.[^67]

The document further states that immediately after the April 3rd uprising, "to further strengthen and develop the armed forces, the 'self-defense units' were dissolved and from each township thirty persons possessed of revolutionary spirit and combat experience were selected to form the 'People's Guerrilla Corps' (popularly known as People's Army); moreover, to ensure its mobility and responsiveness, it was organized into regiments and platoons: △ First Regiment = Choch'ŏn, Jeju, Kuja face – 31 detachment (Yi Tŏk-ku); △ Second Regiment = Aewŏl, Hallim, Taejŏng, Andŭk, Chungmun face – 27 detachment (Kim Pong-ch'ŏn); △ Third Regiment = Sŏgwip'o, Namwŏn, Sŏngsan, P'yosŏn face – 43 detachment (?)." Beyond this, special corps (*t'ŭkgong-dae*) performing reconnaissance duties as independent units and special police corps (*t'ŭkgyŏng-dae*) monitoring the activities of 'reactionaries' and shortcomings of local self-defense units were organized, with political cadres assigned to each unit and subunit for ideological education of the guerrilla forces. It further states that concurrent with the People's Guerrilla Corps reorganization, "to cause the people's mass struggle to be conducted with greater initiative and vigorously, powerful self-defense units (10 members each) were also organized at each township and administrative level."[^68]

Organizing the complexly described guerrilla composition at the point immediately after the uprising, we may summarize: △ elite "People's Guerrilla Corps" actively operating in the mountains (30 members from each township); △ "self-defense units" operating at each administrative level (10 members); △ "special corps" performing reconnaissance duties; △ "special police corps" monitoring local situations; △ "political cadres" conducting ideological education for the guerrilla forces, and so forth.

The *Jeju Island 4·3 Armed Struggle History* introduced the provincial party leadership (*to-dang-bu*) as follows: △ Provincial Party (*to-dang-bu*) responsibility: An Yo-gŏm, Cho Mong-gu, Kim Yu-hwan, Kang Ki-ch'an, Kim Yong-gwan; △ Provincial Military Bureau (*to-dang kunsa-bu*) responsibility: Kim Tal-sam (pen name; birth name Yi Sŭng-jin), Kim Tae-jin, Yi Tŏk-ku; △ General Affairs Bureau: Yi Cha-gu, Kim Tu-bong; △ Organization Bureau: Yi Chong-u, Ko Ch'il-jong, Kim Min-saeng, Kim Yang-gŭn; △ Peasant Bureau: Kim Wan-bae; △ Accounting Bureau: Hyŏn Bok-yu; △ Propaganda Bureau: Kim Ŭn-han, Kim Sŏk-hwan; △ Supply Bureau: Kim Kwi-han; △ Information Bureau: Kim Tae-jin; △ Women's Bureau: Ko Chin-hŭi.[^69]

The fact that multiple individuals held responsibility for provincial leadership and military positions suggests that the above organizational chart represents all responsible persons across different periods rather than a single point in time. Meanwhile, one guerrilla veteran witness described the guerrilla organization as follows: there was a provincial command below which were township commands for each township; the provincial command had a captain (*kae*), organization director, and military affairs director—these three persons formed a "struggle committee" that made all decisions.[^70]

Different testimony reveals somewhat varying organization systems at the township party level. For instance, the Taejŏng Township Party organization had a captain and organization, self-defense (military), general affairs, and propaganda divisions, with major decisions made by three persons: the captain, organization director, and self-defense director. Below the self-defense director were special police corps (3 platoons) and self-defense units organized by village.[^71] The Kuja Township Party had a general director (Officer K) under whom were organization director (Officer O), propaganda director (Officer A), and general affairs—three bureaus—with a separate "special action corps" organized under the organization director's supervision.[^72]

[^67]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-chu, previous book, 77–81.
[^68]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-chu, previous book, 88–89.
[^69]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-chu, previous book, 89.
[^70]: Jeju 4·3 Research Institute, *4·3 Long March* 6, 1993, 76.
[^71]: Jeju 4·3 Research Institute, previous book, 76–77.
[^72]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, previous book, 100.

**[p. 181]**

##### ② U.S. Military Investigation Report

The "South Korean Labour Party Investigation Report, Jeju" prepared under the direction of Colonel Brown (Rothwell H. Brown), the U.S. military commander stationed on Jeju, records the organization and composition of the guerrilla forces in considerable detail. This report, prepared on June 20, 1948, was compiled from information obtained from prisoners who had been in contact with the People's Liberation Army and self-defense unit organizers, and summarized information about both the party and military dimensions of the SKLP, including organizational structure and command systems. A summary of the report's contents is as follows:

First, the "SKLP organization" consists of the South Chŏlla Province Committee, the Jeju Province Committee, the Jeju City Committee, township committees, village committees, and people's committees. The South Chŏlla Province Committee is the superior body issuing directives to the Jeju Province Committee. The Jeju Province Committee is the superior body issuing directives to all leftist organizations in Jeju. The Jeju Province Committee cadres are: chairman Kim Yu-hwan, vice-chairman Cho Mong-gu, cadre affairs director Hyŏn Tu-gil (coordinating and controlling other division directors), organization director Kim Tal-sam, propaganda director Kim Yong-gwan, peasant affairs director Yi Chong-u, youth director Kim Kwang-jin, women's affairs director Kim Kŭm-sun, finance director Kim Kwang-jin. The Jeju City Committee differs from other township organizations. Here two independent committees—a regular committee and a special committee—are organized and operational, with both receiving directives through the Jeju Province Committee.

△ Jeju City Committee (Regular Committee) exercises judicial authority over the party's legal activities in the city. This committee operates on the same basis and has the same composition as other township committees. Committee members are: chairman Kang Kyu-ch'an, vice-chairman and organization director, general affairs director Ko Kap-su, cadre affairs director Kang Tae-sŏk, propaganda director Ko Ch'il-jong, youth director Im Tae-sŏng.

△ The Jeju City Special Committee directs the party's underground organizations in Jeju City alone. It receives all orders from the Jeju Province Committee. Its task is to place informants in strategic intelligence centers such as the Military Government, the National Police, the Constabulary, schools, and rightist organizations, and to expand small secret "cells" within the aforementioned organizations to continue the party's work. These secret cells continuously provide information to the Jeju City Special Committee chairman. A remarkable example demonstrating the ruthless efficiency of this organization engaged in subversive activities was the assassination of the 11th Regiment Constabulary commander. This special committee was created to ensure that if the SKLP were outlawed, the daily inefficiency or obstruction related to it would not occur, allowing it to assume the functions of the Jeju Province Committee. In this regard, the Jeju City Special Committee would expand its responsibilities and become the highest committee of the Jeju underground organization. The Jeju City Special Committee consists of the following persons: Chairman Kim Ŭng-hwan, organization director Kang Tae-sŏk, propaganda director Yi Ch'ang-su, student affairs director Han Kuk-sŏp, finance director Yi Ch'ang-uk.

The township committees are organized like the Jeju Province Committee with various divisions. All are structured like lower military and paramilitary organizations and managed by one member. Village committees, however, have individual committee members handle multiple division responsibilities. Nevertheless, the interrogation report states that village committees consisted of at least three members—a chairman, propaganda director, and organization member. The chairman of people's committees is selected by the bandits forcibly designating and acclaiming individuals among the villagers. In this case, a person who had previously served as chairman of a people's committee organized after Japan's surrender in 1945 was selected. This electoral procedure took place under the surveillance of armed bandits. The current imprisoned people's committee chairman from the civilian detention camp on Jeju states that after the bandits left the village, he was appointed as propaganda and organization director according to their orders.

Second, the "SKLP Military Division" was reorganized as the "Salvation Struggle Committee" in early April 1948, but we will continue using the term "Military Division" here. The armed forces consist of two major branches: the "People's Liberation Army" and "self-defense units." The People's Liberation Army consists of mountain bandits and constitutes the front-line combat force. The self-defense units supplement the mountain bandit force and are responsible for liaison between villages and the bandit units, and also perform supply functions like those of a regular army's supply corps. In addition to the People's Liberation Army and self-defense units, the Military Division includes "cells" and "affiliated organizations." In organizing the People's Liberation Army, each township provides sufficient personnel to form one company of bandits. These companies and battalions often use their own village names. Depending on circumstances, troop strength varies. Regarding the People's Liberation Army's combat order of battle, there is evidence that battalion commanders have intelligence, weapons, supply, and medical sections. A battalion comprising approximately 80 men is divided into two companies and one mobile unit. The mobile unit operates only under direct orders from the bandit commander. Officers are identified by Japanese swords, pistols, and helmets. The composition and average personnel of each unit are as follows: △ Battalion (60–80 men) = 1 or more per township; △ Company (25–35 men) = 2 per battalion; △ Platoon (13–15 men) = 2 per company; △ Squad (5–7) = 2 per platoon.

Regarding the People's Liberation Army's training, reports vary somewhat. One report states: "There are three daily roll calls in two-column formation at the barracks to allow commanders to account for personnel, and following the morning roll call, there is a one-hour running session comprising strict physical training." Another report on a different unit mentions only domestic chores such as gathering wood, making charcoal, and transporting food from supply warehouses while unit members remain in camp. The unit entrance has sentry posts (called *pinkae* in Korean), with two checkpoints approximately 100 yards distant. All persons living in one building are strictly segregated from newly arrived persons and ordered not to leave the vicinity of the barracks. Moreover, buildings within the unit are spaced approximately 100 yards apart, with no personal contact occurring between occupants of adjacent buildings. Only when actually launching attacks do personnel from multiple buildings form into two-column formations for roll call and receive weapons and live ammunition. The People's Liberation Army's political education is conducted by political guidance officers. The political guidance officers continuously educate on the goals of the SKLP, emphasizing particularly before attacks that "all people in certain villages are worthless and deserve death, and are traitors to the Korean People's Republic." They also teach techniques such as not disclosing unit positions and personnel when captured, or giving false information or lies. For security reasons, cadres are not called by name but simply referred to by titles such as "commander." The People's Liberation Army's weapons and ammunition are predominantly Japanese-made equipment, with Type 99 Japanese rifles forming the main component, though American carbines and M1 rifles have been confirmed; one report notes that approximately 25-member mobile units were armed with 10 American carbines and 15 Japanese Type 99 rifles. Live ammunition is distributed only when actual attacks are planned—20 to 50 rounds per person. Bandits possessing rifles return unused ammunition after attacks conclude. Other weapons include Japanese officer swords, bayonets, canes or clubs, bamboo spears, and dirks. No Soviet-made equipment has been discovered over the past three weeks.

The People's Liberation Army's supply is conducted through the SKLP within villages of the township. This SKLP provides food, clothing, funds, personnel reinforcements, orders, and intelligence according to prior plans. The self-defense units were organized at the village level throughout Jeju in February 1948 and were renamed People's Self-Defense Units in early May 1948. The self-defense units' organization is ostensibly created to protect villages, but in fact serve as temporary supply bases and village bases for the cunning bandit forces. Orders to village self-defense units are issued by the SKLP village committee chairman. The self-defense units' task, contrary to their name, is not to protect villages but to join bandit units when they attack villages and actively participate in terror attacks. Furthermore, the self-defense units perform supply functions. Food and fund collection is conducted periodically, with collected food and funds handed to the SKLP village committee chairman for delivery to the bandit units' liaison representatives. Very close liaison exists between bandit units in township areas and SKLP village organizations. Beyond these, there are "cells" and "affiliated organizations." Affiliated organizations such as the Democratic Youth League, Democratic Women's League, and General Federation of Labor are ostensibly independent bodies but support SKLP policies, with many members holding dual membership.[^73]

This investigation report was prepared by Sergeant R. Hunger of the U.S. 24th Corps intelligence staff. Hunger, appearing to be an intelligence analysis specialist, states that in preparing this report, he primarily utilized interrogation reports prepared by the Jeju interrogation team, and additionally consulted as information sources the U.S. Counterintelligence Corps Jeju area team, police, Constabulary, documents seized from, and printed materials from prisoners.[^74] Thus we can see that the U.S. military intelligence network operated in diverse ways.

[^73]: "Report on South Korean Labor Party, Cheju Do," June 20, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Command(1942-), US Army Forces in Korea 1945-49, Entry No. 11071, Box No. 2, NARA, Washington, D.C.
[^74]: Ibid.

**[p. 182]**

##### ③ Captured Guerrilla Documents

Meanwhile, the captured guerrilla document *Jeju Island People's Guerrilla Corps Struggle Report* contains references to organization, personnel, and weapons across different periods, which may be restated as follows:

△ Prior to Armed Uprising
1. Organizational Structure: 10 members = 1 squad; 2 squads = 1 company; 2 companies = 1 battalion
2. Organization: ① Guerrilla units (organized in 8 townships: Jeju City, Choch'ŏn, Aewŏl, Hallim, Taejŏng, Chungmun, Namwŏn, P'yosŏn; 5 townships excluded: Kuja, Sŏngsan, Sŏgwip'o, Andŭk, Chuja); ② Special police corps (provincial military committee direct control)
3. Personnel: 320 (guerrilla units 100, self-defense units 200, special police corps 20)
4. Weapons: 27 Type 99 rifles, 3 pistols, 25 hand grenades (dynamite), 7 smoke grenades, remainder bamboo spears

△ First Organizational Reorganization (immediately after armed uprising)
1. Personnel Reorganization: Guerrilla units reorganized to 250 (this reorganization was conducted because after the initial armed uprising and subsequent combined operations with the self-defense units, confusion arose in joint mountain living and supply problems emerged), then subsequently expanded to approximately 400 due to recognized need for personnel expansion

△ Second Organizational Reorganization (prior to May 10 Elections)
1. Motive: For operational necessity to ensure strict discipline, tight secrecy, and swift action, each township struggle committee military division's guerrilla units were reorganized under provincial command
2. Structural reorganization: 3 members = 1 squad; 3 squads = 1 platoon (10 members); 2 platoons = 1 company (23 members); 2 companies = 1 battalion (49 members); 2 battalions = 1 regiment (110 members)
3. Personnel: 3 regiments totaling 370 members (including 20 Special Tasks Division). Special police abolished, with all personnel reassigned to respective provincial command divisions

△ Third Organizational Reorganization (late May)
1. Motive: As the National Defense Constabulary deployed in force (4,000 troops) and encirclement suppression operations unfolded, personnel were drastically reduced to avoid confrontation and strengthen illegal posture
2. Structural reorganization: 5 members = 1 squad; 3 squads = 1 platoon (16 members; the 3rd squad being a cooking squad)
3. Personnel Reduction: 370 members reorganized to 240

△ Fourth Organizational Reorganization (commencing June 18)
1. Motive: Urgent need for organizational strengthening to prepare for new struggle
2. Structural reorganization: (illegible in original)
3. Personnel: 3 platoons = 1 detachment (*chi-dae*). Each detachment = 60 members. Total 4 detachments = 240 members plus 26 provincial command members = total 266 members

△ Fifth Organizational Reorganization (completed July 15)
1. Personnel: 501 (leadership cadres at all levels 35, communications unit 34, guerrilla units 120, special tasks unit 312)
2. Weapons: ① Rifles 147 (M1 6, carbine 19, Type 99: 117, Type 44: 4, Type 30: 2); ② Rifle ammunition 7,740 rounds (M1 1,396, carbine 1,912, Type 99: 3,711, Type 44 and Type 30: 721); ③ Light machine gun (Japanese) 1; ④ Hand grenade launchers (*t'okt'al-t'ong*) 2 (8 rounds); ⑤ Hand grenades 43; ⑥ Dynamite 69; ⑦ Signal flares 2; ⑧ Military swords (*kun-do*) 16; ⑨ Pistols 8 (1 six-shot, 6 eight-shot, 1 ten-shot), total ammunition 119 rounds; ⑩ Other: hand grenades 103, landmine grenades 8, artillery rounds 4.
3. Notes: ① Political officers at all levels are subordinate to superior political officers, with the highest political officer subordinate to the provincial party secretary; ② Special Tasks Unit (*t'ŭkmu-dae*) is subordinate to the detachment intelligence section (duties: intelligence gathering, personal terror, assistance in military operations and supply; organization: one special tasks unit director and several liaison personnel per township, with remaining 3-person squads, 1 platoon (10 members), and 1–2 members per village; special tasks unit members are excluded from cells); ③ Provincial command and detachment guidance consists of 3-person top leadership: commander (detachment commander), political officer, and operations staff (operations training director); ④ Each detachment's special tasks units are stationed in townships and villages, while detachment guidance, communications unit, and guerrilla platoons live concentrated around detachment guidance center.[^75]

This guerrilla-side struggle report is recorded very thoroughly. The original document was obtained when the police special operations corps killed Yi Tŏk-ku on June 7, 1949, was retained at the Jeju Police Headquarters, and copies came to be held by police officers of section chief rank and above.[^76] According to this report, which records the organizational changes of guerrilla and self-defense units through July 1948, the guerrilla forces' personnel, which numbered 320 prior to the 4·3 incident, were first reduced to 240, then expanded to 501 by July. However, at that time, the actual guerrilla units armed with rifles numbered only 120. Therefore, existing materials recording the guerrilla forces as numbering in the thousands require revision.[^77]

[^75]: *Jeju Island People's Guerrilla Corps Struggle Report* (edited by Mun Ch'ang-song, previous book, 17–29).
[^76]: Testimony of Mun Ch'ang-song (age 77, Yongdam 1-dong, Jeju City; former chief of Hwabuk Police Box, recorded Nov. 11, 2001).
[^77]: The original report dates the desertion as May 21, but the actual desertion occurred on the night of May 20. This is confirmed by testimony from 9th Regiment soldiers and Taejŏng Police Box police at the time, as well as death dates of the deceased Taejŏng Police Box police and assistant. The deserting soldiers attacked Taejŏng Police Box on the night of May 20, killing police, then departed. On the morning of May 21, nearby guerrilla forces arrived at Taejŏng Police Box and burned the station building. The U.S. military report appears to have recorded both events as occurring on May 21. (See Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, previous book ③, 1995, 106–137).

**[p. 183]**

###### ②

**U.S. Army Investigation Report on the SKLP**

A "Jeju Island South Korean Labour Party Investigation Report" prepared under the orders of Colonel Brown, U.S. Army commander stationed on Jeju Island, records in considerable detail the organization and composition of the armed guerrillas. This report, prepared on June 20, 1948, was compiled from information obtained from informants who had maintained contact with members of the People's Guerrilla Corps and the support organizations known as Self-Defense Units, and it summarizes information concerning the SKLP's party and military divisions, organizational structure, command systems, and related matters. A summary of the report's contents is as follows.

First, the "SKLP organization" consisted of the South Chŏlla Province Committee, the Cheju Island Committee, the Cheju Town Committee, township committees, village committees, and People's Committees. The South Chŏlla Province Committee was the superior organ issuing directives to the Cheju Island Committee. The Cheju Island Committee was the superior organ issuing directives to all leftist organizations on Cheju Island. The executives of the Cheju Island Committee were: Chairman Kim Yuk-hwan, Vice-Chairman Cho Mong-gu, Executive Affairs Director Hyŏn Tu-gil (coordinating and controlling other department directors), Organization Director Kim Tal-sam, Propaganda Director Kim Yong-gwan, Peasant Affairs Director Yi Chong-u, Youth Affairs Director Kim Kwang-jin, Women's Affairs Director Kim Kŭm-sun, and Finance Director Kim Kwang-jin. The Cheju Town Committee differed from other township organizations. Here, two independent committees—a general committee and a special committee—were organized to conduct activities, with both committees receiving directives through the Cheju Island Committee. △ The Cheju Town Committee (General Committee) held judicial authority over legal activities of the party within the town. This committee operated on the same basis as other township committees and possessed the same structure. Its members were: Chairman Kang Kyu-ch'an, Vice-Chairman and Organization Director concurrent with General Affairs Director Ko Kap-su, Executive Affairs Director Kang Tae-sŏk, Propaganda Director Ko Ch'il-jong, and Youth Affairs Director Im T'ae-sŏng. △ The Cheju Town Special Committee directed the party's underground organizations existing only in Cheju Town. It received all orders from the Cheju Island Committee. Its tasks were to plant operatives (ch'akpach'i) in strategic information-gathering centers such as the Military Government, National Police, Constabulary, schools, and rightist organizations, and to expand small secret "cells" within these aforementioned organizations to continue the party's work. These secret cells continuously provided information to the Chairman of the Cheju Town Special Committee. A striking example demonstrating the reckless and harsh efficiency of this subversive organization was the assassination of the commander of the 11th Regiment of the Constabulary. This Special Committee was created to allow the SKLP—if it were declared illegal—to carry out the functions of the Cheju Island Committee without suffering the usual inefficiencies and impediments resulting from that illegalization. In this regard, the Cheju Town Special Committee would expand its responsibilities and become the highest committee of the Cheju Island underground organization. The Cheju Town Special Committee was composed of the following personnel: Chairman Kim Ŭng-hwan, Organization Director Kang Tae-sŏk, Propaganda Director Yi Ch'ang-su, Student Affairs Director Han Kuk-sŏp, and Finance Director Yi Ch'ang-uk. Township committees were organized with multiple departments, similar to the highest committee of Cheju Island. They were also organized like all subordinate military and paramilitary organizations and were managed by a single member.

**[p. 184]**

Village committees had individual members serving concurrently in multiple departments. However, the interrogation team report clarified that village committees consisted of at minimum three members: a chairman, a propaganda director, and an organization director. The chairman of the People's Committee was selected through a method in which insurgents forced villagers to designate and acclaim a person. In this case, a person was selected who had previously served as chairman of a People's Committee organized after Japan's surrender in 1945. This selection procedure took place under the surveillance of armed insurgents. A People's Committee chairman currently detained in a civilian internment camp on Cheju Island stated that he was appointed as propaganda and organization director following orders given by the insurgents after they left the village. Second, the "SKLP Military Division" was changed to the "Committee for National Salvation through Struggle" in early April 1948, but the term "Military Division" will continue to be used here. The armed forces were composed of two major divisions: the "People's Guerrilla Corps" and the "Self-Defense Units." The membership of the People's Guerrilla Corps consisted of mountain insurgents who functioned as the frontline combat units. The Self-Defense Units, in addition to their functions of supplementing the mountain insurgent manpower and maintaining contact between villages and insurgent units, performed the supply functions of a regular military. Beside the People's Guerrilla Corps and Self-Defense Units, the Military Division included "cells" and "related organizations." In organizing the structure of the People's Guerrilla Corps, each township provided sufficient personnel to form one insurgent company. These companies and battalions frequently used the names of their respective home villages. The force levels varied according to circumstances. Regarding the combat order of the People's Guerrilla Corps, there is evidence that battalion commanders possessed organizations including an intelligence section, weapons section, supply section, and medical section. One battalion composed of approximately eighty men was divided into two companies and one mobile unit. The mobile unit moved only under direct orders from the insurgent commander. Officers were identified by Japanese-style swords, pistols, and steel helmets. Regarding the composition and average personnel strength of each unit: △ Battalion (60–80 men) = one or more per township △ Company (25–35 men) = two companies per battalion △ Platoon (13–15 men) = two platoons per company △ Squad (5–7 men) = two squads per platoon. Regarding the training situation of the People's Guerrilla Corps, reports vary somewhat in their contents. One report states: "There are three daily roll calls arranged in two-line formation in front of barracks so that commanders can ascertain their personnel, and after the morning roll call strict physical training is planned, such as running for one hour." Another report concerning a different unit mentions that while there, members perform only domestic duties such as gathering wood, making charcoal, and transporting supplies from grain storage. At the unit entrance a sentry (Korean: "pinnae") is stationed, and some hundred yards away there are two checkpoints. All people living in one building are strictly isolated from newly arrived persons and are ordered not to leave the vicinity of the barracks. Furthermore, the buildings at this unit are separated by intervals of approximately one hundred yards, and personal contact between persons in adjacent buildings does not occur. Only when actually going on raids do personnel from multiple buildings assemble in two-line formation, receive roll call, and are issued weapons and live ammunition. Political instruction of the People's Guerrilla Corps is conducted by political officers. Political officers constantly educate members about the objectives of the SKLP, and especially before raids they emphasize that "all people in certain villages have no value and deserve to die, and are traitors to the Korean People's Republic." They also teach expedients such as, if captured, not to disclose the location or number of personnel in the unit, but rather to mislead or tell lies. For security reasons, leaders are not called by name but simply by such names as "commander." The weapons and ammunition of the People's Guerrilla Corps were overwhelmingly Japanese-manufactured equipment, with the Type 99 Japanese rifle forming the mainstream; currently identified are American-made carbines and M1 rifles, and one report clarifies that a mobile unit composed of approximately twenty-five members was armed with ten American-made carbines and fifteen Type 99 Japanese rifles. Live ammunition is issued—only twenty to fifty rounds—when an actual raid is planned. After a raid ends, insurgents possessing rifles turn in unused ammunition. Other weapons include Japanese officer swords, bayonets, clubs or cudgels, bamboo spears, and daggers. During the past three weeks no Soviet-made equipment has been discovered. The supply of the People's Guerrilla Corps took place through the SKLP in villages within townships. This SKLP organization provided, according to advance planning, food, clothing, funds, personnel replacements, orders, and intelligence. The Self-Defense Units were organized in early February 1948 on a village-by-village basis throughout Cheju Island, and were renamed the "People's Self-Defense Units" in early May 1948. The organization of the Self-Defense Units, while ostensibly created to protect villages, was in fact a cunning temporary supply dump and base of the insurgent unit. Orders to the village Self-Defense Units were issued by the chairman of the SKLP village committee. The tasks of the Self-Defense Units—unlike their name—were not to protect villages but, when any insurgent unit attacked a village, to join them and actively participate in terrorist attacks. Additionally, the Self-Defense Units performed supply functions. The collection of food and funds was undertaken regularly, and the collected food and funds were turned over to the chairman of the SKLP village committee for transmission to insurgent unit liaison officers. Very close coordination took place between insurgent units in township areas and SKLP village organizations. Beyond this, there were "cells" and "related organizations." Related organizations such as the Civil Defense Corps, the Democratic Women's Alliance, and the All-Korea Federation of Labor, were nominally independent organizations but supported SKLP policy, with many members having dual membership.[^1]

This investigation report was prepared by Sergeant R. Hunger of the G-2 (Intelligence) Section, USAFIK's 24th Corps. Hunger, appearing to be an intelligence analysis specialist, primarily utilized interrogation reports prepared by the Cheju Island interrogation team while preparing this report, and additionally cited, as information sources, captured documents and printed materials from the USAFIK Counterintelligence Corps Cheju District, the National Police, the Constabulary, and prisoners of war.[^2] Ultimately, it is evident that the U.S. military team's intelligence-gathering apparatus was operating in diverse ways.

[^1]: "Report on South Korean Labor Party, Cheju Do," June 20, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Command(1942-), US Army Forces in Korea 1945-49, Entry No. 11071, Box No. 2, NARA, Washington, D.C.

[^2]: Ibid.

**[p. 185]**

###### ③

**Captured Documents from the Armed Guerrillas**

Meanwhile, the "Struggle Report of the Jeju Island People's Guerrilla Corps," a captured document from the armed guerrillas, contains references to organization, troop strength, and weapons organized by time period, which can be reorganized as follows:

#### 1)

**Immediately Before the Armed Uprising**

1. Organizational System: One squad of ten men, one company of two squads, one battalion of two companies

2. Organization: ① Guerrillas (organized in eight townships: Cheju Town, Cho-ch'ŏn, Aewŏl, Hallim, Taejŏng, Chungmun, Namwŏn, and P'yosŏn; five townships—Kujo, Sŏngsan, Sŏgwi, Andŏk, and Chu-ja—were excluded) ② Special Operations Unit (direct subordinate of the Provincial Military Committee)

3. Personnel: 320 members (Guerrillas 100, Self-Defense Units 200, Special Operations Unit 20)

4. Weapons: Type 99 rifles 27, pistols 3, hand grenades (dynamite) 25, smoke grenades 7, remainder bamboo spears

#### 2)

**First Organizational Restructuring (Immediately Following the Armed Uprising)**

1. Force Restructuring: The guerrillas were consolidated to 250 members (this was to strengthen and systematize the unit because, after the initial armed uprising when the frontline guerrilla unit and the follow-up Self-Defense Units conducted joint operations and subsequently lived together in the mountains, there was confusion and supply problems); subsequently, feeling the necessity for force expansion again, the unit was expanded to approximately 400 members

#### 3)

**Second Organizational Restructuring (Prior to the May 10 Elections)**

1. Motivation: To guarantee strict discipline, thorough security, and rapid action, for operational necessity, the various township-level guerrilla units under the Military Division of each township Struggle Committee were reorganized as direct subordinates of the Provincial Command

2. Systemic Restructuring: One squad of three men, one platoon of three squads (10 men), one company of two platoons (23 men), one battalion of two companies (49 men), one regiment of two battalions (110 men)

3. Force Strength: Three regiments totaling 370 men (including 20 Special Operations personnel). The special operations unit was dissolved and its entire personnel reassigned to various divisions of the Provincial Command

#### 4)

**Third Organizational Restructuring (End of May)**

1. Motivation: With the massive arrival of National Defense Constabulary (4,000 men) and the development of their encirclement and suppression campaigns, large-scale reduction and reorganization of personnel became necessary to avoid conflict and to strengthen illegal status

2. Systemic Restructuring: One squad of five men, one platoon of three squads (16 men, the third squad being an elimination squad)

3. Personnel Reorganization: 370 men were reorganized into 240 men

#### 5)

**Fourth Organizational Restructuring (Commenced June 18)**

1. Motivation: In preparation for new struggle, the necessity for urgent organizational restructuring and strengthening

2. Systemic Restructuring: (illegible)

**[p. 186]**

3. Force Strength: Formation of one support unit from three platoons. One support unit had 60 men. Total of four support units, total 240 men, plus 26 men at the Provincial Command, for a grand total of 266 men

#### 6)

**Fifth Organizational Restructuring (Completed July 15)**

1. Personnel Strength: 501 men (leadership at all levels 35 men, communications unit 34 men, guerrillas 120 men, special operations unit 312 men)

2. Weapons: ① Rifles 147 (M1: 6, Carbine: 19, Type 99: 117, Type 44: 4, Model 30: 2) ② Rifle ammunition 7,740 rounds (M1: 1,396, Carbine: 1,912, Type 99: 3,711, Type 44 and Model 30: 721) ③ Light machine gun (Japanese-made) 1 ④ Grenade launchers (tossed grenade tubes) 2 (ammunition: 8 rounds) ⑤ Hand grenades 43 ⑥ Dynamite 69 ⑦ Signal flares 2 ⑧ Military swords 16 ⑨ Pistols 8 (one 6-shot, six 8-shot, one 10-shot). Total ammunition 119 rounds ⑩ Other items: hand grenade powder 103 rounds, mine powder 8 rounds, field artillery rounds 4

3. Notes: ① Political officers at all levels are subordinate to higher-level political officers. The highest political officers are subordinate to the Provincial Party. ② Special Operations Units (under the Provincial Command Intelligence Section; tasks include intelligence gathering, individual terrorism, and assistance with military activities and supplies; organization consists of one special operations leader and several liaison officers in each township, with elsewhere three-man squads, one 10-man platoon per unit, organized with one to two personnel per village section, but special operations unit members are excluded from cells). ③ The provincial command and support units establish the highest leadership through three persons: the commanding officer (unit commander) and political officer and operations staff officer (operations instruction and personnel). ④ Among each support unit, the special operations unit is stationed in each township and village section; the support unit leadership, communications unit, and each guerrilla platoon are concentrated under the support unit leadership.[^3]

It is evident that the armed guerrillas' struggle report was recorded in considerable detail.
