According to reports, the original document was obtained during the process in which a police special operations unit killed Yi Tŏk-ku on June 7, 1949, and was subsequently deposited with the Cheju Police Agency; copies were retained by police administrators of the section chief level and above.[^4] According to this report, which recorded the organizational changes of the guerrilla and Self-Defense Units up to July 1948, the 320 members comprising the armed guerrilla forces immediately before the 4·3 Incident were reduced to 240 at one point but expanded to 501 by July. However, even at that time, only 120 guerrillas were actually armed with firearms. Accordingly, the existing materials recording guerrilla force numbers in the thousands would appear to require revision.

[^3]: Jeju Island People's Guerrilla Corps Struggle Report (edited by Mun Ch'ang-song, cited book, pp. 17–29).

[^4]: Testimony of Mun Ch'ang-song (age 77, Yondam 1-tong, Cheju City, former chief of the Hwabuk Police Substation, recorded November 11, 2001).

**[p. 187]**

### d. U.S. Military Government Response

#### 1)

**Establishment of the Cheju Emergency Defense Command**

The U.S. Military Government initially regarded this incident as a "public order situation." At that time, with the May 10 general elections imminent, there was sharp confrontation between proponents and opponents of single-slate, unilateral elections, and armed conflicts frequently erupted nationwide. According to U.S. secret reports, between February and March 1948, in the two months immediately preceding the armed uprising on Cheju Island, fifty-five police officers were killed and 144 leftist youth were executed in armed conflicts nationwide.[^5] Lieutenant Colonel Paek Sŏn-yŏp (白善燁), Deputy Chief of Staff of the Constabulary 3rd Brigade, happened to be inspecting the 9th Regiment stationed at Mosolpo on April 2, and was in Cheju Town on April 3 when the armed uprising occurred; after receiving the incident report, he judged it to be a "public order problem" and immediately left Cheju.[^6] When the armed uprising occurred, the U.S. Military Government, on the morning of April 5, organized and dispatched approximately 100 police officers from South Chŏlla Province as reinforcements, while simultaneously establishing the Cheju Emergency Defense Command within the Cheju Police Inspectorate, dispatching the Director of the Public Security Bureau (公安局長) Kim Chŏng-ho (金正浩) as commander.[^7] The U.S. Military Government also issued a Cheju Island Directive that completely severed maritime transportation with other regions and, mobilizing U.S. naval vessels, blockaded the coastline.[^8] Subsequently, on April 10, 100 officer candidate trainees from the National Police Military Academy were dispatched as a second contingent, further strengthening police capability.[^9] Additionally, additional Sŏbuk Youth Corps members were dispatched. Mun Pong-je (文鳳濟), who served at that time as commander of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps central organization, testified in an interview with the magazine North Korea that when "the 4·3 Incident occurred, Police Commissioner Cho Pyŏng-ok (조병옥) called me and asked me to send 500 men with thoroughly anti-communist resolve, and I sent them."[^10]

The central headquarters of the Taedong Youth Corps also specially dispatched members.[^11] Shortly after the incident broke out, Emergency Defense Commander Kim Chŏng-ho claimed that "the disturbance was caused by vicious, disreputable people from the mainland who infiltrated and incited the islanders." Moreover, on April 14, Police Commissioner Cho Pyŏng-ok published an address to the islanders, defining the nature of the incident as follows: "You have been duped by the vicious plot and stratagem of communist elements attempting to enslave you under the Soviet Union by betraying the nation."[^12] However, instead of prescribing remedies for the incident's causes, the policy of suppressing it through the combined force of reinforcement police and rightist youth organizations met with islander resistance and actually worsened the situation. The Sŏbuk Youth Corps, having fled North Korea and harboring extreme hatred toward communism, had already been judged by the senior leadership of the Police Commissioner's office one year earlier to consist of an island population "90% of which was leftist,"[^13] and they inflicted harsh oppression, attracting considerable criticism. In this regard, in early May 1948, Prosecutor Kim Hŭi-ju (金禧周) of the Kwangju District Prosecutor's Office, who inspected Cheju, pointed out that if opposition to the May 10 elections was the direct cause of the incident, then the resistance of the people, indignant at the excessive attitudes of police officers from the northwest, was the indirect cause.[^14] Reinforcement police, arriving without proper training, caused conflicts with islanders through their rash actions. As the situation deteriorated accordingly, General Dean met with reporters on May 6, explaining: "Half of the police dispatched to Cheju Island received only five days of training, and as a result they may have acted excessively. Two police officers who engaged in brutal conduct will be dealt with through a U.S. military court martial."[^15] However, Emergency Defense Commander Kim Chŏng-ho, who sought to suppress the situation through strong police force, implemented a harsh campaign, announcing that "after 8 p.m., passage throughout the island will be prohibited, and violators will be shot."[^16] As a result, on the night of April 24, around 9 p.m., two residents walking on a street in Cho-ch'ŏn-ri, Cho-ch'ŏn Township, came under police gunfire, with Mun Hong-mok (21) dying instantly.[^17] Meanwhile, police, dissatisfied that the Constabulary was not actively participating in incident suppression, experienced friction with it. Police even set fires to mountain villages on their own initiative to mobilize the Constabulary, then claimed that the armed guerrillas had done so. The *History of the Korean War*, published by the Ministry of National Defense, described the military-police conflict of that period as follows:

> The police also sought to ascertain the Constabulary's true intentions. That is, in order to sever the hideouts of the rebels, they set fire to villages adjacent to the mountains. When villagers came running, police claimed that the police had set the village fires, and reported this to the Constabulary. Major O Il-gyun, the battalion commander, refused to grant the order to mobilize, but some subordinate officers mobilized without informing the battalion commander. The police account was that rebels had attacked the village, set it ablaze, and engaged in firefights resulting in many casualties. However, when it became clear that this had been fabricated as a stratagem to mobilize the Constabulary, an even more unseemly gap developed between the military and police.[^18]

[^5]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Weekly Summary, No. 133, April 2, 1948.

[^6]: Paek Sŏn-yŏp, *Sil'rok Chi-ri-san* (True Record of Jiri Mountain), Koryo Won, 1992, pp. 107–108.

[^7]: *Dokrup Sinbo* (Independence News), April 7, 1948; *Cheju Sinbo* (Cheju News), April 10, 1948.

[^8]: *Chosŏn Ilbo* (Korea Daily), April 17, 1948.

[^9]: *Tongkwang Sinmun* (East Light News), April 13, 1948.

[^10]: *Pukhhan Yŏngu-so* (North Korea Research Institute), *Pukhhan* (North Korea), April 1989, p. 127.

[^11]: *Tae-dong Sinmun* (Taedong News), April 13, 1948.

[^12]: *Cheju Sinbo*, April 8 and April 18, 1948.

[^13]: *Hansŏng Ilbo* (Seoul Daily), March 13, 1947.

[^14]: *Hansŏng Ilbo*, May 22, 1948.

[^15]: *Nŭri Sinmun* (Our News), May 8, 1948.

[^16]: *Sŏul Sinmun* (Seoul News), April 30, 1948.

[^17]: *Dokrup Sinbo*, May 4, 1948.

[^18]: Ministry of National Defense War History Compilation Committee, *History of the Korean War*, Vol. 1, p. 439.

**[p. 188]**

#### 2)

**Promotion of Peace Negotiations**

##### ①

**Military Government Commanding Officer's Surrender Negotiations**

When the armed uprising first occurred, the 9th Regiment of the Constabulary regarded this incident as a conflict among islanders, police, and the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. The leadership of the Constabulary in Seoul also viewed the Cheju incident as a public order situation and maintained the position that suppressing it was the police's responsibility, not a matter for military involvement.[^19] The friction that had previously occurred between the military and police also made the Constabulary hesitant to mobilize. The military thought the Constabulary was the nucleus of the future National Army to be established, but the police dismissed the Constabulary with its implications of "police reserves." This caused friction between Constabulary personnel and police in locations throughout the country, resulting in armed clashes and casualties.[^20] As the situation gradually worsened, the Constabulary 9th Regiment stationed at Mosolpo dispatched a special unit (commander Captain Kim Yong-sun) to Cheju Town on April 13. However, this was not for suppression operations but for "security patrol," and the personnel numbered fewer than ten. Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, commander of the 9th Regiment, and Captain Yi Yun-nak (이윤락), the information officer, also issued statements in succession, declaring that the military had mobilized to protect government property and the people's lives and property, thereby clarifying its purpose.[^21]

However, feeling the limits of police capability alone, the U.S. Military Government command, on April 17, ordered the Constabulary 9th Regiment to participate in suppression operations through Colonel John S. Mansfield (John S. Mansfield), commander of the U.S. Army Military Government Detachment 59 stationed on Cheju. It also ordered one battalion of the 5th Regiment from Pusan (stationed at Chinhae) to be dispatched to Cheju as of April 20, and had Captain Clarence Dog De Reus (Clarence Dog De Reus), the U.S. military advisor to the Pusan 3rd Brigade, join and participate.[^22] On April 18, Military Government Commander Dean subsequently issued the following instructions to Colonel Mansfield:

> 1. You are conversant with the situation on Cheju Island.
> 2. The two L-5 liaison aircraft that arrived on Cheju Island on April 18 are under your command.
> 3. Additional Korean National Defense Constabulary forces will arrive on Cheju Island on April 20. Upon arrival, this battalion will be placed under your operational control, just like the other Korean Constabulary units currently there.
> 4. Utilizing military units under your disposition, you will suppress the rioters on Cheju Island and restore law and order.
> 5. Before undertaking large-scale operations, make every effort to contact the leaders of the riotous groups and give them the opportunity to surrender.
> 6. Do not turn over to the police the prisoners captured as a result of Constabulary operations. Place them in barracks prepared and guarded by the Constabulary, and arrange for their rapid transfer to the mainland as soon as possible.
> 7. Report daily situation reports via radio communications to headquarters.[^23]

This order from Commander Dean contained several important matters. First, the order commanded operational control of the 5th Regiment's one battalion to be dispatched on April 20 and the existing 9th Regiment forces. Second, rather than the police, the Constabulary was being set as the target to carry out incident suppression together with the U.S. military. The instruction not to turn prisoners over to the police should be read in the same context. Third, the instruction to negotiate with armed guerrilla leaders before undertaking large-scale suppression operations. This becomes an important clue for understanding the significance of the subsequent peace negotiations that took place between Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, commander of the 9th Regiment, and Kim Tal-sam, the chief of the armed guerrillas. Based on comprehensive U.S. secret documents, it appears the Constabulary first engaged in suppression operations on the night of April 22. The U.S. 6th Infantry Division daily report states: "During the night of April 22–23, 1948, police and Constabulary, in joint operations, arrested sixty leftists suspected of participating in recent disturbances. Police continued to raid houses suspected of harboring guerrilla fighters, and the Constabulary provided support by defending certain areas of the village."[^24] However, as the report's content indicates, what the Constabulary accomplished in these joint operations was not direct confrontation with armed guerrillas but rather indirect support for police search operations.

The peace negotiations between Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl and Kim Tal-sam, chief of the armed guerrillas, on April 28 were promoted in the course of these developments. These peace negotiations represented a very significant crossroads in the development of the April 3rd Incident. Following the negotiations' failure, bloody incidents ensued, and Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl subsequently endured hardship. However, the peace negotiations were not decided by Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl alone. As previously explained, General Dean had already, on April 18, ordered Mansfield to "make every effort to contact the leaders of the riotous groups and give them the opportunity to surrender" before undertaking large-scale attacks. This is also confirmed through Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl's testimony. According to Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl's memoirs,[^25] the course of the talks was as follows: Receiving General Dean's instructions, Colonel Mansfield promoted surrender negotiations by requesting that the Governor (道知事) Yŏ Hae-jin, Emergency Defense Commander Kim Chŏng-ho, Cheju Police Inspector Ch'oe Ch'ŏn, and the Cheju Island Ethnic Youth Group commander take responsibility, but they either feigned fear or claimed to be ill, made excuses, or went on business trips without contacting him, failing to appear on the promised date. Thus, Kim Il-ryŏl states that he became the fifth person to be requested by Mansfield, and Mansfield ordered him to meet directly with the armed guerrillas and settle the matter.

Receiving the request from Colonel Mansfield, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl immediately created leaflets requesting peace negotiations with the armed guerrillas and distributed them from aircraft on April 22. One press report introduced the leaflet's contents as follows:

> The struggle on Jeju Island, undertaken to the death in opposition to single-slate, unilateral elections, is expanding day by day, and the island of peace, Jeju, is becoming a bloodstained battlefield. Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl (金益烈), commander of the National Defense Constabulary 9th Regiment currently mobilized in the area, has just distributed the following leaflet from an aircraft on the 22nd, depicting the fierce nature of the combat.
> 
> "Dear Brothers: We have personally observed your struggle spanning the past half month. From now on, we have come to fully understand your burning patriotism and your firm resolve toward complete national sovereignty and independence, as well as your naked sincerity transcending life and death. Accordingly, we recognize that causing further internecine conflict and bloodshed among fellow countrymen would be fruitless. We of the National Defense Constabulary are not a political tool. I am prepared, with wholehearted intention, to shake hands firmly with you, brothers, in order to prevent any further expansion of conflict among fellow countrymen. We eagerly await your response to this matter. Please indicate, through whatever means, an appropriate date and place for our meeting."[^26]

Thereupon, a response from the armed guerrillas came stating: "The regiment commander must appear in person; accompanying persons cannot number more than two. The meeting place must be in the armed guerrillas' territory; the place and time will be communicated subsequently." After accepting the armed guerrillas' demands, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl reported this fact to Colonel Mansfield and Captain De Reus, the military advisor, and requested detailed instructions. Colonel Mansfield gave Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl the following instructions: "① As the 9th Regiment commander Kim Il-ryŏl, you are authorized to exercise all necessary authority to conduct peace talks with the rioters on behalf of Military Government Commander General Dean. You are granted authority to promise amnesty that would spare perpetrators of murder, arson, and other crimes from capital punishment, and to promise not to pursue other crimes. The fulfillment of all promises signed in writing shall be the responsibility of Military Government Commander General Dean. ② Required conditions are: immediate cessation of combat, disarmament, surrender of perpetrators and submission of a report containing the location, date, and list of perpetrators of the criminal acts," along with instructions regarding the authority granted and the approach to take in the negotiations.[^27]

Meanwhile, according to military documents, Kim Tal-sam had, through a Cheju Island unit commander, requested a meeting with Yi Hyŏng-gŭn (이형근), Chief of Staff of the Headquarters of the Unified Command (通衛部 參謀總長). This fact appears in a "Surrender Recommendation Letter" that General Yi Hyŏng-gŭn, then commander of the East Sea Division as of March 20, 1950, sent to Kim Tal-sam, who had come from North Korea leading partisans. Specifically, Yi Hyŏng-gŭn stated in this recommendation letter: "I recall that when I was Chief of Staff of the Headquarters of the Unified Command, your unit requested a meeting with me through a Cheju Island unit commander."[^28] Whether this reference to the "Cheju Island unit commander" refers to Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl is uncertain. Even if the "Cheju Island unit commander" does refer to Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, it is impossible to confirm whether the timing of Kim Tal-sam's request for a meeting with Yi Hyŏng-gŭn was before or after the "April 28 peace negotiations." However, it demonstrates that Kim Tal-sam harbored doubts about the "validity" of the peace negotiations. This is confirmed in the fact that when Kim Tal-sam opened negotiations with Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, 9th Regiment commander, he first asked "how much authority you have to implement promises." Therefore, Kim Tal-sam's request for a meeting with Chief of Staff Yi Hyŏng-gŭn appears to have been made to secure the validity of the peace negotiations.

[^19]: Chang Ch'ang-kuk, *Yuksa Chol'opsengsaeng* (Military Academy Graduate), Chung-ang Ilbosa, 1984, pp. 115–116.

[^20]: Han Yong-wŏn, *Ch'ang'gun* (Army Creation), Pok Yŏngsa, 1984, p. 134.

[^21]: *Cheju Sinbo*, April 16 and April 18, 1948.

[^22]: "Message," April 17, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Command(1942-), US Army Forces in Korea, 1945-49, Bulk Declass E. O. Misc. Series, Box No. 119, NARA, Washington, D.C.

[^23]: "Cheju-Do Operation," April 18, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Command(1942-), US Army Forces in Korea, 1945-49, Entry No. 11071, Box No. 68, NARA, Washington, D.C.

[^24]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 918, April 24, 1948.

[^25]: Kim Il-ryŏl, "Sil'rok Yugo 4·3ŭi Chinshil" (Memoir Fragments: The Truth of 4·3), in 4·3ŭn Malhanda ② (4·3 Speaks ②), Chŏn Ye-wŏn, 1994 (Cheju Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team).

[^26]: *Dokrup Sinbo*, April 30, 1948.

[^27]: Kim Il-ryŏl, cited essay (Cheju Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, cited book, pp. 315–317).

[^28]: Ministry of National Defense War History Compilation Committee, *Tae-Pirŏk Chŏnsa* (History of Counterinsurgency Warfare), 1988, p. 129.

**[p. 189]**

##### ②

**Intervention of Commander Hodge**

As the date of talks approached, the U.S. Military Government command moved with urgency. Following the instructions of Colonel A. C. Tychsen (A. C. Tychsen), operations officer of the U.S. 24th Corps, Colonel M. W. Schewe (M. W. Schewe) arrived on Cheju on April 27, the day before the negotiations with the armed guerrillas. Colonel Schewe's tasks and activities are recorded in detail in a report he sent to Colonel Tychsen.[^29] The report describes Colonel Schewe's task as: "To meet Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield, commander of the 59th Military Government Detachment on Cheju Island, assess the situation on the island, observe activities, and confirm the plan proposed by Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield to control the civilian population and weaken guerrilla activity. A result report will be submitted to headquarters." In other words, the core of Colonel Schewe's task was to confirm the progress of the implementation of the plan (psychological operations) proposed by Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield. However, several important facts must be emphasized here. First, although the peace negotiations initially originated from the instructions of Military Government Commander Dean, the U.S. 24th Corps command intervened just before the negotiations. The instruction from the 24th Corps operations officer is the instruction of General Hodge, who is both commanding general of the U.S. Forces in Korea and commander of the 24th Corps, indicating that General Hodge directly intervened in the peace negotiations. Second, Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield, commander of the 59th Military Government Detachment on Cheju, initially received instructions from Commander Dean and consulted with military advisor Captain De Reus, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, 9th Regiment commander, and others, conducting preparations for negotiations according to his own judgment; however, from this point forward he had to operate under the orders of the 24th Corps command. The U.S. 24th Corps handles military affairs while the Military Government Administration handles civilian affairs, with the latter being a subordinate organization of the U.S. Forces in Korea command. Moreover, although Military Government Commander Dean can issue some military orders at the local level through the 59th Military Government Detachment while handling civilian affairs outside military purview, military command authority clearly rests with the commanding general of the U.S. Forces in Korea. What role, then, did General Hodge actually play regarding the peace negotiations? This is also recorded in detail in the report submitted by Colonel Schewe, dispatched from the U.S. 24th Corps. According to this report, upon arriving on Cheju Island on April 27, Colonel Schewe first met with Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield, commander of the 59th Military Government Detachment, Colonel Rothwell H. Brown (Rothwell H. Brown), commander of the U.S. 20th Regiment, Captain Geist (Geist), a company-grade officer responsible for U.S. 20th Regiment forces dispatched to Cheju, and Captain De Reus, the military advisor to the Constabulary 5th Regiment, to discuss the situation. Some U.S. military personnel, including Colonel Brown of the U.S. 20th Regiment stationed at Kwangju, had already come to Cheju before Colonel Schewe. At this meeting, Colonel Brown informed Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield of the instructions from General Hodge, commanding general of the U.S. Forces in Korea. These instructions were: ① The Constabulary will immediately undertake operations. ② All forms of civil disorder will be terminated. ③ To rapidly weaken armed guerrilla activity, the Constabulary and police will ensure firm unity. ④ The U.S. military will not intervene. In addition, Colonel Brown asked Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield whether the current available Constabulary forces could control the situation, and Mansfield answered affirmatively.[^30]

Looking at General Hodge's instructions, there is only content to suppress the situation by mobilizing the Constabulary; there is no mention whatsoever of "peace negotiations." This indicates that General Hodge did not fully accept the position of Military Government Commander Dean. Of course, the "peace negotiations" proposed by Military Government Commander Dean were by no means predicated on coexistence with the armed guerrillas. Commander Dean's conception was "surrender negotiations"—to extract surrenders before undertaking full-scale armed suppression in order to prevent large-scale bloodshed. Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl also used the term "peace negotiations" in his memoirs, but the concrete content was "surrender negotiations." However, General Hodge's emphasis appears not to have been simply on "incident suppression" but on "early termination." Colonel Schewe, having arrived on Cheju Island on April 27, conducted large-scale operations within merely thirty minutes. Utilizing the Pusan 5th Regiment's one-battalion forces that had been dispatched to Cheju a week earlier, he conducted searches of villages. With all ports and roads sealed off, house searches were conducted to locate weapons, spades, hoes, axes, and wire cutters. Additionally, police were mobilized to identify suspects, organization leaders, and communists. However, the results were meager. There were few young people in the villages, and when women were questioned about where their husbands had gone, they answered that they had died or gone to the mainland or Japan. When U.S. soldiers determined these answers to be false and pressed for more detailed information, the women responded, "I don't know."[^31] U.S. military personnel deployed reconnaissance aircraft to ascertain the operational situation. Colonel Schewe, accepting Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield's advice that an excessive U.S. military presence would be undesirable, did not go to the operational site but directed all operations and received reports. Operations continued on April 28 as well. Before the operations, Colonel Schewe thoroughly discussed the operational plan with Constabulary 5th Regiment officers, military advisor Captain De Reus, police advisor Captain Burns (Burns), and Merritt (Merritt) of the Criminal Investigation Division (CID). At this meeting, Colonel Schewe conveyed that "operations on Cheju Island must succeed, and moreover, the outcome of the military and police operations currently being conducted on Cheju Island has become the focus of the attention of the South Korean people," as stated by General Hodge. Operations on April 28 commenced at 10 a.m. under Colonel Schewe's observation from the air in an L-5 reconnaissance aircraft. 5th Regiment forces entered four villages via truck and foot march, gathering civilians found on roads and in villages. Colonel Schewe returned to the 59th Military Government Detachment headquarters at 11:50 a.m., then departed Cheju at 12:40 p.m. without receiving an operational results report, returning to the U.S. Military Command headquarters in Seoul.[^32]

This concludes the operations Colonel Schewe conducted on Cheju Island over two days (April 27–28), but questions arise as to why "large-scale operations" were conducted at a time immediately preceding the April 28 peace negotiations, and also why, excluding existing 9th Regiment forces, personnel from the 5th Regiment dispatched from Pusan were mobilized. This appears to be because General Hodge pursued two objectives regarding incident suppression. While confirming and supervising the surrender negotiations promoted by Military Government Commander Dean, General Hodge simultaneously pursued a second objective of probing the armed guerrillas' military capability in preparation for future armed suppression operations. Thus, he had Colonel Schewe conduct operations in such a way that the 9th Regiment was excluded so that Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl could conduct peace negotiations, while simultaneously mobilizing 5th Regiment forces to conduct operations. Moreover, examining the content of the "operations" conducted over these two days from April 27 onward, it was not full-scale armed conflict that would obstruct the peace negotiations but rather reconnaissance-in-force or operations for show of strength. Such analysis is confirmed through content related to Constabulary operations on April 27 and 28 in daily intelligence reports from the U.S. 6th Infantry Division and the U.S. 24th Corps.[^33] The content of the report below exactly corresponds with Colonel Schewe's operations. That is, Colonel Schewe's operations involved mobilizing 5th Regiment personnel and U.S. 20th Regiment forces stationed in the Cheju Town area, conducting searches only in the vicinity of Cheju Town.

○ On April 27, 1948, a trench intended to impede access and exit to the road entering Yŏngp'yŏng-ri village, Cheju Town, was discovered.[^34]

○ Between noon on April 27 and noon on April 28, 1948, a Constabulary reconnaissance unit discovered new road obstacles and cut telephone lines in Oedo-ri, Cheju Town, and Hagwi-ri, Aewŏl Township, located 3–5 miles west of the Cheju airfield runway.[^35]

○ On April 28, 1948, a reconnaissance unit of the U.S. 20th Infantry Regiment obtained a leaflet that, in order to "construct a free and unified Korea," stated that Kim Ku and Yi Sŭng-man must be killed, which was seized in Norŭng-ri, Cheju Town.[^36]

Meanwhile, Colonel Schewe's report, regarding "future operations," noted that operations would be undertaken again on April 29 and 30, but added the caveat that, depending on results of operations already conducted, further operations might not be undertaken. From the fact that there is no record of Constabulary operations on April 29 or 30 in the U.S. 6th Infantry Division and U.S. 24th Corps daily intelligence reports, it appears that after Colonel Schewe's departure from Cheju at noon on April 28, there were no further U.S. military operations.

[^29]: "Disposition Form, Report of Activities at Cheju Do Island," April 29, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Command(1942-), US Army Forces in Korea, 1945-49, Entry No. 11071, Box No. 68, NARA, Washington, D.C.

[^30]: Ibid.

[^31]: Ibid.

[^32]: Ibid.

[^33]: The superordinate units of the 59th Military Government Detachment on Cheju ascend in the sequence: U.S. 20th Regiment, U.S. 6th Infantry Division, U.S. 24th Corps. Daily intelligence reports of the U.S. 6th Infantry Division and U.S. 24th Corps contain daily records of conditions on Cheju Island.

[^34]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 922, April 28, 1949.

[^35]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 823, May 1, 1948.

[^36]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 923, April 29, 1948.

**[p. 190]**

##### ③

**The Kim Il-ryŏl–Kim Tal-sam Negotiations Succeed**

Immediately after the U.S. military concluded its search operations in Cheju Town, peace negotiations opened between Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, 9th Regiment commander, and Kim Tal-sam, chief of the armed guerrillas, in Kuŏk-ri, Taejŏng Township, located in the southwestern region of Cheju Island. Based on Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl's memoirs, the full account of the negotiations is as follows. On April 28, 1948, at noon, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl departed from the 9th Regiment headquarters at Mosolpo, Taejŏng Township, for Kuŏk-ri, Taejŏng Township, the location of the talks, and met with Kim Tal-sam. Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl first informed Kim Tal-sam: "While the 9th Regiment has not commenced combat until now, the military, regardless of individual will, obeys orders alone and engages in combat. Should these negotiations break down, combat will commence immediately." Kim Tal-sam expressed doubt, asking: "You are a military unit under the U.S. Military Government—how much authority do you have to carry out promises resulting from negotiations with me?" In response, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl stated: "I have come following the instructions of the U.S. Military Government Commander. The authority I possess represents that of U.S. Military Government Commander General Dean, and today my decision is the Military Government Commander's decision," and Kim Tal-sam clarified, "I too have been granted full authority by the representatives of the Cheju Island people," whereupon the negotiations proceeded.

Following ups and downs, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl and Kim Tal-sam reached the following agreement: ① Combat would be completely ceased within seventy-two hours; if sporadic clashes occurred, they would be regarded as communication failures, and combat after five days would be considered betrayal; ② disarmament would proceed gradually, and if promises were violated, combat would be immediately resumed; ③ should disarmament and descent from the mountains proceed smoothly, the security of the initiators would be guaranteed. Moreover, the agreed-upon surrender procedures were that, on the day following the talks, surrender reception centers would be established respectively at the Mosolpo Regiment headquarters and Cheju Island airfield, and gradually additional reception centers would be established at such places as Sŏgwi-p'o and Sŏngsan P'o, all to be directly managed by the military with police access controlled. In his memoirs, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl states that, having successfully concluded four hours of negotiations, he crossed to Cheju Town late that evening and reported to Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield, who expressed great satisfaction and stated that, as he had requested, he issued orders prohibiting all police from conducting activities outside their police stations. However, four days after the negotiations, on May 1, the so-called "Ora-ri Arson Incident" occurred when a rightist youth organization set fire to Ora-ri village in Cheju Town, and on May 3, the U.S. military ordered the Constabulary to launch total assault. The negotiations collapsed, and thereafter the April 3rd Incident descended into uncontrollable bloody conflict.

#### 3)

**Adoption of Suppression Policy**

##### ①

**The Ora-ri Arson Incident**

The so-called "Ora-ri Arson Incident" that occurred on May 1, four days after the peace negotiations, played a decisive role in the breakdown of the negotiations. This incident began when members of a rightist youth organization, in broad daylight, descended upon Ora-ri, a waterfront village approximately two kilometers from central Cheju Town, and burned some ten civilian dwellings. Since the April 3rd armed uprising, Ora-ri village had experienced several deaths at the hands of both armed guerrillas and police. On April 29, the deputy commander and a member of the Taedong Youth Corps from Ora-ri village were abducted and their whereabouts remained unknown; on April 30, two wives of east-west-oriented Taedong Youth Corps members were abducted, one of whom was beaten to death while the other barely escaped and reported the fact to police.[^37]

The Ora-ri arson occurred on May 1. At around 9 a.m., a funeral service for a woman killed by armed guerrillas the previous day was conducted. The funeral, held near the village, was attended by three or four police officers and some thirty Sŏbuk Youth Corps and Taedong Youth Corps members. After the funeral, the truck departed carrying only police officers, leaving the youth corps members. Among these were Taedong Youth Corps members from Ora-ri. As they entered Ora-ri village, they sought out houses of persons known to have engaged in leftist activity and set fire to five households comprising twelve houses. Around 1 p.m., as the rightist youth group members were exiting the village after setting fires, some twenty armed guerrillas, carrying guns and bamboo spears, appeared in pursuit. The youth group hastily fled; although there were no casualties in this process, around this time the mother of a police officer from the village was killed at the village entrance. At around 2 p.m., receiving word of the armed guerrillas' appearance from Sŏbuk Youth Corps and Taedong Youth Corps members, a police rapid response unit, divided between two trucks, deployed to Ora-ri village. When the police arrived at the scene, the armed guerrillas had already left the village, and residents were extinguishing the burning houses. However, as police advanced, firing from the village entrance, residents scattered in all directions and fled toward the mountains. In this process, a woman surnamed Ko was struck by police gunfire and died. At this time, Park Ki-ha, who had lost his mother, testified: "When my mother was struck by gunfire and died, an airplane circled overhead for a long time."[^38] The police hastily departed when Constabulary jeeps and trucks appeared. Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, a party to the peace negotiations, received news of the incident and rushed directly from Mosolpo to conduct an on-site investigation. Following the investigation, he determined that the arson had been committed by rightist youth organizations such as the Sŏbuk Youth Corps and Taedong Youth Corps with police support. He reported this to the U.S. Military Government but was ignored. According to police reports, the incidents were said to be the work of armed guerrillas. Regarding this sequence of events, Captain (then Lieutenant) Yi Yun-nak (이윤락), the 9th Regiment's information officer at that time, testified: "When Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl and I went to see Lieutenant Colonel Mansfield to report, unlike the previous occasion when he had been satisfied with the peace negotiation results, he now wore a perplexed expression and told us, 'There are U.S. Army CIC (Counterintelligence Corps) and G-2 (Intelligence Section) senior officers at the Tonghwa Inn; please meet with them and consult.' Upon meeting, the G-2 senior officer was a lieutenant colonel and the CIC senior officer was a major, and regarding our investigative report they obstinately insisted, 'That differs from the police report. It was the rioters' doing,' and we returned with a sense of despair."[^39]

Undeterred, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl continued his investigation of the Ora-ri incident and, the following day on May 2, arrested, in Cheju Town, a Taedong Youth Corps member from the village, identified by Ora-ri residents as the arson instigator (surname Park), and placed him in confinement in Mosolpo. However, after Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl was dismissed, the newly appointed Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng released Park and he surrendered to the police. This caused dissatisfaction among Constabulary personnel, and when the assassins of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng later appeared in court, pointing out Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng's misconduct, this incident was mentioned as one of his shortcomings.[^40]

Moreover, the Ora-ri arson incident was three-dimensionally filmed by U.S. military camera crews. The burning Ora-ri village was photographed from the air by U.S. aircraft, while on the ground the appearance of police rapid response units entering Ora-ri was simultaneously filmed. The circumstances unfolding rapidly at the time were photographed, suggesting that preparation had been made in advance. The film from this silent movie is preserved at the U.S. National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), and is known to be the only visual record dealing with the initial stages of the April 3rd Incident. The film's title is "May Day on Cheju-Do," and its contents are discussed in John Merrill's essay "The Cheju-do Rebellion."[^41] Through this film, the U.S. military edited the Ora-ri arson incident to appear as if it had been perpetrated by the armed guerrillas. Therefore, this film appears to have been produced for the purpose of obtaining justification for a harsh suppression policy. This suggests that by that point, the U.S. military's hard-line policy had already been determined.

[^37]: Testimony of Im Kap-saeng (Cheju City, Ido 2-tong; the wife of a Taedong Youth Corps member who was abducted and escaped), in Cheju Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, *4·3ŭn Malhanda ②* (*4·3 Speaks ②*), p. 152–153; Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 819, April 27, 1948.

[^38]: Testimony of Park Ki-ha (daughter of Ko, surnamed Kŏmosŏng), in Cheju Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, cited book, p. 164.

[^39]: Testimony of Yi Yun-nak (9th Regiment information officer at the time), in Cheju Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, cited book, pp. 167–168.

[^40]: Testimony of Park (Taedong Youth Corps member at the time), in Cheju Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, cited book, pp. 168–169.

[^41]: *Hansŏng Ilbo*, August 14, 1948.

**[p. 191]**

##### ②

**U.S. Military's Attack Order**

On May 3, 1948, Military Government Commander Dean and other U.S. military leadership ordered the Constabulary to launch a total assault on the armed guerrillas and bring the Cheju incident to a conclusion, deciding to adopt a hard-line suppression policy.

**[p. 192]**

The U.S. 6th Division daily report recorded: "Between the night of April 22–23, 1948, police and Constabulary conducted joint operations and arrested sixty leftists suspected of participating in the recent disturbances. The police continued to raid houses suspected of harboring guerrillas, while the Constabulary supported by defending certain areas of the village."[^96] As the report's content indicates, the Constabulary's role in this joint operation was not to engage the armed guerrillas in direct confrontation, but to provide indirect support for police search operations.

The peace negotiations between 9th Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl and armed guerrilla chief Kim Tal-sam on April 28 were pursued through this process. These negotiations were an extremely important juncture in the course of the April 3rd Incident. As the negotiations ultimately failed, a bloodbath ensued, and Commander Kim Il-ryŏl subsequently faced great difficulties. However, the peace negotiations were not Commander Kim Il-ryŏl's decision alone. As noted earlier, General Dean had already, on April 18, issued orders to Mansfield to "make every effort to contact the leaders of the dissident groups before launching a large-scale attack and offer them an opportunity to surrender." This is confirmed by Commander Kim Il-ryŏl's own testimony.

According to Kim Il-ryŏl's memoir,[^97] the course of the negotiations is as follows. Mansfield, acting on General Dean's instructions, sought to implement surrender operations by requesting Jeju Governor Yu Hae-jin, Jeju Emergency Garrison Commander Kim Chŏng-ho, Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner Ch'oe Ch'ŏn, and the Jeju Provincial National Youth Corps leader to come forward as responsible parties. However, whether out of fear, or feigning illness, or simply departing on business trips without notification, they failed to appear on the scheduled date. Thus Kim Il-ryŏl became the fifth person Mansfield requested, and Mansfield ordered him to meet directly with the armed guerrillas and reach a settlement.

Upon receiving Mansfield's request, Commander Kim Il-ryŏl immediately prepared leaflets calling for peace negotiations with the armed guerrillas and distributed them by aircraft on April 22nd. One news outlet introduced the content of the leaflet as follows:

The struggle on Jeju Island that seeks to resist the separate elections and separate government through death continues to expand daily, and the peaceful island of Jeju is turning into a bloodstained battlefield. Army Lieutenant Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, 9th Regiment Commander of the National Defense Constabulary, currently deployed on-site, distributed the following leaflet by aircraft on the 22nd, depicting the fierce nature of the battle.

[^96]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 918, April 24, 1948.
[^97]: Kim Il-ryŏl, *Silrok yugo: 4·3 ŭi chinsil* [True Record and Essays: The Truth of 4·3] (compiled in Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, *4·3 ŭn Malhanda* [4·3 Speaks], vol. 2, Chŏnyewŏn, 1994).

**[p. 193]**

> "Dear Brethren: Over the past half-month we have seen firsthand your struggle. From this moment forward we fully understand your burning love of homeland, your unyielding resolve toward complete self-determination and independent reunification, and the raw sincerity of your willingness to transcend life and death. Accordingly, this command recognizes that further fratricidal conflict and strife among blood relations is entirely without benefit. Our National Defense Constabulary is not a political instrument. In order not to expand further conflict among fellow Koreans, I am ready to extend a firm hand of fellowship to you. This command awaits your reply. Please indicate, by whatever means, an appropriate date and place where we may meet."[^98]

In response, the armed guerrillas sent the following message: "The regiment commander must come in person; attendants must not exceed two. The meeting place must be in the armed guerrilla camp; location and time will be notified later." Commander Kim Il-ryŏl accepted the armed guerrillas' conditions and reported the matter to Colonel Mansfield and Military Advisor Captain Drust, requesting detailed instructions. Colonel Mansfield conveyed to Commander Kim Il-ryŏl the following authority and operational guidance: "(1) 9th Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl acts as the representative of Military Government Director General Dean in all matters requiring authority for peace negotiations with the armed contingent. You are granted authority to promise amnesty sparing murderers and arsonists among the lawbreakers from capital punishment, and authority to promise immunity for other crimes. The execution of all written agreements shall be the responsibility of Military Government Director General Dean. (2) The conditions for agreement are: immediate cessation of combat, disarmament, self-surrender of lawbreakers, and submission of a record including the location, date, and names of perpetrators of criminal acts."[^99]

Meanwhile, according to military records, Kim Tal-sam had previously requested through a Jeju military unit commander a meeting with Yi Hyŏng-gŭn (이형근), Chief of Staff of the Constabulary Headquarters. This matter appears in a "surrender recommendation letter" that Brigadier General Yi Hyŏng-gŭn, then commander of the East Coast Division, sent to Kim Tal-sam on March 20, 1950, after Kim had led North Korean partisans. In the letter, Yi Hyŏng-gŭn stated: "I recall that during my tenure as Chief of Staff of the Constabulary Headquarters, you requested through the military unit commander on Jeju Island a meeting with me."[^100] Whether the "Jeju military unit commander" refers to Commander Kim Il-ryŏl remains uncertain.

[^98]: *Tongnip Sinbo*, April 30, 1948.
[^99]: Kim Il-ryŏl, op. cit. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 315–317).
[^100]: Ministry of National Defense, War History Compilation Committee, *Tae Bijonggyu Chŏnsa* [Counterinsurgency War History], 1988, p. 129.

**[p. 194]**

Even if the "Jeju military unit commander" does refer to Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, it cannot be confirmed whether Kim Tal-sam requested the meeting with Chief of Staff Yi Hyŏng-gŭn before or after the April 28 peace negotiations. What this does demonstrate is that Kim Tal-sam harbored doubts about the "efficacy" of the peace negotiations. This is further confirmed by the fact that when Kim Tal-sam began negotiations with 9th Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, he first asked, "How much authority do you have to fulfill promises?" Therefore, Kim Tal-sam's request for a meeting with Chief of Staff Yi Hyŏng-gŭn appears to have been intended to secure a guarantee for the effectiveness of the peace negotiations.

###### ② General Hodge's Intervention

As the scheduled meeting date approached, the U.S. Military Government leadership moved carefully behind the scenes. On orders from Colonel A. C. Tychsen, Operations Staff Officer of the U.S. 24th Army Corps, Major M. W. Schewe arrived on Jeju Island on April 27th, one day before the scheduled negotiations with the armed guerrillas. Major Schewe's mission and activities are recorded in detail in his report to Colonel Tychsen.[^101] The report states that Major Schewe's mission was "to meet with Colonel Mansfield, commander of the U.S. 59th Military Government Company stationed on Jeju Island, evaluate the situation on the island, observe activities, and confirm the plan proposed by Colonel Mansfield for controlling the civilian population and weakening guerrilla activity on Cheju Island; findings are to be reported to headquarters." In essence, the core of Major Schewe's mission was to confirm the progress of the plan (surrender operations) proposed by Colonel Mansfield.

However, there are important facts that must be pointed out here. First, although the peace negotiations originally originated from instructions by USAMGIK Director General Dean, the U.S. 24th Army Corps Command intervened immediately before the negotiations. The order from the 24th Army Corps Operations Staff Officer constituted an order from General Hodge, commander of the 24th Army Corps and Commander of U.S. Forces in Korea—meaning General Hodge directly involved himself in the peace negotiations. Second, although Colonel Mansfield, commander of the U.S. 59th Military Government Company stationed on Jeju, had initially received orders from Director General Dean and prepared for negotiations according to his own judgment in consultation with Military Advisor Captain Drust and 9th Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, he now had to operate under orders from the 24th Army Corps Command. This is because military affairs fell under the 24th Army Corps while civil affairs fell under USAMGIK, and USAMGIK was a subordinate organization of the U.S. Forces in Korea Command. Although USAMGIK Director General Dean could issue certain military orders through the 59th Military Government Company at the provincial level beyond civil affairs matters, command authority clearly resided with the Commander of U.S. Forces in Korea.

[^101]: "Disposition Form, Report of Activities at Cheju Do Island," April 29, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Command (1942–), US Army Forces in Korea, 1945–49, Entry No. 11071, Box No. 68, NARA, Washington, D.C.

**[p. 195]**

How, then, did General Hodge approach the peace negotiations? This too is recorded in detail in the report of Major Schewe, dispatched by the 24th Army Corps. According to this report, immediately upon arriving on Jeju Island on April 27th, Major Schewe met with Colonel Mansfield (59th Military Government Company commander), Colonel Rothwell H. Brown (20th Regiment commander), Major Geist (officer responsible for 20th Regiment forces dispatched to Jeju Island), and Captain Drust (5th Regiment military adviser) to discuss the situation. Colonel Brown and some U.S. troops from the 20th Regiment stationed in Kwangju had already arrived on Jeju ahead of Major Schewe. At this meeting, Colonel Brown conveyed General Hodge's instructions to Colonel Mansfield. The instructions were: (1) the Constabulary must immediately carry out its duties; (2) eliminate all types of civil disorder; (3) the Constabulary and police must establish firm coordination to rapidly weaken guerrilla activity; and (4) U.S. forces must not intervene. Additionally, Colonel Brown asked Colonel Mansfield whether the currently available Constabulary forces could control the situation, and Mansfield answered affirmatively.[^102]

Examining General Hodge's instructions, one finds directives to mobilize the Constabulary to suppress the disturbances, but there is no mention whatsoever of "peace negotiations." This indicates that General Hodge did not fully endorse Director General Dean's approach. Of course, the "peace negotiations" proposed by Director General Dean were by no means premised on coexistence with the armed guerrillas. Dean's conception was a "surrender operation" to extract surrenders before launching full-scale military suppression, thereby avoiding a large-scale bloodbath. Although Commander Kim Il-ryŏl used the term "peace negotiations" in his memoir, the concrete content was a "surrender operation." General Hodge's emphasis, however, appears to have been not simply on "suppressing the disturbances" but on "early resolution."

Within thirty minutes of arriving on Jeju Island on April 27th, Major Schewe launched large-scale operations. Using the one-battalion contingent of the 5th Regiment dispatched from Pusan a week earlier, he conducted village searches. All ports and roads were blockaded while house-to-house searches for weapons, shovels, pickaxes, axes, and wire cutters were conducted. Additionally, police were mobilized to identify suspects, organizational leaders, and communists. However, operational results were nearly nil. There were almost no young men in the villages, and women answered questions about their husbands' whereabouts by saying they had died or gone to the mainland or to Japan.

[^102]: Ibid.

**[p. 196]**

When U.S. troops, judging these answers to be false, pressed for more detail, the women replied simply: "I don't know."[^103] U.S. forces flew reconnaissance aircraft to assess the operational situation. Heeding Mansfield's advice that too visible a U.S. military presence was undesirable, Major Schewe did not go to the operational site but directed all operations and received reports. Operations continued on April 28th. Before the operations, Major Schewe thoroughly discussed the operational plan with 5th Regiment officers, Military Advisor Captain Drust, Police Advisor Captain Burns, and CID (Criminal Investigation Division) investigator Merritt. At this meeting, Major Schewe conveyed General Hodge's words: "Operations on Jeju Island must succeed; moreover, the attention of the South Korean people is focused on the success or failure of military and police operations on Jeju Island." The April 28th operation began at 10:00 a.m., with Major Schewe confirming progress from above in an L-5 reconnaissance aircraft. 5th Regiment forces entered four villages by truck and on foot and assembled civilians on roads and in village squares. At 11:50 a.m., Major Schewe returned to 59th Military Government Company headquarters and, without receiving an operational report, departed Jeju at 12:40 p.m. and returned to U.S. military headquarters in Seoul.[^104]

Such were the operations Major Schewe conducted on Jeju Island over the two days of April 27–28. It is puzzling why "large-scale operations" were launched on the eve of the April 28th peace negotiations. It is also questionable why—excluding the existing 9th Regiment forces—5th Regiment forces dispatched from Pusan were mobilized. This appears to stem from General Hodge's pursuit of dual objectives regarding the situation. General Hodge directed Major Schewe's operations while simultaneously monitoring the surrender operations proposed by Director General Dean and, on the other hand, probing the armed guerrillas' military strength in preparation for future military suppression operations. Thus, the 9th Regiment was excluded to allow Commander Kim Il-ryŏl to conduct peace negotiations, while 5th Regiment forces were mobilized to conduct operations. Furthermore, examining the content of the "operations" conducted over April 27–28, they constituted not full-scale armed confrontation that would impede the peace negotiations, but rather reconnaissance or show-of-force operations. This analysis is confirmed by the U.S. 6th Division and 24th Army Corps daily intelligence reports[^105] for April 27–28 regarding Constabulary operations.

[^103]: Ibid.
[^104]: Ibid.
[^105]: The chain of command above the 59th Military Government Company stationed on Jeju runs through the 20th Regiment, the 6th Division, and the 24th Army Corps. The 6th Division and 24th Army Corps daily intelligence reports record the situation on Jeju Island each day.

**[p. 197]**

The content of the reports below matches exactly with Major Schewe's operational account. That is, Schewe's operation mobilized 5th Regiment forces and U.S. 20th Regiment forces then stationed on Jeju and conducted searches only in the vicinity of Cheju Town.

○ On April 27, 1948, a ditch blocking entry to the road leading to Yŏngp'yŏng village in Cheju Town was discovered.[^106]

○ Between noon on April 27 and noon on April 28, 1948, Constabulary reconnaissance teams discovered new road obstacles and severed telephone lines in Waedo-ri, Cheju Town, and Hag wi-ri, Aewŏl Township, located 3–5 miles west of Cheju Airfield.[^107]

○ On April 28, 1948, U.S. 20th Infantry Regiment reconnaissance teams obtained leaflets distributed in Nohyŏng-ri, Cheju Town, stating that Kim Ku and Yi Sŭng-man must be killed to "build a free and unified Korea."[^108]

Meanwhile, Major Schewe's report, regarding "future operations," noted that operations would continue on April 29th and 30th but added the qualifier that further operations might not occur depending on results already achieved. Since the 6th Division and 24th Army Corps daily intelligence reports contain no record whatsoever of Constabulary operations on April 29th and 30th, it appears that no further U.S. operations occurred after Major Schewe departed Jeju at noon on April 28th.

#### 3) The Kim Il-ryŏl–Kim Tal-sam Negotiations Concluded

Immediately after U.S. forces completed search operations in Cheju Town, peace negotiations were held between 9th Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl and armed guerrilla chief Kim Tal-sam in Ku'ŏk-ri, Taejŏng Township, in the southwestern part of Jeju Island. The full account of the negotiations as presented in Kim Il-ryŏl's memoir is as follows.

On April 28, 1948, Commander Kim Il-ryŏl departed the 9th Regiment headquarters in Mosulp'o at noon, proceeded to the negotiation site at Ku'ŏk-ri, and met Kim Tal-sam. Kim Il-ryŏl first told Kim Tal-sam: "The 9th Regiment has not initiated combat up to now, but the military will comply with orders regardless of individual will and will engage in combat if orders are issued." He thus made clear that combat would immediately commence should the negotiations break down. Kim Tal-sam then questioned: "You are an army under the U.S. Military Government; how much authority do you have to fulfill negotiated promises?"

[^106]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 922, April 28, 1948.
[^107]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 823, May 1, 1948.
[^108]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 923, April 29, 1948.

**[p. 198]**

In response, Kim Il-ryŏl stated: "I have come under the orders of the Military Government Director, and the authority I possess represents the authority of Military Government Director General Dean. My decision today is the decision of the Military Government Director." Kim Tal-sam replied: "I too have been entrusted with full authority by the representatives of the Jeju people," and the negotiations proceeded.

After considerable discussion, Kim Il-ryŏl and Kim Tal-sam reached the following agreement: (1) combat would be completely ceased within 72 hours; sporadic clashes, if they occurred, would be regarded as failures in communication, while combat after five days would constitute betrayal; (2) disarmament would be gradual, but if promises were violated, combat would immediately resume; and (3) if disarmament and coming down from the mountain proceeded smoothly, the safety of the ringleaders would be guaranteed. The agreed surrender procedures stipulated that reception centers for surrenders would be established the day after the negotiations at the Mosulp'o Regiment headquarters and Cheju Airfield respectively, gradually expanding to centers at Sŏngsan-p'o, Sŏgwip'o, and other locations, with the military directly managing them and police entry subject to control.

In his memoir, Commander Kim Il-ryŏl recounts that after successfully completing four hours of negotiations, he crossed to Cheju Town late that night and reported to Mansfield. Mansfield expressed great satisfaction and, as requested, issued orders to all police prohibiting any activities outside police stations.[^109] However, just three days after the negotiations, on May 1st, the so-called "Ōra-ri arson incident" occurred when right-wing youth groups burned the hamlet of Ōra-ri in Cheju Town. Then on May 3rd, U.S. forces ordered the Constabulary to launch a full-scale attack, the negotiations were broken, and the April 3rd Incident thereafter descended into uncontrollable bloody conflict.

#### 3) Adoption of Suppression Policy

###### ① The Ōra-ri Arson Incident

The so-called "Ōra-ri arson incident" of May 1st, just three days after the peace negotiations, played a decisive role in breaking the agreement. The incident occurred when right-wing youth group members descended on the hamlet of Ōra-ri, about 2 kilometers from the center of Cheju Town, in broad daylight and burned more than ten civilian homes. Since the April 3rd armed uprising, several fatal incidents with casualties inflicted by both the armed guerrillas and police had occurred in Ōra-ri. On April 29th, the deputy leader and a member of the Taedong Youth Corps from Ōra-ri were abducted and subsequently disappeared. On April 30th, the wives of two Taedong Youth Corps members from the same district were abducted; one was beaten to death and the other barely escaped and reported the incident to police.[^110]

[^109]: Kim Il-ryŏl, op. cit. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 319–331).
[^110]: Im Kap-saeng (임갑생, Cheju City Ido 2-dong, wife of a Taedong Youth Corps member who was abducted and escaped at the time), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, *4·3 ŭn Malhanda*, vol. 2, pp. 152–153); Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 819, April 27, 1948.

**[p. 199]**

The Ōra-ri arson incident occurred on May 1st. Around 9:00 a.m., a funeral service was held for a woman killed by armed guerrillas the previous day. The funeral service, held near the village, was attended by three or four police officers and approximately thirty Sŏbuk Youth Corps and Taedong Youth Corps members. After the burial, the truck departed carrying only the police officers while the youth corps members remained. Among them were Taedong Youth Corps members from Ōra-ri. Entering the village, they sought out homes of people known to have engaged in leftist activities and burned five households comprising twelve buildings. As the right-wing youth corps members were leaving the village after setting fires—around 1:00 p.m.—approximately twenty armed guerrillas carrying rifles and bamboo spears pursued them. The youth corps members fled hastily; there were no casualties in this process, but around this time the mother of a police officer from the village was killed near the village entrance. Around 2:00 p.m., upon receiving word of armed guerrilla presence from Sŏbuk Youth Corps and Taedong Youth Corps members, a police mobile unit divided between two trucks rushed to Ōra-ri. When police arrived at the scene, the armed guerrillas had already left the village and residents were extinguishing the burning homes. However, as police began firing from the village entrance and advancing, residents scattered in all directions and fled toward the mountains. In this process, a woman named Ko Mu-saeng was shot by police and died. Pak Ki-ha (박기하), who lost her mother that moment, testified: "When my mother was shot and killed, an airplane circled overhead for a long time."[^111]

When Constabulary jeeps and trucks appeared, police hastily departed the village. Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, one of the peace negotiation signatories, also heard of the incident, rushed directly from Mosulp'o, and conducted an on-site investigation. Based on his investigation, he concluded that the arson had been committed by right-wing youth groups including the Sŏbuk Youth Corps and Taedong Youth Corps under police sponsorship. He reported this to USAMGIK but was ignored; according to the police report, the acts were attributed to the armed guerrillas. Regarding this process, Yi Yun-nak (이윤락), then First Lieutenant and Intelligence Officer of the 9th Regiment, testified: "When we went to report to Colonel Mansfield together with Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, unlike his previous satisfaction with the peace negotiations results, he wore a troubled expression and said, 'U.S. CIC and G-2 senior personnel are staying at the Tonghwa Inn; go consult with them.' When we met them, the G-2 senior officer was a major and the CIC officer was a captain. They flatly insisted regarding our investigation report: 'It differs from the police report. That was the work of the bandits,' and we left feeling utterly defeated."[^112]

[^111]: Pak Ki-ha (박기하, daughter of Ko Mu-saeng), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 164).
[^112]: Yi Yun-nak (이윤락, then Intelligence Officer of the 9th Regiment), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 167–168).

**[p. 200]**

However, Commander Kim Il-ryŏl did not give up and continued investigating the Ōra-ri incident. The following day, May 2nd, he apprehended a Taedong Youth Corps member from the village surnamed Pak, whom residents identified as the arson ringleader, in Cheju Town, and placed him in custody at Mosulp'o Camp. However, after Commander Kim Il-ryŏl was relieved from command, the new commander, Pak Chin-gyŏng, soon released the man and allowed him to join the police.[^113] This incurred the discontent of Constabulary personnel, and when those who later assassinated Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng appeared at trial, this was cited as one of Pak's misdeeds.[^114]

The Ōra-ri arson incident was filmed from multiple angles by a U.S. film crew. The burning village of Ōra-ri was photographed from the air by a U.S. aircraft while simultaneously, on the ground, the police mobile unit entering Ōra-ri was filmed. The fact that the rapidly unfolding events were captured on film indicates advance preparation. This silent film reel is preserved at the U.S. National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) and is known as the only surviving visual record of the early stages of the April 3rd Incident. The film, titled "May Day on Cheju-Do," is also discussed in John Merrill's article "The Cheju-do Rebellion."[^115] Through this film, the U.S. military edited the Ōra-ri arson incident to appear as though it had been perpetrated by the armed guerrillas. Accordingly, this film appears to have been produced for the purpose of obtaining justification for forceful suppression—suggesting that the U.S. military's hard-line policy had already been determined at that point.

###### ② U.S. Military Orders to Attack

On May 3, 1948, Military Government Director Dean and other U.S. military leadership ordered the Constabulary Headquarters to resolve the Cheju situation decisively within the shortest possible time by launching a full-scale attack on the armed guerrillas.

[^113]: Pak ○○ (then Taedong Youth Corps member), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 168–169).
[^114]: *Hansŏng Ilbo*, August 14, 1948.
[^115]: John Merrill, "The Cheju-do Rebellion," *Journal of Korean Studies*, vol. 2, 1980, p. 169.

**[p. 201]**

The "surrender operations" designed to give the armed guerrillas an opportunity to lay down their arms and peacefully resolve the situation were abandoned, and a policy of forceful military suppression was adopted in their place.

Meanwhile, May 3rd was also the day when people coming down from the mountains in the character of "surrender" in accordance with the Kim Il-ryŏl–Kim Tal-sam peace negotiations were fired upon by unidentified persons, providing the occasion for the peace negotiations to collapse. Commander Kim Il-ryŏl testified that when he captured those who had fired, they proved to be police officers, and upon interrogation they confessed to being "a special operations unit tasked with killing bandits, U.S. soldiers, and Constabulary personnel under orders from superiors, in order to obstruct the progress of the guerrillas' surrender operations." Regarding such police conduct, Commander Kim Il-ryŏl stated: "The National Police were not intent on suppressing the uprising; rather, in order to conceal their own mistakes and crimes, they were actually instigating and expanding the uprising. The police, disguised as bandits, set fires to civilian homes and then propagandized them as bandit activities; when this happened, the bandits too descended from the mountains and attacked each police station, and fighting that had been stopped broke out again," adding that when fighting resumed, Colonel Mansfield also became very angry.[^116]

However, regardless of the police shooting incident that occurred that day, the breakdown of the peace negotiations was already predetermined. While Colonel Mansfield, commander of the U.S. 59th Military Government Company stationed on Jeju, and Commander Kim Il-ryŏl had been making efforts to implement the peace negotiations on Jeju Island, the central U.S. military leadership had already adopted a military suppression policy and directed the Constabulary Headquarters to implement it. Regarding this, Colonel Chŏng Il-gwŏn (丁一權), Chief of Staff of the Constabulary Headquarters, testified: "After May 3rd, in accordance with orders from Major General Brown, Military Government Director Dean, and other field command authority, we proceeded with decisive attack operations as the short-term solution."[^117]

Military Government Director Dean had, on April 18th, ordered Colonel Mansfield: "Before undertaking large-scale offensive operations, make every effort to contact the leaders of the dissident groups and give them an opportunity to surrender." He had thus been the very one who provided the initial impetus for the peace negotiations that opened between Kim Il-ryŏl and Kim Tal-sam. Dean's change of stance appears to have resulted from the decision of General Hodge, commander of the U.S. 24th Army Corps and Commander of USAFIK. As examined earlier, when General Hodge observed that the "surrender operations" initiated by Dean's instruction to Colonel Mansfield, commander of the U.S. 59th Military Government Company on Jeju, were becoming concrete as "peace negotiations" between 9th Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl and armed guerrilla chief Kim Tal-sam, he sent Major Schewe to Jeju on April 27th, one day before the scheduled negotiations, to confirm and oversee the matter.

[^116]: Kim Il-ryŏl, op. cit. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 334–335).
[^117]: *Hansŏng Ilbo*, August 14, 1948; *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, August 14, 1948.

**[p. 202]**

Major Schewe conducted large-scale search operations over the two days of April 27–28 using Constabulary forces, then returned to Seoul around noon on April 28th. Although the operations on April 27th and 28th were limited to search operations, they were the first operations led by the Constabulary rather than the police. This accords with Constabulary Headquarters Commander Yu Tong-yŏl's statement regarding the Jeju incident: "On April 27th the guns of bandit suppression first opened fire."[^118] Subsequently, the peace negotiations were held on April 28th, and the following day, April 29th, Military Government Director Dean visited Jeju Island.[^119] Why Dean came to Jeju at that time, and what reaction he showed as the original proposer of the peace negotiations (surrender operations) to their results, is not known.

However, from the testimony of Lieutenant Yi Yun-nak, then Intelligence Officer of the 9th Regiment—regarding how central U.S. CIC and G-2 personnel dismissed Commander Kim Il-ryŏl's report on the Ōra-ri arson incident and forced harsh suppression—it appears that the U.S. military leadership had already established a military suppression policy.

General Hodge, responsible for USAFIK, sent Major Schewe to Jeju on April 27th to examine two approaches to controlling the situation. He simultaneously monitored and oversaw the surrender operations while conducting large-scale search operations to probe the armed guerrillas' military strength in preparation for military suppression. With focus on early resolution of the situation ahead of the May 10 Elections, General Hodge ultimately appears to have adopted the second approach—military suppression. This policy direction is suggested in Major Schewe's April 29th report, submitted after concluding operations on Jeju Island and returning to Seoul. In his report, Major Schewe evaluated the Jeju Island situation as follows: "If the U.S. 59th Military Government Company commander can firmly control the troops currently on Jeju Island, the present troop strength alone would be sufficient to stabilize the situation. Since communists and guerrilla forces are positioned on the orŭms, swift and vigorous operations are required to suppress them."[^120] This report—concluding that suppression was possible with existing troop strength alone—appears to have been the decisive factor solidifying General Hodge's determination.

[^118]: *Sŏul Sinmun*, June 3, 1948.
[^119]: According to *Sŏul Sinmun*'s May 7, 1948 report, when Military Government Director Dean told reporters on May 6th that he had visited Jeju Island "last Thursday and again this past Wednesday," "this past Wednesday" refers to May 5th, and "last Thursday" refers to April 29th.
[^120]: "Disposition Form, Report of Activities at Cheju Do Island," April 29, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Command (1942–), US Army Forces in Korea, 1945–49, Entry No. 11071, Box No. 68, NARA, Washington, D.C.

**[p. 203]**

###### ③ Rapid Replacement of the Regiment Commander

On May 5, 1948, Military Government Director Dean led Civil Minister An Chae-hong (安在鴻), Police Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok (趙炳玉), Constabulary Commander Brigadier General Song Ho-sŏng (宋虎聲), and others to visit Jeju Island and held a secret conference. Nine people attended the conference: Colonel Mansfield (59th Military Government Company commander), Jeju Governor Yu Hae-jin (柳海辰), 9th Regiment Commander Lieutenant Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl (金益烈), Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner Ch'oe Ch'ŏn (崔天), and Dean's personal interpreter. All participants—including the head of the Military Government—were top military and police officials. The primary source revealing the content discussed at this conference is the memoirs of Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, one of the participants.

According to these memoirs, Colonel Mansfield presided over the conference and declared beforehand: "Any participant may speak freely, but the contents of this conference are strictly confidential. Anyone who leaks information will be referred for military court-martial." The first to present the situation, Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner Ch'oe Ch'ŏn, characterized the nature of the incident as an uprising planned in advance by international communists and emphasized that only large-scale joint military-police operations could suppress it.

The second to speak, Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, pointed out that the uprising had complex causes and argued that the increase in those going to the mountains was also due to police mistakes. Commander Kim Il-ryŏl further presented his solution: "We must separate those bandits with hostile intent from general civilians and sympathizers, and isolate the bandits from the people. To accomplish this, we must deploy operations combining military pressure with propaganda and surrender operations." He further proposed: "To achieve operational unity, place the Jeju Island National Police under my command." Commander Kim Il-ryŏl then presented physical evidence and photographs demonstrating suspicious police conduct.

When these unexpected photographs and physical evidence appeared, Director Dean turned to Police Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok and said: "Doctor Cho, what is the meaning of this? This does not accord with your report." However, Cho Pyŏng-ok insisted that Kim Il-ryŏl's explanation was wrong and that all the physical evidence and photographs were fabrications. Then, pointing at Kim Il-ryŏl, he cried out: "There sits a communist youth!" He alleged that Kim Il-ryŏl's father was an international communist who had received training in the Soviet Union and was currently serving as a communist cadre in the North, and that Kim Il-ryŏl was acting under his father's direction. When Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, enraged by Cho Pyŏng-ok's remarks, rushed forward and a physical altercation broke out, the conference descended into chaos.[^121]

[^121]: Kim Il-ryŏl, op. cit. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 338–342).

**[p. 204]**

When the fighting continued, Director Dean terminated the conference and proceeded directly to the airport and departed for Seoul. Thus Dean's party departed for Jeju at 7:00 a.m., concluded the conference immediately upon arrival, and returned to Seoul at 5:00 p.m. that same afternoon.[^122] The next day, May 6th, however, Director Dean told reporters the following:

> Our party arrived on Jeju Island on the 5th. In the morning, each member independently investigated different areas from his own perspective. Around 1 p.m. we convened and exchanged what each had investigated. Upon examining these reports, we discovered that this uprising was brought about by a small number of communist elements who infiltrated from outside the province, instigating innocent civilians who then went to the mountains and are killing supporters of the current government. In fact, a small number of disruptive elements went into the mountains to carry out such activities, but generally the atmosphere on Jeju Island was peaceful. We believe this incident will soon be resolved through the efforts of the National Police and the Constabulary.[^123]

Three points merit attention in Director Dean's press conference. First, his party conducted independent investigations from different perspectives, indicating that the party members' views regarding the Jeju situation differed. Second, he identified "communist elements infiltrating from outside the province" as the force behind the incident—the "external communist infiltration theory" appeared here for the first time. Third, he expressed optimism about the situation on Jeju Island.

Meanwhile, the day after Director Dean completed his visit and returned to Seoul, on May 6th, the 9th Regiment Commander was abruptly replaced—Lieutenant Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng (朴珍景) taking the position previously held by Lieutenant Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl.[^124] In his memoirs, Commander Kim Il-ryŏl described his shock: "Around 11 a.m., Lieutenant Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng, a senior official from the Constabulary Headquarters, suddenly arrived. I thought it was a visit from top command. But it turned out he had come to inform me that I had been relieved as regiment commander on orders received that morning."[^125]

[^122]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, May 6, 1948.
[^123]: *Taedong Sinmun*, May 7, 1948.
[^124]: Constabulary Headquarters Special Order No. 52, May 6, 1948.
[^125]: Kim Il-ryŏl, op. cit. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 343).

**[p. 205]**

Examining the content of Director Dean's May 6th press conference and the abrupt replacement of the regiment commander that same day, it appears that Director Dean had already solidified his military suppression policy before the May 5th Jeju conference. The May 5th conference appears to have been a procedure to persuade those in his party who held differing views regarding military suppression and to obtain their agreement. The targets of persuasion appear to have been Constabulary Commander Song Ho-sŏng and Civil Minister An Chae-hong. This is because, since its establishment, the Constabulary had been in conflict and confrontation with the National Police over its status. Moreover, it was the general sentiment within the Constabulary at that time that the Constabulary, which would become the foundation of the future national military, could not conduct its first operation against fellow Koreans rather than external enemies. On June 2nd, the Constabulary Headquarters Commander issued a statement: "The Army and Navy Constabulary, in order not to leave a tragic history of fratricidal conflict during this sacred nation-founding period, has exercised restraint."[^126]

A notable passage in Commander Kim Il-ryŏl's memoirs pertains to this.
