When, on May 5th, Police Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok accused Commander Kim Il-ryŏl of being a communist, provoking a physical altercation, neither Constabulary Commander Song Ho-sŏng nor Civil Minister An Chae-hong actively intervened to stop the fight. Commander Kim Il-ryŏl testified that he heard Constabulary Commander Song Ho-sŏng shouting "You bastard! You bastard!" but the target was unclear; he sensed it might have been directed at Cho Pyŏng-ok. After his removal from command, when Kim Il-ryŏl went to Seoul and reported the Jeju situation to Constabulary Commander Song Ho-sŏng, the commander expressed worry, saying: "The people of Jeju are now all dead." Seoul-based Constabulary officers uniformly praised and supported his conduct. Furthermore, when the fight did not cease, An Chae-hong suddenly pounded the table and wept loudly: "Alas, how shameful, how infuriating! Colonel, I beg you to endure this! It is precisely because our liberation was not achieved through our own people's strength but came by borrowing others' strength that we suffer such unjust hardship. Colonel, please bear it!"[^127]

In any case, what must be emphasized is that the May 5th "Jeju Conference" and the May 6th replacement of the regiment commander were measures taken by the U.S. Military Government leadership to achieve early suppression of the Jeju situation ahead of the May 10 Elections.

[^126]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, June 5, 1948.
[^127]: Kim Il-ryŏl, op. cit. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 342–347).

**[p. 206]**

### d. Jeju Island's Failure in the May 10 Elections

#### 1) Attacks on Electoral Commission Members and Election Offices

As the scheduled election date of May 10, 1948 approached, the situation throughout the nation descended into chaos. Throughout the country, repeated attacks on police stations and election offices occurred by those opposing the May 10 Elections. Military Government Director Dean, accepting Police Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok's recommendation that the 35,000-strong National Police could not protect some 13,800 election offices throughout South Korea, authorized the organization of Village Defense Corps on April 16th to support the National Police.

However, not only leftists but also the right-wing Kim Ku (김구) faction and the centrist Kim Kyusik (김규식) faction—both of which enjoyed popular support—argued that the May 10 Elections constituted a scheme for separate elections and a separate government that would permanently divide the Korean peninsula, and strongly opposed them. In particular, Kim Ku and Kim Kyusik departed northward on April 19th to participate in the "Conference of All Korean Political Parties and Social Organizations Convened in Pyongyang" to oppose the unilateral elections, generating a major wave of anti-election sentiment that greatly alarmed the U.S. Military Government. On Jeju Island in particular, "opposition to separate elections and separate government" was one of the major slogans of the armed guerrillas who launched the armed uprising on April 3rd, making the U.S. Military Government especially concerned. The Military Government's focus was on how to suppress the Jeju situation early and ensure the May 10 Elections, the pressing task at hand, proceeded smoothly. Accordingly, the U.S. Military Government implemented countermeasures including: (1) replacement of the 9th Regiment commander; (2) strengthening of Constabulary troop strength; (3) dispatch of reinforcement police; (4) deployment and placement of Village Defense Corps; and (5) on-site inspections by Military Government leadership.

However, the final registration results showed that Jeju Island's registration rate was 64.9%, falling far short of the national average of 91.7% and ranking last in the nation. As election day approached, incidents of election offices being attacked and electoral commission members being killed occurred repeatedly. By mid-April, attacks on election offices had begun. In the early morning of April 18th, the polling place in Top'yŏng-ri, Cheju Township, was attacked and election records were seized. On April 19th, the polling place in Sinch'on-ri, Choch'ŏn Township, was attacked and burned, resulting in the loss of all records.[^128]

[^128]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 815, April 22, 1948.

**[p. 207]**

Following attacks on polling places, electoral commission members were killed. A U.S. military report recorded incidents concentrated from the night of April 21st as follows:

> ○ Inho-ri—On the night of April 21–22, 1948, an unknown number of persons raided the voter registration office and seized all election records. (C-3)
>
> ○ Naedo-ri—On the night of April 21–22, 1948, all registration records at the district office were seized. (C-2)
>
> ○ Tongil-ri—On the night of April 21, 1948, at 10:30 p.m., unidentified persons raided the registration office, killed an electoral commission member, and seized all election records. (C-2)
>
> ○ Mosulp'o—At 11 p.m. on April 21, 1948, an unknown number of persons in police uniforms attacked the police station and township office. The attackers were repelled by Constabulary personnel from a nearby camp. One rightist was killed. (C-2)
>
> ○ Taejŏng—On April 22, 1948, a group in police uniforms, apparently the same group that carried out the incident at Mosulp'o, attacked and killed an electoral commission member and seized registration records.[^129]

As attacks on election offices continued, electoral commission members refused to transport ballots and ballot boxes to polling places, demanding that U.S. forces use transportation means to perform this work instead.[^130] In the early morning of April 30th, an electoral commission member was stabbed to death in Sinp'yŏng-ri, Taejŏng Township.[^131] Attacks on election-related workers continued into May. On May 1st, a village electoral commission chairman was stabbed to death in Top'yŏng-ri, Cheju Township.[^132] Then on May 5th, the electoral commission chairman in Hwabuk-ri was killed.[^133] As attacks on election-related workers did not cease, electoral commission members residing in mountain villages evacuated to coastal villages.[^134] Electoral commission members also resigned. On May 3rd, all electoral commission members in Choch'ŏn-ri, Choch'ŏn Township, resigned.[^135] Due to resignations of electoral commission members, the Constabulary assumed election management duties such as ballot distribution in many areas.[^136]

While concerned about nationwide anti-election movements, the U.S. military prohibited American soldiers from entering polling places to avoid appearing to intervene in elections.[^137] However, because the situation on Jeju appeared serious, U.S. soldiers appeared directly at election sites to encourage voting. A U.S. military report recorded the atmosphere on Jeju Island when even military government officials participated in election work:

[^129]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 916, April 22, 1948.
[^130]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 918, April 24, 1948.
[^131]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 823, May 1, 1948.
[^132]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 925, May 1, 1948.
[^133]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 827, May 6, 1948.
[^134]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 826, May 5, 1948.
[^135]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 927, May 3, 1948.
[^136]: Hq. 7th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 109, May 10, 1948.
[^137]: "Special Edition, G-2, GHQ, FEC Daily Intelligence Summary, Report on Recent Korean Tour," May 30, 1948, RG 6: Records of General Headquarters, Far East Command (FECOM), reel 647, MacArthur Memorial Library, Virginia, U.S.A.

**[p. 208]**

> Election-related government employees on Jeju Island, fearing retaliation by communist bandits, were reluctant to handle all election-related work including inspection of voter registration lists, creating difficulties in conducting elections. In Choch'ŏn Township, approximately 50% of the electoral commission members resigned. A total of 65 polling places failed to function on election day. Military government officials assisted in distributing and collecting election materials in approximately 50% of Puk Cheju County. (U.S. Military Government report)[^138]

U.S. soldiers, conducting inspections of the Cheju region and checking ballot box transportation, became angry upon discovering poor performance in Choch'ŏn Township and took the township chief into custody. Township Chief Kim Min-gyu (金玟奎) at the time testified: "Several days before the election, U.S. soldiers with rifles came to the Choch'ŏn Township office with an interpreter to check ballot box deployment. They suddenly became angry. When I explained the difficult local situation, the U.S. soldiers insisted 'You're not cooperating' and continued expressing anger, then forcibly put me in a jeep and dragged me away. Only after I protested through the interpreter was I released."[^139]

In Andŏk Township, U.S. soldiers directly went to mountain villages to conduct elections. Kim Pong-sŏk (金奉錫), who accompanied U.S. soldiers carrying ballot boxes to mountain villages, testified: "The ballot boxes sent to Kwangp'yŏng-ri had been seized twice, creating problems. Then one U.S. soldier came with a three-quarter-ton truck and volunteered to transport the ballot boxes. Despite warnings that it was dangerous, he actively stepped forward, so reluctantly I went with him to Kwangp'yŏng-ri and set up the ballot boxes at the ri office. In the end, elections were conducted in Kwangp'yŏng-ri with the U.S. soldier and me keeping watch."[^140]

Amidst such chaos, proxy voting also occurred. In Sangch'ŏn-ri, Andŏk Township, Nam Cheju County, the village head and three others gathered at the village head's home and cast ballots on behalf of nearly 100 village voters, all for a single specific candidate.[^141] During this period, the National Police's counteroffensive was intense. According to U.S. military records, during the four days from May 7th to 10th, a total of 29 deaths occurred on Jeju Island. By affiliation: police = 1 dead, 9 wounded, 4 missing; rightists = 7 dead, 3 wounded; bandits = 21 dead.[^142]

[^138]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 831, May 11, 1948.
[^139]: Kim Min-gyu (김민규, then Choch'ŏn Township Chief), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 211).
[^140]: Kim Pong-sŏk (김봉석, then Vice Township Chief of Andŏk Township), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 211–212).
[^141]: Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 236.
[^142]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 830, May 10, 1948.

**[p. 209]**

...classified as "bandits."[^142] However, the U.S. military report is based on information compiled from reports submitted by the military government headquarters of Cheju dispatched there at the time, the U.S. 20th Infantry Regiment, U.S. counterintelligence personnel, and also includes information reported by local police and the Constabulary. Consequently, the U.S. military report is written from a perspective that accords with American interests, and the information contained therein creates the impression that rather than recording actual conditions in their entirety, unfavorable content was either omitted or concealed. In the casualty statistics from May 7 to 10 introduced above, 21 out of a total of 29 deaths were classified as "bandits," yet among the U.S. military reports from this period, the only record concerning "suppression of bandits" stated: "On May 10, 1948, the Constabulary killed one male who attempted to escape while being interrogated in Chochŏn-ri."[^143]

Meanwhile, the armed guerrillas employed a boycott of the elections as a method to send residents into the mountains. The residents' exodus to the mountains began around May 5. The inhabitants would go to volcanic cones or forests near their villages and remain there until after the elections were completed, returning to their villages only then. Thus, on the day of the elections, the villages contained only a small number of people—police families, Taedong Youth Corps cadres, election management commission members, and the like. A daily report from the U.S. 6th Division recorded the tense situation during the day of May 10, 1948, as follows:

△Chungmun-myŏn—May 10, 1948: Polling places were raided and all ballots were destroyed.

△Sŏngsan-myŏn—May 10, 1948: Polling places were set on fire by groups of 60 persons.

△Cheju town—May 10, 1948: Two sticks of dynamite were detonated near the Cheju town office. No casualties reported.

△Cheju Airport—May 10, 1948, 11:40 a.m.: Two groups, one of approximately 250 persons and another of approximately 50 persons, exchanged gunfire at a point 200 yards south of the airport. The larger group was carrying a large white flag. One Constabulary squad was dispatched to the location, but the larger group disappeared toward Nohyŏng-ri. No casualties reported.

△Pyŏksŏn-myŏn—May 10, 1948: One polling place was attacked. Two persons were killed and all ballots were destroyed.

△Kuja-myŏn, Songdang-ri—May 10, 1948, 11:00 a.m.: Two persons were killed, one was wounded, and seven houses burned. Details are under investigation.

△Chochŏn-myŏn—May 10, 1948: All fourteen polling places failed to function properly.

[^142]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 830, May 10, 1948.

[^143]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 935, May 11, 1948.

**[p. 210]**

△Chochŏn-myŏn, Puksŏn-ri—May 10, 1948, 4:00 p.m.: A polling place burned. All ballots were destroyed.

△Pyŏksŏn-myŏn, Kassi-ri—May 10, 1948: When the polling place was attacked, the village headman and school principal were killed. The principal's wife was wounded.

△Sŏngsan-myŏn—May 10, 1948: Four persons were killed when the polling place was attacked. Details are under investigation.

△Chochŏn-myŏn, Chochŏn-ri—May 10, 1948: The Constabulary killed a person who attempted to escape during interrogation. (B-2)

△Aewŏl-myŏn, Shinarim-ri—May 10, 1948, 6:00 p.m.: A military government official met five Constabulary personnel in the outskirts of Shinarim-ri. These Constabulary personnel claimed they had been driven out of Shinarim-ri by 16 guerrillas. However, subsequent investigation conducted during the night revealed that no such incident had occurred, there were no guerrillas in that village, and the five Constabulary personnel had abandoned their posts. The U.S. military advisor reported that he would dismiss these five Constabulary personnel from their positions.[^144]

### 2) Invalidation of Two Electoral Districts

The U.S. Military Government mobilized U.S. forces, the Constabulary, National Police, and even the Village Defense Corps to encourage the elections, but except for the main commercial district of Cheju town, where security was stringent, the elections could not be conducted properly. The Central Election Management Commission announced that having conducted elections in 200 electoral districts throughout the nation, out of a total of 7,840,871 eligible voters, 7,487,649 cast ballots, representing a voting rate of 95.5 percent. In Jeju Island, out of a total of 85,517 eligible voters in three electoral districts, 53,698 voted, representing the lowest voting rate of 62.8 percent. Among these, South Cheju County electoral district showed a voting rate of 86.6% (32,062 out of 37,040 eligible voters), with the independent Oh Yong-guk (吳龍國) confirmed as elected. However, North Cheju County District A showed 43% (11,912 out of 27,560 eligible voters), and North Cheju County District B showed 46.5% (9,724 out of 20,917 eligible voters), both falling short of a majority.[^145]

Hong Sun-jae (洪順才), an election management commission member who went to Seoul to report the election results of Cheju Island to the National Assembly Election Committee, disclosed the election situation in Cheju Island as follows:

△North Cheju County, District A: Out of 73 polling places, ballot boxes from only 33 polling places were brought in, and of these, 2 were empty. Consequently, elections in 43 polling places could not be conducted. Even though Yangm Kuijin (梁貴珍) showed the highest vote count of approximately 3,000, since more than half of the registered 37,040 voters did not vote, along with the case of Yangm Byŏng-jik of North Cheju County District B, the matter awaited the decision of the National Assembly Election Committee.

△North Cheju County, District B: Out of 61 polling places, elections were conducted in only 31.

△South Cheju County: Out of 87 polling places, elections were conducted in 86 polling places, and the incumbent legislator Oh Yong-guk (吳龍國, 44, independent) was elected with approximately 16,000 votes.[^146]

Ultimately, the voting situation in Cheju Island was tallied as elections being conducted in only 31 out of 73 polling places in North Cheju County, District A, and 32 out of 61 polling places in North Cheju County, District B. Consequently, the National Assembly Election Committee, on May 18, pursuant to Article 44 of the Election Law, recognized the invalidity of the election of Yangm Kuijin (梁貴珍), the highest vote-getter in District A, and Yangm Byŏng-jik (梁秉直), the highest vote-getter in District B, and on the 19th recommended to the Military Government Commander the invalidation of the elections in two Cheju Island electoral districts.[^147]

Accordingly, Military Government Commander Dean issued a proclamation (effective May 24) on May 26 stating: "The elections in two Cheju Island electoral districts are hereby invalidated, and pursuant to Article 44 of the Election Law, new elections shall be conducted under the supervision and direction of the Chairman of the National Assembly Election Committee on June 23."[^148]

However, the Cheju Incident did not show signs of abatement and instead entered a path of further deterioration. In early June, the Cheju Island Election Management Commission, citing the following reasons, requested a postponement of the scheduled June 23 National Assembly runoff elections, stating it was impossible to conduct them. The statement read:

> The National Assembly runoff elections for North Cheju County Districts A and B scheduled for June 23 cannot be conducted, as normal administration on the island is paralyzed. The Cheju Election Management Commission thereby requests postponement of the runoff election date from the Central Election Management Commission. The reasons are as follows:

> (1) There were 1,206 election committee members in 2 counties, 1 town, and 12 townships in Cheju Island, of which 15 were killed and 5 were seriously wounded. Many South Cheju election committee members are hiding, and most fear future retribution and are reluctant to come forward.

> (2) The voter registers kept at 133 polling places in North Cheju County have been largely lost through theft or arson, making it impossible to prepare new ones at this time.

> (3) The four candidates from North Cheju County District A and four from District B who ran in the May 10 elections have no one to take their place, making it impossible to conduct an uncontested election.[^149]

[^144]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 934, May 10, 1948; No. 935, May 11, 1948.

[^145]: Chosŏn Ilbo, May 20, 1948.

[^146]: Chosŏn Ilbo, May 19, 1948.

[^147]: Wuri Sinmun, May 21, 1948.

[^148]: Chosŏn Ilbo, May 27, 1948.

**[p. 211]**

Even Military Government Commander Dean, who had demonstrated willingness to enforce the elections, was forced to issue the following administrative order on June 10 to indefinitely postpone the elections:

### Indefinite Postponement of Runoff Elections in Cheju Island

#### Article 1

Pursuant to the authority vested in the undersigned by Article 44 of the National Assembly Member Election Law, which became effective on March 17, 1948, and upon the recommendation of the Central Election Management Commission, the undersigned declared on May 24, 1948, that the elections held on May 10, 1948, in North Cheju Island Districts A and B were invalid, inasmuch as the voting in fewer than half of the polling places in both electoral districts was conducted due to the actions and violence of destructive elements. Simultaneously, the undersigned ordered that new elections be conducted in said districts on June 23, 1948.

Since destructive elements in Cheju Island continue to make efforts to disrupt public order and peace, in order to guarantee peaceful and orderly elections truly representative of the will of the people of North Cheju Island Districts A and B, the undersigned hereby orders that the runoff elections in said districts be indefinitely postponed.

#### Article 2

This order shall become effective upon proclamation.

Thus Cheju Island became the only region in South Korea that rejected the May 10 Elections, a fact recorded in history. The National Assembly runoff elections were conducted exactly one year later on May 10, 1949.[^149]

[^149]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, June 10, 1948.

**[p. 212]**

## 2. Early Armed Conflict Period (May 11, 1948 - October 10, 1948)

### a. Suppression Operations Directed by the U.S. Military Commander

#### 1) Dispatch of the U.S. 20th Regiment Commander

When the May 10 Elections failed to materialize in Cheju Island, the U.S. Military Government, around May 20,[^1] dispatched Colonel Rothwell H. Brown, commander of the 20th Regiment stationed in Kwangju under the 6th Division, as the U.S. military commander of Cheju Island to direct and control all suppression operations on the ground. Colonel Brown had previously visited Cheju shortly before the "April 28 Peace Negotiations" and inspected suppression operations with Colonel Mansfield of the 59th Military Government Company stationed in Cheju Island under his command. This time, with the June 23 runoff elections looming ahead, he was dispatched under a special order from Military Government Commander Dean to suppress the situation early, coming not as a member of the 6th Division or the 20th Regiment, but in a special capacity.[^2]

During the April 3 uprising, Lim Sŏn-ha, who served as commander of the 3rd Regiment of the Constabulary in Chŏlla Province—a region under Brown's jurisdiction—testified regarding Brown's position, disposition, and the significance of his dispatch to Cheju as follows:

> Colonel Brown was at that time the commander of the 20th Regiment of the 6th Division. The headquarters of the 20th Regiment was in Kwangju, and responsibility for security in South Chŏlla, North Chŏlla, and Cheju fell under his authority. Consequently, I, who held the position of commander of the 3rd Regiment stationed in North Chŏlla, also received operational directives from Colonel Brown. He gave the impression of a typical career military officer. He had considerable combat experience. At that time, there were two representative U.S. military commanders in Kwangju—one was Colonel Brown, and the other was Colonel Price. Under Colonel Price's command there were no tactical units; he was the so-called civil administrator (military government commander) at the provincial government office. However, Colonel Brown actually had the 20th Regiment's combat units stationed at the Kwangju arsenal. Therefore, within the 20th Regiment's occupation area, police and Constabulary personnel had to receive directives from Brown. That Colonel Brown, occupying such a position, took up residence in Cheju shows how seriously the U.S. Military Government regarded the Cheju Incident. Since I was in Chŏnju at that time, I have no way of knowing about Colonel Brown's activities in Cheju.[^3]

Colonel Brown's role and activities in Cheju are described concretely in an article written by Jo Tŏk-song (조선통신 special correspondent) in June 1948. The relevant section from Jo's article, which appeared in the July 1948 issue of New World (新天地), is as follows:

> Currently in Cheju, besides police dispatched from the mainland, even private organizations are being incorporated as temporary police. Cooperating with the police, the Village Defense Corps still exists on Cheju Island, and under police direction they are maintaining vigilance day and night in the areas near village police substations. In this way, the Constabulary takes the initiative in combat in the dangerous mountainous areas, while the coastal police maintain watch over the island's coastline and transport of increasing dispatch from the mainland. The supreme commander overseeing and directing all of this is the American Colonel Brown. "Since I came, the land and coastal police and the National Police have been cooperating and working well together," stated the supreme commander, who added that "the incident will be pacified in about two weeks according to my plans"—though now more than a month has passed since Colonel Brown made his two-week statement—and "I have no interest in the cause of the incident. My responsibility is suppression only. After I have put things in order, if it flares up again, that is the responsibility of Korean administrators, not mine."[^4]

In the same article, Jo Tŏk-song repeatedly emphasized: "The supreme commander who came here under Special Order from Military Government Commander Dean to restore public security, Colonel Brown, stated: 'I have no interest in the cause. My mission is suppression only.' This is a view entirely natural for Colonel Brown, a foreigner, but I cannot help feeling a certain regret."[^5]

At that time, legal experts, media, and Cheju residents and others from various sectors repeatedly presented the solution: "The outbreak of the April 3rd Incident has multiple complex causes rooted in people's outrage at oppression. It is important to identify and address these root causes." However, the U.S. Military Government turned a blind eye to such remedial measures and instead pursued unwavering strong suppression operations, a fact implicitly revealed in Colonel Brown's brief statement: "I have no interest in the cause. My mission is suppression only."

[^1]: The exact date of Colonel Brown's arrival in Cheju is uncertain. However, based on a letter dated May 19, 1948, written by his superior, the 6th Division commander, when sending Brown off (The Rothwell H. Brown Papers, Box 3, US Army Military History Institute, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.), the statement "I arrived approximately ten days ago" reported in the Hyŏndae Ilbo on June 3, 1948, and the fact that Brown's first activity report begins from May 22 (Hq. USAMGIK, Major General William Dean to Major Edgar Noel, July 17, 1948), it is estimated that Brown arrived in Cheju around May 20.

[^2]: "Letter from Brown to Ward," July 2, 1948, The Rothwell H. Brown Papers, Box 3, US Army Military History Institute, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.

**[p. 213]**

Colonel Brown developed powerful operations from the outset, just as he himself said: "If things proceed according to my plan, the situation will be pacified in about two weeks." In his article, Jo Tŏk-song described the land, sea, and air operations directed by Colonel Brown: "In the sky, U.S. military reconnaissance aircraft fly; at sea, the patrols of U.S. naval cruisers never cease; and on land, U.S. military officers directing the frontlines race east and west by horseback and in jeeps—such is Cheju Island today."[^6]

The reasons Colonel Brown pursued an uncompromisingly stern policy of operations include the assassination of Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng that occurred after he assumed office, but more importantly, they stemmed from Brown's own disposition and his understanding of the Cheju Incident. When Major General Orlando Ward, commander of the 6th Division and Brown's superior, learned that his subordinate Colonel Brown was departing to become commander of suppression operations on Cheju Island, he sent the following letter on May 19, offering counsel:

> I wish to draw one matter to your attention, though I am not sure if it is appropriate. I cannot help but recall from the past that there have been enormous-scale disturbances and revolutions in South America and other regions that had absolutely nothing to do with communist instigation. This does not mean there is no communist instigation on Cheju Island. Furthermore, I seriously sense that the principal problem arose from hatred toward the police. Such disturbances will continue until measures are taken and the irritants are removed by replacing people in whom the citizens have no confidence.[^7]

Though expressed in circumlocutory language, General Ward was advising that the Cheju Incident should not be viewed simply as a problem of communist instigation. He was further pointing out that police misconduct was one cause of the incident, and counseling Brown to prevent police abuses and restore public confidence to resolve the situation. Upon assuming his position, Colonel Brown did implement measures to prevent police abuses upon receiving this advice. However, in his reply of July 2, Colonel Brown contested General Ward's counsel:

> The fact that Cheju Island was organized as a communist stronghold is too evident. The evidence was undeniable when we actually looked into the problem. The brutality of police and inefficient government were also causes, but compared to the plans the communists had for this island, they were peripheral causes.[^8]

He thus expressed his determination to continue with strong-line operations. Moreover, in his report, Brown stated: "Regiment commanders negotiating with communist agitators while employing delaying tactics in situations requiring decisive operations resulted in a failure to act effectively. Had the military government company's civilian affairs officer acted decisively and vigorously, the Constabulary would have been able to respond immediately and effectively."[^9] This was regret regarding the "April 28 Peace Negotiations" between Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl and armed guerrilla chief Kim Tal-sam, in which Brown himself was somewhat involved, noting that it was merely exploited by the armed guerrillas and stronger measures should have been taken at that time.

Such disposition and understanding of the Cheju Incident on the part of Colonel Brown translated into strong suppression operations. About ten days after assuming his post as U.S. military commander of Cheju Island, Colonel Brown disclosed the operations he was conducting through press reports:

> Before I came, I heard that police and the military were not cooperating. Since my arrival, that has not occurred. [Brown went on to outline the division of roles among police, Constabulary, and coastal guard:] The National Police are conducting security operations from roads surrounding Halla Mountain to a distance of four kilometers. The National Defense Constabulary is conducting operations that will sweep everything from the western to the eastern part of Cheju Island. The coastal police maintain patrols along the coast twice daily.[^10]

However, the results of Colonel Brown's stated operations to "sweep everything from the western to the eastern part of Cheju Island" produced only adverse consequences without resolving the situation, as subsequent developments demonstrate.

[^3]: Lim Sŏn-ha (then 3rd Regiment Commander, retired as major general), testimony. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, *4·3 Speaks* ②, Chŏnyewŏn, 1994, p. 253).

[^4]: Jo Tŏk-song, "Bleeding Cheju Island," *New World*, July 1948 issue, p. 89.

[^5]: Jo Tŏk-song, preceding article, pp. 94-95.

[^6]: Jo Tŏk-song, preceding article, p. 96.

[^7]: "Letter from Ward to Brown," May 19, 1948, The Rothwell H. Brown Papers, Box 3, US Army Military History Institute, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.

[^8]: "Letter from Brown to Ward," July 2, 1948, ibid.

[^9]: "Report of Activities on Cheju-Do Island from 22 May 1948 to 30 June 1948," July 1, 1948, ibid.

[^10]: Hyŏndae Ilbo, June 3, 1948.

**[p. 214]**

#### 2) Suppression Operations of Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng (朴珍景)

The day after Military Government Administrator Major General Dean presided over a military government leadership conference in Cheju and departed, on May 6, a sudden change in command of the 9th Regiment took place. Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, who had been pursuing a peace policy, was dismissed, and Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng, Chief of Personnel of the Constabulary Headquarters, was appointed as his replacement. It is known that Colonel Pak was selected as the new regiment commander not only because he was fluent in English, having graduated from Osaka Foreign Language School during the Japanese period and communicating well with the Americans, but especially because, having been stationed in Cheju Island as a Japanese military officer in the final stages of the Japanese period, he was well acquainted with the fortifications and terrain constructed by the Japanese military.[^11]

However, the Constabulary General Headquarters, when transferring the 11th Regiment, which was established in Suwŏn on May 4, 1948, to Cheju Island on May 15, ordered the consolidation of the existing 9th Regiment into the 11th Regiment. At the same time, Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng was appointed as the inaugural 11th Regiment commander.[^12] In other words, Colonel Pak, who arrived in Cheju on June 6 in his capacity as commander of the 9th Regiment, was reassigned as 11th Regiment commander on May 15, and remained in Cheju for roughly a month and ten days until his assassination on June 18, directing suppression operations.

After May 15, the personnel under the 11th Regiment commanded by Colonel Pak consisted of one battalion from the existing 9th Regiment, one battalion drawn from the 5th Regiment in Pusan, one battalion drawn from the 6th Regiment in Taegu, and one battalion from when the 11th Regiment was established in Suwŏn, plus regiment staff personnel. The regiment also established a regimental staff to complete its organizational structure. According to the testimony of Kim Jong-myŏn (金宗勉), who came to Cheju to assist in forming the 11th Regiment Staff in late May, the following were dispatched to S-1 (Personnel): Major Ch'oe Kab-jung; S-2 (Intelligence): Colonel Kim Jong-pyŏng; S-3 (Operations): Captain Im Pul-taek; and S-4 (Supply): Major Paek Sŏn-jin. However, Kim Jong-myŏn stated: "Based on their ranks alone, we should be considered U.S. military advisors, not regimental staff officers. That is why, when Colonel Pak died, only Captain Im Pul-taek, who handled operations, remained, while the rest all left Cheju."[^13]

Shortly after assuming office on May 20, Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng, 41 Constabulary soldiers deserted en masse and joined the armed guerrillas. The deserters were primarily from Cheju. This incident resulted in intensified suppression operations. Furthermore, as a result of this incident, soldiers from Cheju were marginalized in suppression operations. One witness, a Cheju native who enlisted as a soldier in the 9th Regiment's 3rd cohort and was at that time assigned to the 11th Regiment, testified:

> Since 90 percent of the deserters were from Cheju, we were treated as bastards and communists. It was said that all Cheju people were communists. At that time, all Cheju natives formed a single battalion under the name 'Mosulp'o Battalion.' After the desertion incident, our Mosulp'o Battalion was separated and placed in tents at Odŭngni in Cheju town, and we were not given suppression operations.

A witness who was from the 9th Regiment's 1st cohort stated: "Once personnel from the 5th and 6th Regiments arrived, operations began, but they were troublemakers. People of poor quality came."[^15] Thus, with Cheju natives marginalized, conducting operations only with military personnel from other regions unfamiliar with local conditions became a background factor that further exacerbated the situation.

Evaluations of Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng's operations are starkly divided. First, there is the evaluation that "he emphasized persuasion operations to the unit commanders to turn public sentiment in favor of the population."[^16] Furthermore, Chae Myŏng-shin (蔡命新), who served as a platoon commander under Regiment Commander Pak at that time, testified as follows:

> Some say that Colonel Pak committed massacres of civilians. However, he was not committing massacres but rather attempting to rescue people from death. In the early stages of the April 3rd Incident, police mishandled the situation, and many residents evacuated to the mountains. However, Colonel Pak placed the emphasis of his operations not on suppressing the bandits but on bringing down from the mountains the residents who had evacuated. This kind of civilian protection operation is both humanitarian and strategically appropriate.[^17]

However, there is also a negative evaluation characterizing his operations as "brutal and aggressive." The claim that Regiment Commander Pak pursued reckless aggressive operations is further amplified in conjunction with testimony from the previous regiment commander that at his assumption-of-office ceremony, Colonel Pak stated, "To suppress the rebellion, it is acceptable even to sacrifice 300,000 Cheju residents."[^18] In particular, Son Sŏn-ho (孫善鎬), who directly assassinated Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng, criticized him in court as follows:

[^11]: National Defense Ministry Military History Compilation Committee, *History of the Korean War*, Vol. 1, p. 440; Paek Sŏn-yŏp, *Records of Mt. Chiri*, Koryŏwŏn, 1992, p. 125.

[^12]: Army Headquarters Military Affairs Bureau, *Army History Log*, 1st Collection, May 15, 1948.

[^13]: Kim Jong-myŏn (age 80, Bundang-gu, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi Province, then named Kim Jong-pyŏng, recorded January 22, 2002), testimony.

**[p. 215]**

> Colonel Pak's brutal aggressive operations against the 300,000 residents, in comparison to the persuasion operations of the previous regiment commander Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, left me with no choice but to be dissatisfied with his operations. As a result of that error, the following incidents were created. When we went to a village called Hwabu, seeing a boy of about 15 years old holding the corpse of his father, we unconditionally killed him. [Passage omitted] We conducted shooting practice and indiscriminately slaughtered cattle and other livestock in the village. When civilians who knew where the bandits were located brought us to those places, if there were no bandits there, we simply shot them dead. Furthermore, there was the expectation that each person should capture one bandit daily, and there was absolutely no compassion for his subordinates. I had the opportunity to assassinate Colonel Pak and escape, but since it was for the sake of 300,000 residents, there was no need for that. My single life is for 300,000 Cheju residents and 30 million Korean people, so I will gladly accept punishment.[^19]

Such assertions by Son Sŏn-ho are similar to the testimony of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng's staff officer, Captain Im Pul-taek (林富澤). In court, Captain Im testified regarding Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng's orders: "① For the entire Korean nation, it is acceptable to sacrifice 300,000 residents. ② Regarding anyone fleeing, regardless of whether they are civilian or bandit, upon the third warning to stop, if they do not comply, execute them by firing squad."[^20]

The character of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng's operations can be partially glimpsed through the reports of Colonel Brown, the top U.S. military commander for suppression operations on Cheju. Since Colonel Brown was at the apex of suppression operations on Cheju Island, Colonel Pak's operations were included within the broader operations directed by Colonel Brown. A U.S. secret report written on June 16 recorded the following regarding the suppression operations being conducted at that time:

> Colonel Roswell Brown is conducting operations as follows:
>
> a). Four battalions of the National Defense Constabulary are stationed in the east, west, south, and north of Cheju Island respectively. These battalions, under combat area command, are composed of approximately two companies each. The police are responsible for security in coastal areas, while the National Defense Constabulary is responsible for all areas except coastal regions.
>
> b). All battalions, simultaneously moving toward mountainous areas with shared objectives, will complete their search operations in the Constabulary operational areas moving inland, and military personnel are scheduled to return to their units today. (June 16)
>
> c). In this operation, approximately 3,000 persons were captured and interrogated. Currently, 575 persons, including two women, are in a prisoner-of-war facility in Cheju and are undergoing interrogation by four interrogation teams.[^21]

Around this time, media reports stated: "Those captured by the Constabulary and police amount to approximately 6,000 persons."[^22] This operation, which swept through all the mountainous villages of Cheju Island and conducted large-scale arrests of residents, was precisely the "sweep everything from the western to the eastern part of Cheju Island" operation that Colonel Brown himself disclosed to journalists. The Army History Log recorded this operation as follows: "Since arriving on Cheju Island, conducting daily suppression of riots without a moment's respite, on this day the 2nd General Assault on Halla Mountain was initiated and completely surrounded."[^23] However, in a situation where the armed guerrillas were only confronting police and avoiding the Constabulary, these operations resulted not in capturing the guerrillas but only in generating casualties among innocent residents.

The achievements reported through May 27, as announced by the Defense Security Command, indicated that 3,126 prisoners and surrendering personnel were captured and 8 were executed, while captured materiel consisted of three Type 99 rifles, 15 hand grenades, 12 bamboo spears, five dragon-pattern swords, and 17 steel helmets, with Constabulary losses being 5 wounded.[^24] The commander of the South Cheju County dispatch stated: "The number of prisoners was 670, but there were no weapons, and they were sent to regiment headquarters."[^25] A U.S. military report indicated: "The 11th Regiment, during six weeks of operations on Cheju Island, captured approximately 4,000 persons, of whom approximately 500 were detained after interrogation. Constabulary casualties during the operation were 3 killed and 2 seriously wounded."[^26]

Of course, the loss of life during Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng's tenure was not large compared to the mass execution operations conducted during that winter as part of strong suppression operations. However, this was because the armed guerrillas strategically confronted only police and avoided clashes with the Constabulary. One officer who participated in suppression operations around June 1948 stated: "The so-called People's Army fights desperately against police, but when the Constabulary deploys, they generally flee. The Constabulary's headache is the difficulty in distinguishing between civilians and bandits, as they refrain from indiscriminate fire."[^27] In this manner, the Constabulary indiscriminately arrested inhabitants of mountainous villages under the pretext that it was "difficult to distinguish between civilians and bandits." Fast-moving young people were not captured, while the prisoners were slow-moving old and weak people.

[^14]: Kang Tŏk-yun (age 75, Ora 1-dong, Cheju City, then a soldier of the 11th Regiment, recorded February 1, 2002), testimony.

[^15]: Yun Tae-jun (age 73, Chŏnnong-dong, Tongdaemun-gu, Seoul, then a soldier of the 11th Regiment, recorded May 2, 2001), testimony.

[^16]: National Defense Ministry Military History Compilation Committee, preceding work, p. 440.

[^17]: Chae Myŏng-shin (age 75, Hu'am-dong, Chongno-gu, Seoul, then platoon commander of 9th and 11th Regiments, recorded April 17, 2001).

[^18]: Kim Il-ryŏl, *Records of Truth Concerning the April 3rd Incident*, (*4·3 Speaks*, Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, preceding work, p. 345).

[^19]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, August 15, 1948.

[^20]: Hansung Ilbo, August 19, 1948.

[^21]: "Disturbances on Cheju Island," Despatch No. 199, dated July 2, 1948, from American Political Advisor Joseph Jacobs to State Department.

[^22]: Chosŏn Ilbo, June 12, 1948.

[^23]: Army Headquarters Military Affairs Bureau, *Army History Log*, 1st Collection, June 14, 1948.

[^24]: Tongah Ilbo, June 4, 1948.

[^25]: Seoul Sinmun, June 4, 1948.

[^26]: Weeka, No. 27, July 2, 1948, RG-9: Radiograms (Messages), 24th Corps, Jan-Aug 1948, reel 274.

[^27]: Chosŏn Ilbo, June 9, 1948.

**[p. 216]**

In June 1948, Jo Tŏk-song, who had come as a "war correspondent" to cover the Cheju Incident, described the appearance of "prisoners" as follows:

> Prisoners are being transported. Young persons packed in automobiles. In a line, shuffling and being dragged on foot, old and young as well as women. The rain still has not stopped. Silent, hunched over, being led away—their complexion matches the color of their clothing. How can they be called bandits from any perspective? Thirteen- to twelve-year-old boys and people over sixty, as well as women—for what reason must they be designated as bandits? I tried to speak to them. But they just turned away their heads. Probably in their eyes, one wearing a military helmet and army raincoat like myself appeared to be a Constabulary soldier. You prisoners being led away, turning your heads, from your own villages which had been your homes. Indeed, will the day come when these countless prisoners cease to exist that peaceful tones of flutes might finally be heard in this village?[^28]

Meanwhile, Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng, conducting operations under the direction of Colonel Brown, was recognized by the Americans and promoted to colonel on June 1.[^29] His promotion at that time was a special advancement that jumped over his seniors, effected through General Dean's consideration.[^30] From the perspective of Cheju residents, it was an "indiscriminate arrest operation," but General Dean evaluated it as a "successful operation" and specially promoted him to colonel less than a month after his arrival in Cheju.

However, Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng was assassinated on June 18 by subordinates dissatisfied with his operational approach. Thus, Colonel Pak's operations, which ranged through mountainous villages and within a short month generated thousands of "prisoners," only drove residents further into the mountains and, through his assassination, not only failed to resolve the situation but became a factor that further aggravated it.

#### 3) Reinforcement of Police

Chief Constable Cho Pyŏng-ok dispatched 450 reinforcement police officers drawn from various police bureaus and a detective squad commanded by Police Commander Ch'oe Lan-su (崔蘭洙) to Cheju Island. The reinforcement police consisted of 350 railroad police and 100 personnel selected from the 6th and 8th Police Bureaus. Departing Seoul in special trains in the early morning of May 18, they proceeded via Mokpo to arrive in Cheju on the morning of the 19th.[^31] On May 20, additional reinforcement police numbering in the dozens departed Seoul for Cheju.[^32] As a result of the large influx of reinforcement police around this time, by around July 1948, police personnel had increased to approximately 2,000 members (existing Cheju police 500, reinforcement police 1,500).[^33]

By dispatching reinforcement police in large numbers without knowledge of Cheju's actual conditions, harboring only the perception that "Cheju is a communist island," numerous negative consequences ensued. A media outlet reported the following regarding the extrajudicial executions of civilians by police around the May 10 Elections:

> A group of journalists visited Aewŏl-myŏn, Hagwi-ri, which had suffered the greatest casualties and where exchanges of fire between both sides had been intense. They heard from a school principal and student about the situation at that time: "The actual conditions at the time are still vivid before my eyes, and just talking about them makes me shudder. When the incident occurred, about a month later, police reinforcements arrived saying they would suppress the bandits, and they shot to death eighteen residents of the village, including 70-year-old elderly persons and elementary school students. Among those killed were people with sympathies cooperative with the police, but it was indiscriminate gunfire. Two of our young students at that time were mercilessly sacrificed."[^34]

Police atrocities served as a cause of incident expansion, a fact that Colonel Brown himself recognized to some degree. In his report to his superiors, Colonel Brown stated: "Excessive brutality and terrorism was perpetrated by the reinforcement police who arrived in Cheju Island," and noted that as a measure to suppress the rioting on May 22, he halted police terrorism and killings of civilians.[^35] He judged that police harsh treatment was worsening public opinion and was instead hindering the suppression of the incident.

In a press conference in mid-June, Colonel Brown also stated: "There were instances where police engaged in the suppression operation massacred civilians and stirred up public opinion, but such police will be strictly disciplined. Currently, five or six police officers are being detained for questioning on murder charges. Furthermore, for all police on the island, we have drawn up pledge statements to abolish torture, and are making efforts to handle the incident in the spirit of democratic policing."[^36]

However, police brutality did not cease. On June 7, police from the Chŏji Police Substation raided Kŭm'ak-ri, a mountainous village, capturing one disabled boy, one elderly person, and seven women, and executed five of them during transfer.[^37] When police from the Chŏji Substation, including 40 reinforcement police, raided Kŭm'ak-ri, a mountainous village, most residents hurriedly fled, but the disabled and elderly as well as women who had difficulty moving remained at home and were captured. The crime was being slow to flee, unlike the young people. According to testimony from villagers, among the victims of that day were 80-year-old Keumdong-saeng (金丁生), disabled person Pak Tu-ok (朴斗玉), pregnant woman Pak Kyŏng-saeng (朴庚生), and a woman with the surname Yang.[^38] This incident eventually became known, leading to investigation by the U.S. CIC, and 31 police officers including the chief of the Chŏji Substation were arrested.[^39]

[^28]: Jo Tŏk-song, preceding article, p. 91.

[^29]: National Defense Ministry Military History Compilation Committee, preceding work, p. 441.

[^30]: Paek Sŏn-yŏp, preceding work, p. 125.

**[p. 217]**

Meanwhile, Police Inspector Ch'oe Ch'ŏn of the Cheju Police Inspection Bureau received a formal reprimand on June 11. It is surmised that this reprimand was related to his being held accountable for the torture and killings by reinforcement police that caused public concern. A media outlet, reporting on Ch'oe Ch'ŏn's reprimand, speculated on the reason, stating: "It is said that he is being held responsible for insufficient supervision of subordinates in handling the incident."[^40]

The reason civilian casualties caused by police continued unabated was not only due to mistakes on the part of reinforcement police who were unfamiliar with Cheju's actual conditions, but also due to the indiscriminate utilization of members of private organizations such as the Sŏbuk Youth Corps as temporary police. Furthermore, the police authorities' measure of prioritizing the recruitment of young men from families of victims who had suffered casualties from armed guerrilla attacks also encouraged retaliatory acts.[^41] Some police officers, using leave, committed vigilante justice against families and relatives of suspected armed guerrillas, causing public concern.[^42] Regarding this, Police Advisor Lester Chorpening, who was stationed on the island, stated: "The reinforcement police stationed on the island, seeking revenge for the killing of fellow police and their families, committed cruel retaliation."[^43]

As strong suppression operations failed to assist in suppressing the situation and instead only worsened public opinion, the U.S. Military Government pursued reconciliatory measures through personnel policy. The U.S. Military Government, which had already removed Yut Hae-jin (柳海辰), Cheju governor pursuing an anti-communist hard-line policy, on May 28 and appointed Yi Kwan-ho (任琯鎬), a native of Cheju, as the new governor, next, on June 17, appointed Kim Pong-ho (金鳳昊), also a Cheju native and vice-chief of the 8th Police Bureau, as the new police chief of Cheju.[^44]

Before assuming office, Police Chief Kim Pong-ho revealed that Chief Constable Cho Pyŏng-ok had told him: "There is no one but you for handling the Cheju Island incident," and stated: "As a person from Cheju, I will appeal to local patriotism to correct defects in our police force, and if there are defects on the civilian side, I am determined to correct them. Having heard the information so far, I cannot help but shed tears of blood."[^45] This suggested that Kim Pong-ho would pursue reconciliatory measures.

When Police Chief Kim Pong-ho assumed office on June 24, though the atmosphere was poor due to the Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng assassination incident that had occurred just before his assumption of office, at a meeting held on July 1 attended by the newly appointed Regiment Commander Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok and township heads throughout the province, he declared: "First, we will eradicate and purge our police's own illegal wrongdoings. Simultaneously with resolving blocked traffic, we will not hinder or interfere with imports and exports of goods. Those who engage in wrongful conduct will not be punished, and those who surrender will be recognized as civilians."[^46] Moreover, on the same day, July 1, he implemented practical reconciliatory policies such as lifting the fishing prohibition and abolishing the domestic travel permit system.[^47] On July 5, he further abolished the domestic travel permit system for passengers on regular sea transport between Mokpo and Cheju.[^48]

However, Chief Constable Cho Pyŏng-ok, one day before Police Chief Kim Pong-ho's assumption of office in Cheju, issued a statement on June 23, contending that attributing the cause of the riot to non-democratic police misconduct was an error in thinking and judgment, and stating: "The fundamental policy for handling the Cheju riot is unchanged from before,"[^49] thereby forecasting that Police Chief Kim Pong-ho's efforts to restore public confidence to resolve the situation would inevitably fail.

[^31]: Chosŏn Ilbo, May 18, 1948; Tongnip Sinbo, May 19, 1948; Seoul Sinmun, May 21, 1948.

[^32]: Chayu Sinmun, May 21, 1948.

[^33]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, July 30, 1948.

[^34]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, July 23, 1948.

[^35]: "Report of Activities on Cheju-Do Island from 22 May 1948 to 30 June 1948," July 1, 1948, The Rothwell H. Brown Papers, Box 3, US Army Military History Institute, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.

[^36]: Chayu Sinmun, June 18, 1948.

[^37]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 966, June 11, 1948.

[^38]: Pak Pyŏng-kyŏ (resident of Kŭm'ak-ri, Hallim-eup), testimony. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, preceding work, p. 263).

[^39]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 968, June 13, 1948.

[^40]: Hansung Ilbo, June 18, 1948.

[^41]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, July 24, 1948.

[^42]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, July 17, 1948.

**[p. 218]**

### b. The Regiment Commander Assassination Incident and Reorganization of the Constabulary

#### 1) The Death of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng

On the morning of June 18, 1948, the 11th Regiment Commander Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng was assassinated by his subordinates at his quarters. After attending a celebration of his promotion on June 17 and drinking, Colonel Pak returned to his quarters to sleep. In the early morning of the next day, at approximately 3:15 a.m., he was shot and killed by M-1 rifle fire.[^50] The U.S. side, which had placed great trust in Colonel Pak, who had been actively engaged in suppression operations under Colonel Brown's direction, received a great shock from the assassination incident. When the U.S. military report transmitted an urgent notification on the day of Colonel Pak's assassination, it commented: "Colonel Pak was considered the most outstanding regimental commander and field commander in Korea."[^51] This demonstrates how much the Americans trusted Colonel Pak and lamented his death.

In particular, Military Government Commander Dean, who had personally selected Pak Chin-gyŏng as the appropriate person for full-scale suppression operations, appointed him as the new regiment commander, came directly to Cheju on June 1 to promote him, and personally placed the insignia of colonel rank on him, was greatly angered by Pak's death. According to testimony from Wesley Rosky, a U.S. military advisor, Police Advisor Roberts was also present at Pak's promotion ceremony and came directly to Cheju when Pak was assassinated.[^51] General Dean, accompanied by two firearms researchers, hastened to Cheju at noon on the 18th when the assassination occurred.[^52] After investigating the local situation, he returned to Seoul in the evening carrying Colonel Pak's body.[^53] Colonel Pak's funeral was held on June 22 at 2 p.m. at the Constabulary Headquarters in Namsan-dong, Seoul, as a military funeral attended by numerous officials from all sectors including the commander of the Defense Security Command, unit-related personnel and family members, Military Government Commander Dean, and Civil Minister Ahn Chae-hong.[^54]

Immediately upon the incident, military police, as well as U.S. CIC (Counterintelligence Corps) and CID (Criminal Investigation Division) personnel were deployed for investigation.[^55] Soldiers stationed at regiment headquarters were assembled on the parade ground for questioning. Military police conducted individual inspection of magazines and rifles, and separately assembled and interrogated soldiers who were carrying ammunition or had rifles in poor condition. In particular, the firearms researchers brought by General Dean conducted a thorough examination of all 11th Regiment soldiers' weapons. Colonel Kim Jong-myŏn, at that time a staff member of the Defense Security Command's Intelligence Bureau, was temporarily dispatched to Cheju and served as the regimental intelligence officer, sleeping just one partition wall away from Colonel Pak. He recounted: "The Americans, noting that the scratch marks made by rifling as the bullet passes through the barrel differ for each rifle, conducted a meticulous investigation by having all soldiers fire one round from their M-1 rifles and comparing the bullets."[^56] Kim Jong-myŏn also testified: "Colonel Brown said, 'How can you sleep in the next room and not know when someone was being killed?' and looked at me with suspicion, which I found very uncomfortable. Since I held the same rank of colonel, when Colonel Pak was promoted and I was not, he suspected me of being dissatisfied."[^57]

The investigation was broken open through an anonymous letter. The letter contained the message: "If you apprehend Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil and the senior sergeant of the regiment intelligence section, you will be able to clarify the full details of the assassination incident."[^58] Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil (文相吉), who attended Military Academy class 3, had served as platoon commander and subsequently company commander since the early stages of the Mosulp'o 9th Regiment establishment. Beginning with Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil, persons involved in the assassination incident were arrested one after another. They were Mun Sang-gil (文相吉, Lieutenant), Son Sŏn-ho (孫善鎬, Sergeant), Pae Kyŏng-yong (裵敬用, Sergeant), Yang Hoe-ch'ŏn (梁會千, Private First Class), Yi Chŏng-u (李禎雨, Sergeant, not apprehended), Shin Sang-u (申尙雨, Sergeant), Kang Sŭng-gyŏ (姜承珪, Sergeant), Hwang Chu-bok (黃柱福, Sergeant), and Kim Chŏng-do (金正道, Sergeant)—a total of 9 persons.[^59] According to armed guerrilla records, among those involved in the assassination incident, Yi Chŏng-u went into the mountains with one M1 rifle and joined the armed guerrillas.[^60]

It was determined that Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho, dispatched from the Pusan 5th Regiment, was the one who directly fired the fatal shots killing Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng. The Constabulary Headquarters, given the gravity of the incident, transported the assassins to Seoul on July 12 for referral to the High Military Court Martial.[^61] The assassination of Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng was recorded as the first Army Funeral, the death of a high-ranking officer, and thus became a topic of public interest, with media coverage of the trial process given considerable emphasis.

[^50]: Chosŏn Ilbo, June 19, 1948.

[^51]: Charles L. Wesolowsky (age 80, Florida, USA, then 11th Regiment military advisor, recorded October 28, 2001), testimony.

[^52]: Chosŏn Ilbo, June 19, 1948.

[^53]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, June 20, 1948.

[^54]: Kyŏnghyang Sinmun, June 23, 1948.

[^55]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 973, June 18, 1948.

**[p. 219]**

The High Military Court Martial opened on August 9 in the Defense Security Command's high military court martial chamber, with Colonel Yi Ŭng-jun (李應俊) as presiding judge. The focus of the trial was directed toward clarifying the motives and background of the regiment commander's assassination. At this hearing, the prosecutor of the High Military Court Martial, Colonel Yi Chi-hyŏng (李智衡), read an indictment stating that Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil, instigated by Kim Tal-sam, the responsible person of the armed guerrillas, had planned the assassination, and Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho had assassinated Colonel Pak with a pistol.[^62]

However, in court, Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil denied the "Kim Tal-sam instigation claim." Mun Sang-gil stated that he had agreed with the position of former Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl to avoid fratricidal conflict, and although he had met with Kim Tal-sam to arrange a meeting with Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, he had not assassinated Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng under his instigation. Mun Sang-gil further stated: "After electrical torture, with my eyes covered, I signed the interrogation record without knowing its contents—my testimony here in this court is the truth."[^63]

Subsequently, other defendants, while comparing the operations of former Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl with those of Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng, uniformly stated that their motive for assassination was to prevent reckless suppression operations. Sergeant Shin Sang-u testified: "Colonel Pak slaughtered fellow countrymen and was promoted. An American directly pinned the insignia on him."[^64] In particular, Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho, who directly assassinated Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng, testified: "Colonel Pak insisted that for 30 million Koreans, it was acceptable to sacrifice 300,000 Cheju residents and that we must engage in internecine conflict. In his actual actions, he pressured innocent civilians and caused them to be massacred. Colonel Pak was clearly anti-national. To rescue the people and build a noble National Defense Constabulary, there was no choice but to sacrifice Colonel Pak."[^65]

The "Kim Tal-sam instigation claim" regarding the assassination of Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng spread to affect even former Regiment Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, who had conducted peace negotiations with Kim Tal-sam on April 28, and Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl was suddenly arrested on suspicion of involvement. At that time, one media outlet reported: "According to those close to the Defense Security Command, Colonel Kim's detention is not particularly related to the Colonel Pak assassination incident," but added: "However, it is said that Colonel Kim directly met with the so-called People's Liberation Army commander Kim Tal-sam, and it is suspected that Colonel Kim's detention is related to such contact."[^66]

The suspicion was: why had Colonel Kim not earlier pursued strong suppression operations and instead negotiated with the armed guerrillas? However, Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl appeared in court and testified: "All military operations were under the direction of U.S. Army Captain Drues, the chief operations officer of the supreme war council at that time, and I have absolutely no knowledge of Colonel Pak's murder." He clarified that all military operations, including negotiations with the armed guerrillas, were conducted under U.S. direction, and when Colonel Chŏng Il-gwŏn (丁一權), the chief of staff of the Constabulary Headquarters, concurred with this testimony, Colonel Kim was able to avoid the suspicion.[^67]

The defense lawyers focused on the assassination suspects' motives. Defense counsel Kim Hŭng-su (金興洙), a major appointed by the court, argued: "Lieutenant Mun and the others received no orders from anyone alive and had no conspiratorial background; their actions arose solely from national love and a sense of justice, so please give special consideration."[^68] Subsequently, civilian defense counsel Kim Yang (金養) made the following argument:

> The direct cause of the Cheju disturbance incident is already identified by various responsible parties as stemming from the misconduct of some vicious police and corrupt officials. In this liberated land, how could these young people, witnessing the grievances of Cheju residents and the death of fellow countrymen who fell in unintended fratricidal conflict, prevent this and save 300,000 residents? After agonizing over this, with a painful resolve that their own lives were worthless, they committed this crime, even if foolishly and narrowly conceived. Of course, assassinating Colonel Pak is a crime and a mistake. However, such crime also bears the responsibility of today's chaotic society. With the transfer of power under the August 15 system imminent and our country seeking complete independence and sovereignty, foreign forces are rearming and eyeing our land for renewed invasion. I request that these brave young lives be spared so that they may instead have the opportunity to die for our homeland, and I trust that they will certainly fight for the nation.[^69]

However, Prosecutor Colonel Yi Chi-hyŏng characterized it as a "serious crime violating military discipline, the very lifeblood of the army, under the mistaken ideology of national supremacy," and demanded capital punishment for the defendants.[^70] The sentencing judgment was delivered on August 14, one day before the establishment of the Republic of Korea. The court sentenced Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil, Sergeants Shin Sang-u, Son Sŏn-ho, and Pae Kyŏng-yong to death by firing squad. Yang Hoe-ch'ŏn received a life sentence, and Kang Sŭng-gyŏ received a five-year prison sentence, while Sergeants Hwang Chu-bok and Kim Chŏng-do were acquitted due to insufficient evidence.[^71]

[^56]: Kim Jong-myŏn testimony. (Paek Sŏn-yŏp, *Records of Mt. Chiri*, Koryŏwŏn, 1992, pp. 123-124).

[^57]: Kim Jong-myŏn testimony. (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, *4·3 Speaks* ③, Chŏnyewŏn, 1995, p. 201).

[^58]: National Defense Ministry Military History Compilation Committee, preceding work, p. 441.

[^59]: Kukje Sinmun, August 10, 1948; Kyŏnghyang Sinmun, August 15, 1948; Hansung Ilbo, August 15, 1948.

[^60]: Cheju Island People's Guerrilla Corps Struggle Report (Moon Chang-song, *Halla Mountain Knows: The Hidden Truth of April 3*, 1995, p. 82).

[^61]: Chayu Sinmun, July 13, 1948.

**[p. 220]**

This sentence, handed down by Presiding Judge Colonel Yi Ŭng-jun, the Inspection General of the Defense Security Command, was to be executed after receiving approval from Defense Security Command Commander Yu Dong-yŏl and Military Government Commander Dean.[^72] However, with the submission of defense counsel's petition for commutation and statements from various sectors requesting leniency, public opinion arose opposing the death sentences. Perhaps as a result, the death sentences for Sergeants Shin Sang-u and Pae Kyŏng-yong were commuted to life sentences by special pardon just before execution.[^73] However, Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil and Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho were ultimately executed by firing squad on September 23 on a mountainside in Suksŏk, Kyŏnggi Province.

Before execution, Mun Sang-gil was given the opportunity to make a final statement. He said: "At age twenty-three, Mun Sang-gil departs. You are the Korean military. My final hope is that you not become a Korean military serving under the × × × and the × × × do × × ×." Thereafter, Son Sŏn-ho sang a military song beginning with "Patriotic waves rushing through the blood vessels…" and then prayed: "O God! Let the National Defense Force become one that fights for the nation,"[^74] and was executed by firing squad.[^75]

#### 2) Later Suppression Operations of the 11th Regiment

When Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng was assassinated, the U.S. military command appointed Colonel Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok (崔慶祿) as the new 11th Regiment commander on June 21, and Major Song Yo-ch'an (宋堯讚) as deputy regiment commander.[^73] Both were former Japanese military warrant officers who had accumulated combat experience during the Japanese period and had a shared background of having attended military English school during the U.S. Military Government period.[^74]

Upon arriving in Cheju, Colonel Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok and Major Song Yo-ch'an immediately focused on searching for the assassins of former Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng while continuing suppression operations similar to those of their predecessor. Examining all entries in the U.S. 24th Army Corps daily reports from this period, the Constabulary deployed large-scale search operations from the day Colonel Ch'oe assumed office. From 6 p.m. on June 21 for 24 hours, 48 persons were apprehended in Songdang-ri area, Kuja-myŏn, and one carbine was recovered. During the same period, 29 persons were apprehended in Samyang-ri, Cheju town.[^75] On June 25 as well, from 6 p.m. to 6 p.m. the next day, search operations yielded the apprehension of 176 persons, with money totaling approximately 30,000 wŏn and food supplies for about 50 people seized.[^76]

Synthesizing the content of these reports, the Constabulary conducted search operations on June 21, 22, 25, and 26, achieving results in capturing 253 "bandits." However, only one firearm was recovered. On June 25 in particular, 176 "bandits" were apprehended without a single captured weapon. One media outlet reported the Constabulary operation of June 26, in which 200 persons were apprehended without any recovered weapons, as follows:

> The National Defense Constabulary, which had been attempting to maintain security in mountainous villages, from the early morning of June 26, employed force in the northern Cheju direction, surrounding each mountainous village and apprehending numerous persons suspected of participating in the disturbance. This operation was a large-scale action encompassing coastal regions as well. By afternoon on this day, the number of persons apprehended in Kuja-myŏn, Chochŏn-myŏn, Cheju town, and other areas had reached about 200, among whom were 2 persons recognized as leaders of the riot groups, but the majority were farmers, and not a single weapon was discovered, it is said.[^77]

Such circumstances are confirmed in Colonel Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok's own statements. On July 14, merely 20 days after arriving in Cheju, Colonel Ch'oe stated in a meeting with journalists: "The elite units actually engaged in combat have not yet been apprehended."[^78]

On July 15, the final day of the suppression operations under Colonel Ch'oe's command, he met with journalists and stated: "So far, there have been four sweeps centered on Halla Mountain, but we have been unable to capture the mountain people. Looking at the ages of the mountain people, they range from youth to the elderly. The youngest is twelve years old and the oldest is over sixty. Among them, young people are the most numerous, followed by middle-aged people and youth, and the elderly are third."[^79]

Around this time, armed guerrilla activity was almost nonexistent. On June 22, U.S. military commander Colonel Brown, after guiding journalists on a tour of Cheju Island, stated: "As you have yourselves witnessed, the roads we traveled today a month ago were dangerous and impassable. However, now [we] are no longer concerned about enemy activity."[^80]

[^62]: Kukje Sinmun, August 10, 1948.

[^63]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, August 14, 1948.

[^64]: Hansung Ilbo, August 14, 1948.

[^65]: Hansung Ilbo, August 14, 1948.

[^66]: Kukje Sinmun, July 23, 1948.

[^67]: Kukje Sinmun, August 14, 1948; Hansung Ilbo, August 14, 1948.

[^68]: Hansung Ilbo, August 15, 1948.

[^69]: Hansung Ilbo, August 15, 1948.

[^70]: Chŏng Tong-ung, "The New Tragedy of Chaotic Cheju—Trial Record of Colonel Pak's Assassins," *New Korea News*, October 1948, early issue.

[^71]: Kyŏnghyang Sinmun, August 15, 1948.

[^72]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, August 15, 1948.

[^73]: Kyŏnghyang Sinmun, September 25, 1948.

[^74]: Chayu Sinmun, September 25, 1948; Seoul Sinmun, September 26, 1948.

[^75]: "Memorandum to Colonel Brown," June 21, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948-1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949-1953), Box 4, NARA, Washington, D.C.

[^76]: Han Yong-wŏn, *Army Establishment*, Poyŏng-sa, 1984, p. 58.

**[p. 221]**

They were minor figures. In June 1948, Cho Tŏk-song (趙德松), covering the Jeju situation as a "war correspondent," described the appearance of "prisoners" as follows:

> The prisoners are being escorted back. Young people packed into trucks. A procession dragged along on foot—old and young alike, women mixed in. The rain still has not let up. The complexion of those being led away hunched and silent matches the color of their clothing. From any angle, how could one dare call them rioters? Boys of twelve or thirteen, the elderly past sixty, and women—what has brought them to the state of being designated rioters? I tried speaking to them. But they only turned their heads away. In my field helmet and military rain gear, I must have appeared to their eyes just another Constabulary soldier. You prisoners, being led away past your own village, your own home, with averted eyes—will the sound of peace truly be heard in this village only when this flood of countless prisoners finally ceases?[^28]

Meanwhile, Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng (朴珍景), who had been conducting operations under Colonel Brown's direct command, was promoted to colonel on June 1st with U.S. recognition.[^29] His promotion at that time leapfrogged his seniors—a special advancement granted through General Dean's special consideration.[^30] From the perspective of Jeju islanders, it was an "indiscriminate arrest operation," but General Dean evaluated it as a "successful operation" and promoted Pak to full colonel through special advancement before he had even been on Jeju a full month. However, Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng was assassinated by subordinates dissatisfied with his operational policy on June 18th.

In this manner, Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng's operations—roaming through mid-mountain villages, generating thousands of "prisoners" in the span of a single month—only drove more residents further into the mountains, and with his own assassination, became not a means of resolving the incident but rather a catalyst for its further deterioration.

#### 3) Dispatch of Reinforcement Police

Police Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok dispatched 450 reinforcement police selected from each police precinct, along with a detective unit commanded by Seoul Metropolitan Police Bureau Inspector Ch'oe Nan-su (崔蘭洙), to Jeju Island. The reinforcement police consisted of 350 railroad police and 100 personnel selected from the 6th and 8th precincts. Departing Seoul by special train in the early morning of May 18th, they passed through Mokp'o and arrived on Jeju on the morning of the 19th.[^31] On May 20th as well, dozens of reinforcement

[^28]: Cho Tŏk-song, op. cit., p. 91.
[^29]: Ministry of National Defense, War History Compilation Committee, op. cit., p. 441.
[^30]: Paek Sŏn-yŏp, op. cit., p. 125.

**[p. 222]**

police departed Seoul for Jeju.[^32] Around this time, with the large influx of reinforcement police, by July 1948 police personnel had grown to approximately 2,000 officers—500 existing Jeju police plus 1,500 reinforcement police.[^33]

By dispatching large numbers of reinforcement police who were unfamiliar with local Jeju conditions and operated solely under the perception that "Jeju is a communist island," adverse effects proliferated. One news outlet reported the following regarding police extrajudicial killings of civilians around the time of the May 10 Elections:

> Our reporter group visited Hag wi-ri in Aewŏl Township, which had suffered the greatest casualties and where the crossfire between both sides had been most intense, and heard from instructors and students of the local middle school about conditions at the time: "Even now, talking about the reality before our eyes at that time makes me shudder. About a month after the incident broke out, reinforcement police came to suppress the rioters and shot dead eighteen residents of the village—including an elderly person of seventy and elementary school students. Among the killed were people who sympathized with the police, but the shooting was indiscriminate and made no distinctions. Two young students from our school were ruthlessly sacrificed at that time."[^34]

That police atrocities were a factor in the incident's expansion was recognized to some extent even by Colonel Brown. In reports submitted to his superiors, Colonel Brown stated that "excessive cruelty and terrorism were perpetrated by reinforcement police who arrived on Jeju Island," and revealed that on May 22nd, as a measure for suppressing the uprising, he had ordered the cessation of police terror and killings directed at innocent civilians.[^35] He had determined that police brutality was worsening public opinion and actually hindering the suppression of the incident rather than assisting it. At a press conference in mid-June, Colonel Brown also stated: "There were cases where police officers participating in incident suppression massacred innocent civilians, inflaming public opinion. I will crack down thoroughly on such officers, and currently have five or six officers under arrest and interrogation on murder charges. Meanwhile, I am having all police officers in the province sign pledges to 'abolish torture' and endeavoring to handle the incident in the spirit of democratic policing."[^36]

[^31]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, May 18, 1948; *Tongnip Sinbo*, May 19, 1948; *Sŏul Sinmun*, May 21, 1948.
[^32]: *Chayu Sinmun*, May 21, 1948.
[^33]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 30, 1948.
[^34]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 23, 1948.
[^35]: "Report of Activities on Cheju-Do Island from 22 May 1948 to 30 June 1948," July 1, 1948, The Rothwell H. Brown Papers, Box 3, US Army Military History Institute, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.

**[p. 223]**

However, police brutality did not cease.
