On June 7th, police from the Chŏji Substation arrested a disabled boy, one elderly man, and seven women from Kŭm'ak-ri and, during their transport, shot dead five of them.[^37] When Chŏji Substation police—including forty reinforcement officers—descended on the mid-mountain village of Kŭm'ak-ri that day, most residents fled hastily, but those with limited mobility—the disabled, the elderly, and women—remained in their homes and were captured. Being unable to flee as quickly as the young men was their crime. According to village residents' testimonies, among that day's victims were eighty-year-old elder Kim Chŏng-saeng (金丁生), the disabled Pak Tu-ok (朴斗玉), pregnant woman Pak Kyŏng-saeng (朴庚生), and a woman surnamed Yang.[^38] When this incident later came to light, U.S. CIC investigators launched an inquiry, and thirty-one police officers including the Chŏji Substation chief were arrested.[^39]

Meanwhile, Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner Ch'oe Ch'ŏn received a suspension from duty dated June 11th, which is presumed to constitute disciplinary action in connection with the controversies caused by torture and killings by reinforcement police. One news outlet, reporting Ch'oe Ch'ŏn's suspension, speculated about the reasons: "It is said that he bears responsibility for insufficient supervision of subordinates and other matters in handling the incident."[^40]

The proliferation of civilian casualties at the hands of police stemmed not only from the mistakes of reinforcement police thrust into suppression operations without knowledge of Jeju's circumstances, but also from the indiscriminate deployment of members of private organizations such as the Sŏbuk Youth Corps as temporary police. Additionally, the police authorities' measure of preferentially recruiting and deploying youths from families who had suffered casualties in armed guerrilla attacks[^41] also encouraged retaliatory acts. Some police officers, using leave time, inflicted vigilante punishment on families and relatives of those suspected of being armed guerrillas, creating controversies.[^42] Regarding this, Lester Chorpening, the police adviser dispatched to Jeju Island, stated: "Reinforcement police troops who entered the island resolved to take revenge for the killing of fellow officers and their families, and carried out brutal reprisals."[^43]

[^36]: *Chayu Sinmun*, June 18, 1948.
[^37]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 966, June 11, 1948.
[^38]: Pak Pyŏng-gyu (朴丙圭, resident of Kŭm'ak-ri, Hallim Township), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 263).
[^39]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 968, June 13, 1948.
[^40]: *Hansŏng Ilbo*, June 18, 1948.
[^41]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 24, 1948.
[^42]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 17, 1948.
[^43]: USAFIK, *HUSAFIK*, part III, chapter IV, "Police and Public Security," pp. 420–421.

**[p. 224]**

As harsh operations proved not only unhelpful in suppressing the situation but actually harmful to public opinion, the U.S. Military Government pursued a conciliatory policy through personnel appointments. Having already removed Jeju Governor Yu Hae-jin (柳海辰), who had pursued an exclusively anti-communist hardline policy, on May 28th and appointed Im Kwan-ho (任琯鎬), a Jeju native, as the new governor, the U.S. Military Government then appointed Kim Pong-ho (金鳳昊), also a Jeju native and Deputy Chief of the 8th Police Precinct Bureau, as Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner on June 17th.[^44]

Before taking office, Police Commissioner Kim Pong-ho disclosed that he had heard Police Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok say "There is no one but you to handle the Jeju incident," and stated: "I, a native of Jeju, appeal to my love of homeland to rectify the faults of our police itself, and if there are faults on the civilian side, I am determined to rectify them—but having heard the information thus far, I cannot help but shed tears of blood."[^45] This signaled his intention to pursue a conciliatory policy.

Taking office on June 24th, despite the unfavorable atmosphere caused by the assassination of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng that had occurred just before his arrival, Police Commissioner Kim Pong-ho declared the following at a July 1st conference attended by newly appointed Commander Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok and township and county magistrates throughout the island: "First, I will eradicate and purge illegal and corrupt acts by the police themselves. While resolving blocked transportation, I will not interfere with or obstruct incoming and outgoing goods. Those who were swept along by others will not be punished, and those who surrender will be recognized as law-abiding civilians."[^46] And indeed, that same day—July 1st—he lifted the fishing ban and abolished the intra-provincial travel permit system as concrete conciliatory measures.[^47] On July 5th, he further abolished the travel permit system for passengers on the regular Mokp'o–Jeju shipping line.[^48]

However, Police Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok issued a statement on June 23rd, one day before Police Commissioner Kim Pong-ho's arrival in Jeju, asserting that seeking the cause of the uprising in the undemocratic errors of police officers was "a mistake in thinking and judgment," and declaring that "the fundamental policy for handling the Jeju uprising atrocities remains unchanged from before."[^49] This was an omen that Police Commissioner Kim Pong-ho's efforts to resolve the situation by appeasing public sentiment were bound to fail.

[^44]: *Tong-A Ilbo*, June 19, 1948.
[^45]: *Tongkwang Sinmun*, June 20, 1948.
[^46]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 11, 1948.
[^47]: *Taedong Sinmun*, July 6, 1948.
[^48]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 17, 1948.
[^49]: *Hyŏndae Ilbo*, June 24, 1948.

**[p. 225]**

### b. The Assassination of the Regiment Commander and Reorganization of the Constabulary

#### 1) Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng Killed

In the early morning of June 18, 1948, 11th Regiment Commander Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng was assassinated at his quarters by subordinates. After attending a promotion celebration on June 17th and returning to sleep, Colonel Pak was struck by M-1 rifle fire at approximately 3:15 a.m. the following morning and killed. The U.S. side had placed great trust in Colonel Pak for actively pursuing suppression operations under Colonel Brown's command and was greatly shocked by his assassination. The U.S. military report transmitted an urgent dispatch on the day of Colonel Pak's assassination, commenting: "Colonel Pak is evaluated as the most outstanding unit commander and field commander among Korean officers."[^50] This remark demonstrates how much trust the U.S. had placed in Colonel Pak and how they mourned his death.

In particular, Military Governor Dean—who had identified Pak Chin-gyŏng as the ideal person for full-scale suppression operations, appointed him as the new regiment commander, and on June 1st had personally traveled to Jeju to pin colonel's rank insignia on him—is said to have reacted with great anger to Pak's death. According to testimony from Charles L. Wesolowsky, then the U.S. adviser to the 11th Regiment, Roberts, who was then the U.S. adviser to the Constabulary Headquarters, also attended Pak Chin-gyŏng's promotion ceremony and personally came to Jeju when Pak was assassinated.[^51] Military Governor Dean, on the day of the assassination, June 18th at noon, hastily departed for Jeju with two firearms researchers.[^52] After investigating the local situation, he returned to Seoul that evening carrying Colonel Pak's remains.[^53] Colonel Pak's funeral was held on June 22nd at 2 p.m. at the Constabulary Headquarters in Namsan-dong, Seoul, as a military funeral attended by numerous dignitaries including the Constabulary Commander, unit officials, bereaved family members, Military Governor Dean, Civil Minister An Chae-hong, and others.[^54]

Immediately following the incident, military and police personnel as well as U.S. CIC (Counterintelligence Corps) and CID (Criminal Investigation Division) investigators were deployed to conduct an investigation.[^55] All enlisted men stationed at regiment headquarters were assembled in the parade ground for questioning.

[^50]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 863, June 18, 1948.
[^51]: Charles L. Wesolowsky (age 80, Florida, U.S.A.; then adviser to the 11th Regiment; recorded October 28, 2001), testimony.
[^52]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, June 19, 1948.
[^53]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, June 20, 1948.
[^54]: *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, June 23, 1948.
[^55]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 973, June 18, 1948.

**[p. 226]**

Military police conducted individual inspections of ammunition magazines and rifles, separately assembling and interrogating soldiers who possessed bullets or whose rifles were in poor condition. In particular, the firearms researchers whom Military Governor Dean had brought with him conducted a comprehensive inspection of all 11th Regiment troops' weapons.

Lieutenant Colonel Kim Chong-myŏn (金宗勉), then serving as an officer of the Constabulary Headquarters Intelligence Bureau and temporarily assigned to Jeju as 11th Regiment intelligence staff officer, slept with only a single partition separating him from Colonel Pak and thus witnessed this assassination. Regarding this, Kim Chong-myŏn stated: "The Americans noted that when a bullet exits a gun barrel, the friction with the rifling leaves marks that differ for each rifle. They had all soldiers fire their M-1 rifles once each and conducted a meticulous investigation comparing the bullets."[^56] Kim Chong-myŏn further testified: "Colonel Brown looked at me with suspicious eyes, saying 'How could you not know someone was being killed while sleeping in the next room?'—which made me feel quite put out. He suspected that since Pak and I were the same rank but he had been promoted while I had not, I must have harbored resentment."[^57]

The investigation is said to have gained a breakthrough through an anonymous letter. The letter contained the message: "If you arrest Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil and the regiment information section's senior sergeant, you will be able to reveal the full truth of the assassination."[^58] Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil (文相吉), a graduate of the Military Academy 3rd Class, had served as platoon commander from the establishment of the 9th Regiment at Mosulp'o and was then serving as a company commander. Beginning with Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil, those implicated in the assassination were arrested in succession. There were nine in total: Mun Sang-gil (文相吉, Lieutenant), Son Sŏn-ho (孫善鎬, Sergeant), Pae Kyŏng-yong (裵敬用, Sergeant), Yang Hoe-ch'ŏn (梁會千, Second Sergeant), Yi Chŏng-u (李禎雨, Sergeant, not apprehended), Sin Sang-u (申尙雨, Sergeant), Kang Sŭng-gyu (姜承珪, Sergeant), Hwang Chu-bok (黃柱福, Sergeant), and Kim Chŏng-do (金正道, Sergeant).[^59] According to armed guerrilla records, Yi Chŏng-u, one of those implicated in the assassination, entered the mountains and joined the armed guerrillas while in possession of one M-1 rifle.[^60]

The person who directly fired the shot assassinating Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng was identified as Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho, who had been dispatched from the 5th Regiment in Pusan. The Constabulary Headquarters, in light of the gravity of the incident, transported the assassins to Seoul for referral to the High Military Court on July 12th.[^61] The assassination of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng was recorded as the first Army Funeral (陸軍葬) in history and represented the first death among senior officers, generating great public interest.

[^56]: Kim Chong-myŏn testimony (Paek Sŏn-yŏp, *Silrok Chirisan* [True Record: Chiri Mountain], Koryŏwŏn, 1992, pp. 123–124).
[^57]: Kim Chong-myŏn testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, *4·3 ŭn Malhanda* [4·3 Speaks], vol. 3, Chŏnyewŏn, 1995, p. 201).
[^58]: Ministry of National Defense, War History Compilation Committee, op. cit., p. 441.
[^59]: *Kukche Sinmun*, August 10, 1948; *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, August 15, 1948; *Hansŏng Ilbo*, August 15, 1948.
[^60]: *Cheju-do inmin yugyŏktae t'ujaeng pogosŏ* (Mun Ch'ang-song, *Halla-san ŭn algo itta: mudyŏjin 4·3 ŭi chinsang* [Halla Mountain Knows: The Hidden Truth of 4·3], 1995, p. 82).
[^61]: *Chayu Sinmun*, July 13, 1948.

**[p. 227]**

The trial proceedings received prominent coverage in the press. The High Military Court was convened on August 9th at the Constabulary Headquarters court chamber with Colonel Yi Ŭng-jun (李應俊) presiding as judge. The focus of the trial was on establishing the motive and instigators behind the assassination of the regiment commander. At this proceeding, Lieutenant Colonel Yi Chi-hyŏng (李智衡), prosecutor of the High Military Court, read an indictment stating that Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil, at the instigation of Kim Tal-sam, chief of the armed guerrillas, had devised the assassination plan, and that Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho had assassinated Colonel Pak with a pistol.[^62]

However, Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil denied the "Kim Tal-sam directive theory" in court. Mun Sang-gil stated that, agreeing with the policy of former Commander Kim Il-ryŏl of avoiding fratricidal conflict, he had met with Kim Tal-sam to promote a meeting with Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl, but had not assassinated Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng upon Kim Tal-sam's orders. Mun Sang-gil further stated: "The signatures and seals on the interrogation record were affixed blindly after electric torture, without knowledge of the record's content, under compulsion and without condition. What I have stated in this courtroom is the truth."[^63]

The other defendants, in turn, uniformly compared the operations of former Commander Kim Il-ryŏl with those of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng, stating that preventing reckless suppression operations was their motive. Sergeant Sin Sang-u testified: "Colonel Pak received promotion through the massacre of our fellow Koreans. An American soldier directly pinned the rank insignia on him." Particularly, Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho, who directly shot Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng, testified: "Colonel Pak maintained that for the sake of thirty million Koreans, all three hundred thousand Jeju islanders could be sacrificed—that fratricide was necessary. Through his actual operations, he oppressed innocent civilians and carried out massacres. Colonel Pak was certainly anti-national. To save our fellow Koreans and to establish a sacred National Defense Constabulary, there was no choice but to sacrifice Colonel Pak, I thought."[^64]

The "Kim Tal-sam directive theory" regarding the assassination of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng sent shockwaves to former Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, who had conducted peace negotiations with Kim Tal-sam on April 28th. Lieutenant Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl was summarily arrested on suspicion of being the mastermind. One contemporary news outlet reported: "Officials close to the Constabulary Headquarters stated that the reason for Colonel Kim's detention had little connection to the Colonel Pak assassination incident," yet also noted: "However, it is alleged that he had direct contact with so-called People's Liberation Army Commander Kim Tal-sam, and Colonel Kim's detention is believed to stem from such a connection."[^65]

[^62]: *Kukche Sinmun*, August 10, 1948.
[^63]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, August 14, 1948.
[^64]: *Hansŏng Ilbo*, August 14, 1948.
[^65]: *Kukche Sinmun*, July 23, 1948.

**[p. 228]**

The suspicion was why he had not conducted strong-arm suppression operations from the start instead of negotiating with the armed guerrillas. However, Lieutenant Colonel Kim Il-ryŏl appeared in court and stated: "All military operations were under the command of U.S. Captain Drust, who was the operational staff officer at the highest-level command conference at that time. I knew absolutely nothing about Colonel Pak's killing." He disclosed that all military operations, including negotiations with the armed guerrillas, had proceeded under U.S. command. Colonel Chŏng Il-gwŏn, Chief of Staff of the Constabulary Headquarters, also concurred with this account, thereby clearing Kim Il-ryŏl of suspicion.[^66]

The defense attorneys focused their arguments on the defendants' motives for the crime. Appointed defense counsel Major Kim Hŭng-su (金興洙) argued: "Lieutenant Mun and those below him received no orders from any living superior and have no suspicious ideological background whatsoever. Their crime arose solely from national patriotism and a sense of justice. I respectfully ask for special consideration." Subsequently, private defense counsel Kim Yang (金養) presented the following argument:

> The direct cause of the Jeju Island disturbance is already recognized by those in charge to have resulted from the misconduct of some vicious police officers and corrupt officials. In this liberated land, these young men, having witnessed all the grievances held by the Jeju people and the deaths of compatriots fallen in an unintended fratricidal conflict, deliberated on how to prevent this and save the three hundred thousand islanders. Despite their simple and narrow judgment, they resolved with painful determination that even sacrificing their own lives would be acceptable, and carried out this action. Of course, the assassination of Colonel Pak is unlawful and wrong. However, such a crime also bears the responsibility of today's troubled society. As the government transition of August 15th approaches and this land moves toward complete self-reliant independence, foreign powers are rearming and scheming to invade this land once more. I therefore hope that these courageous young lives will be spared and given the opportunity to die instead for our motherland, and I believe that they will surely fight for the nation.[^67]

However, prosecutor Lieutenant Colonel Yi Chi-hyŏng characterized it as "a grave crime against military discipline—the lifeblood of an army—arising from a misguided ideology of national supremacy" and sought the death penalty for all defendants.[^68] The sentencing judgment was held on August 14th, one day before the proclamation of the Republic of Korea. The court sentenced Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil and Sergeants Sin Sang-u, Son Sŏn-ho, and Pae Kyŏng-yong to death by firing squad. Yang Hoe-ch'ŏn received life imprisonment, and Kang Sŭng-gyu received a five-year sentence. Sergeants Hwang Chu-bok and Kim Chŏng-do were acquitted on grounds of insufficient evidence.[^69]

[^66]: *Kukche Sinmun*, August 14, 1948; *Hansŏng Ilbo*, August 14, 1948.
[^67]: *Hansŏng Ilbo*, August 15, 1948.
[^68]: Chŏng Tong-ung (鄭東熊), "Tongnan Cheju ŭi sae pigyŏk—Pak Taelyŏng salhae beom chaep'an ki" [The New Tragedy of Turbulent Cheju: Record of the Trial of Colonel Pak's Killer], *Saehan Minbo*, October 1948, first half.
[^69]: *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, August 15, 1948.

**[p. 229]**

The verdict handed down by Judge Colonel Yi Ŭng-jun, Constabulary Headquarters Inspector General, was to proceed through ratification by Constabulary Commander Yu Tong-yŏl and approval by Military Governor Dean before execution.[^70] However, when the defense submitted a petition for sentence reduction and various quarters issued statements demanding commutation, public opinion opposed to the death sentences emerged. Thanks to this, the death sentences for Sin Sang-u and Pae Kyŏng-yong were commuted to life imprisonment through special pardon just before execution.[^71] However, Lieutenant Mun Sang-gil and Sergeant Son Sŏn-ho were ultimately executed by firing squad on September 23rd on a mountainside in Suksŏk, Kyŏnggi Province. When given his final opportunity to speak before execution, Mun Sang-gil said: "At twenty-three, Mun Sang-gil departs. You are soldiers of Chosŏn. My final wish is that you not become a Korean military serving under ×××, beneath ×××, doing ×××—I depart with this hope." Then Son Sŏn-ho sang a military song beginning with "The flag of patriotism surging in our blood…" and offered a prayer: "O, God! Let the national defense forces fighting for our nation become so," before being executed by firing squad.[^72]

#### 2) Later Suppression Operations of the 11th Regiment

When Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng was assassinated, the U.S. military command appointed Lieutenant Colonel Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok (崔慶祿) as new 11th Regiment Commander and Major Song Yo-ch'an (宋堯讚) as deputy commander on June 21st.[^73] Both were former Japanese Imperial Army warrant officers who had accumulated combat experience during the Japanese period and shared the common background of attending the U.S. Military Government's Military English School together.[^74]

Upon arriving in Jeju, Commander Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok and Deputy Commander Song Yo-ch'an devoted themselves simultaneously to tracking down the assassins of former Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng and continuing the same suppression operations as their predecessor. Examining all Constabulary operations recorded in U.S. 24th Army Corps daily reports from this period, the Constabulary launched large-scale search operations from the day Commander Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok assumed command. From 6 p.m. on June 21st through the following twenty-four hours, forty-eight people were arrested in the vicinity of Songdang-ri in Kujwa Township and one carbine was recovered.

[^70]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, August 15, 1948.
[^71]: *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, September 25, 1948.
[^72]: *Chayu Sinmun*, September 25, 1948; *Sŏul Sinmun*, September 26, 1948.
[^73]: "Memorandum to Colonel Brown," June 21, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 4, NARA, Washington, D.C.
[^74]: Han Yong-wŏn (韓鎔源), *Ch'anggŭn* [Army Founding], Pakyŏngsa, 1984, p. 58.

**[p. 230]**

[^77]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 868, June 24, 1948.

[^78]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 871, June 28, 1948.

[^79]: Chosŏn Ilbo, July 4, 1948.

[^80]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, July 21, 1948.

[^81]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, July 29, 1948.

**[p. 231]**

stating that "there have been no major incidents; roads and communication lines throughout the province are all being restored, and security conditions have improved to some degree."[^80] On June 23, Yŏ Dong-yŏl, Minister of National Defense, also stated that "the actual actions and capabilities of the insurgent side appear to be declining."[^81] The fact that the armed guerrillas eased their offensive pressure and temporarily suspended operations appears to have been a covert maneuver to prepare countermeasures as the Constabulary, which had been observing the situation up to that point, began full-scale operations. Regarding this, Ch'oe Nan-su, a police superintendent at the Police Administration Bureau, analyzed: "After May 20, the insurgents changed their methods of struggle. Some are feigning surrender while some elite units have scattered into several smaller units and are hiding armed in the mountains. In other words, they appear to have entered a 'protracted war.'"[^82] A press report stated: "The insurgents continue their struggle against the police. When the National Defense Constabulary arrives, they disappear without resistance. They are expressing their attitude toward the Constabulary through leaflets saying 'Dear Constabulary, overthrow the black dogs' [referring to policemen in this manner]. The insurgents are showing the Constabulary their position in this way."[^83] Meanwhile, as strong suppression operations continued, petitions calling for peaceful resolution were submitted one after another, centered on gatherings of Jeju natives in various regions. On June 19, the Kwangju Society of Jeju Natives (光州濟友會) submitted a petition to the Jeju police authorities requesting: △ abandoning forceful measures and adopting peaceful methods while strictly limiting the possession of weapons; △ guaranteeing the livelihood of the provincial residents; △ immediately dissolving terror-conducting private organizations; △ abolishing the maritime travel certificate system, particularly between Jeju and Mok'po; △ severely punishing those who torture suspects to death or commit assault—and other demands.[^84] On July 1, the Seoul Society of Jeju Natives submitted recommendations to more than forty patriotic political parties and social organizations requesting: △ that a consultative meeting on countermeasures for the Jeju incident be convened including all patriotic political parties and organizations; △ that appropriate measures be devised for dispatching field investigation teams including representatives from all patriotic political parties and organizations; △ that a countermeasures committee be composed of leading figures from all sectors and that the incident be resolved politically through consultation with military government officials.[^85] Following this proposal, on July 6, more than forty political parties and social organizations gathered in the Democratic Independence Party conference room to discuss the composition of a countermeasures committee.[^86] The Pusan Society of Jeju Natives (釜山濟友會), which had approximately fifty thousand members from Jeju, on July 5

**[p. 232]**

pointed out that "there is no doubt that this incident involves an armed uprising undertaken by all provincial residents as the final peaceful method," and then submitted a petition with content requesting "since it is wholly improper to launch military attacks in handling this incident, resolve it quickly through peaceful methods," which was conveyed to commanding generals including Ha Chi, Military Governor Dean, the Police Administration Bureau director, the chief of the Judiciary, the UN Commission on Korea, and other relevant quarters. Furthermore, to concretely understand the actual situation, the delegation, together with newspaper reporters from Pusan, departed for Jeju in the afternoon of July 6.[^87] Despite large-scale search operations and the detention of residents, as the Constabulary faced no armed response from the guerrillas and encountered mounting pressure from all sectors to resolve the situation peacefully, it declared 'suspension of operational activities.' Colonel Choe Kyŏng-nok took measures including lifting the transportation blockade, canceling the fishing prohibition, and extending travel hours, and issued a statement with content reading: "Henceforth, except in special cases, operational activities shall be suspended, but we will cooperate with the police and each administrative agency and accelerate the restoration of order."[^88] With this, the Jeju situation entered a period of relative calm. However, the operations conducted by the 11th Regiment of the Constabulary, which swept through midmountain villages and detained residents, left many aftereffects. A press outlet covering Jeju at the time reported that the tent detention camp monitored by the National Defense Constabulary contained young people in their twenties through elderly people in their sixties, and quoted detainees as saying to reporters: "These people were not captured in the mountains but were seized while engaged in farming in their own villages. 'This is now our third month,' they pleaded to our group."[^89] This situation caused provincial residents to spurn not only the police but also the Constabulary, further complicating the resolution of the incident. Around that time, a journalist dispatched to Jeju recorded the actual state of the village in this manner:

> Everywhere, collapsed houses remain sprawled across fields and ridges as they are, while only the steadily remaining police box barriers stand impossibly high. Fifty to forty villagers return from labor service constructing the police communication line and police box stone wall. Our group passes them listlessly. When someone in our group from Jeju speaks, they suddenly surround us on all sides and create a sea of weeping. While striking hoes on rocks, they cry out: "We cannot die even if we want to die, and we cannot live even if we want to live." An elderly woman in her sixties, seized with fear and indignation, pounds her chest while pointing at something. After the house signs that were affixed disappeared overnight, all villagers were taken into the police box and beaten and tortured. Is the crime with the insurgents or with the villagers? The gunfire is quiet, but why are wrinkled faces so filled with sadness and sobs ready to burst? When the journalist asks what conditions are now like, they respond by shouting: "Guns from far away are not frightening, but the gun barrel from near is terrifying."[^90]

In particular, indiscriminate detention drove young people into the ranks of the armed guerrillas. A press report stated: "Across the six-hundred-li expanse of Jeju Island, it is difficult to see young people in village after village. They have thrown themselves into what is called the vanguard of the People's Liberation Army to avoid indiscriminate mass arrests," and quoted responses to a question about the greatest suffering: "We won't tolerate being told to produce sons and daughters not even registered in the family registers."[^91]

3) Reorganization of the 9th Regiment

The Constabulary Headquarters revived the Constabulary's 9th Regiment as of July 15, appointing Major Sŏng Yo-ch'an, the deputy commander of the existing 11th Regiment, as regimental commander, and Captain Sŏ Chong-ch'ŏl (徐鐘喆), the battalion commander of the existing 11th Regiment, as deputy regimental commander respectively.[^92] Colonel Choe Kyŏng-nok's 11th Regiment, having released the original Jeju-origin 9th Regiment personnel for assignment and stationed them on Jeju Island, withdrew to Suwon in Kyŏnggi Province, the regiment's original founding location, on July 24.[^93] Prior to this, the Constabulary Headquarters had, on July 21, withdrawn two battalions under the 3rd Brigade from Pusan and transferred them to Jeju for assignment to the 9th Regiment.[^94] In other words, the 9th Regiment, which had been incorporated into the 11th Regiment on May 15, was reorganized with different personnel after exactly two months. Additionally, the responsibility for the Constabulary forces stationed in Jeju was transferred from Choe Kyŏng-nok to Sŏng Yo-ch'an. Why did the Constabulary go through such a complex process to change the stationed forces in Jeju? First, one reason could be that the Jeju situation had been brought under control. On July 12, shortly before the Constabulary's reorganization, Staff General Yi Hyŏng-gŭn (李亨根) of the Ministry of National Defense announced at a press conference that "the Jeju situation has been brought to closure."[^95] However, if the situation had truly been brought to closure, there would have been no need to move forces; rather, the remaining troops should have continued cleanup operations.

**[p. 233]**

— were taken to the substation and beaten, and moreover were subject to mistreatment, so they say. Is the crime on the side of the rioters, or on the side of the villagers? The sound of gunfire has fallen silent, yet why are the furrowed brows so full of gloom, sunken in an indignation that seems ready to burst? When the reporter asked "How are things now?", the answer came back with a shout: "Gunfire from afar isn't frightening — it's the rifle barrel up close that's frightening."[^90]

In particular, indiscriminate mass arrests produced the perverse result of driving young men toward the armed guerrilla side. One newspaper reported: "In village after village around the 600-ri circumference of Jeju Island, young men are nowhere to be seen. They have reportedly thrown themselves into the vanguard of the so-called People's Liberation Army to escape indiscriminate mass arrests." When asked what caused the greatest suffering, people answered: "Having to produce sons and daughters who don't even appear in the family registry is unbearable" — thus conveying the atmosphere of the time.[^91]

#### 3) Reorganization of the 9th Regiment

The Constabulary Headquarters, effective July 15, revived the Constabulary's 9th Regiment, appointing former 11th Regiment Deputy Commander Major Song Yo-ch'an (宋堯讚) as regimental commander and former 11th Regiment battalion commander Captain Sŏ Chong-ch'ŏl (徐鐘喆) as deputy commander.[^92] Commander Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok's 11th Regiment detached only the original 9th Regiment troops of Jeju origin and left them on Jeju Island, then withdrew to Suwon, Kyŏnggi Province — where the regiment had been founded — on July 24.[^93] Prior to this, the Constabulary Headquarters, in preparation for the 11th Regiment's withdrawal, drew two battalions from the 3rd Brigade on July 21 and moved them to Jeju, attaching them to the 9th Regiment.[^94] In other words, the 9th Regiment, which had been merged into the 11th Regiment on May 15, was reorganized exactly two months later with its personnel changed. And command responsibility for Jeju garrison troops was transferred from Ch'oe Kyŏng-nok to Song Yo-ch'an.

What was the true reason for changing Jeju garrison troops through such a complex process? One reason that might be cited is the apparent stabilization of the Jeju situation. On July 12, just before the Constabulary's reorganization, Directorate of National Defense Chief of Staff Yi Hyŏng-gŭn (李亨根) announced at a press conference: "The Jeju Island situation has reached a turning point."[^95] However, if the situation had indeed reached a turning point, the appropriate response would have been to remain and complete mopping-up operations — there would have been no need to move troops anew.

[^90]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, July 11, 1948.
[^91]: *Sŏul Sinmun*, July 13, 1948.
[^92]: Constabulary Headquarters Special Order No. 88, July 6, 1948.
[^93]: Army Headquarters Military Inspection Office, *Yukkun Yoksa Ilji* [Army History Daily Record], vol. 1, July 24, 1948.
[^94]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Weekly Summary, No. 149, July 23, 1948.
[^95]: *Tong-A Ilbo*, July 13, 1948.

**[p. 234]**

The true reason for the troop exchange can be attributed to the intention to definitively conclude the Jeju situation ahead of the establishment of the government. Initially, Major General W. L. Roberts, an advisor to the Ministry of National Defense, stated the following to Colonel Brown, the U.S. military commander in Jeju, when dispatching the 11th Regiment command to Jeju on June 21:

> In response to your request to dispatch, I am informing you of the mission we expect from the officers we will dispatch. Today we are dispatching Major Choe (Kyŏng-nok) as regimental commander of the 11th Regiment and Major Sŏng (Yo-ch'an) as deputy regimental commander. Major Choe was selected from all commanders here, I like him myself, and his background appears to be very good. Major Sŏng, as I understand him, is a strong and courageous person. We will not be sending an inspector. This is because Major Sŏng is the finest officer we have available, and we can utilize him as an inspector.[^96]

The U.S. military praised the deputy regimental commander Sŏng Yo-ch'an for his 'strong and courageous' qualities. In comparison, they viewed the regimental commander Choe Kyŏng-nok as having the confidence of those around him but as being moderate. However, a U.S. secret document of July 12, shortly before the 9th Regiment's reorganization, recorded regarding the Jeju situation: "The rebellion has continued, but as the latter half of this month (June) progressed, the Constabulary commander revealed that 'pacification is becoming visible; the reason operations are slow is that the Constabulary is reluctant to suppress the rebellion through the sole means of massacre.'"[^97] While Colonel Choe Kyŏng-nok's operations also involved the indiscriminate detention of residents, they represented a moderation compared to the previous period. It was analyzed that operations were slower because Colonel Choe Kyŏng-nok was reluctant to engage in 'massacre.' By contrast, Sŏng Yo-ch'an, appointed as commander of the reorganized 9th Regiment after taking a position as deputy regimental commander of the 11th Regiment in Jeju, possessed a different character from Choe Kyŏng-nok. Major General Roberts evaluated Sŏng Yo-ch'an as a 'strong and courageous' person. Harold Fischgrund, who was the 9th Regiment's advisor at the time, testified that Sŏng Yo-ch'an was "a very tough person."[^98] Kim Chŏng-mu (金貞武), who was dispatched to Jeju on July 15 when the 9th Regiment was reorganized and served as the 9th Regiment's supply officer until December 15 of that year, testified about his experience with Sŏng Yo-ch'an as follows:

> I went to Jeju as supply officer of the 9th Regiment. At that time, the personnel officer was Choi Se-in, the administrative officer was Tak Sŏng-nok, and the operations officer was Han Yŏng-ju. Not long after arriving in Jeju, one day I noticed that my direct subordinate, Purchasing Officer Kang Ŭi-wŏn (姜義遠), a 4th-class military academy graduate, was missing. Upon inquiry, I learned that he had been executed under the orders of Regimental Commander Sŏng Yo-ch'an. I was promoted to captain on September 1. One day in September the regimental commander called me and suddenly said, 'You conduct a court martial. This guy must be put to death!' He was ordering me to pass a death sentence on someone I did not know and whose alleged crime I was unaware of. While I had studied military law courts for a few hours at the military academy, I had never actually conducted a trial and did not know what to do. When I went to the courtroom, I saw the defendant was almost dead from torture. The defendant was Pak Kyŏng-hun, who had been the Jeju provincial governor. The alleged crime was that rice was given to communist insurgents from the governor's residence. A person's life hung in the balance over a mere measure of rice. But I recalled from my childhood in the countryside that the key to the rice warehouse was always carried by grandmother, so I asked: 'Did the defendant personally give the rice?' The governor, Pak Kyŏng-hun, who was about fifty-seven or fifty-eight years old at the time, replied: 'No. I learned this only while detained and being investigated. My wife's relatives came and told me they would starve, so I heard the story that rice was given to help them.' Even though the rice came from the governor's residence, I judged it not guilty because one cannot be held responsible for one's spouse's actions. So I entered consultation with several legal advisors. One person said, 'They're ordering execution, but how about twenty years?' Others spoke of fifteen years or ten years. After anguished deliberation, I proposed to the panel: 'This is technically not guilty, but since it would be problematic if they claimed the wife was executed, how about three years?' We obtained agreement and passed a three-year sentence. I still have a guilty conscience for sentencing someone to three years when they were in fact not guilty. In any case, when I reported the verdict to the regimental commander, he said 'This communist bastard!' and beat me with his helmet. I was beaten so badly my head swelled considerably. Personnel Officer Choi Se-in, who participated in the trial with me, was also beaten severely. And Colonel of the Military Police Sŏng Hyo-soon was my classmate. He drank every day and suffered. I would not ask him about it. But from what I heard, the military police were putting dozens of people in caves and beating them. So I think he was troubled. He was such a good person. In the end, he managed to leave Jeju in the middle of his term. Also, Operations Officer Yŏng-ju was beaten by the regimental commander day and night.[^99]

Meanwhile, regarding the reason for the regimental change, another U.S. report provided insight. Strikingly, the reason for the troop change was 'training.' Approximately a week after July 21, when two battalions of the 3rd Brigade from Pusan arrived in Jeju, and July 24, when the 11th Regiment departed Jeju, completing the 9th Regiment's reorganization, Military Governor Dean told Police Administration Bureau Chief Chŏ Pyŏng-ok: "We have a policy of rotating the National Defense Constabulary regiments in Jeju for field training. One regiment will be stationed in Jeju for four to six weeks. Regiment-level training will always be conducted in the mountainous areas."[^100] The policy was to utilize the Jeju situation as a field training ground for the Constabulary.

다. 8‧25 Underground Election and Armed Guerrilla Reorganization

1) The 'South Korean Delegate' Election

From mid-July 1948, 'underground elections' were being held throughout South Korea. This resulted from North Korea's establishment of its government. In establishing its government, North Korea maximized the use of 'South-North negotiations' to provide legitimacy. With the goal of establishing a unified government, the First Conference of Political Parties and Social Organizations of North and South Korea was convened in P'yongyang on April 19, 1948. This conference was attended by Kim Ku and others. At this meeting, a joint statement was issued with the content: "Even if a South Korean separate election is held, its results will not be recognized, and instead a unified legislative body election will be conducted to establish a Korean constitution and establish a unified democratic government."[^101] However, as the May 10 election was held in South Korea and government establishment drew near, the Second Inter-Korean Conference convened in P'yongyang from June 29 to July 5, 1948. The Second Inter-Korean Conference was attended by representatives of fifteen political parties and organizations from the North, including the Sŏbuk Labour Party, and more than twenty political and organizational representatives from the South who had either remained in North Korea after the first conference or had secretly crossed the 38th parallel. However, Kim Ku and Kim Kyou-sik refused to attend, saying: "We promised to prevent national division and ethnic separation, but now that a separate South Korean government is being established, for North Korea to also establish a separate government is an act of ethnic division."

The Second Inter-Korean Conference emphasized that the soon-to-be-established 'Democratic People's Republic of Korea' would be a unified government. Therefore, the election was decided to be held not only in North Korean territory but also throughout South Korea. However, since it was practically impossible to conduct an open election in South Korea, South Korea decided to conduct a 'two-stage election.' That is, representatives (1,080 persons) selected five to seven from each city and county would convene as the South Korean People's Delegate Assembly from August 21 in Haeju to select 360 delegates to the Supreme People's Assembly on August 25.

**[p. 235]**

I went to Jeju as the 9th Regiment's logistics staff officer. At the time, the personnel staff officer was Ch'oe Se-in, the information staff officer was T'ak Sŏng-rok, and the operations staff officer was Han Yŏng-ju. It had not been long since I arrived in Jeju when one day a subordinate of mine — Procurement Officer Kang Ŭi-wŏn (姜義遠, a graduate of the Military Academy's 4th Class) — was nowhere to be found. When I inquired, I was told he had been executed on Regimental Commander Song Yo-ch'an's orders. I was promoted to captain effective September 1, and one day in September the regimental commander summoned me and bluntly said: "You be the judge. This bastard has to die!" He was ordering me to pronounce a death sentence on someone I didn't know and whose criminal facts I didn't know. I had studied court-martial proceedings for a few hours at the military academy, but I'd never conducted a trial and didn't know what to do. When I went to the courtroom, the person had been so badly tortured that he was half dead. The defendant was Pak Kyŏng-hun, who had been governor of Jeju Island. The criminal charge was that a *mal* [unit of measure] of rice from the governor's residence had been given to the communist bandits. A person's life was hanging on a *mal* of rice. But I remembered from childhood that in the countryside, the key to the rice storehouse was always carried by grandmother, and so I asked: "Did the defendant personally give the rice?" To this, Governor Pak, who appeared to be about fifty-seven or fifty-eight years old at the time, replied: "No. I only learned of this while being detained and investigated myself. I was told that a relative of my wife came by saying they were starving and had it given to them." Since even if rice had come from the governor's residence, the wife's actions couldn't be held against him, I judged it as not guilty. So I went into deliberations with the legal officer and several others. One person said: "They're telling us to execute him — what about twenty years?" There were those who said fifteen years, others who said ten. After much deliberation, I proposed to the judges: "This is a not-guilty verdict, but if they're going to take the wife and execute her, that will be a problem — so how about three years?" I obtained agreement and announced a three-year sentence. To this day I feel a pang of conscience about pronouncing a three-year sentence on someone who was innocent. In any case, when I reported the trial result to the regimental commander, he beat me over the head with his helmet screaming "You son-of-a-bitch communist!" I was beaten so badly my head swelled greatly. Personnel Staff Officer Ch'oe Se-in, who had also participated in the trial, was beaten badly too. And Provost Marshal Commander Song Hyo-sun was a classmate who drank every day and suffered. When I asked, he wouldn't say anything. But what I heard was that the military police were taking people to caves in groups of dozens and beating them. He seemed tormented by that. He is a genuinely good person. In the end he found a way to leave Jeju in the middle of his tour. And Operations Staff Officer Han Yŏng-ju was beaten by the regimental commander day and night.[^99]

Meanwhile, another U.S. military report shed light on the reasons for the troop replacement. Remarkably, the reason for the troop replacement was "training." About one week after the 9th Regiment reorganization was completed — two battalions of the Pusan 3rd Brigade having arrived in Jeju on July 21 and the 11th Regiment having departed on July 24 — Military Governor Dean informed Police Administration Bureau Director Cho Pyŏng-ok that he had "a policy of rotating the Constabulary regiments on Jeju Island for field training.

[^99]: Kim Chŏng-mu (金貞武, age 77, Puam-dong, Chongno-gu, Seoul City; then logistics staff officer of the 9th Regiment; retired as brigadier general; chairman of Military Academy 2nd Class alumni association; recorded September 25, 2002), testimony.

**[p. 236]**

One regiment will be stationed on Jeju Island for four to six weeks. Regimental training in the mountainous areas will always be conducted."[^100] It was a policy of using the Jeju Island situation for field training of the Constabulary.

### c. The August 25 Underground Elections and Armed Guerrilla Reorganization

#### 1) Elections for "South Korean Representatives"

From around mid-July 1948, "underground elections" were held throughout South Korea. This was connected to North Korea's establishment of a government. North Korea made maximum use of the "inter-Korean negotiations" to establish a justification for building its government. The First Joint Conference of All-Korean Political Parties and Social Organizations, aimed at establishing a unified government, was held in Pyongyang on April 19, 1948. Kim Ku and others also attended this conference. At this time a joint communiqué was announced stating: "Even if separate elections in South Korea are held, we will not recognize the results, and instead we will hold elections for a unified legislative body to enact a Korean constitution and establish a unified democratic government."[^101]

However, as the May 10 Elections were held in South Korea and the establishment of a government was imminent, the Second Joint Conference was held in Pyongyang from June 29 to July 5, 1948. The Second Joint Conference was attended by representatives of fifteen parties and organizations from the North, including the North Korean Labour Party, and representatives of over twenty parties and organizations from the South who had either remained in North Korea after the First Conference or had secretly crossed the 38th parallel again. However, Kim Ku and Kim Kyu-sik refused to attend, stating: "Having promised to prevent the division of the national territory and the splitting of the nation, for North Korea to now establish a separate government because a separate government has been established in the South amounts to an act of national division." The Second Joint Conference emphasized that the forthcoming "Democratic People's Republic of Korea" must be a unified government. Therefore, it was decided that elections would be conducted not only in North Korea but throughout South Korea as well. However, since openly holding elections in South Korea was impossible under the prevailing conditions, it was decided to hold "double elections" in South Korea. That is, representatives chosen five to seven per city and county (1,080 persons) would open a South Korean People's Representatives' Congress in Haeju from August 21 and on August 25

[^100]: "A Summary Report on the Jei-Ju Police," from Military Governor Major General William Dean to Korean National Police Director, July 30, 1948.
[^101]: Kim Nam-sik (金南植), *Namnodang Yŏn'gu* [Study of the South Korean Labour Party], Tolbegae, 1984, p. 335.

**[p. 237]**

Therefore, the 'underground election' that stirred all of South Korea at that time was actually the election to select 1,080 South Korean delegates to participate in the People's Delegate Assembly to be held in Haeju. A high-ranking SKLP official at the time testified as follows:

> First of all, during the day, even under U.S. military government, in areas where the left was strong at night, elections were conducted semi-openly. This mainly applied to remote villages in South Kyŏngsang, North Kyŏngsang, South Chŏlla, and Kangwŏn provinces. In these areas, villagers were assembled in one place at night, and first full-power delegates gave explanations about the election and introduced the candidates for people's representatives. Next, SKLP members or left-leaning figures in the area came forward to make speeches supporting the candidates, and voting immediately followed. However, in areas where semi-open voting was impossible, a petition sheet was circulated to collect signatures. The petition sheet, written on thin mimeograph paper, contained text supporting the adoption of the People's Constitution, the election of Supreme People's Assembly delegates, and the candidates from the relevant area. People signed and affixed their seals to the petition sheets, though many simply affixed their seals without signing. When assembling residents, they used the method of appealing through different pretexts to village leaders like those in charge of athletic fields and left-leaning collaborators while concealing the fact of the election. It was a surprise underground election. In areas where it was difficult to hold meetings, full-power delegates visited houses individually with the petition sheets, collecting signatures and seals in a rough-and-ready manner. When residents did not comply with the demands, they reportedly used intimidating methods such as threats and coercion.[^102]

However, the underground election in Jeju, where the 4·3 incident was occurring at that time, was conducted mainly in the form of writing names on blank paper or collecting thumbprints. It was not the kind of situation where residents could be assembled in one place as on the mainland, where full-power delegates could give explanations about the election or introduce 'people's delegate candidates.' Therefore, many ordinary people came to understand the 'blank thumbprint' not as participating in and voting in the underground election but as signing in support of those in the mountains. The underground election in Jeju was conducted widely targeting midmountain villages and rural areas. While many people who signed did so with apprehension, they could not refuse the demands of the armed guerrillas. So some reluctantly wrote false names and affixed their thumbprints.[^103]

An August 1948 U.S. military report recorded the Jeju situation as follows:

> On August 19, forty insurgents, including twenty armed guerrillas, invaded Sehwa-ri and forced residents to support the North Korean election and forcibly sign their names on blank ballot papers. The police attacked these insurgents and repulsed them, with no reported casualties on either side. According to the report, the insurgent group is forcing all residents throughout Jeju Island to sign blank papers and to provide money and provisions in support of the People's Army. Village residents were being forced by the insurgents to choose between two options: cooperating with the insurgents would at worst result in arrest by police and a few days of detention before release, while refusal to cooperate would result in death and arson of the house. On August 18, in Sŏgwi-myŏn, located in the central-southern region of Jeju Island, five village residents were stabbed and severely wounded by insurgents. The reason was that they had refused to sign a petition supporting the North Korean government and elections.[^104]

The police, in anticipation of the underground election, extended the curfew hours while again implementing the travel certificate system between Mok'po and Jeju, entering a state of emergency alert through the end of August.[^105] Jeju, which had been showing relative calm for a time, again descended into a situation with civilian casualties as a result of clashes between armed guerrillas attempting to promote the underground election and police trying to prevent it. A U.S. military report recorded: "On August 2, police engaged fifty insurgents, including twenty armed guerrillas, near Sŏgwang-ri. In this engagement, two insurgents were killed and one police officer was wounded. The police confiscated one grenade, three Japanese-made rifles, and sixty rounds of ammunition."[^106] According to a villager's testimony from this area, the firefight that day occurred when police officers from the Andŏk police box, having received information that the guerrillas were collecting 'blank thumbprints,' mobilized to respond.[^107] In the early morning of August 19, four police officers belonging to the Hallim police box engaged in gunfire at Hyŏpjae-ri in Hallim-myŏn, resulting in one police officer being killed.[^108]

In this way, Jeju residents did not understand the meaning of this election. There was not even a ballot paper. It took the form of affixing a thumbprint on blank paper, and they understood its purpose only to mean 'support for the armed guerrillas.' This is why most Jeju residents call this incident that occurred throughout Jeju in the summer of 1948 the 'blank thumbprint incident.' A witness testified as follows:

> In the summer, people came down from the mountains and had their seals taken. When we heard cricket sounds or other chirping noises, it was a signal that they had come from the mountains. When those people came and told us to affix our seals, we were so frightened that we all affixed our thumbprints. Houses where seals were affixed were marked with stones. They said we should affix our thumbprints in support of their organization.[^109]

In this manner, the 'blank thumbprint' was mostly done under coercion. Moreover, this blank thumbprint became a universal occurrence throughout South Korea, with millions participating nationwide. However, in Jeju, this act later became a pretext for tremendous loss of life. A jailer at a police station at that time described how a woman he came to know in the detention cell came to be shot:

> A maiden named Yi Ok (李玉) was locked in the detention cell and then shot by soldiers. When I asked what crime she had committed to be brought there, I was told she was detained for the crime of affixing her thumbprint to the blank sheet. She said that when shown the blank sheet, she was asked repeatedly to affix it, so she did. I had some connection with this maiden and tried to save her, but it was futile.[^110]

In this way, in Jeju, affixing one's thumbprint once became grounds for execution. Regarding this, a researcher of the SKLP pointed out: "If at that time the principle that people could be killed because of 'affixing a seal' was accepted, then by that logic millions of people could lose their lives because of this act on South Korean soil."[^111]

2) Kim Talsam's Defection to North Korea

The South Korean People's Delegate Assembly, which would be attended by 1,080 regional representatives from throughout South Korea, was scheduled to be held in Haeju on August 21. Because of this, from early August throughout South Korea, processions heading north proliferated. Though seventy-eight did not participate due to arrest en route or other reasons, all 1,002 who participated convened for the assembly on August 21. Among the 1,002, six were representatives from Jeju. Armed guerrilla leader Kim Talsam also left Jeju in August 1948 to participate in this assembly held in Haeju. While it is uncertain exactly when Kim Talsam departed, based on a U.S. military report stating "On August 2, five communists departed by ship for Mok'po. Presumably these individuals are heading to P'yongyang to participate in the North Korean elections,"[^112] it can be inferred that Kim Talsam departed on August 2 with four companions. Left-wing sources recorded that the Jeju people's representatives who participated in the Haeju conference were six in number: An Se-hun, Kim Talsam, Kang Kyu-ch'an, Yi Chŏng-suk, Ko Chin-hŭi, and Mun Tŭng-yong.[^113] Among these, 'Mun Tŭng-yong' is an unfamiliar name and is probably not an alias.

On August 21, the first day of the Haeju conference, there was a presidium election selecting thirty-five persons. Kim Talsam, in his mid-twenties, was selected as a presidium member alongside left-wing luminaries such as Hŏ Hŏn, Pak Hŏn-yŏng, and Hong Myŏng-hŭi.[^114] Subsequently, on August 25, there was the election of delegates to the Supreme People's Assembly, equivalent to the South Korean National Assembly. Concurrent with the total election selecting 212 North Korean delegates, the South Korean delegates, 360 in number, were selected from the People's Delegate Assembly. With 360 candidates announced, a formal process of affirmative and negative voting was conducted. At this time, Jeju Province's representatives An Se-hun, Kim Talsam, Kang Kyu-ch'an, Yi Chŏng-suk, and Ko Chin-hŭi were elected as delegates to the Supreme People's Assembly.[^115]

Prior to the August 25 Supreme People's Assembly delegate voting, Kim Talsam appeared as a discussant during the 'discussion period on candidates' and delivered a speech regarding the Jeju 4·3 incident. At this juncture, after first expressing support for Pak Hŏn-yŏng, Kim Talsam argued that regarding the causes of the armed uprising, resistance to repression and anger at the implementation of separate elections erupted into a spontaneous general uprising. Kim Talsam then provided lengthy explanations of the armed guerrillas' 'achievements' in boycotting the May 10 election, concluding his speech by exclaiming: "Long live the Democratic Chosŏn's complete independence! Long live the great Soviet Army, our homeland's liberation army, and its brilliant leader, Supreme Commander Stalin!"[^116]

While Kim Talsam's defection to the North, who had led the armed uprising, did not immediately change the fundamental character at the time of the Jeju 4·3 incident's outbreak, the fact that the armed guerrilla leadership supported the North Korean government as two antagonistic governments were established in the divided North and South meant that Jeju came to face even stronger government countermeasures. Meanwhile, as Kim Talsam, who had been the armed guerrillas' supreme leader, left Jeju, the armed guerrilla organization could not but be reorganized. As a result, Yi Tŏk-gu (李德九) succeeded Kim Talsam as the supreme commander of the armed guerrillas.

**[p. 238]**

On August 19, forty armed guerrillas including twenty armed rioters infiltrated Sehwa-ri and forced residents to support the North Korean elections, compelling them to sign blank voting slips by force. Police attacked these guerrillas and repelled them; no casualties were reported on either side. According to reports, guerrilla groups across all areas of Jeju Island were forcing residents to sign blank papers and demanding that they provide money and food in support of the People's Army. Village residents were being forced by the guerrillas to choose one of two options: those who cooperated with the guerrillas would at worst be arrested by police and released after a few days' detention, while those who refused to cooperate would be killed and their homes burned. On August 18, in Sŏgwi Township in the south-central part of Jeju Island, five village residents were severely stabbed by guerrillas. The reason was that they had refused to sign a petition supporting the North Korean government and elections.[^104]

The police, anticipating the underground elections, extended curfew hours and revived the travel certificate system on the Mokp'o–Jeju route, entering a state of emergency alert through the end of August.[^105] Jeju Island, which had been in a lull for some time, was drawn back into a situation of casualties as clashes occurred between the armed guerrillas seeking to advance the underground elections and the police seeking to prevent them. One U.S. military report recorded: "On August 2, police engaged some fifty guerrillas including twenty armed guerrillas near Sŏgwang-ri. In this engagement, two guerrillas were killed and one police officer was wounded. Police seized one grenade, three Japanese rifles, and sixty rounds of ammunition."[^106] According to testimony from a resident of that village, the engagement that day occurred when police from the Andŏk Substation received intelligence that the armed guerrillas were collecting blank-paper signatures and deployed.[^107] In the early morning of August 19, four police officers from the Hallim Substation engaged in a gunfight in Hyŏpchae-ri, Hallim Township, and one police officer was killed.[^108]

In this way, Jeju Island residents did not know the meaning of these elections. There were no ballot papers. It was in the form of pressing a thumbprint on blank paper, and most residents understood the purpose only to the extent of "supporting the armed guerrillas." Therefore, most Jeju Island residents call this affair — which occurred throughout Jeju Island in the summer of 1948 — the "blank-paper thumbprint incident" (*paekchi nallin sagŏn*). One witness testified as follows:

In the summer they came down from the mountains and took thumbprints. When the sound of cicadas or a "quacking" sound was heard, it was the signal that someone had come from the mountains. When those people came and told us to press our thumbprints, we were afraid and

[^104]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 920, August 20, 1948.
[^105]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, August 15, 1948.
[^106]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 907, August 10, 1948.
[^107]: Ko Po-hwa (高普和, age 68, resident of Sŏgwang-ri, Andŏk Township; recorded July 12, 2002), testimony.
[^108]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 923, August 28, 1948.

**[p. 239]**

everyone pressed their thumbprints. Houses that had pressed their thumbprints were marked with a stone. They told us to press our thumbprints to express support for their organization.[^109]

In this way, the "blank-paper thumbprint" was in most cases done under compulsion. And this blank-paper thumbprinting was a universal phenomenon throughout South Korea, with millions across the country participating. However, on Jeju Island this incident later became a pretext for enormous loss of life. A former jail guard at the police station described the process by which a woman he had come to know in the detention cell was subjected to execution by firing squad:

> A young woman named Yi Ok (李玉) was detained in the jail and then shot by soldiers. When I asked what crime she had been imprisoned for, she said she had been dragged in for the crime of pressing a thumbprint on a blank paper. She said some people had come carrying blank paper and kept telling her to press her thumbprint, so she did. I had a slight connection with this woman and tried to save her, but it was futile.[^110]

Thus in Jeju, pressing a thumbprint once became the cause of execution by firing squad. On this, a SKLP researcher pointed out: "If at that time the logic that someone could be killed 'for the thumbprint-pressing affair' held sway, this logic leaps to the proposition that hundreds of millions of people on South Korean soil could lose their lives for the same reason."[^111]

#### 2) Kim Tal-sam's Departure to the North

The South Korean People's Representatives' Congress, which was to be attended by 1,080 representatives from each region of South Korea, was scheduled to open in Haeju on August 21. For this reason, in early August people began crossing to North Korea in a stream from throughout South Korea. Although 78 people did not attend due to being arrested en route or for various other reasons, the congress opened on August 21 with all 1,002 persons present. Among the 1,002 were six representatives from Jeju Island.

Kim Tal-sam, the overall commander of the armed guerrillas, also left Jeju in August 1948 to attend this congress held in Haeju. It is not certain exactly when Kim Tal-sam departed, but from the U.S. military report that "on August 2, five communists departed by boat for Mokp'o — presumably they are on their way to Pyongyang to participate in the North Korean elections"[^112]

[^109]: Chang Su-jin (張守珍, age 80, resident of Hyŏpchae-ri, Hallim-ŭp; recorded December 12, 2001), testimony.
[^110]: Kim Si-hun (金時訓, age 79, Kasi-ri, P'yosŏn Township; then police officer of Sŏbuk Youth Corps origin; recorded February 26, 2002), testimony.
[^111]: Interview with Kim Nam-sik (金南植), *Cheju Sinmun*, December 5, 1989.

**[p. 240]**

— it can be inferred that Kim Tal-sam departed Jeju on August 2 with four companions. Left-wing materials list the names of six Jeju Island people's representatives who attended the Haeju Congress as An Se-hun, Kim Tal-sam, Kang Kyu-ch'an, Yi Chŏng-suk, Ko Jin-hŭi, and Mun Tŭng-yong.[^113] Among these, "Mun Tŭng-yong" is an unfamiliar name and is probably a pseudonym.

On August 21, the first day of the Haeju Congress, there was an election for a thirty-five-member presidium, and Kim Tal-sam — who was in his mid-twenties — was elected as a member of the presidium alongside such leftist heavyweights as Hŏ Hŏn, Pak Hŏn-yŏng, and Hong Myŏng-hŭi.[^114] Then on August 25, the election of Supreme People's Assembly deputies — equivalent to National Assembly members in South Korea — was held. At the same time as a general election choosing 212 North Korean deputies, 360 South Korean deputies were to be selected at the People's Representatives' Congress. With 360 candidates announced, it was a formality of going through a yes/no vote. At this time, Jeju Island representatives An Se-hun, Kim Tal-sam, Kang Kyu-ch'an, Yi Chŏng-suk, and Ko Jin-hŭi were elected as Supreme People's Assembly deputies.[^115]

Kim Tal-sam, during the "debate on candidates" that preceded the August 25 Supreme People's Assembly deputies vote, appeared as a speaker and also gave a speech about the Jeju 4·3 Incident. On this occasion Kim Tal-sam first expressed support for Pak Hŏn-yŏng, then argued in connection with the causes of the armed uprising that it was a spontaneous general uprising that had erupted as resistance to oppression and rage over the holding of separate elections. Kim Tal-sam then gave a lengthy account of the armed guerrillas' "record of achievements" in boycotting the May 10 Elections, before concluding his speech with the words: "Long live a completely self-reliant and independent democratic Korea! Long live the great Soviet Army — liberating force of our homeland — and its brilliant leader, Supreme Commander Marshal Stalin!"[^116]

Kim Tal-sam's departure to the North, as the leader of the armed uprising, does not immediately change the fundamental character of the Jeju 4·3 Incident when it broke out, but in the process of the establishment of two mutually hostile regimes in a divided South and North, the fact that the armed guerrilla leadership came out in support of the North Korean regime meant that Jeju Island now faced an even harder government stance.

Meanwhile, with Kim Tal-sam — the armed guerrillas' overall commander — departing Jeju Island, the armed guerrilla organization inevitably had to be reorganized. Accordingly, Yi Tŏk-ku (李德九) stepped forward as the overall command successor to Kim Tal-sam.

[^112]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 907, August 10, 1948.
[^113]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-ju, op. cit., p. 154.
[^114]: Kim Nam-sik, op. cit., p. 344.
[^115]: Kim Nam-sik, op. cit., pp. 530–531.
[^116]: *Namchosŏn Inmin Taep'yocha Taehoe Chungyobunkŏnjip* [Collection of Important Documents of the South Korean People's Representatives' Congress], Seoul: In'min Ch'ulp'ansa, 1948, pp. 102–109.

**[p. 241]**

3. Period of Collective Civilian Massacre (1948. 10. 11～1949. 3. 1)

가. Adoption of a Strong Suppression Policy

1) Domestic and International Political Circumstances

From mid-November 1948, large-scale military suppression operations unfolded. From mid-November 1948 to March 1949, over approximately four months, the suppression forces set fire to midmountain villages and carried out mass killings of residents. This was the most atrocious period in the development of the 4·3 incident. During this period, the greatest number of Jeju residents were killed, and most midmountain villages were burned, literally 'scorched earth.' The responsibility for this scorched-earth policy is attributed to the government and the U.S. Military Advisory Group in Korea at that time. Syngman Rhee, as president, held supreme military command authority; the U.S. military held operational control of the Korean military at that time. Therefore, in order to clarify why large-scale strong suppression operations occurred, it is necessary to examine the political situations in Korea and the United States. To find clues to these issues, based on domestic and foreign research results, a chronology was compiled as in the table below. Examining just the fragmentary chronological content first, 'mid-November 1948' was a period when division became fixed as governments were being established respectively in North and South. Even amidst this, unification efforts centered on Kim Ku and Kim Kyou-sik were still underway. Second, despite President Syngman Rhee's opposition, the National Assembly passed the 'Law for the Punishment of Pro-Japanese Collaborators,' causing major conflict among ruling circles, and when soldiers of the 14th Regiment in Yŏsu and the 6th Regiment in Taegu launched a mutiny, the domestic situation was very chaotic. The 'National Security Law' was also enacted during this period after much controversy. Third, as the U.S.-Soviet Cold War intensified, many disputes arose concerning the withdrawal of both armies. Some U.S. forces stationed in South Korea began to withdraw.

① Domestic Political Circumstances

□ Domestic and International Political Circumstances

**[p. 242]**

##### ① Domestic Political Situation
