**[p. 243]**

The circumstances of President Syngman Rhee following government establishment can be summed up as a 'political crisis.' Syngman Rhee's crisis had begun before government establishment. A conflict arose over presidential versus cabinet-responsible systems. Syngman Rhee pressed down the Democratic Korea Party, which sought to share power through a cabinet system, and was elected president on July 20, 1948. When the cabinet was announced on August 4 and the Democratic Korea Party was thoroughly excluded, the conflict between the two sides intensified. Prior to this, Yi Yun-yŏng (李允榮), whom Syngman Rhee had appointed as the first Prime Minister, was rejected by the National Assembly.[^1] The first trial Syngman Rhee faced in launching the new government originated from the Democratic Korea Party, which had been his political backing until then. What made Syngman Rhee's situation even more difficult was the enactment of the 'Law for the Punishment of Pro-Japanese Collaborators' (abbreviated 'Anti-Collaborator Law'). It was a historical imperative to bring collaborators—who had actively cooperated with Japan and driven the nation into war, and who had tortured and killed independence activists—to account and restore national moral integrity. However, after liberation, collaborators had reinvented themselves as 'anti-communists' and had already taken root throughout society in police and political circles. Syngman Rhee harbored collaborators. For Syngman Rhee, whose domestic political base was weak, collaborators were his greatest political backing. Attempts to punish collaborators had begun as early as July 20, 1947, during the military government period, when the Legislative Assembly passed a 'Special Law Regarding Pro-Japanese Collaborators, Traitors, War Criminals, and Other Wrongdoers.' However, Military Governor Dean refused to promulgate this law.[^2] However, as government establishment approached, the question of settling accounts with collaborators arose once more. Even amidst an atmosphere in which threatening letters were distributed in the National Assembly chamber stating 'Those arguing for severe punishment of collaborators are communists,' the Anti-Collaborator Law finally passed the National Assembly on September 1, 1948.[^3] Initially, Syngman Rhee avoided promulgating the law, but when it appeared that other bills would not pass in the National Assembly if he rejected this law, he reluctantly promulgated it on September 22. Accordingly, the 'Special Investigation Committee for Pro-Japanese Collaborators' (abbreviated 'Anti-Collaborator Committee') was established. However, for Syngman Rhee, losing his political base of collaborators at a moment when he had also broken with the Democratic Korea Party was something he could not tolerate in terms of his political survival. The counterattack by Syngman Rhee and pro-Japanese forces began immediately. On September 23, the day after the Anti-Collaborator Law was promulgated, an 'Anti-Communist National Mobilization Rally' opposing the law was held at the Seoul Athletic Stadium. The person who took the initiative in organizing the rally was Yi Chong-yŏng (李鍾滎). During the Japanese period, he had taken the lead in capturing and killing patriotic independence activists; after liberation, he had run the Taedong Daily and Korea Daily News while engaging in extreme rightist rhetoric—a typical 'pro-Japanese collaborator and anti-communist.' From the morning of that day, police encouraged residents' participation by saying "If you don't come to the athletic field today, you're a communist."[^4] Regarding this atmosphere, Congressman Kim Ung-jin (金雄鎭) criticized the 'Anti-Communist National Mobilization Rally' through National Assembly questioning of the government, saying: "The Jeju incident unfolding south of the 38th parallel, the assertion that all people residing on Jeju are communists—this such conspiracy to treat all innocent civilians there as criminals will, I believe, greatly disorder our future security situation."[^5] Subsequently, assassination plots against Anti-Collaborator Committee members by collaborationist police and government-organized rallies continued. Syngman Rhee even went so far as to become incensed on February 11, 1949, during a Cabinet Meeting upon learning that representative collaborator police officer No Tŏk-sul (盧德述) had been arrested by an Anti-Collaborator Committee investigator, and instructed the arrest and legal prosecution of the investigating committee members and their supervisors.[^6] On June 6, 1949, the Anti-Collaborator Committee was attacked by police and rapidly weakened. In the end, only seven people were prosecuted under the Anti-Collaborator Law, and all of them were released through sentence reduction or suspension of execution by spring of the following year, so the effort to cleanse collaborators achieved little.[^7]

**[p. 244]**

After government establishment, it was urgent for Syngman Rhee to suppress opposition forces and stabilize his regime, and securing legitimacy by obtaining UN recognition for his state was also a major task. For this, he desperately needed U.S. military and economic assistance. Furthermore, issues that had emerged as central political concerns—the collaborator question, the unification question, and the land reform question—were unavoidable issues. Among the core issues, the collaborator question and the unification question were especially vexing for Syngman Rhee. The unification debate was closely intertwined with the question of U.S. troop withdrawal. For Syngman Rhee, all of these were grave issues related to national legitimacy and state survival. The international status of the Republic of Korea remained uncertain, and whether the government could obtain UN approval during the General Assembly session was still unclear.[^8] Meanwhile, on September 15, 1948, Kim Ku and Kim Kyou-sik sent letters to the UN General Assembly meeting in Paris, France, demanding all-Korea general elections, further embarrassing Syngman Rhee.[^9]

On October 13, forty-seven representatives centered on younger progressives submitted an 'Urgent Motion for Withdrawal of Foreign Forces.'[^10] U.S. forces had already begun secretly withdrawing since September 15. Syngman Rhee believed the U.S. was neglecting its obligations under the Korea-U.S. Military Security Interim Agreement to support the organization, arming, and training of the South Korean military. Striving to delay the withdrawal of U.S. forces, Syngman Rhee requested on November 2 training and equipment provision for fifty thousand troops called the 'National Army.'[^11] However, the U.S. was not yet willing to even acknowledge the existence of the 'National Army.'[^12] Promised military and economic assistance was proceeding slowly. Moreover, U.S. policy at that time was to complete withdrawal of U.S. forces by the end of December, making Syngman Rhee increasingly anxious. Subsequently, Syngman Rhee was even so concerned about U.S. troop withdrawal that on May 27, 1949, he stated in a Cabinet Meeting: "Regarding U.S. troop withdrawal, there seems to be opinion even within the U.S. that it is inadvisable. So we should cultivate public opinion within the country, and arrange for the U.S. to fulfill its responsibilities and purposes by having them continue stationing forces until such time as Korea's defense capabilities can be assured."[^13]

When Syngman Rhee faced difficulties both domestically and internationally, the so-called 'Yŏsu Mutiny Incident' broke out. With the U.S. military's operational control, Yŏsu was recaptured on October 27, and the situation was concluded within eight days. For Syngman Rhee, the Yŏsu incident was a crisis, but on the other hand, it was an opportunity to crush his opposition in one stroke. To calm public sentiment, the situation should have been reported accurately or minimized; instead, the Syngman Rhee regime exaggerated it.[^14] Moreover, rumors circulated that the elder statesman Kim Ku was behind the Yŏsu incident. An Tu-hŭi, who recently died after assassinating Kim Ku, stated in his memoir: "Unable to bear indignation that the Master [Kim Ku] was involved in the Congressional Faction Incident and moreover in rumors concerning the Army's Crossing to the North incident and the Yŏsu-Sunchŏn Mutiny incidents,"[^15] thereby revealing his assassination motive. For Syngman Rhee, the Yŏsu incident was a 'fortunate opportunity' with which to suppress opposition forces and drive his greatest political enemy, Kim Ku, into a corner.

As Syngman Rhee had already demonstrated in the early liberation period, the ultimate issue he presented to overcome crisis was 'anti-communism.' The rightist Democratic Korea Party, which had broken with Syngman Rhee over power struggles, shared common interests with him on this point. A purge fever swept through the military. On November 2, the Taegu 6th Regiment launched a mutiny. However, this incident only accelerated the pace and intensity of the purge. The National Security Law, which passed the National Assembly on November 20 and was promulgated on December 1, was born precisely in this atmosphere. On the previous day, November 19, the National Assembly passed a resolution requesting the continued stationing of U.S. forces.[^16] The political arena rapidly shifted from a 'collaborator administration,' through an 'unification administration,' to an 'anti-communist administration.' The National Security Law encountered significant opposition from younger progressive representatives, with arguments such as 'this is a reversion of the Japanese colonial security maintenance law' and 'if this law is promulgated, there will be no one who can escape arrest.' However, such arguments were overwhelmed by logic such as 'while picking thorns one may sometimes pick one's finger; does that mean one should not pick thorns at all?'[^17] The National Security Law could be abused to eliminate political enemies, a concern that soon became reality through the so-called 'National Assembly Faction Incident.' The symbolic victims under this law were younger progressive representatives who had taken the lead in the enactment of the Anti-Collaborator Law and the activities of the Anti-Collaborator Committee and who had opposed the National Security Law.[^18] The 'National Assembly Faction Incident' was a situation that occurred alongside the police attack on the Anti-Collaborator Committee and the Kim Ku assassination incident—all occurring in June 1949. And at the end of June, U.S. forces withdrew.

②②

**[p. 245]**

② U.S. Policy Toward Korea

Following the conclusion of World War II, U.S. global strategy underwent a major turning point with the promulgation of the so-called 'Truman Doctrine' announced on March 12, 1947. The Truman Doctrine, which proclaimed the containment policy toward socialism, was intended to escape the crisis the U.S. faced in the rapid demobilization and dismantling of the war economy following the conclusion of World War II. With this began the 'U.S.-Soviet Cold War.' From this point, the question of U.S. troop withdrawal from Korea became the most important issue in U.S. policy toward Korea. It appears paradoxical that U.S. troop withdrawal was being pursued when the Cold War had just begun. This was because U.S. Soviet containment policy was concentrated in Europe, and the Korean Peninsula was a secondary region. However, soon a protracted controversy began between the U.S. State Department, which advocated prolonged U.S. military presence, and the military departments (Department of Defense, Army Department, etc.), which advocated early withdrawal.[^19]

The U.S. State Department emphasized the importance of Korea as an ideological battleground. In the U.S.-Soviet confrontation, abandoning the Korean Peninsula after defeat was a matter of prestige. Therefore, it advocated prolonged U.S. military presence. In contrast, the military departments valued the strategic importance of the Korean Peninsula lowly. Moreover, due to post-World War II demobilization and military budget cuts, they strongly argued that U.S. forces could no longer be stationed in Korea. The Far Eastern Command under General MacArthur in Japan also shared the military departments' opinion.

Amidst such controversy, the U.S. dilemma lay in accomplishing two objectives: establishing a 'communist barrier' in South Korea, and yet unavoidably withdrawing due to military constraints—or in other words, pursuing 'catching two rabbits at once.' Eventually, the result of the dispute between the U.S. State Department and the military departments over U.S. troop withdrawal was determined by the National Security Council (NSC) attended by the U.S. president. After much deliberation and compromise, 'NSC-8' and 'NSC-8/2,' serving as the final policy guidelines for U.S. policy toward Korea, were announced as a compromise between the two positions.

Examining in more detail the U.S. military's 'withdrawal schedule,' it is as follows:

△ April 8, 1948 = U.S. President approves 'NSC-8,' provisionally deciding to withdraw U.S. forces by the end of December 1948

△ September 15, 1948 = U.S. forces in Korea begin secret withdrawal

△ September 19, 1948 = Soviet Union announces plan to withdraw Soviet troops stationed in North Korea by year's end pursuant to UN resolution

△ November 12, 1948 = U.S. special envoy Muccio requests postponement of U.S. troop withdrawal, citing the Yŏsu incident as evidence that chaos in South Korea has reached its peak

△ December 12, 1948 = UN General Assembly approves the Republic of Korea and demands prompt withdrawal of both U.S. and Soviet forces

△ December 17, 1948 = U.S. State Department requests reconsideration of 'NSC-8,' which had decided to withdraw U.S. forces by the end of December

△ December 25, 1948 = Soviet Union announces complete withdrawal of troops from North Korea

△ March 23, 1949 = U.S. President approves 'NSC-8/2,' a new Korea policy guideline postponing U.S. troop withdrawal until the end of June 1949

△ June 29, 1949 = U.S. troop withdrawal completed, military advisory group of five hundred remains

Examining the above chronology, first, it is evident that internal chaos in South Korea affected the postponement of U.S. troop withdrawal. Furthermore, with respect to 'NSC-8,' which had decided withdrawal by the end of December 1948, and to this nation's

**[p. 246]**

situation, the Korean government repeatedly requested postponement.[^19]

**[p. 247]**

Korea as an ideological battleground. Yielding to the Soviet Union in the U.S.–Soviet confrontation and abandoning the Korean Peninsula was a matter of prestige. Consequently, it argued for long-term U.S. military stationing. The military, by contrast, assessed Korea's strategic value as low. It also strongly argued that force reductions and military cuts stemming from post-World War II demobilization made it impossible to maintain U.S. troops in Korea any longer. MacArthur's Far East Command in Japan also sided with the military's position.

Even amid this debate, America's dilemma lay in catching "two rabbits at once" — the task of building a "communist bulwark" in South Korea, while nonetheless having to withdraw unavoidably. In the end, the outcome of the debate between the U.S. State Department and the military over the withdrawal of U.S. forces from Korea was decided at the National Security Council (NSC), which the U.S. President attended. After extensive compromise, the final policy guidelines on Korea — NSC-8 and NSC-8/2 — were issued as compromise proposals between the two parties.

A more detailed look at the U.S. military "withdrawal timeline" is as follows:

- △ April 8, 1948 — U.S. President approves NSC-8, making a provisional decision to withdraw U.S. forces by the end of December 1948
- △ September 15, 1948 — U.S. forces in Korea begin secret withdrawal
- △ September 19, 1948 — The Soviet Union announces it will withdraw Soviet forces from North Korea by year's end in accordance with UN resolution
- △ November 12, 1948 — U.S. Special Envoy Muccio requests postponement of U.S. troop withdrawal, citing the extreme disorder in South Korea following the Yŏsu Incident
- △ December 12, 1948 — UN General Assembly approves the Republic of Korea government and requests the prompt withdrawal of both U.S. and Soviet forces
- △ December 17, 1948 — U.S. State Department requests reconsideration of NSC-8, which had set the December deadline for U.S. force withdrawal
- △ December 25, 1948 — The Soviet Union announces it has completely withdrawn troops from North Korea
- △ March 23, 1949 — U.S. President approves NSC-8/2, the Korea policy directive extending the U.S. troop withdrawal deadline to the end of June 1949
- △ June 29, 1949 — U.S. forces in Korea complete withdrawal, leaving behind a military advisory group

Examining the above timeline, one can first see that domestic disorder in South Korea influenced the postponement of U.S. troop withdrawal. Furthermore, it is apparent that the large-scale harsh suppression operations were launched at the very point when the State Department was raising objections to NSC-8, which had set the end of December 1948 as the withdrawal deadline.

[^19]: For the process of changes in U.S. policy toward Korea surrounding the withdrawal of U.S. forces from Korea, see Kim Ch'ŏl-bŏm, *Hankuk Chŏnjaeng kwa Miguk* [The Korean War and the United States] (P'yŏngmin-sa, 1990).

**[p. 248]**

The Defense Ministry's objection reopened the debate over the timing of withdrawal just as strong suppression operations were unfolding on Jeju Island. However, by November 1948, the core of the withdrawal debate within the United States centered on a single question: "Does the South Korean government possess sufficient self-reliance to serve as a bulwark against communism?" Unlike the military establishment's initial unconditional advocacy for withdrawal, it now needed to persuade the State Department and demonstrate the self-reliance of the South Korean government. Meanwhile, in a document prepared by the United States in advance of the United Nations General Assembly in Paris in December 1948, to counter Soviet offensives, one can see how great a burden the United States bore regarding the Jeju Island incident, which had resulted in a boycott of the May 10 elections. The United States compiled seven anticipated questions that the Soviet Union might raise at the UN General Assembly regarding the May 10 elections in South Korea and the August 25 elections in North Korea: ① The North Korean elections are the only elections held throughout the entire Korean peninsula, but they were driven underground by American and South Korean suppression; ② All geographic, economic, social, and political sectors of the people participated in the elections; ③ Unlike the South Korean elections, the North Korean elections were free from threats and bloodshed, whereas South Korea suffered two vacant National Assembly seats due to election boycotts on Jeju Island; ④ Most South Korean people did not support the May 10 elections, but only capitalists and pro-Japanese collaborators participated in this election that divided the nation to maintain their authority; ⑤ In North Korea, 97.97 percent of registered voters cast ballots, whereas in South Korea only 77.48 percent participated; ⑥ The highest people's assembly of North Korea included 360 representatives from South Korea; ⑦ All key positions in both North and South Korea were filled—this was the Soviet offensive the United States anticipated.[^20] In the end, on December 12, the United Nations General Assembly approved the Republic of Korea government, but in the meantime, the United States was deeply anxious about such Soviet offensives.

[^20]: Enclosures to Despatch No. 51 from Seoul, Korea, October 22, 1948.

**[p. 249]**

## 2) Command and Control by U.S. Military Forces

### ① The Korea-U.S. Military Security Provisional Agreement

On August 15, 1948, U.S. Military Government ended and the establishment of the Republic of Korea government was proclaimed, yet operational control of Korean forces remained with the U.S. military. This derived from the "Korea-U.S. Military Security Provisional Agreement" concluded on August 24, 1948, between President Syngman Rhee and U.S. Military Commander General John R. Hodge. The core content of the agreement was as follows:

Article 1: The Commander of U.S. Forces in Korea shall, in accordance with instructions from his home government and within his own authority, continue to organize, train, and arm the existing Republic of Korea National Defense Forces.

Article 2: (omitted) Until the completion of U.S. military withdrawal, the Commander of U.S. Forces in Korea shall retain authority to exercise overall operational control over the Republic of Korea National Defense Forces (including the Constabulary, the Coast Guard, and National Police dispatch units stationed in emergency areas) as he deems necessary for mutual security or to facilitate the organization, training, and equipment of the Republic of Korea National Defense Forces.[^21]

As the agreement demonstrates, its essential content was that the U.S. Military Commander would retain not only operational control of Korean forces but also dominion over bases and facilities necessary for the U.S. military presence until the U.S. military completely withdrew. Substantive authority over Korean forces continued to belong to the U.S. military even after the Republic of Korea government was established. Roberts, the chief of the advisory mission, made this fact clearly known to Defense Minister Yi Bŏm-sŏk. For this reason, Yi Bŏm-sŏk reported to the National Assembly on October 28, 1948, that even as Defense Minister, he could not arbitrarily direct military operations, stating:

> (excerpt) Although we say that military rule has been transferred, since this is not an army made by our own hands, it is not as easy as in other administrative sectors to make arrangements based on the necessity of ensuring mutual security, and we have not completely taken over equipment... (excerpt) I am the subject of military administration, and regarding the military organization law that has not yet been submitted, I know only about military. Just as with America, we are instructed up to a certain point... The Defense Minister does not arbitrarily direct operations concerning the names of units and matters of the military. (excerpt)[^22]

The Provisional Military Advisory Group (PMAG) emerged as the core element to command and control Korean forces. The PMAG was given legal standing on August 15, 1948, by General Order No. 31, and was officially established when the Korea-U.S. Military Security Provisional Agreement was concluded. Brigadier General William L. Roberts was appointed as head of the advisory mission.[^23] Roberts had been serving since May 20, 1948, as an advisor to the Directorate of National Defense under military rule before being newly appointed head of the Provisional Military Advisory Group.[^24] Captain F. V. Burgess was appointed as the advisor to Cheju.[^25] Roberts, a man in his mid-fifties at the time and a graduate of the U.S. Military Academy, was known to have served with valor through both world wars and, when the war ended, volunteered for service in Korea because he expected to be promoted.[^26] During his tenure as advisor to the Directorate of National Defense, Roberts was the one who selected Sŏng Yo-ch'an as deputy regimental commander of the 11th Regiment to be dispatched to Cheju when the garrison on Jeju Island was to be rotated from the 9th Regiment to the 11th Regiment. At that time, Roberts introduced Sŏng Yo-ch'an, who later as the 9th Regiment commander would conduct reckless strong suppression operations, as "a tough and courageous man" to Brown, the U.S. military commander stationed on Jeju.

Meanwhile, General Hodge, the U.S. military commander who had been overall commanding U.S. forces in Korea, left Korea on August 27, shortly after the Korea-U.S. Military Security Provisional Agreement was concluded, and General John B. Coulter arrived as U.S. Military Commander and Commander of the 24th Corps.[^27] As U.S. military withdrawal proceeded, after the 24th Corps under Coulter's command left for Japan on January 15, 1949, to disband, Roberts assumed the position of U.S. Military Commander in Korea as well.[^28] Roberts also assumed command authority over the 5th Combat Regiment formed during the withdrawal of the 24th Corps.[^29] The advisory mission grew from approximately 100 personnel around August 1948 to 241 by the end of 1948.[^30]

The influence of the Provisional Military Advisory Group over Korean forces was considerable. As apparent in the Korea-U.S. Military Security Provisional Agreement, Korean forces at that time were controlled by U.S. military advisors in all aspects—organization, training, armament, and even operations. Captain James H. Hausman, who served as advisor to the commander-in-chief of Korean forces among the PMAG personnel, testified in his memoir that from the establishment of the government, a Military Security Committee convened weekly with regular participation of six people: himself, President Yi, the Defense Minister, the Army Chief of Staff, Chief PMAG advisor Roberts, and others.[^31] The Yŏsu Incident symbolically demonstrated the role of the advisory group. Chief Roberts dispatched eight senior advisors, including Hausman and Colonel Fuller, to the scene, where they played a decisive role in suppressing the incident.[^32]

**[p. 250]**

### ② Activities of the U.S. Military Advisory Group

As mentioned earlier, after the establishment of the Republic of Korea government, operational control of Korean forces remained with the U.S. Military Commander in Korea. On September 29, 1948, Chief Roberts sent a letter to Prime Minister and Defense Minister Yi Bŏm-sŏk stating, "It is very important that the operational control of the Korean National Defense Constabulary remains with the U.S. Military Commander in Korea, and that all orders concerning the operations of the Constabulary must pass through the relevant U.S. advisor before being issued," thereby clarifying the source of operational control and its significance.[^33] This parallels the statement by Prime Minister and Defense Minister Yi Bŏm-sŏk in his National Assembly report on October 28, 1948, that he too could not arbitrarily direct military operations.

Operational control was not merely symbolic but highly concrete and direct. On October 9, Chief Roberts sent the following memorandum to Captain Treadwell, advisor to the 5th Brigade at Gwangju:

> Recent operations on Cheju Do emphasize defects that American advisors should have corrected beforehand. In the past, Cheju Do operations were normally controlled by headquarters of the Directorate of National Defense. However, at present, according to one unit commander, operations are entirely under Korean responsibility. It is believed that the support and execution of the currently planned operation, with minimum American interference, should be controlled by the Koreans in the proper chain of command. For this reason, it is desirable that all tactical and logistic support for current operations of the 9th Regiment be delegated to the 5th Brigade. As previously noted, the 5th Brigade has failed to provide adequate support, and accordingly, American advisors should take immediate corrective action through the Korean chain of command.[^34]

The above report can be summarized as: "Since defects have emerged in Cheju Do operations, take immediate corrective measures through the Korean chain of command (5th Brigade) while minimizing U.S. exposure." Prior to this, on October 6, Major Edgar A. Noel, commander of the 59th Military Government Detachment stationed on Cheju, sent a memorandum to Chief Roberts requesting "immediate shipment of radio equipment, weapons, and ammunition."[^35]

Such requests from the U.S. military unit commander on Cheju and the instructions sent by Chief Roberts to the U.S. advisor of the 5th Brigade in Gwangju were immediately realized. Specifically, on October 11, the Cheju Island Garrison Command was established under the command of Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm of the 5th Brigade in Gwangju. Chief Roberts received all situation reports regarding Cheju Island operations through Captain Burgess, the advisor dispatched to Cheju Island, and regularly reported these to the U.S. Military Commander in Korea. He was also directly involved in military operations by Prime Minister Yi Bŏm-sŏk and Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo. In particular, Chief Roberts focused on the CIA, 9th Regiment Commander Sŏng Yo-ch'an, and the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, utilizing them as cornerstones of the strong suppression operations. In his November 8, 1948, report to the U.S. Military Commander in Korea, Chief Roberts assessed that "the CIA is performing its duties excellently, and 9th Regiment Commander Colonel Sŏng Yo-ch'an is engaged in strong and active operations."[^36] In his November 15 report, Roberts again praised the CIA's activities, stating that "the plan is to recruit Korean military units, primarily from members of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps."[^37] In a December 18 letter to President Syngman Rhee, Defense Minister Yi Bŏm-sŏk, and Chief of Staff Chae Byŏng-dŏk, Roberts again praised Sŏng Yo-ch'an, who was conducting strong operations, stating: "Colonel Sŏng Yo-ch'an demonstrated great leadership in changing the initially hostile attitude of the islanders to a friendly and cooperative attitude," and recommended that "these facts should be widely publicized through newspapers, broadcasting, and presidential statements."[^38] In response to Roberts' recommendation, Chief of Staff Chae Byŏng-dŏk sent an immediate reply on December 21, saying, "I will have him introduced through statements, and I will recommend that the President issue a statement," thereby reciprocating Roberts' recommendation. Chae Byŏng-dŏk also promised in this reply that he would award Sŏng Yo-ch'an with a decoration.[^39]

While the specific nature of the "CIA's duties" is not confirmed, Chief Roberts praised the 9th Regiment Commander Sŏng Yo-ch'an, who was conducting indiscriminate strong suppression operations, or mobilized the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, whom the U.S. military itself recognized as "extremely anti-communist," in large numbers into the military, thereby encouraging harsh operations.

**[p. 251]**

Furthermore, on November 17, 1948, when President Syngman Rhee proclaimed martial law without even having a martial law statute, military commanders were confused about what to do, so on December 1, Chief Roberts sent a document to the Deputy Chief of Staff of the Defense Ministry regarding martial law, putting measures in place so that martial law could be implemented.[^40] Meanwhile, Chief Roberts appended the report content from Captain John P. Reed, an advisor, which stated: "Many innocent civilians have been killed on Cheju Island. Most of them were killed by guerrilla attacks, and some were undoubtedly killed by Korean forces," and reported it to Mr. Kenneth C. Royall, U.S. Secretary of the Army, on February 7, 1949.[^41] Although he partially admitted killings by Korean forces, the statement that "most were killed by guerrilla attacks" was actually a distortion of fact.

Meanwhile, while the U.S. military leadership at home was treating the withdrawal of U.S. forces as a fait accompli and proceeding with it, Chief Roberts confessed, "I do not know the answer to the question of whether U.S. forces should be withdrawn from here or not," but added, "However, if U.S. forces disappear, I believe that immediately North Korea will absorb or attack South Korea, or the opposite situation will develop," thereby opposing the withdrawal.[^42]

When the 24th Corps left for Japan on January 15, 1949, to disband, Roberts also assumed the position of U.S. Military Commander in Korea. On March 11, 1949, Roberts told Everett Drumright, Counselor of the U.S. Embassy, "I have had the CIA established and operated on Cheju Island."[^43]

Chief Roberts explained to Major General Charles L. Bolte, Chief of Operations and Plans of the Army, the reason for the existence of the advisory group after U.S. withdrawal, saying that "Korean military forces do not know the fact that they are shedding blood in place of Americans," as follows:

> I would like to point out that the results are gradually materializing as our advisors perform their duties faithfully and honestly. Korean soldiers are intelligent, but up to now they do not know the reason why we are working so hard without any selfish purpose. Because (as they see it) there is no selfish motive. They think we should obtain money, promotion, plunder, or something more. But since we are not obtaining anything more than that, they seem to be embarrassed. They do not know that we are carrying out our assigned duties. Korean soldiers do not know that the purpose of our training them is for them to bleed instead of Americans, and to shoot for America.[^44]

### ③ Dissemination of the "Mystery Vessel" Appearance Theory

The so-called "mystery vessel appearance theory" or "Soviet submarine appearance theory," which intensively emerged from around October 1948, functioned as an important justification for strong suppression operations. Such theories were first made known through U.S. military reports and were widely disseminated through domestic media. At that time, most domestic media expressed serious concern about fratricidal conflict and opposed resolving the situation through military force. Furthermore, statements made by central government officials who had inspected Jeju Island made the suppression authorities even more uncomfortable. Prosecutor Park Kŭn-yŏng said, "The cause originated from the wrong conduct of police toward Jeju islanders,"[^45] and Prosecutor General Yi In stated, "Bad conduct by corrupt officials is a major cause,"[^46] pointing out that "it is better to send even one capable person to rectify public sentiment than to send one hundred police officers."[^47] On July 18, twenty-two political parties and civic organizations gathered to establish the "Jeju Island Incident Response Committee," advocating for peaceful resolution of the Jeju crisis.[^48] After August 20, when some 800 reinforcement police were dispatched to Jeju, Korean media reported that "it has become common consensus that military methods could rather worsen the situation,"[^49] conveying the atmosphere of the time. On September 6, twelve political and civic groups issued a joint statement calling to "stop fratricidal conflict."[^50] Thus public opinion was concerned about large-scale bloodshed and argued that military force should be exercised with restraint.

**[p. 252]**

Although opposition to military suppression was strong, at a time when mutually hostile regimes had already been established in both North and South, news reporting "a mystery submarine flying a flag with a red background and single star" appeared was no less than shocking. Central media uniformly reported on October 8, 1948, that a submarine had been discovered in Jeju waters, and the source was a U.S. military report:

> On October 8, 1948, at 5:10 p.m., a U.S. military carrier aircraft pilot discovered one submarine at latitude 33°56' north and longitude 126°48' east. The submarine, which was flying a flag with a red background and a single star, was heading east at a speed of 10 to 12 knots. Although its identity could not be confirmed, the vessel was not American or British.[^51]

The urgent U.S. military intelligence report submitted on the day of the submarine's discovery did not confirm the submarine's nationality, but by mentioning "a flag with a red background and a single star," it hinted that it could be a Soviet or North Korean submarine. Days later, this event began to be reported by domestic media. Citing Navy General Officer Colonel Kim Sŏng-sam, the Dong-A Ilbo reported: "Around 9 a.m. on the 8th, a U.S. military aircraft discovered an unidentified submarine in the waters near Jeju Island, and at the same time on that day, a submarine appeared 8 miles north of Jeju Island, but its nationality is unclear."[^52] The story continued to grow, and in the next day's report, the "flag with a red background and a single star" had somehow become the "People's Republic flag." The U.S. military civil administrator in Cheju, whom the record identifies as "Noel" (assumed rank of Lieutenant Commander), stated:

> At approximately 11:50 a.m. on October 8, I was conducting patrol flights in a reconnaissance aircraft and discovered one foreign submarine in a floating state about 5 miles offshore in front of Sŏngsan. Also, at approximately 1 p.m. that same day, I discovered another submarine, again in that area about 20 miles offshore, heading toward Busan. It is reported that the identification number of the mystery submarine was "C-53," and the People's Republic flag was confirmed to be displayed on the ship's stern as observed from the aircraft.[^53]

From these records alone, it is difficult to determine conclusively whether the "mystery submarine appearance theory" was factual. However, what is notable is that the mystery vessel appearance theory occurred more than once at other times as well and regularly erupted at critical junctures in the development of the 4·3 Incident. The first mystery vessel appearance theory occurred on July 11, 1948. A U.S. military report urgently stated that "one North Korean vessel flying a red flag was seized by a coastal security vessel near Jeju Island."[^54] However, two days later, it issued a corrected report stating "the seized vessel was in the process of going to market to sell brass,"[^55] ending the incident as merely a misunderstanding. However, more than a month later, on August 17, another mystery vessel appearance theory emerged. This time, the mystery vessel's identity was confirmed as "a Soviet vessel." On the day of discovery, the U.S. military transmitted what it called a "CIC (Counter-Intelligence Corps) Urgent Report":

> At 4 a.m. on August 17, the coastal security guard patrol vessel "Gwangju," operating off the coast of Jeju Island, discovered a 500-ton-class Soviet vessel. The Soviet vessel ignored an order to stop and fired in the direction of the guard vessel with a machine gun mounted on the deck. No reported damage or casualties (F-6).[^56]

What is notable here is that while the U.S. military specifically mentioned "Soviet vessel" and "machine gun fire" in this report, it itself indicated the lowest reliability level, "F-6," for its credibility. Although this was intelligence-level reporting from the coastal security force, days later, the media reported the same content as follows:

> At approximately 4 a.m. on the 17th, a coastal security guard patrol vessel on duty patrolling the waters off Biyang Island in Haemdŭm-myŏn discovered a mystery vessel of unknown identity in nearby waters and immediately ordered it to stop; instead, the mystery vessel threatened with machine guns and attempted to charge at the patrol vessel, then fled northward. The patrol vessel immediately contacted headquarters, which notified the U.S. military side, and from the U.S. military side, there was contact with Kimpo Air Base and emergency measures were taken. The U.S. military aircraft that hurriedly scrambled appear to have searched the nearby waters for a long time, but subsequent news is unclear. According to investigation, the aforementioned mystery vessel was a speedboat flying a flag with two colors—blue and white—with two red stars on the white.[^57]

Thus the mystery vessel appearance theory originated from U.S. military reports and was widely disseminated through media reporting. Some media pointed out that the mystery vessel appearance theory was a rumor, noting: "After the incident, the U.S. military lent several aircraft to the security forces to disseminate leaflets, and since the world's foremost U.S. Navy guards both sea and air, external supplies are impossible except for the authorities managing public order to admit,"[^58] but reports that North Korean or Soviet mystery vessels had appeared served as justification for strong suppression operations. Specifically, on August 25, 1948, shortly after the mystery vessel appearance theory emerged, the Jeju Emergency Security Command predicted that there would be "the greatest suppression campaign to bring about a complete resolution of the situation in a short time" and cited four reasons for such strong measures, with "the appearance of a mystery vessel in the waters near Jeju Island"[^59] as the first reason.

**[p. 253]**

The aforementioned "submarine appearance theory of October 8" erupted again two months later in this atmosphere, occurring just four days before the Cheju Island Garrison Command (Commander Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm) was established on October 11. Not only mystery vessels but even mystery fighter aircraft appearance theories emerged. On November 17, 1948, the day martial law was proclaimed on Jeju Island, a U.S. military report stated:

> Six unidentified fighter aircraft in formation and one bomber with four engines were observed over Cheju Island. The fighters broke formation at an altitude of 10,000 feet and flew off in different directions. A South Korean National Defense Constabulary pilot took a communication aircraft and deserted, heading toward North Korea.[^60]

Ten days later, the U.S. military corrected its report, identifying the unidentified fighter aircraft as "U.S. military aircraft,"[^61] preventing further escalation, but the theory of Soviet and North Korean vessel appearance continued thereafter. On January 4, 1949, Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn, commander of the 2nd Regiment stationed on Cheju, requested the garrison command to withdraw the lifting of martial law because it might hinder operational execution. In doing so, Ham Byŏng-sŏn appended the following content to justify the reinstatement of martial law:

> Two 3,000-ton-class vessels bearing Soviet markings were discovered at approximately 5 miles from Samyang-ri on Jeju Island by Korean Coast Guard personnel on January 3 at 2 p.m. The coast guard patrol vessel pursued them, but since they were in international waters, they could not be stopped. The unconfirmed vessel was last sighted on January 4 at 2:30 p.m., heading toward Busan. <Commentary> Korean forces claimed that the vessel was carrying supplies from the North to support Jeju Island guerrillas. This view has been expressed before by Korean forces, but it is unverified.[^62]

Despite the U.S. military's assessment that it was "unverified," when the Dong-A Ilbo reported the same content the next day,[^63] it was transformed into public opinion. Meanwhile, as the Soviet submarine appearance theory did not cease, the Soviet Union issued the following rebuttal:

> [Moscow, the 19th, AFP Combined] The Soviet naval organ Leningrad stated on the 18th that there was no case of Soviet submarines appearing in the waters near Jeju Island, reporting as follows: "Recent statements by South Korean government spokesmen are false, and the South Korean government could not avoid making such claims in order to maintain martial law proclaimed two months ago and scheduled to be lifted last December, on Cheju Island. The U.S. military still maintains troops in South Korea, which is consistent with the plans of American monopoly capitalists regarding Korea."[^64]

The Soviet vessel appearance theory continued again in March 1949, coinciding with the establishment of the Cheju District Combat Command (Commander Colonel Yu Chae-hŭng), the final suppression operation. On March 10, 1949, Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk and Interior Minister Shin Sŏng-mo, who undertook inspection on Cheju, emphasized final suppression, reporting that "Soviet vessels have appeared several times, and North Korean vessels are showing up and exchanging light signals with bandits on Hallasan."[^65]

Thus the "mystery vessel appearance theory" erupted just before the launch of strong suppression operations and actually served as a pretext for large-scale bloodshed. However, the U.S. military, while acknowledging that the continuing mystery vessel appearance theories were based on uncertain intelligence or rumors, only issued the following corrective and confirmatory report around April 1949, when the armed guerrillas were mostly annihilated and the incident was nearly concluded:

> Although there are rumors that guerrillas are receiving logistic support from the mainland or North Korea, there is no evidence to prove these reports. The continuous patrol of the Korean navy, aerial reconnaissance, and the tight defense of coastal villages by police preclude the possibility of outside support.[^66]

It appears that now that the armed guerrillas were nearly annihilated and the incident was winding down, the U.S. military judged that further "mystery vessel appearance theories" were unnecessary. Supporting the U.S. position, Navy Chief of Staff General Sŏn Wŏn-il met with journalists on April 14, 1949, stating: "The rumor that foreign vessels are appearing in the waters near Jeju Island is nothing but communist propagandists' deceptive propaganda claiming that Soviet vessels are assisting us. Although there are Soviet vessels operating more than 4 miles offshore from Jeju Island, this is likely because Soviet vessels are following the internationally recognized sea lanes heading toward Vladivostok."[^67] In particular, the commander of the Navy's 3rd Special Operations Group, who was in charge of the naval blockade of Jeju, said: "Although there are rumors of Soviet submarine appearance in the waters, Jeju Island experiences large waves daily, so our small vessels only see mirages, and the general public mistakes these for rumors and spreads them, which affects the rectification of public sentiment."[^68]

Thus the mystery vessel appearance theory that continued to emerge from the Jeju region was revealed to be "baseless rumor" only near the end of the incident, but it served as an important pretext for strong suppression operations at the time, which raises suspicions of manipulation to suppress opposition to strong operations. The fact that the Cheju Island Garrison Command was established on October 11, just three days after the October 8 mystery vessel appearance theory, further supports such suspicions.

**[p. 254]**

### 3) Establishment of the Cheju Island Garrison Command

#### ① Strengthening of Military and Police Forces

As September 1948 began, signs of the situation worsening appeared, breaking a period of relative calm that had lasted until then. Already, on August 29, some 800 reinforcement police drawn from each provincial police precinct throughout the nation had arrived on Jeju, and again a large-scale reinforcement police contingent was dispatched. During this time, the armed guerrillas delayed their offensive as key leadership figures such as Kim Tal-sam left Jeju to participate in the "August 25 underground elections" and the Assembly of South Korean People's Representatives held at Haech'ŏn. The suppression forces also delayed suppression operations due to the rainy season and with the government establishment imminent, thus maintaining a period of relative calm. Fears that the situation would again worsen were concretized in early September through Kim Pong-ho, the chief of the Jeju Police Inspection Bureau:

> [From Jeju - Chosŏn] According to what Chief Kim Pong-ho, who recently traveled to Seoul to attend a chief conference and has now returned to his post at the Jeju Police Inspection Bureau, said: Currently, for the settlement of the Jeju Island situation, reinforcement forces of XXXX are en route to the island. This is not simple reinforcement as previously seen, but is intended to bring complete resolution of the situation in a short time, and when he traveled to Seoul, this had already been planned by central authorities including Military Governor Dean. In the initial stages of the incident, it was planned to gradually settle the matter with 800 police forces increased from the original 500 police from Seoul's Metropolitan Police Bureau, but because the situation rapidly deteriorated, reinforcement units were dispatched repeatedly. The situation subsequently maintained a period of relative calm due to this, but recently shows signs of deteriorating again, so such measures are being taken as described above.[^69]

Chief Kim Pong-ho went on to point to △the appearance of mystery vessels in the waters near Jeju Island △the re-emergence of armed guerrillas △the killing of the Hallim Police Box commander △the fact that armed guerrilla leader Kim Tal-sam attended the Assembly of South Korean People's Representatives held at Haech'ŏn and reported on the Jeju situation—as objective circumstances, stating that based on such "objective conditions," the central authorities had taken strong measures regarding the Jeju Island incident.

However, as examined earlier, the "mystery vessel appearance theory" was based on intelligence of unclear provenance. The re-emergence of armed guerrillas and the killing of the Hallim Police Box commander were not special incidents compared to previous circumstances. That armed guerrilla leaders such as Kim Tal-sam left Jeju to attend the Haech'ŏn assembly was actually a condition that could ease the situation. Moreover, following public opinion to resolve the situation peacefully, since the police inspection bureau chief had been replaced with someone from Jeju, a period of relative calm had continued and peaceful resolution was anticipated. Nevertheless, the news that reinforcement police were arriving in large numbers to "bring complete resolution of the situation in a short time" aroused both bewilderment and fear. One media outlet questioned the intentions of the suppression authorities, asking: "What then is this new cause of friction that makes kinfolk shed blood again? Can the 'objective conditions' mentioned above alone become the inevitable condition for fratricidal strife?"[^70]

The large-scale suppression operation targeting Jeju Island had been predetermined from the moment the South Korean independent government was established on August 15, 1948, as Chief Kim Pong-ho stated, that it "had already been planned by central authorities including Military Governor Dean." One scholar analyzed the strong suppression by the U.S. military and government that developed right after the establishment of the South Korean government as follows:

> From now on, any internal resistance within one side was perceived not simply as resistance to the government that was its object, but as a challenge linked with the other side intended to bring about its collapse. In the case of South Korea, this applied to both the United States, which had brought the regime into existence, and the government. Moreover, the emergence of two mutually hostile nations provided those seeking to suppress such resistance with an important ideological tool—no matter how justified the resistance (for example, resistance to corruption, corruption, repression, and exploitation), all such resistance could be attacked as collapsed conspiracy connected with the hostile other side. It was undoubtedly the most useful resource the suppression forces could mobilize, apart from the legitimacy of the suppression forces themselves or matters of right and wrong. (excerpt) Suppression on Jeju Island could be justified for the U.S. military and government forces as responding to North Korea's challenge and eliminating communists linked with the Soviet Union and North Korea, and such justification developed into the logic that harsh methods could be used in suppression and would be justified.[^71]

Now for the South Korean government, the Jeju Island situation was not a simple local issue but a powerful challenge to the legitimacy of the regime, and for the United States facing withdrawal of its military forces, it had emerged as the greatest obstacle to the world order it sought to establish in the early Cold War. In fact, ominous signs began to appear beginning in late August, right after the establishment of the South Korean independent government. Central authorities established a sweeping elimination policy and continually dispatched reinforcement units to Jeju Island. Following the proclamation by the Emergency Security Command on August 25 stating that there would be "the greatest suppression campaign,"[^72] on August 28, 800 police officers from the Seoul Metropolitan Police Bureau set out from Seoul Station for Jeju.[^73]

**[p. 255]**

At that point, Jeju Police Inspection Bureau Chief Kim Pong-ho, who had traveled to Seoul for a police chief conference and returned, conveyed news from the central authorities that "several thousand armed reinforcement troops are en route to the island, and a decisive suppression campaign will ensue."[^74] On August 30, Colonel O Dŏk-jun, Chief of Staff of the 5th Brigade in Gwangju, which commanded the 9th Regiment on Jeju, went to "inspect the island to promptly resolve the situation" in his capacity as acting brigade commander.[^75] The period of relative calm up to that point was a preparation period for the large-scale suppression operations to come and was like the calm before a storm.

Strong suppression operations became more concrete through the sudden replacement of the Cheju Police Inspection Bureau Chief and the establishment of the Cheju Island Garrison Command, both implemented beginning in October. On October 5, the central government removed Police Chief Kim Pong-ho, who was from Jeju and had been pursuing moderate policies, and appointed Hong Sun-bong, Chief of the Police Administration Bureau's Public Security Division, from P'yŏngnam Province, as his successor.[^76] This meant that the central government's policy of deploying key officials from Jeju to rectify public sentiment had been abandoned. To such a degree did Police Chief Kim Pong-ho's moderate approach play a major role in rectifying public sentiment that Albert Philipson, the chief of the U.S. Information Service on Jeju Island, stated: "The root cause of the Jeju Island issue is the brutality of corrupt police. However, after Chief Kim Pong-ho took office, there were no such incidents."[^77] His replacement was a symbolic personnel decision indicating that a strong policy would be adopted.

Following Hong Sun-bong's appointment, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, which had been a source of extreme conflict for Jeju Islanders, also arrived in large numbers. Supporting the Sŏbuk Youth Corps and adopting a hard-line policy, Chief Hong, following his appointment on October 6, would stand at the center of the greatest bloodshed until July 28, 1949, ten months later.[^78]

The Constabulary General Headquarters ordered the establishment of the Cheju Island Garrison Command on October 11. Since June 1, the 9th Regiment on Jeju Island, which had previously been a unit under the 3rd Brigade in Busan, had been transferred to the 5th Brigade in Gwangju,[^79] and thus Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm, commander of the 5th Brigade in Gwangju, was appointed commander. Colonel Sŏng Yo-ch'an, commander of the 9th Regiment, was appointed deputy commander. The grade of the suppression commander was elevated from regiment level to brigade level. Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm was an elderly officer of 65 at the time, who had served as major in the Russian Army and colonel in the Polish Army in the 1920s, and after liberation, served as commander of the Transportation Police School (rank of Commissioner) and Chief of the Constabulary General Staff.[^80]

Jeju Island Garrison Commander Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm was granted authority to exercise unified command over the 9th Regiment, one battalion of the 5th Regiment from Busan, one battalion of the 6th Regiment from Daegu, naval vessels (under the command of Navy Captain Choe Yong-nam), and the Jeju Police Bureau (under Police Chief Hong Sun-bong). The Constabulary General Headquarters also ordered that one battalion of the 14th Regiment stationed in Yŏsu, a unit under the 5th Brigade, be dispatched to Cheju Island.[^81] It was not merely military personnel that was augmented. More fundamentally regarding the large-scale bloodshed that would follow, the change lay in "the utilization of personnel." The daily report of August 5 prepared by the U.S. military advisor of the 9th Regiment noted: "S-2 is being reorganized. S-2 personnel from Jeju Island origins have been transferred to the 1st Battalion. Communications between police, Constabulary, and CIC regarding intelligence operations have improved."[^82] The daily report of August 12 noted: "Personnel of Jeju Island origin have been transferred out of S-3."[^83] Jeju Islanders were thoroughly excluded from the intelligence section (S-2) and operations section (S-3), the most important divisions for suppression. This measure, taken under the assumption that Jeju Islanders with intimate local knowledge and kinship relationships with residents would be unable to conduct strong suppression operations, presaged the mass killing that would develop into a large-scale suppression campaign.

**[p. 256]**

Meanwhile, the suppression authorities had previously cut off communications between Jeju and the mainland. The travel permits system, which had been abolished on July 5, was revived from August 13, making it difficult even to board ships to Mokpo.[^84] Indeed, the twenty-two political parties and civic organizations that gathered to establish the "Jeju Island Incident Response Committee" to pursue peaceful resolution of the Jeju situation dispatched a "local investigation team to Jeju Island," but after arriving at Mokpo on August 28, they were ultimately denied boarding and their trip to Jeju was thwarted.[^85] Jeju Island was becoming increasingly isolated and abandoned.

#### ② Proclamation of a Proclamation

On October 17, 1948, just six days after establishing the Cheju Island Garrison Command, 9th Regiment Commander Colonel Sŏng Yo-ch'an issued the following proclamation:

> Regarding certain undesirable elements who have destroyed public order on this island, threatened the peaceful livelihood of the people, and attempted to violate national sovereignty, the military, guided by the highest directives of the government, will take resolute and thorough action against such treacherous conduct in order to maintain peace on this island and accomplish the great undertaking of national glory and security, requesting the active and sacrificial cooperation of all islanders.

> To annihilate treacherous extremists who are hiding throughout the Hallasan area and committing atrocities worthy of universal condemnation, the military proclaims that from October 20 onward, during the period of military operations, passage without authorization beyond 5 km from the coastline and in mountainous areas is prohibited. Any person who violates this proclamation, regardless of the reason, shall be deemed an insurgent and executed by shooting. However, for those who require passage through mountainous areas for special purposes, a special travel permit issued by the military will be provided, guaranteeing their safety.[^86]

This proclamation was a signal that the suppression authorities, having reorganized their internal structure through the October 5 police chief replacement and October 11 establishment of the Cheju Island Garrison Command, were finally launching full-scale strong suppression operations. It was publicly announced as a strong suppression policy and received media attention. However, this proclamation contained important passages that must be carefully examined.

First, the "region beyond 5 km from the coastline" established as the suppression target based on Jeju Island's geography was not limited to mountainous areas such as Hallasan but included all mid-elevation villages except the coastal areas. Thus the prohibition on passage for residents in mid-elevation villages was effectively equivalent to prohibiting residence itself. The proclamation stated that anyone appearing in mid-elevation villages would be "deemed an insurgent and executed by shooting" regardless of reason, raising the question of how such immediate summary judgment could be executed even in wartime conditions. Regarding this matter, U.S. military advisor Fishgrund testified: "The 9th Regiment considered anyone inland toward Hallasan to be an enemy."[^87]

What is particularly noteworthy is the passage stating that this proclamation was issued according to the government's "highest directive." While one can point to the President or military leadership as the organ ordinarily capable of issuing "highest directives" to the military, the specific issuer of the "highest directive" has not been confirmed. However, the U.S. military's strong suppression policy had already taken shape in the early stages of the 4·3 Incident.

**[p. 257]**

General Kim Ik-ryŏl, who became the 9th Regiment commander right after the 4·3 Incident and pursued peaceful negotiations with the armed guerrillas, revealed in his memoirs: "The political counselor to Military Governor Dean General emphasized that the only way to rapidly resolve the Jeju Island insurrection was a scorched-earth operation," and said that when he refused, he was offered arrangements for a trip to America and $100,000.[^88] Yi Yun-nak, who was the intelligence officer of the 9th Regiment during the tenure of Colonel Kim Ik-ryŏl, also testified: "A CIC major ordered Colonel Kim Ik-ryŏl and me to consider areas beyond 5 km from the coastline as enemy territory and suppress them."[^89] The operation that a U.S. CIC officer had proposed to Colonel Kim Ik-ryŏl in May that year manifested in actual form five months later.

The Jeju Island coast was immediately sealed off the day after the proclamation was issued, on October 18. The navy deployed seven vessels and 203 sailors to blockade the Jeju coast.[^90] Additionally, one battalion of the 14th Regiment stationed in Yŏsu was scheduled to be dispatched to Jeju Island. However, on October 19, the 14th Regiment, which was scheduled to be sent to Cheju, turned its guns on its comrades and rebelled, causing strong operations to escalate further. Some claim that the authorities conducted strong suppression operations in response to the crisis caused by the "Yŏsu Incident," but the policy of strong suppression operations had already been determined through the proclamation of 9th Regiment Commander Sŏng Yo-ch'an on October 17. The Yŏsu Incident was not so much the cause of strong suppression operations as an impetus for accelerating them. Meanwhile, Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm, who was serving as commander of the 5th Brigade and concurrently as commander of the Cheju Island Garrison Command, found himself just eight days after his appointment as commander dealing with the rebellion of the 14th Regiment stationed in Yŏsu, a unit under the 5th Brigade.

**[p. 258]**

**[p. 259]**

Thus the "ghost vessel appearances" consistently preceded the launching of harsh suppression operations and actually served as pretexts for large-scale bloodshed. Yet even as the U.S. military acknowledged that the continuing ghost vessel appearances were based on unreliable intelligence or rumors, it was not until around April 1949 — when the armed guerrillas had been largely annihilated and the incident was nearly concluded — that it finally issued the following confirming correction:

> There are rumors that the guerrillas are receiving logistical support from the mainland or from North Korea, but there is no evidence to substantiate these reports. Continuous patrols by Korean naval vessels, air patrols, and the tight police defense of coastal villages are precluding the possibility of outside support.[^66]

It appears that by the time the armed guerrillas had been nearly eliminated and the incident was winding down, the "ghost vessel appearances" were no longer deemed necessary. In a manner that supported the U.S. military's position, Navy Chief of Staff Rear Admiral Son Wŏn-il (孫元一) told reporters on April 14, 1949: "The rumor that foreign vessels are appearing in the waters around Jeju Island amounts to nothing more than communist agitation propaganda claiming that Soviet vessels are assisting us. While Soviet vessels navigate past waters four miles off the Jeju coast, this is because Soviet ships are using internationally recognized shipping routes heading toward Vladivostok."[^67] In particular, the commander of the Naval 3rd Special Task Force, responsible for the Jeju coastal blockade, stated: "There are rumors of Soviet submarine appearances in the area, but since Jeju Island has high waves every day and our small task force can only see up to the mast-tops, people are confusing this and spreading groundless rumors, which is having an effect on public order."[^68]

Thus the ghost vessel appearances that kept erupting in the Jeju area were only revealed as "groundless rumors" as the incident was nearly concluded — but at the time they served as important pretexts for harsh suppression operations. This fact raises the suspicion that they were fabricated to neutralize opposition opinion against harsh operations. That the Jeju Island Garrison Command was established on October 11, just after the ghost vessel appearance on October 8, adds further to these suspicions.

[^65]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, March 17, 1949; *Sŏul Sinmun*, March 17, 1949.
[^66]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1097, April 1, 1949.
[^67]: *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, April 15, 1949.
[^68]: *Kyŏnghyang Sinmun*, May 17, 1949.

**[p. 260]**

#### 3) Establishment of the Jeju Island Garrison Command

##### ① Military and Police Force Buildup

As September 1948 arrived, signs began emerging that the situation would worsen, breaking the lull that had continued for a while. Following the arrival on Jeju on August 29 of some 800 reinforcement police drawn from each police district across the country,[^69] large numbers of additional reinforcement police were dispatched yet again. During this time, the armed guerrillas had slowed their offensive as key leadership including Kim Tal-sam had left Jeju to prepare for the August 25 underground elections and to attend the South Korean People's Representatives' Congress held in Haeju; the suppression forces had also delayed operations because of the imminent establishment of the government and the rainy season. The concern that the situation would worsen again became concrete in early September through Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner Kim Pong-ho:

> [Dispatch from Jeju — Chosŏn:] According to Police Surveillance Commissioner Kim Pong-ho, who has returned to his post after traveling to Seoul to attend the police superintendents' conference, a force of ○○○○ men of reinforcements is currently en route to the island. This is not a mere reinforcement of the kind seen previously, but is intended to bring the situation to a definitive overall conclusion in a short period. At the time he went to Seoul, he said, this had already been planned by central authorities including Military Governor Dean. When the incident first broke out, the plan was for a gradual resolution using the existing 500 police supplemented by 300 additional officers — 800 in all — but as the situation deteriorated rapidly, reinforcements were repeatedly dispatched. The situation had been in a lull due to these measures, but recently there are signs it may worsen again, and therefore these measures are being taken.[^70]

Commissioner Kim Pong-ho then pointed to △ the fact that ghost vessels had appeared in Jeju waters, △ the re-emergence of the armed guerrillas, △ the killing of the Hallim substation chief, and △ the fact that armed guerrilla chief Kim Tal-sam had attended the South Korean People's Representatives' Congress in Haeju and reported on the Jeju situation — stating that in light of these "objective circumstances," the central authorities had decided to take hard-line measures regarding the Jeju Incident. However, as already noted, the "ghost vessel appearances" were based on intelligence of uncertain reliability. The re-emergence of the armed guerrillas and the Hallim substation chief killing were not exceptional events compared to the previous situation. The departure of guerrilla leadership including Kim Tal-sam for Jeju to attend the Haeju Congress was actually a condition favorable to easing the situation. Moreover, with police surveillance commissioner changed to a Jeju native in accordance with popular pressure to resolve the situation peacefully, a period of calm had continued and a peaceful resolution had been anticipated.

[^69]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, September 1, 1948.
[^70]: *Sŏul Sinmun*, September 3, 1948.

**[p. 261]**

Despite this, news that large numbers of armed reinforcement police were en route with the aim of bringing the situation to a "definitive overall conclusion in a short period" provoked both bewilderment and fear. Accordingly, one newspaper questioned the intentions of the suppression authorities in their rush toward a hard-line approach: "What can the new source of trouble requiring brothers to shed each other's blood again possibly be? Can the 'objective circumstances' mentioned above alone constitute a sufficient condition for the unavoidability of fratricidal conflict?"[^71]

The large-scale suppression operation on Jeju Island had been decided from the moment South Korea's separate government was established on August 15, 1948 — just as Commissioner Kim Pong-ho had stated: "this had already been planned by central authorities including Military Governor Dean." One scholar analyzed the U.S. and government hard-line suppression that unfolded immediately after the establishment of the South Korean government as follows:

> Now any resistance internal to one side took on meaning not merely as resistance to the government that was its object, but was perceived as a challenge linked to the other side aimed at destroying itself. In the South Korean case, this applied equally to the United States, which had launched that system, and to the government. Moreover, the emergence of two mutually hostile states provided an important ideological tool by which any resistance — however legitimate (for example, resistance to injustice, corruption, oppression, and exploitation) — could all be attacked as destruction conspiracies linked to the hostile counterpart. Whatever the legitimacy — or the rights and wrongs — of the suppressive forces, this was undoubtedly the most useful resource they could mobilize. (…) The suppression on Jeju Island could be justified to the U.S. and the government army as responding to North Korea's challenge and eliminating communists linked to the Soviet Union and North Korea, and such justification developed into a logic that any method, however brutal, used for suppression was legitimate.[^72]

The Jeju Island situation was now no longer a mere local problem for the South Korean government, but a powerful challenge to the government's legitimacy; for the United States, facing the withdrawal of U.S. forces from Korea, it had emerged as the greatest obstacle to the world order they were trying to establish in the early Cold War. Indeed, ominous signs had already begun appearing from late August, immediately after the South Korean separate government was established. Because the central authorities decided on an all-out sweep policy and dispatched reinforcements one after another to Jeju Island. On August 25, following the Jeju Emergency Garrison Command's[^73] proclamation of "the largest suppression operation ever,"[^74] on August

[^71]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, September 3, 1948.
[^72]: Pak Myŏng-rim (朴明林), "Cheju-do 4·3 minchung hangjaeng e kwanhan yŏn'gu" [Study on the Jeju 4·3 People's Uprising], M.A. thesis, Korea University, 1988, pp. 143–144.

**[p. 262]**

28, some 800 police officers from the Seoul Metropolitan Police District departed from Seoul Station bound for Jeju.[^75] Then Police Surveillance Commissioner Kim Pong-ho, who had returned to Jeju after attending the police superintendents' conference in Seoul, conveyed the news from the center that "several thousand armed reinforcements are en route and a decisive suppression battle is shaping up."[^76] On August 30, Lieutenant Colonel O Tŏk-jun (吳德峻), acting brigade commander in place of the 5th Brigade commander, traveled to Jeju for "inspection in order to resolve the situation quickly."[^77]

The period of calm that had continued was a preparation period for the imminent large-scale suppression operation — like the calm before a storm.

The hard-line suppression operation became more concrete with the abrupt replacement of the Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner on October 5 and the establishment of the Jeju Island Garrison Command on October 11. The central government had Commissioner Kim Pong-ho — a Jeju native who had pursued a moderate approach — resign effective October 5 and posted Hong Sun-bong (洪淳鳳), a native of South P'yŏngan Province and chief of the Police Administration Bureau's Public Security Section, as his successor.[^78]

This signaled a change from the central authorities' earlier policy of appointing Jeju natives to key positions to settle inflamed public opinion. Albert Philipson, director of the U.S. Information Service on Jeju Island, had said: "The cause of the Jeju Island disturbance was the brutality of malicious police officers. However, since Commissioner Kim Pong-ho took office, such things have ceased"[^79] — to such a degree that Commissioner Kim Pong-ho's moderate approach had played a major role in settling public opinion. His dismissal was therefore a symbolic personnel decision signaling the adoption of hard-line policies. On the heels of new Commissioner Hong Sun-bong, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps — an extreme source of conflict for Jeju residents — also arrived in large numbers. Commissioner Hong, who supported the Sŏbuk Youth Corps and adopted a hard-line policy, assumed his position on October 6 and would find himself at the center of the greatest bloodshed until July 28, 1949 — ten months later.[^80]

The Constabulary Headquarters ordered the establishment of the Jeju Island Garrison Command on October 11. Since June 1, the 9th Regiment stationed on Jeju had been placed under the Kwangju 5th Brigade rather than the Pusan 3rd Brigade.[^81]

[^73]: The "Cheju Emergency Garrison Command" (제주비상경비사령부) was a police organization organized within the Jeju Police Surveillance Office immediately after the 4·3 outbreak on April 5, 1948. In contrast, the similarly named "Jeju Island Garrison Command" (제주도경비사령부; Commander Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm, established October 11, 1948) and "Jeju Island District Combat Command" (제주도지구전투사령부; Commander Colonel Yu Chae-hŭng, established March 2, 1949) were military organizations.
[^74]: *Sŏul Sinmun*, September 3, 1948.
[^75]: *Tong-A Ilbo*, August 29, 1948.
[^76]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, September 3, 1948.
[^77]: *Tong-A Ilbo*, September 7, 1948.
[^78]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, October 6, 1948.
[^79]: *Sŏul Sinmun*, September 26, 1948.
[^80]: Cheju-do Kyŏngch'al-guk (濟州道警察局), *Cheju Kyŏngch'al-sa* [History of Jeju Police], 1990, p. 489.

**[p. 263]**

Accordingly, the garrison commander was Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm (金相謙), commander of the Kwangju-based 5th Brigade. Major Song Yo-ch'an (宋堯讚), the 9th Regiment commander, was appointed deputy garrison commander. The suppression command was thus elevated from regimental to brigade level. Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm was a seasoned veteran of sixty-five at the time who had served as a lieutenant colonel in the Russian Army and a colonel in the Polish Army in the 1920s, and after liberation had held diverse positions including Director of the Transportation Police School (chief superintendent) and Chief of Staff of the Constabulary.[^82]

Jeju Island Garrison Commander Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm was given authority to exercise integrated command over the 9th Regiment, one battalion of the 5th Regiment from Pusan, one battalion of the 6th Regiment from Taegu, a naval detachment (Navy Major Ch'oe Yong-nam's unit), and the Jeju Police Force (Jeju Police Surveillance Commissioner Hong Sun-bong). The Constabulary Headquarters also ordered one battalion of the 14th Regiment stationed in Yŏsu — a subordinate unit of the 5th Brigade — to be dispatched as reinforcements to Jeju Island.[^83]

It was not merely a reinforcement of troop numbers. The more fundamental background to the large-scale bloodshed that would follow lay in "the change in force utilization." A daily report dated August 5 prepared by the 9th Regiment's U.S. military adviser states: "S-2 is being reorganized. S-2 members of Jeju Island origin have been transferred to the 1st Battalion. Liaison between police, Constabulary, and CIC regarding intelligence operations has been improved."[^84] The August 12 entry of this daily report reads: "Members of Jeju Island origin have been transferred out of S-3."[^85] Jeju-origin soldiers were thoroughly excluded from the most important intelligence section (S-2) and operations section (S-3) during the suppression campaign. This measure — taken on the premise that soldiers of Jeju origin, who knew the local conditions well and were intertwined with residents through family and kinship ties, would be unable to carry out hard-line suppression operations — foreshadowed the large-scale massacre that would unfold.

Meanwhile, the suppression authorities had earlier cut off travel between Jeju and the mainland. The travel certificate system, which had already been abolished as of July 5, was revived from August 13, making it difficult even to board the regular ferry or aircraft to Mokp'o.[^86] Indeed, when the 22-party and social organization Jeju Incident Countermeasures Committee, formed to seek a peaceful resolution to the Jeju situation, dispatched a "Jeju Island on-site investigation team," the team arrived in Mokp'o on August 28 but was ultimately denied boarding at Mokp'o harbor and their journey to Jeju was blocked.[^87] Jeju Island was becoming increasingly isolated and without succor.

[^81]: Army Headquarters Military Inspection Office, *Yukkun Yoksa Ilji*, vol. 1, June 1, 1948.
[^82]: Han Yong-wŏn (韓鎔源), *Ch'anggŭn* [Army Founding], Pakyŏngsa, 1984, p. 97.
[^83]: Army Headquarters Military Inspection Office, *Yukkun Yoksa Ilji*, vol. 2, October 11, 1948.
[^84]: Charles L. Wesolowsky, Advisor 9th Regiment, Korean Constabulary, Cheju-Do, Daily Report, August 5, 1948.
[^85]: Ibid., August 12, 1948.
[^86]: *Tonggwang Sinmun*, August 25, 1948.
[^87]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, September 5, 1948.

**[p. 264]**

##### ② Proclamation of the Proclamation

Six days after establishing the Jeju Island Garrison Command — on October 17, 1948 — 9th Regiment Commander Major Song Yo-ch'an issued the following proclamation:
