> In order to maintain the peace of this island and carry out the great task of national glory and security by firmly striking the handful of impure elements who are destroying the public order of this island, threatening the peaceful settlement of law-abiding residents, and attempting to infringe upon national sovereignty, the Army, pursuant to the highest orders of the government, hereby proclaims: From October 20 onward, during the period of military operations, the unauthorized passage of persons at points more than 5 km from the coastal perimeter and in mountainous areas throughout the island is hereby prohibited. Any person who violates this proclamation shall, regardless of reason, be regarded as a member of the bandit force and shall be shot on the spot. However, persons who require passage through mountainous areas for special purposes shall be issued special passes by the Army upon petition, guaranteeing their safety.[^88]

This proclamation was a signal flare by which the suppression authorities — having reorganized their internal structure through the police surveillance commissioner change on October 5 and the establishment of the Jeju Island Garrison Command on October 11 — declared that they would now launch large-scale harsh suppression operations in earnest. As it publicly proclaimed a hard-line suppression policy, it attracted media attention.

However, this proclamation contains several critically important passages that must be addressed. First, given Jeju Island's terrain, the "points more than 5 km from the coastal perimeter" designated as the target suppression area are not limited to mountainous areas such as Halla Mountain — they encompass all mid-mountain villages[^89] where residents live, excluding only coastal areas. A curfew on movement in villages where residents live thus amounted to a prohibition on residence itself. The proclamation said that "anyone visible" in mid-mountain villages would be "regarded as a member of the bandit force, without regard to reason, and shot on the spot" — yet it is questionable how summary execution powers, difficult even to exercise in wartime, could have been invoked. In this connection, U.S. adviser Fischgrund testified: "The 9th Regiment regarded anyone in the interior facing toward Halla Mountain as the enemy."[^90]

[^88]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, October 20, 1948.
[^89]: Jeju's villages are divided into "coastal villages" and "mid-mountain villages" (*chunsangan maŭl*). In administrative terms, "mid-mountain" (*chunsangan*) is defined as "the area between the 200-meter and 600-meter contour lines" (*Special Act on Development of Jeju Island*, Article 2). However, the term "mid-mountain village" ordinarily refers to villages located approximately 5 km or more from the coast. Even villages located within 5 km of the coast are commonly called "mid-mountain villages" if they are situated inland from the coastal perimeter road.

**[p. 265]**

Particularly noteworthy is the passage stating that this proclamation was issued pursuant to the government's "highest orders." Ordinarily, agencies capable of issuing "highest orders" to the military can be identified as the President or the supreme military leadership — but the specific issuer of the "highest orders" cannot be confirmed.

Now, the U.S. military's hard-line suppression policy had already been established in its broad outlines at the very outset of the 4·3 outbreak. Former 9th Regiment Commander General Kim Il-ryŏl, who had pursued peace negotiations with the armed guerrillas immediately after the 4·3 outbreak, disclosed in his memoir that "the political adviser of Military Governor General Dean emphasized that the only way to quickly resolve the Jeju Island disturbance was scorched-earth operations," and that when he refused, "he was tempted with the offer to arrange his passage to the United States and give him $100,000."[^91] Yi Yun-nak, who had served as intelligence staff officer of the 9th Regiment under Commander Kim Il-ryŏl, also testified: "A CIC major ordered Commander Kim Il-ryŏl and me to treat the mid-mountain area more than 5 km from the coastal line as hostile territory and to suppress it."[^92] The operation that a U.S. CIC officer had proposed to Commander Kim Il-ryŏl in May of that year became a reality five months later.

The Jeju coast was immediately blockaded on October 18, the day after the proclamation was issued. The Navy mobilized seven vessels and 203 sailors to cut off the Jeju coast.[^93] Moreover, one battalion of the 14th Regiment garrisoned in Yŏsu was expected to be reinforced to Jeju Island. However, on October 19, the very day the 14th Regiment was to be dispatched to Jeju, it turned its rifles around and launched a rebellion — thus further escalating the harsh operations. While there are claims that the authorities launched harsh operations because of the sense of crisis induced by the Yŏsu Incident, the policy of harsh suppression operations had already been set through 9th Regiment Commander Song Yo-ch'an's proclamation on October 17, before that. The Yŏsu Incident was not so much the cause of the harsh suppression operations as it was the catalyst that further accelerated them.

Meanwhile, Colonel Kim Sang-gyŏm — who was concurrently serving as 5th Brigade commander and Jeju Island Garrison Command commander — found that eight days after his appointment as garrison commander, the Yŏsu 14th Regiment, a subordinate unit of the 5th Brigade, had launched a rebellion.

[^90]: Harold Fischgrund (age 81, Virginia, U.S.A.; then adviser to the 9th Regiment; recorded October 20, 2001), testimony.
[^91]: Kim Il-ryŏl (金益烈), *Sillok yugo: 4·3 ŭi chinsil* [Record and Essays on 4·3's Truth] (reprinted in Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, *4·3 ŭn Malhanda* [4·3 Speaks], vol. 2, Chŏnyewŏn, 1994, pp. 312–313).
[^92]: Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 168.
[^93]: "Report on the Internal Insurrections after April, 1948, made by Minister of National Defence, Lee Bum Suk," December 14, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 11, NARA, Washington, D.C.; *Kukhoe Sogirok* [National Assembly Stenographic Records], 1st session, No. 124, December 8, 1948; *Kukche Sinmun*, December 9, 1948.

**[p. 266]**

As a result, Song Yo-ch'an, commander of the 9th Regiment, assumed responsibility for the Cheju Island Garrison Command in place of Kim Sang-gyŏm, placing both the Cheju police and naval vessels under his command—thus establishing himself as the supreme commander of the suppression forces with full authority in both name and practice.

**③ Mobilization of Sŏbuk Youth Corps and other groups**

To conduct aggressive operations, the suppression authorities actively utilized civilian private organizations. Among these, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps and the Taedong Youth Corps played roles at the forefront that were scarcely inferior to those of the military and police. In particular, bereaved families whose relatives had been killed by the armed guerrillas were incorporated into police organizations, which encouraged retaliatory actions. Additionally, the authorities organized civil defense units (민보단) among the residents to handle village security, and young men from among these civil defense units were reorganized into "special assault squads" (특공대) to spearhead the suppression operations. The Student Federation (학련) was also mobilized for suppression operations. However, the mobilization of these civilian organizations actually worsened the situation by providing causes for further escalation, deepening mistrust and conflict among the residents, and producing significant negative side effects. In particular, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps had been a source of conflict with islanders even before the outbreak of the 4·3 incident and had been identified as a contributing factor to its initiation. However, before launching aggressive operations, the Syngman Rhee government and the U.S. military fully incorporated the Sŏbuk Youth Corps into the military and police. This ultimately exacerbated the situation and resulted in massive civilian casualties. The police were reorganized around the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, and a separate "Sŏbuk Corps company" (서청중대) was formed within the military. The arrival of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps on Cheju can be divided into three major phases. The first occurred immediately after the March 1, 1947 incident, when Yu Hae-jin, the newly appointed governor from North Chŏlla Province, began using Sŏbuk Youth Corps members as an honor guard. The total number of Sŏbuk Youth Corps members who arrived on Cheju by the time of the 4·3 outbreak is estimated at approximately 500–700. Until this point, lacking steady employment, they maintained themselves by forcibly selling the Taegeuk flag and photographs of Syngman Rhee. White terror attacks by the Sŏbuk Youth Corps occurred frequently, stirring public sentiment. This is noted by some as one of the causes of the 4·3 outbreak.

The second major influx occurred immediately after the 4·3 outbreak. According to testimony by Mun Bong-je, who was the Central Sŏbuk Youth Corps commander at the time, the Police Administration Bureau chief Cho Pyŏng-ok requested that 500 Sŏbuk Youth Corps members be dispatched as Cheju emergency suppression personnel. These Sŏbuk Youth Corps members were dispersed to various locations throughout Cheju and participated in suppression operations together with the already-present members.

[^1]

[^2]

**[p. 267]**

The third phase occurred in the immediate aftermath of the Yŏsu-Sunch'ŏn incident: between November and December 1948, a minimum of 1,000 additional members hastily changed uniforms to become police or garrison soldiers and took their places in the thick of suppression operations. As these members poured in, Cheju Island became, in effect, a "Sŏbuk Youth Corps domain." When they committed atrocious massacres, there was virtually no institution to restrain them. In connection with this, Harold Fischgrund, a U.S. adviser at the time, stated: "I could not understand why North Korean defectors from the Sŏbuk Youth Corps became police on Cheju Island when in America, police come from the local region."[^3]

However, behind the Sŏbuk Youth Corps stood the U.S. military and Syngman Rhee. Evidence that the U.S. military supported the Sŏbuk Youth Corps on Cheju is confirmed in intelligence reports from the U.S. military itself.[^4] Furthermore, a U.S. military intelligence report records that "on October 24, Sŏbuk Youth Corps members operating as 'temporary policemen' were dispatched to the scene of an incident in Sinheung-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn, driving out the guerrillas,"[^5] thereby already recognizing them as police at that time. Another U.S. military intelligence document states that Sŏbuk Youth Corps members were dispatched to Cheju openly and, at times, through "the secret induction" of members.[^6]

Syngman Rhee also took the lead in rapidly replacing military and police personnel with Sŏbuk Youth Corps members. This fact is corroborated by both U.S. classified documents and testimony from Sŏbuk Youth Corps members. A U.S. intelligence report, under the heading "Youth Corps Strengthens Military and Police," contains the following record:

According to the report, following the recent agreement between the President (Syngman Rhee) and the Minister of Interior (Sin Sŏng-mo), members of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps are scheduled to be supplied at 6,500 to the Korean Army and 1,700 to the National Police. They will be assigned to nine garrison commands and police stations throughout South Korea. Following mutual agreement among all organizations, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps agreed to place members in the police at the following ratios: one sergeant for every twenty members, one police officer for every fifty members, and one police superintendent for every two hundred members.[^7]

Park Hyŏng-yo, who arrived on Cheju on December 19, 1948, together with 250 fellow Sŏbuk Youth Corps members who became police, testified that President Syngman Rhee personally attended a Sŏbuk Youth Corps general assembly and delivered a speech advocating recruitment, after hearing which he went to Cheju.

[^1]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 951, October 1, 1948.
[^2]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 977, November 1, 1948.
[^3]: Harold Fischgrund (81 years old, Virginia, U.S.A., former 9th Regiment adviser, recorded October 20, 2001) testimony.
[^4]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1023, December 28, 1948.
[^5]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1005, December 6, 1948.
[^6]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1011, December 13, 1948.

**[p. 268]**

I was running an electrical supply shop in the North, but because I disliked Kim Il-sŏng, I came south in May 1948. In Seoul, I worked as a member of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps cultural division. It was December 10, 1948, as I remember. A Sŏbuk Youth Corps general assembly was held at the Seoul city municipal office in Myŏngdong, and President Syngman Rhee attended. At that assembly, he said, "The nation has entered a state of emergency due to the 4·3 incident on Cheju Island and the rebellion in Yŏsu and Sunch'ŏn. To overcome this national crisis, I will deploy the ideologically sound Sŏbuk Youth Corps throughout the nation." The next day, a general assembly of the Kŭmho-dong chapter was held. The chapter chairman, Im Sŭng-hyŏn (later appointed as a sergeant and soon promoted to police officer, serving as station chief of Pyosŏn Police Substation and chief of information at the Cheju Provincial Police Agency), proposed, "The president has ordered the dispatch of Sŏbuk Youth Corps members to address the Yŏsu, Sunch'ŏn, and Cheju incidents, so let us all volunteer." I responded to this proposal. On December 19, I arrived on Cheju with 250 fellow members. Of those who could read and write, 25 became police; the remaining 225 became soldiers. At that time, there were many illiterate people. When I arrived on Cheju, I found that a "200-person unit" wearing police uniforms had already arrived ahead of us. Among the Sŏbuk Youth Corps members, more became soldiers than police.[^8]

Park Hyŏng-yo further stated: "Without President Lee's permission, what authority could anyone have to kill civilians without trial?" He went on to say, "Without regard to consequences, citing only the justification of eliminating communists, the authorities deployed large numbers of members who were unfamiliar with local conditions. Syngman Rhee used us."

The fact that so many Sŏbuk Youth Corps members were deployed to garrison units and police forces was not merely a matter of troop augmentation; it also meant that soldiers and police originating from Cheju were pushed to the background. The suppression authorities apparently judged that Cheju-origin military and police personnel, being thoroughly familiar with local conditions and capable as fellow islanders of showing compassion, would become obstacles to aggressive operations.

Let us now examine in detail the process by which Sŏbuk Youth Corps members arrived on Cheju in police or military uniforms in November 1948. As mentioned earlier, the process by which Sŏbuk Youth Corps members were incorporated into government authority in unusual ways involved the participation of the highest authority at the time, President Syngman Rhee. Around mid-November that year, 200 Sŏbuk Youth Corps members were first assigned to the Cheju police. They were hastily trained as police in Seoul. Far more members were also sent to Cheju as soldiers. The 200-member police contingent became known as the "200-person unit." Kim Si-hun, who arrived on Cheju as a member of this unit, described the recruitment process as follows:

I belonged to Chin-yong Comrades Society (진용동지회), a subordinate organization of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. It was called Chin-yong because it was composed of people who had come south from Chinnampo and Yongganggun, taking the first syllables of those place names. One day when I went to the office, friends were all excited saying they were becoming police and going to Cheju. The story was: "To suppress the 4·3 incident on Cheju, one must have firm ideology. Even in Seoul, it is difficult to distinguish left from right, so only the Sŏbuk Youth Corps can be trusted—we will select police from them." So I, following my friends to Gangnam fashion, took the police examination. The police professional school at the time was located in front of Gwanghwamun in Seoul, where I had a simple interview. To become a policeman and conduct interrogations, one must at least know the Korean alphabet. In the end, those who could read became police, while those who could not became soldiers or private soldiers. This time, the number of police graduates reached two hundred, so we became known as the "200-person unit." The number who became soldiers was far greater.[^9]

Kim Si-hun also revealed that when recruiting Sŏbuk Youth Corps members at that time, Hong Sun-bong, the Cheju Province Police Bureau Director from South P'yŏngan Province, and Kim Chae-nŭng (金在能), the Cheju Sŏbuk Youth Corps commander, came directly to Seoul to encourage enlistment. The "200-person unit" was admitted to the police professional school and, after completing a two-week intensive training course, came to Cheju in police uniforms. Kim Si-hun's account continues:

After training for about three days, the chapter chairman, Chŏng Yong-ch'ŏl (who was specially appointed as a police officer and soon promoted to station chief at Samyang Police Substation), nudged me and said, "Now that Mr. Kim Chae-nŭng is returning to Cheju, you can just follow him and they will appoint you as a police officer." So eleven of us, including myself, trained in Seoul for just three days and followed Commander Kim Chae-nŭng to Cheju. When we arrived on Cheju, we had to undergo police training again. Because of this, the 189 who received proper fourteen-day training in Seoul became "Cheju Police Class 9," while we eleven became "Cheju Police Class 10."[^10]

However, the Cheju Police History states that "Cheju Police Class 9 consisted of 58 members who received training from November 18 to December 2, 1948,"[^11] so the Sŏbuk Youth Corps "200-person unit" appears to have been treated separately.

Meanwhile, there is a record in U.S. military intelligence documents that appears related to this matter:

[^7]: Park Hyŏng-yo (Sŏbuk Youth Corps-origin police officer) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, 4·3 Speaks ④, 1997, Jeon Ye-won, 151 pp.).
[^8]: Kim Si-hun (79 years old, Kasi-ri, Pyosŏn-myŏn, Sŏbuk Youth Corps-origin police, recorded February 26, 2002) testimony.
[^9]: Kim Si-hun testimony.
[^10]: Jeju Provincial Police Bureau, Jeju Police History, 1990, 182 pp.

**[p. 269]**

Recently, 620 volunteers from the Sŏbuk Youth Corps underwent twelve days of training under the supervision of Seoul police. After completing that training, they were dispatched in the capacity of regular police to Yŏsu, Cheju Island, and Kangwŏn Province. The current plan is for these personnel to return to Seoul after completing one month of duty in these riot-affected areas. According to a police official, no other youth organization offered to carry out these duties. Both the Taedong Youth Corps and the Young Alliance for National Unification had opportunities for their organizations to cooperate with police, but no volunteers have been forthcoming.[^12]

The Sŏbuk Youth Corps not only transformed into police but also into soldiers. Commander Song Yo-ch'an incorporated Sŏbuk Youth Corps members into the military, forming the so-called "special company." Though they were "soldiers who were not soldiers," lacking rank insignia, they possessed virtually unlimited power, with which even 9th Regiment military police and officers did not interfere, because Song Yo-ch'an granted them such authority. No Yun-bok, who came to Cheju as a civilian and became a special company member, testified as follows:

I arrived on Cheju in July 1948 with twenty members of an enlightenment drama troupe. But since no one came to watch the plays, things fell through. Then Commander Song Yo-ch'an armed us and formed a special company. One company consisted of four platoons totaling about eighty men. The company commander wore the insignia of a captain, but we did not. Commander Song Yo-ch'an gathered the 9th Regiment military police and officers and said, "Don't touch the special company. If you do, know that you'll be beaten to death." We felt good about this. We stationed ourselves in rotation in Hallim, Wŏljŏng, Sŏngsan, and other locations. When the 9th Regiment withdrew and the 2nd Regiment took up position, we eighty-eight special company members stationed at Wŏljŏng became incorporated into the 11th Company of the 2nd Regiment. When executions took place, we would go when asked if anyone wanted to volunteer. Once, when there was an execution in Kwidŏk-ri, Hallim-myŏn, I deliberately fired wide to save someone. I did not know those people's identities—I was just following what others said. But if I did not fire, I would come under suspicion, so I deliberately shot wide.[^13]

November and December 1948 on Cheju Island were scenes of unrelenting bloodshed. What did the U.S. military intelligence documents mean by "duty"? That the U.S. command condoned or at minimum acquiesced to the Cheju bloodbath is evident from such classified documents. When the Syngman Rhee government sent large numbers of Sŏbuk Youth Corps members to serve as police or soldiers without properly establishing systems for their wages and supplies, instead telling them to obtain supplies locally, this created problems. Park Hyŏng-yo, another Sŏbuk Youth Corps-origin police officer who came to Cheju after the "200-person unit," testified: "When I was assigned to a frontline substation, Police Bureau Director Hong Sun-bong delivered a speech saying, 'The current situation is not favorable, so I cannot send provisions or wages. Go out to villages and obtain food while conducting suppression operations.' "[^14]

[^12]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1011, December 13, 1948.
[^13]: No Yun-bok (78 years old, Sŏngsan-ri, Sŏngsan-ŭp, Namjeju-gun, former special company member of Sŏbuk Youth Corps, recorded March 28, 2002) testimony.
[^14]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1011, December 13, 1948.

**[p. 270]**

Of course, police support associations were organized at each frontline substation to back them, but this was bound to reveal its limits from the start. Naturally, this structure could only result in imposing burdens on the residents. However, a more fundamental problem was that the central government itself established a black-and-white ideological framework. Specifically, the authorities promoted the logic that "Cheju islanders are ideologically unreliable; most are tainted by communism; therefore, the ideologically sound Sŏbuk Youth Corps must suppress this region." This ideology became a major driving force pushing the situation toward bloodshed. Regarding this, Mun Bong-je, commander of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps central headquarters, made a clarifying statement in an interview with a magazine. He stated: "We dispatched the Sŏbuk Youth Corps whenever any region reported a surge in left-wing activity." He continued: "In that process, local political rivals would accuse each other of being communists, but since we had no way of knowing, we would eliminate the accused person. The most prominent example of this was Cheju Island." He further asked rhetorically: "What objective evidence could we possibly have had?"[^15]

Under the sponsorship of Syngman Rhee and the U.S. military, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, now positioned at the forefront of the Cheju situation, wielded virtually unrestricted power over both military and police. Ko Chi-dŏn, a resident of Kwangnŭng-ri, Aewŏl-myŏn, a midmountain village, was relocated to Hagŭi-ri, where he was startled by indiscriminate executions. He was relocated again to Oe-do-ri outside Cheju City. Since the civil defense unit leader in Oe-do-ri was a relative of his wife's family, he obtained civilian casualty exemption papers relatively quickly through this connection and managed to survive by being incorporated into a special assault squad. Ko Chi-dŏn testified about the cruel actions of police officers from the Sŏbuk Youth Corps that he witnessed during his time in the special assault squad:

I cannot forget the massacre committed by Lee Yun-do (李允道), a police officer from the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, during my work with the special assault squad at the Oe-do substation. On that day, the substation brought so-called "fugitive family members" to the station and subjected them to harsh torture. By the time they were taken away to be executed, they were already so exhausted that they could barely walk. Lee Yun-do ordered the special assault squad members to stab them, and when they hesitated, he drew a sword himself and stabbed each person in the back. Their eyes bulged out and they died writhing. About eighty people were killed at that time, and there were more women than men. Among the women were some nursing infants. When a nursing baby squirmed in front of its dead mother, Lee Yun-do stabbed the baby with his sword, lifted it up to show off his power. Babies from Toep'yŏng-ri died at that time. He was not human. When I saw that, I could not eat for several days.[^16]

The death by torture of Kim Tu-hyŏn (金斗鉉, aged 53), the Cheju Province Administration Bureau chief, at the hands of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps on November 9, 1948, is a symbolic incident demonstrating the power wielded by the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. The second-highest administrator in the provincial government fell victim to members of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps who harbored grievances about supply issues. In particular, Sŏbuk Youth Corps Cheju commander Kim Chae-nŭng beat Kim Tu-hyŏn severely in his office until he lost consciousness. Even as Kim Tu-hyŏn was not yet dead, Kim Chae-nŭng threw him outside, where he ultimately died. The U.S. military reported on this incident as follows:

On November 9, Sŏbuk Youth Corps members killed the Cheju Province Administration Bureau chief, Kim Tu-hyŏn by beating. Those Sŏbuk Youth Corps members responsible for his death said that Kim Tu-hyŏn was a known communist and that they were merely attempting to interrogate him, not kill him (A-1).[^17]

Regarding such illegal murders, the measures taken by the U.S. military intelligence agency, which had absolute control at that time, or by the 9th Regiment investigation authorities were lukewarm. A world in which merely claiming to have "executed a communist" could justify murder existed. The police were no exception to this attitude. The authorities did not punish the Sŏbuk Youth Corps members who participated in the murder; instead, they closed the case by having them enlisted in the military. That the police should have arrested and detained the murderers involved in the General Affairs Bureau chief murder case was obvious, but this did not occur. Police Chief Hong Sun-bong also deferred to Sŏbuk Youth Corps Commander Kim Chae-nŭng. The incident was hushed up by assigning the murderers to the 9th Regiment. The murderers not only escaped punishment but became soldiers.[^18]

[^15]: North Korean Research Institute, North Korea April 1989, 127 pp.
[^16]: Ko Chi-dŏn (Kwangnŭng-ri, Aewŏl-ŭp, former special assault squad member of Oe-do substation) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, 4·3 Speaks, Issue 442, April 23, 1999).
[^17]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 987, November 13, 1948.
[^18]: Kim Ho-gyŏm (Yeokchon 1-dong, Eunpyeong-gu, Seoul, former chief of Sŏgwipo Police Station) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, preceding book ④, 163 pp.).

**[p. 271]**

Additionally, the National Unified Student Federation (全國統一學生總聯盟, hereafter abbreviated as Student Federation) also dispatched enlightenment units to Cheju. The Student Federation on Cheju became a quasi-suppression organization operating in coordination with suppression authorities. The Civil Defense Unit (民保團) became the most important external organization of the police. Originally, the predecessor organization was the Village Defense Unit (향보단). The Village Defense Unit was proclaimed by Military Government Administrator Dean on April 16, 1948. The Village Defense Unit organization established three principles: ① all men from age 18 to 55 became mandatory members of the Village Defense Unit; ② both men and women from age 15 to 65 shared joint responsibility for local homeland defense; ③ operational expenses for the Village Defense Unit were to be covered by voluntary contributions from local residents.[^19]

The Village Defense Unit was organized following a recommendation by Police Administration Bureau Chief Cho Pyŏng-ok, who argued that police forces alone could not handle the election-opposition disturbances occurring nationwide in the run-up to the May 10 election. However, strong opposition arose, characterizing it as a coercive organization of a nature similar to the police assistance corps or national volunteer corps of the Japanese colonial period. The Village Defense Unit continued patrol duties in the respective regions after the election, but as repeated problems arose, it was dissolved on May 22, 1948. However, a contemporary press account of the Cheju situation in early June reported that "the Village Defense Unit, organized under police protection, was maintaining an all-night watch,"[^20] suggesting that the Village Defense Unit continued operating longer in Cheju.

The Village Defense Unit was revived as the Civil Defense Unit around the end of October 1948. The Director of the Capital Police Bureau declared through a statement dated October 29, 1948, that the Civil Defense Unit organization had already been completed. However, on Cheju Island, the Civil Defense Unit was organized much earlier. According to available Civil Defense Unit materials, the Cheju Civil Defense Unit was established on August 11, 1948. At that time, a provincial-level Civil Defense Unit organization was established, followed by the organization of Civil Defense Units in each region. The record "Civil Defense Unit Security Operations in Hwasun-ri, Andŏk-myŏn," which Chi Yŏn-bu (池連富), who served as battalion commander of the Civil Defense Unit in Hwasun-ri, Andŏk-myŏn, recorded while living, vividly demonstrates the organization and operations of the Civil Defense Unit at that time. According to this record, the Andŏk-myŏn Hwasun-ri Civil Defense Unit was organized in August 1948. It was structured with a commander (Jang Haeng-gyu) under whom were a vice-commander (Ji Hui-on and Yang Mun-il), an adjutant (Ji Bong-il), a self-defense section (Chi Yŏn-bu), a mobilization section (Ahn Chang-hyŏn), a liaison section (Lee Chung-man), and a women's section (Oh Tae-jŏng).

[^19]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, 4·3 Speaks ③, Jeon Ye-won, 1995, 268–270.
[^20]: Chosun Central Daily News, June 6, 1948.

**[p. 272]**

Not long afterward, the self-defense section was redesignated as a self-defense unit, strengthening the security organization. That is, village security duties were assigned to all men between ages 18 and 65. The self-defense unit organization was also reorganized into a structure of battalions, companies, platoons, and squads. Battalion Commander Chi Yŏn-bu served as self-defense unit commander, Kang Mun-p'al was appointed first company commander, and Yang Tae-hŭi was appointed second company commander.

The first task undertaken by the Civil Defense Unit was to construct stone walls as barriers around the substation. After mobilizing residents and constructing walls 5 meters high and 3.5 meters wide, seven watchtowers were installed at 15-meter intervals. That winter, as the village sought to cut itself off from the mountainside, a stone wall was begun around the village perimeter. The wall extended 1,500 meters, from the so-called slaughterhouse on the village's eastern side, through the area north of the elementary school and the old shrine site, to the so-called east-facing hill on the village's southern side. The wall was constructed to be 2.5 meters wide and 3.5 meters high, with seventeen watchtowers installed at 50-meter intervals. The suffering residents endured while building this wall was indescribable. As labor was scarce, everyone from small children to elderly grandmothers was mobilized. In the depths of winter, residents had to endure this difficult labor while shivering from cold and hunger.

Civil Defense Unit members stood watch day and night, carrying bamboo spears and iron spears. In the case of the Hwasun Civil Defense Unit, the first company would perform one day's guard duty, and the second company would stand watch the next day, adopting a rotating duty system. A different password was issued each day. The password was literally a lifeline. If someone forgot the password in the middle of the night and went out, their life could end at any moment. The night watch detail of the Hwasun Civil Defense Unit required 177 members to be mobilized daily. According to position assignments, this included: △ 30 members for duty at the substation watchtower and six main gates; △ 125 members for duty at seventeen village watchtowers, five gates, and three strategic positions; △ 8 members as watchtower patrol officers; △ 6 members as rotation patrol officers; and △ 8 members as duty confirmation patrol officers. For daytime security duty, 50 members were assigned to: △ 30 members for the five village gates; △ 12 members for village perimeter security; △ 4 members for substation security duty; and △ 4 members for headquarters office standby. In total, 222 members were mobilized daily for morning and evening duty to guard a single village. An emergency siren was installed at the substation. When the siren sounded, Civil Defense Unit members had to assemble within minutes. Any delay in this emergency drill resulted in harsh punishment.

The operational expenses for the Civil Defense Unit fell to the local residents. Women were busy preparing meals for the suppression troops and security units. Civil Defense Unit members were frequently mobilized for military and police suppression operations. Armed with extremely inadequate weapons such as bamboo spears, they were pushed to the front lines ahead of soldiers and police officers. Among the members, many lost their lives when they participated in suppression operations or stood watch and came under surprise attack by armed guerrillas. In some cases, they were executed by gunfire from the military and police on suspicion of collusion with the armed guerrillas or for negligence in their duties.

According to a classified document prepared by the Headquarters of the U.S. Military Command in Korea as of April 1, 1949, titled "Cheju Island Report," the Civil Defense Unit personnel on Cheju Island numbered as many as 50,000. This classified document also makes the puzzling assessment that "Cheju Island men prefer standing watch or going on anti-guerrilla suppression campaigns to working in the fields."[^21] The content is as follows:

The Civil Defense Units, which support the military and police, are composed mostly of adult males from among the island's population and are armed with crude bamboo spears. Estimated at roughly 50,000, they prefer standing watch or going on anti-guerrilla suppression campaigns to working in the fields. When engaging in joint operations with the military, they were immediately deployed to the front lines, resulting in numerous casualties among these civilian combatants.[^22]

However, in most villages, it was difficult to find young people. A considerable number had gone into the mountains or fled to other places. There was an absolute shortage of young people to constitute the Civil Defense Units. As a result, the obligation of homeland defense was assigned to everyone—men and women, young and old. Elderly people of sixty-five and even women had to endure participating in building stone walls to block guerrilla incursions and, at night, taking shifts to guard the walls. Among the Civil Defense Unit members, young men were reorganized under the name "special assault squads" and deployed as "cannon fodder" in the suppression operations conducted by the military and police. The special assault squad members are estimated to number approximately 3,000. A Seoul-based AP correspondent reported that "about 3,000 citizens armed with spears are assisting the government in the mop-up operations."[^23] This is confirmed in a statement by Chief of Staff Chae Pyŏng-dŏk released on November 30, 1948, which states: "In recent operations on Cheju Island, 3,000 armed civilian auxiliaries accompanied by a small number of soldiers."[^24]

[^21]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, 4·3 Speaks ③, preceding citations; Government Records Preservation Office, Hwasun-ri Civil Defense Unit records, 1986, 270 pp.; Jeju Yearbook, Jeju Yearbook Company, 1969.
[^22]: Dae Dong Daily News, December 17, 1948.
[^23]: Dae Dong Daily News, December 31, 1948.
[^24]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1097, April 1, 1949.

**[p. 273]**

## II. Proclamation of Martial Law

### 1) The Reality of Martial Law

On November 17, 1948, President Syngman Rhee proclaimed martial law on Cheju Island. Whether this martial law was proclaimed under direct orders from the U.S. military, which held operational control over Korean military forces at that time, or whether it received post-hoc ratification is uncertain. However, PMAG Commander General Roberts was deeply involved in the implementation of martial law. In the development of the Cheju 4·3 incident, martial law became the most crucial turning point related to civilian casualties. Whereas previous casualties had been limited relatively to younger men, the aggressive suppression operations begun around mid-November 1948 following the proclamation of martial law resulted in the execution of people of all ages and genders—from four-year-old children to octogenarians.

Martial law came to be perceived by Cheju islanders as providing the legal basis for numerous summary executions without trial. Because of this, the term "martial law" has been deeply imprinted on the hearts of Cheju islanders, and even elderly grandmothers unfamiliar with written Korean have not omitted the term "martial law" (계엄령) in their testimony. After emphasizing that their husbands, sons, or grandsons were innocently sacrificed to military and police suppression forces, they would invariably conclude with "It was during martial law…" or "Because of martial law…" and attributed the responsibility to the times. For these people, martial law was a symbol of resignation, the "reason" their family members died. Village elders even conceived of martial law as an "all-powerful system that permitted indiscriminate killing of people."

However, martial law remained, both at the time and until recently, extremely obscure in its actual substance. Despite the fact that most "4·3" related records mention martial law, there has been only confusion about what specific content martial law contained, when it was proclaimed, when it was lifted, or even whether martial law was proclaimed at all. Not only has there been no questioning of the martial law itself, but even the date of proclamation varies from source to source.

The questions remain: When exactly was martial law proclaimed? Were the aggressive suppression operations conducted under the name of martial law legitimate? Was martial law itself legal?

First, looking at martial law proclamation dates as they appear in existing records, they are broadly divided into three categories: the "October 8 theory," the "November 17 theory" (or November 16), and the "November 21 theory." Military documents, which are the responsible agencies for martial law, record mostly October 8. The "Irregular Warfare History" published by the Ministry of Defense War History Compilation Committee states: "Martial law was proclaimed on Cheju Island on October 8, 1948,"[^25] in brief notation. The Jeju Police History, a police document, similarly records: "Martial law was proclaimed throughout the island on October 8,"[^26] in short form. Additionally, the Jeju Yearbook, the Cheju Province Gazetteer (Vol. 1), the Grand River Record of Jeju's Hundred Years, and Liberation Jeju 30 Years, as well as most village local histories citing these works, record October 8.[^27]

Academic papers also cite the Cheju Province Gazetteer (Vol. 1) and Liberation Jeju 30 Years, stating "Martial law was proclaimed throughout the island on October 8 that day."[^28] Regarding the timing of martial law lifting, it is generally consistent at "December 31, 1948." The Korean War History issued by the Ministry of Defense states, without specifying when martial law was proclaimed: "Martial law over the Cheju Island district was lifted on December 31, 1948,"[^29] and most documents, including the "Irregular Warfare History," which nearly paraphrases the Korean War History, list December 31 as the martial law lifting date.[^30]

On the surface, from these materials, "martial law proclaimed on October 8, 1948, and lifted on December 31 that year" seems entirely unproblematic. (Only a few exceptions are notable: the Kimnyŏng-ri local history compiled for the Kimnyŏng-ri village records the martial law proclamation date as October 1, 1948, and the Jeju Yearbook records the martial law lifting date as October 12, 1949.)[^31]

However, U.S. materials and contemporary press reports tell a different story. The U.S. Military Command in Korea's intelligence report records that a state of emergency was proclaimed on November 17.[^32] In a National Assembly report released on December 8, 1948, the government first made mention of Cheju martial law. In that session, Defense Minister Rhee Bŏm-sŏk announced that martial law was proclaimed on Cheju Island on November 16, 1948.[^33] Furthermore, the domestic press reported that martial law was proclaimed on November 21.[^34]

As such, martial law proclamation dates in existing records are divided into three or four different dates. For most of these records lacking source attribution, it is impossible to verify which primary sources they are based on.

[^25]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, 4·3 Speaks ①, Jeon Ye-won, 1994, 434–437 pp.
[^26]: Ministry of Defense, War History Compilation Committee, Irregular Warfare History, 1988, 60 pp.
[^27]: Jeju Provincial Police Bureau, Jeju Police History, 1990, 312 pp.
[^28]: Jeju Yearbook, Jeju Yearbook Company, 1969; Cheju Province, Cheju Province Gazetteer (Vol. 1), 1982, 622 pp.; Kangryong-san and Lee Gyung-soo, Grand River Record: Jeju's Hundred Years, Tae Kwang Publishing, 1984, 640 pp.; Mun Man-gŭn, Liberation Jeju 30 Years, Cultural Tide Publishing, 1975, 457 pp.
[^29]: Yang Han-gwŏn, A Study on the Background of the Cheju Island 4·3 Upheaval, Seoul National University Master's Thesis, 1988, 16 pp.; Park Myŏng-rim, A Study on the Cheju Island 4·3 Popular Resistance, Korea University Master's Thesis, 1988, 150 pp.
[^30]: Ministry of Defense, War History Compilation Committee, Korean War History Vol. 1—Liberation and Military Founding, 1967, 445 pp.
[^31]: Ministry of Defense, War History Compilation Committee, Irregular Warfare History, 1988, 65 pp.; Song Hyo-shun, 30 Years of North Korean Provocations, North Korean Research Institute, 1978, 75 pp.; 30 Years of Liberation History, Semantic Publishing, 1977.
[^32]: Park Soo-yang, Kimnyŏng-ri Local History, 1986, 270 pp.; Jeju Yearbook, Jeju Yearbook Company, 1969.
[^33]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1056, February 5, 1949.
[^34]: National Assembly Stenographic Record, 1st session, No. 124, December 8, 1948.

**[p. 274]**

However, a more careful examination of documents concerning martial law creates even greater confusion. Aside from date confusion, doubt arises about whether martial law was even proclaimed. In a military record documenting the early history of the military on the basis of extensive military materials, it is recorded that martial law was proclaimed in the Yŏsu district on October 22, 1948, yet nowhere can be found mention that martial law was proclaimed on Cheju. The Army History Daily Log, which carefully compiles military records, is similarly devoid of any mention of Cheju martial law. Even more remarkably, the U.S. military, which consistently reported on Cheju "martial law" (martial law), later left a record stating "martial law was not proclaimed on Cheju Island," as follows:

On February 5, Kim Dong-sŏng, Director of Korean Public Information, announced that he was lifting the state of emergency previously issued on October 25 for the Yŏsu region, South Chŏlla Province, and the Jirisan district. He further stated that the state of emergency proclaimed on November 17, 1948 for the Cheju Island region had been lifted a month earlier, but there had been no official prior statement regarding its effect. He also stated that the Korean government did not intend to proclaim a state of emergency in regions near the 38th Parallel and was unaware of any reports or rumors that a state of emergency would be proclaimed. **Commentary:** The state of emergency has been called martial law by both Koreans and Americans. However, this is incorrect because martial law has not been proclaimed by the current Korean government.[^35]

The confusion regarding the substance of martial law and its proclamation date was clarified through Presidential Decree No. 31, preserved in the Government Records Preservation Office under the Ministry of Interior and Safety. It is hereby proclaimed after the passage of a National Assembly resolution that Martial Law be proclaimed for the Cheju Island district. President Syngman Rhee (李承晩) Tanjŏn Year 4281, November 17 Cabinet Minister Prime Minister and Minister of Defense Lee Bŏm-sŏk (李範奭) Cabinet Minister Minister of Interior Yun Ch'i-yŏng (尹致暎) Cabinet Minister Foreign Minister Chang Taek-sang (張澤相) Cabinet Minister Finance Minister Kim To-yŏn (金度演) Cabinet Minister Justice Minister Yi In (李 仁) Cabinet Minister Education Minister Ahn Ho-sang (安浩相) Cabinet Minister Agriculture and Forestry Minister Cho Bong-am (曺奉岩) Cabinet Minister Commerce and Industry Minister Im Yŏng-sin (任永信) Cabinet Minister Social Affairs Minister Chŏn Chin-han (錢鎭漢) Cabinet Minister Transportation Minister Hŏ Chŭng (許 政) Cabinet Minister Communications Minister Yun Sŏk-gu (尹錫龜) Cabinet Minister Yi Yun-yŏng (李允榮)

**Presidential Decree No. 31 Proclamation of Martial Law for the Cheju Island District**

In order to swiftly suppress the rebellion on Cheju Island, the district is hereby designated as a cordon zone, and martial law is proclaimed as of the date of promulgation of this decree.

The Martial Law Commander shall be the Commander of the 9th Regiment of the Army stationed on Cheju Island.[^36]

This document, bearing the handwritten signatures of the President and all Cabinet Ministers, clearly confirms that martial law was proclaimed on November 17, 1948. The document concerning the lifting of martial law is Presidential Decree No. 43, which states: "Martial law for the Cheju Island district is hereby lifted as of December 31, Tanjŏn Year 4281."[^37] With this, the date of martial law proclamation is confirmed as accurate only in the U.S. military report, though that report limited itself to referring to a "state of emergency" rather than "martial law."

This raises the question: Why do military documents, which are the responsible agencies for martial law, contain no record of martial law at all, or record the incorrect date of "October 8"?

Meanwhile, two newspaper articles published by the same news agency with only a ten-day gap between them clearly illustrate the confusion of the time:

There are rumors circulating that martial law has been proclaimed on Cheju Island, but on the 19th, the Defense Ministry Public Information Office denied this as entirely groundless as follows. △ Defense Ministry Public Information Office Statement = Just as rumors circulating in all newspapers and among the public suggest, there has been no proclamation of martial law in the Cheju Island area. As riots are occurring in various places, the military has merely strengthened operational vigilance, which appears to have been misunderstood by civilians.[^38]

On the 21st, martial law was implemented throughout the nation, and on the 23rd, through Martial Law Proclamation No. 1, seven categories were released: traffic restrictions, censorship of mail, communications, newspapers and magazines, village relocation, restrictions on educational institutions, maintenance of roads and highways, and penalties related to rebellion. (Ministry of Defense, 29th Review.)[^39]

[^35]: Chosun Daily News, November 30, 1948.
[^36]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1056, February 5, 1949.
[^37]: Official Gazette No. 14, November 17, 1948.
[^38]: Official Gazette No. 26, December 31, 1948.
[^39]: Chosun Daily News, November 20, 1948.

**[p. 275]**

Martial law was proclaimed only in the Official Gazette based on National Assembly resolution, without public announcement through the press, leading to allegations that it was proclaimed in secret. Meanwhile, although martial law is said to have been proclaimed, what martial law specifically meant was not made known. Syngman Rhee appointed Song Yo-ch'an, the 9th Regiment commander stationed on Cheju, as Martial Law Commander, but Song Yo-ch'an did not know what martial law was. Kim Chŏng-mu, who served as military supply officer under Commander Song Yo-ch'an, testified that he "did not clearly understand the concept of martial law."[^40]

Regarding this, Kim Ho-gyŏm, who served as police in Cheju during the 4·3 period, testified as follows:

At that time, martial law was something without legal basis and ambiguous. This was because even the Martial Law Commander Song Yo-ch'an did not know what martial law was. One day when I was with Police Chief Hong Sun-bong, Song Yo-ch'an came. We three had all served together in Manchuria during the Japanese period and in the Japanese military, and after liberation we had worked together at the police professional school, so we knew each other well. Song Yo-ch'an asked Hong Sun-bong, "People are telling me from above to proclaim martial law, but what should I do?" If martial law were proclaimed, it should have some legal basis and concrete guidelines, but there were none, so Song Yo-ch'an was also frustrated. Furthermore, Song Yo-ch'an was rather ignorant. By contrast, Hong Sun-bong was an extremely capable Japanese-era police officer who had achieved the highest position possible for a Korean while serving in Manchuria. In any case, Hong Sun-bong drafted all the martial law proclamations and other proclamations. But unconditionally killing people by labeling them as coming from the midmountain region cannot be justified even under martial law. In Manchuria during the Japanese period, such things did occur. It involved designating a specific region and indiscriminately shooting.[^41]

As examined above, the Cheju martial law was proclaimed on November 17, 1948, and remained in effect until it was lifted on December 31, lasting about a month and a half. However, the question remains: Why did Syngman Rhee keep the martial law proclamation, which had passed through a National Assembly resolution, secret from the press? Why did even the Martial Law Commander charged with implementing martial law fumble about not understanding its content? And why did the U.S. military, which held operational control over Korean military forces at the time, later deny the substance of martial law? Such questions were resolved through recently discovered U.S. military materials.

On December 1, 1948, PMAG Commander General Roberts issued the following instructions to the Chief of Staff of the Ministry of Defense:

I enclose a document concerning martial law. This document should be circulated to all your commanders so that they can become familiar with what martial law is, when it can be proclaimed, who proclaims it, and what its effects are.[^42]

Fourteen days after the proclamation of martial law on November 17, this document sent to the Chief of Staff of the Ministry of Defense confirms several crucial facts:

First, at the time of martial law proclamation, no martial law law existed in Korea. Second, because no martial law law existed, the highest command of the Korean military did not know the specific content of martial law. Third, it was only through the document sent by the U.S. military that the Korean military learned of martial law's content.

From this material alone, it is unclear whether the U.S. military directly instructed Syngman Rhee to proclaim martial law or merely gave post-hoc approval. Nor is it possible to know what content the martial law document sent by the U.S. military contained. However, the fact that the U.S. military sent a document on martial law, explaining the method of its implementation, indicates that the U.S. military was closely involved with martial law.

Therefore, the later recording by the U.S. military, long after martial law had been lifted, that "the state of emergency has been called martial law by both Koreans and Americans. However, this is incorrect because martial law has not been proclaimed by the current Korean government," appears to be an attempt to evade responsibility regarding "martial law without a martial law law" and their own involvement.

### 2) The Question of Illegality

**① Martial Law and Trial**

Article 64 of the Constitution of the Republic (Proclamation of Martial Law) stipulates: "The President proclaims martial law according to procedures established by law." However, at the time martial law was proclaimed on November 17, 1948, the corresponding law—the Martial Law Act—did not exist. The Martial Law Act was not enacted and promulgated until November 24, 1949, as Law No. 69, almost a year after martial law had been lifted.[^43]

Because of this, there has been recent controversy over whether martial law was legally proclaimed. In 1997, the Jemin Ilbo reported under the headline "4·3 Martial Law Was Illegal" that "the martial law proclaimed during the Cheju 4·3, known as providing the legal basis for the mass slaughter of Cheju islanders, was revealed to have been proclaimed illegally by President Syngman Rhee."[^44]

The Jemin Ilbo based its report on the following: △ The presidential decree (Presidential Decree No. 31) by which former President Syngman Rhee proclaimed martial law for the Cheju Island district on November 17, 1948; △ Article 64 of the Constitution of the Republic explicitly states that "the President proclaims martial law according to procedures established by law," delegating martial law proclamation to law, yet martial law was proclaimed a full year before the actual enactment of the Martial Law Act on November 24, 1949; △ Article 99 of the Constitution of the Republic, which states that "provisions that cannot be realized without the enactment of a law shall take effect when such law takes effect"; △ A declassified U.S. military document reporting that the "state of emergency" (the state of emergency) was being incorrectly used as "martial law" (martial law).

The Jemin Ilbo further added, on the basis of testimony by Kim Ho-gyŏm, who had served as chief of Sŏgwipo Police Station and other positions, that even Song Yo-ch'an, the Martial Law Commander of Cheju Island district at the time, did not know the specific content of martial law.

In response, counterarguments were raised that 4·3 martial law was legal and that no killing of civilians occurred. These arguments included the following points: △ During the colonial period under Japanese rule in Korea, martial law as it existed in Japan was implemented in accordance with Japanese imperial decrees; △ Following liberation, Japanese Military Government Ordinance No. 11 specifically identified laws to be repealed, and martial law was not included in this repeal, so it continued to be effective; △ Through Japanese Military Government Ordinance No. 21, all laws and orders previously in effect except those specifically repealed were continued, so martial law likewise continued to be effective; △ Even after the establishment of the Republic of Korea government, the Constitution of the Republic (Article 100) states that "current laws and ordinances shall have effect unless they conflict with this Constitution," and therefore current laws and ordinances from before the Constitution of the Republic—that is, laws and ordinances from the Japanese period—must be recognized; thus, it is argued that Japanese martial law continued to be effective. In other words, even though the Martial Law Act had not been enacted at the time of the Cheju 4·3 incident, since it had been proclaimed on the basis of Japanese martial law, which continued to be effective until then, the reporting that it was "proclaimed without legal basis" is argued to be incorrect.[^45]

[^40]: Chosun Daily News, November 30, 1948.
[^41]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1056, February 5, 1949.
[^42]: Official Gazette, Extra, November 24, 1949.
[^43]: "Martial Law," December 1, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 4.
[^44]: Kim Chŏng-mu (77 years old, Buam-dong, Jongno-gu, Seoul, former military supply officer of the 9th Regiment, promoted to major general, president of the graduating class of Korean Military Academy Class 2, recorded September 25, 2002) testimony.
[^45]: Jemin Ilbo, April 1, 1997.

**[p. 276]**

These contending interpretations regarding the legality of martial law reflect deeper issues concerning the Korean government's authority and the relationship between Korean sovereignty and U.S. military authority in 1948. Regardless of which interpretation one accepts, what remains beyond dispute is that the proclamation and execution of martial law created the conditions for the systematic killing of civilians without trial. The use of martial law as legal justification for mass killings of civilians marked a dark chapter in Korea's early modern history.

[^45]: Jemin Ilbo, April 1, 1997.

**[p. 277]**

is also stated. The police document *History of Jeju Police* (제주경찰사) likewise records briefly: "On October 8, martial law was proclaimed throughout the island."[^120] Beyond these, the *Jeju Almanac*, the *Jeju-Do Gazetteer* (upper volume), *Grand Record: One Hundred Years of Jeju* (대하실록 제주백년), *Liberation Jeju: Thirty Years* (광복제주 30년), and most village gazetteers that appear to have cited these sources also record October 8.[^121] Academic papers citing the *Jeju-Do Gazetteer* (upper volume) and *Liberation Jeju: Thirty Years* respectively also state: "On October 8 of that same day, martial law was proclaimed throughout the island."[^122] The date of martial law's lifting is largely consistent at "December 31, 1948." The Ministry of National Defense's *History of the Korean War* records, without specifying the date of martial law's proclamation: "On December 31, 1948, martial law in the Jeju Island District was lifted."[^123] Most materials, including the *History of Counter-Irregular Warfare* (대비정규전사), which largely reproduced the *History of the Korean War*'s content, also take December 31 as the date of martial law's lifting.[^124]

Taken together, it appeared as though there was no problem whatsoever with "the martial law proclaimed on October 8, 1948 being lifted on December 31 of the same year" (though it is worth noting that for some reason the village gazetteer for Kimnyŏng-ri records the martial law proclamation date as October 1, 1948, and the *Jeju Almanac* records the martial law lifting date as October 12, 1949[^125]).

However, when one examines U.S. source materials and contemporary press reports, the situation changes. A U.S. Forces Korea headquarters intelligence report records that a state of emergency was proclaimed on November 17.[^126] The government first mentioned the Jeju martial law in a National Assembly report held on December 8, 1948, at which Defense Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk disclosed that martial law had been proclaimed on Jeju Island on November 16, 1948.[^127] And the domestic press reported that martial law was proclaimed on November 21.[^128] 

Thus the dates of martial law proclamation appearing in existing materials divide into three or even four versions. Since most of these records do not cite their sources, it is impossible to verify what primary sources they are based on.

[^119]: Ministry of National Defense, War History Compilation Committee, *Tae-Bijonggyu Chŏnsa* [History of Counter-Irregular Warfare], 1988, p. 60.
[^120]: Cheju-do Kyŏngch'al-guk, *Cheju Kyŏngch'al-sa* [History of Jeju Police], 1990, p. 312.
[^121]: *Cheju Yŏn'gam* [Jeju Almanac], Jeju Almanac Company, 1969; Cheju-do, *Cheju-do Chi* (上) [Jeju-Do Gazetteer, upper vol.], 1982, p. 622; Kang Yong-sam and Yi Kyŏng-su, *Taeha Sillok Cheju Paengnyŏn* [Grand Record: One Hundred Years of Jeju], T'aegwang Munhwasa, 1984, p. 640; Pu Man-gŭn, *Kwangbok Cheju 30-nyŏn* [Liberation Jeju: Thirty Years], Munjosa, 1975, p. 457.
[^122]: Yang Han-gwŏn, "Cheju-do 4·3 Pokttong ŭi Paegyŏng e kwanhan Yŏn'gu" [Study on the Background of the Jeju 4·3 Riot], M.A. thesis, Seoul National University, 1988, p. 16; Pak Myŏng-rim, "Cheju-do 4·3 Minchung Hangjaeng e kwanhan Yŏn'gu" [Study on the Jeju 4·3 People's Uprising], M.A. thesis, Korea University, 1988, p. 150.
[^123]: Ministry of National Defense, War History Compilation Committee, *Hankuk Chŏnjaengsa* [History of the Korean War], vol. 1, 1967, p. 445.
[^124]: Ministry of National Defense, War History Compilation Committee, *Tae-Bijonggyu Chŏnsa*, 1988, p. 65; Song Hyo-sun, *Pukkoe Tobal 30-nyŏn* [Thirty Years of North Korean Provocations], North Korea Research Institute, 1978, p. 75; *Kwangbok 30-nyŏnsa* [History of Thirty Years of Liberation], Semunsa, 1977.
[^125]: Pak Su-yang, *Kimnyŏng-ri Hyangto-ji* [Kimnyŏng-ri Village Gazetteer], 1986, p. 270; *Cheju Yŏn'gam*, 1969.
[^126]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1056, February 5, 1949.
[^127]: *Kukhoe Sogirok* [National Assembly Stenographic Records], 1st session, No. 124, December 8, 1948.
[^128]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, November 30, 1948.

**[p. 278]**

When one examines documents concerning martial law more carefully, the confusion deepens further. Setting aside the confusion over dates, questions arise as to whether martial law was even proclaimed at all. *Army Founding* (창군), which recorded the history of the early Army based on extensive military materials, records that martial law was proclaimed in the Yŏsu District on October 22, 1948, but contains no reference anywhere to martial law being proclaimed on Jeju.[^129] The *Army History Daily Record* (육군역사일지), in which military records are meticulously compiled, likewise contains no mention of the Jeju martial law. Indeed, the U.S. military, which had continuously reported on the Jeju martial law, belatedly left the following record stating that "no martial law was ever proclaimed on Jeju Island":

> On February 5, Kim Tong-sŏng, the Korean government's Public Information Director, announced the lifting of the state of emergency applicable to the Yŏsu area, South Chŏlla Province, and the Chirisan District that had been declared on October 25. He also stated that the state of emergency for the Jeju Island area that had been proclaimed on November 17, 1948, had been lifted one month ago but that there had been no prior official announcement of its effect. He further stated that the Korean government has no intention to proclaim a state of emergency for areas near the 38th parallel and has no knowledge of any reports or rumors that a state of emergency would be proclaimed. [Comment] The state of emergency (the state of emergency) has been called martial law (martial law) by both Koreans and Americans. However, this is incorrect. This is because martial law has never been proclaimed by the current Korean government.[^130]

The confusion surrounding the true nature of martial law and the date of its proclamation was resolved through the following Presidential Decree No. 31, preserved in the Government Records Preservation Center under the Ministry of Government Administration and Home Affairs:

> The matter of the Proclamation of Martial Law in the Jeju Island District, enacted with the approval of the State Council, is hereby promulgated.
>
> President Yi Sŭng-man (李承晩)  
> Year 4281 of the Tangun Era, November 17  
> State Council Member, State Prime Minister concurrently Minister of National Defense Yi Pŏm-sŏk (李範奭)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Home Affairs Yun Ch'i-yŏng (尹致暎)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Foreign Affairs Chang T'aek-sang (張澤相)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Finance Kim To-yŏn (金度演)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Justice Yi In (李仁)

[^129]: Han Yong-wŏn (韓鎔源), *Ch'anggŭn* [Army Founding], Pakyŏngsa, 1984, Appendix "National Defense History Chronology."
[^130]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1056, February 5, 1949.

**[p. 279]**

> State Council Member, Minister of Education An Ho-sang (安浩相)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Agriculture and Forestry Cho Pong-am (曺奉岩)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Commerce and Industry Im Yŏng-sin (任永信)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Social Affairs Chŏn Jin-han (錢鎭漢)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Transportation Hŏ Chŏng (許政)  
> State Council Member, Minister of Communications Yun Sŏk-ku (尹錫龜)  
> State Council Member Yi Yun-yŏng (李允榮)

> **Presidential Decree No. 31**  
> **Matter of the Proclamation of Martial Law in the Jeju Island District**
>
> In order to rapidly pacify the rebellion on Jeju Island, the said district is hereby designated a state of siege (*hapwi*, 合圍), and it is proclaimed that martial law shall be enforced from the date of promulgation of this decree. The martial law commander shall be the commander of the Army's 9th Regiment garrisoning Jeju Island.[^131]

This document, bearing the personal signatures of the President and all cabinet members, clearly establishes that martial law was proclaimed on November 17, 1948. The document regarding the lifting of martial law — "Presidential Decree No. 43" — states: "The martial law in the Jeju Island District is hereby terminated as of December 31 of Year 4281 of the Tangun Era."[^132] This establishes that, although the U.S. military report had qualified it as a "state of emergency rather than martial law," only the U.S. military report proved accurate regarding the date of the martial law proclamation. The question then arises as to why military materials — the primary government agency responsible for martial law — contain no record of martial law whatsoever, or record the erroneous date of "October 8."

Meanwhile, the following two news articles from the same news outlet, published just ten days apart, clearly illustrate the confusion of the time:

> These days there are rumors circulating that martial law has been proclaimed on Jeju Island, but on November 19 the Ministry of National Defense's Press Section denied this entirely as follows. △ Ministry of National Defense Press Section Statement: As all newspapers and circulating rumors have it, there has been no proclamation of martial law throughout Jeju Island. Since uprisings have broken out in various places, the military has strictly tightened operational security, but this appears to have been misunderstood by civilians.[^133]

> On the 21st of last month, martial law was implemented throughout the island, and subsequently on the 23rd, Martial Law Proclamation No. 1

[^131]: *Kwanbo* [Official Gazette] No. 14, November 17, 1948.
[^132]: *Kwanbo* No. 26, December 31, 1948.
[^133]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, November 20, 1948.

**[p. 280]**

announced seven items: restrictions on transportation, censorship of postal communications, newspapers, and magazines; village evacuation; restrictions on educational institutions; cutting of trees along roadsides; maintenance of roads; and penalties for incidents related to the uprising (Ministry of National Defense, Censorship Approved, November 29).[^134]

Martial law was declared through the approval of the State Council and published only in the Official Gazette — not disclosed to the press — and so has been criticized as having been proclaimed in secret. Meanwhile, although martial law was said to have been proclaimed, it was not publicly known what martial law specifically meant. Rhee Syngman had appointed Regimental Commander Song Yo-ch'an, then garrisoning the 9th Regiment on Jeju, as martial law commander, but Song Yo-ch'an did not know what martial law was. Kim Chŏng-mu, who had served as logistics staff officer under Regimental Commander Song Yo-ch'an, also testified that "I did not have a clear concept of martial law."[^135] In this connection, Kim Ho-gyŏm, who served as Jeju police during the 4·3 period, testified as follows:

> At the time, martial law was something ambiguous with no legal basis. That's because even Martial Law Commander Song Yo-ch'an didn't know what martial law was. One day when I was with Police Commissioner Hong Sun-bong, Song Yo-ch'an came to visit. We three had served together in the Manchurian Army and the Japanese Army during the Japanese period, and after liberation we had worked together at the Police Training Academy, so we knew each other well. Song Yo-ch'an asked Hong Sun-bong: "They're ordering from above to declare martial law — what do I have to do?" For martial law there should be some basis and concrete guidelines, but there were none, so Song Yo-ch'an was frustrated too. Song Yo-ch'an was also not very educated. Hong Sun-bong, on the other hand, was so capable as a Japanese colonial police officer that he had attained the highest position of any Korean while serving in Manchuria. In any case, Hong Sun-bong drafted everything for Song Yo-ch'an — what should be in a martial law declaration, in a proclamation, and so on. But to kill someone unconditionally just because they're in the mid-mountains — that's not right, even under martial law. In Manchuria during the Japanese period there was something like that. You'd designate a specific area and fire unconditionally.[^136]

As examined above, it has been established that the Jeju martial law was proclaimed on November 17, 1948 and lasted for about one month and fifteen days until it was lifted on December 31.

[^134]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, November 30, 1948.
[^135]: Kim Chŏng-mu (77세, Puam-dong, Chongno-gu, Seoul; then logistics staff officer of the 9th Regiment, retired as brigadier general, chairman of Military Academy 2nd Class alumni association; recorded September 25, 2002), testimony.
[^136]: Kim Ho-gyŏm (former chief of Sŏgwip'o Police Station), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., pp. 377–378).

**[p. 281]**

However, questions about its true nature remain: why did Yi Sŭng-man keep even martial law approved through State Council deliberation so secret that it was hidden from the press; why did even the martial law commander responsible for implementing martial law flounder about not knowing its contents; and moreover, why did the U.S. military — which then held operational control of the Korean military — later deny the true nature of martial law?

These questions were resolved by a recently unearthed U.S. military document. PMAG Commander Brigadier General Roberts issued the following directive to the Ministry of National Defense's Chief of Staff on December 1, 1948:

> Enclosed is a document concerning martial law (martial law). This document can be issued to all your commanders so that they are well informed as to what martial law is, when it can be declared, who declares it, and what its effects are.[^137]

Through this document sent to the Ministry of National Defense's Chief of Staff fourteen days after the proclamation of martial law on November 17, the following several important facts can be confirmed. First, no martial law legislation existed domestically at the time martial law was proclaimed. Second, because no martial law legislation existed, even the senior leadership of the Korean military did not know the specific contents of martial law. Third, it was only through a document sent by the U.S. military that the Korean military came to know the contents of martial law.

From this document alone, it cannot be determined whether the U.S. military directly ordered Yi Sŭng-man to proclaim martial law or whether it ratified it after the fact. Nor can the content of the document on martial law sent by the U.S. military be determined. However, in that it enclosed a document on martial law and provided information on how to implement it, the U.S. military and martial law were in a close relationship. Accordingly, the U.S. military's later record — made long after martial law had been lifted — that "the state of emergency (the state of emergency) has been called martial law (martial law) by both Koreans and Americans. However, this is incorrect. This is because martial law has never been proclaimed by the current Korean government" — appears to be an attempt to disavow any relationship between themselves and "martial law without a martial law statute."

#### 2) The Illegality Controversy

##### ① The Martial Law Trial

Article 64 of the founding Constitution (the right to proclaim martial law) stipulates: "The President shall proclaim martial law as prescribed by law." However,

[^137]: "Martial Law," December 1, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 4.

**[p. 282]**

at the time martial law was proclaimed — November 17, 1948 — the relevant legislation, the Martial Law Act, did not exist. The Martial Law Act was promulgated only on November 24, 1949 — nearly a year after martial law had been lifted — as Act No. 69.[^138]

For this reason there has been recent controversy as to whether the martial law was lawfully proclaimed. In 1997, *Jemin Ilbo* reported under the headline "The 4·3 Martial Law Was Illegal": "The martial law that has been known as the legal basis for the mass massacre of Jeju residents during the Jeju 4·3 has been found to have been illegally proclaimed by then-President Yi Sŭng-man."[^139] *Jemin Ilbo* presented as the grounds for this report: △ Presidential Decree No. 31, through which former President Yi Sŭng-man proclaimed martial law in the Jeju Island District on November 17, 1948; △ the point that although Article 64 of the founding Constitution explicitly stated "the President shall proclaim martial law as prescribed by law," thereby delegating the proclamation of martial law to legislation, martial law was in fact proclaimed a full year before the Martial Law Act was enacted on November 24, 1949; △ the founding Constitution's Article 99, which states "provisions that cannot be realized without the enactment of legislation shall be enforced from the time that legislation comes into effect"; △ a classified U.S. military document at the time reporting that a "state of emergency" was being incorrectly used as "martial law." *Jemin Ilbo* also added, through testimony from Kim Ho-gyŏm — who had served as Sŏgwip'o Police Station chief and other positions — that even Regimental Commander Song Yo-ch'an, the Jeju Island District martial law commander at the time, did not know the specific contents of martial law.

In response, a counterargument was presented that the 4·3 martial law was lawful and that there had been no massacre of innocent civilians. Specifically, it was argued that: △ in colonial Chosŏn under the Japanese, Japan's martial law applied under Japanese imperial decree; △ after liberation, Military Government Act No. 11 explicitly listed the laws to be abolished — and martial law was not among them, so it continued in effect; △ through Military Government Act No. 21, all laws and ordinances enacted prior to liberation that had not been abolished were continued in force, so martial law also continued to have effect; △ even after the establishment of the Republic of Korea government, the founding Constitution (Article 100) stated "existing laws and ordinances shall remain in force insofar as they do not conflict with this Constitution" — meaning existing laws and ordinances predating the founding Constitution, namely Japanese laws and other old ordinances, must be recognized. Therefore Japanese-era martial law continued to have effect, and so the report that martial law was "proclaimed without legal basis" was wrong.

[^138]: *Kwanbo* Extra Edition, November 24, 1949.
[^139]: *Jemin Ilbo*, April 1, 1997.

**[p. 283]**

...had been rendered.140) Under such claims, the adopted son of former President Syngman Rhee brought a lawsuit against the Jemin Ilbo for a retraction and demanded 300 million won in damages. The "Martial Law Trial" proceeded for approximately one year and eight months, from the first trial judgment on July 20, 2000, through the appellate judgment on December 22, and concluding with the Supreme Court's final judgment on April 27, 2001. The Supreme Court (Chief Justice Lee Kyu-hong) acknowledged in its judgment141) that "in the process of suppression of the armed guerrillas by military and police suppression forces around the time of the declaration of martial law, the Cheju upland villages were laid waste, and it is a fact that many residents without direct relation to the armed guerrillas suffered harm including being killed without trial." However, regarding the illegality of the Cheju 4·3 martial law, the court stated that "it is difficult to conclude definitively that the act of declaring martial law itself was an illegal measure without any legal foundation," but then noted that "this martial law was actually proclaimed before the Martial Law was enacted in accordance with the Constitution of the Republic; in the Constitutional Assembly there were disputes regarding the legality of martial law proclaimed in relation to the Yŏsu Rebellion incident; regarding whether the martial law ordinance enforced during the Japanese colonial period continued to remain valid until the time of this martial law declaration, there is scholarly debate requiring high-level legal judgment of the relevant provisions of the Military Government Ordinance and the Constitution of the Republic, and currently some National Assembly members claim that the martial law declaration of this case is illegal in relation to the Special Law for the Investigation of the Truth of the Cheju 4·3 Incident and the Restoration of Honor to the Victims; in light of these circumstances, while the defendant failed to introduce different opinions in a balanced manner in reporting on the legality of the martial law of this case and made a definitive judgment, insofar as the defendant's reporting was made based on reasonable data and grounds through truth-investigation work in its own way rather than biased subjective judgment on the basis of fragmentary data, there is a reasonable basis for believing that the facts the defendant presupposed were true." With this statement, the court dismissed all of the plaintiff's claims. In the end, the Supreme Court reserved its judgment on the illegality of the Cheju 4·3 martial law.142)
