[^140]: Regarding the disputes surrounding the "Martial Law Trial," see Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit. ⑤, 1998, pp. 365–392.

[^141]: Supreme Court, April 27, 2001, Decision 2001da7216.

[^142]: Kim Sun-tae, "The Illegality of Martial Law during the Cheju 4·3," Cheju 4·3 Research, Yeoksa Bipyeong Press, 1999; Kim Chang-rok, "Constitutional Article 100, 1948—The Effect of Japanese Colonial Law through the Cheju 4·3 Martial Law," Legal Research, vol. 39, no. 1, Busan National University Research Institute of Law, 1998.

**[p. 284]**

Meanwhile, around the time of the "Martial Law Trial," legal scholars such as Professor Kim Sun-tae (Department of Law, Korean National Open University) and Professor Kim Chang-rok (Department of Law, Busan National University) clarified the issue of the illegality of the Cheju 4·3 martial law academically through their papers.

**②Disputes in the National Assembly**

As examined above, the legal judgment regarding the illegality of the Cheju 4·3 martial law was left as an academic matter. Yet apart from legal issues, there remained a historical question: did Syngman Rhee actually proclaim the Cheju 4·3 martial law on the basis of the Japanese colonial martial law? This can be understood by comparing the martial laws proclaimed before and after the enactment of the Martial Law Act. After the Martial Law Act was enacted on November 24, 1949, the martial law declarations that followed stated: "The President of the Republic of Korea hereby proclaims martial law according to Article 1 of the Martial Law Act in the following manner..."143) This faithfully followed Constitutional Article 64, which stipulated that "the President shall proclaim martial law as prescribed by law." Therefore, if the Cheju 4·3 martial law had been proclaimed on the basis of the Japanese colonial martial law, it should also have stated: "pursuant to Article ○ of the (Japanese) Martial Law, martial law is proclaimed for the Cheju area." However, before the Martial Law Act was enacted, the Cheju 4·3 martial law proclaimed by Presidential Decree, as well as the Yŏsu area martial law proclaimed some twenty days earlier, both stated: "This government hereby promulgates the resolution passed by the State Council regarding the declaration of martial law for (the Cheju Island area)..."144) The State Council did not submit a bill; it directly "resolved" and "enacted" it. The fact that the Cheju 4·3 martial law and the Yŏsu area martial law were not actually proclaimed on the basis of Japanese colonial martial law is clarified more explicitly in the National Assembly records. The first National Assembly discussion of martial law occurred right after the first martial law declaration was issued on October 25, 1948, in response to the Yŏsu incident. On October 27, 1948, Assemblyman Cho Hŏn-yŏng (趙憲泳) pointed out that there was talk of a martial law declaration, but why was it not formally reported to the National Assembly?146) In response, the next day Defense Minister Lee Bum-suk stated: "Regarding the local situation, a martial law declaration was issued by Presidential Decree in the Yŏsu and Sunch'ŏn areas to facilitate the handling of the incident. Therefore, the military was made responsible for incident management, and the direction of military and police forces was entrusted to the military."147) He acknowledged the fact of the martial law declaration.

[^143]: Official Gazette, no. 383, July 8, 1950.

[^144]: Official Gazette, no. 7, October 25, 1948; Official Gazette, no. 10, November 17, 1948.

[^145]: The Yŏsu area martial law, announced as Presidential Decree no. 13, was proclaimed on October 25, 1948 (Official Gazette, no. 10, October 25, 1948), but martial law had already been proclaimed by local commanders even before that, and such was reported in the press, so the term "martial law" was being carelessly circulated. On this, see Kim Deuk-joong, "Syngman Rhee Government's Understanding of the Yŏsu Incident and the People's Suffering," Materials on the Yŏsu Incident (Yŏsŭ Local History Research Institute, 1999).

[^146]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 89, October 27, 1948.

[^147]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 90, October 28, 1948.

**[p. 285]**

The discussion concerning martial law intensified further on October 30. The assemblymen who had confirmed the fact of the martial law declaration through the Defense Minister raised more substantive problems. Assemblymen Cho Hŏn-yŏng and Kim Yak-su (金若水) repeatedly criticized the declaration of martial law without following National Assembly approval procedures.148) While Assemblymen Cho Hŏn-yŏng and Kim Yak-su pointed out the "procedural issue" of National Assembly approval, on the same day Assemblyman Yu Sŏng-gap (柳聖甲) raised a more fundamental question concerning the legal basis for the declaration of martial law. Constitutional Article 64 stipulates that "the President shall proclaim martial law as prescribed by law," but there is no Martial Law Act yet—this was his point.149) Such an objection erupted intensively three days later, on November 2. On that day, Assemblyman Ch'oe Kuk-hyŏn (崔國鉉) asked Justice Minister Yi In (李仁): "This time a martial law declaration was issued, but on which article of the Constitution was this martial law declaration based? If it was Article 57 or Article 67, then it is nothing but the kind of martial law the Japanese Emperor issues."150) The flustered Justice Minister Yi In answered nonsensically: "The declaration of martial law is not something the President does, but rather something that the local military commander puts into effect."151) As we have seen, the Yŏsu area martial law was proclaimed by the President on October 25, 1948, with the President and State Council members in attendance. Moreover, all State Council members, including Justice Minister Yi In himself, affixed their signatures to this martial law. Furthermore, Defense Minister Lee Bum-suk had already appeared before the National Assembly and acknowledged that martial law had been proclaimed by Presidential Decree. Yet despite all this, Justice Minister Yi In answered that martial law had been proclaimed "by the local military commander." As a Justice Minister who knew the law better than anyone, he told a lie out of embarrassment. In response, Assemblyman Kim Byŏng-hoe (金秉會) pointed out that the Martial Law Act had not yet been enacted and countered that the local military commander's martial law declaration was unconstitutional. When Assemblyman Kim Byŏng-hoe thus pressed with reference to Constitutional Article 64, Justice Minister Yi In finally acknowledged: "I am truly sorry to say so, but in any case, our country has not yet enacted something called a Martial Law Act."151) He clearly admitted the absence of a Martial Law Act, contradicting his own earlier statement in the same place that "the local commander put the martial law declaration into effect in accordance with the Martial Law Act." However, Justice Minister Yi In told yet another lie.

[^148]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 92, October 30, 1948.

[^149]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 92, October 30, 1948.

[^150]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 94, November 2, 1948.

[^151]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 94, November 2, 1948.

**[p. 286]**

Minister Yi stated: "Martial law is something that the local military commander puts into effect in urgent times, and without any cessation of administrative authority or transfer of judicial authority, it is merely an emergency measure to prevent disorder."152) Recognizing that the "martial law declared by the President in the absence of a Martial Law Act" was unconstitutional, he shifted responsibility to the local military commander and deliberately minimized the significance of martial law.

### d. The Lay Waste Campaign in the Upland Villages

#### 1) Background of the Lay Waste Campaign

Having completed preparations for severe operations through the establishment of the Cheju Island Defense Headquarters on October 11, 1948, and the declaration of martial law on November 17, the suppression forces implemented severe suppression operations, burning all evacuated upland villages to the ground and firing on all civilians, regardless of age or gender, without distinction. However, martial law was so ambiguous in its character that even Martial Law Commander Song Yo-ch'an asked: "What exactly is this martial law supposed to do?" Moreover, through his answer in the National Assembly, Justice Minister Yi In made the absurd statement that martial law is put into effect by the local military commander, and that for this reason administrative matters are not suspended, nor are matters such as judicial authority. Such confusion was an inevitable consequence of the failure to enact the Martial Law Act. Presidential Decree no. 31, which proclaimed the martial law, contained only vague language stating: "In order to quickly suppress the rebellion in Cheju Island, the said area is hereby designated a martial law zone, and martial law shall be enforced from the date of promulgation of this ordinance. The Martial Law Commander shall be the commander of the 9th Regiment stationed in Cheju Island"—it contained no other provision whatsoever. Fourteen days after the martial law declaration, the United States military informed the Chief of Staff of the Defense Ministry about the contents of the martial law declaration, but what those contents were remains unclear. However, looking at the events that actually unfolded in Cheju under the name of martial law, it appears the contents were not even properly conveyed or observed. A civilized nation could not possibly have martial law provisions ordering the indiscriminate massacre of unarmed civilians from eighty-year-old grandmothers to nursing infants.152) The only prior experience was that soldiers who had served as Japanese troops or in the Kwantung Army had experience massacring Chinese people. In September 1948, while a court-martial was underway concerning the assassination of Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng, one newspaper reported concerning the testimony of an accused soldier that "Colonel Pak Chin-gyŏng massacred numerous innocent civilians," stating: "The wanton massacres inflicted by the Japanese Army against non-combatant Chinese civilians during the Sino-Japanese War are something we know all too well, and one can surmise what sort of history this might unfold in future Sino-Japanese relations, let alone in a domestic incident like this."153) Indeed, Song Yo-ch'an, the 9th Regiment commander who led the severe suppression operations, and Ham Byŏng-sŏn, the 2nd Regiment commander, were both former volunteer officers under Japanese rule. However, even if there had been experience with massacre in China, it would not have been something a field military commander could arbitrarily carry out—burning all upland villages to the ground and massacring one's own people, regardless of age or gender, without trial, under the name of martial law. With regard to this matter, government documents such as National Council records clarify the situation well. The National Council records, which had not been previously known, were collected from the Government Archives Management Service under the Ministry of Government Administration and Home Affairs.154) A statement made at the National Council meeting on January 21, 1949, clearly demonstrates the will of President Syngman Rhee:

"Instructions on General State Matters (by the President) = The United States recognizes the importance of Korea and expresses much sympathy, but it is necessary to completely eliminate and root out the aftermath of the Cheju Island and South Chŏlla incidents so that their aid will be activated, and it is requested that local banditry, rebellion, and theft be repressed through severe methods to demonstrate the majesty of law."155)

This was an instruction to use severe methods of repression to achieve a speedy resolution of the Cheju 4·3 incident. Such an instruction from President Syngman Rhee was immediately transmitted down to the field prosecutorial organizations and concretely realized. Specifically, on January 26, the Justice Minister sent an official letter to the Prosecutor General conveying the President's intent,156) and the Prosecutor General transmitted it the very next day, January 27, to the Chief Prosecutors of each High Prosecutor's Office, District Prosecutor's Office, and Prosecutor's Branch Office,157) and on February 21 to each Prosecutor's Branch Office Chief.158) The Prosecutor General then, on February 23, sent an official letter to each Prosecutor's Office under his command, criticizing them for being excessively lenient in handling leftist cases and issuing an order for severe punishment.159)

[^152]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 94, November 2, 1948.

[^153]: Chosŏn Ilbo, September 9, 1948.

[^154]: Unfortunately, the Government Archives Management Service does not have National Council records from 1948; it holds them beginning from 1949.

[^155]: National Council Records, January 21, 1949.

[^156]: Attached legal-prosecution confidential letter no. 439 (January 26, 1949) "Regarding the Notification of Presidential Instruction," General Notification no. 41 "Internal Affairs and Justice-Related Matters," Records on Prosecutorial Regulations.

[^157]: Chief Prosecutor confidential letter no. 23 (January 27, 1949) "Regarding the Notification of Presidential Instruction," Records on Prosecutorial Regulations.

[^158]: District Prosecutor letter no. 18 (February 21, 1949) "Regarding the Notification of Presidential Instruction," Records on Prosecutorial Regulations.

[^159]: Chief Prosecutor confidential letter no. 114 (February 23, 1949) "Regarding the Handling of Leftist Cases," Legal Ministry-Related Documents.

**[p. 287]**

However, no matter how much the National Security Law or other statutes were mobilized, there was no basis anywhere for "repression through severe methods." Even in wartime, one cannot indiscriminately massacre unarmed civilians without trial. To accomplish this, President Syngman Rhee made use of martial law. Although no Martial Law Act had been enacted and the effectiveness of the martial law declaration itself was unclear, at that time martial law was regarded by military, police, and civilians alike as something through which "the military exercises unlimited authority."160) In relation to this, Prime Minister and Defense Minister Lee Bum-suk reported to the National Assembly on December 8, 1948: "Through the implementation of martial law, we are seeing a rapid improvement in the situation."161) However, martial law had already been lifted as of December 31, 1948, so by 1949 the state was no longer one of martial law. Nevertheless, the fact of the martial law lifting was not widely known to the public. On February 5, Kim Tong-sŏng, Director of the Korean Public Information Office, announced that the state of emergency for the Yŏsu area and South Chŏlla region declared on October 25, and for the Chiri Mountain area, was being lifted. He also stated that the state of emergency for the Cheju Island area proclaimed on November 17, 1948, had been lifted one month earlier, but there had been no official prior mention of its effect.162) The fact that the Cheju Island martial law was lifted while its effect was intended to continue is revealed through the testimony of military personnel at the time. Chŏn Pu-il (全富一), who served as a battalion commander of the 2nd Regiment stationed in Cheju in 1949, testified: "I thought martial law continued to be in effect."163) Even Kim Yong-ju (金龍周), commander of the Independent Battalion who began stationing in Cheju from July 1949, said: "At that time I was appointed as the Martial Law Commander of the Cheju Island area."164) Moreover, a National Assembly member who returned after inspecting Cheju after the martial law had been lifted said: "When I returned, I heard that martial law had been reinstituted,"165) revealing that a "martial law that was not martial law" continued to be implemented.

Meanwhile, President Syngman Rhee's instruction that "only by suppressing the Cheju 4·3 incident completely through severe methods can the United States, which recognizes Korea's importance, provide active aid" hints that the severe suppression operations unfolded in consonance with the United States. In the context of intensifying U.S.-Soviet Cold War, American determination to construct a bulwark against communism in Asia was reflected in this. When the suppression operations were completed and police forces returned, Prime Minister Lee Bum-suk said in his welcome address: "Your achievements—the complete suppression of Cheju Island—represent not only great loyalty to the Republic of Korea but also great achievement in defending Southeast Asia and the Pacific from communist dictatorship."166) While "achievement for Southeast Asia and the Pacific" in reference to suppression of the Cheju incident sounds somewhat exaggerated, the following National Council record shows United States Cold War policy toward Korea linked to Syngman Rhee's request for aid:

"Reporting Matter (by President) Regarding instructions on general state matters = Regarding the statement of a U.S. Senate member calling for anti-communist conditional aid to Korea, it is appropriate for the Foreign Minister to send a note of thanks. Reporting Matter (Public Information) Regarding press briefing = The U.S. House Foreign Relations Committee passed 150 million dollars but with anti-communist conditions."167)

That the U.S.-Soviet Cold War brought about the tragedy of the Cheju 4·3 incident was a common understanding of the contemporary press. After introducing the catastrophe of Cheju, one newspaper stated: "Foreign journalists refer to this incident as the most interesting 'Marshall and Molotov test site,' 'Sino-Soviet clash site,' 'miniature version of the 38th parallel,' and there are instances where they have written of this place, Cheju Island, with its tearful actual conditions using every journalistic means, calling it here and there..."168) and pointed out that "(Cheju Island) has become a new testing ground as a bulwark against communism in the Far East." Another newspaper also analyzed that the Cheju incident was "less a pioneer battleground of the anti-communist struggle than rather a testing ground."169)

[^160]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 124, December 8, 1948.

[^161]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1056, February 5, 1949.

[^162]: Chŏn Pu-il (Kuseong-eup, Yongin-si, Gyeonggi-do, 2nd Regiment 1st Battalion Commander at the time, recorded September 24, 2002) Testimony.

[^163]: Kim Yong-ju (45 years old, 1st Independent Battalion Commander at the time, recorded February 16, 1967) Testimony (as recorded in the Participant Testimony Records held by the National Institute of Military History).

[^164]: Chosŏn Ilbo, January 12, 1949.

[^165]: Jayou Shinmun, May 19, 1949.

[^166]: National Council Records, Session 63, July 1, 1949.

[^167]: Chosŏn Jungang Ilbo, September 1, 1949.

[^168]: Seoul Shinmun, September 1, 1949.

**[p. 288]**

#### 2) Press Control and Coastal Blockade

Before implementing the severe operations, the military controlled the press and blockaded the coastline, isolating Cheju Island. As a result, whatever occurred on Cheju Island became unknown to the outside world. This resulted in an inability to check reckless suppression operations. The Cheju coastline had already been blockaded from October 18, a month before the martial law declaration. The Navy mobilized seven warships and 203 sailors to cut off Cheju's coast.169) From this point onward, Cheju Island became an isolated and helpless island. Meanwhile, conflict between the press and suppression authorities was inevitable as severe operations developed. Kim Ki-o (金基五), a journalist with the Cheju Shinbo at the time, testified to his experience in this way:

"It was during the time when Ch'oe Ch'ŏn was Superintendent of Surveillance, when one day I was sent to cover a suppression area. I went to the scene with Colonel Song Yo-ch'an and Captain Lee, a second-generation Korean at G-2 (U.S. Military Intelligence). But among those who had been executed as so-called bandits, there was, surprisingly, an acquaintance of mine. I immediately wrote the story and had it published in the newspaper. Then I immediately received a protest from Superintendent Ch'oe Ch'ŏn asking: 'How could you publish such false information sowing discord between civilians and police?' Moreover, Tong T'ae-un, a surveillance officer from the North, threatened me: 'You, what is your connection to the Halla Mountain bandits? Your ideology is suspicious,' and locked me up in a detention cell. I was released after protesting, but then another incident occurred. Police from the Oedo Station brought in a civilian who turned up dead and was left on a street. I wrote about this as well. Then rumor reached me that the police had held a meeting to decide to eliminate me. Park Un-bong, a sergeant from Aewol Elementary School and Cheju Agricultural School, was worried about me and gave me a hint to be careful. Fearing for my life, I left for Seoul that very day. This was around July 1948."170)

Though Kim Ki-o barely escaped Cheju and avoided disaster, the persecution of the Cheju Shinbo, the only newspaper published in Cheju Island, continued. On July 22, 1948, the Cheju Police Agency's Special Investigation Unit arrested journalists from the Cheju Shinbo on the charge of "organizing the National Salvation Struggle Committee and carrying out underground activities."171) The arrested journalist received a fine and was released, but the Cheju Shinbo had to suspend publication for some time due to the arrest of journalists and other employees.

Nevertheless, news of the Cheju incident was being treated seriously in various central newspapers. On September 15, 1948, several central newspapers simultaneously reported the following:

"[From Cheju] A shocking incident occurred in which a boy barely thirteen years old was tortured to death on the grounds of being a member of a mob. A boy named Jin In-su (13), residing in Samyang-ri, Cheju-up, was pointed out as a mountain mob member, dragged to the local police station on the 7th, beaten with clubs by Police Chief Min Yong-gi (閔用基) and two patrolmen, and died the early morning of the next day (8th). Upon receiving this report, the Cheju Prosecutor's Office dispatched a prosecutor to the scene to conduct an autopsy, and immediately began activities, arresting the two patrolmen who assisted in the torture while urgently searching for the whereabouts of the station chief who had fled."172)

When this shocking content about a thirteen-year-old boy beaten to death was published in central newspapers, the suppressed authorities became increasingly anxious and began to control the press more intensely. Around October 1948, journalists in Cheju-up were dragged off one after another to military units. Hyŏn In-ha (玄仁廈), director of the Kyŏnghyang Shinmun Cheju branch, was said to have been a student conscript during the war and briefly served with the police after liberation. Having established a branch in Cheju around December 1947 and even posted a resident correspondent, the Kyŏnghyang Shinmun was apparently quite influential in Cheju at the time.173) One witness stated: "Hyŏn In-ha was an intellectual good with English and good with commerce, so the Kyŏnghyang Shinmun sold very well. It was all Kyŏnghyang Shinmun."174) Another witness spoke of the reason Hyŏn In-ha was taken away: "When the Military Police Corps tried to investigate the Kyŏnghyang Shinmun, Hyŏn In-ha resisted and drove them out. The Military Police Corps held a grudge about this and, when the situation became dangerous, abducted him and executed him."175) Subsequently, Lee Sang-hi (李尙熹), director of the Seoul Shinmun Cheju branch, was taken away and became a victim. By November 1948, when the indiscriminate suppression operations had only just begun, a full pre-publication censorship system was put into effect. On November 20, the Defense Ministry, under the pretext of protecting military operations and military secrets and ensuring accuracy of reporting, announced pre-publication censorship of military-related articles.176) Despite public opinion that this was press persecution, the Defense Ministry reiterated its will to control reporting on November 22: "This is for the sake of unified and accurate reporting and in no way press suppression or interference with freedom of the press, which must be preserved."177) In fact, thereafter military-related articles carried the notation "passed censorship" or "censorship completed."

Thus, with the Cheju coastline blockaded and the press controlled, Cheju Island became, literally, an isolated and helpless island. How isolated Cheju was from the outside world and how much it was outside the public's attention can be seen through the National Assembly's deliberations. The first National Assembly discussion of the Cheju incident occurred on June 11, 1948, when Assemblyman O Yong-guk (吳龍國) from Cheju and five others submitted an urgent motion requesting "the establishment of a temporary special committee to urgently address the Cheju incident." However, this urgent motion was not taken up because the proposer's numbers were insufficient. At that time, Assemblyman Ch'oe Kuk-hyŏn opposed the urgent motion itself, stating: "The Cheju Island problem that Assemblyman O just mentioned is indeed serious. However, if we were to take up this problem, I understand that there are many other serious problems coming to the capital, such as the power problem and barley extraction."178) The seriousness of the Cheju incident was not recognized, treated as on the same level as Seoul's power problem.

[^169]: "Report on the Internal Insurrections after April, 1948, made by Minister of National Defence, Lee Bum Suk," December 14, 1948, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group(1948-1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group(1949-1953), Box 11; National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 124, December 8, 1948; International Shinmun, December 9, 1948.

[^170]: Kim Ki-o (79 years old, Yangch'ŏn-gu, Sŏngjeong 2-dong, Seoul, Cheju Shinbo journalist at the time, recorded February 1, 2002) Testimony.

[^171]: Chosŏn Jungang Ilbo, August 8, 11, and 18, 1948.

[^172]: Seoul Shinmun, Chosŏn Ilbo, Chosŏn Jungang Ilbo, Hanseong Ilbo, September 15, 1948.

[^173]: Cheju Shinbo, December 6, 1947.

[^174]: Ch'oe Kil-tu (Topyŏng-dong, Cheju-si, Government Property Management Office employee at the time) Testimony (as recorded in Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, 4·3 Speaks ④, 1997, p. 165).

[^175]: Kang Sun-hyŏn (Yongdam 2-dong, Cheju-si, Government Property Management Office employee at the time) Testimony (as recorded in Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., p. 154).

**[p. 289]**

On September 27, Assemblyman Kim Ung-jin (金雄鎭) raised concerns, stating: "The Cheju Island incident unfolding south of the 38th parallel—this conspiracy to treat all those living in Cheju as communists and turn all civilians there into criminals—I believe this will greatly disrupt our internal security in the future."179) However, this was merely mentioned in passing while pursuing questions about the government's obstruction of the Anti-Collaboration Act, not a full discussion of the Cheju incident itself. When bloody tragedy was unfolding in Cheju, the National Assembly's discussions of Cheju revealed how isolated and outside public attention Cheju had become.

On October 7, a controversy began when Assemblyman Cho Kuk-hyŏn (曺國鉉) advocated at the first reading of the "Local Administration Organization Act" for the restoration of Cheju Island (濟州島), which had been elevated to the status of province (道) in August 1946, back to island status. This controversy fully developed at the second reading on October 13. At the second reading, Assemblyman Cho Kuk-hyŏn took the lead in opposing elevation to province status, arguing: "If Cheju Island were to be elevated to the status of province, then all other islands, whether large or small, such as Ulleung Island or Tsushima Island, would also have to be made provinces."181) Ultimately, after arguments for and against by various assemblymen, Cheju Island retained its status as a province by a single vote margin. On October 11, the Cheju Island Defense Headquarters was established, and as the Cheju incident moved toward a bloody crisis, the National Assembly's debate on whether it should be island (島) or province (道) symbolically represented the image of Cheju Island, cut off from the outside world.

[^179]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 75, September 27, 1948.

#### 3) Arson and Evacuation of Upland Villages

**[p. 290]**

Severe suppression operations began to develop in mid-November 1948. Though indiscriminate massacres had occurred in scattered locations before this time, "from mid-November onward" shows a clear difference from the preceding period in both intensity and scale of casualties and in that such operations were carried out simultaneously across all areas. It was precisely at this time that martial law was declared on Cheju Island. During the approximately four-month period from November 1948 to February 1949, when severe suppression operations were unfolding, most upland villages were consumed by fire, and Cheju Island was, in effect, laid waste. In particular, whereas from before mid-November mainly young males were victims, during the severe suppression operations the suppression forces massacred residents regardless of age or gender, so that the majority of the Cheju 4·3 incident victims died during this period. Looking at victim statistics reported to this Commission, 76.5 percent of all child victims under fifteen years of age were killed during the period from November 1948 to February 1949. Moreover, among victims sixty-one years of age and older, 76.6 percent were killed during this period.

Kim Chŏng-mu (金貞武), who did not directly participate in the suppression operations due to his position but stood in the thick of the severe suppression operations as Quartermaster of the 9th Regiment, testified about operations during this period: "At that time the term 'scorched-earth campaign' was used, meaning to sweep everything clean. Since people were present in the upland villages and the armed forces in the mountains received support from them, the concept was to separate them. In other words, the operational concept was that people there were the enemy."182) The armed forces destroyed the upland villages as bases for the armed guerrillas. Yun Tae-jun (尹太準), a senior staff sergeant of the 9th Regiment, testified: "Regiment Commander Song Yo-ch'an carried out a scorched-earth campaign. He ordered: eliminate places where people could live, or burn them down. This kind of thing did not happen even on the mainland. Whether the scorched-earth campaign was an order from above or the regiment commander acting on his own authority, I don't know, but Regiment Commander Song Yo-ch'an, a former Japanese military officer, killed people without mercy."183)

Regarding such reckless operations, on July 28, 1949, when the situation was almost concluded, the U.S. Military Advisory Group sent a letter to Brigadier General Mullins (C. L. Mullins Jr.), commander of the 2nd Army Command in Maryland, noting: "The expansion of resistance by the inhabitants on Cheju Island was partly due to incompetent leadership of police and military. Like communists, they ruthlessly murdered village residents and set villages on fire."184)

However, in materials produced by military and police authorities, the massacre of unarmed civilians was completely ignored, and instead it was recorded as if, during this period, there had been exchanges of fire with armed guerrillas and great achievements had been recorded. The following material is highly suggestive in this regard:

"The regiment commander stationed on Cheju Island reported capturing 122 rebels alive from November 20 to November 27, and killing 576. Between October 1 and November 20, 1,625 were reported killed and 1,383 captured. Many items were captured, but only a few Japanese-style rifles were obtained as weapons. A total of some 60 weapons were captured over two months."185)

Looking at daily intelligence reports of the U.S. Forces Command in Korea for this period, there appear such records of achievement as "'November 13: As a result of Constabulary operations, 115 armed guerrillas killed at Haengwŏn-ri, Kucha-myŏn"186) or "'November 24: In fighting near Nohyŏng-ri, Cheju-up, 79 armed guerrillas killed,"'187) yet there is not a single casualty reported on the suppression forces' side. Such content appeared repeatedly in domestic press reports. That is, "'From November 21 for two days, the Halla Mountain area near Pungmun-myŏn, South Cheju County, was mopped up, killing 88 bandits, with no losses on the army side"'188) or "'On November 14 at Nohyŏng-ri, 79 bandits were killed, with no losses on the army side"'189) appeared in reports. The common feature in press reports that went through Defense Ministry pre-publication censorship was, again, "many enemy killed, no army losses." Such highly unbalanced and therefore unbelievable achievements became even more starkly apparent in Prime Minister Lee Bum-suk's National Assembly report on December 8, 1948. At this session, Prime Minister Lee reported achievements for the nine-month period from April to December 1948, and the contents show that against "enemy abandoned corpses" of 421 and "prisoners" of 5,719, army losses were 3 killed and 8 wounded.190) It is a commonly observed phenomenon in guerrilla warfare that the suppressing side sustains greater casualties. Yet despite allegedly killing and capturing many armed guerrillas, no weapons were captured, and although fighting allegedly took place, there were virtually no deaths among the Constabulary and only armed guerrillas were killed. Such reports indicate that rather than through combat between suppression forces and armed guerrillas, unarmed civilians were massacred.

[^180]: Kim Chŏng-mu (77 years old, Puam-dong, Chongno-gu, Seoul, 9th Regiment Quartermaster at the time, Brigadier General (ret.), 2002, September 25, recorded) Testimony.

[^181]: Yun Tae-jun (73 years old, Chŏnan-dong, Tŏngdaemun-gu, Seoul, 9th Regiment Supply Section Senior Staff Sergeant at the time, Colonel (ret.), 2001, May 2, recorded) Testimony.

[^182]: "Letter from Roberts to Mullins," July 28, 1949, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group(1949-1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group(1949-1953), Box 8.

[^183]: "Report from PMAG to USAFIK," December 1, 1948, ibid., Box 4.

[^184]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 989, November 16, 1948.

[^185]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 998, November 27, 1948.

**[p. 291]**

The suspicion that military and police suppression forces massacred unarmed civilians on a large scale while falsifying the victims as armed guerrillas becomes clear when comparing the numbers of "bandits" reported by military and police authorities with their "achievements":

**[p. 292]**

△April 1948—Number of bandits approximately 300-400 (Police Administration Bureau Public Information Director)191)
△April 1948—Number of bandits not exceeding 300 in the entire island (Brigadier General Kim Ik-ryŏl, 9th Regiment Commander)192)
△December 1948—Armed bandits now number only 50-60 (Prime Minister's report)193)
△January 1949—Armed bandits number 150-400 (Interior Minister's report)194)
△March 1949—Armed bandits approximately 200 (Prime Minister's report)195)
△March 1949—Armed bandits 150-600 (Interior Minister's report)196)

Despite continuing announcements of achievement by the suppression forces following April 3, 1948, the number of "bandits" did not decrease. The press had already pointed out: "The number of the People's Liberation Army (rebels) has consistently been said to be 500, but despite the competent authorities' reports of suppression achievements on several occasions, the number of the enemy's main force is still said to be around 500; is this 500-person figure truly a mystery?"197) However, military and police suppression authorities and government leaders denied severe suppression operations and did not keep records of them. Even U.S. military reports, which had previously recorded even minor details, left almost no records during the severe suppression campaign period. The following U.S. military report is one of the rare records relating to the severe suppression operations and gives us a sense of the situation at the time:

"Sanch'ŏn—At 12:45 p.m. on November 19, 1948, approximately 10 Constabulary soldiers set fire to 4 houses (F-1). At 1 p.m., the U.S. military spotted Constabulary soldiers in the village from an L-5 airplane. At that time, the village was burning (A-2)."198)

There are also reports of massacres in addition to arson:

"January 25, 1949, Ora-ri, Cheju Island. A U.S. military advisor to the 2nd Regiment of the Korean Army found 97 corpses of men, women, and children. Each corpse had been struck by 5-6 bullets. Based on empty clips and shell casings, it appears M-1 rifles were used. However, police and military denied involvement in the incident."199)

There is also a report of direct eyewitness observation of killing:

"February 20, 1949, Todu-ri: 76 inhabitants were killed by spear stabs by the Civil Guard. Among the casualties were 5 women and many children of middle school age. Police and military police (Korean Army Military Police) supervised the operation. Commentary: When 4 U.S. military advisors arrived, 38 had already been executed and 38 executions were witnessed by chance."200)

As revealed in U.S. military reports, the severe suppression operations consisted of arson against upland villages and massacres of residents of all ages and genders without distinction. The suppression forces' initial operational concept was to evacuate upland village residents to coastal villages, establish a resident monitoring system in coastal villages, and thereby eliminate the armed guerrillas' bases. Prime Minister and Defense Minister Lee Bum-suk explained this operation as follows in his National Assembly report:

"a. Enemy Situation: Due to the national army's fierce annihilation campaign, food shortage, and insufficient supply of weapons and ammunition, as well as the decrease in collaborators, armed bandits now number merely 50-60. However, it is truly regrettable that numerous Cheju Island residents are mentally supporting the bandits.

b. The 9th Regiment soldiers secure key points throughout Cheju Island and resettle civilian residents in upland hamlets to coastal cities to implement the baojia [保甲] system [i.e., collective responsibility system], fell trees on both sides of roads throughout the island to prepare for bandit surprise attacks, completely cut off supply lines to bandits, and carry out thorough elimination of those collaborating with bandits, thereby devoting themselves to rebuilding Cheju."201)

Thus, the evacuation order was implemented on the premise that numerous Cheju Island residents were "mentally supporting the bandits." Under this system, residents were collectively relocated and then a collective responsibility resident monitoring system called the "baojia system" was established to separate ordinary residents from armed guerrillas. In relation to this, Yi Ki-bong (李基鳳), a senior staff sergeant of the 9th Regiment at the time, testified: "All people in isolated mountain areas were ordered to come down to coastal areas, and anyone who was not voluntarily brought down was recognized as a communist."202)

However, the suppression forces attacked some upland villages before evacuation orders were fully carried out, set houses on fire, and began massacring residents. Moreover, for some upland villages to which evacuation orders had been delivered, even those residents who had been evacuated to coastal villages were, if even a single family member had gone missing, killed as "deserter families." Pu Sŏng-bang (夫成邦), who lost twelve family members including his mother and young children due to his own hiding, lamented: "What is wrongful and heartbreaking is that among our daughters, the most mature was only twelve years old, and below her were children of ten, eight, and six years old. What crime did these children commit to be killed?"203)

[^188]: Chosŏn Ilbo, November 26, 1948.

[^189]: Independent Shinmun, November 27, 1948.

[^190]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 124, December 8, 1948; Hanseong Ilbo, December 10, 1948.

[^191]: Chosŏn Ilbo, May 6, 1948.

[^192]: Kim Ik-ryŏl, "The Truth of Cheju 4·3" (as recorded in Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit. ②, 1994, p. 299).

[^193]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 124, December 8, 1948.

[^194]: National Council Records, Session 10, January 17, 1949.

[^195]: National Council Records, Session 30, March 16, 1949.

[^196]: National Assembly Records, Session 2, no. 59, March 21, 1949.

[^197]: Chosŏn Ilbo editorial, date unspecified in source.

[^198]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1102, November 19, 1948.

[^199]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1055, February 4, 1949.

[^200]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1077, March 3, 1949.

[^201]: National Assembly Records, Session 1, no. 124, December 8, 1948.

[^202]: Yi Ki-bong (40 years old, Sŏngju-gun, North Gyeongsang Province, 9th Regiment Senior Staff Sergeant at the time, Colonel (ret.), March 15, 1966, recorded) Testimony (as recorded in Participant Testimony Records held by the National Institute of Military History).

[^203]: Pu Sŏng-bang (86 years old, Chochen-ri, Chochen-eup, December 12, 2002, recorded) Testimony.

**[p. 293]**

Such operations drove residents to hide in the mountains, resulting in numerous additional civilian casualties and prolonging the incident. Later, a U.S. military advisor pointed out that the military's indiscriminate killing was in fact driving residents to join the armed guerrillas:

"(1) Liaison aircraft are dropping propaganda leaflets and indiscriminately dropping hand grenades and mortar shells.
(2) The superior attitude of Korean Army soldiers and local police toward local residents appears to be an unnecessary factor that stimulates peaceful citizens to engage in bandit activities.
(3) Provisional Military Advisory Group personnel are unnecessarily exposed to danger for the following reasons: numerical inferiority and lack of communication, reliance entirely on the Korean Army for safety, poor environment and clearly evident civilian executions without trial that continuously provoke the mood in various villages, as well as violations of civil rights by soldiers who have not received appropriate training and discipline."204)

After the evacuation order, the suppression forces treated those hiding in upland villages or mountainous areas as enemies and killed them on the spot if discovered. However, examining the circumstances of those who could not endure the lengthy life in hiding and "surrendered," the reckless nature of the severe suppression operations becomes starkly evident. Meanwhile, around the middle of December 1948, the original operational concept was distorted, becoming an even greater cause of civilian casualties. That is, the original operational plan—to achieve the extermination of armed forces by evacuating upland village residents to coastal areas to cut their connection with the armed guerrillas—was distorted into "racking up achievements." A U.S. military report dated December 17, 1948, assesses: "The recent suppression operations of the 9th Regiment continue to be successful," and identifies one reason as "a desire to carry out high-level operations and a desire to match the outstanding achievements of the successful 2nd Regiment."205)

The 9th Regiment was scheduled to be replaced by the 2nd Regiment from Daejeon, which had achieved distinction in suppressing the Yŏsu Rebellion, at the end of December 1948. Before the 9th Regiment departed Cheju, it wanted to achieve results that would stand up to the accomplishments of suppressing the Yŏsu Rebellion, and the report notes that the expression of this desire led to successfully conducting the suppression operations. In fact, looking at the 9th Regiment's "achievements" around mid-December 1948, there was the impression of eager "body-count politics." Typical examples of this include the so-called "surrogate killings," "confession incidents," "trap suppression," and the indiscriminate massacring of residents hiding in the mountains regardless of age or gender.

First, massacres were carried out under the name of "surrogate killings." Suppression forces would gather people—whether those evacuated from upland villages or originally residing in coastal villages—and kill as "deserter families" those people from families in which a young person had gone missing. On December 13, 1948, 48 residents of Sangmo-ri and Hamo-ri in Taejeong-myŏn were massacred as "deserter families." In this village, after residents were gathered, they were made to watch the massacre, so this incident is also called "spectator massacre." Among the victims was a 73-year-old grandmother.206)

Second, "confession incidents" involved massacring people who had confessed to past crimes. The suppression forces would gather residents and threaten them: "Anyone who has done anything wrong in the past should confess. If you confess, you will be spared, but if you are discovered later, you cannot escape being shot. We already have a list of those involved." The fact that suppression was encouraged itself contradicted the claim of already having a "list of those involved," yet many frightened residents confessed. They primarily confessed to activities in the Committee for the Preparation of Korean Independence or People's Committees immediately after liberation, participation in demonstrations in protest of police firing on March 1, 1947, or provision of food and other supplies in response to demands by armed guerrillas when they had control of the village. However, contrary to promises, the suppression forces massacred confessors in groups. A typical case of a "confession incident" is the so-called "Park Sŏng-nae incident." Having been holding 200 people who had confessed within Chochen-myŏn, on December 21, 1948, suppression forces told 150 of them to "get in the vehicles for those following the suppression," and massacred them in a group at a stream called "Park Sŏng-nae" in Cheju-up.207)

**[p. 294]**

The so-called "trap suppression" is an example of reckless suppression operations in which the suppression forces were eager to pile up achievements. The typical form of trap suppression was when suppression forces dressed in armed guerrilla uniforms, entered civilian houses, and massacred those who responded to requests for cooperation. Those who lived through that time commonly said there was a phrase: "Both bears and tigers were frightening"—meaning that both suppression forces and armed guerrillas were frightening. During the day, suppression forces controlled villages and massacred those they called "bandit suspects"; at night, after suppression forces withdrew, armed guerrillas would appear and "purge reactionaries." In such circumstances, ordinary residents could not refuse the demands of armed guerrillas. Yet suppression forces employed traps as a means of identifying armed guerrilla supporters. Trap suppression occurred throughout the island, and the mass death of residents of Topyŏng-ri in Cheju-up is a typical example of trap suppression. In the early morning of January 3, 1949, a group dressed in shabby civilian clothes (gal junge) and carrying guns arrived in Topyŏng-ri, Cheju-up. They greeted residents they encountered on the street with "Comrade, comrade" and offered their hands to shake. Some were carrying red flags. They rushed into houses and demanded of residents: "Why aren't you supporting those in the mountains?" gathering residents in a school athletic field. However, this group carrying red flags and dressed in civilian clothes were not armed guerrillas but rather police from the nearby Oedo Station and special forces soldiers who had set a trap for residents. Some residents, noticing familiar faces in the group, made every effort to avoid the trap. Yang Kyŏng-ha (梁京河, 22) stepped forward saying: "If you're communists, I'll fight you." Kim Byŏng-hae (金秉海, 58), a village elder, cursed at Kim Yŏng-ch'ŏl (金永哲), the chief of Oedo Station, and shouted "Long Live the Republic of Korea!" However, around 70 residents including these individuals were massacred. One witness testified: "An elderly man over sixty named Kim Sŏng-gyu was identified at the school athletic field, and he, his wife, son, daughter-in-law, and young grandchild—a total of 6 people—were all massacred."208)

Meanwhile, those hiding in caves or forests in mountainous areas to escape indiscriminate suppression operations were found, and all residents regardless of age or gender were massacred. However, most of these individuals were, just as President Syngman Rhee's statement mentioned, "people deceived by communist propaganda or those whose homes had been burned down and who had nowhere to go and went up the mountains." After the situation eased, they "came down with men and women and children who could hardly be described as such."209) Those among them who, despite being chased here and there through the depths of winter, managed to preserve their lives until the situation eased could come down the mountain and survive. However, during the height of the severe suppression operations, anyone discovered was immediately massacred.

The unearthing of eleven skeletal remains at "Tarang-shui Cave" in the upland areas of Kucha-eup, Cheju Island, in 1992 symbolically demonstrates the reckless operations against those in hiding during the Cheju 4·3 incident. Investigation revealed that the owners of these remains were refugees who went into hiding and were killed by 9th Regiment suppression operations on December 18, 1948. Particularly striking is that the victims' identities were residents of Chongdal-ri and Hadori in Kucha-eup, among whom were three women and a nine-year-old child, creating shock.210)

[^204]: "Report of Ordnance Advisor's Trip to 2nd Regt-Cheju," February 10, 1949, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group(1948-1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group(1949-1953), Box 13.

[^205]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1015, December 17, 1948.

[^206]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit. ⑤, pp. 321–322.

[^207]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit. ④, pp. 405–408.

[^208]: Kim Se-wŏn (Topyŏng-dong, Cheju-si, son of victim Kim Byŏng-hae) Testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, "4·3 Speaks," Session 445, May 21, 1999).

[^209]: Chosŏn Jungang Ilbo, April 13, 1949.

[^210]:

**[p. 295]**

These same claims were reported multiple times in the domestic press as well. Reports such as "Over two days beginning November 21, sweep operations were conducted across the slopes of Halla Mountain near Chungmun-myŏn, South Cheju County, killing 88 insurgents, with no losses on the national army side,"[^188] and "On November 14, 79 insurgents were killed at Nohyŏng-ri, with no losses on the national army side,"[^189] were typical. The common thread in press reports that had passed through the Defense Ministry's prior censorship was invariably "large numbers of enemy killed, no losses on our side."

These extraordinarily lopsided and thus difficult-to-believe battle results were laid out even more starkly in Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk's report to the National Assembly on December 8, 1948. At that session, Prime Minister Yi reported the results of nine months of operations from April through December 1948: "enemy bodies abandoned" numbered 421 and "prisoners" 5,719, while losses on the national army side amounted to only 3 killed in action and 8 wounded.[^190]

In guerrilla warfare it is generally accepted that the suppressing side typically suffers heavier casualties. Yet the reports above — claiming that large numbers of armed guerrillas had been killed and captured while no weapons were seized, and that battles had been fought in which almost no security forces died while only guerrillas were killed — reveal that it was not combatants fighting combatants but rather defenseless civilians who were being shot.

The charge that military-police suppression forces massacred unarmed civilians en masse while falsifying the victims as armed guerrillas becomes self-evident when one compares the official "insurgent" counts with the claimed "battle results":

- **April 1948**: Number of insurgents approximately 300 to 400 (Director of Public Information, Home Affairs Ministry)[^191]
- **April 1948**: Number of insurgents does not exceed 300 island-wide (Kim Ik-nyŏl, Commander, 9th Regiment)[^192]
- **December 1948**: Armed insurgents currently no more than 50–60 (Prime Minister's report)[^193]
- **January 1949**: Armed insurgents said to number 150 to 400 (Interior Minister's report)[^194]
- **March 1949**: Armed insurgents approximately 200 (Prime Minister's report)[^195]
- **March 1949**: Armed insurgents 150–600 (Interior Minister's report)[^196]

[^188]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, November 26, 1948.
[^189]: *Tongnip Sinmun* [Independence News], November 27, 1948.
[^190]: *Kukhoesokkirok* [National Assembly Stenographic Record], Session 1, No. 124, December 8, 1948; *Hansŏng Ilbo*, December 10, 1948.
[^191]: *Chosŏn Ilbo*, May 6, 1948.
[^192]: Kim Ik-nyŏl, "The Truth of 4·3" (in Cheminilbo 4·3 Ch'wijaebak, op. cit., vol. 2, 1994, p. 299).
[^193]: *Kukhoesokkirok*, Session 1, No. 124, December 8, 1948.
[^194]: *Kukmuwŭirok* [State Council Minutes], Session 10, January 17, 1949.
[^195]: *Kukmuwŭirok*, Session 30, March 16, 1949.
[^196]: *Kukhoesokkirok*, Session 2, No. 59, March 21, 1949.

**[p. 296]**

Despite the suppression forces' continuous announcements of battle results following April 3, 1948, the press had already noted the puzzling fact that the number of "insurgents" never decreased: "The number of the People's Liberation Army (rebels) has consistently been cited as 500 throughout — yet this figure remains unchanged despite the authorities' repeated announcements of sweep successes, so one must ask: is this figure of 500 truly a riddle?"

Meanwhile, the military-police suppression command and the government's senior leadership denied that they were conducting a severe crackdown, and they left no record of it. The U.S. military reports, which had meticulously recorded even minor details up to that point, left almost no records during the period of harsh suppression operations. The following U.S. military reports are among the rare surviving records related to those operations and give some indication of what was happening at the time.

**Sanchŏndan** — November 19, 1948, 12:45 p.m.: approximately 10 security force members set fire to 4 houses (F-1). At 1:00 p.m. U.S. troops observed from an L-5 aircraft that security force members were present in the village. At that time the village was burning (A-2).[^197]

Beyond arson, there were also reports of mass shootings.

**Ora-ri, Jeju Island, January 25, 1949**: A U.S. military adviser attached to the Korean Army's 2nd Regiment discovered the bodies of 97 men, women, and children. Each body had been shot 5–6 times. Based on empty clips and spent casings, it was believed that M-1 rifles had been used. However, the police and military denied any involvement in the incident.[^198]

There were also reports from those who witnessed killings directly.

**Todu-ri, February 20, 1949**: 76 rebels were killed by bamboo spears wielded by Civil Defense Units (Minbodan). Among the dead were 5 women and many children of middle-school age. The police and military police (Korean Army MPs) supervised the operation. **Comment**: When 4 U.S. military advisory group members arrived, 38 had already been executed, and the execution of the remaining 38 was witnessed by chance.[^199]

[^197]: Hq. 6th Inf Div, USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1102, November 19, 1948.
[^198]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1055, February 4, 1949.
[^199]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1077, March 3, 1949.

**[p. 297]**

As the U.S. military reports reveal, the harsh suppression campaign took the form of burning mid-mountain villages and shooting residents indiscriminately — men, women, old and young alike. The suppression forces' original operational concept had been to evacuate the residents of mid-mountain villages to coastal villages, and to eliminate the guerrillas' base of support by establishing a resident-surveillance system in those coastal villages. Prime Minister and Defense Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk described this operation in his National Assembly report as follows:

> **a. Enemy Situation:** Due to the national army's fierce annihilation campaigns, food shortages, insufficient supply of weapons and ammunition, and the reduction of collaborators, the armed insurgents currently number no more than 50–60, though it is truly regrettable that many of the island's residents remain ideological sympathizers of the insurgents.
>
> **b.** The 9th Regiment's officers and men are securing key positions throughout Jeju Island; relocating residents of mountain villages to coastal towns in order to implement the *pojabje* (baojia) [collective household surveillance system]; clearing forests on both sides of the island's roads to guard against surprise guerrilla attacks; completely severing supply lines to the insurgents; and thoroughly punishing those who collaborate with the insurgents — all while pressing forward single-mindedly toward the reconstruction of Jeju.[^200]

In this way, the evacuation order — premised on the assumption that the majority of Jeju's residents were "ideological sympathizers of the insurgents" — called for the forced relocation of residents, to be followed by establishment of the *pojabje*, a system of collective household surveillance, in order to cut off ordinary residents from the armed forces. In this connection, Yi Ki-bong (李基鳳), who served as a senior sergeant in the 9th Regiment at the time, testified: "Everyone in the remote mountain areas was ordered to come down to the coastal zones, and anyone who didn't come down was deemed a communist [*kongbi*] in its entirety."[^201]

However, in some mid-mountain villages the suppression forces descended before the evacuation order had even been communicated, burned the houses, and began shooting residents. Moreover, even among those who had received evacuation orders and complied by moving to coastal villages, anyone whose family included even one missing member was labeled a "fugitive's family" (*topi chagajok*) and shot.

Pu Sŏng-bang (夫成邦), who lost his mother, young children, and 12 family members and relatives because he himself had gone into hiding, lamented: "What is heartbreaking and unjust is that at the time our oldest daughter was only twelve years old, and below her were children aged ten, eight, and six — what crime had they committed to deserve death?"[^202]

This campaign ended up driving residents to flee into the mountains instead, rendering meaningless Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk's National Assembly statement that "armed insurgents number no more than 50–60" — and ultimately caused massive civilian casualties and the prolongation of the crisis.

[^200]: *Kukhoesokkirok*, Session 1, No. 124, December 8, 1948.
[^201]: Testimony of Yi Ki-bong (age 40, Sŏngju, North Kyŏngsang Province; senior sergeant, 9th Regiment at the time; retired as lieutenant colonel; recorded March 15, 1966), in Ministry of National Defense War History Compilation Committee, *Participant Testimonies*.
[^202]: Testimony of Pu Sŏng-bang (age 86, Choch'ŏn-ŭp, Choch'ŏn-ri; recorded December 12, 2002).

**[p. 298]**

Later, a U.S. military adviser pointed out that the army's indiscriminate killings were in fact driving residents to join the armed guerrillas:

> (1) Liaison aircraft are dropping propaganda leaflets and indiscriminately dropping grenades and mortar rounds.
>
> (2) The overbearing attitude of Korean Army soldiers and local police toward local residents is believed to be a factor unnecessarily provoking peaceful civilians into joining insurgent activities.
>
> (3) KMAG personnel are being unnecessarily exposed to danger for the following reasons: numerical inferiority and lack of communications; complete reliance on the Korean Army for security; the atmosphere in several villages continuously provoked by poor conditions and what are clearly extrajudicial executions of residents; and violations of civil rights by soldiers lacking adequate training and discipline.[^203]

After the evacuation order, the suppression forces treated anyone found hiding in mid-mountain villages or mountain areas as an enemy and shot them on sight. Yet the backgrounds of those who "surrendered" (*kwisun*) after proving unable to endure a prolonged life in hiding vividly demonstrate just how reckless the harsh suppression campaign had been.

Meanwhile, around mid-December 1948, the original operational concept was perverted, causing even greater civilian casualties. The original operational policy — to evacuate mid-mountain village residents to coastal villages and cut their links to the armed forces, thereby destroying the guerrilla force — degenerated into "score-boosting" (*chŏn'gwa olligi*). On this point, a U.S. military report dated December 17, 1948 assessed that "the 9th Regiment's suppression operations have been achieving continuous success recently," attributing this in part to "the desire to conduct high-caliber operations and to match the excellent achievements of the 2nd Regiment's heroes."[^204]

The 9th Regiment was scheduled to be relieved at the end of December 1948 by the 2nd Regiment, stationed in Taejŏn and decorated for its role in suppressing the Yŏsu-Sunch'ŏn Incident. The report's observation was that the 9th Regiment, before departing Jeju, was venting its ambition to achieve battle results worthy of standing against the 2nd Regiment's glory from the Yŏsu-Sunch'ŏn suppression — and this was what was driving its "successful" suppression operations.

[^203]: "Report of Ordnance Advisor's Trip to 2nd Regt-Cheju," February 10, 1949, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 13.
[^204]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1015, December 17, 1948.

**[p. 299]**

In reality, looking at the 9th Regiment's "battle results" in mid-December 1948, one gets the overwhelming impression of desperate "head-count padding." The most representative examples were the so-called "substitute killings" (*taeksal* 代殺), "self-surrender executions" (*chasu sakŏn*), "entrapment suppression" (*hamjŏng t'obŏl*), and the mass shooting of residents — men, women, old and young alike — who had been hiding in the mountains.

**Substitute killings (*taeksal*)** were carried out under that very name. The suppression forces would gather together residents who had evacuated from mid-mountain villages and residents who had originally lived in coastal villages alike, then identify households whose young men had disappeared — labeling them "fugitives' families" (*topi chagajok*) — and shoot them. On December 13, 1948, 48 residents of Sangmo-ri and Hamo-ri in Taejŏng-myŏn were shot on the grounds of being fugitives' families. Because residents were assembled and made to watch the executions, this incident is also known as the "sightseeing execution" (*kwangwang ch'ongsal*). Among the victims was a grandmother of 73.[^205]

**Self-surrender executions (*chasu sakŏn*)** involved shooting people who confessed their past misdeeds. The suppression forces would gather residents and threaten them: "Anyone who has done the slightest wrong in the past should turn themselves in. Turn yourself in and you will be spared; if discovered later, you cannot escape execution. We already have a list of those involved." The very fact that they were soliciting surrenders was itself proof that they had no such list — but many terror-stricken residents came forward. They confessed mainly to: participation in the Building Preparation Committee or People's Committees in the immediate post-liberation period; having protested or demonstrated against the police's shooting of marchers on the March 1st anniversary in 1947; and having participated in demonstrations or provided food at the guerrillas' demand when the armed forces had seized control of a village. Yet the suppression forces, in breach of their promise, mass-executed those who surrendered.

The most representative case of a "self-surrender execution" is the so-called "Paksŏngnaech'ŏn Incident." Two hundred people who had surrendered in Choch'ŏn-myŏn were being held when, on December 21, 1948, they were deceived with the announcement "Anyone willing to join the sweep operation, get on the truck" — and 150 of them were loaded onto trucks and mass-executed at the riverbank of "Paksŏngnaech'ŏn" in Cheju Town.[^206]

One cannot discuss the reckless suppression operations driven by the suppression forces' desperate score-boosting without mentioning **entrapment suppression (*hamjŏng t'obŏl*)**. The typical form of entrapment suppression was for suppression troops to dress in guerrilla uniforms, enter civilian homes, and shoot those who responded to their requests for assistance. Among the expressions used in unison by those who lived through that period is the phrase "a world where bears are terrifying and tigers are terrifying" (*kom do musŏpko pŏm do musŏun sesang*): both the suppression forces and the guerrillas were to be feared. By day, the suppression forces controlled the villages and shot anyone labeled a "suspected insurgent"; by night, after the suppression forces withdrew, the guerrillas descended and purged anyone labeled a "reactionary element." In this situation, ordinary residents had no way to refuse the guerrillas' demands. Yet the suppression forces

[^205]: Cheminilbo 4·3 Ch'wijaebak, op. cit., vol. 5, pp. 321–322.
[^206]: Cheminilbo 4·3 Ch'wijaebak, op. cit., vol. 4, pp. 405–408.

**[p. 300]**

nonetheless dug traps as a means of rooting out those who were supplying the armed forces. Entrapment suppression was carried out across the entire island; the mass killing of the residents of Top'yŏng-ri in Cheju Town is a representative case.

In the early morning of January 3, 1949, a group clad in rough *kaljungi*[^207] work clothes and carrying weapons descended on Top'yŏng-ri in Cheju Town. Encountering residents on the road, they greeted them with "Comrade, Comrade!" and offered handshakes; some among them carried People's Republic flags. They burst into homes demanding "Why haven't you people been cooperating with the mountain [guerrillas]?" and herded residents to the school grounds. But this group carrying the People's Republic flag and dressed in *kaljungi* was not the armed guerrillas — they were police from the nearby Oedo police sub-station and special forces troops who had laid a trap for the residents.

Some residents recognized faces among the group and struggled frantically to avoid being ensnared. Yang Kyŏng-ha (梁京河, age 22) stepped forward declaring "If you're reds, I'll fight you." Kim Pyŏng-hae (金秉海, age 58), a respected village elder, cursed at Oedo sub-station chief Kim Yŏng-ch'ŏl and shouted "Long live the Republic of Korea!" Yet they and approximately 70 other residents were shot. One witness testified: "A man named Kim Sŏng-gyu, who was past sixty, was pointed out on the school grounds and was annihilated together with his wife, son, daughter-in-law, and a young grandchild — six people in all."[^208]

Meanwhile, those hiding in mountain caves and forests to escape the indiscriminate suppression campaigns were hunted down and shot regardless of sex or age. But the vast majority of these people were, as President Syngman Rhee himself had acknowledged in a public statement, those who had "gone into the mountains because they were deceived by Communist propaganda or because their homes had burned down and they had nowhere to go" — people who, after conditions eased, "came back down as men and women in unspeakable condition, bringing small children with them."[^209] Those who managed to survive throughout the dead of winter by fleeing from place to place were able to come down from the mountains and live once conditions eased; but during the height of the harsh suppression campaign, they were shot on the spot the moment they were discovered.

The 1992 excavation of the "Tararangswi Cave" (*Tararangswigyul*) in the mid-mountain zone of Kujwa-ŭp, Jeju Island — where 11 skeletal remains were uncovered — symbolically illustrates the indiscriminate operations against those in hiding during the Jeju 4·3 Incident. Investigation confirmed that the remains belonged to people who had fled into the mountains and were killed in the 9th Regiment's suppression operations on December 18, 1948. The victims were particularly identified as residents of Chongdal-ri and Hado-ri in Kujwa-ŭp; among them were 3 women and a nine-year-old child, making the discovery all the more shocking.

[^207]: Work clothes dyed with persimmon juice (*kamul*); the traditional Jeju work garment.
[^208]: Testimony of Kim Se-wŏn (Top'yŏng-dong, Cheju City; son of victim Kim Pyŏng-hae), in Cheminilbo 4·3 Ch'wijaebak, *4·3 ŭn Malhanda*, installment 445, May 21, 1999.
[^209]: *Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo*, April 13, 1949.

**[p. 301]**

In the Daragswich Cave, plastic glasses, white buttons, belts, buckles, fabric, rubber shoes, ceramic vessels, brass vessels, brass spoons, cauldrons, jars, water buckets, plates, brass ritual vessels, water containers, frying pans, scissors, chamber pots, bundles of wire, grills, braziers, jars of grain, and wooden ladles and other household items were discovered, as well as tools such as scythes, axes, saws, crowbars, picks, hoes, and whetstones. Additionally, next to one isolated human skull, there were a steel helmet, military boots, an iron spear, and a long sword that appeared to have belonged to that person.[^210] A U.S. military intelligence report recorded: "On December 18, the 2nd Battalion of the 9th Regiment carried out its final operation before departing Cheju Island. In this operation, the 9th Regiment, with the assistance of civilians and police, killed 130 men and captured 50 prisoners, and confiscated one rifle and 40 swords and 32 spears."[^211] During the final week of the 9th Regiment's remaining troops' withdrawal, from December 21 to 28, as many as 463 people were executed by firing squad.[^212] The inexplicable disparity between the number of those killed and the weapons confiscated demonstrates how indiscriminately the suppression operation was carried out. The U.S. report noted that the 9th Regiment's operations were conducted under the assumption that the mid-mountain village residents were cooperating with the armed guerrillas. The 9th Regiment immediately replaced the 11th Regiment's "non-response" policy with indiscriminate rule by terror. The 9th Regiment conducted considerably successful combat against the rebels. However, the 9th Regiment simultaneously adopted a program of mass slaughter against the village populations located in the mid-mountain regions, operating under the assumption that all residents were clearly providing help and convenience to the guerrilla units in order to eradicate all resistance root and branch. During the 9th Regiment's occupation period until December 1948, most of the killing of island residents occurred. While such a plan appeared outwardly effective—resulting in almost no rebel activity during the final two months of 1948—the 9th Regiment's indiscriminate operations produced a new generation of recruits for the rebel cause. Thus, when the 2nd Regiment and 9th Regiment exchanged places in December, the ripples of a new wave of guerrilla terror began."[^213] As revealed in the above U.S. military report, during the period of harsh suppression operations, there was almost no "rebel activity." Yet despite this, the "program of mass slaughter" that had been initially adopted was implemented concretely across the entire island of Cheju.

[^210]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, op. cit., vol. 2, pp. 416–439.
[^211]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1021, December 24, 1948.
[^212]: Ibid.
[^213]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1097, April 1, 1949.

**[p. 302]**

This led to the incomprehensible "results" noted earlier: "numerous enemy killed with no losses to our forces." Moreover, the indiscriminate operations resulted in causing residents to newly join the armed guerrillas. Meanwhile, certain villages such as Sewhwa-ri in Kuchua-myŏn, Sŏngyŏp-ri in Pyosŏn-myŏn, Namwŏn-ri and Wimi-ri in Namwŏn-myŏn and others suffered heavily from the armed guerrillas. Initially, the armed forces targeted police, members of right-wing organizations such as the Sŏbuk Youth Corps and the Daedong Youth Group, and right-wing figures who cooperated with the military and police, along with their families. However, after harsh suppression operations by the government forces began in November 1948, they targeted certain villages deemed to have refused cooperation and turned toward the suppression forces, and indiscriminately killed residents. These killings were primarily acts of retaliation against the "substitute killing" of fugitives' families in villages where military and police were stationed.

### d. Replacement of Suppression Forces

#### 1) The Significance of the Regiment Replacement

On December 29, 1948, the military garrison on Cheju was replaced from the 9th Regiment to the 2nd Regiment.[^214] The 2nd Regiment (regimental commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn), which had been garrisoned in Taejŏn, and the 9th Regiment garrisoned in Cheju underwent mutual replacement. However, unlike the previous garrison replacements, the exchange of the 9th and 2nd Regiments raises questions about the reasons. That is, the incorporation of the 9th Regiment into the 11th Regiment on May 15, 1948 is understood as an accountability measure for the 9th Regiment's lack of active engagement in suppression, followed by the reinforcement of manpower and reorganization into a new formation. The reorganization of the 9th Regiment on July 15, 1948, and the subsequent withdrawal of the 11th Regiment appear to have been intended to overcome the aftereffects of the assassination of 11th Regiment Commander Pak Chin-gyŏng that had occurred a month earlier. But then, why was the 9th Regiment withdrawn at the end of December 1948, and the responsibility for the Jeju 4·3 Incident suppression operation transferred to the 2nd Regiment? When the 9th Regiment was incorporated into the 11th Regiment, and again when the 9th Regiment was reorganized and the 11th Regiment was withdrawn, it was not the entire Cheju garrison that relocated; rather, only the regimental commander was replaced and some personnel moved, while the existing 9th Regiment soldiers from Cheju remained in place. However, the replacement of the 9th and 2nd Regiments involved a complete exchange of the existing Cheju garrison with the Taejŏn garrison, which raises questions about its background. Moreover, the 9th Regiment's harsh suppression operations had been deemed "successful" even by the Americans in neutralizing the activities of the armed guerrillas, and as a result, martial law was lifted on December 31, 1948, immediately after the regiment replacement. Therefore, unless it was merely to give the 2nd Regiment "combat experience," the timing of the regiment replacement is a curious matter.

The key to understanding these questions can be found in the attitude of President Syngman Rhee, who issued an instruction on January 21, 1949, to "suppress the miscreants with harsh methods and eradicate the Jeju Incident in order to receive American aid." Regarding Syngman Rhee's impatience concerning the Jeju situation, Yi Ŭng-jun (李應俊), who served as the first chief of the Army General Staff, wrote in his autobiography: "The suppression of the communist bandits on Cheju Island was taking a long time, and the president [received] prompting from the minister. [He] stopped hearing reports of how the bandit suppression operation was going—he wanted to hear reports that the communists had disappeared."[^215]

The replacement of the 9th Regiment with the 2nd Regiment appears to have been planned from around November 17, 1948, when President Syngman Rhee proclaimed martial law to urgently resolve the Jeju situation. This was because he could not trust the existing Cheju garrison soldiers, who had deserted on several occasions and even assassinated a regimental commander. Moreover, the suppression force leadership believed that certain soldiers, including those from Cheju, were communists. After 41 members of the Constabulary deserted on May 21, 1948, one U.S. military report recorded: "Some observers believe many of the officers and men of this regiment cannot be trusted because they appear to be sympathetic with the attackers in the mid-mountain region. In the short term, it would be desirable to eliminate the regiment, but in the long term, even if there are currently communists within the unit, they will eventually reveal themselves through desertion, and the remaining troops can be trusted."[^216]

Accordingly, Syngman Rhee identified the Sŏbuk Youth Corps as a "trustworthy suppression force." The Americans also began developing specific plans for the deployment of Northwest Youth Association members into the military around mid-November 1948, when indiscriminate harsh suppression operations were just beginning. The plan was to fill most military positions with Northwest Youth Association members.[^217] From mid-December onward, members of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps were deployed to the Cheju garrison and police. The Americans praised and encouraged the support provided by the Sŏbuk Youth Corps to the Cheju garrison and police,[^218] and their perception of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps was as follows: "The Northwest Youth Association consists of refugees from North Korea. They are noted for their extreme anti-communism, and they demonstrate whenever they discover communists. They have been actively engaged in tactical defense and intelligence gathering operations with the police and Constabulary for a long time."[^219]

[^214]: Army Headquarters Military Inspection Office, Army History Log, vol. 2, December 29, 1948.
[^215]: Yi Ŭng-jun, Retrospection of 90 Years, Sanŭn Memorial Project Association, 1982, p. 270.
[^216]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Weekly Summary, No. 141, May 28, 1948.
[^217]: "Weekly Activities of PMAG," November 15, 1948, RG 338: Record of U.S. Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 4.
[^218]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 951, October 1, 1948.
[^219]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1005, December 6, 1948.

**[p. 303]**
