The Americans and suppression authorities focused on the 2nd Regiment as the lead force in the Jeju 4·3 Incident suppression operation, following the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. The 2nd Regiment, garrisoned in Taejŏn, was precisely the unit that had suppressed the Yŏsun Incident and possessed combat experience. The regiment replacement plan was finalized by early December at the latest. Colonel H. E. Fuller of the Advisory Group reported on December 6 that he would "relocate the 9th Regiment from Cheju Island to the mainland and replace it with the 2nd Regiment."[^220] According to the regiment replacement plan, the 2nd Regiment advance party arrived in Cheju on December 16,[^221] and the 9th Regiment advance party arrived in Taejŏn on December 19.[^222] Furthermore, to avoid repeating the February 1948 incident where the 14th Regiment from Yŏsun, scheduled to be deployed to Cheju on October 19, turned its guns around and rebelled, so-called "preventive measures" appearing as "ideological vetting" were implemented on the 2nd Regiment troops.[^223] Regarding this, a U.S. military report recorded: "All communist elements in the 2nd Regiment were cleanly removed before departure from Taejŏn."[^224]

As examined above, the replacement of the Cheju garrison from the 9th Regiment to the 2nd Regiment and the inclusion of "extreme anti-communist" Sŏbuk Youth Corps members in the suppression forces were measures intended to end the situation quickly through even more severe operations than those of the 9th Regiment, in line with President Syngman Rhee's instruction to "suppress the miscreants with harsh methods." Meanwhile, this regiment replacement plan and its implementation became an important backdrop for stimulating competitive spirit in the 9th Regiment, accelerating its reckless suppression operations. That is, rather than withdrawing a 9th Regiment carrying out "successful operations," the regime replacement plan stimulated competitive spirit in the 9th Regiment, promoting "successful operations." In fact, from mid-December when the advance party began moving to Taejŏn through the end of December when the full move was completed, the 9th Regiment carried out its most severe suppression operations. During this period, the 9th Regiment assembled people living peacefully in coastal villages and, calling families with young men who had disappeared "fugitives' families," executed them by firing squad. Moreover, it promoted the idea that deserters would be spared and then subjected surrenderers to mass firing squad execution—all creating an impression of recklessness focused on inflating military "achievements."

#### 2) Enhancement of Combat Power

Second Regiment Commander Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn (咸炳善), who was dispatched to Cheju, was a former Japanese military warrant officer and military aide, making him the third consecutive former Japanese military warrant officer appointed as commander of Cheju district suppression forces, following 11th Regiment Commander Ch'oe Kyŏng-rok and 9th Regiment Commander Song Yo-ch'an.[^225] His combat record fighting Chinese forces and anti-Japanese partisans in Manchuria and other areas during his service as a Japanese soldier appears to have earned him the assignment of the Cheju suppression operation. Ham Byŏng-sŏn was dispatched to Cheju less than a month after being appointed 2nd Regiment Commander on December 7, 1948.[^226] The military leadership assigned Commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn the task of conducting harsh operations and worked to strengthen the 2nd Regiment's combat power to enable the execution of that mission. The first measure was the dispatch of Northwest Youth Association members to the military and police. Subsequently, organizational strengthening was achieved through the establishment of Sŏngsan Police Station and Moslpo Police Station. Furthermore, aircraft from the Air Command and the Ranger Battalion under the 6th Brigade were dispatched to Cheju Island to operate under the command of the 2nd Regiment commander. Additionally, on January 20, 1949, Army Headquarters dissolved the 2nd Regiment from its existing 2nd Brigade assignment and made it a unit directly under Army Headquarters.[^227]

The plan to deploy Northwest Youth Association members to the military and police was pursued in secret from no later than the end of November 1948. President Syngman Rhee, in agreement with the Interior Minister, established a plan to dispatch Sŏbuk Youth Corps members throughout South Korea. The plan was to supply approximately 6,500 members to the military and about 1,700 to the police.[^228] Through this process, the 3rd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment came to be composed mostly of Sŏbuk Youth Corps members.[^229] In particular, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps members dispatched to Cheju Island on December 20, 1948, were given a special mission called "elite company." On December 20, 200 members of the Sŏbuk Youth secretly enlisted in the Constabulary in Taejŏn. They were newly arrived in Cheju Island and assigned to the 9th Regiment, and soon received uniforms. Their enlistment was secretly planned between the Sŏbuk Youth leader and the 2nd Brigade commander. (Commentary) This secret enlistment of 200 men indicates that the 2nd Brigade's plan to form an "elite company" from the Sŏbuk Youth Corps was being completed.[^230]

The mission of this elite company was special in the literal sense: "To assign Northwest Youth members as officers and to receive special consideration regarding supplies and equipment, this elite company is utilized in the 2nd Brigade to ferret out opponents and as the vanguard in the event of a North Korean invasion."[^231] Thus, the deployment of the elite company transcended the mere meaning of troop augmentation, as it was also tasked with military police functions of "ferreting out opponents" within the military. The power wielded by the Sŏbuk Youth Corps in this manner functioned as virtually unlimited authority in Cheju Island. However, according to the testimony of one person who had served in the Sŏbuk Youth special company, the special company was not newly formed during the 2nd Regiment's stationing but had already been organized during the 9th Regiment's period and possessed such strong authority that even military police did not dare interfere. The distinction is that whereas the special company during the 9th Regiment's stationing operated as private soldiers without rank insignia, during the 2nd Regiment's stationing it was finally incorporated into the regular army.[^232]

[^220]: "Weekly Activities of PMAG," December 6, 1948, RG 338: Record of U.S. Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 4.
[^221]: Charles L. Wesolowsky, Advisor 9th Regiment, Korean Constabulary, Cheju-Do, Daily Report, December 17, 1948 ; Yi Yun, Field Log 2, pp. 3. Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1017, December 17, 1948 records the date as December 17.
[^222]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1017, December 20, 1948.
[^223]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1015, December 17, 1948.
[^224]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1097, April 1, 1949.
[^225]: Sasaki Harutaka, Secret History of the Korean War, vol. 1: Founding and Trial, trans. Kang Ch'ang-gu, Military Studies Press, 1977, p. 283.
[^226]: Army Headquarters Military Inspection Office, Army History Log, vol. 2, December 7, 1948.
[^227]: Army Headquarters Military Inspection Office, Army History Log, vol. 3, January 20, 1949.
[^228]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1005, December 6, 1948.
[^229]: Army Headquarters Command, Directive 甲 No. 81, May 5, 1949.
[^230]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1023, December 28, 1948.
[^231]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1018, December 21, 1948.
[^232]: Noh Yun-bok (age 78, Sŏngsan-ri, Sŏngsan-ŭp, former member of Sŏbuk Youth special company, recorded March 28, 2002). Testimony.

**[p. 304]**

The Northwest Youth Association members were deployed intensively not only in the military but also in the police. President Syngman Rhee actively recruited Sŏbuk Youth Corps members into the police by offering incentives: those who brought 20 members were given the initial rank of police sergeant (kyŏngsa), those bringing 50 were given the rank of police lieutenant (kyŏngwi), and those bringing 200 were given the rank of police captain (kyŏngam). In fact, Chŏng Yong-ch'ŏl, a former Sŏbuk Youth Corps mid-level cadre who served as head of Samyang Police Box, was specially recruited directly as a police lieutenant in exchange for bringing youth corps members under his leadership.[^233] The Sŏbuk Youth Corps members, having received only 12 days of training, were dispatched as regular police to Cheju Island in early December 1948.[^234] Simultaneously with the dispatch of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps to the Cheju police, President Syngman Rhee had a proposal passed at the state council meeting to increase the number of police stations in Cheju Province. The number of police stations was doubled from two to four in Cheju Province. The increase in police stations was announced as Presidential Decree No. 50 on January 18, 1949, and implemented immediately; Moslpo Police Station and Sŏngsan Police Station were newly established. Moslpo Police Station, located in Daejeong-myŏn of South Jeju County, was assigned to cover Daejeong-myŏn and Andeok-myŏn in South Jeju County and Hallim-myŏn in North Jeju County, while Sŏngsan Police Station, located in Sŏngsan-myŏn of South Jeju County, was assigned to administer Sŏngsan-myŏn, Pyosŏn-myŏn of South Jeju County and Kuchua-myŏn of North Jeju County, taking over portions of the jurisdiction of the existing Jeju Police Station and Sŏgwipo Police Station.[^235] Then on February 19, a special police unit (commander Colonel Kim T'ae-il) numbering 505 men was dispatched, substantially augmenting the police force.[^236]

The Americans also engaged in suppression operations. The extent to which the U.S. military participated in the operations is unclear, but the following statement by Syngman Rhee provides some insight into the American role: "The U.S. Navy made port calls and achieved good results" regarding the Jeju situation. The Presidential Instruction on General Administration Matters: Regarding the Jeju situation, it is said that the U.S. Navy made port calls and achieved good results; since one battalion of military personnel and 1,000 police are being reinforced, I hope to complete pacification quickly, and there are many requests to lift martial law in the Yŏsun and Ch'unch'ŏn area, so please consider these matters.[^237]

Army Headquarters also dispatched two aircraft from the Army Air Command to Cheju Island until January 24, 1949, to assist in suppression operations.[^238] Army Headquarters subsequently moved the Ranger Battalion, a specialized guerrilla warfare unit under the 6th Brigade, to Cheju Island on January 31, 1949, to conduct suppression operations under the command of the 2nd Regiment commander.[^239]

#### 3) Continuation of Mass Killings

The initial objective of the 2nd Regiment was to "conduct encirclement and isolation operations pursuing the annihilation of the enemy down to the last man, departing from the mild and passive operations of the past."[^240] It declared that it would develop suppression operations in three stages. Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk explained these three-stage operations in the National Assembly as follows:

"Regarding the institutions, equipment, and military operations of bandit suppression in Cheju Island, the operations proceeded with three stages. Initially, with the change of units, from January onward units were first distributed evenly across various regions, and becoming familiar with local geography, understanding local conditions, and thus separating the so-called armed bandits appearing from within the villages, that is, expelling them from the villages. This was the preparation period of the first stage. The second stage involved concentrating forces and conducting one sweep of the village areas. This is the second stage. Now entering the third stage, from around February 25, units have been driven out of the villages and are heading toward the peak of Halla Mountain, surrounding and pursuing the armed bandits fleeing there. Thus there have been many engagements, and during this period the military has not descended into the village areas, but rather, while the bandits are engaged in labor at the peak of Halla Mountain, has secured absolute safety in the rear guard areas while tightening the absolute encirclement and pressing forward."[^241]

A journalist who visited Cheju on assignment reported on these three-stage operations as follows: "First, the operation objective was divided into three stages: the first stage from January through the end of January was focused on coastal defense posture and winning the hearts and minds of the innocent people, initiating propaganda work and commencing surrender persuasion operations. Entering the second stage from February 1 through the end of the month, to relieve the anxiety and fear of the innocent people and to avoid fratricidal killing, propaganda pamphlets were actively distributed for rebel surrender operations, while a peace delegation composed of surrendering innocent people was dispatched for 20 days to the vicinity of the Halla Mountain bandits with the purpose of persuading surrender. After that, contact was lost, and thereupon the Korean military, which had thus far taken a moderate and softened approach, adopted a final resolution and entered the third stage of military annihilation, which is precisely the all-out extermination campaign from March 1 through the end of the month."[^242]

With slight date differences, combining the content of the above two sources, one can see that what was called "psychological operations" was established as the first stage of the operation. The second stage involved severing connections between the armed guerrillas and residents, and by the third stage, operations would commence for sweeping mountainous areas. The reason the 2nd Regiment, charged with quickly resolving the situation, declared that it would conduct psychological operations rather than harsh operations during its first month after replacement appears, as Yi Pŏm-sŏk stated, to be a necessary measure to buy time to become familiar with terrain and understand regional conditions. Additionally, it appears to have been based on the judgment that the situation was nearly resolved by the 9th Regiment's harsh suppression operations before the regime replacement.

After the 2nd Regiment's relocation was decided, the advance party arrived in Cheju on December 17, 1948, but it was December 29, 1948, when the 2nd Regiment completed its relocation from Taejŏn to Cheju Island and began fully assuming command of the Cheju district operations. However, this period coincided with the harshest phase of the 9th Regiment's suppression operations. During this period, unarmed civilians were indiscriminately executed by firing squad, and with the armed guerrillas entering a concealed state, military "achievements" were reported such as "killed 105 bandits with no losses to our side,"[^243] resulting in virtually no military casualties. Accordingly, Second Lieutenant Yi Ch'ang-jŏng (李昌禎), chief of the Defense Ministry press section, held a press conference on December 30, 1948, immediately after the regiment replacement, praising the activities of the 9th Regiment commander who had completed the Cheju operation and stating that "since the suppression of the bandits has come to an end, martial law will be lifted within a few days."[^244] General Staff Chief Ch'ae Byŏng-dŏk (蔡秉德) also stated: "Regarding the martial law order for Cheju district that has been in effect since November 17, as the Jeju situation is becoming increasingly peaceful day by day, we will lift it by the end of this year."[^245]

Thus, even before the 2nd Regiment replacement, the military leadership had assessed that "bandit suppression has come to an end" and that "the situation is becoming increasingly peaceful," to such an extent that the armed guerrillas' activities were nearly neutralized. Therefore, the 2nd Regiment appears to have intended to embark on "psychological operations" with some leeway to grasp the situation before carrying out "encirclement and isolation operations to annihilate the enemy down to the last man." However, this operational plan of the 2nd Regiment was forced to be revised due to the armed guerrillas' offensive during the regiment replacement period, and it also conducted harsh suppression operations similar to those of the 9th Regiment. However, the 2nd Regiment's operations were limited to retaliation against people in coastal villages, without ever reaching the mountainous areas where the armed guerrillas were located, and were conducted through mass summary executions of residents without trial.

Regarding such 2nd Regiment operations, the U.S. military report recorded as follows: "Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn commanding the 2nd Regiment initially established quarters in coastal villages. Colonel Ham established a propaganda plan for the enlightenment of the island people and appealed to the bandits to come down from the mountain. The cessation of attacks against the armed bandits hiding in the mountains by such a plan became a laughingstock, and emboldened by this, the guerrillas even attacked the coastal villages under the nose of the defensive forces to obtain food. Thereupon the 2nd Regiment became somewhat more aggressive. However, their actions were limited mainly to retaliation against coastal village residents suspected of helping the rebel forces, and they sometimes executed large numbers of village residents on the spot without the benefit of trial."[^246]

[^233]: Kim Si-hun (age 79, Gasil-ri, Pyosŏn-myŏn, former police officer from the Northwest Youth Association, recorded February 26, 2002). Testimony.
[^234]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1011, December 13, 1948.
[^235]: Official Gazette No. 32, January 18, 1949.
[^236]: Freedom Newspaper, May 19, 1949.
[^237]: State Council Minutes, No. 14, January 28, 1949.
[^238]: Army Headquarters Command, Directive 甲 No. 18, January 22, 1949.
[^239]: Hq. USAFIK, G-3 Operations Report, No. 5, February 5, 1949.
[^240]: National News, April 21, 1949.
[^241]: National Assembly Stenographic Record, vol. 2, No. 56, March 17, 1949.
[^242]: Kyŏnghyang Newspaper, June 29, 1949.
[^243]: Daeдong Newspaper, December 17, 1948.
[^244]: International Newspaper, December 31, 1948.
[^245]: Chosun Central Daily, January 1, 1949.

**[p. 305]**

The armed guerrillas' offensive resumed beginning with a surprise attack on the 3rd Battalion stationed in Ot'ŭng-ri (Oge-ri), Jeju-ŭp, on January 1, 1949, immediately after the regiment replacement. Regarding this incident, the U.S. military advisory group reported: "600 guerrillas attacked the 2nd Regiment on January 1; 30 guerrillas killed, 10 captured, no Constabulary casualties."[^247] However, military records indicate that on this day, 7 soldiers of the 3rd Battalion were killed in action.[^248] The casualty list recorded in the back of the 2nd Regiment album lists a total of 10 soldiers as having died in action on this day, including Second Lieutenant Ko Byŏng-sŏn (高炳善) (who was specially promoted to captain after his death).[^249] Among the soldiers who died in action during the 2nd Regiment's stationing on Cheju Island, only three held ranks of commissioned officer or above, with Ko Byŏng-sŏn being the highest ranking. This incident, in which an officer of rank was killed immediately after the regiment replacement, resulted in a complete revision of the 2nd Regiment's operational plan. On January 3, a fire of unknown origin broke out in the Jeju Provincial Government Building, located at the center of Jeju-ŭp, further heightening tension among the suppression authorities.[^250] This incident was later revealed to be the act of an insider motivated by personal grudges,[^251] but the shock it caused was profound—the police, apparently fearing accountability, still had not received a report by January 5 and denied the incident itself.[^252] In particular, this incident was discussed at a state council meeting, where the Interior Minister reported that "the cause is strongly suspected to be arson by unsavory elements."[^253] The Americans also reported that they viewed it as "an incident related to a strike."[^254]

Thus, although it was later revealed to be false, immediately after the incident it was presumed to be the work of the armed guerrillas and served as an impetus for promoting harsh operations. Thereupon the 2nd Regiment immediately changed its first-stage operational plan to conduct psychological operations while becoming familiar with terrain and understanding regional conditions. The 2nd Regiment first moved to create an atmosphere for conducting harsh operations. Specifically, it claimed that "two Soviet-marked merchant ships of about 3,000 tons were discovered about 5 miles from Samyang-ri on January 3" and asserted that "these vessels were carrying supplies from the North in support of the Cheju armed forces."[^255] While this report of Soviet vessel appearances was a claim that frequently appeared before the Constabulary engaged in harsh operations—the Americans even commented that "while this view has been raised by security forces before, it is uncertain"[^256]—the document dated January 4 requesting the continuation of martial law shows how alarmed Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn became by the armed guerrillas' offensive immediately after his arrival in Cheju:

"On January 4, the commander of the 2nd Regiment stationed in Cheju requested by wireless to the Constabulary headquarters that martial law imposed on Cheju Island be continued. He pointed out that if the nighttime traffic restrictions and relocation restrictions under martial law did not continue, it would be greatly disadvantageous for suppressing the guerrillas and preventing the supply of materials from North Korean vessels. The two surprise attacks by bandit forces on January 1 may have prompted the 2nd Regiment commander to make such a request. The first attack by approximately 100 estimated bandits was directed against his regiment stationed in Ot'ŭng-ri.[^256] Twenty bandits were killed, but 27 members of the Constabulary were wounded. There was also a bandit surprise attack in the nearby Toduri area on the same day. As a result, 10 residents were killed, several were wounded, 26 houses were destroyed, and much food and clothing was plundered (Constabulary report)."[^257]

A military publication written apparently based on testimony from Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn recorded this operation as follows: "On January 4, a combined operation of ground, naval, and air forces was conducted. Before the operation, leaflets urging surrender were dropped on Halla Mountain issuing a final ultimatum, and operations commenced. Naval vessels threatened with fire from 37-millimeter guns, and Air Force L-4 and L-5 liaison aircraft dropped homemade hand grenades and bombs. Unit forces searched Jeju-ŭp and the Halla Mountain area, but failed to discover the bandits' bases."[^258]

However, because no encounter with the armed guerrillas and combat occurred, there is no record of this day's operation in newspaper reports or American materials. It is possible this was confused with the combined ground, naval, and air operation that occurred on February 4.[^259] The 2nd Regiment appears to have begun full-scale suppression operations on January 6. "Since landing on the peninsula on December 28, the Korean military's 2nd Regiment had maintained silence, but finally began operations at 3:00 a.m. on January 6, defeating the enemy's main forces in various locations. The ○○ unit of the 2nd Regiment, arriving at Myŏngdŏk-ri (明德里, approximately 8 km west of Jeju-ŭp)[^260] at 6:30 a.m. on January 6, discovered enemy main forces estimated at about 70 armed bandits with rifles and about 250 armed bandits armed with iron spears and bamboo spears lying in ambush. Under the direct command of the regimental commander, overcoming unfavorable terrain conditions, the enemy was completely surrounded with the aim of total annihilation, and the encirclement was gradually compressed. Simultaneously, for the purpose of reconnaissance and internal sweeping of the enemy position, the regimental commander himself, breaking through the enemy's vigilant sentinel line in single fashion, infiltrated the enemy position, cleverly posing as a bandit, and met with a bandit and attempted to use the password employed by bandits, but when the password did not work, ultimately escaping the enemy position at a critical juncture and returning, launched a fierce encirclement annihilation operation. After four hours of fierce combat, the enemy was brought into a state of complete annihilation, and the following remarkable achievements were obtained: 153 enemy corpses left behind, 1 M1 rifle, 500 rounds of ammunition, multiple Japanese swords and bamboo spears, uniforms, multiple propaganda leaflets confiscated, military casualties: 3 killed in action, 5 wounded (current location military inspection).[^261]

This day's battle—as evidenced by the achievements of "153 enemy corpses left behind, 1 M1 rifle, 500 rounds of ammunition, multiple Japanese swords and bamboo spears, uniforms, multiple propaganda leaflets confiscated" against military casualties of "3 killed in action and 5 wounded"—appears to have been very fierce. The 2nd Regiment album records the names of 7 soldiers killed in action on January 6.[^262] With this day's battle as the beginning, the 2nd Regiment's operations were fully activated.

[^246]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1097, April 1, 1949.
[^247]: "Weekly Activities of PMAG," January 4, 1949, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 4.
[^248]: Ministry of Defense, War History Compilation Committee, Korean War History, vol. 1, 1967, p. 445.

[^250]: Chosun Daily, January 6, 1949.
[^251]: Kang Yong-sam and Yi Kyŏng-su, Grand River: A Hundred Years of Jeju, T'aegwang Culture Company, 1984, pp. 654–656.
[^252]: Chosun Central Daily, January 6, 1949.
[^253]: State Council Minutes, No. 2, January 5, 1949.
[^254]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1030, January 6, 1949.
[^255]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1029, January 5, 1949.
[^256]: Apparently a reference to Ot'ŭng-ri; the text appears to have a transcription error.
[^257]: Ibid.
[^258]: Ministry of Defense, War History Compilation Committee, op. cit., p. 445.
[^259]: Chosun Central Daily, February 9, 1949.
[^260]: There is no place name "Myŏngdŏk-ri" in Jeju Island.
[^261]: Independent Newspaper, January 12, 1949.

**[p. 306]**

The armed guerrillas also did not ease their offensive, taking advantage of the 2nd Regiment's unfamiliarity with the terrain. Thus the full-scale engagement was renewed in January 1949, but the armed guerrillas' Type 99 rifles could not match the 2nd Regiment's firepower. In particular, the "Ŭigwi-ri Battle" that occurred on January 12, 1949, became a turning point in the rapid weakening of the armed guerrillas' forces. Approximately 200 bandits attacked the 2nd Regiment's 2nd Company stationed in Ŭigwi-ri, Cheju Island at 6:30 a.m. on January 12 but were defeated. After two hours of combat, the bandits withdrew with 51 casualties. By contrast, the Korean military suffered 2 killed and 10 wounded. From the bandits, 4 M-1 rifles, 10 Type 99 rifles, and 3 carbines were confiscated.[^263] The military casualties increased by two more, totaling 4 soldiers killed. According to the stone monument at the Namwŏn-ŭp Loyal Spirits Cemetery, the soldiers who fell on this day were "First Class Sergeant Mun Sŏk-ch'un (文錫春), First Class Sergeant Yi Pŏm-pal (李範八), Second Class Sergeant An Sŏng-hyŏk (安星赫), and Im Ch'an-su (林燦洙)." While the shock to the 2nd Regiment from losing 4 soldiers in a surprise attack was significant, this battle inflicted even greater damage on the armed guerrillas. The number of 200 armed guerrillas is, by various accounts, suggestive of the near-total mobilization of armed guerrilla forces at that time. The armed guerrillas thus committed their full strength to this surprise attack, but withdrawing with 51 casualties would have rapidly weakened their forces. In fact, after this incident, while there were cases in January of armed guerrillas attacking villages, assaulting police, or killing residents, there are no records of them directly attacking the military. Regarding this, in early February 1949, a U.S. military report recorded: "According to additional reports from Cheju Island, while bandit activity persists, there are no large-scale activities as in the past."[^264]

Meanwhile, the armed guerrillas' attacks immediately led to retaliatory firing squad executions of civilians by the suppression forces, creating a vicious cycle. During the "Ŭigwi-ri Battle," approximately 80 residents who had been captured wandering in the mid-mountain areas were confined in the Ŭigwi Elementary School, the military garrison. Immediately after the incident, the 2nd Regiment soldiers moved these residents to the school grounds and executed them all by firing squad. The approximately 80 corpses of those killed were later collectively buried by village residents, and currently at the site of the mass grave in Ŭigwi-ri stands a large monument inscribed "Hyŏnŭi Mass Grave (顯義合葬墓)".[^265]

On January 17, the most tragic incident, the so-called "Pokckon Incident," occurred in Pokckon-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn, a coastal village. That morning, some soldiers of the 3rd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment stationed in Sehwa were attacked by armed guerrillas at the mountain pass at the entrance to Pokckon Village while heading toward Hamdŏk, where the battalion headquarters were located, resulting in the death of 2 soldiers. In response, agitated soldiers burned Pokckon-ri and executed approximately 300 residents by firing squad. Furthermore, soldiers again executed approximately 100 residents from Pokckon-ri, who had survived and been relocated to Hamdŏk under evacuation orders. However, regarding this incident, Kim Byŏng-sŏk, a police officer at the time who was conscripted to serve as the battalion commander's vehicle driver, provided remarkable testimony. Kim Byŏng-sŏk testified: "Because the houses had already been completely burned with no way to house them, they were killed; and to give individual soldiers experience in executions, rifles were used instead of mortars."[^266]

Meanwhile, around January 1949, all coastal villages on Cheju were encircled by walls. Beginning apparently from December 1948, this construction project was described by one news outlet as: "The wall construction has progressed with both men and women, young and old actively participating, completing the construction of approximately 120 kilometers of wall-building in a short time, reminiscent of the Great Wall of China, exercising full effect in the villages' internal purges and preparation against bandit incursions."[^267] Island residents were mobilized for this enormous construction project and suffered greatly. In particular, young men having deserted elsewhere, mainly women, the elderly, and children and other vulnerable persons labored under duress. By encircling all coastal villages with walls and assigning residents sentinel duty, the armed guerrillas became increasingly isolated, and conditions were created whereby the residents relocated to coastal villages no longer needed to face suspicion of assisting the armed guerrillas. Nevertheless, the "execution of fugitives' families" continued. The sacrifice of residents remaining in mid-mountain villages also persisted. Some residents of mid-mountain villages who had already lost their homes to fires set by the military and police during the 9th Regiment's stationing period in November and December 1948 relocated to coastal villages, but others still hid in caves or forests near their villages, enduring the harsh winter. During the 2nd Regiment's mountain region suppression operations, many of these people were discovered and executed by firing squad.

On January 25, 1949, the U.S. military advisory group discovered 97 corpses, including those of children, both male and female. Each body had 5 to 6 bullets embedded in it, and based on empty clips and shell casings, it was determined that M-1 rifles were used. However, both the military and police denied any involvement, according to the U.S. military advisory group report.[^268] On February 20, in Toduri, Jeju-ŭp, under the supervision of the military and police, 76 people, including 5 women and a boy of middle school age, were killed by Civil Defense Units. However, the U.S. military advisory group reported that when they arrived at the scene, 38 were already dead, and they witnessed the execution of the remaining 38.[^269] It is difficult to accept that the Americans, who held operational control authority over the Korean military, merely witnessed the killing scene without stopping it, and this passage demonstrates that the Americans cannot escape responsibility for the large-scale civilian casualties.

As February progressed, civilian casualties increased further. In particular, on February 4, residents of the Pongae district (Pongae-ri, Yonggang-ri, HoeCh'ŏn-ri) in Jeju-ŭp suffered collective extermination. Because it was a large-scale operation conducted jointly by ground, naval, and air forces, this day's "achievements" received major press coverage:

"At 3:00 a.m. on February 4, a combined ground, naval, and air force operation was conducted in the Pongae district of Jeju-ŭp under the command of Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn, and it is said that a fierce engagement with armed bandits is currently in progress. Meanwhile, the courageous soldiers of the 2nd Battalion's 7th Company captured leading bandits including so-called People's Court judge Kang T'ae-mun and assassination corps chief Pak Ŭng-su, while the 3rd Battalion captured rebel First Class Sergeant Ko Yŏng-jun, achieving major results. The confirmed achievements are as follows: 360 killed, 130 captured, various foodstuffs and clothing seized (Ministry of Defense inspection)."[^270]

If 360 armed guerrillas were killed and 130 captured, this exceeded the number of armed guerrillas reported by the Interior Minister to the National Assembly two weeks earlier in mid-January 1949—"between 150 and 400"[^271]—such that the armed guerrillas must be considered completely destroyed by the achievements of this day. However, the casualties were mostly unarmed civilians, and among them were the elderly and infirm who could not escape quickly enough. Residents who had lost their homes to fires set by soldiers, hiding in caves and forests near their villages as winter approached, were subjected to mass execution by soldiers who launched a surprise attack at dawn. The residents of Pongae, Yonggang, and HoeCh'ŏn-ri villages, whose homes had all been burned, became a "wasteland" as a result of the collective extermination of residents in this incident. However, Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn, after the situation had nearly ended and residents had gathered again to rebuild the village, named the new village after his own surname. Currently the village has recovered its original name, but for a time it was called Hamnyŏng-ri. That is, the village name was changed to "Hamnyŏng-ri (咸明里)" by combining Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn's surname and the name of Operations Staff Captain Kim Myŏng (金明).[^272]

Meanwhile, on the very day the 2nd Regiment conducted combined ground, naval, and air operations claiming to have killed 360 armed guerrillas and captured 130, in another area soldiers transporting firearms came under surprise attack by armed guerrillas and suffered great casualties. The military, transporting 150 Type 99 rifles divided between 2 trucks, came under ambush attack by armed guerrillas near Kimnyŏng-ri in Kuchua-myŏn. On this day, the military lost all 150 firearms and suffered 15 deaths.[^273]

[^262]: 2nd Regiment, Cheju Island Stationing Period, August 1949.
[^263]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1037, January 14, 1949.
[^264]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1055, February 4, 1949.
[^265]: Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Team, "4·3 Speaks," vol. 5, Jeonye-won, 1998, pp. 141–142.
[^266]: Kim Byŏng-sŏk (age 73, Hamdŏk-ri, Choch'ŏn-ŭp, police officer at the time, recorded February 1, 2002). Testimony.
[^267]: Freedom Newspaper, March 22, 1949.
[^268]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1055, February 4, 1949.
[^269]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1077, March 3, 1949.
[^270]: Chosun Central Daily, February 9, 1949.
[^271]: State Council Minutes, No. 10, January 17, 1949.

**[p. 307]**

It is difficult to pinpoint precisely when the armed guerrillas received decisive blows and reached a state of complete destruction, but judging from press reports and other sources, it appears to have been around mid-March when the 2nd Regiment commenced mountain sweeping operations. Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk, returning after completing an inspection tour of Cheju, told journalists: "Since February 15, the military has begun searching from the coastal villages and is steadily advancing toward Halla Mountain; the searched and cleared villages are being left to the police while the units are mounting a general offensive against the main bandit force from the southwest of Halla Mountain. The armed bandits on Cheju Island number approximately 260, with additional followers numbering one follower for each gun, armed with bamboo spears and following along. As a result of sweeping operations thus far, the bandits are fleeing into mountain valleys."[^274] One journal even described this operation as the "principal cause of complete annihilation." That is, upon receiving intelligence that the main armed guerrilla force was concealed in the mountainous terrain of Namwŏn-myŏn, the 2nd Regiment went to the site on the night of February 15, camped, and launched a surprise attack on the morning of February 16, achieving the result of killing 160 persons. The journal further recorded: "With more than half the enemy bandits left as corpses and fled, the lightly wounded guerrillas may number only several tens, and the accumulated corpses of unarmed iron-spear troops and others are uncounted, so the achievements are incalculable, while the damage to the mountain bandits was fatal. After receiving this annihilating blow, there were no subsequent surprise attacks coming down the mountain as before."[^275]

Meanwhile, the media's failure to perform its proper function was also largely responsible for the mass extermination of innocent unarmed civilians in the conflict between suppression forces and armed guerrillas. Like the 9th Regiment, the 2nd Regiment also controlled the media. All reports concerning the military, except for official announcements, had to undergo inspection by the Military Police Command,[^276] so the central government had no way of knowing that innocent residents were being sacrificed in Cheju Island. In the National Assembly, only the aftereffects of the Yŏsun Incident were intensely debated, while regarding the Jeju Island Incident, only the government's one-sided announcements continued. The damage inflicted by the police was also extensive. The fact that a sitting prosecutor was arrested at the time suggests the extent of civilian damage. Prosecutor Yang Ul (梁乙), who completed his probationary period as a prosecutor in the Seoul District Prosecutors' Office and was appointed prosecutor at the Jeju District Prosecutors' Office as of February 23, 1949, was arrested on February 28 by the Jeju Police Station's Criminal Bureau detectives who had come to Seoul while he was preparing for his trip to Jeju Island and was transported to Jeju Island. Without an arrest warrant issued by a judge, he was taken as if kidnapped. In particular, this incident—where a sitting prosecutor was arrested without prior approval from the Justice Minister—created a major upheaval in the judiciary. Chief Prosecutor Kwŏn Sŭng-nyŏl hastily met with the Justice Minister to discuss countermeasures, and the Seoul District Prosecutors' Chief expressed regret regarding the police's actions. Additionally, the Supreme Prosecutors' Office made contact with the National Security Bureau and sent a telegram to the Jeju Provincial Police requesting the release of Prosecutor Yang Ul.[^277] However, Interior Ministry Deputy Minister Kim Hyo-sŏk (金孝錫) immediately issued a statement claiming that Prosecutor Yang Ul had been identified as involved in certain criminal matters while residing in Cheju Island in late October 1948, and had been arrested pursuant to an arrest warrant properly issued by the Jeju District Court judge. The deputy minister also threatened that claims that "the police unlawfully arrested a sitting prosecutor" were groundless, and he was investigating the source of the article since police authority had been damaged.[^278]

However, contrary to the deputy minister's claim, Prosecutor Yang Ul was formally arrested on March 14, 1949.[^279] The Jeju District Prosecutors' Office, citing difficulty in reviewing the case given that Yang Ul was a sitting prosecutor, transferred the case to the Kwangju District Prosecutors' Office on May 30. Prosecutor Yang Ul was charged with violations of Military Government Ordinance No. 19, conspiracy in rebellion, and conspiracy to assist rebellion, but at a public trial held in Kwangju District Court on October 31, 1949, he

[^272]: Jeju Newspaper, February 5, 1955.
[^273]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1061, February 11, 1949.
[^274]: Kyŏnghyang Newspaper, March 17, 1949.
[^275]: Sŏ Chae-kwŏn, "Record of Pacifying Cheju Island," New World, September 1949, pp. 176–177.
[^276]: Chosun Central Daily, January 15, 1949.
[^277]: Kyŏnghyang Newspaper, March 1 and 4, 1949 ; Chosun Central Daily, March 4, 1949.
[^278]: Chosun Central Daily, March 6, 1949.
[^279]: Tonggwang Newspaper, November 2, 1949.

**[p. 308]**

The Army Headquarters also dispatched two aircraft from the Army Aviation Command to Jeju Island by January 24, 1949, to assist in the suppression operations.[^1] The Army then moved the guerrilla battalion under the 6th Brigade—a unit specializing in guerrilla warfare—to Jeju Island on January 31, 1949, and placed it under the command of the 2nd Regiment commander to carry out suppression operations.[^2]

## 3) Sustained Mass Killings

The original objective of the 2nd Regiment was "to abandon the hitherto lenient and passive operations and to carry out encirclement and isolation operations aimed at the complete annihilation of the enemy down to the last man."[^3] It announced that it would develop suppression operations divided into three phases. Prime Minister Yi Pŏmsŏk explained these three-phase operations in the National Assembly as follows:

> The organs [of authority] on Jeju Island and the military suppression operations proceeded in three phases. First, as unit rotations occurred, from January onward units were deployed evenly throughout various regions, becoming familiar with local geography and skilled in local conditions. The so-called armed rebels appearing within the villages were separated from the village population—that is, expelled from the villages. This was the preparatory period of the first phase. The second phase involved concentrating troops and once again sweeping the village areas. This was the second phase. Now entering the third phase, around February 25, the units were expelled from the villages and pursued the armed rebels heading toward the very peak of Halla Mountain. There were numerous engagements. Throughout this period, the army did not descend into the village areas but rather kept the rebels on Halla Mountain, ensuring absolute safety in the surrounding areas while putting absolute pressure on the encirclement and advancing.[^4]

A journalist who surveyed the Cheju area reported on these three-phase operations as follows:

The operational objectives were divided into three phases: the first phase would be from January 1 through January 31, with the main emphasis on defense of the coastline and winning the hearts and minds of the civilian population, initiating propaganda work. In the second phase, from February 1 through the end of the month, extensive propaganda efforts would be developed to avoid intra-ethnic killing and to avoid the descent of rebels, while reassuring civilians to relieve their anxiety and fear. A peace delegation composed of civilian refugees and those returning to allegiance would be dispatched near the rebels' bases on Halla Mountain for twenty days with a deadline recommending surrender. But when word thereafter became unclear, the Korean army, which had pursued gentle and mitigating operations until this point, adopted a final determination and entered the third phase—a force-based elimination posture—and so the large-scale annihilation warfare from March 1 through the end of March was precisely that.[^5]

Though there are slight date discrepancies, when the contents of the above two sources are synthesized, it can be understood that so-called "propaganda and persuasion work" was set as the first-phase operation. The second phase would sever the connections between the armed guerrillas and the population. The third phase would involve sweeping the mountainous regions. The 2nd Regiment, charged with rapid resolution of the situation, proclaimed that it would conduct propaganda and persuasion work rather than forceful operations during its first month after taking over, apparently for unavoidable reasons—as Yi Pŏmsŏk stated, to gain time to become familiar with the terrain and topography and to understand local conditions. It also appears that before the regiment rotation, the situation was judged to be nearly resolved through the forceful suppression operations of the 9th Regiment.

The advance units of the 2nd Regiment arrived on Jeju on December 17, 1948, but the 2nd Regiment completed its transfer from Taejŏn to Jeju Island and fully assumed suppression operations in the Jeju district on December 29, 1948. However, this period was when the 9th Regiment's suppression operations were most severe. During this period, unarmed civilians were indiscriminately massacred by the military, and the armed guerrillas entered a state of concealment, resulting in announcement of battle results such as "105 rebels killed, with no losses on the Korean side."[^6] Accordingly, on December 30, 1948—immediately after the regiment rotation—the Defense Ministry's spokesman, Captain Yi Changjŏng, praised the activities of the 9th Regiment commander, who had completed the Jeju operations, and stated, "Since suppression of the rebels has been largely accomplished, we will lift martial law within several days."[^7] Chief of Staff Chae Pyŏngdŏk also stated, "Regarding martial law in the Jeju district now in effect since November 17, as the Jeju situation gradually becomes more peaceful, we will lift it by the end of this year."[^8]

In this way, even before the 2nd Regiment rotation, military leaders had already evaluated that "suppression of the rebels had been largely accomplished" or that "the situation was gradually becoming more peaceful," with the result that the activities of the armed guerrillas were nearly neutralized. Accordingly, before the 2nd Regiment embarked on "encirclement and isolation operations aimed at the complete annihilation of the enemy down to the last man," it appears that it wanted to conduct "propaganda and persuasion work" with some degree of leisure in order to understand the situation. However, this operational plan of the 2nd Regiment was inevitably modified due to the armed guerrillas' offensive that exploited the rotation period, and like the 9th Regiment, it developed forceful suppression operations. But the 2nd Regiment's operations did not reach the mountainous areas where the armed guerrillas were located; instead, they were limited to reprisals against the people of coastal villages, developing in a manner of executing residents at large scale without trial. A U.S. military report recorded the following about such operations by the 2nd Regiment:

Colonel Ham Pyŏngsŏn's 2nd Regiment initially established barracks in coastal villages. Colonel Ham devised a propaganda plan for the enlightenment of the islanders and appealed to the rebels to come down from the mountains. The suspension of attacks against the armed rebels in concealment in the mountains made such plans laughable, and inspired by this, the guerrillas attacked even the coastal villages in front of the defense positions to obtain provisions. Thereupon the 2nd Regiment became somewhat more aggressive. However, their activities were mainly limited to reprisals against the coastal village people suspected of aiding the rebel forces, and they sometimes executed village residents at large scale without the benefit of trial.[^9]

**[p. 309]**

The armed guerrillas' offensives resumed on January 1, 1949, with a surprise attack on the 3rd Battalion stationed in Odeung-ri in Cheju town. Regarding this incident, the U.S. military advisory group reported, "600 guerrillas attacked the 2nd Regiment on January 1. 30 guerrillas killed, 10 prisoners captured, no Korean Constabulary casualties."[^10] However, military records state that 7 soldiers of the 3rd Battalion were also killed in action that day.[^11] In the casualty roster recorded in the 2nd Regiment album, ten soldiers are listed as having been killed that day, including Lieutenant Koh Pyŏngsŏn (promoted to captain posthumously).[^12] Among officers of subaltern rank and above who died during the 2nd Regiment's stationing on Jeju, there were only three, with Koh Pyŏngsŏn being the highest ranking.

Immediately after the regiment rotation, the occurrence of an incident resulting in the sacrifice of a subaltern officer completely revised the 2nd Regiment's operational plan. On January 3, a fire of unknown origin broke out at the Cheju Provincial Office, located in the center of Cheju town, causing the suppression authorities to become even more tense.[^13] This incident was later revealed to be the work of an insider motivated by personal grievances,[^14] but the police were so shocked that they denied even the occurrence of the incident itself, claiming as late as January 5 that they had not yet received a report.[^15] Particularly, this incident was also discussed at a State Council meeting, where the Interior Minister reported, "The cause appears to be strongly suspected of arson by subversive elements."[^16] The U.S. military also reported, "The incident appears to be related to strikes."[^17] In this way, though later revealed to be untrue, immediately after the incident it was presumed to be the work of the armed guerrillas, serving as an impetus to promote forceful operations. Accordingly, the 2nd Regiment immediately changed its first-phase operational plan to gain familiarity with the terrain and topography and understanding of local conditions while conducting propaganda and persuasion work.

The 2nd Regiment first moved to create an atmosphere for conducting forceful operations. That is, it claimed, "Two Soviet-flagged vessels of approximately 3,000 tons each were spotted about 5 miles off Samyang-ri on Jeju Island on January 3," and "These vessels were carrying supplies from the North [North Korea] to support the armed guerrillas on Jeju Island."[^18] Though this Soviet vessel sighting claim was frequently raised by the constabulary before they conducted forceful operations—to the point that the U.S. military commented, "Though this view has previously been raised by the security forces, it is uncertain"—the following document requesting continuation of martial law on January 4, the very next day, shows how panicked Colonel Ham Pyŏngsŏn was in response to the armed guerrillas' offensives that occurred shortly after his arrival on Jeju.

The commander of the 2nd Regiment stationed on Jeju Island requested by radio on January 4 that martial law imposed on Jeju Island be continued. He pointed out that if the nighttime curfew and relocation restrictions under martial law were not maintained, it would be greatly disadvantageous for suppressing the guerrillas and preventing supplies to North Korean vessels. The two surprise attacks by rebellious mobs on January 1 may have led the 2nd Regiment commander to make such a request. The first attack, by an estimated hundred or so rebels, was aimed at his regiment stationed in Ora-ri.[^19] Twenty rebels were killed, but 27 constabulary personnel were also wounded. On the same day, there was a surprise attack by rebels in the nearby area of Toduriŭi. As a result, 10 civilians were killed, several were wounded, 26 houses were destroyed, and much food and clothing were plundered (according to constabulary report).[^20]

A military publication apparently written on the basis of testimony from Colonel Ham Pyŏngsŏn and others recorded the following about this operation: "On January 4, combined land, sea, and air operations were conducted. Preceding the operation, leaflets recommending surrender were scattered on Halla Mountain as a final ultimatum, and the operation was launched. Naval vessels delivered threatening fire with 37-millimeter guns, and the Air Force's L-4 and L-5 liaison aircraft dropped domestically produced grenades and bombs. Each battalion force conducted searches in and around Cheju town and throughout the Halla Mountain area but was unable to discover the rebels' bases."[^21] However, since there were no encounters with armed guerrillas and no combat ensued, there are no records of this operation's date in newspaper reports or U.S. military materials. It is possible that this account was confused with the combined land, sea, and air operation that took place on February 4.[^22]

The 2nd Regiment appears to have begun full-scale suppression operations on January 6. Since landing on the mainland on December 28, the Korean army's 2nd Regiment, which had maintained silence thereafter, finally took action on January 6 at 3:00 AM and broke the enemy's main force in various places. The Korean Army unit that arrived at Myŏngdŏk-ri (approximately 8 km west of Cheju town) at 6:30 AM on the 6th discovered enemy main force in concealment, estimated at approximately 70 rebels with rifles and approximately 250 rebels armed with spears and bamboo spears. Under direct command of the regiment commander, overcoming difficult terrain, in order to achieve complete annihilation, the unit completely encircled the enemy and gradually compressed the encirclement. At the same time, for the purposes of reconnaissance of the enemy position and internal mopping up, the regiment commander himself, breaking through the enemy's vigilant sentinel lines alone, infiltrated the enemy position, skillfully posing as a rebel and meeting face-to-face with one of the rebels. He used the cipher code the rebels used, but when the cipher did not work, at a moment of acute danger he exited the enemy position and returned. He then launched a fierce large-scale encirclement and annihilation operation. After fierce combat lasting approximately four hours, the enemy was finally brought into a state of complete annihilation, achieving the following brilliant battle results: Enemy bodies left behind: 153; M-1 rifle: 1; Ammunition: 500 rounds; Japanese swords and bamboo spears: numerous; Uniforms and propaganda leaflets: numerous confiscated; Korean military casualties: 3 killed in action, 5 wounded [verified by on-site military inspection].[^23]

**[p. 310]**

This battle, with "enemy bodies left behind: 153, M-1 rifle: 1, ammunition: 500 rounds, Japanese swords and bamboo spears: numerous, uniforms and propaganda leaflets: numerous confiscated," appearing very fierce as indicated by the military casualty situation of "3 killed in action, 5 wounded." The 2nd Regiment album records the names of 7 soldiers killed in action on January 6.[^24] Beginning with this battle, the 2nd Regiment's operations came in earnest. The armed guerrillas, seizing the gap before the 2nd Regiment became familiar with the local geography, did not slack off their offensive. In this way, the full-scale battle of January 1949 resumed, but the armed guerrillas' Japanese Type 99 rifles could not match the firepower of the 2nd Regiment. In particular, the "Ŭigwi-ri battle" that took place on January 12, 1949, became a turning point in the rapid weakening of the armed guerrillas' forces.

Approximately 200 rebels launched a surprise attack on the 2nd Regiment's 2nd Company stationed at Ŭigwi-ri, Jeju Island, at 6:30 AM on January 12, but were defeated. After two hours of fighting, the rebels retreated with 51 killed, while the Korean army sustained 2 killed and 10 wounded. Four M-1 rifles, ten Type 99 rifles, and three carbines were captured from the rebels.[^25] The number of military casualties grew by two more, for a total of four killed. Looking at the memorial stone at the Nam-wŏn town Chungghun cemetery, the soldiers killed that day were "Sergeant First Class Mun Sŏkch'un, Sergeant First Class Yi Pŏmbral, Sergeant Yi Sŭnghyŏk, and Lim Ch'ansu."[^26] Though the 2nd Regiment's shock at suffering four casualties in a surprise attack was considerable, this battle inflicted greater damage on the armed guerrillas. The figure of 200 armed guerrilla members appears, from the various circumstances, to represent nearly the entire mobilized force of the armed guerrillas at that time. The armed guerrillas thus concentrated all their power in this surprise attack, but in retreating after suffering 51 deaths, their forces are estimated to have weakened rapidly. In fact, while there were incidents after this event in January in which the armed guerrillas attacked villages, attacked police, or killed residents, there are no records of direct attacks on the military. Regarding this, a U.S. military report from early February 1949 recorded, "According to additional reports from Jeju, the rebels' activities continue, but there are no large-scale activities as in the past."[^27]

Meanwhile, attacks by the armed guerrillas led directly to retaliatory mass shootings of civilians by suppression forces, creating a vicious cycle. Even during the "Ŭigwi-ri battle," approximately 80 residents rounded up while wandering in the midaltitude areas were being held in the Ŭigwi elementary school, the site of the unit's headquarters. Immediately after the incident, soldiers of the 2nd Regiment moved these people to the school's rear field and executed all of them. The approximately 80 corpses of those killed were later interred in a joint burial by village residents. Currently, at the joint burial site located in Ŭigwi-ri, a large stone monument bearing the inscription "Hyŏn'ŭi joint burial tomb" is erected.[^28]

On January 17, the most tragic incident, called the "Pukchos'ŏn incident," occurred in Pukchos'ŏn-ri in Choch'ŏn-myŏn, a coastal village. Early that morning, some soldiers from a company of the 3rd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment, which was stationed in Sehwa, were heading toward Hamdog, where the battalion headquarters was located, when they were ambushed by armed guerrillas on a mountain pass at the entrance to Pukchos'ŏn village. Two soldiers were killed. Excited soldiers then burned down the village of Pukchos'ŏn and executed a mass killing of approximately 300 residents. Furthermore, the soldiers again executed approximately 100 residents of Pukchos'ŏn who had been evacuated to Hamdog among the surviving residents. However, regarding this incident, Kim Pyŏngsŏk, then a policeman who was drafted as the driver of the battalion commander's vehicle, gave striking testimony. Kim stated, "Because all the houses had already been burned and there was no way to detain them, they killed them; and to give individual soldiers experience in mass execution, they used rifles instead of mortars."[^27]

Meanwhile, around January 1949, all coastal villages on Jeju Island became surrounded by stone walls. Beginning apparently around December 1948, regarding this stone-walling work, a newspaper explained: "The stone-walling work proceeded with the earnestness of all, men and women, old and young, completing in a short time the construction of some 120 kilometers reminiscent of the Great Wall of China, and was proving fully effective in internal village purification and defense against rebel incursions."[^28] The residents were mobilized for this enormous construction project and suffered greatly. Especially as young men fled, mainly women, the elderly, and children—the weak and vulnerable—suffered from forced labor. By surrounding all coastal villages with stone walls and having residents stand sentry, the armed guerrillas became increasingly isolated. This created circumstances in which residents evacuated to coastal villages no longer had to fear being suspected of aiding the armed guerrillas. Yet despite this, "executions of families of those who fled" continued. The sacrifice of residents remaining in midaltitude villages also continued. Already during the 9th Regiment's stationing period, in November and December 1948, some residents of midaltitude villages who had lost their homes in fires set by military and police had evacuated to coastal villages. But some still remained near their villages, hiding in caves or forests and enduring the difficult winter. Many of these people were discovered during the 2nd Regiment's suppression operations in mountainous areas and executed.

On January 25, 1949, the U.S. military advisory group discovered the corpses of 97 people, including children, men and women. Each body contained 5-6 bullet wounds, and based on empty clips and shell casings, M-1 rifles appeared to have been used. However, both military and police denied any involvement, the U.S. military advisory group reported.[^29]

**[p. 311]**

On February 20, under the supervision of military and police, five women and a boy of middle school age among 76 people were murdered by Civil Defense Units (Minbodan) in Toduriŭi in Cheju town. The U.S. military advisory group reported that when they arrived at the scene, 38 people were already dead, and they witnessed the execution of the remaining 38.[^30] It is difficult to accept that the U.S. military, which held operational control of the Korean military, merely witnessed these killings without intervening. This demonstrates that the U.S. military cannot escape responsibility for large-scale civilian casualties.

As February began, the sacrifice of residents grew even greater. Particularly on February 4, the residents of the Ponggae district (Ponggae-ri, Yonggangni, Hoech'ŏn-ri) of Cheju town suffered collective massacre. Because this was a large-scale operation conducted jointly by land, sea, and air forces, the "battle results" of that day were reported extensively in the media:

The large-scale combined operation of land, sea, and air forces was developed on February 4 at 3:00 AM in the Ponggae district of Cheju town under the direction of Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏngsŏn, engaging in fierce combat with the so-called armed rebels. Meanwhile, the courageous soldiers of the 2nd Battalion, 7th Company arrested so-called "people's tribunal judge" Kang Taemun, "assassination squad leader" Pak Ŭngsu, and other rebel leaders, while the 3rd Battalion arrested rebel sergeant first class Koh Yŏngjun, achieving substantial battle results. The ascertained battle results are as follows: 360 executed, 130 prisoners, numerous provisions, clothing, and other items confiscated (verified by Defense Ministry inspection).[^31]

If 360 armed guerrillas were executed and 130 were taken prisoner, this exceeds the figure of "150 to 400 members" reported to the National Assembly by the Interior Minister around mid-January 1949,[^32] so as a result of that day's battle results, the armed guerrillas must be considered completely destroyed. However, the casualties were mostly unarmed civilians, and among these, the elderly and weak who could not flee quickly were the victims. Residents of Ponggae, Yonggang, and Hoech'ŏn-ri, who had lost their homes in fires set by soldiers and had been surviving in caves or forests near their villages, were killed in collective massacres by soldiers in a dawn surprise attack. The residents of these three villages, having already lost all their houses in fire, were turned into a "wasteland" by the events of that day with collective massacres. Afterward, after the situation was largely concluded, residents gathered again to rebuild the village. Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏngsŏn took his own family name to give a new name to the village. Currently the area has regained its original name, but for some time this region was called Hamnyŏng-ri—combining the surname of Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏngsŏn and the name of operations staff Captain Kim Myŏng, creating the village name "Hamnyŏng-ri" (咸明里).[^33]

Meanwhile, on the very day the 2nd Regiment displayed the results of its combined land, sea, and air operations, having killed 360 armed guerrillas and captured 130, in a different area soldiers transporting rifles suffered heavy casualties in a surprise attack by armed guerrillas. Military personnel transporting 150 Japanese Type 99 rifles divided between two trucks were ambushed by armed guerrillas near Kimnŭng-ri in Kugwŏn-myŏn. That day, the military lost all 150 rifles and suffered 15 deaths.[^34]

It is difficult to determine precisely when the armed guerrillas received a decisive blow and reached a state of destruction, but synthesizing media reports and other sources, it appears to have been around mid-March when the 2nd Regiment launched its mountain mopping-up campaign. Prime Minister Yi Pŏmsŏk, returning from an inspection tour of Jeju, spoke to reporters as follows: "Since February 15, the military has begun searching from the villages along the coast and is gradually advancing toward Halla Mountain. Searched and purged villages have been left to the police, while the unit in the southwest of Halla Mountain has launched a general offensive against the main rebel force. The armed rebels on Jeju Island number about 260, and apart from these, the people following them number some 20 people to one rifle, each carrying bamboo spears. Through the mopping-up operations thus far, the rebels are fleeing into mountain valleys."[^35]

A magazine also introduced this operation as "the main cause of complete mopping up." That is, having obtained information that the main rebel force was in concealment in the mountainous area of Nam-wŏn-myŏn, the 2nd Regiment went to the site on the night of February 15 and set up camp, then launched a surprise attack at dawn on the 16th, achieving the result of killing 160 people. The magazine further recorded: "The enemy, abandoning more than half the armed guerrilla force as corpses, fled. Even if some of the injured armed guerrillas survived, there could be only a few tens. The accumulated corpses of unarmed and spear-carrying troops do not bear counting; the extent of the battle results is incalculable, while for the mountain rebels the damage was fatal. After receiving this annihilating blow, there were no subsequent descents to attack as before."[^36]

**[p. 312]**

Meanwhile, in the course of clashes between suppression forces and armed guerrillas, innocent unarmed civilians suffered collective massacres, partly because the press failed to perform its function. Like the 9th Regiment, the 2nd Regiment also controlled the press. All reports regarding the military, except for official announcements, had to be submitted to inspection by the Military Police Headquarters,[^37] and so the central government had absolutely no awareness that innocent residents were being sacrificed on Jeju Island. In the National Assembly, only the aftermath of the Yŏsun incident was intensively discussed, while regarding the Jeju incident only one-sided announcements by the government continued.

The damage to residents by police was also considerable. The fact that a sitting prosecutor was arrested at that time suggests the extent of civilian casualties. Prosecutor Yang Ŭl, having completed his training as assistant prosecutor at the Seoul District Prosecutors' Office, was appointed prosecutor to the Cheju District Prosecutors' Office as of February 23, 1949. While preparing to go to Jeju Island, he was arrested on February 28 by the criminal investigation unit of the Cheju Police Department that had come to Seoul and transported to Jeju Island. Without an arrest warrant issued by a judge—as if kidnapped—he was taken away. Particularly, the arrest of a sitting prosecutor without prior authorization from the Minister of Justice caused great turmoil in the judicial sector. Prosecutor General Kwŏn Sŭngnyŏl urgently met with the Justice Minister to discuss countermeasures, and the Seoul District Prosecutors' Office chief expressed regret at police conduct. Also, the Supreme Prosecutors' Office coordinated with the Public Security Bureau and sent a telegram to the Cheju Provincial Police requesting the release of Prosecutor Yang Ŭl.[^38] However, Deputy Interior Minister Kim Hyosŏk immediately issued a statement, claiming that when Prosecutor Yang was residing on Jeju Island in late October 1948, he was found to be involved in some crime case, and that he was arrested under a formal arrest warrant issued by the Cheju District Court judge, which was executed. Deputy Minister Kim also threatened that the article claiming "police illegally arrested a sitting prosecutor" was groundless, and that as police credibility had been damaged thereby, the source of the article was being investigated.[^39]

However, contrary to Deputy Minister Kim's statement, Prosecutor Yang Ŭl was formally arrested on March 14, 1949.[^40] The Cheju District Prosecutors' Office stated it was difficult to examine the matter since Prosecutor Yang was a local prosecutor, and on May 30 transferred the case to the Kwangju District Prosecutors' Office. Prosecutor Yang Ŭl was charged with violation of U.S. Military Government ordinance No. 19, conspiracy for rebellion, and aiding rebellion. However, at a trial held in the Kwangju District Court on

**[p. 313]**

October 31, 1949, he was acquitted of all charges.[^41]

---

[^30]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1077, March 3, 1949.

**[p. 314]**

They were moved to the field behind the school and all were shot. The roughly 80 bodies of the victims were later buried together by village residents, and at the collective grave site in Ŭigwi-ri today stands a large stone monument inscribed "Hyŏn-ŭi Joint Burial Grave."[^1] On January 17, the tragic incident known as the "Pukch'on Incident" broke out in Pukch'on-ri, a coastal village in Choch'ŏn-myŏn. In the early morning that day, some personnel from a company of the 3rd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment stationed in Sehwa were ambushed on a mountain pass at the entrance to Pukch'on village while en route to Hamde'k, where the battalion headquarters was located. Two soldiers died in this ambush. Enraged, the soldiers then burned Pukch'on-ri to the ground and carried out a mass shooting of some 300 residents. Moreover, soldiers also shot about 100 residents from Pukch'on-ri who had been evacuated to Hamde'k among those who survived. However, regarding this incident, Kim Pyŏng-sŏk, who served as a police officer and vehicle driver for the battalion commander at the time, gave remarkable testimony. Kim Pyŏng-sŏk testified: "With the houses already burned, there was no means to accommodate them, so they were killed. Individual soldiers were given practice in shooting by using rifles instead of mortars."[^2] Meanwhile, around January 1949, all coastal villages on Cheju became surrounded by stone walls. This wall-building project, which appears to have started around December 1948, was explained by one press outlet: "Both men and women, young and old, engaged actively in the wall construction, completing in a short time a twelve-kilometer-long wall reminiscent of the Great Wall of China, which achieved great effect in self-purification within the settlements and complete readiness against bandit incursions."[^3] The islanders were mobilized for this massive construction project, which recalled the Great Wall, and suffered considerable hardship. Particularly with young men having fled, mainly women, the elderly, and children—the weak and vulnerable—bore the brunt of the forced labor. With all coastal villages surrounded by walls and residents assigned to stand watch, the armed guerrillas became increasingly isolated. This created conditions in which residents evacuated to coastal villages no longer needed to face suspicion of supporting the armed guerrillas. Nevertheless, "mass shooting of families of those in hiding" continued. The sacrifice of residents remaining in the midland villages also continued. Some residents of midland villages who had lost their homes in fires set by the military and police during the 9th Regiment's stationing period in November and December 1948 had been evacuated to coastal villages, but some remained hiding in caves or forests near their villages, barely surviving the harsh winter. Many of these were discovered and shot during the 2nd Regiment's mountain suppression operations.

[^1]: Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Editorial Team, "4·3 Speaks" ⑤, Jŏnyewŏn, 1998, pp. 141–142.
[^2]: Kim Pyŏng-sŏk (age 73, Hamde'k-ri, Choch'ŏn-ŭp, police officer at the time; recorded Feb. 1, 2002), testimony.
[^3]: Chayu Sinmun, March 22, 1949.

**[p. 315]**

On January 25, 1949, the U.S. Military Advisory Group discovered the corpses of 97 men and women, including children. Each body bore 5–6 bullet wounds, and from the empty clips and cartridge casings, it was determined that M-1 rifles had been used. However, both the military and police denied any connection, according to the U.S. Military Advisory Group's report.[^4] On February 20, under military and police supervision, 76 people—including 5 women and a boy of middle-school age—were killed by the Civil Defense Corps in Tŏtŭri, Cheju-ŭp. The U.S. Military Advisory Group reported that when they arrived at the scene, 38 were already dead, and they witnessed the execution of 38 others.[^5] It is difficult to understand how the U.S. military, which held operational control of Korean forces, would merely witness such a scene while leaving the killing ground untouched. This demonstrates that the U.S. military cannot escape responsibility for the large-scale civilian casualties. As February 1949 progressed, the sacrifice of residents grew. In particular, on February 4, residents of the Pongge district (Pongge-ri, Yonggang-ri, Hoech'ŏn-ri) in Cheju-ŭp underwent mass killings. Because it was a large-scale operation conducted jointly by the Army, Navy, and Air Force, that day's "results" were widely reported in the press: "As of 3 a.m. on the 4th, a joint Army-Navy-Air Force operation was being conducted in the Pongge district, Cheju-ŭp, under the command of Colonel Ham Pyŏng-sŏn, with fierce fighting against the armed bandits underway. In the meantime, the courageous soldiers of the 2nd Battalion, 7th Company have captured so-called people's court chairman Kang Tae-mun and assassination team chief Pak Ŭng-su and other bandit leaders, while the 3rd Battalion captured insurgent first sergeant Ko Yŏng-jun, achieving major results. The confirmed results are as follows: Shot 360; Captured 130; Other food, clothing, and miscellaneous items confiscated (Ministry of Defense censorship)."[^6] If 360 armed guerrillas were shot and 130 captured, this would exceed the figure of "150 to 400 members" reported to the National Assembly by the Interior Minister around mid-January 1949,[^7] suggesting that the armed guerrillas were completely annihilated by this operation's results. However, the victims were mostly unarmed civilians, and among them were the elderly and vulnerable who could not flee quickly enough. Having lost their homes in fires set by soldiers, residents who were passing the winter in caves and forests near their villages were mass-murdered in dawn raids by soldiers. The residents of Pongge, Yonggang, and Hoech'ŏn-ri had already lost all their homes to fire, and with the incident on that day resulting in mass killings of residents, the area became a "desolate wasteland."

[^4]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1055, February 4, 1949.
[^5]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1077, March 3, 1949.
[^6]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, February 9, 1949.
[^7]: Kungnumin Hoeŭi-rok, 10th Session, January 17, 1949.

**[p. 316]**

However, Colonel Ham Pyŏng-sŏn, after the situation had largely ended, also renamed the village after his own surname when residents gathered to rebuild the settlement. Currently the area has returned to its original name, but for a time this region was called Hamyŏng-ri. That is, the village name was changed to "Hamyŏng-ri (咸明里)" by combining the surname of Colonel Ham Pyŏng-sŏn and the given name of Captain Kim Myŏng (金明), an operations staff officer.[^8] Meanwhile, on that very day when the 2nd Regiment paraded its results of shooting 360 armed guerrillas and capturing 130 as prisoners in a joint Army-Navy-Air Force operation, soldiers transporting rifles in another area suffered significant losses in an armed guerrilla ambush. The military was in the process of moving 150 Type 99 Japanese rifles divided between two trucks near Kimnŭng-ri, Kuija-myŏn, when it was ambushed by the armed guerrillas. On that day, the military lost all 150 rifles and suffered 15 deaths.[^9] It is difficult to determine precisely when the armed guerrillas received decisive blows and reached a state of complete annihilation, but based on press reports and other sources, it appears to have been around mid-March when the 2nd Regiment undertook mountain mopping-up operations. Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk, after completing his inspection of Cheju and returning, told reporters: "Since February 15, the military has begun searching from coastal settlements and gradually advanced toward Hallasan. Settlements that have been searched and cleared are turned over to the police, while units have launched a general offensive against the main bandit forces on the southwestern side of Hallasan. The armed bandits on Cheju number approximately 260, and in addition, the common people following them number 20 civilians per rifle, carrying bamboo spears. Through the mopping-up campaign thus far, the bandits are fleeing into mountain valleys."[^10] One magazine even introduced this operation as a cause of "complete annihilation." That is, having obtained information that the armed guerrillas' main force was hiding in the hilly area of Namwŏn-myŏn, the 2nd Regiment went to the site on the night of February 15, set up camp, and conducted a surprise attack at dawn on the 16th, achieving the results of shooting 160 men. The magazine further recorded: "Having abandoned more than half of the armed guerrillas' corpses as they fled, a few dozen armed guerrillas might have avoided serious or moderate injuries, but regarding the accumulated corpses of unarmed civilians and prisoners, these are not counted—thus the scale of this triumph was immeasurable.
