After receiving this annihilating blow, there were no such downhill raids as before."[^11]

[^8]: Cheju Sinbo, February 5, 1955.
[^9]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1061, February 11, 1949.
[^10]: Kyŏnghyang Sinmun, March 17, 1949.
[^11]: Sŏ Chae-gwŏn, "Account of Suppression of Cheju Island" (Pyŏngnyan Cheju-do Kijhaeng), *Sinthǔnji* September 1949, pp. 176–177.

**[p. 317]**

Meanwhile, that the press failed to function properly amid the clashes between suppression forces and armed guerrillas contributed greatly to the mass slaughter of innocent unarmed civilians. Like the 9th Regiment, the 2nd Regiment controlled the press. All reports concerning the military had to be reviewed by the Military Police Headquarters except for official announcements,[^12] so the central government had no knowledge whatsoever that innocent residents were being sacrificed on Cheju. In the National Assembly, only the aftereffects of the Yŏsun Incident were intensively discussed, and regarding the Cheju incident, only the government's one-sided announcements continued. The damage to residents caused by the police was also severe. The imprisonment of an acting prosecutor at the time indicates the degree of harm suffered by ordinary residents. Prosecutor Yang Ŭl (梁乙), who completed his prosecutor training at the Seoul District Prosecutor's Office and was appointed to the Cheju Local Prosecutor's Office as of February 23, 1949, was arrested on February 28 by the Criminal Investigations Division of the Cheju Police Agency while preparing to depart for Cheju, and was transported there. He was abducted without a warrant issued by a judge. In particular, this incident—in which a serving prosecutor was arrested without prior approval from the Minister of Justice—caused great turmoil in judicial circles. Prosecutor General Kwŏn Sŭng-yŏl urgently met with the Minister of Justice to discuss countermeasures, and the Seoul District Prosecutor's Chief expressed regret over the police's actions. Moreover, the Supreme Prosecutor's Office coordinated with the Public Security Bureau and sent a telegram to the Cheju Provincial Police Agency requesting the release of Prosecutor Yang Ŭl.[^13] However, Vice-Minister of the Interior Kim Hyo-sŏk immediately issued a statement claiming that Prosecutor Yang Ŭl had been identified as a party related to certain criminal incidents during his residence on Cheju around late October 1948, and was arrested pursuant to a formal detention warrant issued by a judge of the Cheju District Court. Vice-Minister Kim further threatened that the article claiming "police illegally arrested a serving prosecutor" was without factual basis, and that because this damaged the police's prestige, they were investigating the source of the article.[^14] However, contrary to Vice-Minister Kim's claim, Prosecutor Yang Ŭl was formally detained on March 14, 1949.[^15] The Cheju District Prosecutor's Office stated that it was difficult to conduct proceedings with Prosecutor Yang Ŭl being a local prosecutor, and transferred the case to the Kwangju District Prosecutor's Office on May 30. Prosecutor Yang Ŭl faced charges of violating U.S. Military Government Ordinance No. 19, conspiracy for insurrection, and aiding and abetting, but at a public trial held in the Kwangju District Court on October 31, 1949,

[^12]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, January 15, 1949.
[^13]: Kyŏnghyang Sinmun, March 1 and 4, 1949; Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, March 4, 1949.
[^14]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, March 6, 1949.
[^15]: Tonggwang Sinmun, November 2, 1949.

**[p. 318]**

received a not-guilty verdict (a not-guilty sentence).[^282] Thus Prosecutor Yang, who had suffered greatly after being illegally arrested on February 28, was released after eight months in custody. Just before his transfer from the Jeju District Prosecutors' Office to the Gwangju District Prosecutors' Office on May 25, he had already been removed from office.[^283] In present times, it is unimaginable for police to illegally arrest and torture a sitting prosecutor. However, during the Yŏsu Incident, police conducted immediate executions of sitting prosecutors, so that the overwhelming power of the police over the prosecution was a nationwide phenomenon of the time. Because of this, on February 11, 1949, during a cabinet meeting attended by President Syngman Rhee, direct confrontations occurred between the Minister of Interior and the Minister of Justice.

**Administrative situation report (Minister of Interior)**: The police are arresting red elements and other national security offenders and transmitting the cases to the prosecutors' office, but since they receive not-guilty or light sentences, the police are losing face and harm is being done to national interest, so there is much dissatisfaction—requesting coordination of assistance.

**On military and police discipline and supervision (Minister of Justice)**: The military has recently taken an arrogant stance of "myself alone under heaven and above heaven," showing contempt for all others and causing severe damage to civilians. Under Japanese rule, when the military requisitioned civilian goods, compensation was provided; now we have reached the point where even compensation is not given. The police also take prosecutors to unknown locations (in Jeju, a prosecutor was lured away by a police official and was missing for four months, and a prosecutor general was missing for several weeks)—alarming incidents are occurring, and public resentment is gradually rising. We request that Your Excellency the President personally and strictly supervise this.[^284]

When the Minister of Interior expressed dissatisfaction with the lenient measures of the prosecution, the Minister of Justice immediately pointed out the unlawful excesses of the military and police and requested the President to exercise supervision. However, President Syngman Rhee showed no response to the Minister of Justice's request. Rather, Syngman Rhee sided with the police. On January 21, through a cabinet meeting, Syngman Rhee had already ordered "suppress harshly and uncover the root causes of the Jeju Island incident." And just over ten days after the Interior Minister and Justice Minister clashed in the cabinet meeting, on February 23, the Prosecutor General issued the following official dispatch to subordinate agencies, showing that Syngman Rhee had sided with the police's hard-line position.

**Regarding the handling of leftist cases**: With respect to the above matter, since the establishment of the Republic of Korea government, through the enactment of the National Security Act and repeated statements of national policy, we believe it should be handled without any regret. However, according to recent intelligence reports, in some localities the handling of incidents obstructing national reconstruction has been excessively lenient, creating cause for afterthought—in some cases without absence. Therefore, especially with concern for the firm stability of the national foundation, we request both thorough investigation and severe punishment. For cases violating the National Security Act, regardless of severity, cases shall be reported in detail upon reception, and settlement results shall also be reported fully without omission, and this is requested.[^285]

The Prosecutor General was instructing that the prosecution should not treat violators of the National Security Act leniently but rather with strong measures. This shows that, merely over ten days after the Minister of Interior attacked the Justice Minister for insufficient treatment of so-called "red elements," the Justice Ministry's position had drastically changed.

On February 19, Police Captain Kim Tae-il (金泰日), who led some 500 reinforcement police to Jeju, returned to Seoul on May 18. In a return report on the operations conducted over the preceding three months, he reported: "Since being dispatched to Jeju Island, through more than 140 operations we completely eliminated the bandits, and returned without a single casualty."[^286] A few days later, the Deputy Minister of Interior released more specific details: △The results were 495 bandits killed, 1,524 captured; △Friendly forces casualties were 2 seriously wounded and 3 slightly wounded.[^287] While it is said that the military took responsibility for mountain areas and police focused mainly on guarding coastal villages, the fact that after conducting some 140 operations over three months there were zero police casualties raises questions about what these "operations" actually were. Meanwhile, the 2nd Regiment's harsh suppression campaign lasted approximately two months until early March 1949. The armed guerrillas had already been decimated or scattered in small groups, barely surviving day to day. Yet still many residents remained near the mid-mountain villages. In the reality where discovery by military and police meant immediate execution on the spot, residents were being chased about, unable to go up or come down.

**[p. 319]**

**4. The Stabilization Period (March 2, 1949 – June 24, 1950)**

**a. Combined suppression and propaganda operations**

**1) Establishment of the Jeju District Combat Command**

On March 2, 1949, the Jeju District Combat Command was established. Colonel Yu Jae-hung was appointed commander, and 2nd Regiment Commander Major Ham Byŏng-sŏn was appointed chief of staff.[^1] The status of the stationed forces was elevated. Commander Yu Jae-hung was given authority to command the existing 2nd Regiment stationed in Jeju, a guerrilla unit, the Jeju police, reinforcement police, and rightist youth organizations. The Police Special Unit (reinforcement police, under the command of Police Captain Kim Tae-il) had already been dispatched on February 19 with 505 personnel.[^2] According to a U.S. military intelligence report, as of April 1, the suppression forces under Commander Yu's command were organized with 2,622 Korean military, 1,700 police, and approximately 50,000 Minbodan (Civil Defense Units).[^3] Also, the Army Headquarters, receiving cooperation from the Navy, sent warships to Jeju so that Yu Jae-hung's Combat Command could conduct joint Army-Navy operations.[^4] The Navy dispatched to Jeju at this time was the 3rd Special Naval Unit (under the command of Major Nam Sang-hui), which took on the mission of naval blockade.[^5]

The reason for establishing the Jeju District Combat Command despite the armed guerrillas being almost decimated by the 9th Regiment and then the 2nd Regiment's harsh suppression was, above all, the scheduled May 10 reelection. The fact that the Combat Command was abolished on May 15, immediately after the reelection was safely concluded, makes its mission clear. Furthermore, among the reasons President Syngman Rhee showed impatience in solving the Jeju problem were not only the May 10 reelection but also the U.S. aid issue. As U.S. troop withdrawal scheduled for the end of June approached, President Syngman Rhee was very anxious because arms aid and economic aid were not going smoothly. On April 9, 1949, when President Syngman Rhee visited Jeju, he said, "Both the government and the Americans are always very concerned about Jeju, and relief supplies will be delivered soon."[^6] The reason this President consciously emphasized "American concern about the Jeju incident" appears to be because America's interest and will in making Korea an anti-communist state was substantial. This is well expressed in a proviso clause of the "Korean Aid Bill." At that time, President Truman, emphasizing the importance of Korea as a democratic bulwark in the Far East, requested that the House of Representatives pass a 150 million dollar aid bill for Korea.[^7] This "Korean Aid Bill" was passed by the House Foreign Affairs Committee in a closed session on June 30 after debate amid Republican opposition.[^8] However, the House of Representatives appended a proviso clause to the bill stating: "Notwithstanding any other provision of any other law, if the authorities administering this Act determine that a coalition government that includes one or more members of the Communist Party or members of the party currently controlling the North Korean regime has been organized in the Republic of Korea, aid under this Act shall be immediately terminated."[^9] In response, President Syngman Rhee expressed this in a cabinet meeting the next day as "anti-communist-condition aid bill for Korea."[^10]

However, there is one fact that must be noted here. The Jeju District Combat Command was established from March 2 to May 15, 1949, but Commander Yu Jae-hung was not in Jeju the entire time. A U.S. military secret document states: "Colonel Yu was detailed as (commander of) Jeju Island in early March and, after having a series of meetings with high officials including Lee Bŏm-sŏk and Army Commander General Chae Byŏng-dŏk, arrived (in Jeju) in the last week of March and took command."[^11] Thus, Commander Yu did not arrive in Jeju on March 2 but in the last week of March. Therefore, Commander Yu arrived sometime between March 27 (Sunday) and March 31 (Thursday). Contemporary newspaper articles also support this fact. Until the end of March 1949, the name "Yu Jae-hung" does not appear at all in Jeju incident-related articles, and the name "Ham Byŏng-sŏn" still appears as the highest-ranking official in charge of the stationed forces in Jeju.[^12] The first appearance of "Yu Jae-hung" in articles on the Jeju incident was in early April when Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo visited the island.[^13]

Thus the operations of the Jeju District Combat Command can be divided into two periods: Period One (March 2 – last week of March), where 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn led the operations, and Period Two (last week of March – May 15), where Commander Yu Jae-hung finally arrived in Jeju and personally directed the suppression operations. In actual circumstances, there are clear differences between Period One and Period Two. Regarding the operational policy of the first month after the establishment of the Jeju District Combat Command, 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn stated: "The military forces, which had been conducting cautious and lenient operations, now made a final decision and entered the third stage of armed annihilation—the sweep campaign from March 1 to the end of March was this."[^13]

The Jeju District Combat Command Press Bureau released the following statement about the early operations after the establishment of the command (March 9–15):

On the 16th, the Press Bureau of the Jeju District Combat Command announced the results of operations from March 9, when the 2nd Regiment of the Korean military moved to the third stage of operations and struck back at the bandits making their final desperate resistance, through the 15th, as follows:
- Killed: 181; Captured: 195; Rifles: 3; Carbines: 3; Type 99 rifles: 12; Hand grenades: 2; Ammunition: 34, etc. Seized. [From Jeju—Joint] (Department of Defense, Press Bureau—Censored)[^14]

A National Assembly member who inspected Jeju around this time reported: "At 10 a.m. on March 12, a general assault was commenced."[^15] Regarding this, the Department of Defense Press Bureau reported for March 12–19 the results in the Jeju district: "Jeju district—(Results) Killed: 171; Captured: 196; M1 rifles: 3; Carbines: 3; Type 99 rifles: 13; Live ammunition: 15 rounds. (Losses) Minbodan: 1 killed in action, 3 wounded."[^16]

The comprehensive results announced by the Department of Defense Press Bureau for March (March 1–31) were: "△Results = Killed: 821; Captured: 999; Weapons: 35 guns; Ammunition: 30 rounds; Spears: 12; Food: 49 baskets; Japanese swords: 4; Steel helmets: 16; Communication equipment: 2 units; Secret documents: 166; Rosters: 185. △Friendly forces losses = Military: 27 killed in action, 15 wounded; Police: 17 killed in action; Civilian: 1 killed in action."[^17]

The fact that the captured weapons were so few compared to the numbers of those killed and captured speaks to the nature of the so-called "annihilation campaign" conducted during March under Ham Byŏng-sŏn's command. Among those killed and captured were large numbers of unarmed civilians who had fled to the mountains to save their lives and were hiding in fear of when they might be caught and killed.

**[p. 320]**

Related to this, a U.S.-Korean military report cited the Korean government's announcement that for the period from March 1 to April 30, the suppression forces' results were: "Rebels killed: 1,075; rebels captured: 3,509; rebels surrendered: 2,065." The report then commented: "Most of the 'rebels' mentioned in this report could only be established when the suppression forces automatically classified all residents in the remote inland mountain areas of the island as 'rebels.'"[^18]

The practice was to first classify people in the mid-mountain areas as "rebels," then kill and capture them, and report these as "results." Such operations resulted in massive civilian casualties. A U.S. military report recorded that as of the end of March 1949: "An estimated 14,000–15,000 residents died during the past year, at least 80% of whom were killed by the suppression forces. About one-third of the houses on the island were destroyed, and of the island's 300,000 residents, about one-quarter were evacuated to the coast after their villages were destroyed."[^19]

Commander Yu Jae-hung of the Jeju District Combat Command, who did not actually arrive in Jeju until the last week of March, carried out propaganda and persuasion operations while repositioning forces that had been stationed in coastal villages to the mountain areas. The regiment headquarters and operational forces remained in Jeju town; the 1st Battalion was advanced to deployment near Suamgyo Bridge (水岳橋) on the current East-West crossway, the 2nd Battalion to Kwan'ŭm Temple, the 3rd Battalion to Kyorye-ri in Choch'ŏn District, and the special unit to Norŭ Peak, etc., advancing to deployment in mountain areas.[^20] The special unit referred to here was a 50-man unit under the command of Captain Kim Myŏng, the former operations staff officer of the 2nd Regiment, an organization that roamed the mountain areas and, when encountering armed guerrillas, would gather intelligence while speaking in Jeju dialect.[^21] In the coastal villages, while assembling residents, the suppression and propaganda combined operation was conducted by displaying reconnaissance aircraft flying overhead or conducting demonstration firings of rocket mortars to display force.[^22]

Regarding the operations at this time, Yu Jae-hung stated:

> When I went to Jeju Island, there were about 20,000 refugees in the mountains. And on the coast were police and soldiers, while in the mountains were communist bandits and refugees, with groups separated from each other, and at night there would be curses and fighting. So I said, "Soldiers must absolutely go up into the mountains and eliminate the communist bandits," and I moved three battalions and one guerrilla battalion—four battalions in total—to the heart of Mount Halla. At first, each battalion had its own combat area, so the battalions moved about and conducted sweeps. Finally, I maneuvered the four battalions myself and conducted operations.[^23]

Commander Yu Jae-hung drew up an operational plan to capture the remaining armed guerrillas and particularly to bring down the "approximately 20,000 refugees." A U.S. military report dated April 1, 1949, suggested future operational methods through the following "comment":

> Despite the overall outlook currently being hopeful, in any case, some 250 armed rebels who remain unsuppressed or without their weapons seized will result in unilateral cessation of Korean military operations, leading to outbreak of new terrorism. One captured rebel leader stated that the current policy of his fellows is to hide until the military withdraws, and when that time comes, they will resume their attacks. According to intelligence reports, Korean military leaders are attempting full operations to find the rebels in their hiding places, but since the guerrillas continue moving and have ceased activity, the effects are nil. The current plan is to completely eliminate the guerrillas before mid-April, which is somewhat optimistic. Perhaps it would be necessary to use a different plan currently under consideration: forming lines with military units and Minbodan that completely traverse the island, sweeping the mountains like combing them, driving the rebels to the opposite side of the island where police are positioned. Such operations, given distance and difficult terrain, would take approximately one month but would be effective.[^24]

So from April 1949 onward, the operation was to root out the remaining 250 armed guerrillas by mobilizing Minbodan members to sweep the entire island like a comb. The Jeju District Combat Command, established on March 2 and concluding its mission on May 15, had its comprehensive results for the more than two-month period reported by the press as follows:

**Remarkable progress—According to the announcement by the Jeju combat forces, the comprehensive annihilation results and losses of the unit are as follows.**

**Results (March 1 – May 11)**
- △Weapons seized: Automatic rifles 3, M1 rifles 25, Carbines 28, Type 99 rifles 106, Type 38 rifles 2, Type 44

**[p. 321]**

rifles 4, Pistols 9, Broken guns 25, Other weapons 224
- △Captured and surrendered personnel: 6,117; Killed: 1,117
- △Friendly forces losses: 35 killed in action, 17 wounded, 1 Minbodan killed[^25]

Meanwhile, Police Captain Kim Tae-il, leading some 500 police special unit members drawn from various places, was dispatched to Jeju on February 19 and returned to Seoul on May 18. The Deputy Minister of Interior disclosed the following regarding the results of operations by this police special unit, which operated in Jeju for a similar period to the Jeju District Combat Command:

From February 24 to May 14, the police results were: △Bandits killed: 495; Captured: 1,524; American-made machine guns: 1; M-1, Type 38, Type 99, Carbines seized: about 50; Food supplies: 950 baskets; Clothing: 1,034 items; Bedding: 207 items; Dishes: 834 items; Furniture: 455 items confiscated. △Friendly forces losses = 2 seriously wounded, 3 slightly wounded, 2 vehicles damaged.[^26]

In total, combining the military and police results: captured and surrendered personnel totaled 7,641, and killed totaled 1,612. Yet the government authorities' stated number of armed guerrillas remained "approximately 250" despite the results of extensive suppression operations. This indicates that while the civilian casualties were not as great as during the initial suppression operations of the 9th and 2nd Regiments, the so-called "annihilation campaign" under 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn continued until the end of March 1949, at the beginning of the Jeju District Combat Command's operations.

**2) Propaganda and persuasion operations**

From the time the Jeju District Combat Command first began operations on March 2, the necessity for "propaganda and persuasion operations" arose alongside strong suppression. On March 10, Prime Minister Lee Bŏm-sŏk, who inspected Jeju, suggested future operational policy: "Conduct suppression efficiently and at the same time actively carry out propaganda and persuasion operations, and through a lenient attitude urge the bandits to reflect."[^27]

Prime Minister Lee's statement appears to have had great impact on the island residents. A witness recounted the Premier's lecture at that time as follows:

> Prime Minister Lee Bŏm-sŏk gave a lecture in front of Kwan'dŏk Hall. Since he was a military man, I expected even more brutal statements. But it was completely different from what Cho Pyŏng-ok said. Prime Minister Lee said, "An American military officer showed me several photos of residents being executed by mass firing, and I didn't know it was this tragic." He didn't reflect on the strong suppression campaign or particularly comfort the island residents, but compared to what Syngman Rhee and Cho Pyŏng-ok had said before, I was amazed that he even said that much.[^28]

The propaganda and persuasion policy arose from a recognition that the mid-mountain villages had already been laid waste and the armed guerrillas were nearly decimated. Yu Jae-hung described the situation when he first arrived in Jeju: "In any case, from the coast up, everything had been burned and was gone, and in the mountains, I received reports that there were approximately 20,000 refugees and about 230 armed communist bandits."[^29]

A Ministry of Defense publication described the situation at this time: "Since the settlements within 4 km of the coast on Mount Halla had been laid to waste by the military and police suppression operations, most residents had fled to the mountains and were colluding with the bandits. Approximately 15,000 of these people were brought down from the mountains, accommodated, and civil relief work was conducted."[^30]

When Commander Yu Jae-hung of the Jeju District Combat Command had just returned to Seoul after completing his mission in Jeju, he also revealed his experience: "Among the bandits, the truly communist communist party members were extremely few, and most were swept up in ignorance. We made efforts to convert them to surrender so that no blood would be shed."[^31]

Also, to bring down residents hidden in the mountains and completely end the incident, civilian relief measures were required. Father Austin Sweeney, a priest in Jeju, wrote to a priest in Seoul: "If this were a civilized country, we would immediately implement extensive 'help Jeju Island' plans. The residents are living like beasts, and on average eat only one sweet potato per day."[^32] The condition of Jeju at that time was that tragic.

**[p. 322]**

On March 10, Interior Minister Shin Sŏng-mo, who inspected Jeju with Prime Minister Lee Bŏm-sŏk, pointed out: "The urgent and difficult problems now facing us, aside from the above operations, are the relief of disaster victims and those who have surrendered, who are increasing daily. If we do not prepare food, clothing, and housing for them, they will return to the mountains and join the bandits. The responsibility for this is grave."[^33]

A National Assembly member who inspected Jeju around this time described the actual condition of the disaster victims as follows:

> The number of disaster victims is 86,797. These disaster victims, without opportunity to transport food and clothing, fled in a hurry, so they live directly on the bare ground with straw laid down in houses made literally like pig pens. They barely survive day by day on seaweed and wild vegetables. Without exception they suffer from hunger, and both inside and outside the houses reek with unbearable stench. When we visited to comfort them, they only looked to the heavens and wept; it was impossible to witness this pitiful sight without tears.[^34]

In response, the Jeju District Combat Command, while focusing on military operations, established a Civil Affairs Section within the command to solve the civilian administrative and economic problems arising at this time.[^35] Also, to comfort the residents, on March 18 an Army band was dispatched to Jeju.[^36]

However, as noted earlier, the operations of the Jeju District Combat Command are divided into Period One, where 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn led the suppression operations, and Period Two, after late March when Commander Yu Jae-hung arrived. The propaganda and persuasion operations began only in Period Two. A U.S. military secret document recorded the difference between Period One's "severe tactics" and Period Two's "program of amnesty" as follows:

> Immediately upon taking command, Colonel Yu (Jae-hung) changed the severe tactics of his predecessor Ham Byŏng-sŏn (which included the killing of all persons found in bandit areas regardless of possession of identity documents or weapons) and immediately initiated a program of amnesty. As many prisoners as possible were captured and interrogated by Colonel Yu himself. The prisoners showed signs of remorse, and if they had not been engaged as guerrilla combat personnel, they were released with food and tobacco. The results of this method to date have been satisfactory. Because the released prisoners will guide Colonel Yu's men to weapon caches, and they will spread word to their comrades that if they surrender, they will not all be killed but will be treated fairly. Colonel Yu estimated that each of the prisoners released under his amnesty program brought back at least six others.[^37]

Regarding this, Yu Jae-hung testified: "I prevented the excesses of the Northwest Youth Association members whom the Jeju Island people disliked, and carried out propaganda saying, 'We will not question past matters. Come down with peace of mind.' And when I actually did this in a few places, rumors spread and thousands came down daily, so 20,000 came down quickly."[^38]

Regarding the increase in "surrendered persons," Social Affairs Minister Yi Yun-yŏng, who inspected Jeju from April 7–13, said: "Surrendered persons are increasing nowadays. During just the one week I was there, 898 surrendered, and by April 13, a total of 3,500 had returned. There are 3,174 persons in the five accommodation facilities in Jeju."[^39] Surrendered persons continued to increase, reaching some 6,000 by May 11.[^40]

What kind of people, then, were the rapidly increasing prisoners and surrendered persons? President Syngman Rhee, around April 9, 1949, when the situation appeared to be easing as the armed guerrillas were decimated by harsh suppression, inspected Jeju Island and, upon returning, issued the following statement:

> (Omitted) Surrendered communist elements totaled 2,800, men and women combined, but there are still several thousand who have not dared come down out of fear. The most difficult problem is that many villages have been burned and people have nowhere to stay and nothing to eat or wear, reaching the point of death. (Omitted) Ignorant men and women, some of whom were deceived by communist propaganda, and others who, with their homes burned and nowhere to go, went back up the mountains, but unable to survive in the mountains and unable to forage, came down with their small children in a pitiful state—these 2,800 people. We accommodate them in a large public hall divided into sections and feed them two meals a day, though without side dishes….[^41]

The "2,800 surrendered communist elements" and the "pitiful men and women with their small children numbering 2,800" mentioned in the statement are the same people described differently. One witness testified to the process of becoming a "bandit" by going to the mountains in the fall of 1948 while attending elementary school, being captured by soldiers and becoming a "prisoner," and being confined in a prisoner-of-war camp set up in a distillery in April 1949, as follows:

> Because my parents left for Japan before the 4·3 Incident, my sister (age 4) and I (age 12) lived at grandfather's house. Around September–October 1948, my grandfather (Kim Ŏn-ban, age 61 at the time), who had left home to buy an ox for field work, was caught by soldiers of the 9th Regiment, robbed of money, and shot to death. Not long after, in the fall of 1948, police came to the village around 3–4 p.m. and ordered all men, women, young, and old to gather at the village hall. About 200–300 residents gathered, and the police ordered us to "close our eyes and bow our heads." After a while, when I raised my head, about ten residents were lined up in front. The police said "watch," and immediately shot the people who were led out. While we had our heads bowed, someone pointed—it must have been. After grandfather died and the village hall incident occurred, an uncle who had been missing appeared and said, "To survive now, everyone must go to the mountains." So around November 1948, our entire family—grandmother (in her 50s), uncle's wife, cousin (age 1), myself, and my sister—went to the mountains. At first, we were near Ryŏl'an Peak, but when the suppression forces approached, we climbed deeper into the mountains every day, searching for caves. In the mornings, when we woke, the ground would freeze our backs with a crunching sound—the cave was that cold. We ate only about one meal a day. And whenever we heard gunshots, we would tremble with enormous fear. Enduring this way, around April 1949, our cave was discovered by soldiers. When the soldiers fired shots into the cave, we all came out. We went to an agricultural school warehouse and then were moved to a distillery. Many people had been brought there. We were interrogated one by one. When I said I had no parents, the interrogator said "orphanage!" and classified me. But since my grandmother was present, I didn't go to the orphanage. If I had been just seventeen at the time, I would have died, but since I was only twelve and thin from malnutrition, completely a small child, I survived. But my uncle's wife (Hyŏn Gye-sŏng, age 26) had suffered severe cold in the mountains and, before being sent to the distillery, died. After graduating high school, I wanted to enter the military academy, but my teacher said, "Since you had such a thing in the past, you don't have the qualifications to go to the military academy," so I gave up.[^42]

Just as President Syngman Rhee himself stated in his statement, most of those who went to the mountains were "those deceived by communist propaganda or those who went back up because their homes were burned and they had nowhere to go." Yet these people had been indiscriminately shot to death as "bandits." Despite this, surrendered persons were thoroughly screened. A U.S. military report recorded the following regarding the status of handling surrendered persons as of April 1, 1949:

> The current policy is to detain all people who have come down from the mountains, whether they were captured during operations or voluntarily surrendered. Women, children, and the elderly are mostly classified as refugees, while fighting-age males are thoroughly screened and educated before being granted refugee status.[^43]

**[p. 323]**

Meanwhile, Colonel Yu Jae-hung left Jeju on May 13 after the May 10 reelection was safely conducted.[^44] The Jeju District Combat Command officially ended on May 15, handing over its mission to the 2nd Regiment.[^45] Additionally, the 3rd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment, uniquely composed solely of Northwest Youth Association members, withdrew from Jeju on May 15 along with the Combat Command.[^46] The withdrawal of the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion was carried out on the orders of Brigadier General Roberts, the U.S. military adviser's chief of staff. Brigadier General Roberts instructed Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo to have not only the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion but also those from Northwest Youth Association backgrounds in the police returned to the mainland and widely dispersed.[^47]

Initially, the Americans had praised and encouraged the participation and support of the Northwest Youth Association in both the military and police.[^48] However, after the situation had eased, as the excesses of the Northwest group persisted, resulting in aggravating the problem, the Americans belatedly withdrew them.

On May 18, the police special unit completed its three-month operations and returned to Seoul. With this, the tragic bloodshed began to subside somewhat. However, Colonel Yu Jae-hung had said during his propaganda and persuasion operations: "If you come down, we will not question past wrongs and will guarantee your life." Yet after Colonel Yu left Jeju, approximately 1,600 people were either shot to death or sent to prisons throughout the country.

After the abolition of the Jeju District Combat Command, Jeju again came under the responsibility of 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn. Simultaneously with the command's abolition and the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion's departure from Jeju, on May 25, the guerrilla battalion was detached and withdrawn, greatly reducing the military force.[^49] Subsequently, on June 20, the 2nd Regiment was separated from direct Army Headquarters command and incorporated into the Seoul Metropolitan Defense Command,[^50] and on July 7, it withdrew from Jeju and moved to Inchon (the complete withdrawal of the 1st Battalion was on August 13).[^51] Thus, Major Ham Byŏng-sŏn's stay in Jeju lasted approximately eight months from December 29, 1948, to July.

Meanwhile, after the founding ceremony of the National Guidance League was held in Seoul on June 5, 1949, the organization gradually expanded nationwide.[^52] The Guidance League was launched under the pretense of educating and guiding those who had renounced and converted from the South Korean Workers' Party. However, it originated from the intention to bind leftist forces within a single organization and control them. Unlike the central government, in Jeju most leftist and armed personnel, as well as those even slightly suspected by the military and police, had already been sacrificed in the 4·3 Incident, so there was no real need to organize the Guidance League anew. Yet within the nationwide movement promoted by the government, it took organizational form. The Guidance League set "weeks for voluntary surrender of converts" twice from October 25 to the end of November, launching a major recruitment campaign. The number announced by the Guidance League as of November 27, 1949, was 39,986 nationally and 5,283 for Jeju.[^53] When the Korean War broke out, Guidance League members were immediately placed on preventive detention and executed by mass firing (the specific details are organized in Part IV, Section 2, "Killing of Preventively Detained Persons" of this report).

**3) Conducting the May 10 reelection**

With the armed guerrillas largely decimated and the situation entering a stabilization phase, President Syngman Rhee began preparations to hold the reelection for the two Jeju election districts' National Assembly members that had failed the previous year. Around mid-March 1949, Public Information Director Kim Tong-sŏng conveyed the President's intention to conduct the reelection in the two districts on May 10.[^54]

As the reelection date approached, visits by government officials streamed in. On April 7, Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo[^55] and Social Affairs Minister Yi Yun-yŏng came to Jeju under the President's special orders. They announced: "The President's special orders are to quickly annihilate the remaining armed forces and devise measures for Jeju's economic reconstruction."[^56]

On April 9, President Syngman Rhee himself visited Jeju with his wife to check on the public order situation. The President attended a welcoming rally of tens of thousands in the square in front of Kwan'dŏk Hall and stated: "I hear there are still bandits, which is very regrettable. Therefore, the military, police, and civilians who have received these two ministers should work together to quickly build peace in Jeju," urging rapid suppression of the incident. The President also said: "Both the government and the Americans are always very concerned about Jeju, and relief supplies will be delivered soon."[^57]

Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo returned on the 14th after completing inspections of Jeju and various units throughout the regions, and on the 15th met with the press corps, stating: "The bandits on Mount Halla in the Jeju district have all been dispersed, and as the President said, complete annihilation suppression is a matter of time. I encouraged the troops in the field with the final order that not even one should remain and all should be annihilated," indicating that the Jeju situation was stabilizing.[^58]

Before conducting the reelection, the UN Commission on Korea went to the site on April 18 to check on public order conditions, but due to fog over Jeju airspace, they were unable to land and returned.[^59] They arrived in Jeju on May 8, two days before the reelection, observed the election, and returned on the 14th.[^60]

In addition to local figures, Democratic National Party Propaganda Director Ham Sang-hun (咸尙勳) also ran as a candidate for the two election districts—North Jeju District A and B.[^61] Even Northwest Youth Association Central Commander Mun Bong-je (文鳳濟) ran as a candidate in North Jeju District B.[^62]

The reelection conducted on May 10 proceeded without incident, unlike a year before. Voter registration rates were 96% in District A and 97% in District B, much higher than a year before. Voting rates also recorded 97% in District A and 99% in District B.[^63]

The election results showed Hong Sun-nyŏng (洪淳寧) winning in North Jeju District A and Yang Pyŏng-jik (梁秉直) winning in District B.[^64]

**b. Weakening of the armed forces**

**1) Death of Lee Tŏk-ku**

As a result of the 9th Regiment's and 2nd Regiment's strong suppression from mid-November 1948, followed by the Jeju District Combat Command's "mountain annihilation campaigns" and "surrender operations" established in March 1949, the armed force's strength reached an almost completely decimated state. On April 7, 1949, Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo, who inspected Jeju, received a report from the Combat Command: "Even several days ago, many important leaders of the red bandits in the mountains surrendered. Based on their statements and our patrol reconnaissance and judgment, the remaining armed bandits currently number only 40–50."[^65]

Around that time, the press reported the words of a former armed force member and prisoner: "By March, Lee Tŏk-ku and his remaining twenty-some followers had buried their weapons in the ground and were scattered, and each was watching for an opportunity to surrender."[^66]

Subsequently, in mid-April, important cadres of the armed force were killed or captured, and the armed force substantially disintegrated. Press Director Major Lee Ch'ang-jŏng of the Jeju District Combat Command's press bureau stated: "On April 21, we finally killed Kim Yong-gwan, the leader of the South Korean Workers' Party. With this, the annihilation campaign was completely concluded."[^67]

On June 7, Lee Tŏk-ku, the armed force's supreme leader, was killed. Although the armed force had already been disintegrated, Lee Tŏk-ku was a symbolic figure of the armed force following Kim Dal-sam, so his death had great impact. Encouraged by this, the Ministry of Defense announced that it had been killed by the 2nd Regiment's operations.[^68]

However, Lee Tŏk-ku was killed in police operations. The press reported the news of Lee Tŏk-ku's death as follows:

> **[From Jeju—Joint]** The rebel commander Lee Tŏk-ku (李德九) was shot to death by a police unit on the 7th. Specifically, the police unit under the command of Police Sergeant Kim Yŏng-ju (金英柱) of the Hwa-Buk Sub-station of the Jeju Police Station fought with the rebel commander Lee Tŏk-ku's unit in the jungle near the so-called "little stingray area" on the 7th at approximately 4 p.m. and shot Lee Tŏk-ku to death, while capturing one of his close attendants. The corpse of Lee Tŏk-ku is presently being kept at the Jeju Police Station.[^69]

The police tied Lee Tŏk-ku's corpse to a wooden cross and displayed it in front of the Jeju Police Station for one day, then cremated it. Kang Sil (康實), Lee Tŏk-ku's maternal nephew, who, at the police's request, confirmed the identity of the corpse of his maternal uncle, testified as follows:

> The corpse was clean except for one bullet wound to the temple. The corpse was displayed for only one day. Since it was hung in the rainy season from morning, the smell was quite strong by evening. The police cremated the corpse at the Southern Warehouse and then informed my father, saying, "At least collect the bones." However, the next day there was heavy rain, which washed everything away, so we couldn't collect anything.[^70]

**2) Replacement of stationed units**

**① Independent Battalion**

On July 15, 1949, the "1st Independent Battalion" under the command of Major Kim Yong-ju (金龍周) moved to Jeju and assumed the mission of the 2nd Regiment.[^71] The 1st Independent Battalion was the successor to the Army Reconnaissance School. The Army Reconnaissance School (commander Kim Yong-ju[^72]) had, with the guerrilla battalion under the school commander's command, moved to Sanchŏk in South Kyŏngsan Province by January 31, 1949, and participated in operations in the Jiri Mountain district under the command of the 5th Brigade Commander,[^73] and when the Jiri Mountain district Combat Unit (commander Chŏng Il-gwŏn) was organized on February 27, this guerrilla battalion was included,[^74] a combat unit that had gained actual combat experience mainly in the Jiri Mountains. Subsequently, on June 20, it was redesignated as the "1st Independent Infantry Battalion,"[^75] and on July 15, it moved from its stationed location in Suwŏn, Kyŏnggi Province, to Jeju (the official assumption of all duties related to Jeju Island security from the 2nd Regiment occurred on August 13, 1949[^76]).

The Independent Battalion was stationed in Jeju when the situation had already substantially stabilized and there was no reason for human casualties to occur. The Independent Battalion positioned officers in Jeju Girls' Middle School and Jeju Middle School and conducted military training for students.[^77]

However, the unit commander Kim Yong-ju testified about his "final annihilation campaign" as follows:

> I renamed the unit the 1st Independent Battalion and went to Jeju Island and conducted the final annihilation campaign. Other units had completed suppressing communist bandits, and we did the final cleanup. At that time, I held the rank of major and was appointed Jeju District Martial Law Commander, and my assignment was to the 5th Division. There I conducted the final annihilation campaign, and I conducted operations better than other units. How did I do it? I placed one plainclothes company—in various organizations such as district offices, drivers, teachers, etc. Some of these people are now in positions like vice-principal. Also, some people from district posts advanced and went to the provincial office…. They gathered information. So by just staying in the unit, all information came in. In that way, I made everything completely clean. Regarding those arrested there, when they went to trial, they would almost certainly be executed. It was my job to deal with them. So these people regarded me as someone who should kill them…. How could I bring them before the martial law commander? So I made a structure based on law, dug a cave west of Jeju airfield, and processed everyone there. So I can't go to Jeju now. I just followed orders, but those people will say I killed them. In that way, Jeju was completely pacified.[^78]

The testimony describes placing soldiers in civilian clothing and infiltrating various organizations to set traps. Moreover, the statement—"Regarding those arrested there, when they went to trial, they would almost certainly be executed. It was my job to deal with them…. So I made a structure based on law, dug a cave west of Jeju airfield, and processed everyone there"—essentially describes executions without any judicial process. A more serious problem is that even though the martial law in Jeju had been lifted on December 31, 1948, it had been kept in effect. Kim Yong-ju referred to himself as a "martial law commander."

The Independent Battalion completed approximately five months of stay in Jeju on December 27, 1949, and withdrew from the island.

**[p. 324]**

and were fighting. So I told the soldiers to unconditionally go up the mountain and conduct anti-Communist operations, and I transferred 3 regular battalions and 1 guerrilla battalion—4 battalions total—to the dense forest areas of Hallasan. Initially, since each battalion had its own combat area, each one conducted sweeps as they moved around, and finally I carried out operations by mobilizing the 4 battalions myself.[^1] Commander Yŏu Jae-ŭng established an operational plan for capturing the remaining armed guerrillas and, in particular, for getting down the mountain approximately 20,000 refugees. A U.S. military report dated April 1, 1949, suggested future operational policy through the following "comment":

Despite the current overall outlook being hopeful, the unilateral cessation of Korean military operations while approximately 250 armed insurgents remain unaccounted for or without captured weapons will nonetheless lead to the outbreak of new terrorism. A captured insurgent leader said that his colleagues' current policy is to remain hidden until the military withdraws, at which point they will resume attacks. According to intelligence reports, Korean military commanders are attempting to locate the insurgents in their hiding places through full-scale operations, but the guerrillas continue to move and have ceased their activities with little effect. The current plan to completely eliminate the guerrillas before mid-April is somewhat optimistic. It might be necessary to use a different plan now under consideration: forming a line with military units and Civil Defense Units that completely traverse the island, sweeping the mountain like a comb and driving the insurgents toward the police stationed on the opposite side of the island. Such operations would likely take about one month, given the distance and difficult terrain, but would be effective.[^2]

To ferret out the approximately 250 armed guerrillas, an operation from April 1949 onward was to sweep the entire island with the mobilization even of Civil Defense Unit members. One news outlet reported on the results from the establishment of the Cheju Island District Combat Command on March 2 through May 15 as follows:

**Shining glory: Unified sweep operation results**

According to announcements from the Cheju Island combat unit, the comprehensive sweep operation results and losses of the unit are as follows:

**War Trophies (March 1–May 11):** Weapons seized: 3 semi-automatic rifles, 25 M1 rifles, 28 carbines, 106 Type 99 rifles, 2 Type 38 rifles, 44 Type 44 rifles, 4 Type 30 rifles, 9 pistols, 25 defective rifles, 224 other weapons △Prisoners and defectors: 6,117, enemy killed: 1,117 △Friendly forces losses: 35 combat deaths, 17 wounded, 1 Civil Defense Unit member killed[^3]

**[p. 325]**

Meanwhile, Police Commissioner Kim T'ae-il arrived in Cheju on February 19, leading approximately 500 specially assigned police units from various locations, returning to Seoul on May 18. Regarding the war trophies of this police special unit, which conducted operations during a similar period to the Cheju Island District Combat Command, the Vice Minister of Interior announced as follows:

From February 24 to May 14, the police war trophies were as follows: △Insurgents killed: 495, prisoners: 1,524, American M-1 submachine gun: 1, seizure of approximately 50 M-1, Type 38, Type 99 rifles and carbines, food: 950 bags, clothing: 1,034 items, bedding: 207 items, tableware: 834 items, furniture: 455 items. △Enemy casualties: 2 seriously wounded, 3 lightly wounded, 2 vehicles damaged.[^4]

Combining the war trophies of both military and police, defectors and prisoners totaled 7,641 persons, and those killed numbered 1,612. Yet the number of armed guerrillas disclosed by government authorities remained consistently "approximately 250." While this did not represent losses of civilians as great as during the initial suppression operations of the 9th and 2nd Regiments, it indicates that the so-called "annihilation operations" of 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏng-sŏn continued through the end of March 1949, when the Cheju Island District Combat Command was first established.

## 2) Propaganda and Persuasion Operations

With the Cheju Island District Combat Command's first deployment on March 2, the need for "propaganda and persuasion operations" (sŏnmu kongja)—alongside the hardline military operations—was raised. On March 10, when Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk inspected Cheju, he stated: "While conducting suppression operations efficiently, propaganda and persuasion operations should be actively conducted, and the insurgents should be urged to reflect through a generous attitude."[^5] Prime Minister Yi's statement appears to have had a major impact on the islanders. One witness spoke about Yi Pŏm-sŏk's speech at that time as follows:

Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk gave a speech in front of Kwandŏk Pavilion. Since he came from a military background, I expected him to make even more ruthless remarks. But that wasn't the case at all. It was different from Cho Pyŏng-ok. Prime Minister Yi said that an American officer had shown him several photographs of residents being mass-executed, and he hadn't realized it was this tragic. While he did not express reflection on the hardline suppression operations or utter any special words of comfort to the islanders, compared to what Syngman Rhee and Cho Pyŏng-ok had said before, I was impressed that he said even that much.[^6]

The policy of propaganda and persuasion operations stemmed from a situation assessment that the highland villages had already been devastated and the armed guerrillas had been nearly annihilated. Upon his initial arrival in Cheju, Yŏu Jae-ŭng stated: "In any case, from the coastline upward everything had been burned and destroyed, and I received a report that in the mountains there were approximately 20,000 refugees and about 230 armed bandits."[^7] A publication by the Ministry of Defense noted regarding the situation at this time: "Most settlements within 4 kilometers from the coast to Hallasan had been devastated by military and police suppression operations, so most residents had taken refuge in the mountains and were consorting with insurgents. Approximately 15,000 of them were brought down from the mountains, received in facilities, and rehabilitation efforts were carried out."[^8]

Even when Cheju Island District Combat Commander Yŏu Jae-ŭng had just returned to Seoul after completing his mission in Cheju, he disclosed his experience: "Among the insurgents, true communists who had received communist education were extremely rare; many had been swept up in ignorance. We made efforts to ensure that these people surrendered without bloodshed."[^9]

Furthermore, in order to bring down the residents hidden in the mountains and completely end the incident, measures for civilian relief were required. Father Austin Sweeney on Cheju informed a priest in Seoul: "If this were a civilized country, a broad plan to 'help Cheju' would be implemented immediately. The residents are living like animals and on average eating just one sweet potato a day."[^10] To such an extent was the situation in Cheju at that time tragic.

Interior Minister Shin Sŏng-mo, who inspected Cheju together with Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk on March 10, pointed out: "The urgent and difficult problem at hand, apart from the aforementioned operations, is the relief of disaster victims and defectors. If we fail to prepare food, clothing, and shelter for the rapidly increasing numbers among them, they will return to the mountains and join the insurgents—our responsibility in this matter is grave."[^11]

One National Assemblywoman who inspected Cheju at this time described the actual conditions of the disaster victims as follows:

The number of disaster victims is 86,797. These disaster victims, having fled without time to transport food or clothing, are living literally on straw laid on the bare earth of pig-pen-like structures, barely maintaining life day by day on seaweed and wild mountain plants, groaning in hunger without exception, and both inside and outside the dwellings the stench is unbearable. When we offered condolences, they could only look up at the sky with tears streaming down their faces; one could not look upon this pitiful sight without tears.[^12]

Accordingly, the Cheju Island District Combat Command, while concentrating on military operations, established a Civil Administration Section (Minji-ch'u) within the command to address civilian administrative and economic problems arising at this time.[^13] Furthermore, to comfort residents, the Army Band was dispatched to Cheju on March 18.[^14]

However, as noted earlier, the Cheju Island District Combat Command's operations can be divided into two periods: the first period, when 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏng-sŏn led the suppression operations, and the second period following the arrival of Commander Yŏu Jae-ŭng in the last week of March. Propaganda and persuasion operations only began in the second period. A U.S. military classified document recorded the difference between the first period's "severe tactics" and the second period's "program of amnesty" as follows:

Upon assuming command, Colonel Yŏu immediately changed Colonel Ham Pyŏng-sŏn's severe tactics (which included killing all persons found in rebel areas regardless of whether they possessed identification or weapons) and promptly initiated a program of amnesty. Prisoners were captured whenever possible, and Colonel Yŏu interrogated them directly. The prisoners showed signs of remorse, and if they had not participated as active guerrilla combatants, they were released after being given food and tobacco. The results of this method to date are satisfactory, because the released prisoners will guide Colonel Yŏu's unit to weapons caches, and they will spread word to their comrades that if they surrender, they will not be executed but will be treated fairly. Colonel Yŏu estimates that each prisoner released brought a minimum of 6 more with them.[^15]

Regarding this, Yŏu Jae-ŭng testified: "By stopping the atrocities of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, whom the Cheju people disliked, while conducting propaganda saying, 'We will not inquire into the past; come down with peace of mind,' and this actually happened in several places, word spread and thousands came down every day, resulting in the 20,000 coming down very quickly."[^16]

Concerning the "defectors" increasing in this manner, Social Affairs Minister Yi Yun-yŏng, who inspected Cheju from April 7 to 13, stated: "Defectors are increasing these days. During just the one week that I was there, 898 people surrendered, and by April 13, a total of 3,500 had returned. In the five reception centers in Cheju Province, there were 3,174 persons."[^17] The number of defectors continued to increase, reaching approximately 6,000 by May 11.[^18]

**[p. 326]**

So what kind of people were these rapidly increasing prisoners and defectors? President Syngman Rhee, at a time when the armed guerrillas had been annihilated through hardline suppression operations and the incident was entering a phase of mitigation, made an inspection of Cheju on April 9, 1949, and upon returning issued the following statement:

(Omitted) Communist defectors, both male and female, have numbered 2,800, but there are still several thousand who are afraid to come down. The most difficult problem is that numerous villages have been burned, leaving no place to depend on and nothing to eat or wear, reaching the point of death. (Omitted) Some ignorant men and women were misled by communist propaganda, and some, having no place to go because their homes were burned, went back up the mountain. But they could not live on the mountain, nor could they forage, so in indescribable condition, men and women brought their small children down. These 2,800-odd people—I am housing them by dividing space in a large public building, and I am feeding them two meals a day, but of course without side dishes….[^19]

The "2,800 communists who surrendered" mentioned in the statement and "men and women in indescribable condition bringing their small children down—2,800 persons" represent the same people described differently. One witness testified to the process from the autumn of 1948, when, while a student at an elementary school, he went up the mountain "as a bandit," until he was captured by soldiers in April 1949 and became a "prisoner," eventually being confined to a prisoner reception center set up at a distillery:

My parents had gone to Japan before the 4·3 Incident, so my younger sister (age 4) and I (age 12) were living with our grandfather. Around September-October 1948, our grandfather (Kim Ŏn-ban, then age 61) went out to buy an ox to plow the field and was captured by 9th Regiment soldiers, who robbed him of money and then shot him. Not long after, on a certain autumn day in 1948, police came to the village around 3-4 p.m. and told everyone—men, women, old and young—to gather at the public meeting hall in Section 1, Ora. About 200-300 residents gathered, and the police ordered us to "close your eyes and bow your heads." After some time, when we lifted our heads, about ten residents had been dragged forward. The police told us, "Look well," and immediately shot the people who had been dragged out. While we were bowing our heads, someone pointed at us. After our grandfather passed away and the public meeting hall incident occurred, our uncle, who had been missing, appeared and said, "Now, to live, we all must go up the mountain." So around November 1948, our entire family—grandmother (in her 50s), uncle's wife, cousin (age 1), myself, and my younger sister—went up the mountain. At first we were near Yŏl'an Tŭiŏrŭm, but as the suppression forces approached, we climbed deeper and deeper into the mountains every day, looking for caves. In the mornings when we woke up, our backs were so frozen to the ground that we made a crunching sound. We ate only about one meal a day. Moreover, whenever we heard gunshots, we trembled so much our fear was overwhelming. Enduring like this, around April 1949, soldiers discovered our cave. As soldiers fired into the cave, we all came out. We were taken to an agricultural school warehouse and then moved to a distillery. There were many people there. They interrogated us one by one. When I said I had no parents, the interrogator said "Orphanage!" and classified me accordingly. But since my grandmother was there, I wasn't sent to an orphanage. If I had been just seventeen years old at that time, I would have died, but because I was only twelve and malnourished and very thin—completely a young child—I survived. However, my uncle's wife (Hyŏn Kye-saeng, age 26) suffered severely from a cold on the mountain and, after being captured, died before being sent to the distillery. I graduated from high school and wanted to apply to the Military Academy, but my teacher said, "Because of what happened to you in the past, you are not qualified to enter the Military Academy," and I gave up.[^20]

Just as President Syngman Rhee himself mentioned in his statement, most of those who entered the mountains were "people misled by communist propaganda or those who went back up because their homes burned and they had nowhere to go." Yet during all this time, they had been indiscriminately executed as "insurgents." Nevertheless, defectors underwent thorough screening. A U.S. military report recorded the situation regarding defector processing as of April 1, 1949, as follows:

The current policy is to detain all persons coming down from the mountains, whether captured during operations or voluntarily surrendering. Women, children, and elderly are for the most part classified as refugees, while military-age males are thoroughly screened and educated before being given refugee status.[^21]

**[p. 327]**

Meanwhile, Colonel Yŏu Jae-ŭng left Cheju on May 13 after the May 10 re-election was conducted safely.[^22] The Cheju Island District Combat Command formally ceased operations on May 15, transferring its responsibilities to the 2nd Regiment.[^23] Additionally, the 3rd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment, uniquely composed only of members of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, withdrew from Cheju together with the Combat Command on May 15.[^24]

The withdrawal of the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion was carried out under the direction of Brigadier General Roberts, chief of the U.S. Military Advisory Group. Brigadier General Roberts instructed Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo not only to return the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion to the mainland but also to repatriate those of Sŏbuk Youth Corps origin within the police and have them dispersed widely.[^25]

Initially, the U.S. military had praised and encouraged the military and police in bringing in and supporting the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. However, since the Youth Corps's atrocities persisted even after the incident had been mitigated, it worsened the situation. The U.S. consequently belatedly withdrew the Youth Corps.[^26] On May 18, the police special unit completed three months of operations and returned to Seoul. To this extent, the tragic incident was brought somewhat under control. Yet while Colonel Yŏu Jae-ŭng, during his propaganda and persuasion operations, had said, "If you come down from the mountains, we will not inquire into past crimes and will guarantee your lives," after Colonel Yŏu left, approximately 1,600 people were either executed or sent to prisons throughout the country.

After the dissolution of the Cheju Island District Combat Command, Cheju again fell under the responsibility of 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏng-sŏn. Coinciding with the dissolution of the Command, the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion left Cheju, and on May 25, the guerrilla battalion was reassigned and withdrawn, resulting in a significant reduction in troop strength.[^27] Subsequently, on June 20, the 2nd Regiment was relieved of direct Army Headquarters command and incorporated into the Capital Defense Command,[^28] and on July 7, it withdrew from Cheju and moved to Inchon (the complete withdrawal of the 1st Battalion occurred on August 13).[^29] Thus Colonel Ham Pyŏng-sŏn's period of station in Cheju, from his arrival on December 29, 1948, until this point, lasted slightly more than eight months.

Meanwhile, the founding proclamation meeting of the National Guidance League was held in Seoul on June 5, 1949, after which the organization gradually spread nationwide.[^30] The Guidance League was launched under the pretext of enlightening and guiding those who had renounced the South Korean Workers' Party, but it originated from an intention to bind leftwing forces within a single organization for purposes of control. Unlike the central authorities, in the Cheju area, having already experienced the 4·3 Incident, most people suspected even slightly in the eyes of the military and police, as well as those from the left and the armed guerrillas, had already been killed, so there was no fresh need to organize the Guidance League. However, in the context of nationwide movement being promoted by the government, the organization took form. The Guidance League set "defector self-surrender week" twice, from October 25 through the end of November, and launched large-scale member recruitment. The numbers announced by the Guidance League at that time numbered nationally 39,986 members by November 27, 1949, and 5,283 in Cheju.[^31] When the Korean War broke out, members of the Guidance League were immediately subject to preventive detention and were mass-executed (specific details are presented in Section 2 of Chapter Ⅳ of this report, "Killings of Preventively Detained Persons").

## 3) Implementation of the May 10 Re-election

President Syngman Rhee, as the armed guerrillas were mostly annihilated and the incident entered a phase of being brought under control, proceeded with preparations to hold the re-election of National Assembly representatives for Cheju Province's two electoral districts, which had failed to take place the previous year. In mid-March 1949, Director of Public Information Kim Tong-sŏng conveyed the President's intention that the re-election for both districts would be held on May 10.[^32]

As the re-election date approached, visits by government officials to Cheju came in succession. On April 7, Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo[^33] and Social Affairs Minister Yi Yun-yŏng came to Cheju on special presidential orders. They announced: "The President's special order is to quickly annihilate the remaining armed guerrillas and devise countermeasures for the economic reconstruction of Cheju Province."[^34]

On April 9, President Syngman Rhee, accompanied by his wife, visited Cheju directly to inspect the security situation. The President attended a welcoming rally with tens of thousands gathered at the plaza in front of Kwandŏk Pavilion and stated: "I hear there are still rebels, which is very regrettable. That is why I hope that the military, police, and people who have welcomed the two ministers will work together to build peace on Cheju at the earliest date."[^35] The President also said: "The government and the United States are always concerned about Cheju and relief supplies will be provided soon."[^36]

After inspecting military units in Cheju and other regions and returning on the 14th, Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo met with journalists on the 15th and stated: "The rebels on Hallasan in Cheju Province have all dispersed, and as the President said, annihilation and suppression are a matter of time. I encouraged the soldiers on the front lines to leave not a single person and completely annihilate them," thus indicating that the Cheju incident was being brought under control.[^37]

The UN Commission on Korea, before implementing the re-election, set out for an on-site inspection on April 18 to examine the security situation and other conditions, but was unable to land due to fog over Cheju airspace and turned back.[^38] They arrived on Cheju on May 8, two days before the re-election, observed the election, and returned on the 14th.[^39]

In addition to local figures, Candidate for National Assembly Representative in the two electoral districts of North Cheju County were joined by Ham Sang-hun (咸尙勳), propaganda director of the Democratic Citizens' Party.[^40] Even the central chair of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, Mun Pong-je (文鳳濟), ran as a candidate in North Cheju County's second electoral district.[^41]

The re-election held on May 10 proceeded smoothly, unlike one year before. The voter registration rate was 96% in the first district and 97% in the second district—very high compared to one year prior. Voter turnout also recorded 97% in the first district and 99% in the second district.[^42] In the vote count, Hong Sून-nyŏng (洪淳寧) was elected in North Cheju County's first district, and

**[p. 328]**

Yang Pyŏng-jik (梁秉直) was elected in the second district.[^43]

## b. Weakening of the Armed Guerrillas

## 1) Assassination of Yi Tŏk-ku

As a result of the hardline suppression operations of the 9th and 2nd Regiments beginning in mid-November 1948, followed by the "mountain sweep operations" and "defection mobilization" of the Cheju Island District Combat Command established in March 1949, the armed guerrilla forces reached a state of near-annihilation. On April 7, 1949, when Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo inspected Cheju, he received a report from the Combat Command that "even in the preceding days, many important cadres of the red bandits in the mountains had surrendered, and combining what they stated with our patrol judgment, the armed rebels currently remaining number only 40-50."[^44] Around that time, one news outlet reported the words of a prisoner of guerrilla origin: "By March, Yi Tŏk-ku and some twenty others had come to the fate of burying their weapons in the ground and dispersing themselves, each watching for an opportunity to surrender."[^45]

Subsequently, around mid-April, armed guerrilla cadres were killed or captured, and the armed guerrilla forces substantially disintegrated. Major Yi Ch'ang-jŏng, press officer of the Cheju Island District Combat Command, declared: "On April 21, we finally killed Kim Yong-gwan, the chairman of the South Korean Workers' Party. With this, the sweep operation is completely concluded."[^46] On June 7, the armed guerrillas' supreme commander Yi Tŏk-ku was killed. Although the armed guerrilla forces had already reached a state of disintegration, Yi Tŏk-ku, following Kim Tal-sam, remained a symbolic figure of the armed guerrillas, so his death had considerable impact. Emboldened by this, the Defense Ministry even announced that the 2nd Regiment had killed him through their operations.[^47] However, Yi Tŏk-ku was killed through police operations. One news outlet reported Yi Tŏk-ku's death as follows:

**[From Cheju, Joint Report]** Guerrilla Commander Yi Tŏk-ku (李德九) of Cheju Province was killed on the 7th by police units. That is, the police unit under the command of Sergeant Kim Yŏng-ju (金英柱) of the Hwabuok Branch Office of the Cheju Police Station engaged the Guerrilla Commander Yi Tŏk-ku's unit in combat near the jungle near what is called Small Stingray around 4 p.m. on the 7th, killed Yi Tŏk-ku, and captured one of his close aides. It is said that Yi Tŏk-ku's remains are currently being held at the Cheju Police Station.[^48]

The police bound Yi Tŏk-ku's corpse to a wooden cross and displayed it in front of the Cheju Police Station entrance for one day before cremating him. Kang Sil (康實), Yi Tŏk-ku's maternal cousin, who was asked by the police to confirm the identity of the body, testified as follows:

The corpse was clean except for a single bullet wound at the temple. The corpse was displayed for only one day. In the rainy season, having hung it from morning, by evening a considerable stench had developed. The police cremated the remains at the Namsoo Building and then informed my father, saying, "At least gather the bones." However, the next day it rained heavily, and everything was swept away, so we were unable to collect them.[^49]

## 2) Change of Garrison Troops

### ① Independent Battalion

On July 15, 1949, the "Independent First Battalion" led by Colonel Kim Yong-ju (金龍周) moved to Cheju and took over the 2nd Regiment's responsibilities.[^50] The Independent 1st Battalion was the successor organization of the Army Reconnaissance School. The Army Reconnaissance School (Principal Kim Yong-ju[^51]) had its subordinate guerrilla battalion led by the principal move to Sancheong County, South Gyeongsang Province, by January 31, 1949, to participate in operations in the Jirisan District under the 5th Brigade Commander,[^52] and when the Jirisan District Combat Force (Commander Chŏng Il-gwŏn) was organized on February 27, this guerrilla battalion was also included,[^53] having mainly accumulated combat experience in the Jirisan region up to that point. Subsequently, it was renamed the "Independent Infantry 1st Battalion" on June 20,[^54] and on July 15 moved from its station in Suwŏn, Gyeonggi Province,
