**[p. 330]**

Nevertheless, the defectors were thoroughly searched. A U.S. Military Government report records the following regarding the processing of defectors as of April 1, 1949: "The current policy is to detain all people coming down from the mountains, regardless of whether they were captured during operations or surrendered voluntarily. Most women, children, and elderly people are being classified as refugees, while males of combat age are thoroughly searched and educated before being granted refugee status."[^1] Meanwhile, Colonel Yu Chae-hung departed Cheju on May 13 after the May 10 by-election was successfully held.[^2] The Cheju Regional Combat Headquarters was formally abolished on May 15, transferring its duties to the 2nd Regiment.[^3] Additionally, the 3rd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment, which was composed exclusively of Sŏbuk Youth Corps members, withdrew from Cheju on May 15 along with the Combat Headquarters.[^4] The withdrawal of the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion was carried out on the orders of Major General Roberts, the U.S. Military Advisory Group commander. Major General Roberts instructed Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo to repatriate to the mainland and widely disperse those from the Sŏbuk Youth Corps who were attached to both the military and police.[^5] Initially, the U.S. Military Government had praised and encouraged the cooperation and support of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps in the military and police.[^6] However, when the Sŏbuk Youth Corps continued their excesses even after the situation had calmed, thereby worsening the problem, they were belatedly withdrawn. On May 18, a special police unit returned to Seoul after completing three months of operations. With this, the bloodshed had somewhat subsided. However, although Colonel Yu Chae-hung had declared during his pacification work that "those who come down from the mountains will not be held accountable for their past crimes and their lives will be guaranteed," after Colonel Yu's departure from Cheju, approximately 1,600 people were either executed by firing squad or sent to prisons throughout the country. After the abolition of the Cheju Regional Combat Headquarters, Cheju again fell under the command of 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏng-sŏn. With the simultaneous abolition of the Headquarters and the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion departing Cheju,

[^1]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 1097, April 1, 1949.
[^2]: Tong Kwang Sinmun, May 15, 1949.
[^3]: Army Headquarters Operational Order, Directive 81, May 5, 1949.
[^4]: Ibid.
[^5]: "Minutes of Conference, 16 April," April 16, 1949, RG 338: Records of US Army Commands, Entry: Provisional Military Advisory Group (1948–1949) and Korean Military Advisory Group (1949–1953), Box 8.
[^6]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 951, October 1, 1948.

**[p. 331]**

on May 25 a guerrilla battalion was relieved of its assignment and withdrawn, greatly reducing the troop strength.[^1] Subsequently, the 2nd Regiment was transferred on June 20 from direct control of the Army Headquarters to the Seoul Metropolitan Guard Command,[^2] and on July 7 withdrew from Cheju and moved to Inch'ŏn (complete withdrawal of the 1st Battalion occurred on August 13).[^3] Thus, Lieutenant Colonel Ham Pyŏng-sŏn's period of stationing in Cheju lasted somewhat over eight months from December 29, 1948, when he arrived.

Meanwhile, following the inaugural rally of the National Guidance Federation held in Seoul on June 5, 1949, the organization gradually spread nationwide.[^4] The Federation was launched under the ostensible purpose of enlightening and guiding former members who had renounced the SKWP, but it originated from the intention to bind leftist forces within a single organization for control. Unlike the rest of the country, the Cheju region had already undergone the April 3 Incident, and most leftists, armed guerrillas, and people who aroused even the slightest suspicion in the eyes of the military and police had been victims. Therefore, there was no particular need to newly organize the Federation, but it came to take organizational form in the context of nationwide movements being promoted by the government. The Federation designated "periods of convert voluntary surrender" twice between October 25 and the end of November, launching extensive membership recruitment campaigns. The numbers announced by the Federation at that time were, as of November 27, 1949, 39,986 nationally, and 5,283 in Cheju.[^5] Federation members were placed under preventive detention when the Korean War broke out and were subsequently executed en masse (the specific details are organized in Section 2 of Chapter IV of this report, "Killing of Preventively Detained Persons").

**3) Implementation of the May 10 By-election**

When President Syngman Rhee saw that the armed guerrillas had been mostly annihilated and the situation had entered a phase of calm, he began preparations to rerun the National Assembly elections for the two electoral districts in Cheju that had failed the previous year. In mid-March 1949, Information Minister Kim Tong-sŏng conveyed the President's intention that the elections for both districts would be held on May 10.[^6] As the by-election date approached, visits by government officials to Cheju became frequent. On April 7, Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo

[^1]: Army Headquarters Operational Order, Directive 92, May 10, 1949.
[^2]: Defense Ministry General Order No. 18, June 14, 1949.
[^3]: Army Headquarters Military Information Bureau, Army Historical Diary, 3rd Collection, July 7, 1949.
[^4]: Tong-A Ilbo, June 6, 1949.
[^5]: Seoul Sinmun, December 2, 1949.
[^6]: Freedom Sinmun, March 20, 1949.

**[p. 332]**

and Social Affairs Minister Yi Yun-yŏng arrived in Cheju on the President's special orders. They announced that "the President's special order is to completely exterminate the remaining armed guerrillas as soon as possible and to devise measures for economic reconstruction in Cheju."[^1] On April 9, President Syngman Rhee, accompanied by his wife, personally visited Cheju to inspect the security situation. President Rhee attended a welcoming rally with tens of thousands in front of Kwandŏk Hall, stating: "I hear that there are still bandits remaining, which troubles me greatly. Therefore, I hope that the military, police, and civilians who have met these ministers will work together in consensus to build peace in Cheju as quickly as possible." The President further stated: "Both the government and America are constantly concerned about Cheju and will soon provide relief supplies."[^2]

Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo, after completing inspection tours of Cheju and other regional units, returned on the 14th. Meeting with the press on the 15th, he stated: "The bandits in the Hallasan region of Cheju have all been dispersed, and as the President said, their complete extermination is merely a matter of time. I encouraged the officers and soldiers at the front lines, saying that they should leave no one alive and completely annihilate them."[^3]

The UN Commission on Korea, in order to observe security conditions before the by-election, departed for on-site inspection on April 18, but returned without landing due to fog over Cheju.[^4] They arrived in Cheju two days before the by-election, on May 8, observed the election, and returned on the 14th.[^5]

In addition to local candidates, Puk Cheju County's two electoral districts' National Assembly by-election also included Hahm Sang-hun, propaganda director of the Democratic National Party.[^6] Even Mun Bong-je, the central director of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, ran as a candidate in the Puk Cheju County "b" district.[^7]

The by-election held on May 10 proceeded without incident, unlike the previous year. The voter registration rate was 96% in the "a" district and 97% in the "b" district, much higher than the previous year. The voter turnout was 97% in the "a" district and 99% in the "b" district.[^8] According to the vote count, Hong Sun-nŭng was elected in Puk Cheju County's "a" district, and Yang Pyŏng-jik was elected in the "b" district.[^9]

[^2]: Freedom Sinmun, April 12, 1949.
[^3]: Seoul Sinmun, April 16, 1949.
[^4]: Tong Kwang Sinmun, April 21, 1949.
[^5]: Tong Kwang Sinmun, May 19, 1949.
[^6]: Chosŏn Ilbo, April 16, 1949.
[^7]: Chosŏn Ilbo, May 12, 1949.
[^8]: Kugdo Sinmun, May 15, 1949.
[^9]: Kugdo Sinmun, May 14, 1949; Tong Kwang Sinmun, May 14, 1949.

**[p. 333]**

**b. Weakening of the Armed Guerrillas**

**1) Killing of Yi Tŏk-ku**

Following the forceful suppression operations by the 9th Regiment and 2nd Regiment that began in mid-November 1948, and as a result of the "mountain sweeping operations" and "surrender inducement campaigns" of the Cheju Regional Combat Headquarters established in March 1949, the armed guerrilla forces had reached a state of near total collapse. On April 7, 1949, Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo, who was inspecting Cheju, received a report from the Combat Headquarters stating: "Just days ago, many important cadres of the mountain reds surrendered, and when we consolidate what they stated with our reconnaissance judgment, we conclude that the remaining armed bandits number only 40–50 people."[^1] Around that time, one news outlet reported the words of a former armed guerrilla prisoner: "By March, Yi Tŏk-ku and about twenty others had buried their weapons and become dispersed, watching for opportunities to surrender to each other."[^2]

In mid-April, armed guerrilla cadres were either killed or captured, at which point the armed guerrilla forces were substantially dissolved. Cheju Regional Combat Headquarters Press Officer Captain Yi Ch'ang-jŏng declared: "On April 21, we finally killed Kim Yong-gwan, the leader of the SKWP. With this, the sweeping operation is completely concluded."[^3] On June 7, Yi Tŏk-ku, the chief commander of the armed guerrillas, was killed. Although the armed guerrilla forces had already been dissolved, Yi Tŏk-ku was a symbolic figure following Kim Tal-sam, so his death had a significant impact. Encouraged by this, the Defense Ministry announced that the 2nd Regiment had killed him in combat.[^4] However, Yi Tŏk-ku was actually killed in police operations. One news outlet reported the killing of Yi Tŏk-ku as follows:

[^1]: Chosŏn Ilbo, April 19, 1949.
[^2]: Kugdo Sinmun, April 23, 1949.
[^3]: Kyŏnghyang Sinmun, April 26, 1949.
[^4]: Tong-A Ilbo, June 10, 1949.

**[p. 334]**

"**[From Cheju Joint Report]** The rebel commander Yi Tŏk-ku of Cheju Island was killed by the police on the 7th. Specifically, the police unit led by Police Sergeant Kim Yŏng-ju of the Hwabuk Substation of the Cheju Police Station engaged the rebel commander Yi Tŏk-ku's unit in combat around 4 p.m. on the 7th in the jungle near the so-called 'small manta ray' area, killed Yi Tŏk-ku, and captured one of his bodyguards. It is reported that Yi Tŏk-ku's corpse is now being held at the Cheju Police Station."[^1]

The police tied Yi Tŏk-ku's corpse to a wooden cross and displayed it in front of the Cheju Police Station for one day before cremating it. Kang Sil, Yi Tŏk-ku's maternal cousin, who at the time was asked by police to identify the remains of her maternal uncle, testified as follows: "The body was clean except for one bullet wound at the temple. The body was displayed for only one day. Since it was hung up from morning during the rainy season, there was a lot of smell by evening. The police cremated the body at Namsugak and then told my father, 'Collect even the bones.' However, it rained heavily the next day and swept everything away, so we were unable to collect anything."[^2]

**2) Unit Replacement**

**①Independent Battalion**

On July 15, 1949, the 'Independent 1st Battalion' led by Major Kim Yong-ju moved to Cheju to assume the duties of the 2nd Regiment.[^3] The Independent 1st Battalion was the successor organization to the Army Ranger School. The Army Ranger School (commandant Kim Yong-ju[^4]) had previously moved with its subordinate guerrilla battalion under the school commandant to South Kyŏngsang Province in Sanchuek until January 31, 1949, where it came under the command of the 5th Brigade to participate in operations in the Chirisan area,[^5] and this guerrilla battalion was also included when the Chirisan Area Combat Force (commander Chŏng Il-gwŏn) was organized on February 27,[^6] making it a combat unit that had accumulated substantial combat experience mainly in the Chirisan area. Subsequently, it was redesignated as 'Independent Infantry 1st Battalion' on June 20,[^7] and on July 15 moved from its station at Suwŏn, Kyŏnggi Province,

**[p. 336]**

The Marine Corps had executed this. [^2] Following the withdrawal of the Marine Corps Independent Battalion stationed in Cheju, the Marine Corps (Commander Colonel Shin Hyŏn-jun) arrived in Cheju on December 28, 1949. The Marine Corps, organized with a force of 1,200, established its headquarters in Cheju city alone, while the 1st and 2nd Battalions were stationed in Mosulpo. [^3] Commander Shin Hyŏn-jun, in a report sent to the Naval Chief of Staff on January 17, 1950, stated: "The principal instigators of the communist rebels related to the 4·3 Incident have for the most part fled the island; the communist rebels currently lying hidden on Hallasan are those who have fallen into the tricks and propaganda of the ringleaders. Because most islanders are connected to the communist rebels through blood relations, they have been unable to fully vent their fear and resentment toward the military born from past dread." Thus he recognized the situation at that time. [^4] The Marine Corps stated that its mission consisted of "①the spiritual and physical training of the Marine Corps itself; ②giving the 300,000 island residents, who have been exhausted materially and spiritually since the 4·3 Incident, the strength to recover and guiding them toward the path of prosperity; ③suppressing the communist rebels based on Hallasan," thereby indicating its intention to pursue suppression operations and the restoration of civilian sentiment in parallel. [^5] From February to June 1950—five months—the Marine Corps Headquarters implemented the first suppression operation in the mountainous regions. The Marine Corps Combat History introduced the results of the first suppression operation as follows: △ On February 5–6, search operations near Tol-orŭm killed 8 and captured 8 alive. △ On March 10, a surprise attack on the armed guerrillas' field hospital near the 881-meter height in Chungmun-myŏn killed the hospital director and others. △ Around late March, a surprise attack on an armed guerrillas' meeting place near Handae-orŭm killed 7. △ On March 15, a surprise attack on an armed guerrillas' hideout at the 1394-meter height killed 1 and wounded 3. △ On March 17, a surprise attack on hideouts at the 1394-meter height and the Obaek General position killed 2. △ On March 22, combat against the armed guerrillas 500 meters southwest of Tol-orŭm repelled them. △ On May 20, moved the suppression force headquarters to Ssarŭm on the south side of Hallasan and searched the jungle terrain. [^6]

Meanwhile, when the Korean War broke out on June 25, 1950, the Marine Corps commander assumed the role of Cheju Island District Martial Law Commander.

[^2]: Marine Corps Headquarters, History of Marine Corps Development, 1961, p. 11.

[^3]: Marine Corps Headquarters, Marine Corps Combat History, 1962, p. 18.

[^4]: Marine Corps Headquarters, ibid., p. 17.

[^5]: Marine Corps Headquarters, ibid., pp. 21–22.

[^6]: Marine Corps Headquarters, ibid., pp. 21–22.

**[p. 337]**

However, the Marine Corps failed in one of its two missions—the restoration of civilian sentiment—relative to its suppression operations. Particularly after the June 25 Korean War, they illegally killed many people through mass executions of preliminary detention detainees; they fabricated the so-called "Yuji Incident"—the "People's Army Welcoming Preparation Committee Incident"—and tortured prominent figures within Cheju. Furthermore, in the process of causing harm to civilians, they even illegally executed Kang Sŏng-mo, the serving head of Sŏgwipo-myŏn at that time. Regarding this, Kim Ho-gyŏm, who was serving as the chief of the Sŏgwipo Police Station at the time, testified: "Section Chief Kang was a victim of what was called 'offensive to the eyes' [death without charge]. The Marine Corps caused trouble by coercing the section chief, saying 'Within one hour, prepare a few head of cattle and several hundred bundles of bracken,' to procure food for themselves. When Section Chief Kang did not readily comply, they harbored resentment and killed him." [^7] The bereaved families of Kang Sŏng-mo filed a complaint against Shin Hyŏn-jun when the April 19 Revolution broke out. The complaint was received by the Cheju Prosecutor's Office on July 1, 1960, as complaint #2 for "massacre of innocent civilians." In the complaint, the bereaved family stated: "The cause of the massacre was that our father, Kang Sŏng-mo, when he was serving as head of Sŏgwipo-myŏn, raised an objection to the military's brutal harassment of civilians through activities such as gathering bracken, which offended the military; when the June 25 incident suddenly broke out, they massacred him so thoroughly that even his corpse could not be found." [^8] The Cheju Prosecutor's Office, which received the complaint, proceeded with the investigation by questioning the victim's family and witnesses and making inquiries to military authorities about Shin Hyŏn-jun. [^9] However, soon thereafter the May 16 Military Coup occurred, and efforts to establish the truth were buried.

[^7]: Kim Ho-gyŏm (Ŭnp'yŏng-gu, Seoul; chief of Sŏgwipo Police Station at the time), testimony (Jemini Ilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, "4·3 Speaks" ⑤, pp. 185–186).

[^8]: Cheju Shinmun, July 3, 1960.

[^9]: Cheju Shinmun, January 14, 1961.

**[p. 338]**

## 5. The Period of Termination of the Incident (June 25, 1950 – September 21, 1954)

### a. The Korean War and Preliminary Detention

#### 1) The Korean War and the Situation in Cheju

At 4 a.m. on June 25, 1950, the invasion by North Korean forces initiated what would become the fiercest and most brutal war in Korean history, with enormous loss of life. The ROK Army could not stem the North Korean advance and lost Seoul on June 28. U.S. forces based in Japan were hastily deployed but were defeated by the North Koreans and withdrew to the Nakdong River line by August 1. During this time, the United Nations decided to dispatch UN forces, and U.S. and other troops entered the war as UN forces. The ROK Army and UN forces continued offensive and defensive operations along the Nakdong River front for about one and a half months, then launched a counteroffensive beginning with the Inchon landing on September 15 and recaptured Seoul on September 28. They then broke through the 38th parallel on October 1, occupied Pyongyang on October 19, and continued the northward advance until advance units reached the Yalu River, making reunification seem imminent. However, when large Chinese Communist forces intervened from October 25 onward, the ROK Army and UN forces withdrew repeatedly and by January 4, 1951, relinquished Seoul again to the enemy and withdrew to the P'yŏngtaek line. The ROK Army and UN forces attempted another counteroffensive but, unable to overcome the overwhelming superiority of the Chinese Communists, engaged in positional warfare with little major change in the front line, until an armistice was concluded on July 27, 1953.

The government proclaimed martial law throughout South Korea except for the Chŏlla regions on July 8, 1950. After ROK forces advanced north of the 38th parallel, martial law in the Cheju region was lifted on October 10. Then on November 10, while lifting martial law throughout South Korea, the government proclaimed security martial law in most areas of South Korea on the same day, excluding Cheju and the southern regions of Kyŏngsang provinces. [^1] However, as the military situation deteriorated again, on December 7 the area south of the 38th parallel was converted from security martial law to emergency martial law, and on February 22, 1951, emergency martial law in the Cheju Island District was lifted. [^2]

Meanwhile, in response to wartime circumstances, the Army 5th Training School was established in Cheju on July 16. As the military situation deteriorated, the Army 1st Training School, which had been established in Taegu, was moved to Mosulpo in Cheju on January 22, 1951. [^3]

[^1]: Republic of Korea Government Public Relations Bureau, Gazette No. 383, July 8, 1950; Gazette No. 396, October 10, 1950; Gazette No. 406, November 7, 1950.

[^2]: Tonga Ilbo, February 22, 1951; Republic of Korea Government Public Relations Bureau, Gazette Extra, December 7, 1950; Gazette Extra, February 22, 1951.

**[p. 339]**

The Defense Ministry's 2nd Ordnance Supply Depot and wireless communication relay stations were installed, and prisoner-of-war camps for Chinese Communist prisoners were established at two locations—Cheju and Mosulpo—such that numerous military installations were set up and operated in Cheju. [^3]

From December 1950, refugees began to pour into Cheju in large numbers. On January 12, 1951, the government's State Council reviewed the issue of moving 42,000 refugees in a group to Cheju. [^4] The refugee population increased from approximately 16,000 on January 3, 1951, to approximately 87,000 on January 15, and to 148,794 by May 20. [^5] After the front lines stabilized, some refugees returned home; however, by the end of January 1952, there remained 28,460 refugees in Cheju—approximately 22,000 in North Cheju County and 6,500 in South Cheju County. [^6]

At one point, the Army 1st Training School, which supervised 8 recruit regiments, 2 military prison regiments, 1 noncommissioned officer school and transportation school, and 1 Army hospital, was the largest unit in Cheju. During the three and a half years until its closure in August 1954, [^7] it trained many recruits. Although it did not directly participate in the suppression operations against the armed guerrillas, because the commandant of the 1st Training School also served concurrently as the Cheju District Garrison Commander, when necessary he issued operational directives or security orders. On July 1, 1952, in his capacity as commandant, he issued an operational order to strengthen unit security in preparation for armed guerrilla activity; on October 1, 1952, in his capacity as garrison commander, he issued an operational order to form one security company in preparation for riots by Chinese Communist prisoners and deploy it near the prisoner-of-war camp. These are good examples.

The United Nations Command initially confined prisoners at Pusan, but as the front lines changed dramatically due to the counteroffensive and Chinese Communist intervention, resulting in a massive influx of prisoners, the UN Command established and managed a large prisoner-of-war camp on Koje Island. The number of prisoners, including North Korean and Chinese Communist prisoners, reached a maximum of approximately 166,000. When murders and atrocities frequently occurred between pro-Communist and anti-Communist prisoners, and prisoners even kidnapped the camp commander, the UN Command, from June 1952, separated prisoners into pro-Communist and anti-Communist groups and dispersed them into separate camps—North Korean prisoners on the mainland and Chinese Communist prisoners in Cheju. Among the Chinese Communist prisoners, those "pro-Communist prisoners" who wished repatriation to their home country were confined at Cheju airfield, while those "anti-Communist prisoners" who did not wish repatriation were confined at Mosulpo airfield. On February 1, 1953, the prisoner population was 5,809 at Cheju airfield and 14,314 at Mosulpo. [^8]

The pro-Communist prisoners at Cheju airfield held a celebration on October 1, 1952, commemorating the third anniversary of the founding of the Chinese Communist government, and then held a demonstration. In response, two U.S. Army squads guarding the prisoner-of-war camp suppressed the demonstrating prisoners; in this process, 56 prisoners were killed and 113 were wounded. [^9]

[^3]: Army Headquarters, Army Operational Directive No. 29, January 21, 1951.

[^4]: Minutes of the 6th State Council Meeting, January 12, 1951.

[^5]: Man-gŭn Hu, "Thirty Years of Liberated Cheju," Munch'o Company, 1975, pp. 116–117.

[^6]: Cheju Shinmun, February 2, 1952.

[^7]: Army Headquarters, Directive No. 290, May 2, 1954.

[^8]: National Defense Military Research Institute, "Prisoners of War in the Korean War," 1996, pp. 68–76; 1st Training School, Operational Order No. 7, September 28, 1953.

[^9]: Cheju Shinmun, October 3–4, 1952; Chosun Ilbo, October 3–4, 1952; Army Intelligence Bureau, Regular Intelligence Report No. 276, October 2, 1952; Red Flag, October 15, 1952.

**[p. 340]**

#### 2) Preliminary Detention

When the Korean War broke out on June 25, 1950, the government implemented "preliminary detention" of Bodo League members and those suspected of anti-government sympathies. On the afternoon of June 25 itself, at 2:25 p.m., the Chief of the Police Bureau issued an emergency telegram to each police station nationwide on "the apprehension of national wanted persons and the strengthening of nationwide prison security." This directive was transmitted to the Cheju Police Bureau and contained orders to "immediately arrest all national wanted persons" and "strengthen nationwide prison security." When martial law was proclaimed on July 8, the police, prosecution, courts, and administrative organizations all came under military jurisdiction, and consequently the martial law commander assumed responsibility for preliminary detention.

In accordance with these preliminary detention directives from the government, immediate wholesale arrests of wanted persons also took place in Cheju. From late June through early August, preliminary detention was carried out ranging from civil servants and teachers to students and women. Contemporary preliminary detention population was listed in a police document as 820 people, while a report by a U.S. Embassy staff member recorded that the police separately managed 700 Bodo League officers.

In the Cheju region, from late July through late August, military authorities executed mass shootings of those detained at Cheju city and Sŏgwipo and Mosulpo police stations. Such executions of preliminary detention detainees were carried out with extreme secrecy. The site in Sŏk-alŭ-orŭm at Songak Mountain where people confined in the Mosulpo police station–jurisdiction detention facility were shot was discovered by chance by residents, but the circumstances at the time—including the execution date and location of the others who remained detained at Sŏgwipo police stations in Cheju—were kept strictly confidential. (A detailed account of casualties among preliminary detention detainees is presented in Section IV of this report, "Damage Conditions," subsection 2, "Human Casualties," "Group Killings of Civilians.")

In September 1950, the execution of preliminary detention detainees in the Cheju region was halted. On September 14, the Cheju Police Bureau held a conference of investigative personnel and concluded that those preliminary detention detainees who had hope of changing their allegiance should be released to fulfill their obligations as citizens. Following the authorities' review, 153 preliminary detention detainees from the Cheju Police Station were released on September 18, 1950. [^10] Subsequently, a second group release also occurred involving 48 preliminary detention detainees confined in the Cheju Police Station. [^11] The Sŏgwipo Police Station released all detainees on September 17. The Mosulpo Police Station released 90 of a total of 344 detained persons before September 18. [^12]

Meanwhile, in August 1950, an incident dubbed the "Yuji Incident" occurred—the "People's Army Welcoming Preparation Committee Incident"—in which 16 prominent figures including the court president, prosecutor, Cheju city chief, and lawyers, businessmen, and educators were taken to the Cheju regional martial law command headquarters (Commander Colonel Shin Hyŏn-jun) on suspicion of having organized a "People's Army Welcoming Preparation Committee." The martial law command quickly arrested these people on August 8–9, and Cheju society was gripped by a climate of terror. Governor Kim Ch'ung-hŭi of Cheju regarded this incident as a frame-up to arrest specific individuals and, after consulting with Police Bureau Chief Yi Sŏng-ju, sent a petition to Interior Minister Cho Pyŏng-ok and Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo requesting that the facts be clarified. Additionally, Ch'oe Pyŏng-sun, the chief of the Cheju Special Security Bureau, submitted a report to the Defense Ministry stating that this incident differed from the facts. Sŏn-u Chong-wŏn, chief of the Investigation Guidance Section of the Police Bureau, who was rushed to conduct a local investigation, uncovered the fact that this was a frame-up orchestrated by the martial law command's intelligence officer, Captain Shin In-ch'ŏl. The Cheju society was extremely depressed by the Yuji Incident. The island's residents directly witnessed the sitting court president and prosecutor being arrested by the martial law military and facing the threat of death. Moreover, the progression of this incident coincided with mass executions of preliminary detention detainees on August 20, making August a month of trembling and terror.

[^10]: Cheju Shinmun, September 20, 1950.

[^11]: Cheju Shinmun, September 23, 1950.

[^12]: "Regarding the Submission of Identity Guarantees for Released Preliminary Detention Detainees," September 18, 1950 (Idŏ-yŏng Collection, Preliminary Detention Related Police Bureau Records, p. 386).

**[p. 341]**

### b. The Annihilation of Remnant Armed Guerrillas

#### 1) Activities of Remnant Armed Guerrillas

After the Army's suppression operations in March–April 1949, the activities of the armed guerrillas became infrequent, and the authorities believed the armed guerrillas had been largely eliminated. Subsequently, there were no major attacks even during the operational periods of the 2nd Regiment or the 1st Independent Battalion, and during the Marine Corps operations in the first half of 1950, although there were small-scale engagements, there were no attacks by armed guerrillas. The remnant armed guerrillas on Hallasan numbered approximately 60; they avoided combat as much as possible, concealed their weapons, and sustained themselves by obtaining daily necessities from parents and siblings in their hometowns. Then, one month after the outbreak of the Korean War, on July 25, 1950, they attacked the village of Hawŏn-ri in Chungmun-myŏn and burned 99 civilian houses; this was the first attack by the armed guerrillas after the outbreak of the war. [^13]

Regarding this, leftist sources record: "After the outbreak of the June 25 Korean War in 1950, the remnant People's Guerrillas, from late July 1950, mounted aggressive attacks under a unified operational plan; on July 25, they attacked the Hawŏn Police Substation in Chungmun-myŏn, and the unit centered on Kim Ŭi-bong infiltrated the rural areas of Choch'ŏn-myŏn to carry out political work among the residents, calling them to unite in the Liberation Front struggle and respond to the advance of the People's Army." [^14]

The approximately 60 armed guerrillas on Hallasan, upon hearing news of the outbreak of the war, held a discussion on a certain day in July regarding their future course. At that time, three members of the leadership—Ko Sŭng-ok, Paek Ch'ang-wŏn, and Song Wŏn-byŏng—argued: "The People's Army has come as far as Mokpo; we must not depart until after they have landed in Cheju." However, young people argued: "We must not passively treat the heroic tradition begun by 4·3." Eventually, that night Hŏ Yŏng-sam and Kim Sŏng-gyŭ took the lead in restraining the three people, and the following day they subjected them to a people's trial and killed them. Subsequently, Kim Sŏng-gyŭ led the armed forces into Chungmun, according to testimony from a former police officer. [^15] Hŏ Yŏng-sam became the commander of the armed guerrillas that very day. The armed guerrillas, beginning with the attack on Hawŏn village, sporadically attacked police substations and villages, inflicting damage on the police, killing rightist figures, and obtaining necessary grain. They also abducted young people around twenty years old and expanded their forces. As suppressed forces incurred deaths and captures and surrenders through police suppression operations, the numbers of armed guerrillas should have diminished, but continuous abductions prevented the armed guerrilla numbers from decreasing.

The armed guerrillas on Hallasan carried out sustained attacks in the hope that North Korean People's Army soldiers advancing southward would soon land in Cheju. Island leftists, from July onward, gathered in small groups and, while organizing People's Army Support and Welcoming Committees at each myŏn level throughout the island as well as in Cheju city, undertook activities to support the armed guerrillas. [^16]

[^13]: Hawŏn Village Association, "Hawŏn Local History," 1999, p. 379.

[^14]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-chu, "History of the 4·3 Armed Struggle of the Cheju Island People," Osaka: Mun'u Company, 1963, pp. 257–258.

[^15]: Chŏng Nam-du (74 years old, Ildo 1-dong, Cheju City; former police officer, recorded October 10, 2001), testimony.

**[p. 342]**

By March 1951, the armed guerrilla organization consisted of Commander Hŏ Yŏng-sam (concurrent party secretary of the South Korean Workers' Party Cheju Provincial Organization, from Andŭk), Deputy guerrilla commander for operations Kim T'ae-gil (alias Mun Ho-ch'ŏl), operational staff officer A Certain Person (name unknown, from Taegu), training officer A Certain Person (name unknown), and four detachments: the 11th Detachment (Kim Yŏng-ch'an, 14 people divided into 3 squads), the 50th Detachment (Ko In-su, alias So Hyŏng-sam, 18 people divided into 3 squads), the 1st Detachment (Kim Man-ok, 14 people divided into 3 squads), and the 7th Detachment (Mun Do-gong, 14 people divided into 3 squads)—a total of 64 personnel organized under a command staff and 4 detachments. [^17]

In an Army Intelligence Bureau record dated March 16, 1951: "At midnight on March 13, 40 communist rebels (20 armed, 20 unarmed) invaded Iho-ri in Cheju city, engaged in approximately 20 minutes of combat with police forces, then retreated into the mountains and fled. Twelve civilians were killed, 12 were abducted, cattle numbering 7 head, horses numbering 3 head, clothing, and grain in quantity were looted." [^18]

The armed guerrillas endeavored to expand their forces through active attacks and abductions, but their numbers did not increase due to police suppression operations. By May 1952, the force numbered 65 (30 armed, 35 unarmed) with 30 small arms; the commander was identified as Kim Sŏng-gyŭ. [^19]

In the early morning hours of September 16, 1952, an armed guerrilla force of unknown number, disguised as ROK Army and police, attacked Cheju Broadcasting Station, abducted the broadcasting director Kim Tu-gyŭ and an 18-year-old technical trainee and a young porter—three people total—and looted telephones and broadcasting clocks, then fled. [^20] These three abducted people were savagely killed several days later near the armed guerrillas' hideout and discovered buried in the ground. [^21] And a month later, at 3 a.m. on October 31, despite eight police being on guard, 27 armed guerrillas infiltrated and completely burned the Sŏgwipo Power Plant. [^22] Thus they cut off electrical supply for a considerable period, further destabilizing Cheju society.

Regarding the nature of the armed guerrillas at that time, one former armed guerrilla testified: "Until April 1949, we could be called armed guerrillas; afterward, we were bandits. After June 1949, people remaining in the mountains can hardly be regarded as guerrillas. I believe they were nearly disorderly mobs at that point." [^23]

Meanwhile, when the U.S. Embassy learned on August 13, 1950, that guerrilla activity was resuming in Cheju, it organized a Cheju inspection team consisting of naval attaché John P. Seifert, third secretary Donald S. Macdonald, and vice consul Philip C. Rowe. From August 15 to 17, they met with the Cheju governor, provincial bureau chiefs, Marine Corps commanders, and police leaders to assess the situation, then submitted a report to the embassy. The U.S. Embassy stated that if it intended to pay greater attention to Cheju in the future, it hoped the Korean authorities would do likewise. They recommended that the police have one U.S. advisor with extensive anti-guerrilla combat experience. They further recommended that a combat police company be established, that personnel receive infantry and guerrilla tactics training, and that the Marine Corps be retained as a reserve force for more serious contingencies. [^24]

However, Ambassador Drumright expressed the view that since the Marine Corps was needed on the mainland, it was desirable to withdraw it promptly. [^25] A few days after this recommendation, the Cheju Emergency Security Command established a Combat Vanguard on August 24 to suppress the Hallasan armed guerrillas and began operations. [^26]

[^16]: Kim Pong-hyŏn and Kim Min-chu, op. cit., p. 257.

[^17]: Headquarters National Police, Republic of Korea, Daily Intelligence Report, March 11, 1951.

[^18]: Army Intelligence Bureau, Regular Intelligence Report No. 75, March 16, 1951.

[^19]: Army Intelligence Bureau, Army Regular Intelligence Report No. 137, May 16, 1952. The armed guerrilla commander was identified as Kim Sŏng-gyŭ; however, the date and location where former commander Hŏ Yŏng-sam was killed by police remain unknown.

[^20]: Army Intelligence Bureau, Regular Intelligence Report No. 262, September 18, 1952; Cheju Police Bureau, "Cheju Police History," 1990, p. 317.

[^21]: Pyŏnghwa Shinmun, September 19, 1952.

[^22]: Army Intelligence Bureau, Regular Intelligence Report No. 305, October 31, 1952; Regular Intelligence Report No. 306, November 1, 1952.

**[p. 343]**

#### 2) Police Suppression Operations

After the Korean War broke out, martial law was immediately proclaimed, and in early September the Marine Corps stationed in Cheju was deployed to the front lines, whereupon the police assumed full responsibility for suppression operations against the armed guerrillas. Following the attack on Hawŏn village in Chungmun-myŏn on July 25—one month after the war's outbreak—the police heightened their vigilance toward resumed armed guerrilla activity. The police first undertook intelligence gathering and, upon confirming that a considerable number of armed guerrillas remained active, organized volunteer police units and local defense forces to supplement police operations while forming and operating two units: the Vanguard and the Tiger Force.

The Cheju Police Bureau selected 246 youths from among the youth and, in July, organized the Volunteer Police Force directly under the bureau (commander Kang P'il-saeng, organized into 2 battalions) with combat as its primary mission to use in suppression operations. [^27] Further, under the Emergency Local Defense Proclamation (issued August 4 by Presidential Emergency Order No. 9), the police established Local Defense Forces in mid-August under police station jurisdiction at the neighborhood level, with males aged seventeen to fifty. [^28]

Moreover, on August 12, a proclamation was issued to the effect: "From 7 a.m. to 7 p.m., animals shall be penned; however, penning in the Hallasan direction outside the city is strictly forbidden. Violators of this order shall be dealt with as communist sympathizers, and the animals shall be seized or shot as communist property." [^29]

According to police statistics, for the seven-month period from October 1, 1950, to April 22, 1951, results of operations and losses were as follows: operational results—△ 56 armed guerrillas killed; △ captured weapons: 11 rifles (1 Soviet rifle, 3 carbines, 7 Japanese rifles) and 2 grenades. Losses—△ police: 17 (15 dead, 2 wounded); △ local defense forces: 24 (11 dead, 11 wounded, 2 missing); △ civilians: 42 (1 dead, 3 wounded, 38 abducted); △ livestock lost: 53 head (44 cattle, 9 horses); △ weapons lost: 14 rifles (2 carbines, 12 rifles). [^30]

As civilian defense posture became weakened by the dissolution of the National Defense Force and Local Defense Forces, volunteer police units, volunteer fire brigades, and civilian air defense units were nationwide integrated into the Korean Youth Corps to organize special activity units (特動隊). Following the central government's movement, a Korean Youth Corps special activity unit was established in Cheju on January 25, 1952, and this unit subsequently supported the police's Hallasan suppression campaign. [^31]

The police installed garrisons at major passageways to cut off the suppression force's food supply and communications between residents and armed guerrillas. These garrison posts had begun to be established by each police bureau even before the war's outbreak to limit armed guerrilla activity and enable efficient suppression; by April 1952, there were 32 garrison posts throughout the province. The garrison posts employed village residents to build stone walls; one police officer and five to six village youths were stationed to maintain security; and the posts could accommodate thirty or more suppression troops. Between garrison posts, telephone lines were laid, but because telephone lines frequently were cut, wireless communication was used. Village women who provided meals for suppression troops also lived in these garrison posts. Important garrison posts were large enough to accommodate approximately sixty people for sleeping and eating simultaneously, and were classified into fixed garrison posts where police were permanently stationed and temporary garrison posts (combat garrison posts) occupied only during operations. [^32]

Although the police exerted all their efforts to establish and operate garrison posts while deploying considerable police forces in suppression operations, they obtained no remarkable results. In early January 1952, Police Bureau Chief Yun Sŏk-ryŏl stated: "With the bandit force at 76 people, we will mobilize all police forces to completely eliminate them by early spring. Rumors, gray-area elements, and pro-communist elements will be actively destroyed along with the bandits, and special operations against the bandits will be continued in parallel." [^33]

Moreover, the Interior Ministry's Police Bureau established a "Bandit Elimination Operations Plan" for the Cheju Island District and dispatched a seven-member operations guidance team headed by Senior Police Inspector Kim Chun-jong to Cheju to conduct operations from April 1 through April 30, 1952. The operational policy established by the Police Bureau was to compress the armed guerrillas west of the transport route connecting Cheju and Sŏgwipo, capture and annihilate them there, and, with minimal personnel, gradually infiltrate close to the armed guerrillas' strongholds from the existing garrison posts to reinforce the defensive line. [^34]

For the spring operations, the police issued a closure order for Hallasan in early April; the closure zone was a distance of fifteen *ri* or more from the coastline in the areas under Sŏngsan-po and Mosulpo police stations and the Ara-to-coastal garrison post line and beyond in the areas under Cheju and Sŏgwipo police stations. [^35]

This operation consumed two weeks—one week preparation period and one week processing period—making the actual operational period a sixteen-day short-term operation. Despite being a plan-only operation with no additional personnel that mobilized not only the Interior Ministry but also the Social Affairs and Agriculture and Forestry Ministries, it obtained no remarkable results. Police Bureau Chief Yun Sŏk-ryŏl stated at a press conference on May 19: "The Hallasan bandit elimination operation undertaken from early April was conducted for about one month under direct central direction; however, due to the bandits' flight, it has been difficult to come into contact with the enemy, and due to unfavorable terrain conditions, the anticipated results have not been achieved, which is regrettable. We continue the mopping-up operation currently, and by establishing combat garrison posts at depths of twenty to thirty *ri* in the dense forest regions, we are taking every precaution through troop increases and facility improvements for tightening the encirclement and restricting enemy movements." [^36]

Through this statement, Police Chief Yun acknowledged that the April armed guerrilla suppression operation achieved little result and indicated his intention to advance-deploy combat garrison posts by twenty to thirty *ri* to compress the encirclement. The closure zone in late September after advancing the garrison post deployment was as follows: △ Within Cheju city jurisdiction: the line connecting Myŏngdo-am–Yŏngp'yŏng–Chuksŏng–Kŭm-ak–Yusin-dong. △ Within Aewŏl-myŏn jurisdiction: Yusin-dong–Kŭmdŏk–Sŏgwang–Kŭm-ak line. △ Within Hallim-myŏn jurisdiction: Kŭm-ak–Chŏngmul-orŭm–Toŭl-ak line. △ Within Andŭk-myŏn jurisdiction: Toŭl-ak–Pyŏng-ak–Nokha-ji line. △ Within Chungmun-myŏn jurisdiction: Nokha-ji–Se-ch'o-ji line. △ Within Sŏgwi-myŏn jurisdiction: Saech'o-ji–Mi-ak–Su-ak line. △ Within Namwŏn-myŏn jurisdiction: Su-ak–456 height–Min-ak line. △ Within P'yosŏn-myŏn jurisdiction: Min-ak–Taenok-bong–Modŭn-ak–Kaemi-orŭm line. △ Within Kuja-myŏn jurisdiction: Kaemi-orŭm–Nop'ŭn-orŭm–Ch'e-orŭm line. △ Within Choch'ŏn-myŏn jurisdiction: Ch'e-orŭm–Alba-magi–Ch'ŏn-mi-ak–Sugi-dong–Myŏngdo-am line. [^37]

The police advanced a surrender campaign to induce the remnant armed guerrillas to come down the mountain. They conducted surrender operations through relatives or acquaintances, dropped leaflets urging descent throughout the Hallasan region, and held persuasion lectures on surrender. The Cheju Provincial Assembly issued a statement urging descent reading: "If you surrender, your life will be guaranteed by the state, and we, the provincial assembly, will take responsibility for your future livelihood. Abandon meaningless resistance and participate in building peace in your homeland," which was posted at key locations in each neighborhood and dropped on Hallasan. [^38]

From October 1950, the Cheju Police Bureau operated what was called the "Surrender Operations Team" in its investigation section, composed of six people. The mission of this team was to receive captured or surrendering armed guerrillas, conduct professional interrogations to ascertain the internal circumstances of the armed guerrillas, and devise appropriate countermeasures. That is, operational intelligence was provided to suppression forces; surrender appeals were made through loudspeakers or leaflets dropped by aircraft; and surrender operations were conducted through mobilizing surrendered persons or relatives such as parents and siblings. The work of this intelligence team also included finding employment for surrendered persons and, when there were deaths among the suppression forces during suppression operations, rushing to the scene to identify the deceased. [^39]

Surrender leaflets were initially distributed under the name of the provincial assembly chairman; a short time later they were also distributed under the name of the police bureau chief. Through such surrender operations, beginning around 1952—after about a year—cases appeared where armed guerrillas killed watchers and took their guns before surrendering. Alongside surrender leaflets, the life experiences of the armed guerrillas in the mountains were transmitted through itinerant lectures together with the happiness they now enjoyed after surrender, so that this information could be conveyed through families of those who had entered the mountains to those in the mountains themselves. These surrender operations achieved results comparable to suppression operations. The fact that armed guerrillas were in turmoil became apparent through surrendered persons.

On June 19, two armed guerrillas who surrendered said: "Those on the mountain would like to come down; however, they are prevented from doing so by the rumors of the leaders threatening that anyone who comes down will die, and by the fear that if such intent becomes known, they will face retaliation. If they could genuinely believe in the police's tolerance now, many would come down." [^40]

Kim Ki-yŏl (female, 22 years old), who surrendered on July 22, reported: "Recently the bandit force has been experiencing considerable upheaval and is in a state of dissolution. Not long ago, Yang Sun-ae attempted to escape but was discovered and was killed on the spot; Yi In-sŏn was also executed by beating for engaging in illegal acts within the organization." [^41]

Kang Ch'an-sŏk (farmer, member for nineteen years), who surrendered on July 26, stated: "Recent upheaval within the bandit force has become even more severe; the leaders are absorbed in monitoring their men, and internal purges are even occurring. Currently the bandit force numbers 70, of whom 24 are original guerrillas (those who entered the mountains at the time of or following the 4·3 incident), and even they are experiencing considerable upheaval." [^42]

From the outbreak of the Korean War through June 22, 1952, twenty-two people surrendered through the police's special operations. These individuals found employment in public institutions through the police's job placement services or obtained work at other jobs; some engaged in private occupations at home. [^43]

Effective October 13, 1952, Police Bureau Chief Yun Sŏk-ryŏl retired and Yi Kyŏng-jin, a senior inspector who was chief of the Busan Water Police Station, was promoted to police executive and took office as Cheju Police Bureau chief. Director Yi Kyŏng-jin was an officer with extensive experience commanding suppression operations, having served as commander of police combat operations in the Kŭmsan district, commander-in-chief of police combat operations along the Nakdong River, and commander-in-chief of police combat operations in the East Sea region. [^44]

Upon taking office, the new bureau chief immediately established an aggressive armed guerrilla elimination plan and, on November 1, established the 100 Combat Police Command (hereinafter abbreviated as the "100 Command"). [^45]

The organization of this unit was as follows: the 100 Command (commander: Police Executive Kim Won-yong, headquartered in Sŏgwipo); under its jurisdiction, the 101st Unit (commander: Police Inspector Han Chae-gil, stationed in Sŏngŭp), the 102nd Unit (commander: Police Inspector Han Nak-gu, stationed in Sŏngsan-po), the 103rd Unit (commander: Police Inspector Chang Sŏk-kwan, stationed in Nokha-ji), and the 105th Unit (commander: Police Inspector Hyŏn Byŏng-du, stationed at Tare-orŭm). Each unit, combining the headquarters unit, numbered approximately 110 personnel and was organized into 3 companies; each company consisted of 2 platoons totaling approximately 30 personnel. These companies were also given names such as Paekrok Company, Sinp'ung Company, Sinsŏn Company, and P'ilsŭng Company. The total personnel of the 100 Command, combining the 4 units, headquarters, communications, and supply, numbered approximately 500. [^46]

Separately from the 100 Command, the Cheju Police established and operated a reconnaissance guerrilla company (under Captain Hŏ Ch'ang-sun), whose primary mission was intelligence gathering regarding armed guerrillas, though the unit also performed suppression duties as circumstances required. Each police bureau also organized reconnaissance guerrilla companies that performed the same mission as the reconnnaissance guerrilla company of the provincial police. Personnel of the 100 Command received two weeks of training at the Army 1st Training School in Mosulpo and were armed with carbines and M-1 rifles plus 2 grenades, and conducted suppression operations while living in garrison posts without leaving the mountain. This was due to the police bureau chief's operational policy to conduct annihilation operations while living in the same circumstances as the armed guerrillas.

Furthermore, in November the Cheju Police requested 313.25 million wŏn for winter (冬期) operational expenses, and subsequently requested a total of 687.32 million wŏn, including subsistence and supplies such as underclothing, gloves, and blankets for the operational troops, while pledging: "With this requested amount approved, the suppression troops will cause no harm to civilians, and with determination upon determination, we will do our utmost to completely annihilate the communist rebels." [^47] This passage also suggests that there had been civilian suffering during the suppression process.

The Cheju Police, through a recommendation to the Police Bureau, received three companies of 342 people—one company (114 people) each from Kyŏnggi, South Ch'unch'ŏng, and North Kyŏngsang provinces from December 25, 1952, through June 15, 1953, for a six-month period. [^48] This personnel was used to fill the gap left by personnel transferred to the 100 Command or to serve as operational reserves for each police bureau.

[^27]: Cheju Shinmun, August 5, 1950; Kang Yong-sam and Yi Kyŏng-su, "The Great River Record: 100 Years of Cheju," 1984, Taekwang Cultural Company, pp. 769–775.

[^28]: Cheju Shinmun, August 9, 11, 1950; Republic of Korea Government Public Relations Bureau, Gazette No. 387, August 4, 1950.

[^29]: Cheju Shinmun, August 13, 1950.

[^30]: Headquarters, Korean National Police Force, Republic of Korea, Daily Enemy Activity Report #0006, April 24, 1951.

[^31]: Cheju Shinmun, January 19, 26, 1952.

**[p. 344]**

The 100 Command forbade the suppression force from coming down from the mountain and maintained communications between the command and units and between units using telephone lines laid along the "Hashimaki" (headband) roads built around Hallasan during the Japanese period. However, due to frequent cutting of communication lines by armed guerrillas, wireless was utilized extensively. The 100 commander traveled by helicopter, using a radio to call each company to confirm its position and issue necessary operational directives. The means of confirming company position was smoke grenades. [^49]

The operational zone was roughly divided such that the 100 Command assumed responsibility for terrain of 500-meter height and above, while the reconnaissance guerrilla units of each police bureau handled areas below that height. Accordingly, if 100 Command personnel came down below the 500-meter height, it constituted a violation of operational orders. In such circumstances, 100 Command personnel coming down below the 500-meter height would be caught in ambush by reconnaissance guerrilla units of the police station, resulting in police-to-police combat and casualties. [^50]

The armed guerrillas on Hallasan had numbered 68 on June 1, 1952, but through subsequent suppression operations and surrender campaigns, the number had decreased to 44 by November 20, as ascertained. [^51] Kang T'ae-wŏn (20 years old, from Sinch'on village, Choch'ŏn-myŏn), who was captured on November 26, 1952, having been abducted and lived in the mountains before experiencing internal division and escaping, provided the following account of his experience:

One afternoon in September 1951, while gathering firewood on the mountain and returning home, I was seized by bandits and taken away. I received ideological education from Chŏng Gwŏn-su for three months, [during which I was told] groundless content that Soviet troops would come to this island after waiting half a year to liberate us, and that farmers would receive land distributed for free and that paradise would be created. Afterward I was incorporated into the armed band and engaged in looting several times, but I was exhausted from fatigue. One day while standing guard, I inadvertently fell asleep and was discovered; when they began looking for a shovel and other [tools for] preparing purges, I pretended to go to the toilet, evaded the surveillance of a female bandit, and fled hastily. I wandered alone in the mountains for approximately four months before becoming a captive. The sustenance of the bandits was primitive—sometimes they got by on rice gruel and salt, other times using horsemeat as a substitute diet. In the deepest jungle, they laid branches and two people covered themselves with one blanket, and on rainy days they used the blanket as a roof.

These people, with their words, cry out for liberation and claim to be more concerned with the people than anyone; in reality, however, they practice ruthless tyranny, and as a result there were internal divisions. Specifically, during the period when Hŏ Yŏng-sam was commander, Kwŏn P'al and others had been hiding the fact that the ammunition being distributed was insufficient; after Hŏ Yŏng-sam was killed by police, this fact was discovered by the new commander Kim Sŏng-gyŭ. Kim Sŏng-gyŭ, despite Kwŏn P'al being his close associate, tortured him relentlessly; unable to endure torture, Kwŏn P'al escaped from the unit one day. As Kim Sŏng-gyŭ's discipline and violence toward his subordinates intensified, subordinates one by one defected to Kwŏn P'al, with as many as eleven going over to him. These two forces maintained a cold war until one day they engaged in fierce combat; the weaker Kwŏn P'al faction was completely annihilated, and Kim Sŏng-gyŭ's faction also suffered numerous casualties, causing the bandit force's power to decline sharply. [^52]

The Cheju Police suppressed the armed guerrillas on Hallasan through the 100 Command and the reconnaissance guerrilla company; each police bureau conducted ambush patrols in important areas or stationed mobile units in preparation for armed guerrilla descent and attacks. When Rainbow Unit of the Army was deployed in early 1953, they conducted joint operations for three months.

The 101st Unit's Ch'ŏngryong Company, which began operations on December 22, 1952, engaged approximately 20 armed guerrillas in combat on December 24 near Chŏk-ak to the northwest, killing 4. Subsequently, on January 24, 1953, approximately 20 armed guerrillas infiltrated Chungmun, looted cattle and horses, and fled. The 100 Command pursued the armed guerrillas who infiltrated Chungmun; on January 25, Kim Chong-hwa Company engaged more than 30 armed guerrillas in three hours of fierce combat in the northwest of Hallasan, killing 5; on January 26, the 101st Unit engaged approximately 50 armed guerrillas in four hours of fierce combat between Bulg'ŭn-orŭm and Sae-orŭm, killing 4 and capturing 1. Over these two consecutive days, after fierce combat, they killed 9 and captured 1. The Police Bureau chief sent a congratulatory telegram to the Cheju Police, who had achieved such a notable result. [^53]

Han Chae-gil, the 101st Unit commander who directed the pursuit campaign at that time, testified as follows:

When there was an attack on Chungmun, we started pursuit and went north to Aewŏl, where footprints appeared on the snow. When the footprints appeared to go downward, the company commanders said let's pursue them downward. However, I thought it was wrong for the armed guerrillas with cattle, horses, and grain to go down into the deep mountains; there was no reason for them to go down. I instructed them to pursue upward toward the mountain. When we went up the mountain, sure enough they had constructed fortifications there. To deceive us, they had put on their shoes backward and gone upward. We encircled and attacked them; after fierce combat we took the fortifications and found, in addition to the cattle and horses they had brought from Chungmun, horses they had seized and placed in sacks, as well as female prisoners. [^54]

The Hallasan armed guerrillas facing the police suppression force gradually diminished through the suppression operations by the 100 Command and the reconnaissance guerrilla company and the activities of the intelligence team. On November 20, 1953, the police dismantled most garrison posts and police outposts and withdrew police personnel stationed there. [^55] They also distributed 20,000 leaflets to island residents in which the police appealed for residents' cooperation and promised to grant immediate freedom to surrendering armed guerrillas. Additionally, they offered a bounty of 10 million wŏn for the death, capture, or body delivery of armed guerrilla leader Kim Sŏng-gyŭ, and 1 million wŏn for each original armed guerrilla (those who entered the mountains from the beginning of the 4·3 incident). [^56]

On October 13, 1953, as Police Executive Kim Won-yong, the 100 commander, was relieved of duty, Han Chae-gil, the 101st Unit commander, became the 100 commander, and Police Inspector Chŏng Ch'ung-bin became the 101st Unit commander.

From October 1952 to November 1953, during the one year and one month following the assumption of office by Police Bureau Chief Yi Kyŏng-jin, the operational results, losses, and remaining armed guerrilla forces were as follows: △ Operational results: human casualties: killed = 29, captured = 14, surrendered = 22, internal purges = 15, total = 80. △ Material casualties: firearms = 25, ammunition = 352 rounds, grain = 82 sŏk, livestock = 135 head, other = numerous. △ Losses: combat deaths = 10 (police 1, volunteer police and special activity unit members 9), weapon losses = 7. △ Remnant armed guerrilla forces: total of 11 (original guerrillas 5, abducted guerrillas 6). [^57]

The police continued operations to completely annihilate the 11 remaining armed guerrillas and achieved results in suppression operations in December 1953 and January 1954. On January 15, 1954, the police announced that only 6 armed guerrillas remained and reported the operational results for December 1953 through January 15, 1954 as follows:
