**[p. 345]**

This period of suppression included sustained operations by the police in mountainous terrain and the organization of garrison posts to contain the armed guerrillas. Police Bureau Chief Yi Kyŏng-jin launched aggressive anti-guerrilla operations shortly after taking office, establishing what became known as the 100 Combat Police Command with approximately 500 troops organized into four units. These forces implemented a strategy of compressing the guerrillas into progressively smaller zones while conducting surrender campaigns. By late 1952, the remnant armed guerrilla force had been reduced significantly through these combined suppression and surrender operations.

**[p. 346]**

Operations Results for December 1953 – January 15, 1954

[^54]: Han Chae-gil (81 years old, Sŏgwi-dong, Sŏgwipo City; former police officer, recorded October 23, 2002), testimony.

[^55]: Cheju Shinmun, November 21, 1953.

[^56]: Cheju Shinmun, November 21, 1953.

[^57]: Cheju Shinmun, December 3, 1953.

**[p. 347]**

stated that garrisoning forward positions to compress the perimeter and reinforce facilities to completely block enemy movements had produced little result in the April annihilation campaign, and announced his intention to advance the combat outposts forward by 20–30 *ri* to tighten the cordon. The prohibited zone as of late September, following the forward relocation of outposts, was as follows:

- **Cheju Town area**: The line connecting Myŏngdo-am — Yŏngp'yŏng — Chuksŏng — Kŭmak — Yusin-dong
- **Aewŏl-myŏn area**: The line connecting Yusin-dong — Kŭmdŏk — Sŏgwang — Kŭmak
- **Hallim-myŏn area**: The line connecting Kŭmak — Chŏngmul-orŭm — To-ŭrak
- **Andŏk-myŏn area**: The line connecting To-ŭrak — Pyŏng-ak — Nokha-ji
- **Chungmun-myŏn area**: The line connecting Nokha-ji — Sech'o-ji
- **Sŏgwi-myŏn area**: The line connecting Saech'o-ji — Mi-ak — Su-ak
- **Namwŏn-myŏn area**: The line connecting Su-ak — Hill 456 — Min-ak
- **P'yosŏn-myŏn area**: The line connecting Min-ak — Taerŏk-bong — Modun-ak — Kaemin-orŭm
- **Kujwa-myŏn area**: The line connecting Kaemin-orŭm — Nop'ŭn-orŭm — Ch'e-orŭm
- **Choch'ŏn-myŏn area**: The line connecting Ch'e-orŭm — Alba-magi — Ch'ŏnmi-ak — Sugi-dong — Myŏngdo-am[^37]

The police promoted a surrender campaign (*kwisun chakchŏn*) targeting the remaining armed guerrillas. Methods included working through their relatives and acquaintances to encourage surrender, dropping leaflets across the entirety of Halla Mountain urging descent, and holding lectures to encourage surrender. The police also operated a "security guerrilla company" (*ch'alsal yuggyŏk taedae*) and special operations teams to conduct surrender operations against the armed guerrillas. In concert with these efforts, the Jeju Provincial Assembly drafted a statement urging descent whose gist was: "If you surrender, the state guarantees your life; the Provincial Assembly takes responsibility for the rest of your days. Abandon meaningless resistance and join in building peace in your homeland" — posting it at key locations in every village and also dropping it over Halla Mountain.[^38]

[^36]: *Chejusinbo*, May 20, 1952.
[^37]: *Chejusinbo*, September 30, 1952.
[^38]: *Chejusinbo*, June 5, 1952.

**[p. 348]**

Beginning in October 1950, the Jeju Island Police Bureau began operating an intelligence unit (*chŏkchŏng-ban*) — six personnel from within the Security Section, also known as the Surrender Operations Unit. The unit's mission was to receive guerrillas who had been captured or had surrendered, interrogate them with expert techniques to understand the internal situation of the armed forces, and devise appropriate countermeasures. Intelligence relevant to operations was provided to the suppression forces; surrender encouragement was broadcast via loudspeaker or leaflets dropped from aircraft; and surrender campaigns were conducted by mobilizing surrendered guerrillas themselves or their parents, siblings, and other relatives.[^39]

The unit also arranged employment for surrendered individuals, and whenever there were killed combatants in suppression operations, personnel rushed to the scene to confirm their identities.

Surrender leaflets were initially distributed in the name of the Speaker of the Provincial Assembly and later also in the name of the Chief of Police. Through this surrender campaign, by 1952 — more than a year after its launch — cases began to appear of guerrillas killing their sentries, seizing their weapons, and then surrendering. Alongside the leaflets, the accounts of surrendered guerrillas' experiences in the mountains and the happiness they said they enjoyed after surrendering were disseminated through lecture tours — relayed to those still in the mountains through the family members of those who had gone up.

These surrender operations produced results nearly matching those of the suppression campaigns. The fact that the armed guerrillas were wavering was confirmed by surrendered guerrillas themselves. Two guerrillas who surrendered on June 19 stated: "The compatriots in the mountains want to come down, but the rebel leader's counter-propaganda that they will be killed if they descend, combined with the terror of discovery if such intentions become known, is preventing them from doing so — but if they truly came to believe in the police's genuine magnanimity, many would descend at once."[^40]

Kim Ki-yŏl (female, age 22), who surrendered on July 22, reported: "Recently there has been considerable turbulence and the remnant forces are on the verge of disintegration; not long ago Yang Sun-ae attempted to escape and was discovered and executed on the spot, and Yi In-sŏn was hacked to death on charges of misconduct within the unit."[^41]

Kang Ch'an-sŏk (farmer, age 19), who surrendered on July 26, confessed: "The turbulence among the remnant forces has intensified even further — the commander is fully occupied just keeping watch over his men, and internal purges are taking place. Currently the remnant force numbers 70, of whom 24 are original guerrillas [those who went to the mountains around the time of the 4·3 Incident], and even they are in considerable turmoil."[^42]

From the outbreak of the Korean War through June 22, 1952, a total of 22 persons surrendered through the police's special operations. These individuals found employment in public institutions with the police's job referral, or took other positions; some worked at home in their original occupations.[^43]

On October 13, 1952, Commissioner Yun Sŏng-nyŏl stepped down as director of the island police bureau, and Yi Kyŏng-jin, the superintendent of the Busan Maritime Police Station, was simultaneously promoted to police commissioner and assumed the directorship of the Jeju Island Police Bureau. Director Yi Kyŏng-jin was a veteran of suppression command, having served as commander of the Kŭmsan District Police Combat Command, commander-in-chief of the Naktong River Police Combat Command, and commander-in-chief of the East Sea District Police Combat Command.[^44]

[^39]: Testimony of Chŏng Nam-du (age 74, Illo-1-dong, Cheju City; police officer at the time; recorded October 10, 2001).
[^40]: *Chejusinbo*, June 22, 1952.
[^41]: *Chejusinbo*, July 24, 1952.
[^42]: *Chejusinbo*, July 30, 1952.
[^43]: *Chejusinbo*, June 22, 1952.

**[p. 349]**

The new director immediately drew up an aggressive guerrilla annihilation plan upon taking office, and on November 1 established the 100th Combat Police Command (*100 T'obŏl Kyŏngch'al Saryŏngbu*, hereafter "100 Command").[^45] The unit's organization placed four sub-units under the 100 Command (Commander: Security Section Chief Kim Wŏn-yong, senior superintendent, garrisoned at Sŏgwip'o): Unit 101 (Commander: Han Chae-gil, inspector, garrisoned at Sŏngŭp), Unit 102 (Commander: Han Nak-ku, inspector, garrisoned at Sŏngsanp'o), Unit 103 (Commander: Chang Sŏk-gwan, inspector, garrisoned at Nokha-ji), and Unit 105 (Commander: Hyŏn Pyŏng-du, inspector, garrisoned at Tarae-orŭm). Each unit, including the headquarters, was organized into 3 companies of approximately 110 personnel, with each company consisting of 2 platoons of some 30 men. The companies were also given names: "Paengno Company," "Sinp'ung Company," "Sinsŏn Company," and "P'ilsŭng Company." The 100 Command's total strength, including the 4 sub-units, headquarters, communications, and logistics, reached approximately 500 personnel.[^46]

Separate from the 100 Command, the Jeju Island Police operated a "Security Guerrilla Company" (*Ch'alsal Yuggyŏk Chungdae*, Commander: Hŏ Ch'ang-sun, police lieutenant) under the supervision of the security section chief. This unit's primary mission was intelligence collection on the armed guerrillas, but it was also capable of conducting suppression operations as circumstances required. In addition, each police station organized its own Security Guerrilla Unit (*ch'alsal yuggyŏk-tae*), which carried out the same missions as the provincial police's security guerrilla company.

Personnel of the 100 Command underwent two weeks of training at the Army's First Training Center in Mosŭlp'o and were armed with carbines, M-1 rifles, and 2 grenades each. They carried out suppression operations while eating and sleeping at their outposts without returning from the mountains — in accordance with the police bureau director's operational policy that they must live under the exact same conditions as the armed guerrillas in order to conduct effective sweep operations.

The Jeju police also requested 312.5 million *wŏn* for winter operations from the Provincial Assembly in November, and then made a combined demand of 687.32 million *wŏn* including provisions for operations units, underwear, gloves, blankets, and other supply expenses — pledging that "passage of this requested amount will eliminate inconveniences imposed on the public by the suppression units, and we will redouble our determination and do our utmost to ensure the complete annihilation of the communist guerrillas."[^47] This is itself suggestive of the fact that the suppression process up to that point had caused inconveniences to the civilian population.

[^44]: *Chejusinbo*, December 3, 1953.
[^45]: Jeju Island Police Bureau, *Cheju Kyŏngch'alsa* [History of Jeju Police], 1990, p. 318.
[^46]: Testimonies of Kim Ch'ang-gŭn (age 75, Napŭp-ri, Aewŏl-ŭp; police officer at the time; recorded September 20, 2001); Song Pong-ch'un (age 77, Posŏng-ri, Taejŏng-ŭp; right-wing organization member and police at the time; recorded August 8, 2002); Han Chae-gil (age 81, Sŏgwi-dong, Sŏgwip'o City; police officer at the time; recorded October 23, 2002).
[^47]: *Chejusinbo*, November 14, 1952.

**[p. 350]**

The police requested that 3 companies of 342 personnel — 1 company (114 men) each from Kyŏnggi, South Ch'ungch'ŏng, and North Kyŏngsang provinces — be attached for 6-month periods from December 25, 1952 to June 15, 1953.[^48] This force was used to fill the gap left by units absorbed into the 100 Command or held as mobile reserves at each police station.

The 100 Command prohibited suppression forces from descending from the mountains, maintaining communications between command and units and among units via telephone wire strung along the "hajimakki" (headband) road around Halla Mountain constructed during the Japanese colonial era. Due to frequent severing of communication lines by the armed guerrillas, radio was heavily utilized, and the 100 Command commander would fly by helicopter calling each company by radio to confirm positions and issue necessary operational orders. The means of confirming company positions was smoke grenades.[^49]

Operationally, the area above roughly the 500-meter elevation line was the 100 Command's responsibility, and the area below fell to each police station's security guerrilla unit. Accordingly, any 100 Command member who descended below the 500-meter mark was in violation of operational orders. Because of this arrangement, there were cases in which 100 Command personnel who had descended below the 500-meter line were caught in an ambush by a police station's security guerrilla unit, leading to friendly-fire casualties in firefights between police units.[^50]

The Halla Mountain armed guerrillas, which had numbered 68 on June 1, 1952, were assessed to have declined to 44 as of November 20 through suppression operations and surrender campaigns.[^51] Kang T'ae-wŏn (age 20 at the time), a native of Sinch'on, Choch'ŏn-myŏn, who was captured on November 26, 1952, gave the following account of how he was kidnapped, lived in the mountains, experienced internal strife, and ultimately escaped:

> I was taken by the insurgents one afternoon in September 1951 as I was coming down from the mountains where I had been collecting firewood. I received three months of indoctrination from Chŏng Kwŏn-su, whose content was utter nonsense: that Soviet troops would come and liberate this island within six months, that farmland would be distributed to peasants without compensation, and that it would become a paradise worth living in. I was subsequently incorporated into the armed forces and participated in several raids, but I was exhausted. Then one day I fell asleep while standing sentry and was caught — they were looking for shovels as though preparing to purge me, so I pretended to go to the toilet, slipped past the watch of a female guerrilla, and fled. I wandered alone in the mountains for about four months before I was captured.
>
> The insurgents' food, clothing, and shelter were primitive. We sometimes subsisted on porridge and salt, and used horsemeat as a substitute. We slept in the depths of the jungle, spreading branches on the ground and covering two persons with one blanket; on rainy days the blanket served as a roof.

[^48]: Security Bureau, Security Order No. 84, December 10, 1952; Security Order No. 16, June 8, 1953.
[^49]: Testimony of Cha Ok-yun (age 76, Hanwŏn-ri, Hangyŏng-myŏn; police officer at the time; recorded February 27, 2002).
[^50]: Testimony of Song Pong-ch'un (age 77, Posŏng-ri, Taejŏng-ŭp; right-wing organization member and police at the time; recorded August 8, 2002).
[^51]: *Chejusinbo*, November 19, 1952.

**[p. 351]**

Those people shout liberation with their mouths and claim to act for the people more than anyone, but in reality they practice ruthless dictatorship — and this has caused internal splits. Specifically, from the time when Hŏ Yŏng-sam was commander, Kwŏn P'al and others had been concealing the ammunition that was being issued, claiming it was insufficient. This came to light after Hŏ Yŏng-sam was shot dead by the police, when the new commander Kim Sŏng-gyu discovered it. Kim Sŏng-gyu, without mercy, tortured his close friend Kwŏn P'al; unable to withstand the torture, Kwŏn P'al escaped from his unit one day. As Kim Sŏng-gyu's crackdowns and violence against his subordinates intensified, his men began deserting one by one until eleven had gone over to Kwŏn P'al's side. The two factions maintained a cold standoff that eventually erupted in fierce combat; the weaker Kwŏn P'al faction was annihilated, and the Kim Sŏng-gyu faction also suffered many casualties, causing the guerrilla force to rapidly weaken.[^52]

The Jeju police used the 100 Command and the security guerrilla company to suppress the Halla Mountain armed guerrillas, and at each police station maintained forces in ambush at key locations or kept mobile units on standby against guerrilla descents and attacks. In early 1953, when the Army's Rainbow Unit (*Mujigae Pudae*) was deployed, joint operations were conducted with that unit for three months.

Unit 101's "Blue Dragon Company" launched operations on December 22, 1952, and on December 24 engaged a force of more than 20 armed guerrillas in the jungle at Malch'e-orŭm north of Chŏk-ak, killing 4 and achieving a notable combat result. On January 24, 1953, more than 20 armed guerrillas invaded Chungmun, raiding cattle, horses, and other livestock before fleeing. The 100 Command pursued the guerrillas who had invaded Chungmun: on January 25, Kim Chong-hwa Company engaged more than 30 armed guerrillas in the northwest of Halla Mountain in a firefight lasting more than three hours, killing 5; and on January 26, Unit 101 engaged more than 50 armed guerrillas between Pulgŭn-orŭm and Sae-orŭm in a fierce battle lasting four hours, killing 4 and capturing 1 alive. Over the two days of January 25–26, in two separate fierce engagements, 9 were killed and 1 captured. The Security Bureau chief sent a message of commendation to the Jeju police for their rare major combat results.[^53] Han Chae-gil, who commanded Unit 101 and led the pursuit in person, testified as follows:

> When the attack on Chungmun occurred, we began the pursuit. When we got to Puk-gun Aewŏl, footprints appeared in the snow. The company commanders, seeing that the tracks went downward, said we should pursue downward. But I reasoned that the armed guerrillas must have gone deeper into the mountains taking cattle, horses, and food — there was no reason for them to go downward — and ordered the pursuit upward into the mountains. When we went up into the mountains,

[^52]: *Chejusinbo*, December 5–6, 1952.
[^53]: *Chejusinbo*, January 26, 27, 28, 1953.

**[p. 352]**

they had indeed constructed positions there. They had worn their shoes backwards to deceive us on the way up. We surrounded them and attacked; at the end of a fierce battle we seized their position, and inside we found not only the cattle and horses they had brought from Chungmun but also horses that had been slaughtered and packed in straw sacks, and we also took female prisoners.[^54]

The Halla Mountain armed guerrillas facing the police suppression forces were gradually eliminated through the 100 Command's suppression operations, the security guerrilla company's activities, and the intelligence unit's work. The police, effective November 20, 1953, dismantled most of their outposts and police checkpoints and withdrew the police forces stationed at them.[^55] They also produced 20,000 leaflets and distributed them to island residents; in these leaflets the police appealed for the residents' cooperation and promised immediate freedom to surrendering guerrillas, while posting a bounty of 10 million *wŏn* for the capture, killing, or delivery of the body of armed guerrilla leader Kim Sŏng-gyu, and 1 million *wŏn* per person for "original guerrillas" (those who went to the mountains around the time of the 4·3 Incident).[^56]

On October 13, 1953, when 100 Command commander Kim Wŏn-yong stepped down, Unit 101 commander Han Chae-gil became the 100 Commander, and Inspector Chŏng Ch'ung-bin became Unit 101 commander. The combat results, losses, and remaining strength of the armed guerrillas over the 13 months from late October 1952 through late November 1953, following Director Yi Kyŏng-jin's appointment, were as follows:

- **Combat results:**
  - *Personnel losses*: killed = 29, captured = 14, surrendered = 22, internally purged = 15; **total = 80**
  - *Materiel losses*: firearms = 25, ammunition = 352 rounds, food = 82 *sŏk*, livestock = 135 head, other = numerous
- **Own casualties**: 10 killed (1 police, 9 civil defense special unit); firearms seized = 7
- **Remaining guerrilla strength**: 11 total (5 original guerrillas, 6 kidnapped)[^57]

The police continued operations to annihilate the 11 remaining guerrillas and achieved further results in suppression operations in December 1953 and January 1954. On January 15, 1954, the police stated that the armed guerrillas numbered only 6 and announced the following results for the period from December 1953 to January 15, 1954:

**Combat results: December 1953 – January 15, 1954**

[^54]: Testimony of Han Chae-gil (age 81, Sŏgwi-dong, Sŏgwip'o City; police at the time; recorded October 23, 2002).
[^55]: *Chejusinbo*, November 21, 1953.
[^56]: *Chejusinbo*, November 21, 1953.
[^57]: *Chejusinbo*, December 3, 1953.

**[p. 353]**

△ Personnel: 1 executed, 1 corpse (recognized as internal liquidation), 3 surrenders, total 5. △ Weapons and other items: 13 rifles, food, subversive documents, daily necessities, and other numerous items. △ Remaining armed guerrillas: 6 (male 4, female 2). △ Other intelligence: These comprised 2 groups—one of 4 persons (male 3, female 1) and another of 2 persons (male 1, female 1)—whose mutual communications had been severed. Among these, 2 were sustaining injuries from gunshot wounds: Kim Sŏng-gyu and Chŏng Kwŏn-su.[^58] One month later, on February 13, 1954, Cho Hwa-ok (female, age 19 at the time) surrendered, leaving the armed guerrilla forces with 5 members (male 4, female 1) and 3 rifles. According to Cho Hwa-ok's testimony, "For the past month, food supplies have been completely cut off and we have been surviving on vegetables, sweet potatoes, and horse meat. We could not approach the settlements because of the security net. The four of us armed guerrillas, myself included, feeling unsafe even in caves, built stone walls within thorn thickets and covered ourselves with a single blanket to sleep. The remaining force numbers 5 and we have 3 rifles. The wounded Kim Sŏng-gyu carries 1 rifle and operates with Han Sun-ae, while Chŏng Kwŏn-su, O Wŏn-gwŏn, and Pyŏn Ch'ang-hŭi—three in number—carry 2 rifles and operate separately."[^59] In this way, she disclosed the hardships they had endured and the reality of their internal situation. The police, judging that although 5 armed guerrillas remained they posed no significant threat, issued an order on April 1 to permit the return and resettlement of mid-mountain villages, thereby taking measures to partially open the Halla Mountains.[^60]

3) Army Suppression Operations

① Marine Corps Suppression Operations

When the Korean War broke out on June 25, 1950, the Marine Corps forces stationed on Cheju Island suspended the ongoing suppression operations against the armed guerrillas and intensified coastal defense. With the proclamation of emergency martial law on July 8, the Marine Corps undertook to secure public order, and devoted itself to coastal surveillance and maritime security. It was also assigned the mission of preventing advance landings by enemy forces on Cheju Island and of discovering and annihilating enemy forces that might appear at sea. When North Korean forces advanced southward with tanks in the lead, the Marine Corps relocated one battalion (comprising three companies) from Cheju on July 15 to the Kunsan area and committed it to combat.

**[p. 354]**

Meanwhile, the Marine Corps Command recruited approximately 3,000 new troops from various locations on Cheju Island beginning August 5, and organized one regiment. On September 1, under the command of Commander Shin Hyŏn-jun, the unit departed from Cheju Island,[^61] arrived in Pusan, completed joint training with U.S. Marine Corps, and participated in the Inchon Landing Operation on September 15.[^62]

As the front lines rapidly pushed southward with the intervention of Chinese forces, from December 1950 onward refugees poured into Cheju Island in increasing numbers. The armed guerrillas abandoned their previous passive posture and began active operations. With military facilities such as recruit training centers being established on Cheju Island and refugees arriving continuously, and with armed guerrillas frequently appearing, stability in the Cheju area was deemed desirable. Accordingly, the army dispatched a suppression force of company-level Marine Corps strength to Cheju Island.

The Marine Corps unit, deployed on January 14, 1951, received support from some police combat units and was organized into four mixed platoons, with the Marine Corps forming the main force. The commander of the suppression force was Lieutenant Kwŏn Sŏk-ki, a Marine officer who had earned merit in operations at Tongyŏng. Joint operations by the mixed Marine and police suppression force began on January 17. At that time, the armed guerrillas on Halla Mountain were estimated at approximately 80 in number. The suppression force used Sŏgwipo, on the southern side of Halla Mountain, as its base and conducted intensive searches particularly of the eastern mountain regions of Halla.

On January 27, the first platoon intercepted approximately 20 armed guerrillas at Ŏsŭng-saeng Peak, engaged them in a 40-minute firefight, killed 3, and secured considerable war material. On January 29, the second platoon discovered armed guerrillas—approximately 30 in number—transporting food supplies near Sara Peak, launched a surprise attack, and killed 3. On February 10, the force discovered an armed guerrilla base between Sara Peak and Myŏngdo Peak, launched a surprise attack that killed 15, but found themselves surrounded by approximately 60 armed guerrillas. The Marine second platoon engaged in fierce combat, and when the first platoon nearby, which had been conducting searches, moved to provide supporting firepower, the enemy began to withdraw. In this fierce engagement, the Marine Corps killed 20 armed guerrillas but suffered 11 combat casualties, including First Class Petty Officer Sŏ Chae-yun. On February 22, the third platoon similarly encountered 40 armed guerrillas near North Peak (Puk Peak), fought them, and killed 5.

The company-scale military-police joint suppression force, led by the Marine Corps and continuing operations until the end of March, killed some 50 armed guerrillas and captured numerous weapons, thereby significantly weakening the armed guerrilla forces.

**[p. 355]**

② Army Special Forces Suppression Operations

On January 1, 1952, a secret operations unit was established in Taegu, which was subsequently redesignated as the Army Special Operations Unit (commonly referred to as Unit 9172). Colonel Yi Ki-gŏn was appointed commander of this unit, and Major Pak Ch'ang-am (military affairs) and Major Song Chŏng-hyŏp (political affairs) were appointed as deputy commanders. The unit was organized into operations and assault sections and underwent approximately five months of special training near Hawaii.

After completing special training, the unit was divided into an East Coast Unit and a Halla Mountain Unit, and both underwent training at the Hyŏljŏng training center in Pusan. The East Coast Unit had as its primary objective surprise operations through maritime infiltration, while the Halla Mountain Unit had as its primary objective a final practical exercise on-site, combined with guerrilla suppression operations, to summarize all training conducted to date.

The Halla Mountain Unit, pursuant to an instruction from Army Headquarters on January 25, 1953, was assigned to Cheju Island operations beginning January 20 and ending May 27, 1953, and was placed under the Army Intelligence Unit (Intelligence Unit commander: Colonel Yi Ch'ŏl-hŭi). The unit designation was changed to "Rainbow Unit." The Rainbow Unit, consisting of 86 officers and enlisted men (25 officers and 61 enlisted personnel) under the command of Major Pak Ch'ang-am, arrived at the Hwasun Harbor on Cheju Island on January 29 and conducted a nighttime movement to Sinyo-ri in Sŏgwi District.[^63]

The Rainbow Unit was organized into a headquarters (5 officers, 6 enlisted) and 5 detachments (each with 4 officers and 11 enlisted). Each detachment was composed of a headquarters (1 officer and 2 enlisted) and 3 squads (1 officer and 3 enlisted each). All personnel were issued either carbines or M-1 rifles, and each squad was equipped with a radio.

After completing preparations at Shinheyo National School, the Rainbow Unit commenced operations on February 3, advancing toward snow-covered Halla Mountain under cover of darkness. The Rainbow Unit's operational period came shortly after the police 100 Garrison Command initiated its operations.

The Rainbow Unit, short of personnel, conducted extensive psychological operations against the civilian population. During operations, insignia of rank were removed so that commanders would not become sniper targets. In joint operations with the Police 100 Garrison Command, the unit conducted seven operations (numbered 1 through 7). In most cases, the police surrounded and attacked the armed guerrillas from outside, with the Rainbow Unit lying in ambush and conducting surprise attacks, or the police established an outer perimeter while the Rainbow Unit conducted powerful searches within the encirclement, or the unit lay in ambush at locations suitable for guerrilla refuge and launched attacks.

Operation 6 on March 14 was an operation that delivered a major blow to the armed guerrillas. Based on intelligence received, the police set up an ambush to block the rear while concentrating all 5 detachments on the 14th in a drive from Kyŏnwŏl Peak toward Sŏngpan Peak. When the unit's hideout was discovered that afternoon and subjected to a surprise attack, the armed guerrillas scattered into groups of 2-4 persons, utilized terrain and forest cover, and dispersed toward the Halla summit.

**[p. 356]**

At this time, the police rear block was not entirely effective, and the armed guerrillas that were detected could not be completely eliminated. However, the armed guerrillas lost the base they had been using for three years. Operation 7 continued search operations as an extension of the gains from Operation 6. Over the period of May 1-3, the area around the Halla summit was searched, and on the night of May 3, each detachment descended the mountain and regrouped at the Cheju Police Headquarters, thereby concluding operations that had lasted exactly three months. One police officer who participated in this operation testified: "From the investigation section, six of us, including myself, were dispatched to the special unit called the Rainbow Unit for three months. This was a strange unit—it was organized as 2-person squads with one soldier and one police officer, and we police were integrated into these teams. There were no rank insignia, supplies were delivered by cargo plane, and each person was issued 2 blankets and 2 rain cloaks. We dug 2-person foxholes and spent the nights there. We lived even more roughly than the bandits. Later, thinking about it, the objective was not to catch communist bandits but secretly to conduct training on Halla Mountain for infiltration into North Korea. When we came down from Halla Mountain and were driving by truck to Cheju, we were stopped at a checkpoint. When I showed some kind of pass, the soldier conducting the checkpoint saluted and let us through without incident."[^64] In short, this suggests that the Rainbow Unit undertook operations on Cheju while simultaneously conducting training for North Korean infiltration. In these operations, there were instances of friendly fire resulting from insufficient cooperation between the police and the Rainbow Unit, but the operation played a significant role in dispersing and scattering the enemy. The operational results were: 7 killed, 2 captured, and 2 surrenders. There were no casualties on the friendly side.[^65]

3. The Lifting of the Halla Mountain Closure

1) The Lifting of the Closure and the Final Suppression

On August 28, 1954, Shin Sang-muk, a police chief (police rank), took office as successor to Yi Kyŏng-jin as Cheju Island Police Director. Police Director Shin Sang-muk, observing that there had been no trace of the remaining 5 armed guerrillas for a considerable period of time, issued an order on September 21, 1954, to lift the closure order, fully open Halla Mountain, and dismantle the security fortifications around settlements.[^66]

One year after the opening of Halla Mountain, on September 21, 1955, a "Halla Mountain Peace Commemorative Monument" was erected north of Nokdam. The monument bears an inscription reading, "Let it shine forever. Police Director Shin Sang-muk of the Cheju Island Police opened the Halla seal that had been closed for eight years due to the Jeju 4·3 Incident, in the ninth month of this year of Kapsŭ (1954). Is this not a heroic deed? Rather, let our island express gratitude for the freedom and blessing that have been conferred."[^67]

Since 5 armed guerrillas still existed somewhere on Halla Mountain, the lifting of the Halla Mountain closure order did not mean the police suppression operations had been completely concluded. After the police failed for a long time to find traces of the armed guerrillas, Police Director Shin himself visited the families of the 5 remaining armed guerrillas on February 9, 1955, persuading them by saying, "If they surrender, their lives will be absolutely protected," thus urging their prompt surrender.[^68]

On April 3, 1956, the police investigation guerrilla squad engaged 3 armed guerrillas at Ch'e-orŭm in Songdang-ri, Kujwa District, in combat and killed Chŏng Kwŏn-su, a key member of the armed guerrillas. This achievement was the first since mid-February 1954—2 years and 2 months—and reduced the armed guerrilla force to 4 members. When the police failed to find traces of the armed guerrillas for an extended period, they transferred the responsibility for suppressing the armed guerrillas to each police station at a police headquarters meeting on March 11, 1957, placing each police station commander in charge.[^69]

On March 21, 1957, the Cheju Police Station investigation guerrilla squad spotted armed guerrillas descending to secure food supplies near Wolpyŏng-dong and Kyŏnwŏl Peak, pursued them as they fled, and captured Han Sun-ae, a female armed guerrilla who had fallen behind from the group.[^70] On March 27, the police headquarters investigation guerrilla squad engaged 3 armed guerrillas in combat in the dense forests of the Pinan Peak area in the mid-Halla region and killed 2 including Kim Sŏng-gyu, the leader. The last armed guerrilla, O Wŏn-gwŏn, was captured by the Sŏngsan-po guerrilla unit at Songdang-ri, Kujwa District, on April 2.[^71] In this way, gunfire on Halla Mountain ceased.

2) Relief for the Homeless and Village Reconstruction

① Relief for the Homeless

The most urgent issue arising from the Jeju 4·3 Incident was the relief of those who had lost their homes. While some homelessness resulted from fires set by the armed guerrillas, most was caused by the military and police, in the process of forcibly relocating residents from mid-mountain villages to coastal areas as a means of separating the armed guerrillas from the civilian population.

**[p. 357]**

The evacuation of mid-mountain villages, which began in November 1948, was carried out without any measures in place to support those displaced. Residents who lost their livelihoods due to fires set by the military and police—some of whom retreated to the mountains while many others were evacuated to coastal areas—faced dire straits in their daily lives. The situation of those who had lost their homes during the winter was absolutely appalling. The number of homeless resulting from the Jeju 4·3 Incident reached 80,000 to 90,000,[^72] representing one-third of the Cheju Island population.

The issue of relief measures for the homeless was addressed intermittently in the National Assembly. The homeless problem of Cheju was mentioned in unpublished speech notes by National Assembly Member O Yong-guk (a native of Cheju) and in "'Cheju Fact-Finding Report'" by National Assembly Member Chu Ki-yong. The executive branch provided only limited relief supplies and budget support after Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk and Interior Minister Shin Sŏng-mo visited Cheju in March 1949. At the National Assembly's Foreign Affairs and National Defense Committee meeting on October 22, 1948, discussion took place regarding relief for residents displaced by the Yŏsun Incident in South Chŏlla region, but Cheju matters were not addressed. Subsequently, at the 124th meeting on December 8, 1948, it was resolved to dispatch a pacification delegation consisting of National Assembly members to rebellion-affected areas.[^73]

In accordance with this decision, National Assembly Member O Yong-guk, who conducted an inspection of Cheju Island, submitted a report on his findings on January 11, 1949, that addressed emergency relief measures for the homeless:

*Emergency Relief Measures for the Homeless: Thousands of homeless persons have resulted both from murders, arson, and looting by bandits and from the evacuation of mid-mountain village residents due to military operations. Relief for these people is crucial and is being carried out with the cooperation of Cheju Island's Relief Society and government-civilian cooperation. (1) Food supplies: The homeless are being housed in the homes of coastal village residents or administrative facilities. For those lacking food, supplies are being distributed consisting of grain confiscated by the military from bandits, as well as government stockpiles and voluntary contributions from village residents. In addition, sweet potatoes are being distributed as a substitute, but given Cheju Island's urgent circumstances, substantial supplies from the central government are needed. (2) Distribution of clothing and other items: Due to attacks by bandits, clothing and bedding have been destroyed. Clothing is being collected from local patriots and distributed, and cotton cloth (10,000 bolts) distributed by the central government is being provided, but the shortage of clothing for homeless persons facing severe winter conditions is a tragic sight, and central government supply of clothing is needed.[^74]*

The pitiable condition of Cheju Island's homeless is well described in the "'Cheju Fact-Finding Report'" that National Assembly Member Chu Ki-yong contributed to the Chayou (Freedom) Newspaper after conducting a survey of Cheju Island from March 1 to approximately 10 days afterward in 1949. Regarding the condition of the homeless:

*From mountain regions and livestock-raising areas to coastal village areas, homes destroyed in the Jeju 4·3 Incident in four regions of Namwŏn, Pukchoñ (Northern Village), Tongbok, and Sŏgwipo totaled 16,177 houses containing 30,461 structures. The number of innocent victims was 1,193, the injured 419, and the homeless 86,797. The homeless, having fled without time to carry food or clothing, literally made shelters like pigsties, laying straw on the bare ground and living therein. They barely managed to survive day by day on seaweed and mountain plants, with the stench inside and outside the houses being unbearable. When one visited, people could only look skyward and shed tears; those who witnessed such miserable conditions could not help but weep.... Without food, clothing, or bedding, they lived in rags, and disease invaded, creating suffering that defies description. The government must quickly send food, clothing, and medicines to the various categories of homeless persons. I appeal tearfully to all patriotic countrymen throughout the nation to practice frugality in food consumption from patriotic motives and to distribute clothing and medicines, thereby demonstrating the utmost sincerity in saving our patriotic compatriots of Cheju.*[^75]

After Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk and Interior Minister Shin Sŏng-mo conducted an inspection of Cheju Island on March 10, 1949, and returned to Seoul, supplies of food and clothing for the homeless began to be provided. Prime Minister Yi Pŏm-sŏk, commenting on the inspection results, pointed out the urgency of relief for and reconstruction of homes for the homeless.[^76] At this point, the Social Affairs Ministry sent five trucks of food, clothing, bedding, and daily necessities to Cheju Island, followed by eight more trucks.[^77] The government also established a fund of 120 million wŏn for three months of relief in Cheju Island beginning in July 1949.[^78]

Following the outbreak of the Korean War, war refugees poured into Cheju Island, so that relief needs for the Jeju 4·3 Incident homeless were compounded by refugee relief from the war, making conditions indescribable. War refugees numbered as many as 149,000. Relief operations for these people became an urgent matter, but relief efforts were minimal. On January 30, 1953, at the 16th committee meeting of the Cheju Island Relief Committee, the relief goods allocated were only 2,814 sŏk of rice, 14,500 bolts of hemp cloth, 2,275 blankets, 428 cartons of milk, and 38 cartons of soap.[^79]

**[p. 358]**

From the outbreak of the Jeju 4·3 Incident until September 21, 1954, when the incident was concluded, it is not possible to confirm exactly how much relief was provided due to insufficient records. Given the number of Jeju 4·3 homeless and war refugees and the quantities of relief grain and daily necessities allocated on January 30, 1953, it is certain that the relief provided was absolutely insufficient to meet their needs.

② Reconstruction of Mid-Mountain Villages

The number of villages damaged by the Jeju 4·3 Incident was 160, and the number of damaged houses was 15,228 households containing 35,921 structures,[^80] indicating that most mid-mountain villages had suffered damage. The reconstruction of villages laid waste by military and police evacuation orders began in the spring of 1949 through self-help efforts with police permission, starting from areas closest to the coast.

At the time when no official government plan had been established for village reconstruction, National Assembly Member O Yong-guk (native of Cheju) submitted a report to the National Assembly on January 11, 1949, stating that "for house reconstruction, timber should be supplied from Cheju Island's public forests and other private forests, and for displaced persons unable to carry out reconstruction through their own efforts, the central government should provide construction funds of approximately 30,000 to 50,000 wŏn per household."[^81]

National Assembly Member Chu Ki-yong, who conducted a fact-finding survey of Cheju Island in early March 1949, suggested a solution to the housing problem, stating that "mid-mountain villages are in complete ruins, and (abbreviated) approximately 20,000 wŏn in subsidies should be provided per household for 16,000 houses to set up temporary housing."[^82] However, political efforts did not resolve this issue.

On the other hand, the 2nd Regiment, through the determination of its unit commander, undertook village reconstruction and built new school classrooms to replace ones destroyed by fire. Major Chŏn Pu-il, commander of the 1st Battalion of the 2nd Regiment, utilized timber that had been felled for operational reasons in late February 1949 to build classrooms for the burned Chungmun Middle School, enabling students to conduct classes in these classrooms.[^83]

In addition, around July 1949, Colonel Ham Byŏng-sŏn, commander of the 2nd Regiment, after concluding the Jeju 4·3 suppression operations, undertook the reconstruction of Pongae-ri in Cheju town as part of efforts to return the residents who had been evacuated to coastal areas to their original homes, and renamed the village Ham-myŏng-ri.[^84]

**[p. 359]**

In 1950, the government established a standard providing 15,000 wŏn for completely destroyed houses, 10,000 wŏn for partially destroyed houses, and 5,000 wŏn for households whose families had relocated, distributing a total of 300 million wŏn nationwide as housing relief funds, allocating 27 million wŏn to Cheju Island.[^85] However, such modest financial assistance was insufficient to resolve the housing problem.

In February 1950, Kim Ch'ung-hŭi, Cheju Island Governor and chairman of the Cheju Reconstruction Committee, requested government support, noting that most of the approximately 100,000 homeless from the Jeju 4·3 Incident were living in ramshackle shelters. He estimated that reconstruction of damaged houses and various industrial and public facilities, as well as securing startup capital for the displaced, would require total reconstruction funds of approximately 890 million wŏn, comprising Cheju Island's burden of approximately 2.71 billion wŏn, national treasury subsidies of approximately 1.42 billion wŏn, and loans of approximately 4.77 billion wŏn.[^86]

However, before these loan requests could be realized, the war broke out. During the war period, there appear to have been little effort directed toward village reconstruction. As the government had to devote all resources to conducting the war, it could not provide material support for reconstructing the destroyed villages of Cheju Island. Subsequently, beginning October 1, 1952, the government undertook to collect basic data for reconstruction of houses damaged by the Jeju 4·3 Incident.[^87] Only on April 1, 1954, when Halla Mountain was partially opened and residents were permitted to resettle in mid-mountain villages, did reconstruction efforts proceed actively.

On December 21, 1953, the Cheju Island Relief Committee distributed reconstruction materials for 6,770 households (divided between construction and repair) from a supply allotted for the resettlement of 90,000 homeless, indicating that government support was minimal.[^88]

After the Halla Mountain closure order was lifted on September 21, 1954, Cheju Island authorities actively undertook the "refugee resettlement project." In an official memorandum sent by the Cheju Island Governor to each county magistrate on November 20, 1954, instructions were given: "To resolve the difficulty in obtaining timber, authorization for harvesting national forest timber will be arranged. Identify persons among those desiring to construct or repair houses who wish to fell national forest timber."[^89]

In an official memorandum sent to the government on March 2, 1955, a request was made for "relief rice, construction materials, agricultural implements, and other items necessary for the resettlement of 2,074 households of Jeju 4·3 Incident homeless and 898 households of refugees and indigenous unemployed, totaling 2,972 households primarily engaged in opening uncultivated land. Regarding construction materials, requests were made for timber, lime mortar, and nails,"[^90] thereby showing that the authorities attempted to meet the need for timber essential to housing reconstruction through the harvesting of national forest timber.

**[p. 360]**

Through the opening of Halla Mountain in September 1954, the permitting of residents to resettle in their original village locations, government support, and the independent efforts of village residents, the reconstruction of mid-mountain villages was progressively realized.

[^90] Cheju Island Administrative Records, "Refugee Resettlement—Jeju 4·3 Incident Damage Survey," 1955, pp. 244-247.

---

## IV. Damage Assessment

### 1. Overview

From March 1, 1947, to September 21, 1954, the Jeju 4·3 Incident occurring throughout Cheju Island left enormous human and material losses. More than 50 years have passed since the Jeju 4·3 Incident occurred, and while much discussion has taken place regarding its causes and the unjust suffering of victims, there had been no fact-finding investigation at the national level. Moreover, government authorities had never conducted and officially released investigations into the actual conditions of human and material losses.

However, through announcements by Cheju Island authorities of the time, investigations by National Assembly members from Cheju, and reporting by journalists who were special correspondents for various news organizations in Cheju, the general scale of damage can be inferred. Furthermore, through reports of damage filed with the Special Committee for Investigation of Civilian Massacres of the National Assembly in June 1960, reports of damage received by the 4·3 Special Committee of the Cheju Provincial Assembly in the 1990s, and reports of victims received by the Jeju 4·3 Incident Committee from June 8, 2000, to May 30, 2001, pursuant to the Special Act, it is possible to analyze the overall scale of damage and the damage situation by region, age, gender, time period, and perpetrator.

Naturally, the actual damage situation varies depending on the investigating body and records. In this report, the general damage situation revealed in contemporary records and various reports has been organized into human and material losses.

### 2. Human Casualties

#### 1) Overview of Human Casualties

The figures for victims (dead and missing) and displaced persons from the Jeju 4·3 Incident as mentioned in various domestic and foreign sources including newspapers, government council records, and U.S. embassy documents are as follows:

**[p. 361]**

In 1950 the government established standards of 15,000 *wŏn* for completely destroyed homes, 10,000 *wŏn* for half-destroyed homes, and 5,000 *wŏn* for households whose families had evacuated, distributing a national total of 300 million *wŏn* in housing assistance funds — of which Jeju Island received 27 million *wŏn*.[^85] However, this modest financial support was far from sufficient to resolve the housing problem.

In February 1950, Jeju Island Governor Kim Ch'ung-hŭi, who also served as chairman of the Jeju Island Reconstruction Committee, submitted a funding request to the central government. He stated that the majority of the more than 100,000 displaced persons resulting from the 4·3 Incident were living in dugout shelters (*tomak hyŏlgŏ*), and that the funds required for the reconstruction of damaged housing, various industrial facilities, and public installations, as well as providing start-up capital for displaced persons' livelihoods, totaled approximately 8.9 billion *wŏn* — broken down as approximately 2.71 billion *wŏn* to be borne by Jeju Island, approximately 1.42 billion *wŏn* in central government subsidies, and approximately 4.77 billion *wŏn* in loans.[^86] However, the war broke out before these loan requests could be realized.

During the war years there appears to have been little effort toward village reconstruction. The government had to devote all its resources to prosecuting the war and was unable to provide material support for rebuilding the villages destroyed on Jeju Island. Then the government commenced collecting basic data for reconstruction of housing damaged in the 4·3 Incident beginning October 1, 1952.[^87] And it was not until April 1, 1954, that Halla Mountain was partially reopened and mid-mountain village repopulation and reconstruction were permitted, enabling reconstruction efforts to advance actively.

On December 21, 1953, the Jeju Island Relief Committee distributed reconstruction materials (*kwijŏng chongch'ak charyo*) for 5,000 households among 6,770 households for 90,000 displaced persons — divided between construction and repair purposes[^88] — indicating that government support remained minimal.

After the Halla Mountain movement restriction was lifted on September 21, 1954, the Jeju Island authorities actively advanced the "refugee resettlement project" (*namin chŏngch'ak saŏp*). In a November 20, 1954 directive from the Jeju Island Governor to the county executives (*kunsu*): "To address the shortage of obtainable timber, we intend to arrange national forest felling permits — please identify those wishing to fell national forest timber among those seeking to build or repair housing."[^89]

[^85]: *Tonga Ilbo*, January 12, 1950; *Chosŏn Ilbo*, January 12, 1950.
[^86]: *Cheju-do Hŭnghŭng Chagŭm Yungja Yoch'ŏng ŭi Kŏn* [Request for Jeju Island Reconstruction Fund Loans], Office of the Prime Minister Related Documents, February 10, 1950.
[^87]: *Chejusinbo*, September 28, 1952.
[^88]: *Chejusinbo*, December 23, 1953.
[^89]: Jeju-do, *Namin Chŏngch'ak — 4·3 Sakŏn P'ihaesanghwang Chosa* [Refugee Resettlement — Investigation of 4·3 Incident Damage Status], 1955, pp. 263–264.

**[p. 362]**

In an official communication sent to the central government on March 2, 1955, the Jeju Island authorities made an urgent request for relief rice, building materials, and farm implements for the "resettlement of 2,074 displaced farm households from the 4·3 Incident and 898 households of refugees and original unemployed residents primarily engaged in reclaiming uncultivated land — totaling 2,972 households"; among the building materials, timber, cement, nails,[^90] and other items were requested, revealing that the timber needed for housing reconstruction was to be supplied through national forest felling.

The reconstruction of mid-mountain villages was gradually realized, through the permission for original-site village reconstruction and government support following the September 1954 opening of Halla Mountain, combined with the residents' own efforts.

[^90]: Jeju-do, op. cit., pp. 244–247.

**[p. 363]**

[Table showing recorded human casualty figures by source and date]

The figures in this table show that the number of victims changed significantly across three time periods. First, from February to May 1949, the number of victims was understood to range from 15,000 to 20,000. Second, from June 1949, when the Jeju 4·3 Incident subsided, through April 1950, the number of victims shown in various domestic and foreign sources including newspapers, government council records, and U.S. embassy documents ranged from a minimum of 27,719 to a maximum of approximately 40,000. Finally, following the April 19, 1960 uprising, when demands for fact-finding regarding the incident were widespread in the National Assembly, figures of 40,000 to 65,000 were asserted.

Among the first period investigation materials, the casualty statistics carried in the Associated Press in March 1949 and the report figures of the U.S. Army Command in Korea are judged to be credible. First, the Associated Press statistics, by organizing victim numbers by each town and township, indicates that the results of on-site investigation were involved (see table below).

| Town/Township | Victims |
|---|---|
| Cheju Town | 4,500 |
| Aewol | 5,000 |
| Hallim | 3,000 |
| Choch'ŏn | 2,000 |
| Kujwa | 1,000 |
| Sŏngsan | 500 |
| Pyosŏn | 300 |
| Namwŏn | 600 |
| Sŏgwi | 1,000 |
| Chungmun | 1,500 |
| Andŭk | 400 |
| Taejŏng | 200 |
| **Total** | **20,000** |

The figure of 15,000 victims in the U.S. Army Command in Korea's "Comprehensive Report on Jeju Island Incident" appears to reflect information obtained directly from local police and military intelligence personnel stationed on the island. Moreover, this report states, "It is estimated that 14,000 to 15,000 residents died over the past year, and at least 80 percent of these were killed by suppression forces," thereby indicating that residents—not those killed in combat—were killed by suppression forces at a rate of 80 percent or more.

While these two sources are highly credible, they were prepared as of March 1949, and therefore one must note that the deaths concentrated in the first half of 1949 may not be fully reflected. Accordingly, it is necessary to examine statistics compiled after the situation had substantially subsided, reaching a peak with the May 10, 1949, re-election on Cheju Island and the dissolution of the Cheju Island Area Combat Command on May 15.

The materials from June 1949 through April 1950 deserve attention. The statistical figures of this period are mostly based on official announcements by Cheju Island authorities. The Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo (June 28, 1949), citing an announcement by Cheju Island authorities, reported that the Cheju Island population, which had been 282,942 in 1946, had decreased to 250,400 according to the population census conducted on May 1, 1949, representing a decrease of approximately 30,000 as a result of the Jeju 4·3 Incident. This figure is similar to 29,702, the figure from a June 1, 1949, report in the Seoul Shinmun based on reporting conducted in company with Interior Ministry Vice-Minister Yi Hae-ch'ŏl. Furthermore, it is close to the approximately 30,000 figure disclosed by both Cheju Island Governors Kim Ch'ung-hŭi and Kim Yong-ha in 1950 and the figure of 27,719.

**[p. 364]**

The victim figures announced by Cheju Island authorities represent numbers that include those who died by the latter half of 1949, and thus are judged to approximate the total number of victims. However, it remains unclear whether Cheju Island authorities conducted direct investigations by each town, township, and village, or whether the victim figures were derived by estimation based on population decrease.

In 1953, in its official publication the Cheju Island Overview, Cheju Island authorities recorded the 27,719 figure as the official number of victims of the Jeju 4·3 Incident.

Notably, regarding population statistics, U.S. documentation that surveyed the population as of January 1, 1948, to be 281,000 deserves attention. Additionally, according to the final tabulation of the population census conducted for the first time after government establishment by the Republic of Korea Public Information Office on May 1, 1949, the Cheju Island population was 254,589 (male 114,759, female 139,830). In other words, a decrease of 26,411 occurred over the span of one year and four months.

Of course, the method of inferring the number of victims from population statistics has fundamental limitations. First, it is unclear whether the population figure for January 1, 1948, was derived based on accurate survey methodology. Second, natural population increase must also be considered. On this basis, the population decrease between early 1948 and late 1949 is estimated at 25,000 to 30,000.

It is difficult to conclude that the 25,000 to 30,000 population decrease directly represents victims of the Jeju 4·3 Incident. The reasons are as follows: First, during the turmoil, people fled to Japan and various regions throughout the country to preserve their lives. It is estimated that such flight continued from the March 1 incident in 1947 through 1948, so a population decrease factor resulting from such flight must be considered. Second, following the June 25, 1950, war, approximately 3,000 people died through emergency detention and in deaths occurring while detained in prisons throughout the country,[^1] and these numbers are not included in the May 1949 population statistics. Therefore, it can be said that, to the extent of the excluded number of persons who fled, an additional number of victims from the Jeju 4·3 Incident after 1950 should be added.

Considering these points, there is no great difficulty in regarding the 25,000 to 30,000 population decrease between early 1948 and late 1949 as the total victims of the Jeju 4·3 Incident.

**[p. 365]**

Consequently, the human casualties from the Jeju 4·3 Incident are estimated at 25,000 to 30,000.

2) Human Casualty Situation as Reflected in Reports of Victims

After the conclusion of the Jeju 4·3 Incident, public institutions received reports of victims on three occasions: reports filed with the Special Committee for Investigation of Civilian Massacres of the National Assembly in June 1960; reports received by the 4·3 Special Committee of the Cheju Provincial Assembly from February 7, 1994, to February 29, 2000; and reports of victims received by the Jeju 4·3 Incident Committee from June 8, 2000, to May 30, 2001, pursuant to the Special Act.

Through the content of these reports, it is possible to analyze the overall scale of damage from the Jeju 4·3 Incident and the damage situation by region, age, gender, time period, and perpetrator.

① 1960 National Assembly Report on Civilian Massacres

On June 6, 1960, the Special Committee for Investigation of Civilian Massacres of the National Assembly arrived in Cheju for on-site investigation of civilian massacres related to the Jeju 4·3 Incident. Coordinating with this on-site investigation, the Cheju Sinbo newspaper company, from June 2 to June 10, received reports of "'Jeju 4·3 Incident and June 25 War Period Civilian Massacres: Fact-Finding and Report."' A total of 1,259 reports were filed, accounting for 1,457 victims.

The outline of the report content at that time included: identifying information on those who committed massacres, circumstances of family members, place and date of detention, those executing the detention, date of the final visit, those executing the massacre, location, date and time, circumstances of the massacre, post-massacre disposition, names and addresses of witnesses, requests, and other reference materials.

Analysis of 1,457 victims by gender, place of origin, and age revealed the following: First, with respect to gender, males numbered 1,172 (80.4 percent) and females 285 (19.6 percent). The breakdown by place of origin and age is shown in the following table:

| Aewol Township | 104 | 7.1 |

| Andŭk Township | 216 | 14.8 |
| Taejŏng Township | 8 | 0.5 |
| Sŏgwi Township | 35 | 2.4 |
| Namwŏn Township | 244 | 16.7 |
| Sŏngsan Township | 21 | 1.4 |
| Pyosŏn Township | 223 | 15.3 |
| **Total** | **1,420** | **100** |

(Total shown is 1,420; 37 cases were omitted from the compilation)

② Cheju Provincial Assembly Report

On March 20, 1993, a "4·3 Special Committee" was established in the 4th Cheju Provincial Assembly. On February 7, 1994, the 4·3 Special Committee established a "4·3 Damage Report Office" and began receiving reports of damage. Following the establishment of the damage report office, by February 29, 2000, the damage report office had received reports from a total of 12,243 persons.

These have been organized into tables by region, gender, age, and perpetrator as follows:

③ Jeju 4·3 Incident Committee Reports

The primary intent of the Special Act, as clarified in Article 1 regarding its objectives, is to restore the honor of victims and their bereaved families related to the incident through fact-finding regarding the incident. "Victims" refers to those who died or went missing as a result of the Jeju 4·3 Incident or who bear aftereffects of injury, as determined following deliberation and decision by the Jeju 4·3 Incident Committee.

**[p. 367]**

The committee, from June 8, 2000, established victim reporting centers in domestic and foreign locations and received a total of 13,213 reports by January 4, 2001. However, determining that a considerable number of persons had not yet reported, the committee extended the reporting period for victims and bereaved families by 90 days and relaxed the qualifications for guarantors. Following receipt of second-round additional reports, as of May 30, 2001, the final total of domestic and foreign reporting persons reached 14,028.[^5]

These have been organized into tables by region (village), age, time period (year and month), and perpetrator as follows:

**Reception Summary**

|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Total** | 10,715 | 3,171 | 142 | **14,028** |
| Cheju Island | 10,201 | 3,056 | 134 | 13,391 |
| Domestic (outside Cheju Island) | 462 | 106 | 8 | 576 |
| Japan | 48 | 9 | - | 57 |
| United States | 4 | - | - | 4 |

Additionally, a third round of victim reports will be received from January 2004 for three months.

**② Jeju Provincial Assembly Registry**

On March 20, 1993, a "4·3 Special Committee" was formed within the 4th Jeju Provincial Assembly. On February 7, 1994, the 4·3 Special Committee established a "4·3 Damage Registry Office" and began accepting damage registrations. From the establishment of the registry office through February 29, 2000, the total number of persons who filed damage reports was **12,243**.

These individuals were categorized by region, sex, age group, and perpetrator, and the status tables were compiled as follows:

**③ Jeju 4·3 Incident Committee Registry**

The primary purpose of the 4·3 Special Act, as stated in Article 1 (Purpose), is to restore the honor of victims and their bereaved families related to the incident through a thorough investigation of its truth. "Victims" (*hŭisaengja*) refers to those who died or went missing as a result of the Jeju 4·3 Incident, or who suffer from lasting disabilities, and who have been determined through deliberation and decision by the Jeju 4·3 Incident Committee.

Beginning June 8, 2000, the Committee established victim registration offices domestically and abroad, and accepted 13,213 registrations through January 4, 2001. However, judging that a considerable number of unregistered victims remained, the victim and bereaved family registration period was extended by 90 days and the qualification requirements for guarantors were relaxed. As a result of the second additional registration period, as of May 30, 2001, the total domestic and overseas registrations reached a final count of **14,028**.[^5]

[^5]: As a result of two rounds of 4·3-related victim registration from June 8, 2000 through May 30, 2001,

**[p. 373]**

④ Analysis Results

The notable items in each report are age, period, and perpetrator categories. These are key criteria for understanding the victimization of civilians. First, in the age distribution, those under age 10 reported to the National Assembly, Jeju Provincial Assembly, and 4·3 Committee respectively account for 5.8%, 5.5%, and 5.8%—similar percentages across all three. Similarly, those 61 and older account for 8.4%, 5.8%, and 6.1% respectively. These children under 10 and elderly people over 61 with limited mobility are residents who had absolutely no combat capability. When the 4·3 Committee's victim reports are viewed by period, they show 1948 at 53.1% and 1949 at 34.2%. In particular, analyzing monthly victimization patterns shows that intensive casualties occurred from October 1948 through February 1949, during the harsh suppression operations. Among the 10,761 victims reported from April 1948 through June 1949, 83 percent (8,894 people) were concentrated in those five months. Regarding perpetrators, Jeju Provincial Assembly reports show suppression forces at 84.0% and armed guerrillas at 11.1%, while 4·3 Committee reports show suppression forces at 78.1% and armed guerrillas at 12.6%. When the ratio is calculated using only suppression forces and armed guerrillas, Jeju Provincial Assembly reports show 88.4% to 11.6%, while 4·3 Committee reports show 86.1% to 13.9%—similar proportions. These statistics are consistent with the Headquarters, United States Army Forces in Korea Comprehensive Report on the Jeju Incident, which reported that residents were killed by suppression forces at over 80 percent. This proportion ultimately reveals that of the tens of thousands of residents—between 20,000 and 30,000—the vast majority were victimized by state power. Moreover, the indiscriminate slaughter of the elderly and weak exceeding 10 percent clearly demonstrates the human rights violations of the state power that suppressed the 4·3 Incident.

3) Military, Police, and Right-wing Organization Casualty Situations

① Military Combat Deaths

During the Jeju 4·3 Incident, Jeju had the 9th Regiment established there, along with the 11th Regiment, 2nd Regiment, 1st Independent Battalion, Marine Corps, and Rainbow Unit, which were dispatched from other areas. Among these units, during the latter half of 1948 and the first half of 1949 when armed conflicts were most severe during the 4·3 Incident, the units stationed in Jeju were the 9th Regiment and 2nd Regiment. The 9th Regiment's combat deaths have 12 names remaining in the current National Heroes' Contribution Records, but overall numbers are difficult to ascertain.[^1] However, U.S. military advisors' daily reports of the 9th Regiment at Jeju show that on December 19, 1948, nine members of the 7th Company died in combat near Shin'ŏm-ni, and the Intelligence and Operations Division daily reports of the KMAG show combat deaths on October 6 (1 person near Chongdal-li), November 5 (1 person near Chungmun-ni), and November 18 (2 people near Pukch'on-ni). Additionally, the Tong-A Ilbo and Chosun Ilbo reported combat deaths near Saekdal-li (1 person) and Aral-li (1 person) on October 10. Kang Jong-sŏk, a private in the 9th Regiment, states that "approximately 40 people were sacrificed, and funerals were held in Taejŏn."[^2] Regarding the 2nd Regiment, on June 3, 1949, Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏng-sŏn officially reported 92 combat deaths.[^3] Meanwhile, in the 6th Brigade's Guerrilla Battalion, which was deployed to Jeju Island Combat Command in March 1949, there were also 27 combat deaths.[^4] The 9th Regiment's combat deaths were substantially fewer than the 2nd Regiment and 6th Brigade Guerrilla Battalion. This contrasts with the annual victim distribution shown in the earlier 4·3 Committee report, which indicated 1948 victims at 7,443 (53.1%) and 1949 victims at 4,802 (34.2%), with a greater proportion in 1948. This demonstrates that the 9th Regiment's relocation operations were harsh suppression operations targeting civilians rather than direct armed conflict with armed guerrillas. In contrast, the 2nd Regiment shifted operations into Halla Mountain and engaged directly with armed guerrillas, resulting in numerous combat deaths. Excluding combat deaths of the 9th and 2nd Regiments, the 11th Regiment had 4 deaths[^5] and the Marine Corps 13 deaths[^6] during the 4·3 Incident. Ultimately, while precise statistics on military combat deaths from the Jeju 4·3 Incident cannot be established, they are estimated at approximately 180.[^7]

[^1]: Ministry of Defense, National Heroes' Contribution Records, vols. 1–9 (1996–1998).
[^2]: Kang Jong-sŏk (age 36, Taedeok-gun, South Ch'ungch'ŏng Province, 9th Regiment private at the time, recorded May 20, 1966) testimony (included in Defense Ministry Military Compilation Research Institute Testimony Records of Participants).
[^3]: Kyŏnghyang Newspaper, June 4 and 10, 1949.
[^4]: Army History Daily Record, May 20, 1949 notation records 119 total deaths from five months of combat, including 92 from the 2nd Regiment and 27 from the 6th Brigade Guerrilla Battalion.
[^5]: Incoming Message, July 4, 1948 (document from KMAG Commander).
[^6]: Navy Headquarters, Jeju 4·3 Incident–Related Data Response (Army 12600-111, March 27, 2001).
[^7]: Headquarters, Army, notification to the 4·3 Committee (Military Compilation Research Institute, "Jeju 4·3 Incident–Related Materials Transmission," Personnel 31001-010056, March 23, 2001) reported Army personnel deaths at 83 (11th Regiment 1, 9th Regiment 2, 2nd Regiment 80), but these figures rely on secondary materials and are therefore unreliable.

**[p. 374]**

② Police Combat Deaths

The National Police Agency sent the 4·3 Committee a roster of police officers killed in action and wounded during the Jeju 4·3 Incident, titled "Police Officials Killed in Jeju 4·3 Incident and Wounded Police Officials Register." This list contains 122 police officers killed and 92 police officers wounded.[^8] However, upon direct examination of the memorials at the Jeju City Heroes' Cemetery Police Officer Memorial section, police officer casualties from the 4·3 Incident are identified as 140. Among these, 41 (29%) were casualties from April to November 1948, 17 (12%) from December 1948 to April 1949, and 82 (58%) from May 1949 to September 1956. That fewer police casualties occurred during the period when suppression operations were most forcefully conducted is similar to how the 9th Regiment had fewer casualties than the 2nd Regiment—indicating that suppression forces engaged in unilateral action against residents rather than mutual armed combat with armed guerrillas. Subsequently, after 1950, when police took full responsibility for suppressing remaining armed guerrillas, establishing stationed positions deep in Halla Mountain and engaging in combat resulted in many casualties.[^9]

[^8]: National Police Agency, "Roster of Police Officers Killed in Action and Wounded (Jŏnjin-sago) from Jeju 4·3 Incident, Maintained by the Jeju Regional Police Agency," Materials Transmission (Document No. 63740-141, March 24, 2001).
[^9]: Kim Jong-min, "50 Years After 4·3," Jeju 4·3 Research (Historian's Critique Publishing, 1999), 389.

③ Right-wing Organization Casualty Situation

During the 4·3 Incident, right-wing organizations included the Great East Youth Corps, Sŏbuk Youth Corps, Korean Youth Corps, Village Protection Corps, Civil Defense Units (Minbodan), Youth Self-Defense Corps, Special Operations Unit, and Student League. Members of these organizations participated directly in operations with military and police suppression forces, but they were primarily organized at the village level and mainly conducted village defense, crime prevention, sentry duty, and patrol operations. Among these organizations, the Civil Defense Units (Minbodan) in particular were composed of adult men aged 18 to 55. Many were killed or abducted during armed guerrilla attacks on villages. Meanwhile, despite considerable numbers of Minbodan members being cooperative with military and police, some were executed by firing squad on accusations of negligence during armed guerrilla attacks or collusion with the guerrillas. Right-wing organization members who died from the 4·3 Incident went through proper procedures and were recognized by the state as national heroes and bereaved families. Currently, the 4·3 Committee has obtained a total of 744 national merit recipients related to the 4·3 Incident from the Jeju Veterans Affairs Office.[^10] These are mostly listed as originating from the police and the Patriotic Corps,[^11] with 639 (excluding 105 with police backgrounds) constituting right-wing organization members.

[^10]: The Jeju Veterans Affairs Office sent the Jeju Provincial Assembly a list of 657 national merit recipients related to 4·3 (Jeju Veterans Affairs Office, "National Merit Recipients and Bereaved Family List," December 18, 1996). Additionally, the list sent by the Jeju Veterans Affairs Office to the 4·3 Committee totaled 478 people (Jeju Veterans Affairs Office, "National Merit Recipients and Bereaved Families List for Jeju 4·3 Incident," April 29, 2002). Comparing the two official documents' attached lists, removing duplicates yields a total of 744 national merit recipients related to 4·3.
[^11]: The Patriotic Corps (Aeguksong) refers to right-wing organizations centered on the Civil Defense Units (Minbodan).

## B. Property Damage Overview

Property damage from the Jeju 4·3 Incident can be broadly divided into destruction and loss of village communities, fire damage to public facilities, and damage to industrial sectors. From March 1949 when the 4·3 Incident subsided through 1950, materials mentioned in newspapers, national council records, U.S. embassy documents, and other domestic and foreign sources regarding property damage are shown in the table below. As this table shows, village damage from the 4·3 Incident affected approximately 300 villages (natural settlements and administrative districts), with housing damage recorded as about 20,000 residences and approximately 40,000 structures. These figures align with the official figures released by Jeju Island authorities in 1953 of 19,934 damaged residences (taeho) and 39,285 destroyed structures (dongsŭ).[^12] Investigation results from early 1955 conducted for each township and district show 15,015 households (12,270 destroyed, 2,745 relocated), but considering deficient surveys in Jeju-ŭp, Gujo-myŏn, Choch'ŏn-myŏn, and Taejeong-myŏn, approximately 20,000 households are estimated to have suffered damage.[^13]

Most villages damaged by the 4·3 Incident were restored through continuous "refugee resettlement projects" in the 1950s–60s. However, despite restoration efforts, several villages were abandoned as ruins because original residents did not return. These "lost villages" have been identified and confirmed at 84 locations currently within Jeju Island. During the 4·3 Incident, frequent armed conflicts between armed guerrillas and military/police suppression forces resulted in the burning and destruction of public facilities such as schools, government offices, and police stations. The township offices of Andŭk-myŏn and Gujo-myŏn, Chungmun-myŏn, and Choch'ŏn-myŏn were burned, resulting in loss of household registration records. The 4·3 Incident brought economic stagnation to various industries within Jeju Island. Agriculture, livestock, fishing, women divers' work, and commerce—all sectors of the economy—stagnated, bringing severe hardship to residents' livelihoods. As of May 1949, 28.8 percent of Jeju Island's entire population was in unemployment, resulting in extreme impoverishment of the islanders' living conditions.

[^12]: Jeju Island Government, Overview of Jeju Island Conditions (1953).
[^13]: Jeju Island Government, "Refugee Resettlement – 4·3 Incident Property Damage Survey" (1955).

**[p. 376]**

reported as originating from the Patriotic Corps.[^16] Of these, 744 are classified as members of right-wing organizations, excluding 105 police officers.

## b. Material Damage Overview

Material damage resulting from the Cheju 4·3 Incident can be broadly divided into three categories: destruction and loss of villages and community assets, arson of public facilities, and damage to industrial sectors. From March 1949, when the 4·3 Incident was suppressed, through 1950, accounts of material damage mentioned in newspapers, State Council meeting minutes, U.S. Ambassador documents, and various domestic and foreign sources are shown in the table below. As the table demonstrates, the village damage from the 4·3 Incident affected some 300 villages (natural settlements and administrative units), with approximately 20,000 households and roughly 40,000 dwelling units suffering damage. These figures correspond with the official announcement by Cheju provincial authorities in 1953, which listed 19,934 households in need and 39,285 destroyed dwellings.[^17] A direct investigation conducted by each eup and myŏn in early 1955 revealed 15,015 households affected (12,270 completely destroyed, 2,745 evacuated),[^18] but accounting for incomplete surveys in Cheju eup, Kuja myŏn, Choch'ŏn myŏn, and Taejŏng myŏn, it is estimated that approximately 20,000 households suffered damage.
