The list of national honorees submitted to the 4·3 Incident Committee totaled 478 individuals (Cheju Veterans Affairs Office, "List of National Honorees and Bereaved Families Related to the Cheju 4·3 Incident," April 29, 2002). When the two official documents and their attached lists were cross-referenced, eliminating duplicates resulted in 744 individuals classified as national honorees related to the 4·3 Incident.

[^17]: Cheju Provincial Government, Cheju Provincial Overview, 1953.

[^18]: Cheju Provincial Government, "Refugee Settlement—Investigation of Damage from the 4·3 Incident," 1955.

---

**[p. 377]**

## 2. Human Casualties

### A. Collective Massacre

1) Scorched-Earth Period Killings

In mid-November 1948, harsh suppression operations commenced. An operation was launched with proclamations imposing movement restrictions on residents of midmountain villages, declaring that violators would be executed regardless of circumstances. From mid-November 1948 through February 1949—approximately four months—suppression forces set fires in midmountain villages and massacred residents en masse. This was the most tragic situation that unfolded during the 4·3 Incident. During this period, the greatest number of Jeju residents were killed, and most midmountain villages were literally "scorched"—burned to the ground. Before setting fires in the midmountain villages, suppression forces issued evacuation orders to residents to move down to coastal villages. However, in some villages, evacuation orders were not communicated, or before they could be fully conveyed, suppression forces arrived and, setting fires and opening fire, caused indiscriminate collective casualties among all—men, women, young, and old. At that time, many residents who lost their homes and barely escaped with their lives were too frightened to move down to coastal villages. Instead, they took shelter in midmountain areas and were subsequently arrested by suppression forces and killed. During this period, collective massacres carried out by Sŏbuk Youth Corps members also occurred in some coastal villages. The actual situation of civilian collective casualties caused by military and police is revealed through approximately 14,000 victim reports and testimonies from approximately 500 people recorded by this Committee. These facts are also verified through U.S. military materials. U.S. secret reports state that "the 9th Regiment adopted a mass slaughter plan targeting village residents" or "the 2nd Regiment conducted harsh operations executing everyone found in bandit areas regardless of status or weapons possession." However, operational logs of the 9th Regiment or 2nd Regiment were not discovered. Despite searches of materials from the Ministry of Defense, Army Headquarters, and the Central Documents Management Agency, they could not be located. Therefore, this account will rely primarily on victim testimonies.

□ Case: Kyoryang-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn

In the early morning of November 13, 1948 (lunar October 13), at approximately 5 a.m., military personnel surrounded Kyoryang-ri, a midmountain village in Choch'ŏn-myŏn, and, entering house after house, immediately set fires and began shooting. Residents who awoke from sleep and rushed outside were shot down on the spot. When daylight came and the gunfire ceased, Kyoryang-ri, where approximately 100 households had lived, had already turned to ashes. The confirmed victims that day were as follows: Yang Jae-wŏn (60), Yang Jae-wŏn's wife (60), Kim Man-gap (57), Yang Kwan-sŏk (female, 50), Poo Ja-saeng (44), Poo Young-sook (female, 38), Poo Young-sook's son (3), Ko Kyŏ-saeng (female, 18), Ko Ok-shim (female, 14), Kim Sun-jae (female, 14), and Kim Moon-yong (9) died. Notably, Kim In-saeng lost at once a family and relatives—his grandmother Kang (70), mother Kim Chae-hwa (45), younger sister Kim Young-ja (15), younger brother Kim Sun-saeng (10), unnamed younger brother (5), sister-in-law Yang Nam-sŏn (25), female cousin (8), male cousin (5), male cousin (3), fifth-degree cousin Kim Sung-jin (65), fifth-degree cousin Kim Sung-ji (63), Kim Sung-ji's wife (60), cousin's sister-in-law Shin Bo-bae (25), and form's uncle's "baby" Shin [name unknown] (15)—fourteen family and relatives—in one sweep (Among the victims, Kim Man-gap and Yang Kwan-sŏk did not die immediately but endured several more days without receiving proper medical treatment; Kim Man-gap died on November 17, and Yang Kwan-sŏk died on November 30). All victims were elderly or infirm. Most bodies were shot and burned in fires, and one fourteen-year-old girl was found pierced by a bayonet. Yang Bok-ch'ŏn (梁福天), who lost his nine-year-old son Kim Moon-yong that day, testified about the situation as follows:

That day my husband and nephews had fled beforehand, and I was home with my nine-year-old son and three-year-old daughter. At about dawn, there was loud gunfire. But I never imagined they would kill people. I rushed into the house, desperately begging the soldiers setting fire, "Please save us, please save us," and pleaded with my hands. But the soldiers pushed me aside and shot. As I collapsed carrying my three-year-old daughter on my back, my nine-year-old son ran to me crying "Mother!" Then the soldiers shot my son once more. I still hear those soldiers' voices in my ears—"This bastard isn't dead yet!"—as they shot. My son's chest was hit directly and his heart came out. They were not human beings. After they left, I first dragged my son to the yard fearing he would burn, then looked at my daughter, whom I unwrapped from the blanket. Until then, I did not think my daughter had also been shot. But when I lowered the baby from my back, the blanket was shredded and my daughter's legs had two holes blown through them. The bullet that went through my side pierced through the blanket and shattered my daughter's legs. After that, I hid in the forest for about ten days, and then I heard an order to come down to coastal villages, so I went down to Choch'ŏn-ri. I cannot use my lower back to this day, and my daughter is still a disabled person who cannot walk properly. Before this, there was no notice to flee or go down to the coast. Soldiers suddenly rushed in during the night, and in that confusion many people died—not knowing or understanding what was happening.

Meanwhile, one child, age six, miraculously survived despite being shot in three places. The child who was shot yet escaped death was Kim Yong-gil (given name Kim Ch'ang-shik). His great-grandmother, also shot while dying, hastily wrapped her grandson in a blanket and threw him into a reed field, enabling him to avoid burning. Later, Kim Yong-gil became known to the world through a fire incident that revealed his final appearance. One news outlet reported on the sorrowful life of Kim, who spent fifty years as a disabled person due to suppression force bullets and could not utter a word of complaint:

"Marked by the lingering trauma of 4·3, Kim Yong-gil (age 51), who had spent his entire life supporting himself with disabilities caused by suppression force bullets, met his end on the morning of the 8th in a humble tile-roofed cottage in the nameless orchard in Ara-dong, Jeju City, consumed by flames in a manner as tragic as the knots in his life."[^14]

According to the news report, from the bullet wounds at that time, Kim Yong-gil spent his entire life unable to properly bend his right arm even once. His left leg had its knee joint shattered into pieces, forcing him to rely on crutches his entire life. He married at thirty and had a daughter, experiencing happiness briefly. But when his young daughter turned two, his wife became pregnant with a second child and died. Kim Yong-gil, working hard to raise his one daughter alone, sold his house and fields and did not refuse any work. When his daughter graduated from high school and found employment, he entrusted her to her aunt's home and lived alone in a humble tile-roofed cottage in the unfamiliar Ara-dong. Then a fire broke out in that cottage, and he met his end engulfed in flames, a life marked only by sorrow.

□ Case: "Agricultural School Detention Center," Jeju-ŭp

In the autumn of 1948, at the Jeju Agricultural School, where the 9th Regiment Headquarters was stationed, there circulated the saying that "if you were not confined in the Agricultural School detention center, you were not a notable person." Key figures representing the judiciary, administration, education, and press of Jeju Island were imprisoned there. The detention center, hastily set up with tents on the school grounds, was like a waiting area for death. Identified detainees at the Agricultural School detention center included the following:

▲ Judiciary: Choi Wŏn-sun (Chief Judge, Jeju District Court), Kim Bang-soon (Prosecutor, Jeju District Prosecutor's Office), Song Du-hyŏn (secretary of Jeju District Court, independence award recipient), Kim Jin-young (Court official), Yang Sung-du (Court official), Hong In-pyo (Court official)

▲ Education: Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho (Principal, first Jeju Middle School; co-chairman of Korean Democratic Party), Lee Kwan-sŏk (Chief of Jeju Province School Affairs Division; Jeju Middle School Principal), Kim Wŏn-joong (Principal of Jeju South Elementary School, Jeju Province Education Division), Chae Sae-byŏng (Director of School Affairs, Jeju Province Education Division), Ko Chil-jong (Agricultural School teacher), Mun Jae-soon (teacher), Hyŏn Tu-hwang (Jeju Middle School teacher; son of Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho)

▲ Press: Park Kyŏng-hun (President, Jeju Daily News; former governor; co-chairman of Korean Democratic Party), Shin Du-bang (Executive Director, Jeju Daily News), Kim Ho-jin (Editor-in-Chief, Jeju Daily News), Hyŏn In-ha (Reporter, Kyŏnghyang Newspaper; Jeju Branch Chief), Lee Sang-hee (Jeju Branch Chief, Seoul Daily News)

▲ Jeju Province Officials: Ko Sun-hyŏp (Assessment Division), Kim Wang-jin (Agriculture Division), Kim Hyŏng-joong (Agriculture Division), Yang Sŭng-hun (Social Division), Im Jae-chan (Forestry Division), Choi Sŏng-don (Health Division), Chŏng Hee-jo

▲ Property Management Bureau: Han Pyŏng-tack (Deputy Director; former Agricultural School teacher), Kang Soon-hyŏn (Chief of Sale Division; former Jeju First Middle School Principal), Kang Chang-u (former Agricultural School teacher), Kim In-ji, Kim Tae-ho (General Affairs Director), Kim Hak-nim (former Agricultural School teacher), Yang Mun-soo (Jeju Middle School teacher), Choi Kil-du (General Affairs Division)

▲ New Korea Company: Kang Jae-ryang (Company Director, Liquor Manufacturing Plant), Yang Mun-pil (Jeju Farm Director, New Korea Company), Kim Ki-yoo

▲ Other: O Chang-hŭn (doctor), Pae Tu-bong (anti-Japanese activist), Ko Bong-hyo (mushroom cultivator/manager; participant in National Liberation Assembly; brother of Ko Jin-hee), Ko Ye-gu (Transportation company branch manager; chairman of Jeju-ŭp Joint Struggle Committee, March 10, 1947 strike), Kim Du-hun (Finance Director, Korean Democratic Party), Kim Soo-rim (son of Kim Cha-bong, Jeju-ŭp mayor), Mun Bong-tack (founder member, Korean Nationalist Youth Corps), Yun Chang-sŏk (Choch'ŏn township chief), Cho Tae-soo (independence award recipient), Ch'oe Nam-shik (former deputy vice-chairman, Committee for the Preparation of Korean Independence), Chŏng Sang-jo (former Director of Investigation Division, Korean Democratic Party)

That the Chief Judge of the Jeju District Court and the Prosecutor of the Jeju District Prosecutor's Office were dragged away and tortured demonstrates clearly the ordeal ordinary civilians endured. Some Agricultural School detention center detainees miraculously survived, but many were summarily executed. Soldiers would enter the tent detention center, call out names of people to be executed, and shout "Release!" Frequent "mass releases" occurred, and each time detainees were filled with dread. As the 9th Regiment was set to leave Jeju at the end of December 1948, replaced by the 2nd Regiment in Taejŏn, it concentrated on executing detainees beforehand. One such execution occurred on December 23, 1948, at a rice mill near a place called "Gosan Dongsang." The victims that day included six people from among those previously mentioned detainees: Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho, Kim Wŏn-joong, Pae Tu-bong, Lee Sang-hee, Hyŏn Tu-hwang, and one person whose name was not identified. After executing them, soldiers set fire to the bodies and burned them. Hyŏn Chŏng-ja, Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho's niece, recounted: "A civilian driver who transported my uncle's family as far as Park-sŏng village finally, after much agonizing, secretly told us. Thanks to him, we were able to find the bodies. However, since they had been burned, we confirmed my uncle (Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho) by remains of a charred overcoat, and my cousin (Hyŏn Tu-hwang) by a medicine bottle in his pocket."[^15]

**[p. 378]**

Key figures in the killing of Jeju-ŭp residents included, in addition to 9th Regiment Commander Song Yo-ch'an, Captain Tak Sŏng-nok (卓聖錄), 9th Regiment Intelligence and Operations Officer; Ch'oe Nan-su (崔蘭洙), Police Captain, Chief of Special Investigation Unit under the Jeju Emergency Military Command; and Kim Jae-nŭng (金在能), Commander of the Jeju Sŏbuk Youth Corps—these figures controlled the power of life and death over Jeju residents. A 9th Regiment soldier at the time, Yun Tae-jun, testified as follows:

The regimental intelligence officer was Tak Sŏng-nok, and one word from him meant death. If you were arrested by military police, you might live, but if arrested by Captain Tak, whether civilian or soldier, everyone died. Those Sŏbuk Youth Corps thugs are terrible people. They raped young women, stole chickens to eat, branded people as communists. These bastards made things worse. If we were ordered to suppress, we would just suppress... But residents with nowhere to escape went further up the mountains.

Tak Sŏng-nok's notorious reputation generated numerous testimonies, most describing how "those who displeased him were branded communists and killed" or "he committed multiple sexual assaults against women." Ch'oe Kil-du testified: "Tak Sŏng-nok cannot be called human. He kept changing beautiful women, many times over, and when leaving Jeju, he murdered the woman with whom he had been living at Sarabong. He held power over life and death."[^16] Astonishingly, Tak Sŏng-nok was a drug addict. Ha Tu-yong testified as follows:

When Tak Sŏng-nok arrived in Jeju, he immediately rushed to the hospital demanding an opium injection. However, since drugs could not be handled casually, I called the pharmaceutical director and obtained authorization before administering the injection. He was such a severe opium addict that needles could not penetrate his arms. When nurse An Jŏng-sook tried to inject into his forearm but the needle could not enter, he ordered her to inject under his armpit. Throughout his tenure, he visited the hospital regularly for injections.[^17]

Chang Shih-young also testified: "When I was operating a hospital in Busan with Dr. O Chang-hŭn, Tak Sŏng-nok kept coming to seek opium injections from him."[^18] At that time, 9th Regiment officer Kim Jŏng-mu testified about his experience with Tak Sŏng-nok as follows:

The POW detention area was set up with about twenty tents in the back yard of the Agricultural School. One day, I heard the regiment commander was making an inspection, so I accompanied him as a staff officer. But one detainee's face was noticeably pale, which would have been unnoticed had he been on the mountain. Curious, I asked. He was a doctor named O Chang-hŭn, who said Tak Sŏng-nok, the regimental intelligence officer, had demanded an opium injection, and when he refused, he was arrested. I too had come south fleeing communists from the north and joined the military, but I thought this man could not be tolerated. I went to find Tak with my pistol and said, "Why are you making non-communists into communists? If you keep this up, I will kill you." Only then did he release him. Dr. O, feeling threatened even after his release, followed me around and asked me to arrange for him to become a military doctor. Together we went to Army Headquarters in Seoul and asked a medical officer, but it didn't work out well. Afterward, Dr. O was too frightened to return to Jeju and went to Busan to open the "O Pediatric Clinic." Tak Sŏng-nok was nearly forty years old and unqualified to be an intelligence officer. He was not a graduate of the Military English Academy or a trumpet player from the military band—I don't know how he got a special appointment—but he was promoted to captain before me. Under various pretexts, he committed many sexual assaults against women.[^19]

As the 9th Regiment's intelligence officer, Tak Sŏng-nok was at the core of the core of Jeju's suppression forces at that time. The fact that such a critically important person was a drug addict who recklessly killed and committed sexual assaults symbolically demonstrated the recklessness of the suppression operations then underway. Captain Ch'oe Nan-su (崔蘭洙), Chief of the Special Investigation Unit under the Jeju Emergency Military Command, was also deeply connected to the 4·3 Incident. He first came to Jeju after the May 10, 1948 elections, leading the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency's Criminal Investigation Division.[^20] Ch'oe Nan-su also visited Jeju for inspection in mid-June 1948 and returned.[^21] Ch'oe Nan-su's third visit to Jeju was in early November 1948, just as indiscriminate harsh suppression operations were beginning. At this time, Ch'oe Nan-su appeared as Chief of the Special Investigation Unit under the Jeju Emergency Military Command and as the core of the police. Ch'oe Nan-su replaced all special investigation unit members except one Jeju native with people from other regions.[^22] Kim Ho-kyŏm, who served as a police officer in Jeju at that time, testified as follows:

Jeju-native police officers at the time were not trusted. This was because Jeju society is small, and when we caught mountain bandits, there were often cases where police officers were distantly related. Anyway, because Seoul concluded it couldn't trust Jeju natives, a special investigation unit came down, and Ch'oe Nan-su was the chief. Captain Ch'oe was a former high-ranking detective during Japanese rule, and old habits remained—he tortured as routine, often clashing with me. One night while I was on night duty at the Jeju Police Station, I heard a woman's screams so loud I couldn't sleep. I went to the interrogation room to find soldiers stripping a woman naked, hanging her upside down, and torturing her. I went with a Japanese sword and got angry, and the investigators fled. The next day I questioned Police Chief Hong Shun-bong: "Ch'oe Nan-su is going too far. If this continues, Jeju people will increasingly turn away from mainlanders, making suppression difficult." But Ch'oe was obstinate. People tortured that way could only confess to things they never did. The special investigation unit also made their own leaflets, secretly scattered them in specific villages, and then arrested and tortured villagers. Through this, money and other things came out. It was what you might call the "government-made communists" of the Liberal Party era.[^23]

Ch'oe Nan-su was famous not only in Jeju but also at the national level for the "assassination plot against National Assembly members" incident. As anti-Japanese figures faced crisis following enactment of the Banning Reactionaries Act, pro-Japanese police led by Ch'oe Nan-su plotted to assassinate those involved in the Special Committee on Investigating Collaboration with the Enemy. This assassination plot occurred before Ch'oe Nan-su's third dispatch to Jeju. In late October 1948, Ch'oe Nan-su, along with No Dŏk-sŭl (盧德述), another notorious pro-Japanese police officer, hired professional terrorist Paek Min-tae (白民泰) to assassinate National Assembly members who spearheaded enactment of the Banning Reactionaries Act. Ch'oe Nan-su's plot involved kidnapping a National Assembly member of the Special Committee on Investigating Collaboration with the Enemy, forcing him to submit a resignation letter, dragging him to near the 38th parallel, murdering him, then reporting he had been "discovered betraying the nation and attempting to flee north, thus summarily executed." The list of assassination targets Paek Min-tae received from Ch'oe Nan-su included fifteen people, including Chief Justice Kim Byŏng-ro (金炳魯) and Shin Ik-hee (申翼熙). While plotting this, Ch'oe Nan-su was dispatched to Jeju to take part in harsh suppression operations. After returning to Seoul, in January 1949, Ch'oe Nan-su still provided funds and weapons to Paek Min-tae, urging plan execution. However, as pro-Japanese police began being arrested by the Special Committee on Investigating Collaboration with the Enemy, Paek Min-tae had a change of heart and turned himself in, thwarting the plot. Ultimately, Ch'oe Nan-su was sentenced in June 1949 to two years imprisonment for conspiracy to commit murder.[^24]

Kim Jae-nŭng (金在能), Commander of the Jeju Sŏbuk Youth Corps, likewise wielded unchecked power. He regularly extorted goods and tortured, committed murders, and assaulted women. Kim Jae-nŭng, in particular, murdered Kim Tu-hyŏn (金斗鉉), Director of Jeju Province's General Affairs Bureau, by beating him to death in the Sŏbuk Youth Corps office when the director refused his demands for goods. A U.S. military report on this states: "On November 9, a Sŏbuk Youth Corps member beat to death Kim Tu-hyŏn, Jeju Province General Affairs Director. The Sŏbuk Youth Corps claimed they merely intended to interrogate him, not kill him."[^25] The second-ranking official in Jeju Province administration was beaten to death, and it was only necessary to brand him a communist. Kim Jae-nŭng even forcibly took over the Jeju Daily News, the only press organ in the region at that time. Kim Yong-soo, editor-in-chief of the Jeju Daily News at that time, testified as follows:

There was no particular reason the Jeju Daily News incurred the Sŏbuk Youth Corps' displeasure. The Sŏbuk Youth Corps seized the newspaper to expand its own authority. We simply objected during the "Bongae-ri Operation"—an early 1949 operation led by the 2nd Regiment's 2nd Battalion (Battalion Commander Captain Sŏk Bong), with police, Sŏbuk Youth Corps, and others fully mobilized. Many innocent people died, but the newspaper could not report such facts and had to treat it as "legitimate military operations" as the military announced. However, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps complained that their role in this operation was not covered prominently enough. After that incident, one day Sŏbuk Youth Corps members came to the newspaper office and beat President Kim Sŏk-ho. That night, I was dragged away by Sŏbuk Youth Corps members who burst into my home at 2 a.m. Commander Kim Jae-nŭng was waiting. Kim Jae-nŭng, in his mid-thirties at the time, was tall—six ch'ŏk—with a mustache, wearing long leather boots like the Japanese used to wear. With such an infamous reputation, just seeing his tall frame swagger created chills down the spine. He beat me without a word—not ordinary beating but merciless violence to the point of near-death. When I was nearly a corpse, Kim Jae-nŭng said "Take him away and dispose of him!" The Sŏbuk Youth Corps dragged me away toward the execution grounds. But 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Pyŏng-sŏn (咸炳善), hearing that my family was searching everywhere for me, had me released. The 2nd Regiment dressed me in military uniform and had me conduct propaganda work instead of being editor-in-chief.[^26]

**[p. 379]**

Thus, through terror and intimidation, Kim Jae-nŭng forcibly took over the Jeju Daily News, installed Kim Muk (金黙), a member of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps special company, as editor-in-chief, and personally assumed the position of president, wielding unchecked power even more fully.

□ Case: Ha-ga-ri, Aewŏl-myŏn

Aewŏl-myŏn Ha-ga-ri was a small, ordinary village of approximately 160 households. Though not a coastal village, it was not far from the island-circumferencing road along the coast, making its classification as a "midmountain village" ambiguous. Therefore, Ha-ga-ri did not receive an evacuation order. After the April 3 armed uprising, no incidents occurred in the area, and residents lived without particular worry. However, at approximately 1 a.m. on November 13, 1948, military personnel from the 9th Regiment stationed in Oe-do-ri, outside Jeju-ŭp, entered the village. At that time, in one household, ancestral rites had just been concluded and family members were eating the sacrificial meat, and in Jung-dŏng-nae, several people had gathered at Chŏng Ki-bong's house for a pork and soju feast. The suppression forces first attacked Chŏng Ki-bong's house, which was brightly lit with people gathered inside. Then they rushed into neighboring houses and dragged out sleeping residents by force. Immediately, they set fires to fourteen houses including Chŏng Ki-bong's and dragged the people to a nearby field called "Yuksiwŏn." Suddenly, a horrific scene of gunfire and screams unfolded. That day's victims were: Kim Tu-ch'ŏn (71), Chŏng Ki-bong (67), Lee Jung-hwa (female, 59), Yang Kong-myŏng (48), Jang Soon-ho (45), Im Gun-sŏn (42), Im Young-ŏn (42), Yun Chang-kuk (41), Jang Ŏn-soon (39), Chŏng Soon-ah (39), Im Chi-wan (38), Ko Tae-shik (37), Song Yoo-saeng (33), Im In-wŏn (31), Jang Ki-hyu (30), Park Ch'ŏng-ryang (female, 30s), Ko Wŏn-ryong (29), Park Koon-hwa (female, 27), Chŏng Shin-ah (27), Kang Ki-hwan (26), Yang Kong-ŏn (26), Im Hwa-bong (26), Ko Tu-ch'ŏl (24), Ko Soon-hwa (female, 18), and Chŏng Do-ah (18). Among the victims, Ko Soon-hwa was a pregnant woman in her final month. Ko Wŏn-ryong survived with life remaining and was transported to a hospital in Aewŏl-ri but died two days later. O Ch'ang-gi (in his twenties at the time), who was shot that day but miraculously survived, demonstrates that the line between "life and death," between "the executed and the soldier" is paper-thin. O Ch'ang-gi, with bullets embedded in his body, endured the chaos. When the Korean War broke out, he volunteered and enlisted as a "3rd class Marine," participating in the Inch'ŏn landing operation. Kang Ŭng-mu (姜應武), who lost her husband that day, said: "Three soldiers entered the house and dragged my sleeping husband out. The soldiers gathered people together and shot them with a machine gun. Bullets hit my husband in multiple places, and when he didn't die quickly enough, they slit his throat with a knife, and blood splattered. I don't see them as soldiers but as human butchers."[^27]

Why did soldiers come to Ha-ga-ri that day, and why did they indiscriminately massacre residents? Regarding this, there was circulating talk that right-wing organization members in coastal villages and the son of a Ha-ga-ri resident who had been mistreated had conspired against the village. Alternatively, there were accounts that suppression forces, hearing intelligence that armed guerrillas were concentrated in Won-dong, a midmountain village, rushed out, and when they reached Ha-ga-ri, a midpoint, and saw residents gathered, they mistakenly thought there was "a bandit conspiracy" and carried out the massacre. Considering that soldiers proceeded directly to Won-dong village after the massacre in Ha-ga-ri and also executed residents there, the latter presumption that "it was a mistake" carries more persuasiveness. However, since the victims were ordinary residents including elderly and women and children, no justification could exist for the massacre. A more fundamental reason can be found in the dates of incidents. On November 13, 1948, when Ha-ga-ri incident occurred, similar indiscriminate massacres and arson also occurred in multiple places: Won-dong village, Sogil-ri, Aewŏl-myŏn; Kyoryang-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn; Wahŭl-ri 2-gu, Choch'ŏn-myŏn; Shinhing-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn; Sangch'ŏn-ri, Andŭk-myŏn; Sangch'ang-ri, Andŭk-myŏn; and Ch'angch'ŏn-ri, Andŭk-myŏn. Such massacres and arson incidents occurred widely in other areas of Jeju Island.

□ Case: Won-dong Village, Sogil-ri, Aewŏl-myŏn

**[p. 380]**

Won-dong (院洞) village in Sogil-ri, Aewŏl-myŏn disappeared from the map after November 13, 1948, when indiscriminate harsh suppression operations just began (Won-dong, with approximately fifteen households, is administratively situated with the stream running through the village as boundary—the eastern section corresponds to Sogil-ri and the western to Sangga-ri. However, since only about five households lived near Sangga-ri, it was treated as Sogil-ri). Now only a stone tablet inscribed "Wŏnji" (院址, "former site") at the village entrance testifies that a village once existed here. Won-dong village, formed during the Chosŏn dynasty, was originally a tavern village where travelers passing through the midmountain region would rest. At that time, only one household operated the tavern, with other residents engaged in farming. Because it was such a small village, there were few young people to engage in armed guerrilla activity, so police who came to the village only warned residents: "Don't cooperate with the bandits," then left without beating or executing residents. The armed guerrillas also came down occasionally to gather residents for speeches but did not demand provisions from a village with such low output. Therefore, residents felt no great danger and remained in their homes until soldiers arrived and gathered them. An evacuation order had not yet been issued. The reason soldiers came to Won-dong appears to have been intelligence of armed guerrillas concentrated in the area. These were the same soldiers who had just carried out a reckless massacre in Ha-ga-ri before arriving at Won-dong. When soldiers searched the village, there were no armed guerrillas. Nevertheless, soldiers executed innocent residents indiscriminately, regardless of age or sex. That day's victims included, reportedly, even a person who had momentarily stopped at the village tavern while passing through the midmountain region, though only Won-dong residents actually confirmed are: Yi Tu-ik (64), Kim Ki-yong (60), Kim Sŭng-hong (58), Hyŏn Tu-byŏng (50), Kim Kil-hong (49), Yang Yi-ryong (48), Yi Moo-saeng (Yang Yi-ryong's wife), Ko Byŏng-kyu (43), Yang Chŏng-saeng (female, 41), Kang Ki-song (40s), Kim Yoo-hong (40), Hŏ Hong (female, 40s), Yi Tal-ho (female, 36), Kang Ch'ang-soo (31), Kang Ch'ang-kwon (29), Kang Ch'ang-ŭk (28), Kang Ch'ang-ŭk's wife (25), Kim Kwi-hwan (28), Yang Un-yong (28), Kim Sŏng-man (female, 24), Ko Im-saeng (female, 22), Hong Sŏng-kyu (22), Ko Tae-wŏn (21), Kim Kwi-hyu (21), Yang Ch'un-hee (female, 21), Kang Kong-bu (20), Hyŏn Ch'ang-ha (20), Jang Pong-ho (female, 19), Im Se-ok, Ko Nam-ok (female, 16), Yang Ch'un-saeng (female, 16), Hyŏn Pong-wan (14), Ko Nam-ju (7), and Kang Ch'ang-ŭk's son (4)—a total of 34 people. Ko Nam-bo lost his father (Ko Byŏng-kyu) and two younger siblings (Ko Nam-ok and Ko Nam-ju) in that tragic day at age seventeen. Ko testified about that horrific day as follows:

Soldiers carrying flashlights gathered residents, so it must have been around 5 a.m. when it was still dark. Soldiers tied residents' hands behind their backs and, like bundling dried anchovies, connected people together with rope. As we made a circuit around the village, all residents became bound. Two days earlier, police and coastal village youths from the Great East Youth Corps had come to the village but nothing happened, so at first we were not greatly worried. Soldiers asked, "Point out where the bandits are," but how could we know? Bound, we were dragged around the village searching for bandits. From dawn, hungry all day, we only returned around 5 p.m. to the front of the tavern house. It seemed the soldiers initially did not intend to kill us. So they even untied our bonds at one point. Also, they passed time with nonsensical talk like "If you line people up and shoot, nine will die," or "Adults die silently, but children cry two or three times before dying." Then we were bound again, but this time I was bound with my hands in front. Then one soldier made a radio call somewhere and said, "You will be executed within ten minutes." It must have been orders from the regiment headquarters. Soon a military truck came up from Aewŏl-ri. I hurried to untie the bonds and, while the soldiers were saluting each other, I ran into the forest. Shortly after, I heard loud gunfire. The soldiers covered the bodies with provisions and blankets, set fire to them, and left.[^28]

This is how Won-dong disappeared. So how did the soldiers who carried out the massacre process and record this incident? The following U.S. military report provides a clue to the Won-dong incident:

"Around November 13, through suppression force operations, 115 bandits were executed at Haeng-wŏn-ri, 37 in the coordinate area 937-1133, and 4 near Odŭng-ri. Among executed bandits, one was identified as someone who had escaped from the suppression force. (Counter-intelligence report)"[^29]

In the above report, I confirmed the location recorded as coordinates only (937-1133), as the place name was unknown, through U.S. military maps and found it pointed exactly to Won-dong village. The indiscriminate execution that day—including elderly people in their sixties down to a child of four—was recorded as a military "achievement" in suppressing "bandits."

[^20]: Kim Jŏng-mu (age 77, Puam-dong, Jongno-gu, Seoul, 9th Regiment Supply Officer at the time, recorded September 25, 2002) testimony.

**[p. 381]**

The following individuals were among those imprisoned at the Agricultural School:

**Judiciary**
- Ch'oe Wŏn-sun (崔元淳, President of Cheju District Court)
- Kim Pang-sun (金邦順, Prosecutor of Cheju District Prosecutors' Office)
- Song Tu-hyŏn (宋斗鉉, Korean Independence Movement honoree, Secretary of Cheju District Court)
- Kim Chin-yŏng (Court employee)
- Yang Sŏng-tu (Court employee)
- Hong In-p'yo (Court employee)

**Education Sector**
- Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho (玄景昊, First Principal of Cheju Middle School, Co-Chair of the Democratic Front)
- Lee Kwan-sŏk (李琯石, Head of Cheju Provincial Education Bureau; Principal of Cheju Middle School)
- Kim Wŏn-jung (金元中, Principal of Cheju South Elementary School)
- Ch'ae Se-byŏng (蔡世秉, Scholar-Official of Cheju Provincial Education Bureau)
- Ko Ch'il-jong (高七鍾, Agricultural School Teacher)
- Mun Chae-sun (文在純, Teacher)
- Hyŏn Tu-hwang (玄斗璜, Cheju Middle School Teacher; son of Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho)

**Press**
- Pak Kyŏng-hun (朴景勳, President of Cheju Shinbo; Former Provincial Governor; Co-Chair of the Democratic Front)
- Shin Tu-pang (申斗玤, Executive Director of Cheju Shinbo)
- Kim Ho-jin (金昊辰, Editor-in-Chief of Cheju Shinbo)
- Hyŏn In-ha (玄仁廈, Reporter for Kyŏnghyang Newspaper; Head of Cheju Bureau)
- Lee Sang-hee (李尙熹, Head of Seoul Newspaper's Cheju Bureau)

**Cheju Provincial Government Officials**
- Ko Sun-hŏp (高順協, Audit Division)
- Kim Wang-jin (金王辰, Agriculture Bureau)
- Kim Hyŏng-jung (金亨中, Agriculture Bureau)
- Yang Sŭng-hun (梁升勳, Social Affairs Division)
- Im Chae-ch'an (任才贊, Forestry Division)
- Ch'oe Sŏng-don (崔成敦, Health Division)
- Chŏng Hee-jo (鄭希朝)

**Property Management Bureau**
- Han Byŏng-t'aek (韓秉澤, Deputy Director; Former Agricultural School Teacher)
- Kang Sun-hyŏn (姜淳現, Director of Sale Division; Former Principal of Cheju Number One Middle School)
- Kang Ch'ang-u (姜昌祐, Former Agricultural School Teacher)
- Kim In-ji (金仁志)
- Kim T'ae-ho (General Affairs Director)
- Kim Hak-nim (金學琳, Former Agricultural School Teacher)
- Yang Mun-su (梁文秀, Cheju Middle School Teacher)
- Ch'oe Kil-tu (崔吉斗, General Affairs Division)

**Sinhan Corporation**
- Kang Chae-ryang (康才良, Director of Sinhan Corporation Cheju Plant)
- Yang Mun-p'il (梁文弼, Director of Sinhan Corporation Cheju Farm)
- Kim Ki-yu (金基有)

**Others**
- O Ch'ang-hun (吳昶昕, Physician)
- Pae Tu-bong (裵斗鳳, Independence Movement Activist)
- Ko Pong-hyo (高奉孝, Mushroom Cultivation Operator; Participant in Haechŭ Convention; Brother of Ko Chin-hee)
- Ko Ye-ku (高禮龜, Transportation Company Branch Manager; Member of the Joint Struggle Committee of Cheju Eup during the March 10, 1947 Strike)
- Kim Tu-hun (金斗壎, Democratic Front Financial Director)
- Kim Su-rim (金守林, Son of Kim Ch'a-bong, Cheju Eup Mayor)
- Mun Pong-t'aek (文奉澤, Founding Member of the Korean Youth Corps)
- Yun Ch'ang-sŏk (尹昌錫, Choch'ŏn Myŏn Mayor)
- Cho Tae-su (趙大秀, Independence Movement Honoree)
- Ch'oe Nam-sik (崔南植, Former Deputy Chair of the Korean Preparatory Government)
- Chŏng Sang-jo (鄭相朝, Former Head of Democratic Front Investigative Bureau)

The fact that the President of Cheju District Court and the Prosecutor of the Cheju District Prosecutors' Office were dragged away and tortured suggests that the ordeal experienced by ordinary citizens was surely beyond imagination.

---

**[p. 382]**

Some detainees at the Agricultural School survived by sheer luck, but many were summarily executed. Soldiers would enter the tent compound, call out the names of those to be executed, and shout "Release!" Sudden "mass releases" occurred frequently, and each time, the detainees lived in constant fear.[^3]

When the 9th Regiment was replaced by the 2nd Regiment from Taejŏn at the end of December 1948 and departed from Cheju, they executed detainees intensively beforehand. One such execution occurred on December 23, 1948, near what was colloquially called "Kosan Tongsan," at a mill. The victims that day included six individuals from those detainees mentioned above: Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho, Kim Wŏn-jung, Pae Tu-bong, Lee Sang-hee, Hyŏn Tu-hwang, and one unidentified person. After shooting them, soldiers set fire to the corpses. Hyŏn Chŏng-ja, Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho's niece, testified: "A civilian driver who transported my uncle's family to Pakch'ŏngnae finally revealed the location after much deliberation, enabling us to recover the remains. However, since they were burned, we had to identify my uncle (Hyŏn Kyŏng-ho) by a remaining fragment of his ramie robe, and my cousin (Hyŏn Tu-hwang) by a medicine bottle in his pocket."[^4]

Key figures in the deaths of Cheju citizens included 9th Regiment Commander Sŏng Yo-ch'an, along with 9th Regiment Intelligence Officer Captain T'ak Sŏng-rok (卓聖錄), Cheju Emergency Security Command's Special Investigation Unit Police Inspector Ch'oe Nan-su (崔蘭洙), and Cheju Sŏbuk Youth Corps Commander Kim Chae-nung (金在能). These individuals wielded power over life and death in Cheju. Yun T'ae-chun, a 9th Regiment soldier at the time, testified as follows: "The regiment's intelligence officer was T'ak Sŏng-rok, and a single word from him meant death. At that time, if you were arrested by the military police, you lived, but if you were arrested by Captain T'ak, whether civilian or soldier, everyone died. And those Sŏbuk Youth Corps bastards—they were terrible. They raped young women, ate chickens, falsely branded people as communists. These thugs made the situation worse. If they had just been told to suppress the rebellion, they would have only done that... That's why residents, with nowhere to escape, fled to the mountains."[^5]

T'ak Sŏng-rok's notoriety was so great that many testimonies about him exist, mostly describing how he "branded anyone who displeased him as a communist and killed them" or "sexually assaulted numerous women." Ch'oe Kil-tu testified: "T'ak Sŏng-rok was not human. He kept switching through several beautiful women, and when he left Cheju, he killed the woman he had been living with on Mount Sara. He held the power of death."[^6]

However, shockingly, T'ak Sŏng-rok was a drug addict. Ha Tu-yong testified: "When T'ak Sŏng-rok first arrived in Cheju, he immediately rushed to the hospital demanding what was called an opium injection. Since narcotics cannot be handled carelessly, we called in the pharmacy director to obtain approval before administering the injection. He was such a severe opium addict that the injection needle could not penetrate his arm. When Nurse Ahn Jŏng-suk attempted to inject him in the forearm, the needle could not enter, so he ordered her to inject him under his armpit instead. Throughout his assignment, he repeatedly came to the hospital for injections."[^7]

Chang Si-yŏng also testified: "When I was operating a hospital in Pusan with O Ch'ang-hun, T'ak Sŏng-rok repeatedly came to seek O Ch'ang-hun to request opium injections."[^8]

Kim Chŏng-mu, an officer of the 9th Regiment at the time, testified about his personal experience with T'ak Sŏng-rok: "The prisoner compound was created by pitching approximately twenty tents in the backyard of the Agricultural School. One day, when the regiment commander was conducting an inspection, I accompanied him as his staff officer. What caught my eye was that among the detainees, there was a man whose face was noticeably pale. Curious, I asked why. It turned out to be a physician named O Ch'ang-hun, who said that T'ak Sŏng-rok, the regiment's intelligence officer, had demanded that he administer an opium injection, and when O refused, T'ak had imprisoned him. I myself came from the North, hating communism, and joined the military, but I could not tolerate such a person. I went to T'ak Sŏng-rok with my pistol and said, 'Hey, why are you turning non-communists into communists? If you continue like this, I will kill you.' Only then did he release O Ch'ang-hun. Even after being released, O felt threatened for his life and followed me around, asking me to arrange for him to become a military medical officer. So we went together to Seoul Army Headquarters to request this from the medical director, but it did not work out. Afterward, O could not bring himself to return to Cheju and instead went to Pusan, where he opened an 'O Pediatric Clinic.' T'ak Sŏng-rok was nearly forty years old, yet he was wholly unqualified to be an intelligence officer. He was not even a graduate of the Military English School—he was just a military band member who played the trumpet, yet he received an emergency commission and was promoted to captain before I was. For various pretexts, he committed numerous sexual assaults on women."[^9]

[^3]: Kim Hyŏng-jung (age 88, Cheju City Idong 1-dong, Provincial Government Official at the time, recorded September 13, 2002); Ch'oe Kil-tu (age 85, Cheju City Topyŏngdong, Property Management Bureau Official at the time, recorded June 22, 2001).

[^4]: Hyŏn Chŏng-ja (age 69, Cheju City Yŏndong, 2nd Year Cheju Girls' Middle School Student at the time, recorded July 26, 2001).

[^5]: Yun T'ae-chun (age 73, Seoul City Tŏngdaemungŭ Chŏnnongdong, Senior Sergeant, 9th Regiment Supply Division at the time, recorded May 2, 2001).

[^6]: Ch'oe Kil-tu testimony.

[^7]: Ha Tu-yong (age 75, Cheju City Samdo-dong, Accounting Chief of Cheju Provincial Hospital at the time, recorded June 28, 2001).

[^8]: Chang Si-yŏng (age 81, Cheju City Ildo 2-dong, Hospital Physician at the time, recorded October 19, 2002).

[^9]: Kim Chŏng-mu (age 77, Seoul City Chongno-gu Puamdong, Supply Officer of the 9th Regiment at the time, recorded September 25, 2002).

---

**[p. 383]**

As a 9th Regiment intelligence officer, T'ak Sŏng-rok was at the very core of Cheju's suppression forces. That such a crucial position was held by a narcotics addict who committed indiscriminate killings and sexual assault symbolically exemplifies the recklessness of the suppression operations carried out at that time.

Police Inspector Ch'oe Nan-su (崔蘭洙), Commander of the Special Investigation Unit directly under the Cheju Emergency Security Command, was also deeply involved in the Cheju 4·3 Incident. He first came to Cheju in May 1948, shortly after the May 10 elections, leading a homicide unit from the Seoul Metropolitan Police.[^10] Ch'oe Nan-su also inspected Cheju in mid-June 1948.[^11] Ch'oe Nan-su's third arrival in Cheju came in early November 1948, just as indiscriminate harsh suppression operations were beginning. At this time, Ch'oe Nan-su emerged as a core police figure, serving as Commander of the Special Investigation Unit directly under the Cheju Emergency Security Command. Ch'oe replaced all Special Investigation Unit members with those from outside Cheju, retaining only one native of Cheju.[^12]

Kim Ho-gyŏm, who served as a police officer in Cheju at the time, testified: "At that time, native Cheju police officers were distrusted. Because Cheju society was small, when you caught Mount Halla bandits, you often found that among the police there were those who were 'relatives through marriage'—distant connections—with them. In any case, because they could not trust natives of Cheju, a special investigative unit came down from Seoul, with Police Inspector Ch'oe Nan-su as its commander. Inspector Ch'oe was a colonial-era higher investigation officer, and the habits from that time remained—he resorted to torture. This frequently created friction between us. One night while I was on night duty at the Cheju Police Station, I heard a woman's screams and could not sleep. I went to the interrogation room and found him torturing a woman by stripping her naked and hanging her upside down. I took a Japanese sword and stormed in, and the investigation unit members fled. The next day, I confronted Police Chief Hong Sun-bong, saying, 'Ch'oe Nan-su is going too far. If this continues, Cheju people will increasingly turn their backs on mainlanders. This will make suppressing the situation more difficult.' But Ch'oe was obstinate. When subjected to such torture, people will confess to anything, even things they did not do. The special investigation unit also created pamphlets on their own, secretly distributed them to specific villages, then arrested and tortured those villagers. Money and various things would come out in the process. This was essentially the so-called 'government-manufactured communism' of the Liberal Party era."[^13]

Ch'oe Nan-su was notorious not only in Cheju but also in the capital for the "Assassination Plot Against Congressmen Incident." When anti-Japanese collaborators faced crisis due to the passage of the Law on the Investigation of Anti-Japanese Collaborators (反民法), pro-Japanese police officers led by Ch'oe Nan-su plotted to assassinate those involved in the Special Committee investigating such collaborators. This assassination plot occurred just before Ch'oe Nan-su's third assignment to Cheju. In late October 1948, Ch'oe Nan-su, together with Nŏ Dŏk-sul (盧德述), another police officer famous for pro-Japanese sympathies, hired professional terrorist Paek Min-t'ae (白民泰) to assassinate congressmen who had spearheaded the passage of the anti-collaboration law. Ch'oe Nan-su's scheme involved kidnapping congressmen on the Special Committee investigating anti-Japanese collaborators, forcing them to resign, taking them to the vicinity of the 38th Parallel, killing them, and then reporting that they "were caught attempting to betray the homeland and flee north, so they were summarily executed." The list of fifteen assassination targets that Paek Min-t'ae received from Ch'oe Nan-su included the names of such figures as Kim Byŏng-nŏ (金炳魯, Chief Justice) and Shin Ik-hee (申翼熙). While perpetrating this plot, Ch'oe Nan-su was dispatched to Cheju and took part in the harsh suppression operations. After returning to Seoul, Ch'oe again provided Paek Min-t'ae with funds and weapons in January 1949, urging the plan's execution. However, as pro-Japanese police officers began to be arrested by the Special Committee investigating anti-Japanese collaborators, Paek Min-t'ae had a change of heart and surrendered, causing the plot to come to naught. Ultimately, Ch'oe Nan-su was sentenced to two years' imprisonment for attempted murder in June 1949.[^14]

Kim Chae-nung (金在能), Commander of the Cheju Sŏbuk Youth Corps, exercised power over life and death with true impunity. He engaged in extortion, torture, murder, and assault on women. In particular, when Kim Chae-nung demanded items and was refused by Kim Tu-hyŏn (金斗鉉), the Director-General of Cheju Provincial Administration, he dragged him to the Sŏbuk Youth Corps office and beat him to death. Regarding this, a U.S. military report states: "On November 9, a member of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps assaulted to death the Director-General of Cheju Provincial Administration, Kim Tu-hyŏn. The Sŏbuk Youth Corps stated that it intended only to interrogate him, whom it knew to be a communist, and had no intention of killing him."[^15]

In such a world, one could beat to death the second-ranking administrative official of the Cheju provincial government and escape by simply branding him a communist. Kim Chae-nung even forcibly took over the Cheju Shinbo, the sole press organ in the Cheju region at that time. Kim Yong-su, Editor-in-Chief of Cheju Shinbo at the time, testified as follows: "The Cheju Shinbo had no particular reason to incur the displeasure of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. They simply seized the newspaper to expand their own authority. The only exception was when we expressed dissatisfaction over the 'Bongkae Village Operation.' In early 1949, this was an operation led by the 2nd Regiment's 2nd Company Commander Captain Sŏk Pong under the coordination of police, the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, and security forces, with military mobilization. Many innocent civilians died, but the newspaper could not report any of this—we had to cover it as a 'justified military operation' based on military announcements. The Sŏbuk Youth Corps expressed dissatisfaction because their role was not given sufficient weight in the report. After that incident, one day Sŏbuk Youth Corps members came into the newspaper office and beat President Kim Sŏk-ho. That night, around 2 a.m., I was dragged away from my home by Sŏbuk Youth Corps members. Commander Kim Chae-nung was waiting. At that time, Kim Chae-nung was in his mid-thirties, standing six feet tall, sporting a mustache, and wearing the long leather boots that Japanese soldiers wore. His reputation was so fearsome that just looking at his tall frame striding about made one's spine tingle. Without saying a word, he simply beat me—not an ordinary beating but one so savage it inflicted life-threatening injuries. As I was beaten into unconsciousness, Kim Chae-nung said, 'Take him and dispose of him!' The Sŏbuk Youth Corps dragged me toward the execution site. But thanks to 2nd Regiment Commander Ham Byŏng-sŏn (咸炳善), who learned that my family was searching everywhere for me, I was saved. The 2nd Regiment dressed me in a military uniform and assigned me to conduct propaganda instead of serving as editor-in-chief."[^16]

[^10]: Chosŏn Ilbo, May 18, 1948.

[^11]: Tonga Ilbo, June 24, 1948.

[^12]: Kim Byŏng-t'aek (age 77, Cheju City Yongdamryŏng 1-dong, Special Investigation Unit Member at the time, recorded February 8, 2002).

[^13]: Kim Ho-gyŏm (Seoul City Ŭnp'yŏnggu Yŏkch'on 1-dong, Former Sŏguipo Police Chief) testimony. Cheminilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, *"4·3 Speaks," ④* (Chŏn Ye-wŏn, 1997), pp. 222–223, 386.

[^14]: O Ik-hwan, "Activity and Dissolution of the Special Committee Investigating Anti-Japanese Collaborators," in *Understanding Korea's Liberation and Post-Liberation History, ①* (Hangilsa, 1979), pp. 124–127.

[^15]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 987, November 13, 1948.

[^16]: Kim Yong-su (Kyŏnggi Province Sŏngnamsi) testimony. Cheminilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, *"4·3 Speaks," ④* (1997), pp. 173–174.

---

**[p. 384]**

Having thus forcibly taken over Cheju Shinbo through terror and intimidation, Kim Chae-nung installed Kim Muk (金黙), a member of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps special unit, as editor-in-chief and himself assumed the position of publisher, wielding power over life and death with even greater impunity.

## Case Study: Aewŏl-myon Haga-ri Village

Aewŏl-myon Haga-ri was a small, ordinary village of approximately 160 households. Not a coastal village, but situated not far from the circular island road formed along the coast, it occupied an ambiguous position between a coastal settlement and an inland mountain village, and therefore no evacuation order had been issued. After the armed uprising on April 3, there had been no incidents in the area, so residents lived without particular concern. However, at around 1 a.m. on November 13, 1948, soldiers from the 9th Regiment stationed in Wedo-ri on the outskirts of Cheju eup suddenly descended on the village. At that moment, one household had just finished conducting a jesa (ancestral rite) and was partaking of the ritual meal, while several people had gathered at the house of Chŏng Ki-bong in the middle neighborhood, where they were feasting on pork. The suppression forces first raided Chŏng Ki-bong's house, which was brightly lit with people gathering inside. They also burst into neighboring houses, dragging out sleeping residents. Then, without hesitation, they set fire to fourteen houses including Chŏng Ki-bong's home, while driving people to a nearby field colloquially called "Yuksiwŏnyŏng." What followed was a horrific scene mixed with gunfire and screams.

The victims that day were Kim Tu-ch'ŏn (71), Chŏng Ki-bong (67), Lee Chŏng-hwa (female, 59), Yang Kong-myŏng (48), Chāng Sun-ho (45), Im Kun-sŏn (42), Im Yŏng-ŏn (42), Yun Ch'ang-guk (41), Chāng Ŏn-sun (39), Chŏng Sun-a (39), Im Ch'i-wan (38), Ko T'ae-sik (37), Song Yu-saeng (33), Im In-wŏn (31), Chāng Ki-hyu (30), Pak Ch'ŏng-ryang (female, 30s), Ko Wŏn-ryong (29), Pak Kun-hwa (female, 27), Chŏng Sin-a (27), Kang Ki-hwan (26), Yang Kong-ŏn (26), Im Hwa-bong (26), Ko Tu-ch'ŏl (24), Ko Sun-hwa (female, 18), and Chŏng To-a (18). Among the victims, Ko Sun-hwa was a pregnant woman in her final months. Ko Wŏn-ryong retained enough life to be moved to a hospital in Aewŏl-ri, but died two days later. In the case of O Ch'ang-gi (in his twenties at the time), who was shot that day but miraculously survived, one sees how thin the line between life and death was—the difference between a person shot and a soldier. Despite carrying a bullet embedded in his body, O Ch'ang-gi survived the crisis, and when the Korean War broke out, he volunteered and enlisted as a member of "Marine Corps Class 3," participating in the Inchon Landing operation. Kang Ŭng-mu (姜應武), whose husband was killed that day, testified: "Three soldiers entered our house and dragged my sleeping husband out. After gathering people, the soldiers shot them with a machine gun. Bullets penetrated his body in many places, and when he was not dying quickly enough, they slit his throat with a knife, and blood flowed everywhere. I do not regard them as soldiers but as human butchers."[^17]

The question arises: Why did soldiers come to Haga-ri that night, and why did they indiscriminately execute the residents? There are theories circulating that right-wing organization members from coastal villages and a despised Haga-ri resident's son conspired against the villagers. Alternatively, it is suggested that when suppression forces, having heard intelligence that armed guerrillas were concentrated in the inland mountain village of Wŏndong, arrived at Haga-ri, which lay on the route between the two, they misunderstood residents who had gathered there as plotting a "bandit operation" and opened fire. Considering that soldiers subsequently went to Wŏndong village and executed its residents as well, the latter supposition of a misunderstanding carries more persuasive weight. However, since the victims were ordinary residents, including elderly people and women, there was no justification whatsoever for their execution.

The more fundamental reason may be found in examining the date of the incident. On November 13, 1948, when the Haga-ri incident occurred, indiscriminate killings of men, women, young, and old, along with arson, also took place in numerous locations: Wŏndong in Sokkil-ri, Aewŏl-myon; Kyŏrae-ri, Choch'ŏn-myon; Wahŭl-ri 2-ku, Choch'ŏn-myon; Shinheung-ri, Choch'ŏn-myon; Sangch'ŏn-ri, Anděk-myon; Sangch'ang-ri, Anděk-myon; and Ch'angch'ŏn-ri, Anděk-myon. Such systematic mass killings and arson occurred on a widespread basis throughout the Cheju region.

[^17]: Kang Ŭng-mu (female, age 83, Aewŏl-ŭp Haga-ri, recorded June 1, 2002).

---

**[p. 385]**

was indeed such a figure. When pro-Japanese figures faced a crisis with the enactment of the Special Inquiry Committee Law, pro-Japanese police led by Choe Nan-su organized assassination plans targeting those associated with the committee. This assassination plot occurred before Choe Nan-su's third assignment to Cheju. In late October 1948, Choe Nan-su, together with Noh Dŏk-sul (盧德述), another notorious pro-Japanese police officer, hired White Min-tae (白民泰), a professional terrorist, to assassinate National Assembly members who had led the enactment of the Special Inquiry Committee Law. Choe Nan-su's plot involved kidnapping a National Assembly member belonging to the Special Committee, forcing him to write a letter of resignation, dragging him near the 38th Parallel, killing him, and then reporting that "we discovered him betraying the nation and attempting to defect north, and executed him on the spot." The list of fifteen assassination targets that White Min-tae received from Choe Nan-su included names such as Kim Pyŏng-no (金炳魯, Chief Justice of the Supreme Court) and Shin Ik-hee (申翼熙). While orchestrating this plot, Choe Nan-su was dispatched to Cheju and took charge of a portion of the forceful suppression operation. After returning to Seoul, in January 1949 Choe Nan-su again urged White Min-tae to execute the plan, providing him with funds and weapons. However, when pro-Japanese police began to be arrested following investigations by the Special Inquiry Committee, White Min-tae underwent a change of heart and surrendered, causing the plot to come to naught. Ultimately, Choe Nan-su was sentenced to two years imprisonment for attempted murder in June 1949.[^1]

Kim Jae-nung (金再能), company commander of the Cheju Northwest Youth Association, wielded power without any checks. He was notorious for extortion, torture, murder, and violation of women. Kim Jae-nung was particularly guilty of forcing people to surrender goods; when denied, he dragged Kim Tu-hyŏn (金斗鉉), the commissioner of general affairs of Cheju Province, to the Northwest Youth Association office and beat him to death. Regarding this incident, a U.S. Army report recorded: "On November 9, members of the Northwest Youth Association beat to death Kim Tu-hyŏn, commissioner of general affairs of Cheju Province. The Northwest Youth Association stated they intended merely to interrogate him, whom they believed to be a communist, and did not intend to kill him."[^2] It was a world in which one could beat the second-ranking administrator of Cheju Province's government to death and merely brand him a communist. Kim Jae-nung even forcibly took over the Cheju Sinbo, the only news organization in the Cheju region at that time. Kim Yong-su (金用洙), editor-in-chief of the Cheju Sinbo at the time, testified as follows:

"There was no particular reason the Cheju Sinbo went along with the Northwest Youth Association's sentiments. The Northwest Youth Association simply seized the newspaper to expand their authority. However, we did express dissatisfaction during the 'Bonggae-ri Operation.' In early 1949, it was an operation led by the 2nd Battalion of the 2nd Regiment (Battalion Commander Sŏk Pong-dae, Captain), in which police, the Northwest Youth Association, and the Taechŏng Youth Association were all mobilized. Many innocent civilians were killed, but the newspaper did not carry such stories—"

[^1]: Wu Yi-hwan, "Activities and Dissolution of the Special Inquiry Committee," *Understanding the Period of Liberation and After*, 1st volume, Hangilsa, 1979, pp. 124-127.
[^2]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 987, November 13, 1948.

**[p. 386]**

## Case Study: Aewŏl-myon Sokkil-ri Wŏndong Village

Aewŏl-myon Sokkil-ri Wŏndong (院洞) village vanished from the map after November 13, 1948, when indiscriminate harsh suppression operations were initiated (Wŏndong, where approximately fifteen households once lived, is technically divided by a stream running through the village into Sokkil-ri to the east and Sangka-ri to the west. However, since only about five households lived in the Sangka-ri area, it was administratively classified as Sokkil-ri). Today, only a stone marker inscribed "Wŏnjii (院址)," erected at the village entrance, testifies that a village once existed there.

Wŏndong village, which had formed during the Chosŏn dynasty, was originally an inn village where those passing through the mountain region would rest. At the time in question, only one household operated an inn, while the remaining residents farmed. Because it was such a small village with few young men capable of serving as armed guerrillas, the police who came to the village merely issued warnings: "Do not cooperate with bandits," and departed without beating or executing any residents. The armed guerrillas themselves occasionally came down and gathered residents for speeches but, given the village's poor harvest, did not demand grain supplies. Thus, until soldiers arrived and assembled them, residents harbored no great fears and simply remained at home. Evacuation orders had not yet been issued.

The soldiers came to Wŏndong, apparently having received intelligence that armed guerrillas were concentrated in the vicinity. These were the very soldiers who had already perpetrated a reckless massacre in Haga-ri before arriving in Wŏndong. They searched the village but found no armed guerrillas. Nevertheless, they indiscriminately shot all residents, regardless of age or gender.

The victims that day, including one person who had been temporarily resting at the village inn while passing through the mountain region, numbered thirty-four confirmed Wŏndong residents: Lee Tu-ik (64), Kim Ki-yong (60), Kim Sŭng-hong (58), Hyŏn Tu-byŏng (50), Kim Kil-hong (49), Yang I-ryong (48), this Yi-ryong's wife (anonymous), Ko Byŏng-gyu (43), Yang Chŏng-saeng (female, 41), Kang Ki-song (40s), Kim Yu-hong (40), Hŏ Hong (female, 40s), Lee Tal-ho (female, 36), Kang Ch'ang-su (31), Kang Ch'ang-gwŏn (29), Kang Ch'ang-ug (28), wife of Kang Ch'ang-ug (25), Kim Kwi-hwan (28), Yang Un-yong (28), Kim Sŏng-man (female, 24), Ko Im-saeng (female, 22), Hong Sŏng-gyu (22), Ko T'ae-wŏn (21), Kim Kwi-hyu (21), Yang Ch'un-hee (female, 21), Kang Kong-bu (20), Hyŏn Ch'ang-ha (20), Chāng Pong-ho (female, 19), Im Se-ok, Ko Nam-ok (female, 16), Yang Ch'un-saeng (female, 16), Hyŏn Pong-wan (14), Ko Nam-chu (7), and the son of Kang Ch'ang-ug (4).

Ko Nam-bo lost his father (Ko Byŏng-gyu) and two younger siblings (Ko Nam-ok and Ko Nam-chu) in a single day. Now seventeen years old when he experienced that terrible day, Ko testified as follows:

"The soldiers were carrying flashlights as they assembled residents, so it must have been around 5 a.m., before dawn. After tying our hands behind our backs, the soldiers bound us together with rope, as if threading dried fish. After leading the entire village in a circle this way, all the residents were bound. Two days earlier, police and the Coastal Village Youth Corps had come to the village, but nothing had happened, so initially we were not greatly worried. The soldiers demanded, 'Point out the bandits,' but how could we know that? Bound as we were, we were dragged here and there around the village searching for bandits. From dawn, hungry and tired, we spent the whole day in this manner. Only around 5 p.m. did we return to the front of the inn. At first, the soldiers did not seem to intend to kill us. Perhaps that is why they occasionally untied us. They also passed time with idle talk, saying things like, 'If you line people up and shoot, nine people will die,' or 'Adults die quietly, but children die after crying two or three times.' Then we were bound again. When bound this time, I was tied with my hands in front. Just then, a soldier radioed somewhere, and they announced, 'You will all be executed within ten minutes.' Apparently an order had come down from regiment headquarters. Soon a military vehicle arrived from the direction of Aewŏl-ri. I hastily prepared to loosen my bonds, and while they were exchanging salutes with each other, I bolted into the forest. Shortly after, gunfire erupted loudly. After shooting, the soldiers covered the bodies with food and blankets, set them afire, and left."[^18]

Thus Wŏndong vanished.

How, then, did the soldiers who carried out the executions process and record this incident? The following U.S. military report provides a clue regarding the Wŏndong village incident:

"November 13: As a result of operations by the Constabulary, bandits were killed: 115 in Haengwŏn-ri, 37 in the area with coordinates 937-1133, and 4 near O-dŭngni. Of those bandits killed, one was identified as having escaped from the Constabulary." (Counterintelligence report)[^19]

When the place names in the above report were checked through U.S. military maps, the location marked only as coordinates (937-1133) corresponded precisely to Wŏndong village. The indiscriminate massacre of that day—which included elderly people in their sixties and a four-year-old child—was recorded as "military accomplishment" in the suppression of "bandits."

[^18]: Ko Nam-bo (Cheju City Yongdamryŏng 1-dong) testimony. Cheminilbo 4·3 Coverage Team, "4·3 Speaks," Episode 434 (January 22, 1999).

[^19]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 989, November 16, 1948.

---

**[p. 387]**

dragged to a nearby field. Immediately a horrific scene of gunfire and screams mixed together unfolded. The victims on this day were Kim Tu-chŏn (71), Chŏng Ki-bong (67), Yi Chŏng-hwa (female, 59), Yang Kong-myŏng (48), Chang Sun-ho (45), Im Kun-sŏn (42), Im Yŏng-ŏn (42), Yun Chang-kuk (41), Chang Ŏn-sun (39), Chŏng Sun-a (39), Im Chi-wan (38), Ko Tae-sik (37), Song Yu-saeng (33), Im In-wŏn (31), Chang Ki-hyu (30), Park Ch'ŏng-ryang (female, 30s), Ko Wŏn-ryong (29), Park Kun-hwa (female, 27), Chŏng Shin-a (27), Kang Ki-hwan (26), Yang Kong-ŏn (26), Im Hwa-bong (26), Ko Tu-chŏl (24), Ko Sun-hwa (female, 18), Chŏng To-a (18), and others. Among the victims, Ko Sun-hwa was a pregnant woman in her final month. Ko Wŏn-ryong was transferred to a hospital in Aewŏl-li as there remained a spark of life, but died two days later. The case of O Ch'ang-gi (in his 20s at the time) who was shot but miraculously survived demonstrates how thin the line is between "life and death," between "those shot and soldiers." O Ch'ang-gi, with a bullet embedded in his body, endured the turmoil, and when the Korean War broke out, he volunteered and enlisted in the "3rd Marine Corps," participating in the Inch'ŏn Landing Operation. Kang Ŭng-mu (姜應武), who lost her husband on this day, testified: "Three soldiers entered our house and dragged my sleeping husband out. The soldiers gathered people together and shot them with a machine gun. Bullets pierced his body here and there, and when he didn't die quickly, they cut his throat with a knife, and blood was everywhere. I do not regard them as soldiers but as human butchers."[^3]

But why did the soldiers come to Hagar-ri on that day, and why did they massacre the residents indiscriminately? Regarding this, it was rumored that the plot had been hatched by rightist group members from a seaside village and the son of someone from Hagar-ri, who were despised. Alternatively, there is an account that suppression forces, hearing intelligence that armed guerrillas had assembled in Wŏn-dong, a mountain village, were heading there and when they reached Hagar-ri, a midway point, and saw residents gathered together, they mistook it for "bandits plotting," resulting in a massacre. When considering that after the soldiers carried out the massacre in Hagar-ri, they went directly to Wŏn-dong village and also massacred its residents, the latter supposition about "mistaken interpretation" carries greater persuasiveness. However, since the victims were ordinary residents including elderly and women, nothing could justify the massacre. The more fundamental reason can be traced to the date the incident occurred. On November 13, 1948, when the Hagar-ri incident took place, indiscriminate massacres and arson occurred in various places such as Wŏn-dong village in Sogil-li, Aewŏl-myŏn; Kyorrae-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn; Waheul-li Section 2, Choch'ŏn-myŏn; Sinhŭng-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn; Sangch'ŏn-ri, Andŏk-myŏn; Sangch'ang-ri, Andŏk-myŏn; and Ch'angch'ŏn-ri, Andŏk-myŏn, regardless of age or gender. This type of massacre and arson occurred widely in other regions of Jeju Province as well.

[^3]: Kang Ŭng-mu (female, 83 years old, Hagar-ri, Aewŏl-ŭp, recorded June 1, 2002), testimony.

**[p. 388]**

□ The Case of Wŏn-dong Village, Sogil-li, Aewŏl-myŏn

Wŏn-dong (院洞) village in Sogil-li, Aewŏl-myŏn, vanished from the map after November 13, 1948, when indiscriminate forceful suppression operations had just begun (Approximately 15 households had lived in Wŏn-dong, which administratively corresponds to Sogil-li to the east and Sangar-ri to the west, divided by a stream that ran through the village. However, since only about five households lived on the Sangar-ri side, it was treated as Sogil-li). Now, only a stone marker inscribed with "Wŏnji (院址)" erected at the village entrance tells that a village once existed here. Wŏn-dong village, which formed during the Chosŏn period, was originally a tavern village where people passing through the midland area stopped to rest. At that time, only one household operated a tavern, and the remaining residents lived by farming. Because it was such a small village with few young people capable of serving with the armed guerrillas, the police who came to the village only warned residents not to cooperate with the rebels and left without beating or shooting anyone. The armed guerrillas themselves, while occasionally coming down to gather residents and deliver speeches, did not demand provisions from this village with low agricultural output. Therefore, residents felt no great danger until soldiers arrived and gathered them, simply staying at home. An evacuation order had not yet been issued. The reason soldiers came to Wŏn-dong appears to have been that they had heard intelligence of armed guerrillas assembling in the vicinity. These soldiers, before arriving at Wŏn-dong, had already perpetrated the reckless massacre at Hagar-ri. Though the soldiers searched the village, there were no armed guerrillas. Nevertheless, the soldiers massacred the innocent residents indiscriminately, regardless of age or gender. It is reported that among the victims on this day, even someone who was temporarily staying at the village tavern while passing through the midland area was killed. The confirmed residents of Wŏn-dong victims were: Yi Tu-ik (64), Kim Ki-yong (60), Kim Sŭng-hong (58), Hyŏn Tu-byŏng (50), Kim Kil-hong (49), Yang I-ryong (48), Yi Mu-saeng (Yang I-ryong's wife), Ko Pyŏng-kyu (43), Yang Chŏng-saeng (female, 41), Kang Ki-song (40s), Kim Yu-hong (40), Hŏ Hong (female, 40s), Yi Tal-ho (female, 36), Kang Ch'ang-su (31), Kang Ch'ang-kwŏn (29), Kang Ch'ang-uk (28), Kang Ch'ang-uk's wife (25), Kim Kwe-hwan (28), Yang Un-yong (28), Kim Sŏng-man (female, 24), Ko Im-saeng (female, 22), Hong Sŏng-kyu (22), Ko Tae-wŏn (21), Kim Kwe-hyu (21), Yang Ch'un-hui (female, 21), Kang Kong-bu (20), Hyŏn Ch'ang-ha (20), Chang Pong-ho (female, 19), Im Se-ok, Ko Nam-ok (female, 16), Yang Ch'un-saeng (female, 16), Hyŏn Pong-wan (14), Ko Nam-chu (7), Kang Ch'ang-uk's son (4), and thirty-four others in total. Ko Nam-bo suddenly lost his father (Ko Pyŏng-kyu) and two younger siblings (Ko Nam-ok, Ko Nam-chu) on this day. At seventeen years of age, Ko witnessed that horrific day and testified as follows:

**[p. 389]**

"The soldiers were carrying flashlights and gathering residents, so it must have been around 5 a.m. when it was still dark. The soldiers tied the residents' hands behind their backs and bound them together with rope, as though stringing them like dried fish. When they went around the village like that, all the residents became bound. Although police and young men from a coastal village had come two days earlier without incident, at first we were not greatly worried. The soldiers asked, 'Point out where the bandits are,' but how could we know such a thing? Bound as we were, we were dragged here and there throughout the village searching for bandits. From early morning without food, we spent the whole day doing this, and returned in front of the tavern around 5 p.m. The soldiers did not seem to intend to kill us at first. So they even untied us for a while. And they passed the time saying nonsensical things like, 'If we line people up and shoot, nine will die,' or 'Adults die without a sound, but children cry once or twice before dying.' Then we were bound again, and this time my hands were tied in front. At that moment, one soldier made some kind of telephone call and said, 'You will all be shot within ten minutes.' It seems orders had come down from regiment headquarters. Soon a military truck came up from the direction of Aewŏl-li. I quickly prepared to loosen the restraints, and while they were exchanging salutes with each other, I ran into the forest. A moment later, gunfire rang out loudly. The soldiers covered the corpses with provisions and blankets, set them on fire, and then left."[^4]

Thus Wŏn-dong vanished. So how did the soldiers who carried out the massacre handle and record this incident? The U.S. Army report below provides a clue regarding the Wŏn-dong village incident:

"On November 13, as a result of Guard operations, 115 rebels were killed at Haengwŏn-ni, 37 at coordinates 937-1133, and 4 near Odŭng-ni. Of those killed, one was identified as having escaped from the Guard. (Counterintelligence report)"[^5]

When I checked through U.S. Army maps the location marked only by coordinates (937-1133), where the report maker did not know the place name, it precisely indicated Wŏn-dong village. This indiscriminate massacre on that day—including elderly in their 60s and small children of four years old—was simply recorded as the "military's combat achievement" of "killing bandits."

[^4]: Ko Nam-bu (Yongdam 1-dong, Cheju City), testimony, recorded in *4·3 Speaks* (434th installment) by the Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, January 22, 1999.
[^5]: Hq. USAFIK, G-2 Periodic Report, No. 989, November 16, 1948.

**[p. 390]**

## Tosari-ri, Pyoseongmyeon

The tragedy that befell Tosari-ri in Pyoseongmyeon began on December 15, 1948 (the 11th day of the 11th lunar month). It was a night with a very bright moon. Soldiers surrounding the village assembled all residents at the hyangsagwan, then separated out males aged 18 to 40. They also had the women gaze at the moon, then separated out young, pretty women under age 20. The soldiers took these people to Pyoseong Elementary School, where they detained them and, primarily on December 18 and 19, executed them by firing squad. After this day, Tosari-ri became a village without youth. Many households in this village performed posthumous marriages (死婚). They held spirit wedding ceremonies between unmarried men and women who had died, and adopted sons-in-law to continue the family line.[^1]

Tosari-ri is divided into two villages: Wuttosari[^2] and Altosari. Originally, Tosari-ri had not attracted the attention of suppression forces. Altosari is a coastal village, and Wuttosari is only about 2 km from the coast at most. For this reason, the resettlement order issued in mid-November did not apply to this village. The residents served as sentries under the control of suppression forces and spent anxious days. Then, on December 12, for some reason, soldiers ordered Wuttosari residents to resettle in Altosari. The resettlement proceeded without a single casualty and the soldiers did not set fire to the village. However, immediately after the resettlement, on December 15, soldiers suddenly appeared and took residents away. Several legends circulated regarding this mass execution. First, some three armed guerrillas who were being chased by soldiers escaped toward the seashore, so the soldiers searched nearby Tosari-ri and assembled people. Second, initially only a few people were to be executed and the rest released, but a sudden incident caused greater casualties. Specifically, a detainee at Pyoseong Elementary School escaped and hid in a private home, but when the homeowner tried to stab him with a spear, he actually seized the spear and killed him. Enraged soldiers then executed all the detainees. However, regardless of which story is true, neither can justify the mass execution. The sacrifice of young women especially was a violation of human decency. After the "June Struggle" of 1987, a democratic atmosphere emerged, and residents began speaking about this incident, which had remained only as a grievance until then. That year, residents drafted a petition titled "Record of the Truth of the 4·3 Incident" (實狀記) to clarify the truth and restore honor.[^3]

The person who took the lead in creating this record, Kim Yang-hak (金養學), lost his younger uncle, youngest uncle, and older brother in the mass execution. However, the question that most residents still harbor is: "Why did the residents have to die?" Kim Yang-hak spoke as follows:

Those who were taken away at that time were all simple, ordinary citizens who willingly cooperated with the resettlement and responded to the assembly order. If they use the pretext of ideology, are they claiming that ideological problems existed only among males aged 18 to 40, and that among women, girls were selected arbitrarily based on moonlit faces—with only young, pretty women implicated in ideology? It can only be seen as a sexual matter. My older brother underwent a spirit marriage (死婚) with a widow, making her his sister-in-law. She died at age 16 at the time. However, her younger sister was only 15 and was uniquely among those who returned alive. I visited her several times to ask about the circumstances at the time, but she refuses to speak at all due to shame.[^4]

When the Syngman Rhee regime collapsed due to the April 19 Revolution of 1960, the National Assembly undertook to investigate the state of civilian massacres. Though rushed, over a period of only four to five days, Tosari-ri residents investigated and reported 148 victims. The aforementioned "Record of the Truth of the 4·3 Incident" recorded the number of victims as 157. At that time, Tosari-ri was a small village of about 200 households.

## 2) Killing of Families of Those Who Evaded

Suppression forces executed those they called "families of evaders," killing anyone whose village they entered through resettlement who had a young family member disappear. There was no exception even for residents originally from coastal villages. In some areas, soldiers assembled residents and, comparing them against household registers, identified families of evaders. Young people had disappeared, and mostly the elderly, wives, and small children—primarily the weak—became victims. Residents called this "substitution killing" (代殺). Although the dictionary definition of "substitution killing" is "to execute the murderer," residents used the expression to mean "dying in place of a family member." Executions occurred in coastal villages where suppression forces were stationed. As executions continued without cease, the relocated populations fled to the mountains again, becoming a factor that prolonged the crisis.
