As mass executions of families of evaders continued day after day, one woman, terrified by her husband's entry into the mountains, hid in her parents' storage building and, after weakening in body and mind, finally emerged four years later.

**[p. 391]**

## Hagui-ri, Aewolmyeon

Around mid-November 1948, when strong suppression operations were just beginning against midland villages, no young people could be found in Hagui-ri of Aewolmyeon. When police killings by firearms began in May 1948, young people created hiding places and stayed concealed. However, around December 5, 1948,[^5] the Oedo Police Station issued a mobilization order to Hagui-ri residents. They were told to gather at the police station with saws and axes to prepare firewood for winter. Residents could not refuse the police station's order, but the young people who had been hiding could not emerge boldly and risk danger. After deliberation, mainly the elderly and women came with tools, and some young people, confident they would not attract police attention, revealed themselves. However, the original talk of "firewood preparation" was a trap. The Oedo Police Station had no intention of cutting wood; instead, they immediately began beating, demanding, "You blocked the road!" and "You cut down telephone poles!" Then they selected young people, loaded them into vehicles, and took them away. Many young people were loaded into vehicles at that time, and they never returned home. Prisoners held in detention were executed in mass by the Korean military as the Republic's forces were pushed back by the People's Army following the outbreak of the Korean War. Bereaved families, not knowing the date of death, primarily held memorial services on family members' birthdays. What once informed them that these people were alive was a postcard sent from Daegu Prison about a year later. Among the victims, the elderly mother of Kang Chang-ha reported to the National Assembly's civilian massacre investigation committee after the April 19 Revolution of 1960:

"I reported to Oedo Police Station that I would engage in firewood work and appeared as instructed. They detained me and sent me to Daegu Prison. A letter even arrived from Daegu Prison, but I know nothing of his whereabouts. Mother Shin Jeong-mo (77), Daughter Kang Gu-ja (19)"[^6]

The "labor mobilization orders" and "self-surrender operations" that came down to coastal villages were not limited to Hagui-ri. This represented the typical method deployed throughout Jeju during the early stages of strong suppression operations to draw hidden young people out. This indicates that such orders were not tactics devised at the level of the Oedo Police Station but rather operations planned at a much higher level. After arresting some young people through the "labor mobilization order," the Oedo Police Station proceeded with "self-surrender operations." The first self-surrender operation targeted Gaesudong (蓋水洞, later renamed Hagwondong). Located about 500 meters up the mountainside from the coastal road, Gaesudong was small in size but had many talented people and wielded influence strong enough to control other natural villages of Hagui-ri. The Oedo Police Station first sent a notice ordering young people in Gaesudong to surrender. While some young people in Gaesudong had entered the mountains as armed guerrillas and were active there, the police, for unknown reasons, designated ten young people rumored to be hiding near the village. The notice stated that if these youths appeared and surrendered, all would be safe; otherwise, the entire village would suffer greatly. In response, a village emergency meeting was held with the designated young people and village elders, but no easy conclusion was reached. To protect the village, the designated youths had to appear, but young people who had gone to the Oedo Police Station's earlier mobilization call had not returned; instead, they had been taken to Jeju town. Unable to trust the police's word, after much back-and-forth discussion, one of the ten designated, Kim Ho-jung (金浩中), stepped forward. "I will surrender first," Kim Ho-jung said. "If I am safe, that will prove the police's promise. Then the rest of you should surrender." He went alone to the Oedo Police Station. However, when Kim Ho-jung appeared, the police executed him by firing squad on December 7. Shortly thereafter, early on December 10, Police Station Chief Kim Young-chul (金永哲) and other police, along with members of the Daehan Youth Corps, descended upon Gaesudong. However, residents already possessed "information" that police would come. But the elderly and weak had nowhere to hide. The adults, sensing the crisis, were already prepared for death as they awaited the police. Ko Chang-sun (高昌善) testified to that day as follows:

When Mr. Kim Ho-jung was executed by firing squad, the village was turned upside down, and the nine designated young people and other youths fled to the mountains. Then the adults, saying "My goodness, this is really serious trouble," worried greatly. But the night before the police came to the village, word had already come from the Oedo area that "the situation is not normal; everyone should flee." But where could we go? Sensing the gravity of the situation, the adults gathered their families at home and held emergency meetings. My father said things like, "What is the household debt situation, where is cash hidden so those who survive can use it later, and from now on, follow the words of Mr. So-and-so." He spoke as though making a will.[^7]

The police who descended on Gaesudong searched house to house and assembled residents at a place called 'Bihaegi (飛鶴) Hill.' Then they selected so-called "families of evaders"—the parents and children of young people who had fled—and mass executed them. At that time, Kim Nak-jun (女, 69), Ko Hee-jeon (65), Kim Jae-bong (65), Ko Young-sam (62), Ko Du-ju (60s), Baek Yong-heung (女, 60s), Hyeon Gwi-deok (女, 60s), Kang Hwa-soon (女, 61), Kang Seung-hak (50s), Kim Ji-su (50s), Kang Ki-yu (49), and Ko Jeong-gyu's wife (30) and Kim Gye-saeng (女, 29), Kang Du-jung (16), and others were killed. In addition to families of evaders, thirty-six people total, including those relocated from Jangjeon-ri and Gwangryeong-ri to Gaesudong, were shot to death together.

**[p. 392]**

This "Bihaegi Hill Massacre" is well-known in the surrounding area for its cruelty. An pregnant woman was stripped naked, hung from a large paulownia tree, and pierced to death with bayonets and swords. The pregnant woman was Ko Jeong-gyu's wife. Because Ko Jeong-gyu had evaded, his father (Ko Hee-jeon) and wife were killed in his stead. However, during the massacre, as a mother fell after being shot, she shielded her child, and the boy alone survived. The sole surviving victim of the mass execution was An In-haeng (安仁行), who was thirteen years old at the time. An In-haeng, a native of Jangjeon-ri, was living in Gaesudong with his family following the resettlement order. An In-haeng's father, An Tae-yong (安太龍, 33), had already become a victim of the "Oedo Police Station Firewood Incident" that occurred five days earlier. Having lost both parents five days apart, An In-haeng testified as follows:

After the police gathered residents at Bihaegi Hill and conducted house searches, they brought two people who had remained in homes. They were a father and son who had been relocated from Gwangryeong-ri. The father was an elderly man in his seventies, and the son was around thirty-five. The police began beating the son first. Unable to bear it, the father pleaded, "We are relocated residents. My son is the sole male heir of four generations. Please spare him." Then they beat the father, and the old man, unable to endure the blows, soon died. After the father died, the son tried to escape, but about 150 meters into his flight, he was shot. Watching that scene while trembling in fear, they then pointed out a certain woman. She was about twenty-five, pregnant. The police tied a rope under the woman's armpits and hung her from a large paulownia tree. Then three police pierced her with bayonets. It would have been better if they had just shot her—it was a scene too horrible to witness. As everyone turned away their heads, the police shouted, "Watch carefully!" Then the police began selecting residents. They were identifying so-called "rebel families." We were dragged out because our father had been taken during the "Oedo Police Station Firewood Incident." Father was a relocated resident and was not originally targeted for mobilization by the Oedo Police Station. However, Father had wanted to express gratitude to the elderly owner of the house who had lent us a residence and fields, and when the old man was about to go to the police station with a saw and an axe, Father went instead and met disaster. We were four brothers. I, age thirteen, was the eldest, and below me were brothers aged ten, seven, and four. Mother (姜仁八, 34), sensing death, forcibly separated the younger children. However, my ten-year-old brother and I were tied together with Mother. Due to Mother's tearful pleas, my ten-year-old brother was released, but to me they said, "Your eyes are so round and bright—you look like someone who would contact the rebels"—and would not release me. In the end, twelve of us were tied together and dragged to a field about thirty meters away. The police said to each other, "Let's stab them with swords," then "There's no time; let's shoot." After arguing briefly, gunshots rang out loudly. My mother, tied right next to me, suddenly collapsed on top of me. As the bullets struck Mother's body, it convulsed, and my entire body was covered in Mother's blood. The police, saying, "There might be some not fully hit by bullets," stabbed each body with bayonets. But at that moment, I was covered by Mother and remained safe. This is how we four brothers became orphans. My seven-year-old brother died of measles, and the infant from hunger for lack of milk shortly after. If this were made into a film or play, I could reenact the events of that day exactly as they occurred—they are so vivid in my mind. How could I forget?[^8]

The executions of Gaesudong residents by police continued. Police regularly came to the village and sought out "families of evaders" for execution. Among these, January 24, 1949, was a day of great loss. That evening, Kim San-chun (28), the wife of Ko Dae-gyu (高大奎), received an order to report to the Oedo Police Station. Kim San-chun was the sister-in-law of the woman who had been killed hanging from the paulownia tree in the Bihaegi Hill Massacre. The police had been unaware of Kim San-chun's family relationship at that time, so she had remained safe, but the station later discovered her husband Ko Dae-gyu's evasion. Kim San-chun, carrying a three-year-old baby, was executed by firing squad at the entrance to Oedo-ri along with her child. That day in Oedo-ri, in addition to this mother and child, Kang Seon (女, 60s), Kang Gye-hyo (50s), Sin I-sin (女, 50s), Kim Jeong-bok (女, 37), Kang Jae-saeng (女, 30s), Kim Jeong (女, 30s), Kang Seon-haeng (女), and Kang Gyeong-su (20s) were sacrificed because their husbands or children had gone into hiding. Young people hiding here and there were eventually all captured and executed, and Gaesudong suffered continuous great loss.

To organize the casualty situation in Gaesudong, Ko Chang-sun, who compared household registers and genealogies one by one, said: "In a village of forty-three households and fifty-six households at the time, sixty-three people were killed." The misdeeds of the police did not end even after the situation eased. The sacrifice of one particular girl left an unhealable wound on the hearts of residents. Kim Gye-soon (金癸順), a native of Hagui-1-gu whose natal home was here, gave quite painful testimony of what she experienced at age eighteen:

In the spring following the outbreak of 4·3, from what I remember, a young woman relocated from Geumdeok-ri was brought to Hagui Police Station and received electric torture daily. The pretext was to locate her missing older brother. Unable to endure the torture, she secretly escaped and hid at the seashore, but a few days later was caught by police. The police assembled all male and female members of the Daehan Youth Corps at a field east of Hagui Elementary School and brought her there. Everyone had to become a member of the Daehan Youth Corps at that time. When she was brought before us, she was already in a near-death state. But the police stripped her naked and ordered, "Since she is a woman, the female members of the Daehan Youth Corps should come forward and pierce her with bayonets." We were horrified. Who could do such a thing? But they threatened, "If you don't pierce her, you will die instead," so the captain, a woman, came forward and pierced her first. The police ordered everyone to pierce her once. It was unbearable to watch. Before my turn came, the girl was already dead. The police rolled the corpse around and confirmed death, then told the men to dispose of it. After returning home, I was devastated—I couldn't eat. For a while afterward, I was quite ill. My cousin, who pierced her then, was later so sick she nearly died. I asked friends, and they all suffered like me. It is only natural to suffer after experiencing such things. I will forget only when I die. However, the police did something unspeakable to her because the normal act could not be done. So one friend said sadly, "If she had given her body, she might have lived."[^9]

**[p. 393]**

The victim's name was Kang Jo-soon (姜朝順), a beautiful eighteen-year-old girl at that time, known throughout the village for her beauty. However, her older brother, Kang Jo-haeng (姜朝行), the pretext for Kang Jo-soon's death, had already been taken by police at the beginning of the 4·3 outbreak. Kang Jo-haeng testified as follows:

When Shin-eom Police Station was attacked, the young people in our village had to take turns standing guard at Shin-eom Police Station. One day I went back to my hometown Geumdeok-ri (now Yusuam-ri) and was arrested without any crime. As I set out on the road, not only I but also my older brother and sister, as well as about thirty village people, were tied up and dragged out. We were taken to Shin-eom Police Station, where police brought a person who could not see out of one eye and whose body was in poor condition. They had us close our eyes and then ordered him to point. If he nodded, we would be dragged out; if not, we would remain seated. He came before me and apparently nodded. About fifty residents from Geumdeok-ri and Goseong-ri were taken to the Jeju Police Headquarters. There, we were beaten daily with batons and subjected to electric torture. The demand was to confess to participation in the attack on Shin-eom Police Station. After receiving interrogation for fifteen days in this way, they had me write something and seal it—and so I became an attacker on Shin-eom Police Station. But in April 1948, after entering a holding cell, I spent six months there without trial. In August, I developed heat rash and hives. Every time the guards gave food, they made me stick my hand through a hole and beat my palm with a club—ten blows, thirty blows a day. Later, calluses formed on my palms. Afterward, I was transferred to Gwangju Prison, where about three months later I received trial. The prosecutor dropped the case, and on December 31, 1948, I was released without conviction. However, because Jeju was in turmoil, I could not return immediately and spent about eight months before returning to Jeju in autumn 1949. But during that time, I heard that my younger brother Kang Jo-jeong (20) and younger sister Kang Jo-soon (18) had died horribly. My younger sister was quite pretty, and the police made her their plaything and killed her.[^10]

The chief and most of the police at the Oedo Police Station were from the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. Their conduct was so cruel that they recorded "Complaint No. 1" in the Jeju region during the "Civilian Massacre Truth Investigation" conducted by the National Assembly after April 19. The Jeju Sinbo, which received victim reports at the time, stated: "Even under martial law, how can there be law permitting killing with spears and swords rather than bullets—a family of ten, including a sixty-seven-year-old elder and an infant not ten days old—in one place? Moreover, there is no justification for this, nor any law anywhere that would permit such punishment." The newspaper introduced the charges as follows:

Former Jeju Police Headquarters Oedo Police Station Chief Police Lieutenant Kim Byeong-chae (金炳採) and former station patrolman Lee Yun-do (李允道) were defendants in this complaint. The "facts of the complaint" in the aforementioned complaint document are as follows: The aforementioned defendants, while serving as police at Jeju Police Headquarters Oedo Police Station, on February 17, 1949, at approximately 3 p.m., at No. 252 Oedo-1-dong, Jeju City, commonly called "behind the temple" (寺後), without legal procedure, mercilessly massacred Lee Yang-ho (李亮晧) and nine other family members with bamboo spears... (excerpt) According to the report, the sole pretext was that a son was missing from home. However, the eldest son Won-young (李完榮) had been abducted by rebels. This became the death sentence for not only his parents at No. 252 Oedo-1-dong—Lee Yang-ho (age 67 at the time) and Ko Jeong-suk (age 63)—but also his wife Ko Eui-soon (age 41), his younger brother Ki-young (age 18), his daughter Yeong-hee (age 19) and his daughter-in-law Cho Chun-ja (age 19), as well as other children Bong-hee (age 18), Ok-ja (age 7), and Ok-hee (age 3), and even a grandson ten days old—spanning four generations. It is particularly noted that young Bong-hee was disabled and unable to move, yet was brought by his mother. The ten family members were called by the station chief's order, received not a single statement, were dragged to the location commonly called "behind the temple" in Oedo-1-dong, and around 3 p.m. were massacred by bamboo spear as if they were one family group. According to documents reported by the maternal grandmother of the infant, the then Civil Defense Unit Chief Lee Sang-hun (李尚勳, age 43), resident of Oedo-2-dong, served as witness, and the names of the perpetrators and those responsible were clearly identified as Chief Police Lieutenant Kim Byeong-chae and patrolman Lee Yun-do of the Oedo Station. Investigation of the truth and governmental compensation are requested.[^11]

## Gasiri, Pyoseongmyeon

Suppression forces, operating under the premise that midland villages were bases of armed guerrillas, opened fire indiscriminately, regardless of age or gender. At dawn on November 15, 1948, as suppression forces entered Gasiri in Pyoseongmyeon, they immediately began firing. The gender and age of the approximately thirty victims killed that day reflected the tragedy of the time. While young people fled quickly, the elderly and children left behind in homes were killed. Among the elderly alone, ranging from their sixties to eighties, Kim Su-gye (女), Kim In-ha (女), Kim Jeong-suk (女), Kim Ho-jik, An Man-gyu, O Gyeong-saeng (女), O Yun-bu (女), O Hee-baek (女), Jeong Jae-byeong, and Jeong Jong-eon were killed.

That day, An Heung-gyu managed to escape quickly and saved his life. However, after the soldiers withdrew, An returned home only to witness the tragic sight of his entire family annihilated. Ko Shin-chun (女, 42), Kang Mae-chun (女, 37), An Jae-won (20), An Young-soon (女, 19), An Jae-soon (女, 15), and children not recorded in household registers—An Il-jin, An Ok-hee, and An Ok-soon—along with An's sister An Gyu-ban (age 40s) and her children Kang Jae-ho (12), Kang Soon-i (女, 7), and one of unknown name (4)—twelve people in total—had hidden in a nearby forest and lost their lives in a single morning.

An elderly couple in their sixties, An Man-gyu and Kim In-ha, fled to a stream with their three-year-old granddaughter and one-year-old grandson. They found a cave to hide in, but the baby's cries escaped, and suppression forces threw grenades into the cave. The family met their fate together. However, though soldiers are an organization that obeys orders, some refused to participate in indiscriminate killing. Ko Tae-ok (高泰玉) said: "At dawn that day when gunfire broke out, I fled quickly to the stream, but my wife remained at home with the baby. The soldier who came to our house set fire but also said, 'Other soldiers might come and kill you, so hide quickly,' and then left. Thanks to meeting a good soldier, I saved my life."[^12]

As even the elderly and weak were brutally sacrificed, residents were at a loss. Residents could neither go up the mountain nor down to the coast but remained in the village, enduring days of terror. Then, about a week later, around November 22, word came: "Resettle to the coastal village of Pyoseongri." From that point, residents scattered. Some sought connections and were resettled in Pyoseongri or Tosari-ri, but some residents, terrified, could not readily come down. Residents who missed the opportunity to come down wandered the fields and were caught in suppression forces' sweep operations and executed by firing squad.

**[p. 394]**

Meanwhile, residents relocated to Pyoseongri under the resettlement order were accommodated in Pyoseong Elementary School. However, about a month into their stay, on December 22, suppression forces assembled the accommodated residents in the sports field, then compared them one by one against household registers, dividing families based on whether all members had been resettled or not. If any family member was missing, soldiers labeled them "families of evaders" and dragged them to a field about 200 meters above what was called "Willow Pond" (버들못), where they mass executed seventy-six people. The main victims were elderly people over sixty whose children had disappeared. O Guk-man (吳國晩), who lost his father and mother that day and barely survived, testified to the situation at that time as follows:

When we were living in accommodations at Pyoseong Elementary School, soldiers came one day in large numbers and said, "Everyone gather in the sports field." It was the twenty-second day of the lunar month. When residents gathered, soldiers said, "Those families with all members according to household registers, go stand on one side!" Those whose families were not complete stood in the middle of the sports field. Our older brother was missing, so we were classified as a "family of evaders" and stood in the middle of the field. The soldiers then said, "Mothers of nursing infants and those under fifteen, come forward!" I was sixteen at that time, but my father, sensing the crisis, had originally registered my age as fourteen in the accommodation records. Thanks to that, I survived completely. That day, seventy-six people were mass executed in the field beside what is now the substation. At that time, soil was merely heaped over them, but a year later when the village was rebuilt, the bodies were buried. The village chief identified the remains of my father by the pipe case inside his hanbok (traditional Korean robe).[^13]

Meanwhile, suppression forces, while carrying out executions, forced the assembled people to applaud. O Tae-gyeong, who had resettled to Tosari-ri, testified: "Executions occurred near the Tosari-ri storehouse. They assembled people and told them to watch. They ordered us to applaud during the executions. When a baby crawled upward during an execution, soldiers also shot at the baby."[^14]

## 3) Killing of Self-Surrender Cases

Suppression forces assembled residents and threatened: "Anyone with even the slightest past wrong, surrender. If you surrender, you will be spared, but if discovered later, you will not escape execution. We already possess a 'list of the implicated.'" The fact that self-surrender was demanded meant there was no such list, but many terrified residents surrendered. They primarily confessed to activities in the Preparatory Committee for the Founding of the State (建國準備委員會) or People's Committees after liberation, participation in protests or strikes protesting the police's March 1 shooting incident of 1947, providing food and other support to armed guerrillas when they held hegemony in the village, and participation in their requested rallies. However, contrary to their promises, suppression forces mass executed self-surrender cases.

## Wahuri, Chocheonmyeon

**[p. 395]**

On November 11, 1948, armed guerrillas attacked Chocheon Police Station and retreated. Suppression forces chased them to Wahuri-1-gu and set fire to the village. At that time, residents quickly hid in places like "Wahul Cave," and there were no major casualties. However, when suppression forces surrounded Wahuri-2-gu on November 13, young men, sensing the gravity, evacuated in advance, while the elderly remained in their homes. Soldiers set fire to each house and killed residents. From a seventy-five-year-old grandmother to two- and three-year-old small children, as well as women, all victims were elderly and weak who had not fled the village in advance. After about a week, surviving residents wandered the burnt village ruins when word came to resettle to a coastal village. About two weeks after residents of midland villages resettled to coastal villages, a so-called "self-surrender incident" occurred.

At age seventeen at the time, Kim Tae-woo (金泰宇) followed the resettlement order and relocated with his family to Chocheon-ri. Kim Tae-woo testified to the process of resettlement and self-surrender as follows:

A few days after soldiers set fire to the village, word came from the police station to resettle. Before that, there had been no such talk. So people in Sugi-dong (a natural village of Wahuri-2-gu) who could not flee quickly also died. Anyway, we heard the resettlement order and went down to Chocheon-ri. After a few days, they said, "All refugees must surrender. If you surrender, we will give you a safe-conduct pass and guarantee safety, but if you don't surrender, you will all die." So I went to Hamduk, where the 9th Regiment soldiers were stationed, and surrendered. We were confined in an elementary school classroom in Hamduk, but we did not receive special interrogation. However, though I was young and avoided being taken, the young people were soon dragged to "Park Seong-nae" and executed by firing squad.[^15]

According to another witness, not only relocated residents but also coastal village residents were encouraged to surrender. "If even a hair's worth troubles your conscience, surrender," they said. Self-surrendered people would be freed, came the enticement, accompanied by the threat "We already have the 'list,'" and "If discovered later, you will not escape execution." About 200 young people in the Chocheon area "surrendered." Many of those who surrendered were Wahuri residents. Living in the midlands, they could not refuse the armed guerrillas' demands to "provide food" and "attend gatherings." Relocated residents and coastal village residents who went to Hamduk Elementary School, where soldiers were stationed, were moved to an agricultural school in Jeju town after about two weeks and then dragged to a stream called "Park Seong-nae," where they were executed. The mass execution occurred on December 21. Among the confirmed victims from Wahuri-ri killed at Park Seong-nae were Moon Myeong-guk (40), Baek Su-ok (35), Ko Chang-hun (33), Kim Won-geun (33), Baek Tae-rim (33, also known as Baek Cheon-ok), Kim Si-jin (31), Ko Tae-soon (30), Ko Gong-rim (29), Baek Jung-ok (28), Ko Yun-jong (27), Baek Sang-ok (26), Kim Seok-bong (26), Kim Ki-un (25), Kim Man-chu (25), Lee Han-bong (25), Kim Jung-ok (24), Lee Seong-bae (24), Lee Won-sik (24), Jang Byeong-ho (24), Kim Young-hwa (22), Kim Dong-ok (21), Lee Seong-il (21), Lim Ik-song (21, also known as Lim Su-song), Kim Jung-hwa (20), Park Bong-rim (20), Yang Chi-gyu (20), Lee Se-bong (20), Kang Myeong-sik (20), Lim Gyeong-song (19), Lim Jun-song (19), Hong Young-jong (19), Moon Myeong-bong (18), Yang Chi-ok (18), Yang Su-bong (20s), and others.

Kim Tae-jun (金太準) was the sole survivor who returned alive from this execution site. Because Kim Tae-jun returned alive, word of the self-surrendered persons' deaths spread, and bereaved families could at least find corpses. Kim Tae-jun testified to the circumstances at that time as follows:

It was unavoidable, but because I had once given fifteen won to the mountain, I surrendered. However, rather than having actual guilt, because they said "surrender will guarantee your life," people hiding here and there came out. We were told to gather self-surrendered persons at Hamduk Elementary School. There, without special interrogation, we spent about two weeks in tedium. Then around 5 p.m. on December 21, while saying "to patrol," they called out some of us. Others, hoping they might be freed once they returned from patrol, volunteered. I joined the volunteer ranks. Soldiers distributed one rice ball each, but soon a bus came, and those who received rice balls late were not able to get on the vehicle.[^16]

In this way, suppression forces filled the "head count" for execution without special criteria. In any case, 150 of the 200 self-surrendered persons got on the bus. It was a crossroads between life and death. Kim Tae-jun testified to the execution situation as follows:

**[p. 396]**

When we arrived at the Jeju Agricultural School, soldiers suddenly tied our hands behind our backs with wire rope, then bound us in groups of ten like dried pollock, and loaded us onto trucks. Midway, two people from Wahuri, sensing something was amiss, escaped. Wire rope was insufficient, so only they were bound with straw rope, which they untied and jumped from the moving vehicle. When we reached Park Seong-nae, the commander said, "If you all die gracefully, I will report it to your families so they can at least find your remains." Before the execution, soldiers searched our pockets and took away money and valuables. They dragged us one hundred meters away from the road to rocks above a stream, and shot ten of us at a time as we were bound, knocking us into the stream. I was hit in the left shoulder and right arm. However, without losing consciousness, in the mere three to four minutes while the next group was being dragged out, I broke the wire and hid in a rock crevice. There were people like me who could have survived, but they were discovered squirming by soldiers. Soldiers immediately poured gasoline and set them on fire. I hid until dawn and then escaped the scene. It snowed heavily that night and was bitterly cold. My bare feet developed frostbite and my tattered arm continued to bleed. Without eating anything, using "Wondang Peak" as a lighthouse and traveling only at night, I reached my hometown in five days. Upon arriving home, I fell into a coma.

This incident in which about 150 young people in their twenties were mass executed left deep wounds on remaining families. One witness relayed the story of a mother who, unable to bear the sorrow of losing a child at that time, died mentally ill, as follows:

My cousin (my uncle's son) Kim Hong-gyu (21) was taken from Hamduk Elementary School to Jeju town and killed at Park Seong-nae. At that time, the young people did not know they were going to die. I saw the truck carrying the young men pass through my village Sinchonn-ri, where Hamduk was, and the young people waved their hands from inside the truck and even sang songs. My uncle's wife went into shock at her son's death and developed mental illness. The symptoms were severe—she even jumped fences and tried to jump into water. Eventually, about a year later, she died from the shock.[^17]

## 4) Entrapment Patrol

A typical form of "entrapment patrol" was when military and police, dressed as armed guerrillas, entered private homes seeking cooperation and executed those who responded. People who lived through that period unanimously used the expression, "It was a world where both bears and tigers were frightening"—both soldiers and police were frightening, and armed guerrillas were too. During the day, military and police controlled villages and executed "suspect rebels"; at night when they withdrew, armed guerrillas entered to "purge reactionaries." In such circumstances, ordinary residents could not refuse the guerrillas' demands. Yet military and police set traps to root out supporters of armed guerrillas.

## Dopyeongri, Jeju City

The mass victimization of Dopyeongri residents in Jeju City was a typical case of entrapment patrol. On the morning of January 3, 1949, a group of men in shabby ramie clothes, carrying rifles, descended on Dopyeongri, Jeju City. They greeted residents they met on the road with "comrade, comrade" and shook hands, and some carried red flags. They entered homes demanding, "Why haven't you cooperated with the mountain?" and assembled residents in the school sports field. However, this group in red flags and ramie clothes was not armed guerrillas but police from the nearby Oedo Police Station and special forces, who set a trap for residents. But some residents among the group recognized familiar faces and made desperate efforts not to fall into the trap. Yang Gyeong-ha (梁京河, 22) spoke up: "If you are reds, I will fight back." Village elder Kim Byeong-hae (金秉海, 58) cursed the Oedo Police Station Chief Kim Young-chul (金永哲) and shouted, "Long live the Republic of Korea!" However, including these men, about seventy residents were executed by firing squad. One witness testified: "An elderly man named Kim Seong-gyu, over sixty, was singled out in the school sports field, and he, his wife, son, daughter-in-law, and young grandson—six people total—were annihilated."[^18]

## 5) Massacre of Those Fleeing to the Mountains

When midland villages were set on fire, many people fled to the mountains, having lost their homes. Particularly, residents of villages that were attacked and set on fire by suppression forces even before the resettlement order was fully conveyed had nowhere to go and hid in caves and forests in the midland regions. Moreover, the continuous "families of evaders" massacres in coastal villages caused even those who had relocated to flee to the mountains again. Thus, during the strong suppression operations, people who hid in the mountains, enduring the terror of death not knowing when they would be captured and the bitter winter cold, were discovered during military and police sweep operations of mountain regions and executed by firing squad. Based solely on gender and age, it was immediately clear that these were not armed guerrillas, but suppression forces carried out summary executions without such consideration. And these victims became the "war gains" (戰果) of the suppression forces.

## Yonggangri, Jeju City

**[p. 397]**

At around 10 a.m. on November 20, 1948, soldiers entering Yonggangri, Jeju City, roamed through every corner of the village, setting fires. This was the opening of strong suppression operations against Yonggangri. The place residents fled to that day was somewhere about 10 kilometers away from the village called "Saetmeoheul." Soldiers pursued them, but they dared not enter the dense forest formed by tangled brambles and rock piles. However, as soldiers sprayed machine gun fire from the forest entrance, two residents died and one sustained a gun wound to the arm. Though the village became charred ruins, compared to what happened in other midland villages at that time, the casualties were very few.

Yonggangri was a deep mountain village and thus long remained under the influence of armed guerrillas. Residents felt the direction of events when coastal village people came in large numbers to refuse voting in the May 10 elections. Around that time, some 9th Regiment soldiers who had deserted passed through Yonggangri and went up to the mountains, which greatly affected residents. Armed guerrillas proclaimed, "Even soldiers are on our side," greatly displaying their prestige, and about ten young people became guerrilla members. Moreover, Yonggangri was located at a gateway to mountain regions and was a place armed guerrillas passed through when attacking coastal villages. However, as operations intensified in autumn, residents constantly prepared to flee, and watching "bitgae" (sentries) became the most important task. Military personnel made no distinction between armed guerrillas and ordinary residents. Anyone remaining in Yonggangri was considered an enemy of suppression forces.

After the village burned completely, residents could not leave. Bereft of options, they built shelters on the burnt homesteads and lived, waiting only for the crisis to end. But no sign of the situation easing appeared. The life-and-death game of breath-holding between suppression forces and residents continued. When residents received warning signals from sentries, they fled; when suppression forces came up empty, they burned the shelters and left. Residents rebuilt, and when these burned, they rebuilt again. Up to this point, Yonggangri had not received a resettlement order. However, some residents fled the turmoil by resettling to coastal villages like Hwabuk-ri or Samyang-ri. Of course, young men did not resettle; only women and children set out on resettlement. But most elderly people who relocated to coastal villages had to return to the village in flight. In coastal villages, "families of evaders" or "resettled persons" were constantly assembled for mass executions. Ko Shin-jong (高信鍾), age thirteen at the time when his family relocated to Hwabuk-ri, said: "Though there was no formal resettlement order, after our house burned, we relocated to Hwabuk-ri. But hearing that 'people coming from the mountain will all be captured and killed,' we returned to the village again."[^19]

Afterward, though occasional incidents of individuals being caught and killed during sudden military incursions occurred, residents did not experience collective massacre until the end of December 1948, when the 9th Regiment was stationed. How were residents able to remain relatively safe despite continuous daily patrol operations? Above all, it was due to "information." Though residents maintained thorough self-surveillance with sentries on high hills, without information, they could not have perfectly evaded suppression forces that appeared suddenly. Information came from coastal village residents stationed with military and police. Coastal village residents were compulsorily enrolled in Civil Defense Units or Youth Corps and mobilized for suppression operations, enabling them to access information. However, on February 4, 1949 (the 7th day of the first lunar month), a nightmarish day dawned for Yonggangri residents.

**[p. 398]**

Early that morning, soldiers descended, and residents began fleeing in panic. But Yonggangri had few places to hide. Today when one visits Yonggangdong, pine and cryptomeria trees obscure the view, and in some areas thick undergrowth fills the landscape. However, according to residents' accounts, at that time there were only fields, and everything was visible at a glance. The few places with trees were "Dangkarum" and a stream area called "Daeryeonsu" with small holly trees. Residents, driven by instinct, mostly fled toward Dangkarum and Daeryeonsu. However, soldiers pursued them and shot residents indiscriminately regardless of gender or age. Moreover, most victims that day were elderly and weak who could not flee quickly, adding to the tragedy. 105 Yonggangri residents were killed, and neighboring villages Bonggae-ri and Hoecheon-ri experienced the same circumstances. For this reason, on the memorial service date—the sixth day of the first lunar month—Bonggae, Yonggang, and Hoecheon-ri are bustling like holidays as most families prepare memorial services.

Regarding that day's circumstances, the Ministry of National Defense announced: "On the aforementioned (February) 4, at 3 a.m., joint operations of Army, Navy, and Air Force were deployed under the command of Commander Ham Byeong-seon (咸炳善) in the Bonggae district, Jeju City, engaging in fierce combat with armed rebels and thugs. Enemy dead: 360, prisoners: 130, other foodstuffs and clothing captured." It claimed "360 enemies slain" and "130 prisoners captured," yet the items confiscated included hardly a single firearm, only foodstuffs and clothing, demonstrating how hollow the claim of engaging in "fierce combat with armed rebels" actually was.[^20]

However, why did residents who had long succeeded in evading capture suffer collective massacre that day? Regarding this, Ko Yun-seop (高潤燮), who survived gunshot wounds that day, testified: "That day, soldiers drove people like rabbits from the south near Wolpyeong-ri and from the north near Samyang and Hamduk-ri. Especially, soldiers lay in ambush and shot fleeing people. My older brother died instantly fleeing toward Chiloreum. I fled toward Sonang Cave (near the current landfill site) with about 100 others, but soldiers lying in ambush there opened fire, killing seventy to eighty people on that spot alone. Most were women."[^21]

Meanwhile, Song Ki-jeon (宋基全), who lost his grandmother and young brother's family that day, testified to that day as follows:

That day, information came up in urgency. So the elderly and weak who could not run fast went to "Dangkarum" or "Daeryeonsu" in the village, while young people, as always, ran eastward. This was because soldiers were stationed to the west (in Jukseong village, Odeung-ri). But that day both the garrison troops in Jukseong village and soldiers from the nearby Hamduk-ri garrison came up. So we were completely surrounded. We might have escaped if we could have gotten past the Bonggae-ri Chiloreum, but the elderly who could not run well were caught and died in the middle. I ran to the east of Bonggae-ri and hid in a bamboo grove, saving my life, but that day my grandmother, uncle, and young brother died. When I returned to the village, there were corpses everywhere. In small rock crevices along Daeryeonsu, many elderly who had hidden their heads were found dead, and at Dangkarum, there were corpses hanging from tree branches, pierced by bullets as they had climbed trees. I was drafted into the Marine Corps as the 14th class during the Korean War and fought many battles. Many people probably died by my bullets. But in those battles, we faced each other with weapons in direct combat; it was not like the 4·3 incident with its tragic situation of indiscriminate massacre of the elderly and weak.[^22]

After the 2nd Regiment conducted operations that day and began stationing in the nearby Bonggae-ri, surviving residents could no longer hide near the village. Thereafter, residents retreated deeper into the mountains. Residents came down from the mountains between late March and early April 1949. While some testimony held that they could no longer endure hunger, the most important catalyst for most refugees to come down was "information that things were no longer being killed." This occurred when Colonel Yoo Jae-heung's Jeju District Combat Command Headquarters conducted so-called "civic pacification operations." Earlier residents who had come down went back up to the mountains to inform later residents of these developments, and only then did others come down. This reveals how reckless the collective massacres that had occurred earlier were.

## "Billoemot Cave" Massacre

The fate of a village during the 4·3 Incident was largely determined by its location. Residents of coastal villages where military and police were stationed had no choice but to obey their orders; residents of midland villages where armed guerrillas had long held sway could not refuse their demands. Eoumlri, located in the midland regions of Aewolmyeon, could not escape this pattern. Residents carried out "watssa" (watage solidarity tax) according to guerrilla demands and sent up collected foodstuffs. Some young people formally entered the mountains and became guerrilla members. When suppression forces came up during the day, they failed to find the young people and merely beat the innocent residents before leaving. As was commonly said, "by day it was the suppression forces' world; by night it was the guerrillas' world." The police beatings around this time were so severe that resident Yang Chuk-saeng (梁丑生, 女, 59) lost her life. Her son Kang Tae-ho testified to the circumstances of his mother's death as follows:

**[p. 399]**

My parents had eight sons and one daughter, but they died one after another from illness, and at that time only we siblings remained. My father died when I was five. My mother urged us siblings to hide, saying, "Shouldn't you preserve your lives?" I dug under a stone wall and hid there. At that time, everyone hid in such ways. Then one day suppression forces descended. They demanded of my mother, "Tell us where your husband and children are hidden." When my mother stood firm saying, "I am a woman living alone," she immediately received merciless beatings. The place where I was hiding was near the house, so I heard my mother's screams piercing my ears. Yet I could not come out. After the suppression forces left, I hurried over to find my mother unconscious—it was unbearable to witness. As a child unable to help while my mother reached such a state, I was overcome with frustration and uncontrollable tears. My mother, unable to eat or speak properly, suffered for half a year before dying.[^23]

From around October 1948, police began stationing in Eoumlri. About twenty railway police set up camp at the site of the former village school and began suppression operations. From that point, the appearance of armed guerrillas disappeared from the village, but railway police immediately opened fire at any sign of young people. Around November 15, 1948, suppression forces issued a resettlement order for Eoumlri. Then young people who had been hidden in or near homes appeared one by one. In circumstances where suppression forces indiscriminately beat and shot, with no escape, for young people who had been hiding in terror, the resettlement order to "relocate to a coastal village" was like a guideline for survival. However, when residents gathered, suppression forces called out about ten young people from the "list" they carried, beat them until they were near death, dragged them to Hanrim Elementary School, and five days later, on November 20, executed them by firing squad. The resettlement appears to have proceeded over about three days beginning around November 15. One resident, based on that period's household memorial service date, remembered resettlement being completed on November 18. Suppression forces carried out resettlement operations by having residents set fire to their own homes and burning the entire village completely.

Some young people, taking the resettlement order as an opportunity, came out of hiding and relocated to coastal villages, but many people missed the opportunity. Especially households with evaders among family members could not come down. During the soon-arriving winter, they hid here and there, struggling for survival, and died. Moreover, because "families of evaders" were constantly massacred in coastal villages, people from households whose husbands or children had not relocated were forced to flee back to the village, only to be caught and sacrificed. Related to this, the massacre that occurred at "Billoemot Cave" is widely known not only among Eoumlri residents but also to residents of nearby villages for its brutality. So-called "families of evaders" whose young family members had entered the mountains, or people who had drawn suppression forces' attention, hid in caves that were discovered, resulting in collective massacre. These victims, mainly elderly and women—chiefly the weak—entered the cave following folk beliefs and oral traditions about the cave. The saying went: "During chaos, neither mountain nor sea" (山不近海不近), or "this place can hide as many as thirty people"—such stories were spread through the village. Billoemot Cave, with a total length of 11,749 meters as a single-system lava cave, was said to be the world's longest and was designated as Natural Monument No. 342. The Billoemot Cave in Eoumlri's region was near grazing land and fields residents frequented, but until 4·3, its existence was virtually unknown except to a few people. A narrow entrance barely allowing one person to pass was blocked by a rock. Though the cave's warmth caused mist to rise like smoke in winter, residents only knew of small "gwe" (grottos) scattered nearby and did not realize such a large cave existed. In any case, the cave was discovered on January 16, 1949, and suppression forces massacred all inside regardless of age or gender. Particularly, there is a story, circulating through civil defense members mobilized for suppression operations at the time, that police seized small children's legs and smashed their heads against rocks, a story that continues to be recounted in the area as a symbol of tragedy to this day. However, how was the Billoemot Cave, with its narrow, inconspicuous entrance, discovered, and if people had retreated deep enough, might they have survived? Why were all killed? Yang Tae-byeong (梁泰柄), the sole survivor who hid in Billoemot Cave at that time, testified as follows:

When the resettlement order came down, I took my mother to the neighboring village of Napeung-ri. There we stood watch every night, but I was not given a watch assignment. Finding it strange, I asked why and was told, "You joined the Minaechung [Civil Defense], so there is no need for you to stand watch." Uneasy, a week later when the resettlement order came to Napeung-ri as well, we moved again to Aewol-ri. But again, I was not told to stand watch. And a rumor circulated that members of Minaechung would soon be executed. I was surprised to be called a Minaechung member because, having lost my father young and living with only my mother with difficulty, I had done nothing at all. The list seemed to have been arbitrarily created—with such methods, there would be no young person not caught. In any case, I could not just sit and wait to die, so I fled. Around that time, I discovered Billoemot Cave and hid there. There were twenty-eight residents from Napeung-ri, and from our village were the family of Kang Gyu-nam (mother, wife, son, daughter, sister—five people), Song Si-yeong and his family, Yang Sin-ha, and others.

**[p. 400]**

We all felt reassured by the narrow, secluded entrance, but I remained tense and kept in mind a place we could flee to if necessary. Eventually the cave was discovered. It was probably due to the mist-like steam rising outside the cave. When soldiers, police, suppression troops, and civil defense members entered, we hid quickly. However, when the suppression forces said, "We will spare your lives, so do not worry," everyone came out. Once the suppression forces understood who was inside, they called out my name through a person they had captured, telling me to come out. However, I remained silent and hiding. The suppression forces immediately took people out and massacred them right at the cave entrance. Kang Gyu-nam's son and Song Si-yeong's son were only three or four years old—children so beautiful and fine that they were famous in the neighborhood. The suppression forces seized the children by the legs and smashed their heads against rocks. As a human being, one cannot do such a thing. The killers surely did not die well because of this crime. Meanwhile, Kang Gyu-nam's wife fled while carrying a two- or three-year-old daughter, but unlike me, retreated deep into the cave, became lost, could not escape, and died of starvation. The cave is so large and complex that entering the wrong way results in becoming lost. These remains were later discovered through cave exploration team excavation, as I recall.[^24]

Besides Eoumlri residents, the cave also held numerous residents from nearby villages—Napeung-ri, Jangjeon-ri, and Sangkwi-ri. From Napeung-ri, the family of Hyeon Byeong-gu—Hyeon Yong-seung (75), his wife (75), Byeon Yong-ok (女, 28, Hyeon Byeong-gu's wife), and Hyeon Byeong-gu's son (1)—were annihilated. Also, Yang Ki-won (女, 67), Jin Seung-hee (54), Kim Jeong-hyeon (53), Hyeon Won-hak (51), Hyeon Won-hak's wife (50s), Hyeon Gyu-chil (33), Hyeon Gyu-chil's wife (30s), An In-mu (29), and from Sangkwi-ri, Jin Gwan-haeng (80s), and from Jangjeon-ri, Byeon Jeong-ok (女, 29) and her two young children (5 years, 3 years)—all were captured and sacrificed together. Most were elderly and weak who had hidden to escape the chaos, but suppression forces killed them indiscriminately regardless of gender or age.

**[p. 401]**

A witness testified to the process leading up to it in the following manner: When several days had passed since soldiers set the village on fire, we received word from the police substation that we were to be evacuated. Before that, there had been no such order. As a result, some people who could not flee quickly from Sugi-dong (a natural hamlet in Ward 2 of Wah'ul) died. In any case, hearing the evacuation order, we went down to Chojŏn-ri. But after a few days, we heard: "All refugees must surrender. If you surrender, you will receive a security certificate guaranteeing your safety, but if you do not surrender, you will all be executed." So I too went to Hamdo'ck, where the 9th Regiment soldiers were stationed, and surrendered. We were confined in classrooms at Hamdo'ck Elementary School, but we were not subjected to any special interrogation. However, while I was young and avoided it, the young men were quickly taken to "Pak Sŏng-nae" and shot to death.[^1] According to another witness, they encouraged not only evacuees but also residents of seaside villages to surrender. They said, "If there is anything at all, even hair-thin, that troubles your conscience, surrender." The enticement was that those who surrendered would be free, accompanied by the threat: "Lists have already been secured," and "If you are discovered later, you will not escape execution." About 200 young men in Chojŏn-myŏn surrendered. Among the surrenderers were many residents of Wah'ul. Living in the midmountain region, they could not avoid the armed guerrillas' demands to "provide food" and "attend meetings." Those who surrendered at Hamdo'ck National Elementary School, the military outpost, were transferred to an agricultural school in Cheju City proper about half a month later, and were soon taken to a stream called "Pak Sŏng-nae" and shot to death. This was a mass execution that took place on December 21. Among the victims from Wah'ul confirmed to have died at Pak Sŏng-nae were: Mun Myŏng-guk (40), Paek Su-ok (35), Koh Chang-hun (33), Kim Wŏn-gŭn (33), Paek T'ae-rim (33, alternate name Paek Ch'ŏn-ok), Kim Si-jin (31), Koh T'ae-sun (30), Koh Gong-nim (29), Paek Chung-ok (28), Koh Yun-jong (27), Paek Sang-ok (26), Kim Sŏk-pong (26), Kim Ki-un (25), Kim Man-ch'u (25), Yi Han-bong (25), Kim Chung-ok (24), Yi Sŏng-bae (24), Yi Wŏn-sik (24), Chang Pyŏng-ho (24), Kim Yŏng-hwa (22), Kim Dong-ok (21), Yi Sŏng-il (21), Im Ik-song (21, alternate name Im Su-song), Kim Chung-hwa (20), Pak Pong-nim (20), Yang Ch'i-gyu (20), Yi Se-bong (20), Kang Myŏng-sik (20), Im Kyŏng-song (19), Im Chun-song (19), Hong Yŏng-jong (19), Mun Myŏng-bong (18), Yang Ch'i-ok (18), and Yang Su-bong (20s). Kim T'ae-jun was the only survivor to come out alive from this execution site. Because Kim T'ae-jun returned alive, word of the deaths of the surrenderers was transmitted, and bereaved families were able to find the bodies. Kim T'ae-jun testified to the situation at that time in the following manner.

[^1]: Kim T'ae-u (age 71, Wah'ul-ri, Chojŏn-eŭp; recorded Sept. 26, 2001), testimony.

---

**[p. 402]**

Though it was unavoidable, I had previously given 15 wŏn to support the guerrillas on the mountain, so I surrendered. But rather than because there was any real crime, the reason people hiding here and there came out was that "if you surrender, your life will be guaranteed." The surrenderers were ordered to gather at Hamdo'ck National Elementary. There, without any special interrogation, we spent about two weeks in boredom. In the meantime, on December 21 at around 5 p.m., they called out names saying "we're going on suppression operations." Also, some went of their own accord, hoping they might be allowed to go free if they accompanied the suppression forces. I joined the volunteer contingent too. Soldiers distributed rice balls to each of us, but a bus arrived so suddenly that those who received their rice balls late could not board the vehicle.[^2] In this way, the suppression forces filled their "quota" of those to be executed without any particular standard. In any case, 150 out of the 200 surrenderers boarded the bus as if by fate. It was a fork in the road between life and death. Kim T'ae-jun testified to the execution scene as follows:

When we arrived at the Cheju Agricultural School, soldiers suddenly tied our hands behind our backs with wire rope, then bundled us together like dried fish, ten at a time, and loaded us onto three-ton trucks. Midway, two people from Wah'ul who sensed that something was seriously wrong attempted to escape. There was a shortage of wire rope, so they alone were bound with straw rope, which they untied and then jumped from the moving vehicle. When we reached Pak Sŏng-nae, the commander said, "If you die gracefully, I will inform your families and let them find your bodies." Before shooting, soldiers rummaged through our pockets and stripped us of our money and valuables. They dragged us to the top of a rock about 100 meters away from the roadside beside the stream, and shot those bound in ten-person units and threw them into the water. I was struck in my left shoulder and right arm. But I did not lose consciousness, and in the mere three or four minutes it took for the next group to be brought, I cut through the wire and hid in a crevice between rocks. There were other people like me who could have lived, but they were discovered writhing about by soldiers. The soldiers immediately poured gasoline on them and set them on fire. I hid until dawn and then escaped the scene. It was snowing heavily then and terribly cold; my bare feet got frostbite and my tattered arms kept bleeding. Unable to eat anything, I used "Wŏn-dang Peak" as a beacon and, using only the night hours, reached my hometown in five days. When I arrived home, I immediately fell into unconsciousness.

The mass execution of about 150 young men left deep scars on the remaining families. One witness conveyed the story of a mother who, unable to bear the grief of losing her son at this time, died while suffering from mental illness in the following manner:

[^2]: Kim T'ae-jun (Shin-ch'on-ri, Chojŏn-eŭp), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Group, *The Jeju 4·3 Incident Speaks*, vol. ④, pp. 406–408).

---

**[p. 403]**

My cousin (father's second wife's son), Kim Hong-gyu (21), was taken from Hamdo'ck National Elementary to Cheju City and was executed at Pak Sŏng-nae. At that time, the young men did not know they were being taken to their deaths. I saw the truck carrying the young men pass through our village of Shin-ch'on-ri from Hamdo'ck-ri, and the young men inside waved their hands and even sang songs. My uncle's wife was shocked by the death of her son and suffered a mental breakdown. She exhibited severe symptoms, jumping over fences and attempting to jump into water. About a year later, she died from the shock.[^3]

**4) Decoy Operations**

The typical form of "decoy operations" was when soldiers and police, dressed as armed guerrillas, entered civilian homes asking for cooperation, then executed those who responded. People who experienced that time repeatedly use the expression "a world where you fear both bears and tigers"—meaning both soldiers and police were frightening, and the armed guerrillas were frightening. During the day, soldiers and police controlled the villages, executing those they labeled "bandit suspects"; at night, when soldiers and police withdrew, the armed guerrillas arrived and "liquidated" those they called "reactionaries." In such circumstances, ordinary residents could not refuse the armed guerrillas' demands. Yet soldiers and police set traps as a means of finding supporters of the armed guerrillas.

□ **Case of Topyŏng-ri, Cheju City**

The mass sacrifice of residents in Topyŏng-ri of Cheju City is a representative case of decoy operations. On the morning of January 3, 1949, a ragged group in worn military uniforms and carrying rifles entered Topyŏng-ri. They greeted residents they encountered on the street with "comrade, comrade," offering their hands to shake, and some carried the red flag. They burst into homes demanding, "Why don't you cooperate with those on the mountain?" and assembled residents at the school athletic field. But this group carrying the red flag and wearing worn military uniforms was not armed guerrillas but rather police from the nearby Waedo Substation and special operations personnel—they had set a trap for the residents. However, some residents recognized familiar faces among the group and made desperate efforts not to fall for the trap. Yang Kyŏng-ha (22) stepped forward saying, "If you're communists, I'll fight back." Kim Pyŏng-hae (58), a village elder, cursed at Kim Yŏng-ch'ŏl (58), the Waedo Substation chief, shouting "Long live the Republic of Korea!" But about 70 residents, including these two, were executed.

[^3]: Kim T'ae-gyu (age 66, Ikao-ku, Ōsaka-shi, Japan; recorded Dec. 26, 2002), testimony.

---

**[p. 404]**

One witness testified, "Kim Sŏng-gyu, a man over sixty, was targeted on the school athletic field, and six people—his wife, son, daughter-in-law, and young grandchildren—were all killed together."[^4]

**5) Killing of Mountain Refugees**

When midmountain villages were set on fire, many people lost their homes and fled to the mountains. In particular, in villages where suppression forces arrived and burned and shot before evacuation orders could be communicated, residents had nowhere to go and hid themselves in caves and forests in the midmountain regions. Moreover, the executions of "fugitive families" that occurred daily in seaside villages drove even the already evacuated residents to flee back into the mountains. Thus, people who had fled to the mountains during the fierce suppression campaign and endured the terror of death at any moment and the winter cold, hiding out, were discovered during soldiers' search operations in mountainous areas and executed. Merely from the gender and age of the mountain refugees, it was immediately evident that they were not armed guerrillas, but the suppression forces executed them on the spot without investigating the circumstances. And these victims were converted into the suppression forces' "combat results."

□ **Case of Yong-gang-ri, Cheju City**

On the morning of November 20, 1948, at around 10 a.m., soldiers who entered Yong-gang-ri in Cheju City set fires in every corner of the village. It was the beginning of fierce suppression operations against Yong-gang-ri. That day, the place residents desperately fled to was a location called "Saet-mŏr'ŭl," about 10 kilometers from the village. Soldiers pursued them, but dared not enter the Saet-mŏr'ŭl area, which was overgrown with dense forest tangled with thorny underbrush and boulders. However, as soldiers fired machine guns from the entrance to the forest, two residents died and one was wounded in the arm. Although the village had turned to ashes, compared to what happened in other midmountain villages at that time, the casualties were quite few. Yong-gang-ri was a deep mountain village that had been under the influence of the armed guerrillas for some time. Residents came to sense the direction of events when, during the May 10 elections, large numbers of seaside village residents who refused to vote came flooding in. Some 9th Regiment soldiers who had deserted around that time passed through Yong-gang-ri to the mountains, a fact that greatly affected the residents. The armed guerrillas boasted, "Even soldiers are on our side," and about ten young men from the village became armed guerrillas.

[^4]: Kim Se-wŏn (Topyŏng-dong, Cheju City; son of victim Kim Pyŏng-hae), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Group, *The Jeju 4·3 Incident Speaks*, issue 445, May 21, 1999).

---

**[p. 405]**

Also, because Yong-gang-ri was located at a gateway to mountainous areas, it served as a passage through which the armed guerrillas would pass when attacking seaside villages. But as autumn arrived and suppression operations intensified, residents were constantly prepared to flee, and their most important task became watching the "sentries." Soldiers made no distinction whatsoever between armed guerrillas and ordinary residents. Anyone remaining in Yong-gang-ri was considered an enemy of the suppression forces. After the village had completely burned to ashes, residents could not leave. With nowhere to go, residents built makeshift shelters on the burned-out homesites and waited for the conflict to end. But there were no signs of the situation improving. The life-and-death game of catch-me-if-you-can between suppression forces and residents continued. Residents would flee at the signal from sentries; frustrated suppression forces would return having found only empty shelters. Residents would build shelters again, and when those burned, they would build again. Even at this time, no evacuation order had been issued in Yong-gang-ri. However, some residents evacuated to seaside villages such as Hwa-puk-ri or Samyang-ri to avoid the conflict. Of course, young men did not evacuate; women and children took the evacuation route. But most elderly and infirm people who evacuated to seaside villages were forced to flee back to the village. This was because evacuees and "fugitive families" were frequently assembled in seaside villages for mass executions. Koh Sin-jong (13 years old at the time), who evacuated with his family to Hwa-puk-ri, said: "There was no evacuation order, but we evacuated to Hwa-puk-ri after our house burned. But we heard that they were going to catch and execute all the people who came from the mountains, so we returned to the village again."[^5]

After that, though there were occasional incidents in which people were captured and killed when soldiers made surprise attacks, until the end of December 1948, when the 9th Regiment was stationed, there were no incidents of residents being sacrificed in groups. What allowed residents to remain safe for some time despite the ceaseless suppression operations was, above all, "information." Though residents maintained thorough vigilance by stationing sentries on high hills, without information it would have been impossible to completely avoid suppression forces arriving suddenly. The information came from residents of seaside villages, the military and police quarters. Residents of seaside villages were obliged to join the Civil Defense Units or youth groups and were mobilized for suppression operations, so they were able to obtain information.

But on February 4, 1949 (the 7th day of the first lunar month), a nightmarish day dawned for Yong-gang-ri residents. At dawn on this day, when soldiers arrived, residents began to flee in panic. However, there were not many places to hide in Yong-gang-ri. If one visits Yong-gang-dong today, pine and cedar trees block the view, and in some areas mixed trees are dense. But according to residents' accounts, at that time it was all fields—one could see in all directions with a single glance. The only places with even small amounts of vegetation were a place called "T'ang-k'a-rŭm" and a stream called "T'ae-ryŏn-su" where small evergreen shrubs grew. Residents instinctively fled mostly toward T'ang-k'a-rŭm and T'ae-ryŏn-su. But soldiers pursued them and shot them without regard to age or gender. Moreover, the victims that day were mostly elderly and infirm people and women who could not flee quickly enough, adding to the horror. One hundred and five residents of Yong-gang-ri were killed, and neighboring villages of Pong-gae-ri and Hoe-ch'ŏn-ri experienced the same situation. Thus, on the 6th day of the first lunar month (the memorial day), in Pong-gae, Yong-gang, and Hoe-ch'ŏn-ri, most households are busy preparing for memorial rites, as if it were a holiday.

[^5]: Koh Sin-jong (age 66, Yong-gang-dong, Cheju City; recorded July 12, 2001), testimony.

---

**[p. 406]**

Regarding the situation on that day, the Defense Ministry announced: "On the (2nd) 4th above, at 3 a.m., joint Army, Navy, and Air Force operations have been deployed under the command of Colonel Ham Pyŏng-sŏn in the Pong-gae district of Cheju City, and fierce fighting is taking place with the armed bandits," reporting "combat results" of "360 killed, 130 captured, and numerous other items of food and clothing confiscated."[^6] They killed 360 and captured 130, yet the confiscated items included virtually no firearms—only food and clothing—which reveals how false the announcement of "fierce fighting with the armed bandits" was. But why, when residents had been managing to evade capture until that day, were so many suddenly captured and mass executed? Regarding this, Koh Yun-sŏp, who was wounded by gunfire that day but survived by a miracle, testified: "That day, from the southeast at Wŏl-pyŏng-ri and from the north at Samyang and Hamdo'ck areas and such, soldiers herded the village people like driving rabbits. In particular, they had already lain in ambush and shot at fleeing people. My older brother fled toward Ch'il-o'rŭm Peak and was instantly killed; I ran toward So-nang-gul (near the current landfill), and about 100 people fled together. But soldiers lying in ambush there opened fire all at once, and 70 to 80 people were killed on the spot. Most were women."[^7]

Meanwhile, Songgi-jŏn, who lost his grandmother and young brother and other family members that day, testified as follows:

That day, urgent information came up. So the elderly and infirm who could not run quickly went toward "T'ang-k'a-rŭm" or "T'ae-ryŏn-su" in the village, while young people ran east as always. This was because soldiers were stationed to the west (in Chuksung Village, Ot'ŭng-ri). But that day, not only the occupying troops in Chuksŏng village but also soldiers from Hamdo'ck-ri to the lower village came up. So we were completely surrounded. We might have lived if we could have gotten past Ch'il-o'rŭm in Pong-gae-ri, but elderly and infirm people who could not run well were caught and killed in between. I ran to the east of Pong-gae-ri and hid in a bamboo grove, saving my life, but that day my grandmother, uncle, and younger brother died.

[^6]: Chosŏn Chungang Ilbo, February 9, 1949.

[^7]: Koh Yun-sŏp (age 75, Pong-gae-dong, Cheju City; recorded June 29, 2001), testimony.

---

**[p. 407]**

When I returned to the village, there were bodies everywhere. In small rock crevices along T'ae-ryŏn-su, many elderly and infirm people had died with their heads wedged in; at T'ang-k'a-rŭm, there were also bodies of people who had climbed into trees but were shot and then hung from branches. I enlisted in the Navy as the 14th class during the Korean War and engaged in many battles. Many people probably died from my gunfire. But then, we were in combat exchanging fire with each other—it was not the brutal, indiscriminate slaughter of even elderly and infirm people as happened during the 4·3 incident.[^8]

After the 2nd Regiment conducted operations that day, it began to station itself in the neighboring village of Pong-gae-ri, so residents who survived could no longer hide near the village. Afterward, residents retreated deeper into the mountains. Residents came down from the mountains between late March and early April 1949. Though some testified that they came down because they could no longer endure hunger, the most important factor that caused most refugees to come down was information saying "they won't kill us indiscriminately anymore." This was when Colonel Yu Jae-hung's Cheju Island District Combat Headquarters launched so-called "psychological operations." When people who had come down earlier went back up the mountain and informed residents of these facts, they finally came down. This demonstrates how reckless the mass executions that had previously occurred were.

□ **"Billé-mŏt-gul" Execution Case**

During the Jeju 4·3 Incident, the fate of a village was largely determined by its location. Residents of seaside villages where soldiers and police were stationed had no choice but to follow their orders, and residents of midmountain villages where the armed guerrillas held sway for a time could not refuse the guerrillas' demands. Ŏ-ŭm-ri, situated in the midmountain area of Aewŏl-myŏn, could not escape this pattern. Residents conducted "field inspections" in response to the armed guerrillas' demands and sent up harvested grain. Some young men were mobilized into the mountains to become armed guerrillas. When suppression forces came up during the day, unable to find the young men, they only beat innocent residents before returning. As is commonly said, it was "a world of suppression forces by day, armed guerrillas by night." Around this time, police beatings were so severe that resident Yang Ch'uk-saeng[^9]

[^8]: Song Gi-jŏn (Yong-gang-dong, Cheju City), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Group, *The Jeju 4·3 Incident Speaks*, issue 455, Aug. 20, 1999).

[^9]: Yang Ch'uk-saeng (female, 59).

---

**[p. 408]**

lost her life. Kang T'ae-ho, Yang Ch'uk-saeng's son, testified to the circumstances of his mother's death as follows:

My parents had eight sons and one daughter, but they died one after another from illness, so at that time only my siblings and I remained. My father died when I was five years old. My mother urged us brothers to hide quickly, saying, "Don't you need to preserve your lives?" I dug out a space under a stone wall and hid there. At that time, everyone hid that way. But one day suppression forces arrived. The suppression forces pressed my mother, saying, "Tell us where your husband and children are hiding." When my mother insisted, "I am a woman living alone," she was subjected to brutal beatings. The place where I hid was close to the house, so I could hear my mother's screams piercing my ears. But I could not come out. After the suppression forces left, I quickly went to check on her, but my mother was unconscious and it was unbearable to look upon. Thinking that I could do nothing despite being her son, as her situation reached such a state, my chest tightened and I could not control my flowing tears. My mother, unable to eat or speak properly, suffered for six months and then passed away.[^10]

Around October 1948, police began to station in Ŏ-ŭm-ri. About twenty railroad police established a base at the site of an old village school and launched suppression operations. From this time on, the presence of armed guerrillas disappeared from the village, but the railroad police shot immediately at the sight of any young person. Around November 15, 1948, suppression forces issued an evacuation order for Ŏ-ŭm-ri. Thereupon, young men who had been hiding in houses and nearby areas appeared one by one. In a situation where suppression forces beat and shot indiscriminately, and unable to move in any direction, young men who had been hiding in fear received the "evacuation order" to move to seaside villages as guidance for how to preserve their lives. But when residents gathered, suppression forces called out about ten young men from their own "lists" and beat them nearly to death before taking them to Hallim National School, where they were executed five days later, on November 20. The evacuation appears to have proceeded over about three days starting around November 15. One resident remembered that evacuation was completed by November 18, based on a memorial rite scheduled for that day. Suppression forces conducted the evacuation operation while forcing residents to set their own homes on fire and burning down the entire village.

[^10]: Kang T'ae-ho (age 73, Kŭmsŏng-ri, Aewŏl-eŭp), testimony (Chemin Ilbo 4·3 Reporting Group, *The Jeju 4·3 Incident Speaks*, issue 428, Oct. 16, 1998).

---

**[p. 409]**

Some young men came out from where they had been hiding and descended to seaside villages as a result of the evacuation order, but many people missed the opportunity. In particular, households that had family members in hiding could not come down. During the coming winter, they hid in various places, struggling for survival and dying. Moreover, because "fugitive families" were frequently assembled in seaside villages for mass execution, people from households whose husbands or sons had not evacuated fled back to the village and were captured and killed. In connection with this, the mass execution that occurred at "Billé-mŏt-gul" became widely known not only to residents of Ŏ-ŭm-ri but also to nearby village residents for its atrocity. Households with young family members who had gone into the mountains—so-called "fugitive families"—or people who had attracted the attention of suppression forces, hid themselves, and when the cave was discovered, they were mass executed. These victims, mainly elderly and infirm people and women, are said to have entered Billé-mŏt-gul according to proverbs and oral legends. That is, sayings were prevalent in the village such as "During chaos, shun mountains and shun the sea," or "There is a place where 30 people can easily hide."

Billé-mŏt-gul, with a total length of 11,749 meters, is said to be the world's longest single-system lava cave and is designated as Natural Monument No. 342. The Billé-mŏt-gul in Ŏ-ŭm-ri territory was located near grassland and cultivated fields that residents frequently used, but until the 4·3 incident, except for a few people, its existence was scarcely known. This was because a narrow entrance large enough for only one person to squeeze through was blocked by a rock. In winter, warmth from the cave causes steam to rise like smoke, but residents knew of it only as one of the small "kwae"[^11] scattered in the area and had no idea such a large cave existed. In any case, on January 16, 1949, the cave was discovered, and suppression forces executed all those hiding inside, regardless of age or gender. Particularly notable is the account that at this time police grabbed the legs of small children, only a few years old, and smashed their heads against rocks—a story transmitted through the mouths of Civil Defense Unit members who were mobilized for suppression operations at the time and is still recounted in the area as a symbol of the horror.

But how was Billé-mŏt-gul, whose narrow entrance was barely noticeable, discovered, and if the entrance was narrow and one could have gone deeper into the cave, why were all those inside captured and killed? Yang T'ae-byŏng, the only survivor who hid in Billé-mŏt-gul at that time, testified as follows:

After the evacuation order was issued, I went with my mother to the neighboring village of Nabrŭp-ri. There

[^11]: "Kwae" is a Cheju dialect term referring to a cave that is covered above by a large rock or cliff and is deeply hollowed out below ground.

**[p. 410]**
