[^1]: 金祥甫 (72 years old, Tosari-ri, Pyoseongmyeon, recorded May 22, 2002) testimony; 김대은 (70 years old, Tosari-ri, Pyoseongmyeon, recorded May 23, 2002) testimony.

[^2]: '웃' (wut) in the Jeju dialect means "above" (上); '알' (al) means "below" (下).

[^3]: This record was created and submitted by residents in 1987.

[^4]: 金亮學 (62 years old, Tosari-ri, Pyoseongmyeon, recorded May 22, 2002) testimony.

[^5]: The date victims died is most accurately determined by their memorial service dates. However, those mobilized at this time were not summarily executed, so the mobilization date is imprecise. Consolidating various testimonies, the date appears to be around December 5, 1948. Following April 19, 1960, during the National Assembly's civilian massacre investigation, bereaved family members recorded the date as either "December 3" or "December 5."

[^6]: 第4代 國會, 양민학살사건진상조사보고서(6-1), 1960, 216쪽.

[^7]: 高昌善 (66 years old, Hagui-2-ri, Aewol-eup, recorded August 23, 2001) testimony.

[^8]: 安仁行 (67 years old, Jangjeon-ri, Aewol-eup, recorded August 21, 2001) testimony.

[^9]: 金癸順 (70 years old, Yonghung-ri, Aewol-eup, recorded August 22, 2001) testimony.

[^10]: 姜朝行 (78 years old, Yusuam-ri, Aewol-eup, recorded August 23, 2001) testimony.

[^11]: 濟州新報, June 24, 1960.

[^12]: 高泰玉 (Gasiri, Pyoseongmyeon) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, cited work ⑤, 95 pages).

[^13]: 吳國晩 (70 years old, Gasiri, Pyoseongmyeon, recorded May 29, 2002) testimony.

[^14]: 오태경 (71 years old, Gasiri, Pyoseongmyeon, recorded May 30, 2002) testimony.

[^15]: 金泰宇 (71 years old, Wahuri, Chocheon-eup, recorded September 26, 2001) testimony.

[^16]: 金太準 (Sinchonn-ri, Chocheon-eup) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, cited work ④, 406–408 pages).

[^17]: 金太奎 (66 years old, Ikuno-ku, Osaka, Japan, recorded December 26, 2002) testimony.

[^18]: 金世元 (Dopyeong-dong, Jeju City, son of victim Kim Byeong-hae) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, "4·3 Speaks," 445th installment, May 21, 1999).

[^19]: 高信鍾 (66 years old, Yonggangdong, Jeju City, recorded July 12, 2001) testimony.

[^20]: 朝鮮中央日報, February 9, 1949.

[^21]: 高潤燮 (75 years old, Bonggae-dong, Jeju City, recorded June 29, 2001) testimony.

[^22]: 宋基全 (Yonggangdong, Jeju City) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, "4·3 Speaks," 455th installment, August 20, 1999).

[^23]: 姜泰好 (73 years old, Geumseong-ri, Aewol-eup) testimony (Jemin Ilbo 4·3 Investigation Team, "4·3 Speaks," 428th installment, October 16, 1998).

[^24]: 梁泰柄 (74 years old, Eoumlri-1, Aewol-eup, recorded June 22, 2001) testimony.

**[p. 411]**

## Case of Sŏnhŭl-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn

That Sŏnhŭl-ri, located at the eastern end of the upland area of Choch'ŏn-myŏn, was burned by suppression forces occurred on November 21, 1948. Several days earlier, soldiers had come to the village and, late into the night, raided houses where lights remained on, shooting dead the house owners and four others. Because all residents had gone into hiding following that incident, there were no casualties on this day. After soldiers set fire to the deserted village and withdrew, the residents in hiding received word of an evacuation order. The elderly and infirm with connections to coastal villages were mainly evacuated, and many residents fled into the forests or caves near the village. However, the deaths of the residents began with those who heeded the suppression forces' commands and went down to the coastal villages. Just two days after evacuation, on November 23, approximately five young women in their twenties in the evacuation camp were shot dead at the beach of Hamdŏk-ri. Upon hearing this news, those still remaining in the village reached a state where they could not descend even if they wished. Residents mainly fled to "Sŏnhŭl-gos,"[^45] a vast camellia forest covering tens of thousands of p'yŏng. So thickly overgrown was Sŏnhŭl-gos that one could hardly determine direction, and with natural caves scattered throughout, it was ideal as a hiding place. However, beginning November 25, caves where residents had taken refuge were discovered in succession over four days, and residents of Sŏnhŭl-ri suffered most of their losses in the early stages of the severe suppression operation. On November 25, the "Panmot-gul" cave was discovered for the first time. Soldiers shot dead approximately fifteen people on the spot and brought several others alive to their post at Hamdŏk National School. On November 26, soldiers pressed those they had captured the previous day at Panmot-gul and discovered "Moksiмul-gul," where the largest number of residents were hiding. Soldiers took the elderly and women with babies to Hamdŏk National School, while the remaining residents were shot dead and their bodies were burned with gasoline. Yang Yun-hŭi (梁允熙), who survived this day at age seventeen, testified about hiding in Moksiмul-gul with her sister:

Those captured at Panmot-gul were being interrogated and revealed the location of Moksiмul-gul where we were hiding. When soldiers arrived around noon, we retreated deep into the cave. The soldiers came in with torches and fired indiscriminately. By around 5 p.m., we were all captured and came out. But the soldiers separated children under thirteen, women, and the elderly. Since I was seventeen, I was placed separately. A sudden itch on my back made me ask someone to scratch it, and as I moved toward the children, the young men were dragged away and shot. My sister and I were taken with other elderly people to the battalion headquarters at Hamdŏk National School and detained there. But they could not accommodate so many people in the U.S. military tents. So soldiers took dozens of people out each night for rough interrogation, and the next day executed them. Since I was small in stature, I said I was fifteen. They classified us into groups A, B, and C. Thanks to being listed on the same form as a first-year senior from a school where my relatives in the information section had conducted rescue efforts, I was classified as C-rank and released. However, my sister was shot dead on January 24, 1949 along with many others. Soldiers fired at them from Sŏ-ubong and they fell into the sea.[^46]

**[p. 412]**

Meanwhile, soldiers had one of the residents captured at Moksiмul-gul lead them to search for another cave. Just one day after Moksiмul-gul was discovered, on November 27, "Baenbændi-gul" near Ŭssbam-oreum was found, and those hiding there were shot dead. As Panmot-gul (November 25) → Moksiмul-gul (November 26) → Baenbændi-gul (November 27) were discovered in succession, so many residents that merely listing the names of victims was overwhelming were concentrated in losses in the early stages of the severe suppression operation. Kim Hyŏng-jo managed to survive despite being in both Moksiмul-gul and Baenbændi-gul. In order to pass down to his descendants the grievance of this suffering, even amid fugitive life, Kim kept a list of victims in the cave and buried it in a jar. He testified vividly about that time:

I was hiding in Moksiмul-gul when those at Panmot-gul died. The next day, a few of us went out to collect bodies. Suddenly mortar fire erupted loudly. Everyone was terrified and ran back into Moksiмul-gul. But I judged the cave would soon be discovered, so I fled upward. I went up to near Ŭssbam-oreum and hid in Baenbændi-gul. But the next day that cave was also discovered. We built a defensive wall inside, saying we would fight to the death. At one point someone said there was a breeze coming in from outside. We dug frantically at that spot and broke through to outside. When we came out, soldiers opened machine gun fire. I ran in panic. In the end, five of us escaped from that cave, but the rest died. When I went to Moksiмul-gul where I had been hiding, there was a sight too horrible to witness with open eyes. Bodies had been burned with gasoline and were intertwined. Except for two whose faces had burned severely, we could identify everyone. To prevent crows from attacking, we dug shallow graves and split wood into halves, carved names on them, and stood them beside the bodies. We made two notebooks listing the victims—one I kept and one we buried in a jar underground. I told the people with me, "If I die, find this document in the jar and tell the living." Sŏnhŭl-ri suffered greatly. In the case of Sŏnhŭl 1-district, which had 300 households at that time, I alone confirmed 157 deaths.[^47]

**[p. 413]**

### 6) Retaliatory Killings

When suppression forces were ambushed at their posts or during movement and suffered casualties, they immediately engaged in retaliation. Retaliation was directed mainly at "families of fugitives." This caused a vicious cycle where armed guerrilla raids were followed by killings of fugitive families. Not only did they target specific victims such as fugitive families, as in the case of Puk-ch'ŏn-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn, but they also made all residents of the region where they were ambushed targets of retaliation.

## Case of Puk-ch'ŏn-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn

On January 17, the most tragic incident known as the "Puk-ch'ŏn Incident" occurred in the coastal village of Puk-ch'ŏn-ri, Choch'ŏn-myŏn. Early that morning, part of a company from the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion stationed in Sehwa was ambushed on a mountain path at the entrance to Puk-ch'ŏn village on its way to the battalion headquarters at Hamdŏk. Two soldiers were killed. Panic-stricken village elders, after deliberation, took the soldiers' bodies on a stretcher to the battalion headquarters. Enraged soldiers shot dead ten elderly visitors at headquarters, except for one who was a police family member. Under an officer's leadership, approximately two squad-sized forces descended on Puk-ch'ŏn village. The time was around 11 a.m. Uniformed soldiers surrounded the village, burst into each house pointing rifles, and herded all people—men, women, old, young, and infirm alike—onto the school grounds, then set the entire village ablaze. Over four hundred homes turned to ash in a single morning. Approximately 1,000 villagers gathered on Puk-ch'ŏn National School grounds trembled with fear. The on-scene commander on the platform first had the Civil Defense Unit chief (Chang Un-gwan) called out and executed on the spot in front of the residents, claiming "poor watch posting." Soldiers again had police and military families called out and separated to the west side of the grounds. While soldiers forced children and students to their feet demanding they "identify communist families," frustrated at no success, they began dragging out dozens of residents and shooting them in fields near the school. This massacre continued until the battalion commander issued a cease-fire order around 5 p.m. Villagers testified that approximately 300 residents died that day. Later, the battalion commander told residents to come to Hamdŏk the next day and withdrew his forces. Among the survivors, some fled to the mountains, and others went to Hamdŏk. However, of the one hundred or so villagers who went to Hamdŏk as the battalion commander said, nearly 100 became victims in a "communist family purge operation." The incident left many families in Puk-ch'ŏn village without heirs.[^48]

**[p. 414]**

Regarding this incident, Kim Byŏng-sŏk made a remarkable testimony. Kim's mother was killed by armed guerrillas on May 13, 1948, because she had a police officer son. The police then urged his other sons to become police, and Kim Byŏng-sŏk also was specially recruited into the police at this time. Four of the eight brothers thus became police. Kim Byŏng-sŏk said, "My father told us, 'If you take revenge, their children will again take revenge on you, creating an endless cycle of grudges. Break the chain in your own generation,' and I calmed my anger. Rather, I prevented many unjust deaths." Kim Byŏng-sŏk, who had served in the police and directly experienced the Puk-ch'ŏn incident, testified as follows:

Thanks to having a driver's license, I entered the police and was assigned to the motor pool. One day a senior sergeant from the 2nd Regiment's 3rd Battalion came asking, "The battalion commander's jeep broke down. Can you lend us a vehicle?" The motor pool chief said, "The 3rd Battalion is stationed at Hamdŏk, and since that's your hometown, why not go and visit after so long?" and sent me. I originally drove a GMC, but that day I took an ambulance-type vehicle to Hamdŏk. That day, after taking the battalion commander around and visiting the 11th Company in Wŏl-chŏng and returning to battalion headquarters, the military vehicle ahead of us was ambushed in front of Puk-ch'ŏn National School. So a battalion-wide alert was issued and I went to Puk-ch'ŏn National School with the battalion commander. The soldiers burned all of Puk-ch'ŏn-ri while assembling all the residents on the school grounds. The battalion commander first said, "Extract military and police families," then held a staff conference in the car. Since it was an ambulance-type vehicle, there were long benches on either side, and about seven or eight officers gathered there. Various proposals came up, such as "Let's position mortars on the stone wall and annihilate them." But one officer suggested, "There are soldiers who haven't killed the enemy since enlisting, so it would be better to have each unit take a few people and carry out executions to handle this." This was ultimately adopted. I was almost beside myself. Puk-ch'ŏn-ri was close to my hometown of Hamdŏk-ri, and I had relatives and classmates there. I urgently pleaded with the battalion commander, and he ordered to spare them. But whether I was panicked or not, I couldn't remember the names of my relatives and classmates no matter how hard I tried. The only person I remembered was Jang Yun-sŏk, who was a driver like me, so I managed to get him and his family of seven or eight people out first. Then I continued to implore the battalion commander: "Our family lost my mother to the guerrillas, and four of my nine siblings are police in an anti-communist family. What ideology do these elderly people, women, and children being dragged away have? Please spare them." The battalion commander asked, "I want to spare them too, but how would we handle their food and clothing?" I answered, "Don't worry. If they just go to Hamdŏk-ri, they all have relatives there, and since it's a big village, everything can be arranged." The battalion commander, as I kept pleading, said, "Then you're responsible," and stopped the shooting. But several hundred had already been dragged away and executed.[^49]

**[p. 415]**

This testimony was that, with no means to accommodate people in houses already burned, they killed them, and to give individual soldiers experience in executions, they used rifles instead of mortars. Despite such incomprehensible suffering, Puk-ch'ŏn-ri residents could not speak about this incident afterward. In particular, residents were arrested and maltreated simply for weeping in mourning for the victims, which made the villagers even more withdrawn. The incident, called the "Aigŏ Incident" in the village, occurred on January 23, 1954. It was the memorial service for Kim Sŏk-t'ae (金錫泰), a Korean War casualty. That day, while residents held a farewell ceremony and what they called a "flower viewing celebration" on the school grounds, following one resident's suggestion that "today is the anniversary of the village burning six years ago, and it marks six years since innocent deaths here, let us observe a moment of silence for the spirits of those who died then," they did observe silence. The emotional residents, overcome with sorrow, burst into bitter weeping, which was reported to the police and caused them trouble.[^50]

After the April 19 Uprising of 1960, when the National Assembly undertook an investigation into civilian massacres, one media outlet covering the Puk-ch'ŏn collective killing damage ran a headline "A terrible nightmare, a village of widows...group memorial services every year like this" and reported, "If one calculates the ratio of male to female voters, it's almost 3 to 1, with men being so scarce."[^51] But soon the May 16 coup occurred, and the truth was buried again. The police even published a Cheju Police History in 1990, distorting the Puk-ch'ŏn incident as follows: "Bandits who attacked this village ruthlessly slaughtered or abducted most of the village men excluding children and the elderly. As the suppression forces attacked, bandits retreated to the mountains, while others hid in the village, engaging in looting and arson. Protracted mopping-up operations ensued, leaving Puk-ch'ŏn-ri devastated."[^52]

**[p. 416]**

It completely reversed the perpetrator and the Puk-ch'ŏn-ri residents. Even then, residents said nothing. Later, when the "4·3" truth investigation movement arose and the truth of the Puk-ch'ŏn incident became widely known, the police, when publishing a new Cheju Police History in 2000 ten years later, printed the same content. But unlike ten years before, many Cheju residents, including Puk-ch'ŏn residents, were angered by the book's contents. Faced with island-wide resistance, the Cheju Regional Police Headquarters ultimately recalled all copies of the Cheju Police History, cut out the "4·3 related sections," and redistributed them—a farcical affair.

## Case of Samyang-ri, Cheju-ŭp

Samyang-ri, located at the eastern end of Cheju-ŭp, is a coastal village that experienced unending casualties throughout the incident. Suppression forces constantly built stone barriers on the circumferential road to prevent vehicle passage—"road blockades" occurred frequently—and kept a constant eye on Samyang-ri, where police stations were frequently attacked. Police immediately arrested and beat any young people who appeared, and the youth created hiding places by tearing up the floorboards beneath houses or dug caves in fields to avoid this. However, on October 27, 1948, youths had no choice but to come out. It was the funeral of a village grandmother. Following the custom of mutual aid in times of trouble, youths in hiding appeared one by one to head to the burial site. Police, watching for their opportunity, attacked the burial site, arrested the youths, and detained them at Samyang Police Substation. At that point, no one expected the captured youths would be shot. But the next day, when armed guerrillas attacked Samyang Substation, they were immediately executed in retaliation. Suppression forces' retaliation against armed guerrilla offensives continued. In early November, suppression forces gathered all Samyang-ri residents at Samyang National School, designated approximately 35 young men, and dragged them to the military post at Hamdŏk-ri, half of whom were shot on November 12. Moon Ch'ang-hae (文昌海), whose family lost victims in this incident, said, "That day soldiers kept demanding that people who had previously participated in leftist organizations like the South Korean Workers' Party come forward, and when no one appeared, they arbitrarily picked youths, saying 'You, come out! You, come out!' and dragged them away to shoot them. When indiscriminate killings became the pattern like this, youths fled to the mountains."[^53] On November 27, youths hiding in caves near the village's boatyard were captured and shot dead, leaving no safe place near the village.

**[p. 417]**

The flight to the mountains of youths divided the residents. Those remaining in the village were forced to join the Taedong Youth Group (later reorganized as the Great Korea Youth Group) or Civil Defense Units and had no choice but to obey suppression forces' orders, while those who went to the mountains could not escape armed guerrilla influence. This situation, regardless of individual will, divided relatives and friends into armed guerrilla and suppression force factions. It was a tragedy imposed by the times. For a while, residents who remained in the village could avoid casualties by complying with suppression forces' orders. But on December 27, 1948, another incident occurred in which approximately ten residents lost their lives to suppression forces. This massacre too was soldiers' vindictive killing. That day soldiers went as far as Bonggae-ri Myŏngdo-am area for suppression operations, and contrary to expectation were ambushed by armed guerrillas. Angered soldiers, on the way down, encountered twelve residents standing sentry at the village entrance and unreasonably dragged them to a place called "Kouni-maru" and shot them dead. Kim Chŏng-bong was shot but did not die; he crawled home. His father, seeking to prevent future problems, carried his wounded son to the police station and reported it. But police immediately shot him dead. January 3, 1949, was a day Samyang-ri residents suffered great losses from both armed guerrillas and suppression forces. It was when the 2nd Regiment began replacing the 9th Regiment's duty in Cheju. The armed guerrillas' attack that day, seizing the transition moment, was large-scale. Guerrillas killed about ten residents standing sentry at the village entrance and set fire to houses on the path down from the mountains. Samyang National School also burned at this time. As the guerrillas withdrew, police without exception engaged in retaliatory executions. Those killed in retaliation were mainly so-called "families of fugitives" with missing youths in the family. Kim Yang-gŭn (金良根), a son of a retaliatory execution victim, testified as follows:

**[p. 418]**

On January 3, 1949, Samyang Police Station was attacked. When people in the mountains heard that their parents and siblings had been killed, they became so enraged they attacked the station and killed the sentries. Whenever mountain people attacked the station, the next day police executed people, calling them "mountain people's families." Innocent people killed innocent people. After the January 3 attack, families of mountain people were exterminated—even one-year-old babies. Our family never went to the mountains at all, and I was assigned to the Civil Defense Unit and even worked at the station, yet my father (Kim Yŏ-jin) and sister (Kim Im-saeng) were dragged away by police and died. My father's death was because he was the maternal uncle of someone who went to the mountains. My sister was eighteen at the time. My mother died of heartbreak a month after my father and sister passed away.[^54]

The vicious cycle of "armed guerrilla attacks and suppression force retaliation" was truly lamentable. Upon examining all gravestones in the police section of the Chung-hon Cemetery located in Cheju City, among the total 140 police casualties, △41 (29%) died during the period when armed guerrillas were in the ascendant (April 3–November 7, 1948), △17 (12%) during the severe suppression operation (December 5, 1948–March 1, 1949), and △82 (58%) during the period when the armed guerrillas were nearly wiped out with only dozens of remnant forces remaining (May 25, 1949–September 30, 1956). More police died when the suppression forces held overwhelming superiority after the severe suppression operation than when guerrillas were strong, indicating that the police only entered the "enemy territory" after the armed guerrilla forces had been substantially weakened. This also indicates that most people killed during the severe suppression operation were not armed guerrillas engaging in combat with suppression forces, but residents living in villages. If ambushed, they should have pursued the guerrillas to their location for suppression; why then did they conduct retaliatory executions of innocent residents in villages beyond their control? This is inexcusable under any circumstances.

Armed guerrilla attacks on Samyang-ri were persistent. On February 22, 1949, armed guerrillas again attacked Samyang-ri, killing four residents standing sentry and wounding one police officer. Suppression forces then carried out retaliatory executions starting February 23. Samyang Police Substation's Chief Chŏng was known not only to Samyang-ri residents but also to residents of nearby Doryŏn-ri and those evacuated from the uplands in Bonggae-yong, Hoengch'ŏn-ri. Most residents who experienced that time remember Chief Chŏng of Samyang Police Substation as one who made a habit of saying, "If I don't kill at least one person a day, I lose my appetite." Resident Kim Yang-gŭn testified, "At Samyang Police Substation there were Chief Chŏng, Sergeant Paek, Patrolman Kang, Patrolman Yi, and others—most were members of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps. Especially Chief Chŏng and Sergeant Paek were people who wouldn't eat if they didn't kill people."[^55] Kim Che-jin (金濟珍), a 10th generation Cheju Police who briefly worked at Samyang Substation, testified:

**[p. 419]**

When Samyang Substation was attacked, I was sent there for patrol duty for several days. The Sŏbuk Youth Corps member Chief Chŏng was extremely cruel. I witnessed him stripping women and engaging in obscene acts. On that freezing winter night, he stripped women and left them sitting in the watchtower for long periods. I pitied their trembling and gave them my coat to cover themselves. When morning came, he would drag dozens of men and women to the field beside the substation and kill them. Rather than shoot them with guns, he forced the village Taedong Youth Unit members to stab them with spears.[^56]

The infamous Chief Chŏng's name was Chŏng Yong-ch'ŏl. Ko Pong-su testified about the brutal massacre Chŏng Yong-ch'ŏl carried out on February 24, 1949:

I was serving as platoon commander of the Great Korea Youth Group. One morning I went to the station for regular reporting, and there was a young woman being held, saying her husband had gone to the mountains. For some reason, Chief Chŏng had inserted the gun barrel into a stove and heated it. Then he completely stripped the young woman. She was pregnant, and her belly and chest were exposed. Chief Chŏng thrust the red-hot gun barrel into her body below. It was a sight too horrible to see with open eyes. Chief Chŏng did that, and then burned her head with gasoline and killed her in a field beside the station. He ordered us to cover her body with dirt, but she was still not quite dead, and the dirt kept moving. That Chief Chŏng didn't live long, they say.[^57]

The victimized woman was Kim Chin-ok (金辰玉), age 21, the wife of Kim T'ae-saeng (金泰生), who had fled to the mountains. That day Kim T'ae-saeng lost his wife and parents, and the next day, February 25, he lost his father-in-law. A few days later he lost his mother-in-law and sister-in-law. With Kim T'ae-saeng's going to the mountains, both sides of his family were exterminated. The fact that Kim T'ae-saeng was a "Korean War participant" symbolically reveals how merciless the severe suppression operation was. Kim T'ae-saeng (died 1998) testified before his death:

**[p. 420]**

In autumn, seeing the indiscriminate shooting, I became frightened and fled to the mountains. After wandering here and there for a while, exhausted, I came down to Wŏndang-bong and hid. But then I was spotted by people who had come to plant pine trees at Wŏndang-bong. I was dragged to Samyang Police Station, beaten nearly to death, and then dragged to Cheju for interrogation. During interrogation, I saw that if you met good people you lived, and if you met mean ones you died. I was fortunate to meet good people. When I came home, the chief from before had left, and instead a Chief Ch'oe had come. Soon the war broke out, and I wrote in blood and volunteered for the army. My military number was 0310413. Many of those released with me volunteered for the army, and most died in combat.[^57]

Chief Chŏng Yong-ch'ŏl was an ordinary man who developed feelings for a unmarried woman detained in Cheju Police Station's detention room and showed her kindness. Kim Si-hun, a Sŏbuk Youth Corps member like Chŏng who came to Cheju as a police officer together with him, testified, "Chŏng came with me, and when we arrived he was specially recruited as a police officer, and I was assigned as chief of Samyang Substation. He had a strange personality and would immediately draw his gun to shoot if something displeased him. At that time, a police officer killing one person was as simple as killing a fly. But he was a person who would also meet with Yi Ok (李玉), a young woman he was infatuated with."[^58]

Why were Sŏbuk Youth Corps members turned into police and dispatched to Cheju, and how did they receive preferential treatment such as special recruitment as police officers from the start? According to U.S. military reports, on Syngman Rhee's decision, members of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps, noted as extreme anti-communists, were made into police, and to increase volunteers, they established a system where recruiting 20 members would produce 1 sergeant promotion, 50 members would produce 1 police officer promotion, and 200 members would produce 1 police inspector promotion.[^59]

**[p. 421]**

Chŏng Yong-ch'ŏl was specially recruited as a police officer under this very policy. It was President Syngman Rhee who hastily clothed Sŏbuk Youth Corps members in police uniforms and dispatched them to Cheju. Taking advantage of the circumstances of the Sŏbuk Youth Corps—with no one to rely on, having fled from the north alone and empty-handed, and harboring extreme hatred of communism—was the strategy. One scholar analyzed this as follows: "It may not have been necessary to specifically teach the (Sŏbuk Corps members) about their misdeeds and atrocities. One merely had to whisper the incantation, 'There are communists over there,' and like an automaton with tight springs, once released, it would proceed in its predetermined direction."[^60]

The Sŏbuk Corps members, too, may have been historical victims. The problem lies with those who instigated the Sŏbuk Corps.

### 7) Killings of Provisional Detention Suspects

#### ① Establishment of the "National Guidance League" in Cheju Region

Four months after the passage of the National Security Law on December 1, 1948, on April 21, 1949, a preparation meeting for the establishment of the National Guidance League was held, and on June 5, its founding declaration ceremony was held at Seoul City Hall.[^61] The League's activity goals were specified in its charter as follows:

1. We aim to absolutely support and nurture the Republic of Korea government
1. We aim to absolutely oppose and overthrow the North Korean puppet government
1. We aim to reject and crush communist ideology that disregards human freedom and national character
1. We aim to strengthen theoretical preparation and expose and crush the extermination policies of North and South Korean Workers' Parties
1. We aim to coordinate with political parties and social organizations of the national camp and mobilize all forces[^62]

That is, the establishment of the National Guidance League aimed at converting leftists and crushing the North and South Korean Workers' Parties. Externally, they said they would "guide" (보도) former leftists, but the intent was strongly to exterminate leftist forces through confessions submitted by converts.

**[p. 422]**

The National Guidance League was organized and operated according to the prosecution's organizational structure, with a central headquarters and local leagues in each region, though each province was permitted some degree of autonomous operation. The overall management policy was determined by the National Guidance League Operations Council, and each regional prosecution supervised local league members through regional police. The local branches were established by each provincial headquarters by November 1949 and by each city and county branch by December. The guidance committee, which practically guided and managed local branches, was composed of the chief prosecutor, police chief, police station commander, police station investigation section chief, city/county magistrate, warden, and others, and the specific guidance and operational policies of local league branches were finalized by these members. Each local branch's operating departments included Finance, General Affairs, Guidance, Projects, Organization, Propaganda, and Women's sections. After the local branch organizational and guidance structure was established, each local branch urged the National Guidance League membership through rightist organizations and police. In the Kyŏngnam region, until April-May 1950, Civil Defense Units and police investigation section chiefs at the township-myŏn-ri level directly took the initiative in recruiting members.[^64]

With the full activation of local branch organization and activities, the National Guidance League's membership expanded rapidly nationwide. It is clear that the National Guidance League was also organized in the Cheju region. In a report left by U.S. Embassy staff who investigated local conditions in Cheju on August 17, 1950, the following was recorded:

Approximately 27,000 registered National Guidance League members and some 50,000 relatives of those killed as communists during the past rebellion and afterward exist on Cheju Island as potential destructive elements.[^65]

Unlike the mainland, in the Cheju region, after going through the 4·3 incident, not only armed guerrilla members but also residents even slightly suspected by military and police had largely been killed, so there was no particular need to organize a new National Guidance League. A figure of 27,000 league members could be said to be excessive. The specific timing of the National Guidance League's organization in Cheju is difficult to ascertain due to lack of source materials. However, through the following source material, we can know that the league was organized by late November 1949:

In order to gain the backing of relevant authorities, the National Guidance League set the period from October 25 to late November as Leftist Convert Recruitment Week and was making every effort to recruit leftists, when as of the 27th, leftist elements who had come to surrender across South Korea had reached as many as 39,900. Regarding this, on the 1st, the Security Bureau Chief (Yi) released the following statement: "(Omitted)...The leftist conversion and voluntary surrender week set from October 25 showed unexpected results, and to universalize this grace not limited by time and geography to reach lawbreakers etc. in remote mountainous areas, the period was extended twice after October 25 and widely publicized. Up to November 27, the numbers of converts reported to the Interior Ministry by each regional police bureau were: Seoul 12,196, Kyŏnggi 5,964, Kangwŏn 4,978, North Ch'ungch'ŏng 3,512, South Ch'ungch'ŏng 1,054, North Kyŏngsang 1,938, South Kyŏngsang 2,143, North Chŏlla 1,660, South Chŏlla 115, Cheju 5,283, Railroad 143, total 39,986—showing great results (rest omitted)"[^66]

That is, by late November 1949, 5,283 converts in the Cheju region had been registered with the National Guidance League. Except for Seoul and Kyŏnggi, it was the largest number of voluntary surrenders and league registrations. This number continued to increase, reaching 27,000 members by June 1950, around the time of the Korean War. It is questionable why a region where countless lives had been lost during the 4·3 incident by 1949 needed nearly 30,000 managed targets again.

**[p. 423]**

The organization of the Cheju National Guidance League was by each village, and local police substations were involved in its management and operation, as suggested by the following material:

The defendant (Han Sŏng-bŏm) was appointed a Cheju police patrolman effective December 1, 1952, worked in the Police Station's Security Section for approximately one month, and on January 2, 1953, was ordered to work at Dodu-ri Substation, Cheju-ŭp, handling outside duties. While performing these duties, on March 24, 1953, at 2:30 p.m., by order of the substation chief, heading toward Dodu-ri to collect National Guidance League membership fees and encourage joining in the substation's jurisdiction of Cheju-ŭp, Ihŏ-ri 1st and 2nd districts, passing through Ihŏ-ri 1st district and heading toward the hilly new settlement of Ihŏ-ri 2nd district, I found numerous people gathered in a wheat field at a place called "Odorong" on Ihŏ-ri territory owned by Hong Yŏn-sim of Dodu-ri (and so forth...).[^67]

That is, each village's National Guidance League was managed by the relevant police substation, and police handled all membership fee collection and related administrative tasks.

Synthesizing testimonies from those in the police who were assigned the duty of recruiting people into the National Guidance League and managing them, in the Cheju region, the National Guidance League was made up of people who had engaged in "leftist activities" who had surrendered, and police investigation section staff were involved.[^68] There are also testimonies that the organization was merely for reporting to the government and making a roster, with no actual activities.[^69] However, in the case of Chongdal-ri, Kucha-myŏn, police directly recruited young people,[^70] and Ihŏ-ri residents of Cheju-ŭp attended a launching ceremony held at Dodu National School.[^71]

**[p. 424]**

The National Guidance League was organized in each police station jurisdiction with four locations within Cheju Province. There was a league headquarters in each police station jurisdiction, with local branches under each substation's jurisdiction. The headquarters' organizational structure consisted of a secretary-general, general affairs director, publicity director, and organization director. In the Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station jurisdiction, Kang Sŏk-jun from Sŏngsan was secretary-general, with organization chief Kang Sŏk-hong (Ojo-ri, Sŏngsan-myŏn), organization director Ch'ae Chŏng-ok (Chongdal-ri, Kucha-myŏn), and publicity director Pu Yŏng-sŏng (Hado-ri, Kucha-myŏn). Those who joined the National Guidance League were people with records of so-called "leftist activities," with former people's committee officials, those involved in the March 1 incident, and those who had faced trial or been imprisoned related to the 4·3 incident being the main targets.[^73] However, police forcibly recruited even those sympathetic to military and police, such as village Taedong Youth Unit chairmen and village chiefs, if they had past records. Moreover, in Chongdal-ri, nearly all youths became targets for enrollment. Kim Kye-su of Ŭigwi-ri, Namwŏn-myŏn, had fled to the mountains and subsequently surrendered, was detained in the Sŏgwipo Detention Center, and after being released was living in the village. He joined eagerly after hearing that "entering the National Guidance League would clear away the suspicion of Cheju 4·3 incident for mountain village residents."[^75]

While the National Guidance League was organized in Cheju with considerable membership in each village, no testimony confirms that league members directly engaged in specific activities. Only testimonies exist that league headquarters officials, centered around propaganda activities, held lectures and theatrical performances. In the case of the Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station-jurisdiction league headquarters, the league sponsored lectures visiting each village within the Kucha-myŏn, Sŏngsan-myŏn, and Pyŏgsŏn-myŏn areas under the station's jurisdiction, with considerable numbers mobilized. Although sponsored by the league, the police station's investigation section actually conducted proceedings. They also performed a play called "The Revolutionary's Vigil for Spouse."[^76]

#### ② Provisional Detention Process

When North Korea launched its full-scale attack on June 25, 1950, triggering the Korean War, the government implemented "provisional detention" of National Guidance League members and those suspected of anti-government activities as identified by police departments across the nation. "Provisional detention" is the preventive confinement of individuals likely to commit crimes, allegedly for crime prevention purposes, and its historical origins trace back to Japanese colonial rule. When World War II began, Japan constructed a wartime system, implementing the "Joseon Political Prisoner Provisional Detention Ordinance (1941)" in colonial Chosŏn. Moreover, as Japan's defeat neared in 1945, it sought large-scale provisional detention of anti-Japanese figures through "Emergency Measures No. 1 Against Special Circumstances." The main content of this measure was: if Soviet and Anglo-American forces land, respectively detain communism and nationalism-minded figures for observation; if the front line approaches police stations, move provisional detainees to the rear; if there is no time to move, dispose of them appropriately—that is, massacre them. This measure was sent in code to police station chiefs nationwide when Soviet forces declared war and advanced into Manchuria, rapidly annihilating Japanese forces.[^77]

**[p. 425]**

However, such provisional detention laws from the Japanese colonial period were immediately abolished when the U.S. Military Government began operations after liberation. Under U.S. Military Government Ordinance No. 11 of October 9, 1945, "all policies and measures discriminating against and oppressing the Korean people among the laws applying to the Korean people and their governance in the occupied areas south of the 38th parallel shall be eliminated, and among the laws giving the Korean people just politics and legal equality, Political Crimes Punishment Laws, Provisional Detention Laws, Maintenance of Public Order Laws, Press Laws, Political Criminal Protective Surveillance Ordinances, Shinto Rituals, and judicial police powers shall be repealed."[^78] Under this legal measure, provisional detention could not be implemented during the U.S. Military Government period. However, after the establishment of the Republic of Korea government, the Yŏsu-Sunch'ŏn Incident occurred in October 1948, and the Interior Ministry sought to implement provisional detention in Kwangju and Iri, the adjacent regions of the incident. When this was blocked by the responsible prosecutor at the time, the Interior Minister requested the National Assembly enact a "Communist Control Law" that would codify provisional detention.[^78] Provisional detention was also extensively implemented in Cheju from October 1948 onward.

When the Korean War broke out, the government transmitted an urgent directive at 2:25 p.m. on June 25 via telephone and telegram to each regional police headquarters from the Security Bureau Chief regarding "National Surveillance Target Apprehension and National Prison Security." This directive, transmitted to the Cheju Provincial Police Chief, contained orders to "immediately arrest all national surveillance targets" and "strengthen security of all national prisons."[^79] Subsequently, on June 29, a directive regarding "Arrest of Undesirable Elements" was transmitted, and on June 30, a directive regarding "Processing of Undesirable Elements" was transmitted to the Cheju Provincial Police Chief. The June 29 directive specified, "Arrest the National Guidance League and other undesirable elements and do not release them until receiving directions from this office."[^80] Subsequently, on July 6, a directive requesting "Submission of Provisional Detention Roster for the Entire Cheju Region" was transmitted from the Cheju Regional Martial Law Commander, and on July 11, another directive regarding "Apprehension of Undesirable Elements" was transmitted to the Cheju Provincial Police Chief.[^80]

**[p. 426]**

The fact of provisional detention was treated as "absolutely secret," knowable only through the police authorities' chain of <Security Bureau ↔ Cheju Provincial Police Chiefs ↔ Each Cheju Regional Police Station> and the military authorities' chain of <Ministry of Defense ↔ Cheju Regional Martial Law Command>. The Cheju District Prosecutor's Office at that time claimed that authority to investigate the propriety and illegality of provisional detention implementation lay with prosecutors and agreed to take appropriate measures regarding provisional detention, but the Cheju Provincial Police authorities refused, claiming they were following orders from above.[^81] As martial law was declared on July 8, all police, prosecutor, and court organizations came under military jurisdiction, and accordingly, the martial law commander took charge of provisional detention. Thus, after the declaration of martial law, provisional detention proceeded under military leadership with combined military-police operations, and the authority to process provisional detainees was solely with the military.

Following these directives from the government, immediate mass arrests of surveillance targets were implemented in Cheju. According to the Cheju Provincial Police headquarters' received documents, from late June through early August, provisional detention was implemented ranging from civil servants, teachers, to students and women.[^82] The following memorandum sent by the Mosŭlp'o Police Station chief to the Cheju Provincial Police Chief reveals mass arrests of "undesirable teachers" within the Mosŭlp'o Police Station jurisdiction and subsequent arrests of Posung National School teachers:

Regarding the above matter: Following the wireless order regarding the wholesale apprehension of school staff and those suspected of communist ideological matters on July 28, this office reports having apprehended undesirable teachers, and given that subsequent investigation is proceeding regarding those suspected of communist ideological problems, this office, based on careful deliberation regarding the apprehension of all teachers (Principal Koh Chang-gu et al., four persons) of Posung National School within this office's subordinate Daejŏng Police Substation—recognizing that apprehending all school staff would create serious operational difficulties for that school—given the current grave circumstances and the extremely unclear recent behavior of these individuals, which warrants attention, finds it an unavoidable emergency measure of the present serious period to apprehend all and detain them. Given that the school is currently suspended in management due to the resulting circumstances, it is urgent to contact the education authority for emergency teacher allocation. We report as follows (attachment): The four apprehended teachers are Principal Koh Chang-gu and teachers Yi Yun-bae, Hyŏn Ch'ang-min, and Hŏ Yŏng-p'il, and as of July 30, see Mosŭlp'o Memorandum No. 2942 regarding school staff and those suspected of communist ideology mass apprehension. We respectfully submit the above.[^83]

According to police documents, as of August 4, 1950, 820 persons among surveillance targets within Cheju Province were in provisional detention.[^84] Attention should also be paid to the following in a report from U.S. Embassy staff regarding detention numbers:

A total of 1,120 prisoners are detained on Cheju Island, with most held in various police detention facilities. Despite the need for classification between prisoners arrested under martial law and tried by martial law and other prisoners, information is not available. However, police state that 700 National Guidance League leaders have been arrested since June 25.[^85]

**[p. 427]**

Since the embassy staff were investigating Cheju on August 17, it is evident that 300 additional people were detained in the intervening two weeks. Moreover, given the lack of information about detainees, making classification impossible, we can see that at the time there were no clear criteria for provisional detention. However, police managed the 700 National Guidance League officers separately. The Cheju Provincial Police conducted a secret severity rating examination of these detained persons and transferred them to the Marine Corps martial law commander's headquarters in Cheju. Grading instructions for these persons were issued on July 7, 1950, by the Cheju Provincial Police to each police station.[^86] According to this memorandum, classifications were A, B, C, and D grades, with D grade being the most important persons, C grade being important persons, B grade being minor offenses, and A grade being questionable persons. The basis for these judgment criteria is unclear. Among those provisionally detained at that time were many with no record of leftist organization activity or going to the mountains during the 4·3 incident. This fact appears in police documents. In an August 15, 1950, request for release of provisional detention suspects submitted by the Sŏgwipo Police Station chief to the Cheju Provincial Police Chief, the grounds for detention of the provisional detention suspects were:

○ Spreading baseless rumors that North Korean People's Army has landed on Cheju Island
○ Suspected of interfering with police telephone lines; after receiving a three-year suspended sentence, behavior was ambiguous during wholesale apprehension
○ No past criminal record but behavior ambiguous
○ Suspected in leaflet distribution incident, apprehended under investigation
and other such reasons.[^87]

**[p. 428]**

Additionally, there were cases where individuals were provisionally detained on the grounds of having a bad attitude toward police following a minor argument while drinking with a police officer.[^88] During the provisional detention process, unlawful arrest based on such personal feelings and improper detention due to slander from malicious persons occurred frequently. At that time, the Cheju District Prosecutor's Office requested that police authorities submit a roster of detained persons, which the prosecutors intended to directly supervise to protect human rights. However, the police authorities responded that disclosure of the roster was a matter falling under emergency martial law and could not be submitted to any organization outside the Cheju Regional Martial Law Commander's office.[^89]

#### ③ Collective Killing of Provisional Detention Suspects

As the Korean War began, provisional detention was implemented nationwide, and systematic execution of detained persons by military authorities was planned. In the Cheju region, from late July through late August, large-scale collective killings were repeatedly carried out in Cheju-ŭp, Sŏgwipo, and Mosŭlp'o. This period coincided with when the North Korean People's Army advanced southward, engaging the UN forces on the Naktong River front line on August 8. Therefore, it appears that, preemptively treating Cheju Island as an anti-communist base amid the crisis of potential occupation of South Kyŏngsang Province and Pusan by the people's army, authorities executed the provisional detention suspects. The orders and execution of executions for provisional detention suspects held in police stations at that time were carried out by the Army Headquarters Information Bureau's Cheju Region CIC, the marine forces stationed in Cheju at that time, and the Cheju Provincial Police. The document supporting this is a directive of August 30, 1950, from Naval Captain Kim Tu-ch'an, Information Liaison Officer of the stationed Marine Corps in Cheju, to the Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station, titled "Request for Execution of Provisional Detention Suspects":

Regarding the above matter: Since the implementation of martial law in this province to the present, this office requests that regarding those in D-grade and C-grade provisional detention in your station for whom executions have not been carried out, your station carry out these executions and report the results to the Army Headquarters Information Bureau Cheju Region CIC Commander by September 6.[^90]

**[p. 429]**

According to this brief order, the Army Headquarters Information Bureau's regional organization directly ordered the police to execute, and the regional stationed martial law forces and police carried out the executions. Police could not independently decide or execute provisional detention and the execution of detention suspects. Kim Ho-gyŏm, who was the Sŏgwipo Police Station chief at that time, testified, "Provisional detention was not something that happened at the police level. Even as station chief, I could not save a friend who was provisionally detained."[^91]

During the Korean War, Retired Brigadier General Nam Sang-hui, who was Deputy Commander of the Naval Pohang Guard Post, testified that in early July 1950, on his orders, some 200 residents provisionally detained around Kyŏngju, Pohang, and Yŏngdŏk were loaded onto a naval ship, taken out to sea, executed, and then sunk. According to his testimony, he did not independently issue the execution order but received it from Minister of Defense Shin Sŏng-mo.[^92] There is also testimony that the order for execution of provisional detention suspects was issued by Kim Ch'ang-ryong, Chief of the Fourth Section (Counterintelligence, CIC) of the Army Headquarters Information Bureau.[^93]

Such executions of provisional detention suspects were carried out in extreme secrecy. The execution site of those detained at the Mosŭlp'o Police Station detention facility at "Sŏtal-oreum" on Songak Mountain was accidentally discovered by residents, but the time and place of execution and circumstances of those held at the Cheju and Sŏgwipo police station detention facilities remained strictly classified. The government was so thorough in maintaining security that in the National Security Council, the presidential directive stated, "Do not publicize police provisional detention."[^94] Even fifty years later, bereaved families do not know where the bodies of missing victims are buried, or even the date of death, causing them anguish.

## Case of Cheju-ŭp Provisional Detention

The complete number and execution losses of provisional detention suspects within the Cheju Police Station's jurisdiction in Cheju-ŭp, Choch'ŏn-myŏn, and Aewŏl-myŏn are impossible to ascertain due to the complete absence of related records. Moreover, not a single bereaved family member recovered a victim's body. Only word of mouth from those who directly or indirectly witnessed the execution on the day of death was passed to bereaved families. Synthesizing various reports and testimony containing these facts, the executions of provisional detention suspects under the Cheju Police Station jurisdiction were carried out in two stages.

**[p. 430]**

The first execution was carried out on August 4, 1950.[^95] Several hundred provisional detention suspects held at the Cheju Police Station's liquor warehouse and other facilities were dragged to Cheju Harbor, loaded onto ships, taken to the middle of the sea, and drowned. According to testimony from Chang Si-yong, who was working guard duty with the National Defense Corps at the Cheju Harbor pier dispatch, around 9 p.m., ten trucks arrived carrying 50 people each, and approximately 500 naked persons were loaded onto a ship and taken out to sea, returning empty about two hours later.[^96] Park Ch'un-t'aek, who served as marine force military official, and Kim In-p'yŏng, who was operating a cargo ship at Cheju Harbor, also testified that a substantial number of provisional detention suspects held at the Cheju Police Station liquor warehouse were loaded onto naval patrol boats and taken out to be drowned.[^97]

The second execution was carried out from the night of August 19 through the next morning, 1950. Primarily several hundred provisional detention suspects held in the Cheju Police Station detention room were loaded onto trucks, dragged to Cheju Airfield, executed, and secretly buried. Regarding the date and time of the Cheju Airfield execution, the testimonies of most bereaved families are consistent. Additionally, survivors who were detained together at the Cheju Police Station detention room and released after mid-September have testified to these facts.

## Case of Sŏgwipo Provisional Detention

The complete number and execution losses of provisional detention suspects within the Sŏgwipo Police Station's jurisdiction in Sŏgwi-myŏn, Chungmun-myŏn, and Namwŏn-myŏn are, as in the case of the Cheju Police Station jurisdiction, entirely absent from existing records. Moreover, not a single bereaved family member recovered a victim's body. However, most provisional detention suspects within the Sŏgwipo Police Station jurisdiction were detained in the Sŏgwipo dried sweet potato storage facility, and considerable numbers of bereaved families brought provisions and clothing to the facility through police guards. Therefore, bereaved families could either directly confirm or hear about the circumstances of the disappearance on the execution day. Additionally, testimony from survivors detained together at the Sŏgwipo detention facility could be collected.

**[p. 431]**

According to their testimony, the date of execution of Sŏgwipo provisional detention suspects is confirmed as July 29, 1950. Additionally, a survivor who witnessed their dragging away testified that approximately 150 were executed.[^98]

Some of those detained at the Sŏgwipo detention facility were transferred to the Cheju Provincial Police headquarters on August 12. The Dosun-ri execution roster contains a record of 12 names "transferred to headquarters" on August 12. Additionally, approximately 20 people, including Chungmun Middle School teachers separately detained at the Chungmun facility, went missing.[^99] These provisional detention suspects are presumed to have been executed together with Cheju Police Station jurisdiction suspects at the Cheju airfield. The "June 25 Provisional Detention Victim Survivors' Association of Three Villages" (established August 31, 2001; chairman Yi Pang-ch'un) had identified 77 provisional detention victims from Sŏgwi-myŏn, Chungmun-myŏn, and Namwŏn-myŏn as of July 2002.

## Case of Mosŭlp'o Provisional Detention

In the case of provisional detention in the Mosŭlp'o Police Station jurisdiction covering Taejŏng-myŏn, Hallim-myŏn, and Andŏk-myŏn, existing police records contain related data on execution victim rosters, enabling accurate assessment of the situation. Additionally, because residents who witnessed the execution scene at dawn on the day of execution quickly reported to bereaved families, the actual situation is well known.

First, if we organize the actual situation of Mosŭlp'o Police Station's provisional detention in a table as follows:[^100]

▪ Mosŭlp'o Police Station Provisional Detention Custody Situation: As of 3 p.m., September 3, 1950

Grade | Number | Transfer | Bail | Currently | Remarks
------|--------|----------|------|-----------|----------
D | 109 | 108 | 0 | 4 | In custody at this station; 3 of 4 recently noteworthy
C | 144 | 144 | 0 | 0 |
B | 68 | 9 | 0 | 59 | 59 in this station; 24 in Hallim
A | 23 | 10 | 0 | 13 | 13 in this station; 1 in Hallim
Total | 344 | 252 | 19 | 76 | 51 in this station; 25 in Hallim

**[p. 432]**

That is, the total number of provisional detention suspects in Hallim-myŏn, Taejŏng-myŏn, and Andŏk-myŏn under the Mosŭlp'o Police Station jurisdiction was 344, and of these, 252 were transferred to the military and executed.[^101]

According to the July 7, 1950, directive in which the Cheju Provincial Police ordered each police station to submit provisional detention rosters, the classification criteria specified that D-grade comprised the most important persons, C-grade important persons, B-grade minor cases, and A-grade questionable persons.[^102] Among these, provisional detention suspects in D-grade and C-grade categories were transferred to the military by police and collectively executed. In the case of Mosŭlp'o Police Station, the roster of provisional detention suspects transferred to the military has no record, but the number is listed as 252. These victims were those held at the Mosŭlp'o dried sweet potato storage facility and those detained at Hallim Fisheries Association warehouse under the Hallim Substation jurisdiction.

Those held in the Mosŭlp'o dried sweet potato storage facility were executed at 5 a.m. on August 20, 1950, and those detained at the Hallim Fisheries Association warehouse were executed at 2 a.m. on the same day. The execution location was inside a tunnel in what had been a Japanese ammunition depot in "Sŏtal-oreum" in Sangmo-ri, Taejŏng-myŏn, South Cheju County. Though at the same location, the positions where the victims from Mosŭlp'o and those from Hallim were executed differed slightly. On the execution day, when residents who witnessed the scene gathered 400–500 bereaved families attempting to recover bodies, counterintelligence troops prevented them from doing so. On March 30, 1956, Hallim bereaved families secretly recovered bodies at the execution scene. The Hallim area families recovered 61 bodies and buried them at a place called "Manbændi Communal Burial Ground" (land number 2754, Kŭmak-ri, Hallim-myŏn). In May 1956, bereaved families of those held in the Mosŭlp'o dried sweet potato storage facility gathered, petitioned relevant authorities, received permission, and undertook body recovery. As a result, they recovered 132 bodies, purchased cemetery land at 586-1 Sangmo-ri, Taejŏng-myŏn, buried them, and named it "Paekcho-ilson-chi-chi" (百祖一孫之地—"Land of the Hundred Ancestors' Single Descendants"), signifying "the descendants of Grandfather Paek."

## Case of Sŏngsan-p'o Provisional Detention

**[p. 433]**

In the case of provisional detention in the Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station jurisdiction covering Sŏngsan-myŏn, Kucha-myŏn, and Pyŏgsŏn-myŏn, the execution victim roster is uniquely recorded in existing police records among Cheju's provisional detention cases.

Among the Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station provisional detention suspects, D-grade numbered four and C-grade seventy-six. However, Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station Chief Mun Hyŏng-sun refused military orders and transferred only six to the military. The receipt for these six shows the specific roster: O Hui-sam (51 years), Ko Yŏng-hwan (24), Chin Byŏng-ryong (22), O Kun-p'yo (36), Kang Yong-bin (54), Kang Sŭng-hwan (22).[^103] On July 28 at 3 p.m., they were loaded onto two trucks heading to Sŏgwipo, and the military personnel from the marine force intelligence section who carried out the execution were identified as Ko Kwang-su, Im Mun-sŏn, Park Sŏ-sang, and Kim Chong-man.[^104] Recalling that the drowning of provisional detention suspects under Sŏgwipo Police Station jurisdiction occurred at dawn on July 29, these six were presumed to have been processed the same day as the Sŏgwipo suspects. However, regarding the remaining detention suspects, Mun Hyŏng-sun, Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station Chief, refused the military's execution order, thereby allowing a substantial number to save their lives. That is, as seen in the August 30, 1950, directive from Naval Captain Kim Tu-ch'an, commanding officer of the marine forces stationed in Cheju, to Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station, "Request for Execution of Provisional Detention Suspects," Mun Hyŏng-sun directly stated "improper; not implemented."

#### ④ Release of Provisional Detention Suspects

By September 1950, the execution of provisional detention suspects in the Cheju region ceased. The cause lay primarily in the withdrawal of the marine forces. The marine force (Commander Shin Hyŏn-jun) that had been stationed in Cheju to conclude the 4·3 incident withdrew from Cheju in late August 1950, deployed to the Inch'ŏn Landing Operation along with 3,000 Cheju youths who enlisted as the 3rd and 4th classes of marines. With marine force withdrawal and the large-scale enlistment of Cheju youths, a serious security gap arose in the Cheju region. Moreover, the "Undesirable Leaflet Distribution Incident" of July 27 led to a substantial increase in additional provisional detention suspects.[^104]

**[p. 434]**

The "Undesirable Leaflet" incident of August 31 was revealed to be the work of armed guerrilla members, and several provisional detention suspects under investigation on suspicion were shown to have been detained without cause. Accordingly, in early September 1950, each police station chief recommended the release of provisional detention suspects to the police chief. On September 6, 1950, Cheju Police Station Chief Yu Kŭn-ŏk inquired of the Cheju Provincial Police Chief: "With regard to detainees who show promise of ideological conversion, I believe it would aid popular sentiment and stability to release them and allow them to pursue their livelihoods. I respectfully request your appropriate directive on this matter."[^105]

In response, the Cheju Provincial Police Chief ordered the release of those with promise of conversion after review. Accordingly, the Cheju Provincial Police held a conference of investigation officials on September 14. At this meeting, it was agreed to eliminate the practice of slanderous informants causing innocent civilians to be victimized, release provisional detention suspects with promise of conversion among them, and allow them to fulfill their duties as citizens. After establishing review standards for release candidates, the following was determined: 1. Regardless of circumstances, authorities must prohibit summary execution and apply due process. 2. Authorities must conduct questioning with objective observation free from personal feeling. 3. Detention facility sanitation and facilities must be improved.[^106]

Based on the investigation authorities' review results, on September 18, 1950, 153 provisional detention suspects at Cheju Police Station were released. On the release day, Cheju Police Station held a release ceremony attended by the CIC commander, martial law command information liaison officer, provincial police investigation section chief, and numerous government officials and local notables.[^107] Subsequently, a second group of 48 provisional detention suspects held at Cheju Police Station was released.[^108]

**[p. 435]**

At Sŏgwipo Police Station, on September 17, all A-grade 43 and B-grade 77 suspects were released when the CIC commander came to the police station. However, 10 C and D-grade suspects remained in detention.[^109] At Mosŭlp'o Police Station, the review of provisional detention facility residents was completed before September 18, and 90 of the total 344 detained were released.[^110] This was release of those remaining at the facility after 252 were executed on August 20.

Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station first released six suspects related to the July 27 "Undesirable Leaflet" incident on September 5.[^111] On September 14, Sŏngsan-p'o Police Station held an investigation authorities meeting hosted by the CIC, where, under the CIC commander's direction, responsible personnel at the police station gathered and selected those designated for release. As a result, among a total of 215 provisional detention suspects (177 men, 38 women), 102 A-grade (82 men, 20 women) and 96 B-grade (82 men, 14 women) were designated for release. However, 16 C-grade and 1 D-grade were separated as intended to remain in continued detention.[^112]

#### ⑤ Occurrence of the "Notables Incident"[^113]

In early August 1950, sixteen prominent persons including a court chief judge, prosecutor chief, Cheju-ŭp magistrate, and lawyers, business leaders, and educators were taken into custody by the Cheju Regional Martial Law Command (Commander Colonel Shin Hyŏn-jun), accused of establishing a "Korean People's Army Welcome Preparation Committee."[^114]

According to investigation by Shin In-ch'ŏl, Captain, and others working in the marine force information staff, the accused allegedly held a secret meeting at Hong Sun-wŏn's residence on July 10, 1950, organized the Korean People's Army Welcome Preparation Committee, elected Ch'oe Nam-sik as chairman and selected other officers for each department, and decided to collect 10,000 won per member in membership fees. Secret meetings continued from July 14 through August 5, with even discussion of organizing an army-police assassination unit and selecting assassination targets. Ultimately, Second Section Chief Shin In-ch'ŏl captured six people including Kim Tae-hong, chief of Cheju Provincial Hospital, on August 8, and on August 9 captured and detained Kim Chae-ch'ŏn, Chief of the Cheju District Court, and fourteen other high-ranking provincial and local notables. Over approximately ten days following detention, working with information section staff, including Naval Captain Kim Chong-man, Army Sergeant Park Sŏ-sang, and Constables Yu Ho-sŏn and others, subjected them to water torture, club beatings, and execution threats because they refused to confess. Moreover, from 12 p.m. on August 13 until 3:30 p.m. the next day, Second Lieutenant Min Yong-sik of the information staff tortured the accused Jang Yong-mun with extreme severity, resulting in his death at 4 p.m. on August 14.

**[p. 436]**

The U.S. Embassy was aware of this incident. In an August 17, 1950, report, the following was recorded:

The Marine Force Information and Operations Staff (G-2) alleges that thirteen prominent Cheju figures held meetings on July 10, 15, and 22 to discuss procedures for welcoming North Korean forces if they come to Cheju Island. This information is based on confessions of two persons arrested for distributing so-called communist propaganda leaflets. They successively identified thirteen Cheju notables as being behind the leaflet distribution and connected to the holding of the meetings mentioned above. The Marine Force justifies its actions by claiming several of those arrested were previously arrested as involved in the Cheju rebellion three years ago.[^115]

When prominent figures representing Cheju society—court chief judges, prosecutor chiefs, and the Cheju-ŭp magistrate—were arrested, Cheju Provincial Governor Kim Ch'ung-hui viewed the incident as a frame-up to arrest specific individuals and, in consultation with Police Chief Yi Sŏng-ju, sent affidavits requesting clarification to Interior Minister Cho Byŏng-ok and Defense Minister Shin Sŏng-mo. CIC Commander Kung Byŏng-sun also submitted a report to the Ministry of Defense stating the incident differed from the facts. Eventually, Sŏn Wu-jong-wŏn, Chief of the Interior Ministry's Security Investigation Guidance Section, was dispatched to Cheju for an on-site investigation.

**[p. 437]**

Eventually, on August 28, a military-police joint investigative unit (Chief Army Captain Yi Ok-bong, Advisor Police Chief Sŏn Wu-jong-wŏn) was established, with an investigation team composed of two army members, two navy members, and two police officers dispatched from the capital. The military-police joint investigative unit's investigation revealed that the so-called Korean People's Army Welcome Preparation Committee was a completely nonexistent organization, resulting from false accusation by undesirable elements. That is, Second Lieutenant Shin In-ch'ŏl had falsely extracted from Ch'oe Nam-sik (arrested as a suspect in the July 27 "Undesirable Leaflet Distribution Incident") a confession about establishing a "People's Struggle Committee," and by leading questioning had fabricated the involvement of prominent persons and officials, manufacturing the entire incident. Upon investigation, it was revealed that the incident was fabricated by Second Lieutenant Shin In-ch'ŏl and others. Shin In-ch'ŏl, Park Sŏ-sang, Yu Ho-sŏn, and others were arrested on charges of official misconduct and assault. On September 3, at 6 p.m., the accused, Yi Yun-hui and 14 others, were released. The investigation was completed with the preparation of a comprehensive report on September 10 at 5 p.m., and the joint investigative unit was dissolved.

Although the case was closed, torture during the interrogation of detainees resulted in Jang Yong-mun's death from torture, and Cheju District Court Chief Judge Kim Chae-ch'ŏn suffered mental derangement from torture aftereffects and died prematurely.[^116]

The "Notables Incident" caused extreme psychological withdrawal in Cheju society. Cheju residents directly witnessed the current court chief judge and prosecutor chief arrested by martial law forces facing death. Moreover, because this case proceeded in parallel with the large-scale collective executions of provisional detention suspects on August 20 in Cheju-ŭp and Mosŭlp'o, August was a month of dread and terror.

### 8) Armed Guerrilla Killings

In the early stages of the 4·3 armed uprising, armed guerrillas targeted and killed police, members of rightist organizations like the Sŏbuk Youth Corps and Taedong Youth Units, and rightist sympathizers cooperating with military-police as well as their families. However, after November 1948, following indiscriminate suppression operations, they targeted specific villages they judged to be sympathetic to suppression forces and indiscriminately killed residents. Villages such as Sehwa-ri of Kucha-myŏn, Sŏngyŏp-ri of Pyŏgsŏn-myŏn, Namwŏn-ri and Wimi-ri of Namwŏn-myŏn suffered greatly from armed guerrillas because they were deemed "suppression force bases." As armed guerrilla forces neared annihilation, desperate remnant bands sometimes entered villages seeking provisions and killed residents standing sentry.

## Case of Sehwa-ri, Kucha-myŏn

Sehwa-ri was one of only two police substation locations in Kucha-myŏn. Additionally, it was a village with strong rightist forces that maintained good coordination with suppression forces. On the night of December 3, 1948, around 9 p.m., armed guerrillas, targeting Sehwa-ri, launched a massive attack. Upon entering the village, guerrillas immediately set fire to houses along the road and began indiscriminately killing residents. The guerrillas, effectively taking control of the village, burned approximately 40 homes and 150 other structures before withdrawing around 2 a.m. Including those evacuated from the uplands, approximately 50 residents died. Ji Hyŏng-ch'ang, who lost eight family members and relatives in this incident, testified:

The Sehwa-ri Civil Defense Unit was the strongest in Cheju Province. That day, there was talk that all South Korean Workers' Party members from Cheju participated in the attack. They fired three shots around 9:30 p.m. as a signal and attacked together, setting fire as they came down to the road. They didn't ask if you were "a South Korean Workers' Party member or Civil Defense member"—they just killed anyone they saw. They also stole food and clothing. At the time, there was a relief force at the station and substation personnel, but caught by surprise, the police didn't come out past the gate.[^117]

**[p. 438]**

The Sehwa substation had approximately 15 regular personnel and 20 South Ch'ungch'ŏng relief police, totaling approximately 35 police, yet did not move from inside the substation until the guerrillas withdrew. When day broke, police gathered 16 residents who had been detained at the Sehwa substation in a field beside the station and shot them dead in front of all villagers. A few days later, families of armed guerrillas from Sehwa-ri were dragged out and shot dead.

## Case of Namwŏn-ri and Wimi-ri, Namwŏn-myŏn

Armed guerrillas attacked Namwŏn-ri, the location of Namwŏn police substation and the Namwŏn-myŏn administrative office, at 7 a.m. on November 28, 1948. At that time, approximately 30 police including about 20 relief police were at Namwŏn substation. When guerrillas surrounded the station with earthen walls, police remained inside until the guerrillas withdrew. Guerrillas swept through the village for several hours, indiscriminately killing approximately 30 residents, burning most houses, and looting provisions. Guerrillas simultaneously attacked the neighboring village of Wimi-ri. Wimi-ri had a Wimi police substation. They simultaneously attacked two police substation locations, both in Namwŏn-myŏn. This incident, in which both suppression force posts were ambushed while severe operations were ongoing, was shocking. The U.S. side apparently paid attention, as relatively detailed reports remain.

**[p. 439]**

At dawn on November 28, approximately 200 armed and 500 unarmed rioters attacked Namwŏn-ri and Wimi-ri. According to the report, 250 houses were set ablaze by the rioters, 50 civilians were killed, 70 civilians and 3 police were wounded. Police returned fire but had to withdraw due to lack of ammunition. One constabulary company and 30 police were sent from Sŏgwipo to attack the rioters. Recent reports state that in this operation 30 rioters were killed and 3 captured, with 1 police wounded (police report).[^118]

Armed guerrillas, as in Namwŏn-ri, swept through Wimi-ri killing even elderly and infirm women, looted provisions, and set fires. Hyŏn Pong-hyŏp's mother lost her life trying to retrieve food and clothing from a burning house. Hyŏn Pong-hyŏp testified about the Wimi-ri situation:

The Wimi substation chief was a northerner who treated youths harshly, fearing they might go astray toward the mountains. If anything seemed suspicious, he would arrest them at the station. Because of this, unlike other villages, there were no Wimi-ri youths going to the mountains or sympathizing with them. So next, those in the mountains tried to negotiate during the night, but the station's strict control made this impossible. Seeing Wimi-ri wasn't cooperating, they decided to burn it down. It was extremely severe.[^119]

## Case of Tumo-ri, Hallim-myŏn

**[p. 440]**

Around midnight, December 15, 1948, armed guerrillas arrived at Tumo 2-district, Hallim-myŏn (now Hanwŏn-ri, Hangyŏng-myŏn). The night of the incident, residents stood sentry along the road, centered around the Civil Defense Unit. However, as the wall had not yet been built, guerrillas easily entered the village. Every night a Tumo substation police officer patrolled, but that night did not. Guerrillas, apparently to gauge the situation, set fire to one house at the village entrance and shouted, "Good people, come out and extinguish the fire!" The elderly Kim Kyŏng-sŏk, who came out, was killed. Woman Park Chŏng-saeng came out with a bucket in response to the shout to extinguish fire. This was due to an old saying that urine sprinkled would prevent fire from spreading. Guerrillas brutally killed woman Park with a Japanese sword. Guerrillas then occupied the village, looted provisions, set fire to some houses, and grew bolder. However, as police from Tumo 1-district substation and relief police from Koson-ri advanced, guerrillas, signaling with bugles, quickly withdrew. From attack to withdrawal took less than an hour, but including those who died later from wound complications, 13 residents were killed in this incident.[^120]

Tumo 2-district was located further toward the mountains than Tumo 1-district, but was only about 1 km from the circumferential road and was adjacent to a police substation, so it was excluded from the evacuation zone. Residents cited this as the first reason for their village being attacked—that with all upland villages evacuated, Tumo 2-district became the front line. Additionally, residents stated that with no village members having fled to the mountains and strict security maintained without any support for guerrillas, the village was targeted by the guerrillas.

## Case of Sŏngyŏp-ri, Pyŏgsŏn-myŏn

**[p. 441]**

Sŏngyŏp-ri of Pyŏgsŏn-myŏn is an upland village that did not burn. While suppression forces burned almost all other upland villages, they exempted Sŏngyŏp-ri, the location of the Sŏngyŏp police substation. Because of this, despite being located in the uplands farthest from the coast within Pyŏgsŏn-myŏn, no evacuation order was issued, and instead it became an advance base for suppression forces. Under the strict control of the police substation, residents were incorporated into Civil Defense Units and mobilized for suppression operations. Armed guerrillas could not easily approach this village with military-police stationed there, so for a time things proceeded without incident. However, on January 13, 1949, Sŏngyŏp-ri residents experienced significant events. That day residents were busy from morning preparing for operations. That day's operation, led by the military, involved one military squad, approximately 30 police from Pyŏgsŏn and Sŏngyŏp substations, and most of the special operations unit of Civil Defense Units in Pyŏgsŏn-myŏn—a major joint suppression operation.

Meanwhile, while the joint suppression force was conducting search operations in the mountains, a woman was being executed in the village. The woman, whose husband had fled to the mountains, had been traveling heavily pregnant and gave birth upon arriving at Sŏngyŏp-ri where she had relatives. Police not participating in suppression operations dragged her out, stripped her in front of village women, forced them to stab her with spears, and then shot her. When the baby beside her struggled, police shot it in the face.

**[p. 442]**

Later that afternoon, residents were busily preparing dinner for the time the suppression force returned. Suddenly an air-raid siren sounded and someone shouted, "The bandits are attacking!" It was around 5 p.m., just as darkness was beginning. Armed guerrillas were counterattacking in the gap left by the joint force. Guerrillas killed residents standing sentry on the village periphery and remained about two hours, going house to house to loot provisions. At this point, residents who did not surrender provisions were killed, and some houses burned. Sŏngyŏp National School also burned this day. A monument carved with the names of 38 people killed that day stands at the village entrance.[^121]

## b. Deaths of Imprisoned Inmates

From the March 1, 1947, event that became the starting point of the 4·3 incident, through April 3, 1948, and into 1954, thousands of people related to the 4·3 incident received judicial trials and sentences. Judicial proceedings related to the 4·3 incident included general trials conducted by the Cheju District Court, Kwangju District Court, Taegu High Court, and Supreme Court; military government trials conducted during the U.S. Military Government period; military courts-martial targeting military personnel and civilian auxiliary personnel; military courts-martial targeting civilians declared under martial law in December 1948; and exceptional military courts-martial targeting civilians applying the Korean Constabulary Law in July 1949.

Many people tried in cases related to the 4·3 incident received sentences of fines, detention, or suspended sentences, but those sentenced to imprisonment or hard labor were scattered in penal institutions throughout the nation since Cheju Province had no penitentiary. While some of these imprisoned inmates completed their sentences and were released, many died in the inadequate prison environment, and substantial numbers were executed by military order on the outbreak of the June 25, 1950, Korean War, following superior orders to eliminate undesirable elements. Regarding the deaths of these imprisoned inmates, with no knowledge of the time, place, or circumstances of death, bereaved families reported them as missing persons to the 4·3 Incident Commission.
